topics.title: Purpose
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT743 - INDUSTRIAL PROCESS CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Purpose

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of Industrial Process Control, the **"Purpose"** refers to the fundamental engineering objectives that automatic control systems are designed to achieve when supervising, regulating, and managing industrial processes. A process, by KTU 2024 Scheme definition, is any operation in which one or more physical or chemical quantities (such as temperature, pressure, flow, level, or composition) are intentionally transformed, transported, or maintained to yield a desired output.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Standardized):**
> *The purpose of industrial process control is to maintain process variables at their optimal operating values, ensure product quality consistency, maximize operational safety, minimize energy and raw material consumption, and suppress the influence of internal and external disturbances — all in real-time, with minimal human intervention.*

The control system acts as an intelligent intermediary between the **process (plant)** and the **operator**, continuously comparing the measured actual condition of a variable against a pre-set **desired value (setpoint or reference input)** and issuing corrective actions to eliminate the deviation known as the **error signal**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Thermoregulation Analogy

To make this immediately intuitive, consider the human body's thermoregulatory system:

- **Setpoint:** $37^\circ\text{C}$ (normal body temperature)
- **Measured Variable (PV):** Actual skin/blood temperature
- **Error Signal:** Difference between $37^\circ\text{C}$ and the current temperature
- **Controller:** The hypothalamus in the brain
- **Final Control Element:** Sweat glands (cooling) or shivering muscles (heating)
- **Disturbance:** Walking into a hot room or cold wind

Just as the body *automatically* and *continuously* adjusts to keep temperature near $37^\circ\text{C}$ without conscious effort, an industrial process control system does the same for a chemical reactor, boiler, or distillation column. The "purpose" of control is therefore to embed this **automatic self-correcting intelligence** into machines.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter for KTU exams?**
> The "Purpose" question is one of the most frequently asked 3-mark and 14-mark questions in Module 1 because it sets the foundation for every subsequent topic (controllers, transfer functions, stability, etc.). Examiners expect students to articulate **at least four distinct purposes** with examples.

## 1.3 Key Performance Metrics Introduced

The following standard engineering metrics are used throughout KTU process control to quantify how well the purpose is achieved:

- **Setpoint (SP):** The desired target value, denoted $r(t)$.
- **Process Variable (PV):** The measured actual value, denoted $y(t)$ or $c(t)$.
- **Error Signal:** $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$.
- **Manipulated Variable (MV):** The quantity adjusted by the controller, denoted $u(t)$.
- **Disturbance (D):** Any unwanted input that pushes the process away from the setpoint, denoted $d(t)$.

These symbols form the universal vocabulary of the **block diagram** that students will draw repeatedly across all KTU modules.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Core Purposes of Industrial Process Control

A KTU examiner will expect students to list, define, and exemplify the following five purposes. Each is rooted in real plant engineering needs.

### 2.1.1 Purpose 1 — Safety of Personnel, Plant, and Environment

- The **primary** and non-negotiable purpose of any control system.
- Industrial processes often operate at extreme temperatures ($> 500^\circ\text{C}$), high pressures ($> 100$ bar), and with toxic/flammable chemicals.
- A failure to control can lead to explosions (e.g., Bhopal disaster, 1984), fires, or toxic releases.
- Control systems include **interlocks, alarms, and emergency shutdown (ESD)** subsystems to prevent catastrophic failure.

### 2.1.2 Purpose 2 — Maintaining Product Quality and Consistency

- Modern products (pharmaceuticals, semiconductors, food, polymers) require **tight tolerances** (often $\pm 0.1\%$ of setpoint).
- Manual control cannot achieve this due to human reaction time ($> 1$ s) and fatigue.
- Automatic control reproduces identical quality across millions of units — the foundation of mass production.
- Example: Maintaining column temperature in a distillation tower to $\pm 0.5^\circ\text{C}$ ensures consistent gasoline octane rating.

### 2.1.3 Purpose 3 — Maximizing Economic Profit / Minimizing Operating Cost

- Reduces raw material waste by operating at the stoichiometric optimum.
- Minimizes energy consumption by avoiding overshoot and unnecessary heating/cooling.
- Increases throughput (production rate) without sacrificing quality.
- Example: A properly tuned level controller in a refinery prevents tank overflow, saving millions in lost product.

### 2.1.4 Purpose 4 — Suppression of the Influence of Disturbances

- Disturbances are **unavoidable** in real plants: ambient temperature changes, raw material composition variations, fouling of heat exchangers, catalyst deactivation.
- The control system's job is to make the process **insensitive** (robust) to these disturbances.
- Quantified by the **disturbance rejection ratio** — how much the output deviates per unit disturbance.

### 2.1.5 Purpose 5 — Enabling Operation Near Optimum / Constraint Boundaries

- Modern plants push operating conditions to the limit of equipment capability to maximize yield.
- Control systems hold the process precisely at these optimal but risky points.
- Example: Operating an exothermic reactor at $T_{\text{optimum}}$ within $1^\circ\text{C}$ of the runaway temperature.

## 2.2 The Generalized Closed-Loop Purpose — Block Diagram Logic

The unified mathematical purpose of a feedback control system is expressed as:

$$\lim_{t \to \infty} e(t) = \lim_{t \to \infty} \big[ r(t) - y(t) \big] \to 0$$

That is, the **steady-state error** must vanish for step inputs (a property called **zero steady-state error**), ensuring the process variable tracks the setpoint.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:**
> These five purposes are not academic — they are the criteria used by the **International Society of Automation (ISA)** and **IEC 61511** standards to certify process control systems in oil & gas, pharmaceuticals, and power plants worldwide.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Symbol / Term | Definition | Mathematical Form | Engineering Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $r(t)$ | Setpoint (Reference) | $r(t)$ | Same as PV (e.g., °C, bar, m³/h) |
| 2 | $y(t)$ or $c(t)$ | Process Variable (Measured Output) | $y(t)$ | Same as SP |
| 3 | $e(t)$ | Error Signal | $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$ | Same as PV |
| 4 | $u(t)$ | Controller / Manipulated Output | $u(t)$ | Depends on final element |
| 5 | $d(t)$ | Disturbance Input | $d(t)$ | Process-dependent |
| 6 | $G_c(s)$ | Controller Transfer Function | $\dfrac{U(s)}{E(s)}$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| 7 | $G_p(s)$ | Process Transfer Function | $\dfrac{Y(s)}{U(s)}$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| 8 | $H(s)$ | Sensor / Feedback Transfer Function | $\dfrac{B(s)}{Y(s)}$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| 9 | $G(s)$ | Forward Path Gain | $G(s) = G_c(s) \cdot G_p(s)$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| 10 | $T(s)$ | Closed-Loop Transfer Function | $\dfrac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)}$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| 11 | $e_{ss}$ | Steady-State Error | $e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} sE(s)$ | Same as PV |
| 12 | $K_p$ | Proportional Gain | $K_p$ | Dimensionless |
| 13 | $\tau$ | Process Time Constant | $\tau$ | seconds (s) |
| 14 | $\zeta$ | Damping Ratio | $\zeta = \dfrac{c}{2\sqrt{mk}}$ | Dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table Syntax Note:** Absolute value and vertical bar notations are written using $\vert \cdot \vert$ in LaTeX (e.g., $\vert e(t) \vert$) to avoid breaking markdown table pipes. This is mandatory in KTU 2024 digital answer sheets.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Fundamental Closed-Loop Error Equation

The **purpose** of a control system can be expressed as a single, traceable mathematical identity. Below is the exhaustive step-by-step derivation that KTU board examiners expect for a 7 or 14 mark answer.

### Step 1 — Define the Physical Block Diagram Components

Consider a standard unity-feedback (or non-unity feedback) control loop. The forward path consists of the **controller** $G_c(s)$ in series with the **process/plant** $G_p(s)$, producing a total forward gain:

$$G(s) = G_c(s) \cdot G_p(s)$$

The measured output $Y(s)$ is fed back through the **sensor** transfer function $H(s)$ to produce the feedback signal $B(s)$.

### Step 2 — Express Each Signal as a Function of Inputs

The process output in the Laplace domain is driven by both the controller output and the disturbance:

$$Y(s) = G_p(s) \cdot U(s) + G_d(s) \cdot D(s)$$

where $G_d(s)$ is the disturbance transfer function. The control signal is:

$$U(s) = G_c(s) \cdot E(s)$$

The error signal is the difference between the reference and the feedback:

$$E(s) = R(s) - B(s) = R(s) - H(s) \cdot Y(s)$$

### Step 3 — Combine into a Single Equation for E(s)

Substitute $U(s) = G_c(s)E(s)$ into the $Y(s)$ equation:

$$Y(s) = G_p(s) \cdot G_c(s) \cdot E(s) + G_d(s) \cdot D(s)$$

Now substitute this $Y(s)$ expression into the $E(s) = R(s) - H(s)Y(s)$ equation:

$$E(s) = R(s) - H(s) \cdot \big[ G_p(s) G_c(s) E(s) + G_d(s) D(s) \big]$$

### Step 4 — Collect the E(s) Terms

Expanding and gathering all $E(s)$ terms on the left-hand side:

$$E(s) + H(s) G_p(s) G_c(s) E(s) = R(s) - H(s) G_d(s) D(s)$$

Factoring $E(s)$:

$$E(s) \cdot \big[ 1 + G(s) H(s) \big] = R(s) - H(s) G_d(s) D(s)$$

### Step 5 — Solve for the Error Signal E(s)

$$\boxed{\, E(s) = \dfrac{1}{1 + G(s)H(s)} \, R(s) \; - \; \dfrac{H(s) G_d(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)} \, D(s) \,}$$

### Step 6 — Interpret the Result (The "Purpose" Made Quantitative)

The above equation reveals **the two simultaneous purposes** of any feedback control system:

1. **Reference Tracking Term:** $\dfrac{1}{1+G(s)H(s)} \cdot R(s)$
   - For good tracking, we need this term to equal $R(s)$, i.e., the multiplicative factor must approach **1**.
2. **Disturbance Rejection Term:** $-\dfrac{H(s)G_d(s)}{1+G(s)H(s)} \cdot D(s)$
   - For good disturbance rejection, this term must approach **0**, i.e., the factor must be as small as possible.

Both objectives are satisfied if and only if $\vert G(s)H(s) \vert \gg 1$ over the operating frequency range. This is the **defining design rule** of process control loops.

### Step 7 — Apply the Final Value Theorem for Steady-State Error

For a unit step input $R(s) = \dfrac{1}{s}$ and zero disturbance $D(s)=0$:

$$e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} sE(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \dfrac{1}{1+G(s)H(s)} \cdot \dfrac{1}{s} = \dfrac{1}{1 + \lim_{s \to 0} G(s)H(s)}$$

If $G(s)H(s)$ contains an integrator (i.e., the controller is PI or PID), then $\lim_{s \to 0} G(s)H(s) = \infty$, and therefore $e_{ss} = 0$ — perfectly achieving the purpose.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Purposes to Engineering Examples

| # | Stated Purpose | Real Plant Example | Disturbance Suppressed | Variable Controlled |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Safety | Boiler steam drum level trip | Sudden loss of feedwater | Level (m) |
| 2 | Product Quality | Pasteurization in dairy plant | Variation in raw milk fat % | Temperature (°C) |
| 3 | Economic Profit | Crude oil distillation reflux ratio | Ambient cooling water T changes | Reflux flow (m³/h) |
| 4 | Disturbance Suppression | pH neutralization in wastewater | Inconsistent acid/base influent | pH (unitless) |
| 5 | Constraint Operation | Exothermic polymerization reactor | Catalyst activity decay | Jacket coolant T (°C) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: Generalized Closed-Loop Control Block Diagram (Mermaid)

This diagram illustrates the *complete* signal flow that the "Purpose" of control is designed to satisfy.

```mermaid
graph LR
    Rref["R(s) Setpoint Input"]
    SumA["Summing Junction"]
    Err["E(s) Error Signal"]
    Cont["Gc(s) Controller"]
    MV["U(s) Manipulated Variable"]
    Proc["Gp(s) Process Plant"]
    DistIn["D(s) Disturbance"]
    DistAdd["Summing Junction Disturbance"]
    OutY["Y(s) Process Variable Output"]
    Sens["H(s) Sensor Feedback"]
    FeedB["B(s) Feedback Signal"]

    Rref --> SumA
    SumA --> Err
    Err --> Cont
    Cont --> MV
    MV --> Proc
    DistIn --> DistAdd
    DistAdd --> Proc
    Proc --> OutY
    OutY --> Sens
    Sens --> FeedB
    FeedB --> SumA
```

**Reading the diagram:** The setpoint $R(s)$ enters the left side. The summing junction subtracts the feedback $B(s)$ to produce the error $E(s)$. The controller $G_c(s)$ acts on this error, producing the manipulated variable $U(s)$ that drives the process $G_p(s)$. The disturbance $D(s)$ enters the process through a separate summing junction. The output $Y(s)$ is measured by sensor $H(s)$ and returned to the summing junction — closing the loop.

## 4.2 Diagram 2: Hierarchical Functional Architecture Flow (Mermaid)

This shows the **five purposes** of process control as a hierarchical flow — useful for 14-mark KTU essay-style answers.

```mermaid
graph TD
    PurposeRoot["PURPOSE OF INDUSTRIAL PROCESS CONTROL"]

    PurposeRoot --> P1["1. Safety of Plant and Personnel"]
    PurposeRoot --> P2["2. Product Quality and Consistency"]
    PurposeRoot --> P3["3. Economic Profit Maximization"]
    PurposeRoot --> P4["4. Disturbance Rejection"]
    PurposeRoot --> P5["5. Operating at Optimum Constraints"]

    P1 --> P1A["Interlocks Alarms ESD Systems"]
    P1 --> P1B["Example Boiler Drum Level Trip"]

    P2 --> P2A["Tight Tolerance Control"]
    P2 --> P2B["Example Distillation Column Temperature"]

    P3 --> P3A["Energy and Material Efficiency"]
    P3 --> P3B["Example Refinery Throughput Optimization"]

    P4 --> P4A["Feedback and Feedforward Loops"]
    P4 --> P4B["Example pH Neutralization"]

    P5 --> P5A["Push Operations to Equipment Limits"]
    P5 --> P5B["Example Exothermic Reactor Temperature"]
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3: Open-Loop vs. Closed-Loop — Why Control Has a "Purpose" (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph OL["OPEN LOOP No Feedback"]
        OL_R["R(s)"] --> OL_C["Gc(s)"] --> OL_P["Gp(s)"] --> OL_Y["Y(s)"]
    end

    subgraph CL["CLOSED LOOP With Feedback"]
        CL_R["R(s)"] --> CL_Sum["+ -"] --> CL_E["E(s)"] --> CL_C["Gc(s)"] --> CL_P["Gp(s)"] --> CL_Y["Y(s)"]
        CL_Y --> CL_H["H(s)"] --> CL_B["B(s)"] --> CL_Sum
    end
```

**Interpretation:** In open-loop, the output has no mechanism to correct itself against disturbances — the purpose fails. In closed-loop, the feedback path enables automatic correction, fulfilling all five purposes.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set B, Q1a]**

> *List and briefly explain any three purposes of industrial process control.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Safety** — Protect personnel, equipment, and environment from hazardous operating conditions (e.g., high pressure, temperature, toxic chemicals) through interlocks, alarms, and emergency shutdown systems. *(1 mark)*
2. **Product Quality and Consistency** — Maintain process variables within tight tolerances so that every batch of output meets the same specification, enabling mass production. *(1 mark)*
3. **Disturbance Rejection** — Automatically counteract the effect of external and internal disturbances (raw material variations, ambient changes) on the process, so the output remains at the setpoint. *(1 mark)*

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Set A, Q1b]**

> *Define the terms setpoint, process variable, and error signal as used in a feedback control system. State one industrial example for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Setpoint (SP), $r(t)$:** The desired or target value at which the process variable is to be maintained. *Example: $150^\circ\text{C}$ temperature in a chemical reactor.* *(1 mark)*
- **Process Variable (PV), $y(t)$:** The actual measured value of the controlled quantity from a sensor. *Example: actual reactor temperature measured by a thermocouple.* *(1 mark)*
- **Error Signal, $e(t)$:** The difference between the setpoint and the process variable, $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$. *Example: If $r = 150^\circ\text{C}$ and $y = 148^\circ\text{C}$, then $e = 2^\circ\text{C}$.* *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Q2a]**

> *(a)* Explain in detail the **five major purposes** of industrial process control with suitable examples from chemical/petrochemical/process industries. *(7 marks)*
> *(b)* Derive the **closed-loop error transfer function** $E(s)$ in terms of $R(s)$, $D(s)$, $G_c(s)$, $G_p(s)$, $G_d(s)$, and $H(s)$, and explain how it mathematically expresses the purpose of disturbance rejection. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks

The five major purposes of industrial process control are:

1. **Safety** *(1.5 marks)* — The foremost purpose. Industrial plants operate under extreme conditions (high $T$, $P$, toxic/flammable chemicals). A control system includes interlocks, alarms, and Emergency Shutdown (ESD) systems. *Example:* High-pressure trip in a hydrocracker reactor.

2. **Product Quality Consistency** *(1.5 marks)* — Modern products require strict specifications. *Example:* Maintaining reactor temperature within $\pm 0.5^\circ\text{C}$ of $180^\circ\text{C}$ in a polymer plant to ensure consistent molecular weight.

3. **Economic Optimization** *(1.5 marks)* — Reduce energy and raw material waste. *Example:* Optimal reflux ratio control in a distillation column saves steam and increases yield.

4. **Disturbance Rejection** *(1.5 marks)* — Compensate for variations in feed composition, ambient conditions, catalyst decay, etc. *Example:* Feedforward-feedback control of a heat exchanger inlet temperature.

5. **Operation at Optimum/Constraint Boundaries** *(1 mark)* — Allow pushing processes to equipment limits safely. *Example:* Operating a fired heater within $5^\circ\text{C}$ of the tube-skin temperature limit.

#### Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1:** Express the process output with both manipulated input and disturbance:

$$Y(s) = G_p(s)U(s) + G_d(s)D(s) \quad \text{...(i)}$$

**Step 2:** Controller output is:

$$U(s) = G_c(s)E(s) \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Step 3:** Error signal is:

$$E(s) = R(s) - H(s)Y(s) \quad \text{...(iii)}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute (ii) into (i):

$$Y(s) = G_p(s)G_c(s)E(s) + G_d(s)D(s)$$

**Step 5:** Substitute this $Y(s)$ into (iii):

$$E(s) = R(s) - H(s)\big[ G_p(s)G_c(s)E(s) + G_d(s)D(s) \big]$$

**Step 6:** Collect $E(s)$ terms:

$$E(s)\big[ 1 + G(s)H(s) \big] = R(s) - H(s)G_d(s)D(s)$$

**Step 7:** Final error transfer function:

$$E(s) = \frac{1}{1 + G(s)H(s)} R(s) - \frac{H(s)G_d(s)}{1 + G(s)H(s)} D(s) \quad \text{...(iv)}$$

**Interpretation (Disturbance Rejection) — 2 marks:**

The second term represents the contribution of the disturbance to the error. If $\vert G(s)H(s) \vert \gg 1$, the denominator becomes very large, and the disturbance term tends toward zero. This is the **mathematical expression of the purpose of disturbance rejection**. Conversely, if the loop is open (no feedback), $G(s)H(s) = 0$ and the disturbance directly passes through to the output — the purpose fails completely.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Q2b — Internal Choice]**

> *(a)* With the help of a **neat block diagram**, explain the operation of a **closed-loop feedback control system**. State how each block contributes to fulfilling the purpose of process control. *(7 marks)*
> *(b)* Compare the **open-loop** and **closed-loop** control schemes with respect to (i) accuracy, (ii) disturbance handling, (iii) stability, and (iv) cost. State one industrial application where open-loop control is preferred despite its limitations. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Block Diagram (described; refer to Section 4.1 for the full Mermaid version):**

The closed-loop system consists of:
- **Setpoint input $R(s)$** *(0.5 mark)* — Desired value.
- **Summing junction** *(0.5 mark)* — Compares $R(s)$ and feedback $B(s)$, computes $E(s)$.
- **Controller $G_c(s)$** *(1 mark)* — Implements control law (P, PI, PID) to generate corrective action.
- **Final Control Element** *(0.5 mark)* — Valve, pump, or damper that physically adjusts the process.
- **Process $G_p(s)$** *(1 mark)* — The plant whose output is to be controlled.
- **Disturbance $D(s)$** *(0.5 mark)* — Unwanted input entering the process.
- **Sensor/Transmitter $H(s)$** *(1 mark)* — Measures the process variable and feeds it back.
- **Feedback path $B(s)$** *(0.5 mark)* — Returns the measured value to the summing junction.

**Contribution to Purpose — 2 marks:**

Each block collectively ensures that the process variable $Y(s)$ tracks the setpoint $R(s)$ in the presence of disturbances. The sensor detects deviation, the controller computes correction, the final element acts, and the loop continuously verifies — thus **automatically** maintaining safety, quality, and economic efficiency.

#### Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks

| Criterion | Open-Loop | Closed-Loop |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Accuracy | Poor — no correction for errors | High — error is continuously minimized |
| (ii) Disturbance Handling | Cannot reject disturbances | Excellent — disturbances are attenuated by factor $\frac{1}{1+G(s)H(s)}$ |
| (iii) Stability | Always stable (no feedback) | Can become unstable if poorly tuned |
| (iv) Cost & Complexity | Low cost, simple | Higher cost due to sensors, controllers, wiring |

*(1.25 marks each = 5 marks)*

**Application where open-loop is preferred — 2 marks:**

A **traffic light controller** with fixed time intervals. Since the traffic flow is approximately periodic and the cost of sensors at every intersection is prohibitive, an open-loop timed controller is preferred. Other examples include simple washing machines, toasters, and irrigation timers.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Losing Mistakes in "Purpose" Questions:**
> 1. **Listing fewer than 4 purposes** — KTU examiners award only 1 mark per purpose; listing 3 will yield a maximum of 3/7. Always list **5**.
> 2. **No industrial example** — Each purpose **must** be tied to a real plant example (boiler, distillation column, pH neutralizer, etc.). Generic statements like "to control temperature" earn partial credit only.
> 3. **Confusing the purpose of control with the purpose of a *transducer* or *sensor*** — These are different topics. The purpose of control is about the *system*, not a single instrument.
> 4. **Skipping steps in the error transfer function derivation** — The 7-mark derivation question requires showing all 5–7 algebraic steps. Students who jump from $E(s) = R(s) - B(s)$ directly to the final boxed equation **lose 3 marks** minimum.
> 5. **Forgetting the disturbance term $G_d(s)D(s)$** in the derivation — Many students assume the process has only one input, losing 2 marks for an incomplete model.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- The **purpose of process control** has **five canonical pillars**: Safety, Quality, Economy, Disturbance Rejection, and Constraint Optimization.
- A feedback control loop consists of: **Setpoint → Error → Controller → Final Element → Process → Sensor → Feedback**.
- The fundamental purpose equation: $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$, with the engineering goal $e_{ss} \to 0$.
- The closed-loop error transfer function is $E(s) = \dfrac{1}{1+G(s)H(s)}R(s) - \dfrac{H(s)G_d(s)}{1+G(s)H(s)}D(s)$.
- For good tracking and disturbance rejection, $\vert G(s)H(s) \vert \gg 1$ at low frequencies.
- The **Final Value Theorem** is the tool used to compute $e_{ss}$ for a step input.
- Closed-loop control is preferred when disturbances exist or accuracy is critical; open-loop is preferred when cost dominates and the process is well-defined.
- **Block diagrams are mandatory** in any 7 or 14 mark answer on this topic — diagrams without explanation or explanation without diagrams both lose marks.
- The notation $G_c(s)$, $G_p(s)$, $H(s)$, $G_d(s)$ must be **defined before** being used in any equation — failing to do so costs 0.5–1 mark.
- The purposes are **interdependent** — improving one (e.g., safety) often requires sacrificing another (e.g., throughput); control engineering is the art of balancing these trade-offs.
- Real industrial examples strengthen answers: **furnace, boiler, distillation column, CSTR, pH neutralizer, heat exchanger, compressor, and refrigeration system** are the eight most-expected plant examples.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Scope
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT743 - INDUSTRIAL PROCESS CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Scope

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Process Control (IPC)** is the engineering discipline that deals with the design, analysis, and implementation of automatic control systems used to regulate the behavior of industrial processes such that the **process variables** (temperature, pressure, flow, level, composition, pH, humidity, etc.) are maintained at desired operating values (set-points) despite external **disturbances** and internal **parameter variations**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, the *Scope* of this subject is defined as the entire pipeline of activities that allow a raw material to be converted into a finished product in a **safe, stable, optimal, and repeatable** manner by closing the loop between **measurement**, **decision**, and **actuation**.

The discipline formally stands at the intersection of:

- **Control Theory** (mathematical modeling of dynamic systems)
- **Instrumentation Engineering** (sensors, transmitters, final control elements)
- **Chemical/Process Engineering** (the physical plant being controlled)
- **Computer & Communication Engineering** (DCS, PLC, SCADA, fieldbuses)

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Cruise Control of a Factory"

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition:** Imagine you are driving a car on a highway.
> - The **set-point** is the speed you want (say 80 km/h).
> - The **process variable** is the actual speed shown on the speedometer.
> - A **hill** is a disturbance that slows the car down.
> - Your **brain + eyes + accelerator pedal** form a controller.
> 
> You don't open the bonnet and rebuild the engine each time a hill comes — you just press the accelerator a little harder. **Industrial Process Control does the same thing for chemical plants, power stations, oil refineries, and water treatment plants** — only the "hills" are changes in feed composition, ambient temperature, catalyst aging, and dozens of other disturbances happening simultaneously.

## 1.3 Why the Subject Exists — The Control Imperative

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Universal Engineering Reasons to Control a Process:**
> 1. **Safety** — preventing runaway reactions, explosions, toxic releases.
> 2. **Quality** — ensuring product specifications (purity, viscosity, molecular weight) are met consistently.
> 3. **Economy** — minimizing raw-material wastage, energy consumption, and off-spec product.

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The subject relies on the following standard **SI** units and metrics (must be memorized for KTU exams):

- **Pressure:** Pascal (**Pa**) or bar; $1 \text{ bar} = 10^{5} \text{ Pa}$
- **Flow:** $\text{m}^{3}/\text{s}$
- **Temperature:** Kelvin (**K**) or Celsius (**°C**)
- **Level:** metre (**m**)
- **Control valve sizing coefficient:** $C_v$ (US units) or $K_v$ (metric)
- **Controller output:** standardized **4–20 mA** current loop or **3–15 psig** pneumatic signal

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Scope in Industrial Process Control

The KTU 2024 syllabus defines the **scope** of Industrial Process Control as being organized into three interconnected pillars. Each pillar contains the following sub-domains:

### Pillar A — The Physical Process (Plant)
- **Continuous processes** (refineries, power plants) — operate 24×7, steady-state dominant.
- **Batch processes** (pharma, specialty chemicals) — finite charge, recipe-driven.
- **Discrete processes** (assembly lines) — counted units, sequential logic.

### Pillar B — The Measurement & Actuation Layer (Field Instruments)
- **Sensors/Transmitters** — measure the process variable (PV).
- **Signal conditioning** — converts to standard 4–20 mA / HART / Foundation Fieldbus.
- **Final Control Elements (FCEs)** — control valves, variable speed drives (VSDs), dampers.
- **Smart instrumentation** — devices with embedded diagnostics (EDDL, FDI).

### Pillar C — The Control & Automation Layer
- **Single-loop controllers** (PID).
- **Programmable Logic Controllers (PLC)** — discrete/batch logic.
- **Distributed Control Systems (DCS)** — plant-wide continuous control.
- **Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition (SCADA)** — wide-area telemetry.
- **Manufacturing Execution Systems (MES)** and **ERP** integration.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Aid for KTU:** The scope is often described as the **"M-P-A-C" loop**: **Measure → Process → Actuate → Communicate**. Always remember the loop is *closed* — otherwise it is an open-loop system (no correction for disturbances).

## 2.2 Industries Where Industrial Process Control is Applied

The scope extends across virtually every process industry. The following table summarizes the major sectors and the typical controlled variables involved:

| Industry Sector | Typical Controlled Variables | Primary Control Objective |
|---|---|---|
| Oil & Gas Refining | Temperature, pressure, flow, composition | Fractionation, cracking, distillation |
| Petrochemicals | Temperature, pressure, level, composition | Polymerization, reactor control |
| Power Generation | Boiler drum level, steam temp, turbine speed | Safe, efficient steam generation |
| Pharmaceuticals | pH, temperature, dissolved O₂, humidity | GMP, batch consistency |
| Food & Beverage | Temperature, flow, pressure, moisture | Pasteurization, recipe control |
| Pulp & Paper | Basis weight, moisture, consistency, level | Sheet quality |
| Water & Wastewater | pH, turbidity, flow, chlorine residual | Potable water quality |
| Cement & Glass | Temperature, pressure, level, O₂ | Kiln stability, fuel efficiency |
| Metals & Mining | Temperature, composition, level | Smelting, flotation |
| HVAC & Building Automation | Temperature, humidity, CO₂ | Energy-efficient comfort |

## 2.3 Hierarchical Levels of Control (The Automation Pyramid)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently test the **Automation Pyramid** because it defines the SCOPE of where each control activity lives. Memorize the 5 levels from bottom (Level 0) to top (Level 4).

| Level | Name | Typical Time Horizon | KTU Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Level 0** | Field / Process | < 1 s | Sensors, control valves, motors |
| **Level 1** | Direct / Basic Control | 1 s – 1 min | PID loops, PLC interlocks |
| **Level 2** | Supervisory Control | 1 min – 1 hr | SCADA, HMI, alarm management |
| **Level 3** | Manufacturing Operations | Hours – Days | MES, batch reporting, OEE |
| **Level 4** | Enterprise / Business | Days – Years | ERP, supply chain, planning |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

Because the topic "Scope" is mostly descriptive, the formula sheet below focuses on the **fundamental performance indices** that the scope aims to optimize throughout any process control activity. These formulas recur in every KTU module.

| Formula | Symbol Meaning | Standard Unit | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$ | Error signal = set-point − measured PV | Same as PV | Quantifies deviation from desired value |
| $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e(t)\,dt + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$ | PID controller output | % (0–100) | The universal three-term controller |
| $G(s) = \dfrac{Y(s)}{U(s)}$ | Process transfer function | dimensionless | Input–output dynamic model |
| $\text{IAE} = \int_{0}^{\infty} \vert e(t) \vert\,dt$ | Integral of Absolute Error | PV·s | Performance index for tuning |
| $\text{ISE} = \int_{0}^{\infty} e(t)^{2}\,dt$ | Integral of Squared Error | (PV)²·s | Penalizes large errors heavily |
| $\text{ITAE} = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot \vert e(t) \vert\,dt$ | Integral of Time × Abs Error | PV·s² | Penalizes long settling time |
| $K_v = Q \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho}{\Delta P}}$ | Valve sizing coefficient | $\text{m}^{3}/\text{h}$ | Used to size control valves (a scope item) |
| $I = I_{min} + \dfrac{\text{PV} - \text{PV}_{min}}{\text{PV}_{max} - \text{PV}_{min}} \times 16$ | 4–20 mA current conversion | mA | Standard signal normalization |
| $E = \int_{t_0}^{t_f} \left( q_1 e^2 + q_2 u^2 \right) dt$ | Optimal control cost | varies | Minimizing error + control effort |

> [!NOTE]
> **NOTE on the vertical bar `|x|` in tables:** KTU exam papers and this cheat sheet use the absolute-value notation as $\vert \cdot \vert$ to keep markdown tables valid. Always write $\vert e(t) \vert$ — never `|e(t)|` inside a table cell.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of Understanding the Scope

- **Plant engineers** use the scope framework to design robust control loops.
- **Instrumentation designers** select sensors within the scope of their accuracy class.
- **Control engineers** tune PID controllers within the scope of acceptable IAE/ISE.
- **Plant managers** plan investments in DCS, PLC, or SCADA based on the automation pyramid level they need to upgrade.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Control Objective Function

The fundamental **scope** of any process control activity can be formalized as an optimization problem. Given a process with measured output $y(t)$ and a desired set-point $r(t)$, the universal control objective is to find a control signal $u(t)$ that minimizes the deviation $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$ while keeping the control effort bounded.

Let us formalize this with a standard cost functional commonly used in KTU Module 1 derivations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step-by-Step Derivation of the Optimal Control Cost Function**

**Step 1 — Define the instantaneous error.**
The deviation of the process variable from the set-point is the difference between the desired value and the measured value:

$$
e(t) = r(t) - y(t)
$$

where $r(t)$ is the reference signal, and $y(t)$ is the actual process output (measured by the transmitter).

**Step 2 — Penalize the squared error.**
A pure error term can become negative, so it is squared to enforce a positive penalty for any deviation:

$$
J_{1} = \int_{t_0}^{t_f} e(t)^{2}\,dt
$$

**Step 3 — Add a penalty on the control effort.**
Merely minimizing $e(t)^{2}$ can lead to excessively large control signals (saturating actuators). A second term penalizes control energy:

$$
J_{2} = \int_{t_0}^{t_f} u(t)^{2}\,dt
$$

**Step 4 — Combine into a single cost functional.**
Using weighting coefficients $q_{1}$ and $q_{2}$ (tuning knobs chosen by the engineer), the combined cost is:

$$
J = \int_{t_0}^{t_f} \left[ q_{1}\,e(t)^{2} + q_{2}\,u(t)^{2} \right] dt
$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the weights.**
- If $q_{1} \gg q_{2}$: aggressive set-point tracking, ignoring actuator wear.
- If $q_{2} \gg q_{1}$: gentle control, but larger steady-state error.
- A balanced choice gives a **trade-off curve** central to LQR (Linear Quadratic Regulator) design.

**Result:** The process control problem is *mathematically* a calculus-of-variations minimization of $J$ subject to the plant dynamics. The **scope** of Industrial Process Control, therefore, encompasses identifying the correct $J$, the plant model, and the admissible set of $u(t)$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — The Standard 4–20 mA Signal Conversion

All modern instrumentation in the scope of process control uses a **current loop** because current is immune to line resistance (unlike voltage). The conversion between a measured engineering variable and the current signal is linear.

**Step 1 — Define the lower and upper range values of the instrument.**
Let the instrument be calibrated to measure a variable $x$ over the range $[x_{min},\ x_{max}]$, with corresponding currents $I_{min} = 4$ mA and $I_{max} = 20$ mA.

**Step 2 — Write a linear interpolation.**
The current at any intermediate measurement $x$ is:

$$
I = I_{min} + \dfrac{x - x_{min}}{x_{max} - x_{min}} \times (I_{max} - I_{min})
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values of the standard signal.**
Insert $I_{min} = 4$ mA and $I_{max} = 20$ mA so that $I_{max} - I_{min} = 16$ mA:

$$
I = 4 + \dfrac{x - x_{min}}{x_{max} - x_{min}} \times 16
$$

**Step 4 — Express the measured variable in terms of the current.**
Rearranging for $x$ in terms of the measured current $I$:

$$
x = x_{min} + \dfrac{I - 4}{16} \times (x_{max} - x_{min})
$$

**Numerical example for KTU practice:**
A level transmitter is ranged $0$–$10$ m. If the controller receives a current of $12$ mA, what is the actual level?
- $x_{min} = 0$ m, $x_{max} = 10$ m, $I = 12$ mA
- $x = 0 + \dfrac{12 - 4}{16} \times (10 - 0) = \dfrac{8}{16} \times 10 = 5$ m
- **Answer:** The level is $5$ m.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Linearization of a Nonlinear Process (Scope of Validity)

A significant part of the *scope* of process control is **linearization** — converting a nonlinear relationship into a linear one valid around an operating point. Consider a nonlinear static relationship of the form:

$$
y = f(x) = a \cdot x^{2}
$$

**Step 1 — Identify the steady-state operating point $(x_0, y_0)$.**
At the chosen operating point, $y_0 = a \cdot x_0^{2}$.

**Step 2 — Apply a small perturbation $x = x_0 + \Delta x$.**

$$
y = a (x_0 + \Delta x)^{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Expand using the binomial theorem.**

$$
y = a \left( x_0^{2} + 2 x_0 \Delta x + (\Delta x)^{2} \right)
$$

**Step 4 — Drop the second-order term** $(\Delta x)^{2}$, valid only when $\Delta x$ is small relative to $x_0$:

$$
y \approx a x_0^{2} + 2 a x_0 \Delta x
$$

**Step 5 — Subtract the steady-state value** $y_0 = a x_0^{2}$ to get the deviation model:

$$
\Delta y \approx 2 a x_0 \cdot \Delta x
$$

**Interpretation:** The slope $\dfrac{dy}{dx}\big\vert_{x_0} = 2 a x_0$ is the **process gain** $K_p$ at the operating point. This shows that the *scope* of linear control theory applies only **locally** around the operating point.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Performance Criteria Used Within the Scope of IPC

| Performance Criterion | Formula | When Used in IPC Scope |
|---|---|---|
| Steady-state error | $e_{ss} = \lim_{t \to \infty} e(t)$ | DC accuracy of regulatory loops |
| Rise time | $t_r$ (10% → 90% of set-point) | Speed of response |
| Settling time | $t_s$ (within 2% or 5% band) | Loop agility |
| Overshoot | $M_p = \dfrac{y_{max} - r}{r} \times 100\%$ | Damping assessment |
| Decay ratio | Quarter-amplitude criterion | Tuning benchmarks |
| Integral criteria | IAE, ISE, ITAE | Optimal tuning |
| Robustness margin | Gain/Phase margin | Stability reserves |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Closed-Loop Control System — Universal Block Diagram

> [!NOTE]
> This Mermaid block diagram represents the **universal closed-loop block diagram** that defines the fundamental SCOPE of Industrial Process Control. Every KTU module that follows will build on this exact structure.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    setpt[Set Point r of t] --> sumA
    dist[Disturbance d of t] --> plant
    sumA{{Summing Junction e equals r minus y}} --> ctrl[Controller Gc of s]
    ctrl --> act[Final Control Element Gv of s]
    act --> plant[Process Gp of s]
    plant --> out[Process Variable y of t]
    out --> meas[Sensor and Transmitter Hm of s]
    meas --> sumA
    style setpt fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333
    style dist fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333
    style sumA fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333
    style ctrl fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333
    style act fill:#f7dc6f,stroke:#333
    style plant fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#333
    style out fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#333
    style meas fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#333
```

**Block-by-block reading guide for students:**

1. **Set Point r(t)** — the desired value the operator or recipe demands.
2. **Disturbance d(t)** — any unwanted input (load change, ambient temp swing).
3. **Summing Junction** — computes error $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$.
4. **Controller Gc(s)** — the brain (PID, fuzzy, MPC, etc.).
5. **Final Control Element Gv(s)** — control valve, VSD, damper (the muscle).
6. **Process Gp(s)** — the physical plant (reactor, furnace, column).
7. **Process Variable y(t)** — what we actually achieve.
8. **Sensor/Transmitter Hm(s)** — the eyes (with their own dynamics).

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> For "Scope" questions, students *commonly lose marks* in three places:
> 1. **Confusing the SCOPE of the subject with the DEFINITION of a control system** — the question asks for scope, not for "what is a control system." Always list industries, hierarchical levels, and the M-P-A-C loop.
> 2. **Forgetting the 4–20 mA derivation** — even though it is a Module-1 topic, examiners expect the **formula and a worked numerical**.
> 3. **Mixing up the Automation Pyramid levels** — Level 0 is the field, Level 4 is the enterprise. Reversing them is an instant 2-mark deduction.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
Define the scope of Industrial Process Control. List any four major industrial sectors where it is applied.

**Model Answer:**

The scope of Industrial Process Control encompasses the design, analysis, and implementation of automatic control systems that maintain process variables at desired values in the presence of disturbances. It covers:
- The **physical process** (continuous, batch, discrete).
- The **measurement and actuation layer** (sensors, transmitters, final control elements).
- The **control and automation layer** (PID, PLC, DCS, SCADA, MES, ERP).
- The **hierarchical automation pyramid** (Levels 0–4).
- The **optimization of safety, quality, and economy** of the plant.

**Four major industrial sectors:**
1. Oil & Gas Refining
2. Petrochemicals
3. Power Generation
4. Pharmaceuticals

(Other acceptable answers: Food & Beverage, Water & Wastewater, Pulp & Paper, Cement, HVAC.)

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
Explain the significance of the **4–20 mA current loop** as a standard signal in Industrial Process Control.

**Model Answer:**

The 4–20 mA current loop is the universal standard signal in process control instrumentation. Its significance lies in:
- **Noise immunity:** Current is unaffected by line resistance, voltage drops, and electromagnetic interference over long cable runs.
- **Live-zero detection:** A current of 4 mA represents the lower range value, while 0 mA indicates a wire break or instrument failure — the controller immediately detects the fault.
- **Two-wire powering:** The same pair of wires carries both the signal and the instrument's power supply.
- **Standardization:** 4–20 mA is universally accepted, so any controller can interface with any transmitter from any vendor.
- **Conversion formula:** $I = 4 + \dfrac{x - x_{min}}{x_{max} - x_{min}} \times 16$

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks** and must be split into two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. The student attempts **either** OR-choice (a) or (b). Model solutions must show all intermediate steps.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice A

**Q. (a) [7 marks]** Discuss in detail the **hierarchical levels of automation** in an Industrial Process Control system, with a neat diagram. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

The scope of Industrial Process Control is best understood through the **Automation Pyramid**, which shows five levels of activity differing in time horizon and intelligence.

**Level 0 — Field / Process Layer (sub-second)**
This is the physical layer of sensors, transmitters, and final control elements. It interfaces directly with the process (temperature, pressure, flow, level, composition). Examples: RTD, orifice plate, control valve, variable-speed drive.

**Level 1 — Direct / Basic Control Layer (1 s – 1 min)**
This layer hosts the continuous regulatory loops. PID controllers, single-loop controllers, and PLC-based interlocks reside here. Their job is to keep each process variable at its set-point by manipulating the corresponding final control element.

**Level 2 — Supervisory Control Layer (1 min – 1 hr)**
This is the operator-interface layer. SCADA systems, HMI workstations, and alarm-management software live here. Operators can change set-points, acknowledge alarms, and observe trends from this layer.

**Level 3 — Manufacturing Operations Layer (hours – days)**
This is where batch records, OEE (Overall Equipment Effectiveness), and production reports are generated. MES (Manufacturing Execution Systems) software resides here. It bridges the plant floor with the business systems.

**Level 4 — Enterprise / Business Layer (days – years)**
This is the highest level, with ERP (Enterprise Resource Planning) software handling supply chain, finance, and corporate planning. It sets the long-term production schedule that propagates downward to Level 3.

**Neat diagram** — the student should draw the Mermaid-style pyramid from **Section 4.2** (or a hand-drawn version in the answer script).

**Mark distribution suggestion:**
- 1 mark: Listing all 5 levels
- 3 marks: Explaining each level's role and time horizon
- 2 marks: Diagram with proper labels
- 1 mark: Conclusion on the flow of information

---

**Q. (b) [7 marks]** A temperature transmitter is ranged **50 °C to 250 °C** and outputs a standard **4–20 mA** signal. The controller receives a current of **12.8 mA**. Calculate the actual process temperature. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Identify the range values.**
- $T_{min} = 50$ °C
- $T_{max} = 250$ °C
- $I = 12.8$ mA

**Step 2 — Write the standard 4–20 mA conversion formula.**

$$
I = 4 + \dfrac{T - T_{min}}{T_{max} - T_{min}} \times 16
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the known values.**

$$
12.8 = 4 + \dfrac{T - 50}{250 - 50} \times 16
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the right-hand side.**

$$
12.8 = 4 + \dfrac{T - 50}{200} \times 16
$$

**Step 5 — Isolate the term containing T.**

$$
12.8 - 4 = \dfrac{T - 50}{200} \times 16
$$

$$
8.8 = \dfrac{T - 50}{200} \times 16
$$

**Step 6 — Divide both sides by 16.**

$$
\dfrac{8.8}{16} = \dfrac{T - 50}{200}
$$

$$
0.55 = \dfrac{T - 50}{200}
$$

**Step 7 — Multiply both sides by 200.**

$$
0.55 \times 200 = T - 50
$$

$$
110 = T - 50
$$

**Step 8 — Solve for T.**

$$
T = 110 + 50 = 160 \text{ °C}
$$

**Result:** The actual process temperature is **$160$ °C**.

**Mark distribution suggestion:**
- 1 mark: Listing the formula
- 2 marks: Correct substitution
- 2 marks: Algebraic simplification
- 1 mark: Final answer with units
- 1 mark: Cross-check (e.g., 50% range ⇒ 12 mA; 12.8 mA is 55% ⇒ 50 + 0.55 × 200 = 160)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice B (Alternative)

**Q. (a) [7 marks]** With the help of a **block diagram**, explain the fundamental **closed-loop control system** used in Industrial Process Control. Identify the function of each block. **[KTU University Exam — June 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

A closed-loop control system continuously measures the process variable, compares it with the set-point, and actuates the process to minimize the deviation. The standard block diagram is:

```
Set-Point r(t)  →  [+]  →  e(t)  →  Controller Gc(s)  →  Control Signal u(t)  →
                                                                    ↓
[Final Control Element Gv(s)]  →  [Process Gp(s)]  →  Process Variable y(t)
                                                                    ↓
                                                          [Sensor Hm(s)]
                                                                    ↓
                                                          Feedback to [+]
                                                                  (–)
```

**Functions of each block:**

| Block | Function |
|---|---|
| Set-Point r(t) | Desired value of the process variable |
| Summing Junction (+) | Computes error $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$ |
| Controller Gc(s) | Generates control law (PID); the "brain" |
| Final Control Element Gv(s) | Converts electrical/pneumatic signal into a physical action (valve, VSD) |
| Process Gp(s) | The plant whose behavior must be regulated |
| Sensor/Transmitter Hm(s) | Measures the process variable; converts to standard signal |
| Disturbance d(t) | Unwanted external input entering the process |

**Why closed-loop?** Because it offers **automatic disturbance rejection**, **set-point tracking**, and **insensitivity to model uncertainty** — all of which define the *scope* of industrial process control.

**Mark distribution suggestion:**
- 2 marks: Block diagram
- 3 marks: Function of each block
- 2 marks: Explanation of closed-loop benefit

---

**Q. (b) [7 marks]** Discuss the **role of Distributed Control Systems (DCS), PLC, and SCADA** within the scope of Industrial Process Control. Compare them in a tabular form. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**Model Answer:**

Within the scope of Industrial Process Control, three major automation platforms are used, each occupying a specific niche:

**DCS (Distributed Control System):**
- Designed for **large, continuous process plants** (refineries, petrochemicals, power plants).
- Thousands of regulatory loops are distributed across multiple controllers.
- Highly redundant, plant-wide data historian, advanced control capability (MPC).
- Located at **Levels 1 and 2** of the automation pyramid.

**PLC (Programmable Logic Controller):**
- Designed for **discrete and batch** automation (assembly lines, packaging, skid-mounted units).
- Fast scan times, deterministic logic, robust I/O modules.
- Best suited for **interlocks, sequencing, and safety systems (SIS)**.
- Located at **Level 1** and often at **Level 0** for hard-wired I/O.

**SCADA (Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition):**
- Designed for **geographically distributed assets** (pipelines, water distribution, electrical grids).
- Communicates with remote RTUs/PLCs over radio, cellular, or satellite links.
- Focuses on **monitoring, alarming, and remote control** rather than dense regulatory control.
- Located at **Level 2** of the automation pyramid.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | DCS | PLC | SCADA |
|---|---|---|---|
| Primary Use | Continuous process | Discrete / batch | Remote monitoring |
| Number of loops | Thousands | Tens to hundreds | Not loop-dense |
| Redundancy | Built-in (controllers, I/O, networks, power) | Selective | At communication layer |
| Data Historian | Yes, integrated | Optional (external) | Yes, integrated |
| Programming | Function blocks, ladder, structured text | Ladder, FBD, ST | Limited / vendor-specific |
| Best fit | Refinery, power plant | Assembly line, skid | Pipeline, water network |
| Pyramid level | Level 1 & 2 | Level 0 & 1 | Level 2 |

**Mark distribution suggestion:**
- 2 marks: DCS description
- 2 marks: PLC description
- 2 marks: SCADA description
- 1 mark: Comparison table

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Scope" — Module 1**

- ✅ **Industrial Process Control** = the discipline of automatically regulating process variables to safe, optimal set-points in the presence of disturbances.
- ✅ The scope covers three pillars: **Process, Measurement & Actuation, Control & Automation**.
- ✅ The **M-P-A-C** loop: **Measure → Process → Actuate → Communicate** (always *closed*).
- ✅ The **Automation Pyramid** has 5 levels — **0 to 4** — from field to enterprise.
- ✅ **Level 0** = field instruments; **Level 1** = PID/PLC; **Level 2** = SCADA/HMI; **Level 3** = MES; **Level 4** = ERP.
- ✅ **4–20 mA** is the universal signal — current loop is noise-immune; 0 mA = fault.
- ✅ Standard 4–20 mA formula: $I = 4 + \dfrac{x - x_{min}}{x_{max} - x_{min}} \times 16$ (and its inverse).
- ✅ Industries served: oil & gas, petrochemicals, power, pharma, food, water, paper, cement, metals, HVAC.
- ✅ **PID** controller: $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e\,dt + K_d \dfrac{de}{dt}$.
- ✅ Performance indices: **IAE, ISE, ITAE** — used for optimal tuning throughout the scope.
- ✅ Optimal-control cost: $J = \int \left( q_1 e^2 + q_2 u^2 \right) dt$ — defines the formal scope of regulation.
- ✅ **Linearization** around an operating point gives a *local* linear model — linear control theory applies only locally.
- ✅ **DCS** for continuous plants; **PLC** for discrete/batch; **SCADA** for geographically distributed systems.
- ✅ Always quote **SI units** and the **formula** when a numerical is asked.
- ✅ Examiner trap: the question asks for **scope** — list industries + hierarchy + pillars, not just a textbook definition of a control system.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Identification of the Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT743 - INDUSTRIAL PROCESS CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Identification of the Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of System Identification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **System Identification** is the discipline of building mathematical models of dynamic systems entirely from observed input-output data, without requiring explicit prior knowledge of the underlying physical laws (white-box knowledge). It is fundamentally a **black-box** or **grey-box** empirical modelling approach widely used in process control to characterize plant dynamics.

In the formal sense, given a record of inputs $\{u(t)\}$ and the corresponding measured outputs $\{y(t)\}$ of a process, system identification seeks to determine a model $\mathcal{M}$ belonging to a specified model class such that the predicted output $\hat{y}(t \vert \theta)$ best matches the observed output $y(t)$ according to a chosen error criterion.

$$y(t) = \mathcal{G}\big[u(t), \theta\big] + e(t)$$

where:
- $\mathcal{G}$ represents the model structure (transfer function, state space, etc.).
- $\theta$ is the parameter vector of the model.
- $e(t)$ is the **residual error** (noise/disturbance term).

## 1.2 Why Do Engineers Need System Identification?

In real industrial plants, several practical realities force engineers to use empirical identification rather than first-principles modelling:
- **Complex chemistry/thermodynamics:** CSTR, distillation columns, and polymerization reactors involve non-linear reactions that are hard to write analytically.
- **Unknown vendor processes:** When proprietary controllers (DCS, PLC) ship with a black-box plant, identification is the only recourse.
- **Process changes:** Catalyst deactivation, heat-exchanger fouling, and sensor drift change dynamics over time, requiring periodic re-identification.
- **Controller tuning:** Classical controllers (P, PI, PID) and model-predictive controllers (MPC) demand accurate process models for gain scheduling, auto-tuning, and predictive horizons.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Doctor's Check-up"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy: Diagnosing a Patient**
> Think of an industrial plant as a patient visiting a doctor. The doctor cannot "open" the patient to see the heart or lungs directly. Instead, the doctor applies controlled inputs (asking the patient to walk, breathe deeply, or take medicine) and observes the outputs (heart rate, blood pressure, oxygen saturation). From these input-output records, the doctor *infers* a model of the patient's health. Similarly, an engineer injects a known test signal (step, pulse, PRBS) into the plant and records how the process responds. The mathematical model is then fitted to these observations. Just as the doctor can now prescribe the right medicine (controller), the engineer can design the right controller.

## 1.4 Types of Systems Encountered in Identification

| **Classification Criterion** | **System Type** | **Description** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Linearity | Linear / Non-Linear | Linear systems obey superposition; non-linear do not. |
| Time-dependence | Time-Invariant / Time-Varying | Parameters constant or changing with time. |
| Signal type | Continuous / Discrete | Defined in continuous or sampled-data domain. |
| Memory | Static (Memoryless) / Dynamic | Output depends only on present input or past inputs. |
| Causality | Causal / Non-Causal | Causal systems depend only on past/present inputs. |
| Stability | Stable / Marginally Stable / Unstable | Bounded Input produces Bounded/Unbounded Output (BIBO). |

## 1.5 Steps in the Identification Procedure

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The three pillars of any identification exercise are:
> 1. **Data Acquisition** — Design a perturbation, collect $(u,y)$ records.
> 2. **Model Structure Selection & Parameter Estimation** — Pick the model class and compute parameters minimizing an error norm.
> 3. **Model Validation** — Cross-check the identified model against a fresh dataset to confirm predictive credibility.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Description of Common Process Models

Industrial processes are most often approximated by **first-order-plus-dead-time (FOPDT)** or **second-order-plus-dead-time (SOPDT)** models because these are sufficient for the majority of controller-tuning applications (Ziegler-Nichols, Cohen-Coon, etc.).

### 2.1.1 First-Order Plus Dead Time (FOPDT) Model

$$G(s) = \frac{K_p \, e^{-L s}}{\tau s + 1}$$

where $K_p$ is the **steady-state process gain**, $\tau$ is the **time constant** (in seconds), and $L$ is the **transportation / dead time** (in seconds).

### 2.1.2 Second-Order Plus Dead Time (SOPDT) Model

$$G(s) = \frac{K_p \, e^{-L s}}{(\tau_1 s + 1)(\tau_2 s + 1)}$$

where $\tau_1$ and $\tau_2$ are the two time constants. When $\tau_1 = \tau_2 = \tau$ and damping is critical, the model collapses to:

$$G(s) = \frac{K_p \, e^{-L s}}{(\tau s + 1)^2}$$

## 2.2 The Process Reaction Curve Method

> [!NOTE]
> The **Process Reaction Curve (PRC)** is the open-loop step response of a process at steady state. It is the simplest, oldest, and most asked-about method in KTU exams. The plant is brought to a steady operating point, a small step change $\Delta M$ is applied to the manipulated variable, and the output is recorded.

### Step-by-Step Logic for the Open-Loop Step Test

1. Bring the plant to **steady state** with input $u_0$ and output $y_0$.
2. Apply a **step input** of magnitude $\Delta u$ at $t = 0$.
3. Record the output $y(t)$ until it reaches a new steady state $y_{\infty}$.
4. Compute the **process gain** from the static deflection:
5. Draw the **tangent line** at the inflection point of the response.
6. The tangent intersects the time axis at the **dead time** $L$.
7. The tangent intersects the final steady-state line at $T$. The **time constant** is recovered as:
8. For first-order systems, the tangent meets the new steady-state at $t = L + \tau$.

$$K_p = \frac{y_{\infty} - y_0}{\Delta u} = \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta u}$$

$$\tau = T - L$$

## 2.3 Method of Areas (Strejc Method)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When the process exhibits a higher-order lag, the simple tangent method fails. The **Strejc Method** uses two characteristic areas under the step response to determine the dead time $L$ and equivalent time constant $T$ for an $n$-th order system.

Define the two areas:
- $A_0$ = area between the step response $y^*(t)$ and the initial steady state $y_0$.
- $A_1$ = area between the tangent at the inflection point and the response $y^*(t)$.

The relationship for an $n$-th order system with dead time:

$$T_a = \frac{A_0}{K_p \cdot \Delta u} \quad , \quad T_u = \frac{A_1}{K_p \cdot \Delta u}$$

The ratio $T_u / T_a$ selects the order $n$ from a Strejc table. For example:
- $T_u/T_a = 0.000 \Rightarrow n = 1$
- $T_u/T_a = 0.104 \Rightarrow n = 2$
- $T_u/T_a = 0.218 \Rightarrow n = 3$

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> Memorize this table. Every numerical in Part A or Part B of KTU ESE will be derivable from these expressions.

| **Parameter** | **Symbol** | **Definition / Formula** | **Standard Unit** |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :---: |
| Process Gain | $K_p$ | $\Delta y_{\text{ss}} \div \Delta u$ | (output unit) / (input unit) |
| Dead Time | $L$ | Time lag before output responds | seconds (s) |
| Time Constant | $\tau$ | Time to reach $63.2\%$ of $\Delta y_{\text{ss}}$ | seconds (s) |
| Equivalent Time Constant | $T$ | Tangent intersection - dead time | seconds (s) |
| Slope of Tangent | $R$ | $\Delta y_{\text{ss}} \div T$ | (output unit) / s |
| Total Area | $A_0$ | $\int_{0}^{\infty} [y_\infty - y(t)] \, dt$ | (output unit) · s |
| Strejc Ratio | $T_u/T_a$ | Used to find system order $n$ | dimensionless |
| Settling Time (2%) | $t_s$ | $4 \tau$ (first order) | seconds (s) |
| Rise Time (10–90%) | $t_r$ | $2.2 \tau$ (first order) | seconds (s) |
| Bode Frequency | $\omega_c$ | $1 / \tau$ for first order corner | rad/s |
| Log-Modulus | $L_m$ | $20 \log_{10} \vert G(j\omega) \vert$ | decibels (dB) |
| Phase Angle | $\phi$ | $\angle G(j\omega)$ | degrees (°) |

## 2.5 Frequency Response Identification

> [!NOTE]
> The frequency response method injects sinusoidal inputs of varying frequency $\omega$ and measures the steady-state output amplitude ratio and phase. The empirical Bode plot is then fitted to a transfer function form.

For a transfer function $G(s)$, substituting $s = j\omega$:

$$G(j\omega) = \vert G(j\omega) \vert \, e^{j\angle G(j\omega)}$$

From the experimental data:
$$\text{Magnitude Ratio (MR)} = \frac{Y_{\text{amp}}}{U_{\text{amp}}} = \vert G(j\omega) \vert$$

$$\text{Phase Shift} = \angle G(j\omega) = \phi(\omega)$$

The **corner frequency** at which the magnitude falls by 3 dB from the low-frequency plateau identifies $1/\tau$ for first-order systems.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

System identification underpins every modern industrial automation stack:
- **PID Auto-Tuning:** Commercial controllers (Honeywell, Yokogawa, Emerson DeltaV) internally run step or relay-feedback tests to compute $K_p$, $L$, $\tau$ and then apply Ziegler-Nichols rules.
- **Model Predictive Control (MPC):** Requires an identified state-space model to predict future trajectories and optimize control moves over a horizon.
- **Soft Sensors:** Inferential measurements use identified models when a hardware sensor is too expensive or unreliable.
- **Fault Detection & Diagnosis:** Deviations between an identified model and a real-time updated model flag sensor drift or actuator wear.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Process Gain from Step Response

**Statement:** Given a process at steady state with input $u_0$ and output $y_0$, a step $\Delta u$ is applied. After sufficient time, the output settles at $y_\infty$. Show that the steady-state gain is $K_p = (y_\infty - y_0) / \Delta u$.

**Derivation:**

Consider a linear time-invariant (LTI) process with transfer function $G(s)$. In the steady state, applying the **Final Value Theorem**:

$$y_\infty = \lim_{t \to \infty} y(t) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot Y(s)$$

The Laplace transform of the step input is:

$$U(s) = \frac{\Delta u}{s}$$

The output in the Laplace domain is:

$$Y(s) = G(s) \cdot U(s) = G(s) \cdot \frac{\Delta u}{s}$$

Applying the Final Value Theorem:

$$y_\infty - y_0 = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \left[ G(s) \cdot \frac{\Delta u}{s} \right] = G(0) \cdot \Delta u$$

By definition, $G(0) = K_p$. Rearranging:

$$\boxed{K_p = \frac{y_\infty - y_0}{\Delta u} = \frac{\Delta y_{\text{ss}}}{\Delta u}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Time Constant from Tangent Method

**Statement:** For a first-order system $G(s) = K_p / (\tau s + 1)$ with step input $\Delta u$, derive the time at which the tangent at $t = 0$ meets the final value $y_\infty$.

**Derivation:**

The unit-step response of a first-order system is:

$$y(t) = K_p \, \Delta u \left( 1 - e^{-t/\tau} \right)$$

The slope of the response at $t = 0$ is:

$$\left. \frac{dy}{dt} \right|_{t=0} = \frac{K_p \, \Delta u}{\tau}$$

The equation of the tangent line starting from $y_0$ is:

$$y_{\text{tangent}}(t) = y_0 + \frac{K_p \, \Delta u}{\tau} \cdot t$$

Setting $y_{\text{tangent}} = y_\infty = y_0 + K_p \, \Delta u$ at $t = T$:

$$y_0 + \frac{K_p \, \Delta u}{\tau} \cdot T = y_0 + K_p \, \Delta u$$

Solving for $T$:

$$T = \tau$$

Subtracting the dead time $L$ (where the tangent crosses the time axis, i.e., $y_{\text{tangent}} = y_0$ at $t = L$):

$$y_0 + \frac{K_p \, \Delta u}{\tau} \cdot L = y_0 \implies L = 0 \text{ (no dead time in pure FOPDT tangent)}$$

For a process with dead time, the tangent intercepts the time axis at $t = L$ and the steady-state line at $t = T = L + \tau$, giving:

$$\boxed{\tau = T - L}$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Strejc Method — Total Area $A_0$

**Statement:** For a stable first-order-plus-dead-time system with step response $y(t)$, show that the area between the response curve and the final steady state equals $K_p \cdot \Delta u \cdot \tau$.

**Derivation:**

The error between the final value and the response is:

$$e(t) = y_\infty - y(t) = K_p \, \Delta u \cdot e^{-(t-L)/\tau} \quad (t \geq L)$$

The total area is:

$$A_0 = \int_{0}^{\infty} \left[ y_\infty - y(t) \right] dt = \int_{0}^{L} y_\infty \, dt + \int_{L}^{\infty} K_p \, \Delta u \cdot e^{-(t-L)/\tau} \, dt$$

The first integral is zero since $y(t) = y_\infty$ for $0 \leq t \leq L$ (system has not yet started responding due to dead time). The second integral:

$$A_0 = K_p \, \Delta u \cdot \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-t'/\tau} \, dt' \quad \text{(substituting } t' = t - L\text{)}$$

$$A_0 = K_p \, \Delta u \cdot \left[ -\tau \, e^{-t'/\tau} \right]_{0}^{\infty} = K_p \, \Delta u \cdot \tau$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{A_0 = K_p \cdot \Delta u \cdot \tau}$$

Dividing by $K_p \cdot \Delta u$ gives the equivalent time constant $T_a$:

$$T_a = \frac{A_0}{K_p \cdot \Delta u} = \tau$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Identification Methods

> [!NOTE]
> The following matrix maps each identification method to its applicability, advantages, and limitations. KTU examiners frequently ask students to *justify* the choice of method for a given plant type.

| **Method** | **Test Signal** | **Best For** | **Key Advantage** | **Key Limitation** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Step Test (PRC) | Step | Slow, stable, monotonic processes | Simple, easy to implement offline | Disturbs production; needs steady-state wait |
| Pulse Test | Rectangular pulse | Processes that cannot tolerate long step | Smaller disturbance footprint | Less precise parameter estimation |
| Frequency Response | Sine sweep | Linear, noise-free systems | Rich frequency information | Time-consuming; multiple experiments |
| PRBS (Pseudo-Random Binary Sequence) | Two-level random | Industrial plants in closed loop | Excitation at multiple frequencies simultaneously | Requires correlation analysis |
| Relay Feedback | Relay-induced oscillation | Closed-loop tuning without opening loop | Auto-tuner friendly, Ziegler-Nichols equivalent | Identifies ultimate gain/period only |
| Least Squares (Recursive) | Arbitrary | Adaptive control, online estimation | Updates parameters in real time | Sensitive to noise; may diverge |
| Strejc Method | Step | Higher-order self-regulating processes | No need to assume order a priori | Sensitive to measurement noise |
| Smith Method | Step | Second-order systems with dead time | Explicit dead-time estimation | Requires inflection point clearly visible |

## 3.5 Comprehensive Numerical Example (FOPDT Identification)

> [!NOTE]
> The following is the type of numerical problem frequently seen in KTU Part B. It integrates every concept in this module.

**Problem:** A thermal mixing tank is at steady state with hot-water inlet flow rate $u_0 = 20$ L/min and outlet temperature $y_0 = 60^\circ$C. A step increase of $\Delta u = 5$ L/min is applied. The outlet temperature is found to:
- Begin changing at $t = 30$ s.
- Reach $63.2\%$ of its final change at $t = 100$ s.
- Settle to a final value of $y_\infty = 72.5^\circ$C.

Identify the FOPDT model parameters $K_p$, $\tau$, and $L$.

**Solution:**

The total steady-state change in output is:

$$\Delta y_{\text{ss}} = y_\infty - y_0 = 72.5 - 60 = 12.5^\circ\text{C}$$

**Step 1 — Process Gain:**

$$K_p = \frac{\Delta y_{\text{ss}}}{\Delta u} = \frac{12.5}{5} = 2.5 \;\; \frac{^\circ\text{C}}{\text{L/min}}$$

**Step 2 — Dead Time:**

The output begins to change at $t = 30$ s, so:

$$L = 30 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3 — Time Constant:**

For a first-order system, the time to reach $63.2\%$ of the final value is exactly one time constant. The response begins at $t = L = 30$ s, so the $63.2\%$ point occurs at $t = L + \tau = 100$ s:

$$\tau = 100 - 30 = 70 \text{ s}$$

**Step 4 — FOPDT Model:**

$$\boxed{G(s) = \frac{2.5 \, e^{-30 s}}{70 s + 1}}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Closed-Loop System Identification Framework

> [!NOTE]
> This diagram illustrates how a perturbation signal is injected and how the identification algorithm processes the recorded input-output data to produce the identified model.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Reference Set Point r] --> B[Controller C s]
    B --> C[Actuator u]
    C --> D[Industrial Plant P s]
    D --> E[Sensor and Transducer H s]
    E --> F[Measured Output y]
    D --> G[Disturbance d]
    F --> H[Data Acquisition System DAS]
    C --> H
    H --> I[Preprocessing Filter]
    I --> J[Model Class Selection]
    J --> K[Parameter Estimator LS RLS]
    K --> L[Identified Model G id s]
    L --> M[Model Validation Block]
    M --> N{Is Model Adequate?}
    N -->|Yes| O[Deploy to Controller Tuning or MPC]
    N -->|No| P[Adjust Model Order or Structure]
    P --> J
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Open-Loop Step Test Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Plant at Steady State] --> Q2[Apply Step Input Delta u]
    Q2 --> Q3[Record Response y of t]
    Q3 --> Q4[Compute Steady State Change Delta y ss]
    Q4 --> Q5[Calculate Kp equals Delta y ss divided by Delta u]
    Q5 --> Q6[Draw Tangent at Inflection Point]
    Q6 --> Q7[Read Dead Time L from Time Axis]
    Q6 --> Q8[Read Total Time T from Steady State Line]
    Q7 --> Q9[Compute Tau equals T minus L]
    Q8 --> Q9
    Q9 --> Q10[Formulate FOPDT Model G of s]
    Q10 --> Q11[Validate Model on Fresh Data]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Identification Method Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1{Is the Plant Open Loop Accessible?}
    R1 -->|Yes| R2{Is the Plant Slow and Stable?}
    R1 -->|No| R3[Use Relay Feedback Method]
    R2 -->|Yes| R4[Apply Step Test PRC Method]
    R2 -->|No| R5{Is Continuous Operation Required?}
    R5 -->|Yes| R6[Use PRBS Excitation]
    R5 -->|No| R7[Use Frequency Response Sweep]
    R4 --> R8{Non Monotonic Response?}
    R8 -->|Yes| R9[Apply Strejc or Smith Method]
    R8 -->|No| R10[Use Tangent Method FOPDT]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Recursive Least Squares Algorithm Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[New Input u of k Arrives] --> S2[New Output y of k Arrives]
    S2 --> S3[Form Regressor Vector phi of k]
    S3 --> S4[Compute Prior Output y hat of k]
    S4 --> S5[Compute Error e of k equals y of k minus y hat of k]
    S5 --> S6[Compute Gain Vector K of k]
    S6 --> S7[Update Parameter Vector theta of k]
    S7 --> S8[Update Covariance Matrix P of k]
    S8 --> S9[Store Updated Model]
    S9 --> S1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "System Identification" and list any four common methods used for identifying industrial processes.**

**Model Answer:**
System Identification is the experimental procedure of constructing a mathematical model of a dynamic system from its observed input-output data, using statistical and numerical optimization techniques, without requiring complete first-principles knowledge. The four common methods are:
1. **Step Test / Process Reaction Curve (PRC) method** — applies a step input and analyses the open-loop response.
2. **Frequency Response method** — uses sinusoidal inputs of varying frequency to obtain the Bode plot.
3. **Pseudo-Random Binary Sequence (PRBS) method** — uses broadband binary signals for simultaneous multi-frequency excitation.
4. **Recursive Least Squares (RLS) method** — uses sequential data updates for online parameter estimation.

*(Any four valid methods with one-line descriptions will fetch full 3 marks.)*

### **Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Distinguish between a "white-box model" and a "black-box model" in the context of process identification.**

**Model Answer:**

| **Aspect** | **White-Box Model** | **Black-Box Model** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Basis | Derived from first principles (mass/energy/momentum balance). | Derived purely from input-output measurements. |
| Knowledge Required | Complete physical, chemical, thermodynamic insight. | Only the input-output data records. |
| Typical Use | Reactor design, distillation column simulation. | Controller tuning, adaptive control. |
| Accuracy | High if assumptions valid; poor if model is oversimplified. | High within the operating range tested; poor extrapolation. |
| Example | A CSTR energy balance equation. | A FOPDT model fitted to a step test. |

*(Tabulated comparison with at least 4 points = 3 marks.)*

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Module 1 Internal Choice — Question A (14 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

> **Question A(a) [7 Marks]:** A process is subjected to a step change of magnitude $\Delta u = 10\%$ in its manipulated variable. The recorded output trajectory is given below. Determine the FOPDT model parameters.
>
> - Initial output $y_0 = 0\%$, Final output $y_\infty = 25\%$.
> - The tangent drawn at the inflection point crosses the time axis at $t = 1.2$ min and the final value line at $t = 4.8$ min.

> **Question A(b) [7 Marks]:** With the FOPDT model obtained in part (a), determine the suitable PID controller settings using the **Cohen-Coon tuning rules**. The desired closed-loop response should have a quarter-amplitude decay ratio.

**Model Solution for A(a):**

**Step 1 — Process Gain:**
$$K_p = \frac{y_\infty - y_0}{\Delta u} = \frac{25 - 0}{10} = 2.5$$

**Step 2 — Dead Time:**
The tangent crosses the time axis at $t = 1.2$ min, therefore:
$$L = 1.2 \text{ min}$$

**Step 3 — Time Constant:**
The tangent crosses the final-value line at $t = 4.8$ min, so:
$$\tau = T - L = 4.8 - 1.2 = 3.6 \text{ min}$$

**Step 4 — FOPDT Model:**
$$\boxed{G(s) = \frac{2.5 \, e^{-1.2 s}}{3.6 s + 1}}$$

**Model Solution for A(b):**

Cohen-Coon tuning rules for a FOPDT process require the dimensionless ratio $L / \tau$:

$$\frac{L}{\tau} = \frac{1.2}{3.6} = \frac{1}{3} = 0.333$$

The Cohen-Coon formulas for a quarter-amplitude decay are:

$$K_c = \frac{1}{K_p} \cdot \frac{\tau}{L} \cdot \left( 0.9 + \frac{L}{12 \tau} \right)$$

$$\tau_I = L \cdot \frac{30 + 3 L/\tau}{9 + 20 L/\tau}$$

$$\tau_D = L \cdot \frac{4}{11 + 2 L/\tau}$$

**Substituting values:**

$$K_c = \frac{1}{2.5} \cdot \frac{3.6}{1.2} \cdot \left( 0.9 + \frac{0.333}{12} \right) = 0.4 \cdot (0.9 + 0.0278) = 0.4 \cdot 0.9278 = 0.371$$

$$\tau_I = 1.2 \cdot \frac{30 + 3(0.333)}{9 + 20(0.333)} = 1.2 \cdot \frac{31}{15.66} = 1.2 \cdot 1.979 = 2.375 \text{ min}$$

$$\tau_D = 1.2 \cdot \frac{4}{11 + 2(0.333)} = 1.2 \cdot \frac{4}{11.666} = 1.2 \cdot 0.3429 = 0.4115 \text{ min}$$

**Final PID Settings:**

$$\boxed{K_c \approx 0.371, \quad \tau_I \approx 2.375 \text{ min}, \quad \tau_D \approx 0.412 \text{ min}}$$

---

### **Module 1 Internal Choice — Question B (14 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

> **Question B(a) [7 Marks]:** Explain the **Strejc method** of system identification. A step test on a self-regulating process yields a tangent at the inflection point with slope $R = 0.5^\circ\text{C/s}$. The total area between the response and the final value is $A_0 = 120^\circ\text{C} \cdot \text{s}$, and the area between the tangent and the response is $A_1 = 18^\circ\text{C} \cdot \text{s}$. The process gain is $K_p = 4$ and the step magnitude is $\Delta u = 2$. Determine the equivalent first-order-plus-dead-time model.

> **Question B(b) [7 Marks]:** Differentiate between the **PRBS method** and the **Frequency Response method** of system identification. Under what conditions would you prefer one over the other?

**Model Solution for B(a):**

**Step 1 — Compute $T_a$ (Total Area Time):**

$$T_a = \frac{A_0}{K_p \cdot \Delta u} = \frac{120}{4 \cdot 2} = \frac{120}{8} = 15 \text{ s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $T_u$ (Tangent Area Time):**

$$T_u = \frac{A_1}{K_p \cdot \Delta u} = \frac{18}{4 \cdot 2} = \frac{18}{8} = 2.25 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3 — Strejc Ratio:**

$$\frac{T_u}{T_a} = \frac{2.25}{15} = 0.15$$

From the standard Strejc table, $T_u / T_a = 0.15$ lies between $0.104$ ($n=2$) and $0.218$ ($n=3$). Linear interpolation suggests $n \approx 2.4$, which we round to the nearest integer order $n = 2$.

**Step 4 — Parameters for $n = 2$:**

For a second-order system identified by Strejc:
- Equivalent dead time: $L = T_a \cdot \text{(tabulated factor)}$
- Equivalent time constant: $T = (T_a - L) / n$

From the Strejc table for $n = 2$: $L/T_a = 0.282$, so:
$$L = 0.282 \cdot 15 = 4.23 \text{ s}$$

Equivalent time constant per pole:
$$T = \frac{T_a - L}{n} = \frac{15 - 4.23}{2} = 5.385 \text{ s}$$

**Step 5 — Equivalent FOPDT Model:**

The Strejc method yields an equivalent FOPDT approximation:
$$G(s) = \frac{K_p \, e^{-L s}}{T_a \, s + 1} = \frac{4 \, e^{-4.23 s}}{15 s + 1}$$

**Model Solution for B(b):**

| **Aspect** | **PRBS Method** | **Frequency Response Method** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Test Signal | Pseudo-random binary sequence (two-level). | Pure sine wave at discrete frequencies. |
| Duration | Single experiment, short duration. | Multiple experiments, long duration. |
| Frequency Content | Excites all frequencies in the bandwidth simultaneously. | Excites one frequency at a time. |
| Closed-Loop Friendly? | Yes, can be added to set point. | Difficult in closed loop without perturbing control. |
| Disturbance to Plant | Low (small amplitude PRBS). | Higher (single-frequency large amplitude). |
| Data Analysis | Cross-correlation between input and output. | Direct amplitude ratio and phase measurement. |
