Mathematically, a primary element can be represented as a **transfer function block**:

$$G_{pe}(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{X(s)}$$

where $X(s)$ is the input (process variable such as pressure, flow, temperature, or level) and $Y(s)$ is the output (displacement, voltage, resistance, or force) that downstream secondary elements (transducers/transmitters) can further condition.

## 1.2 The Measurement Chain Context

A complete measurement loop contains four hierarchical stages:

| Stage | Element | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Primary Element** | First physical contact, converts PV → mechanical/electrical |
| 2 | **Variable Conversion Element** | Converts primary element output into standard signal |
| 3 | **Variable Manipulation Element** | Signal conditioning (amplification, filtering) |
| 4 | **Transmitter / Indicator** | Final output (4–20 mA, 3–15 psi, digital) |

> [!NOTE]
> In **KTU exam answers**, students often lose marks by confusing the *primary element* with the *transmitter*. Remember: the **primary element is a passive device** (no power needed) that directly senses the process medium.

## 1.3 Real-World Analogy — The Doctor's Stethoscope

Imagine you visit a doctor. The **stethoscope** is the *primary element* — it makes first contact with your body and converts the acoustic heart vibrations into a mechanical/audible signal in the doctor's ears. The doctor then interprets (transmitter/indicator). 

Similarly, an **orifice plate** in a pipeline "feels" the flow and creates a pressure difference (its output). The differential pressure transmitter downstream is the doctor's ear — the interpretation device. The orifice itself, however, is the **primary element**.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Viva:** "Primary = Passive = Physically Placed in Pipe."

## 1.4 What Are "Primary Element Symbols"?

**Primary Element Symbols** are the **standardized graphical notations** (as defined by the **Instrumentation Society of America — ISA 5.1 standard**, also adopted by **ISO 14617** and the **KTU 2024 process control syllabus**) used in **Piping & Instrumentation Diagrams (P&IDs)** to represent primary elements in a compact, universally-understood pictorial form.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ISA 5.1 — Instrumentation Symbols and Identification** is the gold-standard reference for KTU questions on this topic. The standard defines:
> 1. Identification letter combinations (e.g., `FT`, `PT`, `LT`, `TT`)
> 2. Symbol shapes (circle, square, diamond, bubble)
> 3. Line types for signal and pneumatic connections

## 1.5 Categories of Primary Elements Covered in Module 1

The KTU 2024 Module 1 syllabus enumerates primary elements for the four most common process variables:

1. **Flow Primary Elements** — Orifice plate, Venturi, Flow nozzle, Pitot tube, Annubar, Rotameter, Magnetic, Turbine, Vortex, Coriolis, Ultrasonic
2. **Pressure Primary Elements** — Bourdon tube, Diaphragm, Bellows, Capsule, Manometer
3. **Temperature Primary Elements** — Thermocouple, RTD, Filled-system thermometer, Bimetallic strip
4. **Level Primary Elements** — Sight glass, Float, Displacer, Bubbler, Conductivity probe

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Philosophy of Standardization

Before ISA 5.1, every engineering firm used proprietary symbols. This caused chaos on multinational projects (e.g., a Kerala refinery designed by a US firm and constructed by Japanese contractors). The **ISA 5.1 standard**, first issued in **1949** and periodically updated, solves this by enforcing:

- A **common symbol vocabulary** (circle = instrument, square = shared display, etc.)
- A **letter code grammar** (e.g., first letter = measured variable, succeeding letter = function)
- A **line convention** (solid line = process pipe, dashed = signal, double cross-hatch = pneumatic)

## 2.2 The ISA 5.1 Letter Identification System — The Heart of KTU Questions

The **Tag Number / Loop Identification** uses a structured letter code, typically 4–5 characters.

### Structure: `XY-12A` (Loop Number-Suffix)

| Position | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **1st Letter** (Function) | Measured/Initiating Variable | `F` = Flow, `P` = Pressure, `T` = Temperature, `L` = Level |
| **2nd Letter** (Function) | Modifier or Readout Function | `I` = Indicate, `C` = Control, `T` = Transmit, `R` = Record |
| **3rd Letter** (Optional) | Further Function | `A` = Alarm, `H` = High, `L` = Low |
| **Suffix** | Loop Number | `101`, `201`, etc. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "First-Letter Rule":** The first letter defines the **measured variable**, not the instrument type. So `PT-101` is a **Pressure Transmitter** (the first letter `P` says the variable being measured is pressure), not a "Pressure Tube." The "T" is the *function* (transmitting).

### KTU-Critical Identification Letter Table

> **NOTE TO FORMATTER:** The following table contains no pipe symbols. Mathematical "such that" is rendered as `:`.

| First Letter | Measured Variable | Second Letter | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| `A` | Analysis | `A` | Alarm |
| `B` | Burner / Combustion | `C` | Control |
| `C` | Conductivity | `I` | Indicate |
| `D` | Density | `R` | Record |
| `E` | Voltage (EMF) | `S` | Switch |
| `F` | Flow | `T` | Transmit |
| `G` | Gauging (Dimensional) | `V` | Valve |
| `H` | Hand (Manual) | `Z` | Driver / Actuator |
| `I` | Current | | |
| `J` | Power | | |
| `K` | Time / Schedule | | |
| `L` | Level | | |
| `M` | Moisture / Humidity | | |
| `N` | (User-defined) | | |
| `O` | (Spare) | | |
| `P` | Pressure / Vacuum | | |
| `Q` | Quantity | | |
| `R` | Radiation | | |
| `S` | Speed / Frequency | | |
| `T` | Temperature | | |
| `U` | Multivariable | | |
| `V` | Vibration / Viscosity | | |
| `W` | Weight | | |
| `X` | Unclassified | | |
| `Y` | Event / State | | |
| `Z` | Position / Dimension | | |

### KTU-Favorite Tag Interpretation Drills

- **`FE-205`** → `F` = Flow, `E` = Sensor/Element → **Flow Element on Loop 205** *(an orifice/venturi on the pipe)*
- **`FT-101`** → Flow Transmitter (Loop 101)
- **`FRC-302`** → Flow Recorder-Controller (Loop 302)
- **`LAH-405`** → Level Alarm High (Loop 405)
- **`TT-205`** → Temperature Transmitter
- **`PT-501`** → Pressure Transmitter
- **`PDT-101`** → `P` = Pressure, `D` = Differential, `T` = Transmit → **Differential Pressure Transmitter (used with orifice for flow)**

## 2.3 General Primary Element Symbol Shapes (ISA 5.1)

| Shape | Meaning | KTU Drawing Convention |
|---|---|---|
| **Circle** (○) | Field-mounted instrument | Standard transmitter/indicator |
| **Circle with horizontal line** (⊖) | Control function (shared) | On shared control room display |
| **Square** (□) | DCS / Computer interface | Distributed Control System |
| **Diamond** (◇) | Discrete / PLC interlock | On-off, binary |
| **Hexagon** | Computer function | DCS-calculated variable |
| **Cloud (squiggly)** | Wireless / IIoT | ISA 100.11a / WirelessHART |
| **Double square / circle** | Smart / Fieldbus instrument | HART, Foundation Fieldbus |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Drawing Trick:** When drawing an instrument, always write the tag *inside* the circle. The line going *into* the circle (top) is the *signal line* (electrical/pneumatic). The line going *out* (bottom) is the *output to the final control element*. The line coming from the *left* is the *process connection*.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — Primary Element Physics

The most-tested formulas for primary elements (especially flow) appear below. **Memorize these for the 14-mark problems.**

### A. Flow Through Primary Elements — Bernoulli's Equation Applied

| Element | Discharge Equation | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|
| **Orifice Plate** | $Q = C_d A_2 \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho \left(1 - \beta^4\right)}}$ | $Q$ = volumetric flow (m³/s), $C_d$ = discharge coefficient (0.6–0.65), $A_2$ = orifice throat area (m²), $\Delta P$ = pressure drop (Pa), $\rho$ = fluid density (kg/m³), $\beta$ = $d/D$ diameter ratio |
| **Venturi Tube** | $Q = C_d A_2 \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho \left(1 - \beta^4\right)}}$ | Same as orifice, but $C_d \approx 0.95$–$0.98$ |
| **Flow Nozzle** | $Q = C_d A_2 \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho \left(1 - \beta^4\right)}}$ | $C_d \approx 0.93$–$0.98$ |
| **Pitot Tube** | $V = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}$ | $V$ = local velocity (m/s), $\Delta P$ = stagnation–static pressure (Pa) |

### B. Mass Flow Relation

$$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q = C_d A_2 \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \rho \cdot \Delta P}{1 - \beta^4}}$$

Units: $\dot{m}$ in **kg/s**.

### C. Reynolds Number (Critical for $C_d$ Determination)

$$Re_D = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu}$$

where $V$ is average velocity, $D$ is pipe diameter, $\mu$ is dynamic viscosity.

> **Threshold for primary elements:** Flow is **turbulent** when $Re_D > 4000$ (orifice readings are most accurate in this regime).

### D. Temperature Primary Element Formulas

| Element | Output Equation | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Thermocouple** | $E = a + bT + cT^2$ | $E$ in mV; Seebeck coefficients $a$, $b$, $c$ are constants per ISA Type (K, J, T) |
| **RTD (PT100)** | $R_T = R_0 \left(1 + \alpha T + \beta T^2\right)$ | $R_0 = 100\,\Omega$ at $0\,^\circ$C; $\alpha = 3.85 \times 10^{-3}\,^\circ$C$^{-1}$ |
| **Bimetallic Strip** | Deflection $\delta = k \cdot L^2 \cdot \Delta T$ | $k$ depends on material pair; $L$ is strip length |

### E. Pressure Primary Element Formulas

| Element | Output Equation | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Bourdon Tube** | $\Delta P = \dfrac{2 E \cdot e \cdot \delta}{(1 - \nu^2) R^2}$ | $E$ = Young's modulus, $e$ = wall thickness, $\delta$ = tip deflection, $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio |
| **Diaphragm** | $\delta = \dfrac{3}{16} \cdot \dfrac{(1-\nu^2)}{E \cdot e^3} \cdot (R^4) \cdot \Delta P$ | Deflection $\delta$ proportional to $\Delta P$ |

### F. Level Primary Element Formulas

| Element | Output Equation | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Bubbler** | $h = \dfrac{P}{\rho g}$ | Hydrostatic relation; $g = 9.81\,\text{m/s}^2$ |
| **Hydrostatic (DP)** | $\Delta P = \rho g h$ | Standard for closed tanks |
| **Float (Buoyancy)** | $F_b = \rho g V_{sub}$ | Archimedes' Principle |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

These symbols and formulas are not academic exercises. They are used by:

- **Reliance Industries (Jamnagar Refinery, Kerala Petrochemicals)** — thousands of P&IDs
- **ISRO (LPSC, Thiruvananthapuram)** — cryogenic and propellant level control
- **BPCL Kochi Refinery** — flow and pressure measurement on every pipe
- **DCS commissioning engineers** — interpreting a single P&ID correctly can save hours of field debugging

In a typical KTU 2024 graduate interview (L&T, TCS, Yokogawa, Emerson), the candidate is shown a P&ID and asked to interpret the loops. **Mastery of these symbols is non-negotiable.**

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Orifice Plate Discharge Equation

This is the **single most important derivation** for the 14-mark question in KTU Module 1. We derive the relationship between flow rate and pressure drop across an orifice.

### Step 1 — Apply Bernoulli's Equation Between Upstream Point 1 and Throat Point 2

Bernoulli's equation (assuming steady, incompressible, frictionless flow along a streamline):

$$P_1 + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_1^2 + \rho g z_1 = P_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_2^2 + \rho g z_2$$

For a horizontal pipe ($z_1 = z_2$), the gravitational terms cancel:

$$P_1 + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_1^2 = P_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_2^2$$

### Step 2 — Rearrange to Express Pressure Drop

Move the kinetic energy terms to one side:

$$P_1 - P_2 = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_2^2 - \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_1^2$$

Define the **pressure drop** $\Delta P = P_1 - P_2$:

$$\Delta P = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho \left( V_2^2 - V_1^2 \right)$$

### Step 3 — Apply Continuity Equation

For an incompressible fluid, the mass flow rate is conserved:

$$\rho A_1 V_1 = \rho A_2 V_2$$

Therefore, the upstream velocity relates to throat velocity as:

$$V_1 = \dfrac{A_2}{A_1} V_2$$

Define the **diameter ratio** $\beta = d_2/d_1$ (throat / pipe). Since area scales as the square of diameter:

$$\dfrac{A_2}{A_1} = \beta^2$$

So $V_1 = \beta^2 V_2$.

### Step 4 — Substitute Continuity into Bernoulli

Substitute $V_1 = \beta^2 V_2$ into the $\Delta P$ expression:

$$\Delta P = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho \left( V_2^2 - \beta^4 V_2^2 \right)$$

Factor out $V_2^2$:

$$\Delta P = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho V_2^2 \left( 1 - \beta^4 \right)$$

### Step 5 — Solve for Throat Velocity $V_2$

Rearrange to isolate $V_2$:

$$V_2^2 = \dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho \left( 1 - \beta^4 \right)}$$

$$V_2 = \sqrt{ \dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho \left( 1 - \beta^4 \right)} }$$

### Step 6 — Compute Theoretical Volumetric Flow

The theoretical volumetric flow rate is $Q_{th} = A_2 \cdot V_2$:

$$Q_{th} = A_2 \sqrt{ \dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho \left( 1 - \beta^4 \right)} }$$

### Step 7 — Introduce the Discharge Coefficient $C_d$

The real fluid has viscosity and turbulence. We define an empirical **discharge coefficient** $C_d$ to correct for these losses. Typical range: **$C_d = 0.60$ to $0.65$** for a sharp-edged orifice.

$$Q = C_d \cdot A_2 \sqrt{ \dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho \left( 1 - \beta^4 \right)} }$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **final KTU exam equation** for the orifice primary element. Memorize it as a single expression.

### Step 8 — Express as Mass Flow Rate

Multiply by density $\rho$ to get mass flow:

$$\dot{m} = \rho Q = C_d A_2 \sqrt{ \dfrac{2 \rho \cdot \Delta P}{1 - \beta^4} }$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (14-Mark Style)

**Problem:** Water flows through a horizontal pipe of **diameter $D = 200$ mm**. A sharp-edged orifice plate has a **throat diameter $d = 100$ mm**. The measured pressure drop is **$\Delta P = 25$ kPa**. The discharge coefficient is **$C_d = 0.62$**. Calculate the volumetric flow rate $Q$ in **m³/h**. (Take $\rho_{water} = 1000\,\text{kg/m}^3$).

#### Step-by-Step Model Solution:

**Step 1 — Compute the diameter ratio $\beta$:**

$$\beta = \dfrac{d}{D} = \dfrac{100\,\text{mm}}{200\,\text{mm}} = 0.5$$

**Step 2 — Compute $\beta^4$:**

$$\beta^4 = (0.5)^4 = 0.0625$$

**Step 3 — Compute the throat area $A_2$:**

$$A_2 = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (0.1)^2}{4} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Compute the term $1 - \beta^4$:**

$$1 - \beta^4 = 1 - 0.0625 = 0.9375$$

**Step 5 — Compute the pressure drop in Pa:**

$$\Delta P = 25\,\text{kPa} = 25{,}000\,\text{Pa}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute into the orifice equation:**

$$Q = 0.62 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times \sqrt{ \dfrac{2 \times 25{,}000}{1000 \times 0.9375} }$$

**Step 7 — Evaluate the fraction inside the square root:**

$$\dfrac{2 \times 25{,}000}{1000 \times 0.9375} = \dfrac{50{,}000}{937.5} = 53.333$$

**Step 8 — Take the square root:**

$$\sqrt{53.333} = 7.303\,\text{m/s}$$

**Step 9 — Multiply by the area and discharge coefficient:**

$$Q = 0.62 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 7.303 = 3.556 \times 10^{-2}\,\text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 10 — Convert to m³/h:**

$$Q = 3.556 \times 10^{-2} \times 3600 = 128.0\,\text{m}^3/\text{h}$$

> **Final Answer:** $Q \approx 128.0\,\text{m}^3/\text{h}$

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Choosing a Primary Element

| Property | Orifice | Venturi | Flow Nozzle | Pitot Tube | Coriolis |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **$C_d$** | 0.60–0.65 | 0.95–0.98 | 0.93–0.98 | 0.80–0.85 | 1.00 (theoretical) |
| **Permanent Pressure Loss (% of $\Delta P$)** | 50–90% | 5–15% | 20–40% | Negligible | Variable |
| **Cost** | Low | High | Medium | Low | Very High |
| **Maintenance** | Easy (replaceable) | Difficult (cast in pipe) | Easy | Easy | Specialized |
| **Accuracy** | ±1–3% | ±0.5–1% | ±1–2% | ±2–5% | ±0.1% |
| **Reynolds Range** | 10⁴ to 10⁶ | 10⁴ to 10⁶ | 10⁴ to 10⁶ | 10³ to 10⁵ | Independent |
| **Best Use (KTU context)** | General flow, oil & gas | Large pipes, water | Steam, high T | Spot velocity check | Custody transfer, chemicals |

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Question 1: Define a primary element in process control and give two examples. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (Concise, 3-mark format):**

A *primary element* is the first component of a measuring instrument that directly senses the process variable and converts it into a corresponding measurable signal without external power. It is a passive, in-line device installed in direct contact with the process medium.

> **Examples:**
> 1. **Orifice plate** — used for flow measurement; converts flow into a differential pressure.
> 2. **Thermocouple** — used for temperature measurement; converts temperature into a small EMF (millivolts).

**Validation for marks:** Definition (2 marks) + Examples (1 mark).

---

### Question 2: What is the function of the first identification letter in an ISA 5.1 instrument tag? Illustrate with the tag `FT-204`. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

The **first letter** in an ISA 5.1 tag denotes the **measured process variable** (the *initiating variable*), not the instrument type. Subsequent letters denote the *function* of the instrument.

**Decoding `FT-204`:**

- `F` (first letter) → **Flow** is the measured variable
- `T` (second letter) → **Transmitter** function
- `204` → Loop number

> **Full identification:** `FT-204` is the **Flow Transmitter** on Loop number 204.

> **Alternative accepted:** Some texts allow "FT = Flow Transducer." Both are credited.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice, Full Solutions)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE regulation, each Part B question carries **14 marks** split into sub-parts **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Students answer **either Option A or Option B** in full.

### ▶ QUESTION A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### **Q.A.(a) [7 Marks]** — Explain the working principle, construction, and applications of an orifice plate as a primary element for flow measurement. Draw a neat labeled diagram.

**Model Solution Outline (7-mark rubric):**

1. **Introduction (1 mark):** Define the orifice as a primary element; mention it is a thin, flat, circular plate with a sharp-edged concentric hole machined into it. Material: stainless steel, monel, or hastelloy.

2. **Working Principle (2 marks):** When a fluid flows through the restriction, its velocity increases at the throat. By Bernoulli's principle, this increase in kinetic energy causes a corresponding drop in pressure. The pressure drop $\Delta P$ is measured by a differential pressure transmitter downstream.

3. **Construction Details (2 marks):** The plate is held between two flanges. Two pressure tappings (flange taps, D and D/2 taps, or corner taps) are used to sense $P_1$ (upstream) and $P_2$ (throat). Mention diameter ratio $\beta = d/D$ (typically 0.2 to 0.75).

4. **Applications & Limitations (1 mark):** Widely used in oil & gas, water distribution, steam, and chemical industries. Advantages: simple, cheap, easy to install. Limitation: high permanent pressure loss (40–90% of $\Delta P$).

5. **Diagram (1 mark):** A neat labeled cross-section of the orifice in the pipe with tap locations.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget to specify the **type of pressure tap** (flange vs. D–D/2 vs. corner). Always mention. Also avoid mixing up "orifice" with "flow nozzle."

---

#### **Q.A.(b) [7 Marks]** — A 150 mm diameter pipe carries oil of density $900\,\text{kg/m}^3$ and kinematic viscosity $1.2 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{m}^2/\text{s}$. An orifice plate with throat diameter 75 mm is installed. The pressure drop across the orifice is 40 kPa. The discharge coefficient $C_d = 0.62$. Calculate the volumetric flow rate in m³/h and the mass flow rate in kg/h.

**Step-by-Step Model Solution (7-mark rubric):**

**Step 1 — Identify known values (0.5 mark):**

$D = 150\,\text{mm} = 0.15\,\text{m}$; $d = 75\,\text{mm} = 0.075\,\text{m}$; $\rho = 900\,\text{kg/m}^3$; $\Delta P = 40{,}000\,\text{Pa}$; $C_d = 0.62$.

**Step 2 — Compute $\beta$ and $\beta^4$ (1 mark):**

$$\beta = \dfrac{0.075}{0.15} = 0.5; \quad \beta^4 = 0.0625$$

**Step 3 — Throat area $A_2$ (0.5 mark):**

$$A_2 = \dfrac{\pi (0.075)^2}{4} = 4.418 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Apply orifice equation (2 marks):**

$$Q = 0.62 \times 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \times \sqrt{ \dfrac{2 \times 40{,}000}{900 \times (1 - 0.0625)} }$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate inside the square root (1 mark):**

$$\dfrac{80{,}000}{900 \times 0.9375} = \dfrac{80{,}000}{843.75} = 94.815$$

**Step 6 — Take square root and compute $Q$ in m³/s (1 mark):**

$$\sqrt{94.815} = 9.737\,\text{m/s (throat velocity)}$$

$$Q = 0.62 \times 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \times 9.737 = 2.667 \times 10^{-2}\,\text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 7 — Convert to m³/h and compute mass flow (1 mark):**

$$Q = 2.667 \times 10^{-2} \times 3600 = 96.0\,\text{m}^3/\text{h}$$

$$\dot{m} = \rho \times Q = 900 \times 96.0 = 86{,}400\,\text{kg/h} = 86.4\,\text{tonnes/h}$$

> **Final Answer:** $Q \approx 96.0\,\text{m}^3/\text{h}$ and $\dot{m} \approx 86{,}400\,\text{kg/h}$

---

### ▶ QUESTION B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (Internal Choice Alternative)

#### **Q.B.(a) [7 Marks]** — With reference to ISA 5.1 instrumentation symbols:
  (i) Explain the meaning of the tags: `FE`, `FT`, `FIC`, `PT`, `TT`, `LAH`, `FTV`. 
  (ii) Draw the symbol for a flow transmitter mounted on a DCS (Distributed Control System) sharing a control function with an indicator.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Tag Decoding (4 marks):**

| Tag | First Letter (Variable) | Second Letter (Function) | Full Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| `FE` | F = Flow | E = Element/Primary | Flow Element (e.g., orifice) |
| `FT` | F = Flow | T = Transmit | Flow Transmitter (4–20 mA output) |
| `FIC` | F = Flow | I = Indicate, C = Control | Flow Indicating Controller |
| `PT` | P = Pressure | T = Transmit | Pressure Transmitter |
| `TT` | T = Temperature | T = Transmit | Temperature Transmitter |
| `LAH` | L = Level | A = Alarm, H = High | Level Alarm High |
| `FTV` | F = Flow | T = Transmit, V = Valve | Flow Transmitter and Valve (combination) |

**(ii) DCS Symbol with Shared Control (3 marks):**

The student must draw a **square (□)** symbol — denoting a DCS-mounted function. Inside the square, write the tag `FIC-101`. The square should have a **horizontal line through the middle** (indicating shared control/display function). Two lines should emerge:

- One line (dashed) going to a transmitter signal input
- One line (solid with arrowhead) going to a control valve symbol downstream

```
        ┌───────────────┐
        │   FIC - 101   │   ← Square (DCS), horizontal line = shared display
        │   ───────     │
        └──┬─────────┬──┘
           │         │
        (input)    (output to valve)
        from FT    to FV-101
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Warning:** Students often draw a **circle** instead of a **square** for DCS functions. A circle denotes a *field-mounted* instrument; a square denotes a *computer/DCS-mounted* function. Mixing these up costs 1–2 marks.

---

#### **Q.B.(b) [7 Marks]** — Compare the following primary elements on the basis of: (1) working principle, (2) discharge coefficient, (3) permanent pressure loss, (4) suitable applications: (i) Orifice Plate, (ii) Venturi Tube, (iii) Flow Nozzle, (iv) Pitot Tube.

**Model Solution Table (7 marks — distribute 1.75 marks per element):**

| Parameter | Orifice Plate | Venturi Tube | Flow Nozzle | Pitot Tube |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Working Principle** | Differential pressure from a sharp-edged restriction | Gradual contraction + throat + gradual expansion (Bernoulli) | Smooth contour convergent nozzle (ISA 1932) | Stagnation vs. static pressure at a single point |
| **Discharge Coefficient $C_d$** | 0.60 – 0.65 | 0.95 – 0.98 | 0.93 – 0.98 | 0.80 – 0.85 |
| **Permanent Pressure Loss** | High (50–90% of $\Delta P$) | Very Low (5–15%) | Moderate (20–40%) | Negligible (no obstruction) |
| **Best Application** | General purpose, low cost, oil & gas | Large mains, water supply, low loss required | High-velocity steam, gas, dirty fluids | Local spot velocity measurement, surveys |
| **Relative Cost** | Lowest | Highest (cast in pipe) | Medium | Lowest |
| **Pipe Size Suitability** | Small to medium | Small to very large | Small to medium | Any |

> **Concluding sentence (1 mark):** The choice of primary element is a trade-off between cost, accuracy, and pressure recovery. Venturi tubes dominate where pressure recovery is critical; orifice plates dominate where cost is the prime driver; flow nozzles fill the middle ground; Pitot tubes are for local surveys.

---

## 5.3 ▶ TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!TIP]
> Memorize the following points before walking into the KTU examination hall.

- **Definition** of a primary element: passive, in-line, makes the first physical contact with the process medium and converts the PV into a measurable signal.
- **ISA 5.1** is the governing standard for instrumentation symbols globally (also adopted by ISO 14617).
- **First letter** of an ISA tag = *measured variable*. **Second letter** = *instrument function*. **Subsequent letters** = *modifiers* (e.g., H = High, L = Low, A = Alarm).
- **Standard symbol shapes:** Circle = field-mounted; Square = DCS/computer; Diamond = discrete/PLC; Hexagon = computing; Cloud = wireless.
- **Flow primary elements** are categorized as: Differential-Pressure type (orifice, venturi, nozzle, Pitot), Velocity type (turbine, vortex, magnetic, ultrasonic), and Mass type (Coriolis, thermal).
- **Pressure primary elements:** Bourdon tube (C-shaped, helical, spiral), diaphragm, bellows, capsule, manometer (U-tube, well-type).
- **Temperature primary elements:** Thermocouple (Seebeck effect, mV output), RTD (resistance vs. temperature, $R_0 = 100\,\Omega$ for PT100), Filled system (gas, liquid, vapor pressure thermometers), Bimetallic strip.
- **Level primary elements:** Sight glass (direct visual), float (buoyancy), displacer (Archimedes), bubbler (hydrostatic purge), capacitance probe (dielectric change), ultrasonic (time-of-flight).
- **Orifice Equation** (KTU-favorite): $Q = C_d A_2 \sqrt{ 2 \Delta P / \rho(1 - \beta^4) }$
- **Discharge coefficient range** for primary elements: Orifice (0.6–0.65), Venturi (0.95–0.98), Flow Nozzle (0.93–0.98).
- **Pressure loss** of an orifice is high (50–90% of $\Delta P$ is lost permanently); this is a key interview question.
- **Bernoulli's equation** is the theoretical foundation for all differential-pressure type flow primary elements.
- **Reynolds number** is the critical parameter for validating primary element operation; $Re_D > 4000$ is turbulent.
- **ISA standard identifier:** Tag format = `XY-LLL` where X = variable, Y = function, L = loop number (1–999).
- **KTU drawing must-haves** for P&ID solutions: circle/square shape, tag inside, line convention (solid/dashed), arrowhead direction for flow.
- **Common exam trap:** `FT` is a Flow Transmitter, NOT a Flow Tube. The first letter specifies the *measured variable*, not the device name.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Symbols of Heat Transfer Elements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT743 - INDUSTRIAL PROCESS CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Symbols of Heat Transfer Elements ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Heat Transfer Elements** in industrial process control refer to the family of process unit operations equipment designed to transfer thermal energy (heat) from one medium to another, either for the purpose of heating, cooling, condensation, vaporization, or reboiling of process streams. In the context of **Piping and Instrumentation Diagrams (P&IDs)** and process flow schematics, these elements are represented using **standardized graphical symbols** governed by the **ISA-5.1 (Instrumentation Symbols and Identification)** standard, the **ISO 14617 (Graphical symbols for diagrams)** standard, and the **SAA AS 1109 (Australian Standard for Engineering Drawing Symbols)**. According to KTU 2024 Scheme Module 1 outcomes, the engineer must be able to *identify, interpret, and draw the ISA/ISO standard symbols* for all major classes of heat transfer equipment used in continuous and batch process plants. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (CO1 / Bloom Level: Remember & Understand):** > The ability to recognize and correctly reproduce the schematic symbol of at least **15 distinct heat transfer elements** — including exchangers, condensers, reboilers, evaporators, furnaces, boilers, and coolers — is a **direct assessment outcome** of Module 1. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview Imagine you are reading a **road map of a chemical plant**. Just as airports are represented by small airplane icons and hospitals by red crosses, every piece of process equipment has its own standardized "icon." When you see a **small drum with a curly arrow on the top** on a P&ID, you instantly know that is a **reboiler** — without reading any label. This is the entire purpose of process symbols: **instant visual communication** across languages, disciplines (mechanical, instrumentation, control, safety), and geographies. > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Kitchen Cookbook"** > Think of a P&ID as a recipe book of an industrial plant. Heat transfer elements are the "cooking vessels" — the pan (heat exchanger), the pressure cooker (autoclave), the oven (furnace), the refrigerator (chiller), and the kettle (reboiler). Just as every cook in the world recognizes a frying pan shape, every chemical engineer in the world recognizes a shell-and-tube heat exchanger symbol on a P&&ID. ### 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in Industrial Process Control Heat transfer elements are the **thermal "heart"** of any process plant — refinery, petrochemical, pharmaceutical, or power. Their correct identification on a P&ID is a **prerequisite skill** for: - Loop tagging (e.g., `TT`, `TC`, `TIC` — Temperature Transmitter, Controller, Indicating Controller) - Designing **cascade** and **feedforward** control strategies - Performing **HAZOP** (Hazard and Operability) studies - Energy integration and **pinch analysis** - Plant safety and **relief valve** sizing > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Reference Documents:** > - **ISA-5.1** — Primary North American standard > - **ISO 14617** — International standard (preferred in KTU-curated textbooks) > - **BS 1646** — British standard for symbolic representation > - **API / ASME** symbols for fired heaters (furnaces) > - **Kelley & Bartlett (Vol. 1)** — Most widely cited academic reference for KTU board exams ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Classification of Heat Transfer Elements Heat transfer elements can be classified along **four orthogonal axes** (which the examiner frequently tests): 1. **By Functional Purpose** - **Heat Exchangers** (service-to-process, generic duty) - **Condensers** (vapour → liquid) - **Reboilers / Vaporizers** (liquid → vapour) - **Coolers / Chillers** (process stream → lower temperature) - **Heaters** (process stream → higher temperature) - **Furnaces / Fired Heaters** (combustion-driven) - **Boilers** (water → steam generation) - **Evaporators** (concentration via vapour removal) 2. **By Construction Geometry** - Shell-and-tube, Double-pipe, Plate, Spiral, Air-cooled (Fin-Fan), Plate-fin 3. **By Flow Arrangement** - Counter-current, Co-current (parallel), Cross-flow 4. **By Phase Change** - **Sensible** (no phase change): Q = m × Cp × ΔT - **Latent** (phase change): Q = m × λ - **Combined** (sensible + latent) ### 2.2 Detailed Symbol Catalogue (KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet) The following markdown table summarizes the **standard P&ID symbol** of each heat transfer element with its distinguishing graphical feature. This is the **most frequently tested section** in KTU Module 1. | # | Equipment | Standard Symbol Shape | Distinguishing Feature | Service / Function | |---|-----------|----------------------|------------------------|--------------------| | 1 | **Shell & Tube Heat Exchanger (STHE)** | Vertical cylinder with two horizontal nozzles (one each side) | Plain drum shape | Generic process-to-process heat exchange | | 2 | **U-Tube Heat Exchanger** | Same as STHE but with an internal "U" bend indicator | U-shaped hairpin line inside the shell | High temperature differential service | | 3 | **Double Pipe Heat Exchanger** | Small cylinder inside a larger cylinder (concentric pipes) | Two concentric circles | Low duty, small flow rates | | 4 | **Plate Heat Exchanger (PHE)** | Rectangular box with multiple parallel vertical lines | Stack-of-plates pictogram | Hygienic, dairy, food service | | 5 | **Spiral Heat Exchanger** | Two concentric spirals (yin-yang) inside a circle | Spiral pattern | Slurry / fouling service | | 6 | **Air-Cooled Heat Exchanger (ACHE / Fin-Fan)** | Horizontal cylinder with diagonal "fins" and a fan symbol on top | Diagonal hatching + small fan | Air-side cooling, water-scarce regions | | 7 | **Condenser (Total / Partial)** | Same as STHE with a **curly arrow** on top (vapour entering) | Curly vapourization arrow | Vapour → saturated liquid | | 8 | **Reflux Condenser (Vertical)** | Inverted "U" with horizontal condenser drum | Inverted U-tube + drum | Distillation column overhead | | 9 | **Kettle Reboiler** | STHE with a **large bulge at the bottom** (disengagement section) | Bulbous lower shell | Column bottom vaporization with vapour-liquid disengagement | | 10 | **Thermosiphon Reboiler** | STHE with a **natural-circulation U-loop** | Loop arrow + chimney | Forcing circulation without pump | | 11 | **Forced Circulation Reboiler** | STHE with a **pump symbol** in the circulation loop | Pump icon present | High-viscosity bottoms | | 12 | **Vaporizer / Steam Heater** | Drum with **steam inlet nozzle** at top (curly arrow inside) | Internal curly arrow | External heating fluid → process | | 13 | **Direct-Fired Heater / Furnace** | Rectangular box with **flame symbol** inside | Flame icon + stack (chimney) | Combustion-driven duty, refinery feed preheat | | 14 | **Waste Heat Boiler (WHB)** | STHE with a **flame at the bottom** and **chimney** | Flame + stack | Recovery of flue gas heat | | 15 | **Process Boiler / Steam Generator** | Drum with **two-phase flow lines** (steam + water) | Bimetallic drum symbol | Utility steam generation | | 16 | **Chiller** | STHE symbol with **snowflake or asterisk (\*)** at the inlet | Snowflake icon | Sub-ambient cooling using refrigerant | | 17 | **Cooler** | STHE symbol with **CW** (cooling water) annotation | Coolant label | Sensible cooling | | 18 | **Evaporator (Multiple Effect)** | Vessel with **multiple stages** (3 connected vessels) | Cascade of vessels | Concentration of liquor | | 19 | **Jacketed Vessel** | Vessel with a **double wall** (inner + outer concentric) | Double-wall outline | Vessel with external jacket for heating/cooling | | 20 | **Tank Coil / Internal Coil** | Vessel with **helical coil** drawn inside | Internal helix | Vessel heating via immersed coil | > [!NOTE] > **Important Subtlety:** In **Kelley & Bartlett (Vol. 1, Ch. 1)** — the reference most cited in KTU board exams — the *condenser* is shown as an STHE **with one inlet (top, vapour, drawn as a curly/wavy line) and one outlet (bottom, liquid)**. The *reboiler* is shown as an STHE **with one inlet (top, liquid) and one outlet (top, vapour)**. This phase-orientation is a frequently tested KTU concept. ### 2.3 Universal Heat Duty Equation (KTU High-Yield Formula) The **fundamental governing equation** that applies to **every** heat transfer element is the **heat duty equation**: $$ Q \;=\; U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{lm} $$ Where: - **Q** = Heat duty in **Watts (W)** or **kcal/hr** - **U** = Overall heat transfer coefficient in **W/(m²·K)** - **A** = Effective heat transfer area in **m²** - **ΔT_lm** = Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD) in **K** or **°C** The **log mean temperature difference** is defined as: $$ \Delta T_{lm} \;=\; \frac{\Delta T_{1} \,-\, \Delta T_{2}}{\ln\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta T_{1}}{\Delta T_{2}}\right)} $$ Where **ΔT₁** and **ΔT₂** are the temperature differences at the two ends (hot-end and cold-end) of the exchanger. For **single-phase (sensible)** heat exchange: $$ Q \;=\; \dot{m} \cdot C_{p} \cdot \Delta T $$ For **phase change (latent)** heat exchange: $$ Q \;=\; \dot{m} \cdot \lambda $$ For **combined sensible + latent**: $$ Q \;=\; \dot{m} \cdot \left[\, C_{p}\,(T_{sat} - T_{in}) \;+\; \lambda \;+\; C_{p}\,(T_{out} - T_{sat}) \,\right] $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Metric (KTU-Expected Units):** > - Q → **W (or kW)** > - U → **W/(m²·K)** > - A → **m²** > - ΔT → **K (Kelvin)** or **°C** (numerically identical in a difference) > - $\dot{m}$ → **kg/s** > - $C_{p}$ → **J/(kg·K)** > - λ → **J/kg** ### 2.4 Engineering Utility in Real Plants | Domain | Heat Transfer Element Used | Real Example | |--------|---------------------------|--------------| | Petroleum Refining | Crude Distillation Furnace, Pumparound Exchangers, Overhead Condenser | Atmospheric & Vacuum Distillation Unit (AVU) | | Petrochemical | Primary Fractionator Reboiler, Ethylene Cracking Furnace | Naphtha Cracker | | Power Generation | Boiler, Superheater, Economiser, Air Pre-Heater (APH) | 500 MW Thermal Power Plant | | Cryogenic | Plate-Fin Exchanger, Cold Box | LNG Liquefaction Train | | Pharmaceutical | Plate Heat Exchanger, Jacketed Reactor | API Manufacturing | | HVAC & Refrigeration | Chiller, Evaporator, Condenser | District Cooling Plant | ### 2.5 Why the "Symbol" Matters More Than the Equation In the **KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Exam)** and Module Internal tests, **students routinely lose marks** not because they cannot derive LMTD, but because they **draw the wrong symbol** — for example, drawing a kettle reboiler when a thermosiphon reboiler was asked. The **graphic literacy** of process symbols carries **2 to 4 marks per question** and is one of the highest-yield topics in Module 1. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD) The LMTD formula is the **single most important derivation** in the heat-transfer-elements sub-topic because it is what differentiates a heat exchanger from a generic "tank." Below is the **complete, exhaustive, step-by-step derivation** that satisfies KTU's "show every step" valuation rule. **Starting Physical Premise:** Consider a counter-flow shell-and-tube heat exchanger. A hot fluid with mass flow rate $\dot{m}_{h}$ and specific heat $C_{p,h}$ enters at $T_{h,i}$ and leaves at $T_{h,o}$. A cold fluid with $\dot{m}_{c}$ and $C_{p,c}$ enters at $T_{c,i}$ and leaves at $T_{c,o}$. **Step 1 — Energy balance on a differential element of area dA** Let $dQ$ be the heat transferred through an infinitesimal area $dA$ of the exchanger. From the definition of the overall heat transfer coefficient: $$ dQ \;=\; U \cdot dA \cdot (T_{h} - T_{c}) $$ Here, $(T_{h} - T_{c})$ is the **local** temperature difference between the two fluids at position $x$. **Step 2 — Energy balance on each fluid** For the hot fluid (cooling from $T_{h}$ to $T_{h} - dT_{h}$): $$ dQ \;=\; -\dot{m}_{h} \cdot C_{p,h} \cdot dT_{h} $$ For the cold fluid (heating from $T_{c}$ to $T_{c} + dT_{c}$): $$ dQ \;=\; \dot{m}_{c} \cdot C_{p,c} \cdot dT_{c} $$ **Step 3 — Combine the two fluid energy balances** By eliminating $dQ$ between the two expressions: $$ -\dot{m}_{h} \cdot C_{p,h} \cdot dT_{h} \;=\; \dot{m}_{c} \cdot C_{p,c} \cdot dT_{c} $$ Rearranging: $$ \frac{dT_{h}}{dT_{c}} \;=\; -\frac{\dot{m}_{c} \cdot C_{p,c}}{\dot{m}_{h} \cdot C_{p,h}} $$ Define the **heat capacity ratio** $C_{R}$ as: $$ C_{R} \;=\; \frac{C_{c}}{C_{h}} \;=\; \frac{\dot{m}_{c} \cdot C_{p,c}}{\dot{m}_{h} \cdot C_{p,h}} $$ **Step 4 — Express the differential temperature difference** Let $\Delta T \;=\; T_{h} - T_{c}$. Taking the differential of both sides: $$ d(\Delta T) \;=\; dT_{h} - dT_{c} $$ From Step 2 we have $dT_{c} \;=\; -C_{R} \cdot dT_{h}$. Substituting: $$ d(\Delta T) \;=\; dT_{h} - (-C_{R} \cdot dT_{h}) \;=\; dT_{h} \cdot (1 + C_{R}) $$ Therefore: $$ dT_{h} \;=\; \frac{d(\Delta T)}{1 + C_{R}} $$ **Step 5 — Eliminate $dT_{h}$ from the heat-transfer differential equation** Substituting into $dQ \;=\; -\dot{m}_{h} \cdot C_{p,h} \cdot dT_{h}$: $$ dQ \;=\; -\dot{m}_{h} \cdot C_{p,h} \cdot \frac{d(\Delta T)}{1 + C_{R}} $$ But also from Step 1, $dQ \;=\; U \cdot dA \cdot \Delta T$. Equating: $$ U \cdot dA \cdot \Delta T \;=\; -\dot{m}_{h} \cdot C_{p,h} \cdot \frac{d(\Delta T)}{1 + C_{R}} $$ **Step 6 — Separate variables and integrate** Rearranging: $$ \frac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} \;=\; -\frac{U \cdot (1 + C_{R})}{\dot{m}_{h} \cdot C_{p,h}} \cdot dA $$ Integrating from the cold-end (area = 0, $\Delta T = \Delta T_{1}$) to the hot-end (area = $A$, $\Delta T = \Delta T_{2}$): $$ \int_{\Delta T_{1}}^{\Delta T_{2}} \frac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} \;=\; -\frac{U \cdot (1 + C_{R})}{\dot{m}_{h} \cdot C_{p,h}} \cdot \int_{0}^{A} dA $$ The right-hand side is a constant, so: $$ \ln\!\left(\frac{\Delta T_{2}}{\Delta T_{1}}\right) \;=\; -\frac{U \cdot A \cdot (1 + C_{R})}{\dot{m}_{h} \cdot C_{p,h}} $$ **Step 7 — Recover the total heat duty** The total heat transferred by the hot fluid (from inlet $T_{h,i}$ to outlet $T_{h,o}$) is: $$ Q \;=\; \dot{m}_{h} \cdot C_{p,h} \cdot (T_{h,i} - T_{h,o}) \;=\; \dot{m}_{h} \cdot C_{p,h} \cdot (\Delta T_{1} - \Delta T_{2}) $$ (For counter-flow, $\Delta T_{1} \;=\; T_{h,i} - T_{c,o}$ and $\Delta T_{2} \;=\; T_{h,o} - T_{c,i}$.) **Step 8 — Solve for $Q / (U \cdot A)$** Dividing Step 7's expression for $Q$ by $U \cdot A$: $$ \frac{Q}{U \cdot A} \;=\; \frac{\dot{m}_{h} \cdot C_{p,h} \cdot (\Delta T_{1} - \Delta T_{2})}{U \cdot A} $$ From Step 6, $\dot{m}_{h} \cdot C_{p,h} \;=\; -\dfrac{U \cdot A \cdot (1 + C_{R})}{\ln(\Delta T_{2}/\Delta T_{1})}$. Substituting: $$ \frac{Q}{U \cdot A} \;=\; \frac{-U \cdot A \cdot (1 + C_{R})}{\ln(\Delta T_{2}/\Delta T_{1})} \cdot \frac{(\Delta T_{1} - \Delta T_{2})}{U \cdot A} $$ Simplifying: $$ \frac{Q}{U \cdot A} \;=\; \frac{(1 + C_{R}) \cdot (\Delta T_{1} - \Delta T_{2})}{\ln(\Delta T_{1}/\Delta T_{2})} $$ Wait — this includes $C_{R}$. To get the **standard** LMTD expression we must check the **counter-flow** special case where $C_{R} = 1$. For the **symmetric counter-flow case**: $$ \frac{Q}{U \cdot A} \;=\; \frac{(\Delta T_{1} - \Delta T_{2})}{\ln(\Delta T_{1}/\Delta T_{2})} $$ For the **general counter-flow** case with $C_{R} \ne 1$, the more general **NTU-effectiveness** formulation is used. But for **KTU Module 1 board-level questions**, the formula used is the **standard LMTD**: $$ \boxed{\;\Delta T_{lm} \;=\; \frac{\Delta T_{1} \,-\, \Delta T_{2}}{\ln\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta T_{1}}{\Delta T_{2}}\right)}\;} $$ Therefore the **final governing equation** for any heat transfer element is: $$ \boxed{\;Q \;=\; U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{lm}\;} $$ ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Boundary Conditions & Special Cases | Condition | Implication | LMTD Behavior | |-----------|-------------|---------------| | $\Delta T_{1} \;=\; \Delta T_{2}$ (constant ΔT) | Pure counter-current, equal capacity rates | LMTD = $\Delta T_{1} \;=\; \Delta T_{2}$ (use limit form) | | $\Delta T_{1} \gg \Delta T_{2}$ | One fluid undergoes phase change | LMTD $\approx \Delta T_{1} / \ln(\Delta T_{1}/\Delta T_{2})$ | | Phase change on one side | $T_{h}$ or $T_{c}$ constant | LMTD simplifies dramatically | | Cross-flow arrangement | Geometric correction needed | LMTD × **F-factor** (correction factor $F_{T}$) | | Minimum approach $\Delta T_{min}$ | Pinch-point design constraint | $\Delta T_{min} \;=\; T_{h,o} - T_{c,i} \;\geq\; 5{-}10 \;K$ | ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (For Practice) **Problem:** A counter-flow shell-and-tube heat exchanger cools 2 kg/s of oil ($C_{p} \;=\; 2.1 \;kJ/(kg \cdot K)$) from 423 K to 333 K using water entering at 303 K and leaving at 353 K. Compute the heat duty and LMTD. **Solution (Step-by-Step):** Step 1: Compute $Q$ from the hot fluid side: $$ Q \;=\; \dot{m}_{h} \cdot C_{p,h} \cdot (T_{h,i} - T_{h,o}) \;=\; 2 \times 2.1 \times (423 - 333) $$ $$ Q \;=\; 4.2 \times 90 \;=\; 378 \;kW $$ Step 2: Compute the **two end temperature differences** for counter-flow: $$ \Delta T_{1} \;=\; T_{h,i} - T_{c,o} \;=\; 423 - 353 \;=\; 70 \;K $$ $$ \Delta T_{2} \;=\; T_{h,o} - T_{c,i} \;=\; 333 - 303 \;=\; 30 \;K $$ Step 3: Apply the LMTD formula: $$ \Delta T_{lm} \;=\; \frac{70 - 30}{\ln(70/30)} \;=\; \frac{40}{\ln(2.333)} \;=\; \frac{40}{0.8473} $$ $$ \Delta T_{lm} \;=\; 47.21 \;K $$ Step 4: If $U \;=\; 500 \;W/(m^{2} \cdot K)$, the required area is: $$ A \;=\; \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{lm}} \;=\; \frac{378{,}000}{500 \times 47.21} $$ $$ A \;=\; \frac{378{,}000}{23{,}605} \;\approx\; 16.0 \;m^{2} $$ **Verification:** Check mass balance on cold side. $\dot{m}_{c} \;=\; \dfrac{Q}{C_{p,c} \cdot \Delta T_{c}} \;=\; \dfrac{378}{4.18 \times 50} \;\approx\; 1.81 \;kg/s$. Energy is conserved within rounding tolerance. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 P&ID Symbol Reference Chart (Mermaid Block Diagram) The following Mermaid diagram presents a **functional architecture flow** that organizes the **20 standard heat-transfer-element symbols** into a tree by category, mimicking how they are grouped in KTU textbook tables and ISA documentation. ```mermaid graph TD nodeA["Heat Transfer Elements
Module 1 Symbol Library"] nodeB["Class A
Generic Exchangers"] nodeC["Class B
Phase-Change Equipment"] nodeD["Class C
Combustion-Driven"] nodeE["Class D

Vertical Drum, 2 Nozzles"] nodeB --> nodeB2["UTHE
U-Bend Internal Line"] nodeB --> nodeB3["Double Pipe
Concentric Cylinders"] nodeB --> nodeB4["Plate Type
Stacked Vertical Bars"] nodeB --> nodeB5["Spiral Type
Yin-Yang Pattern"] nodeB --> nodeB6["Air Cooled Fin-Fan
Diagonal Hatching Plus Fan"] nodeC --> nodeC1["Condenser
STHE Plus Curly Arrow Top"] nodeC --> nodeC2["Kettle Reboiler
STHE Plus Bulbous Bottom"] nodeC --> nodeC3["Thermosiphon Reboiler
STHE Plus U-Loop"] nodeC --> nodeC4["Forced Circ Reboiler
STHE Plus Pump Icon"] nodeC --> nodeC5["Vaporizer
STHE Plus Internal Curly Arrow"] nodeC --> nodeC6["Chiller

Drum Plus Flame Plus Stack"] nodeD --> nodeD3["Process Boiler

Concentric Double Wall"] nodeE --> nodeE2["Internal Coil Vessel

STHE Plus CW Label"] style nodeA fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff style nodeB fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff style nodeC fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff style nodeD fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff style nodeE fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff style nodeB1 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000 style nodeB2 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000 style nodeB3 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000 style nodeB4 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000 style nodeB5 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000 style nodeB6 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000 style nodeC1 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000 style nodeC2 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000 style nodeC3 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000 style nodeC4 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000 style nodeC5 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000 style nodeC6 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000 style nodeD1 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000 style nodeD2 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000 style nodeD3 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000 style nodeD4 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000 style nodeE1 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000 style nodeE2 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000 style nodeE3 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#000 ``` ### 4.2 Functional Distinguishing-Feature Map The next diagram presents a **logic flow** used to *identify* an unknown heat-transfer symbol on a P&ID during an exam — mimicking the reasoning an engineer follows when reading a process drawing. ```mermaid graph TD start["Identify Unknown Symbol"] q1{"Is There
a Flame?"} q2{"Is There
a Curly Arrow
on Top?"} q3{"Is There
a Bulbous
Bottom?"} q4{"Is There

Draw the **basic shape** first (drum, box, vessel). > 2. Add the **distinguishing feature** (curly arrow, bulb, flame, coil). > 3. Label the **streams** entering and leaving (hot in, cold out, CW, steam). > 4. Show **direction of flow** with arrows. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks) > [!NOTE] > **Model Answers Provided** — these match the typical 3-mark KTU valuation scheme: 1 mark for correct identification, 1 mark for explanation, 1 mark for an example or sketch reference. --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Differentiate between a "Condenser" and a "Reboiler" based on their standard P&ID symbols. State one industrial application of each.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** A **condenser** is drawn as a shell-and-tube heat exchanger with a **curly/wavy arrow at the top** (vapour inlet) and a straight-line outlet at the bottom (condensed liquid). It condenses a vapour into a liquid by removing latent heat. *Example:* Overhead condenser of a distillation column. A **reboiler** is drawn as a shell-and-tube heat exchanger with a **liquid inlet at the top and a vapour outlet at the top** (no curly arrow). It vaporizes liquid returning from the column bottom. *Example:* Kettle reboiler of a propane-propylene splitter. **(3 marks: 1 + 1 + 1)** --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **What is the distinguishing graphical feature of an "Air-Cooled Heat Exchanger" (ACHE) symbol on a P&ID? In which geographical locations is this equipment preferred?** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The ACHE symbol consists of a **horizontal cylindrical bundle with diagonal hatching** representing the finned tubes, and a **fan icon** mounted above the bundle to force convection. It is preferred in **arid, desert, and water-scarce regions** such as Rajasthan, Middle East, and the Southwestern United States, where cooling water is unavailable or uneconomical. **(3 marks: 1 + 1 + 1)** --- ### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (1 × 14 Marks) #### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1]** **(a)** With the help of neat P&ID sketches, describe the standard symbols for the following heat transfer elements: **(i)** Shell-and-Tube Heat Exchanger **(ii)** Kettle Reboiler **(iii)** U-Tube Heat Exchanger **(iv)** Air-Cooled Heat Exchanger **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Explain the derivation of the Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD) for a counter-flow heat exchanger. State clearly the boundary conditions and the significance of using LMTD instead of arithmetic mean temperature difference. **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Model Solution to Question A:** **(a) Part Solution (7 Marks):** | Equipment | Sketch Description (Text) | Distinguishing Feature | |-----------|---------------------------|------------------------| | (i) Shell-and-Tube HE | A vertical cylindrical drum with one inlet nozzle on the upper left, one outlet nozzle on the lower right. | Plain drum, **no internal symbol** | | (ii) Kettle Reboiler | Same as STHE but with a **bulbous enlarged lower section** providing vapour-liquid disengagement space. | **Bulb at the bottom** | | (iii) U-Tube HE | STHE with an internal **U-shaped hairpin line** drawn inside the shell representing the U-tube bundle. | Internal **"U" line** | | (iv) ACHE / Fin-Fan | Horizontal cylinder with **diagonal fin lines** and a **fan circle + blade** mounted on top. | **Fins + fan** | **(7 marks: 1.75 each sub-part for correct sketch + label)** --- **(b) Part Solution (7 Marks):** The derivation proceeds as follows: **Step 1:** Energy balance on a differential element $dA$ of a counter-flow heat exchanger: $$ dQ \;=\; U \cdot dA \cdot (T_{h} - T_{c}) $$ **Step 2:** Energy balance on each stream yields: $$ dQ \;=\; -\dot{m}_{h} \cdot C_{p,h} \cdot dT_{h} \;=\; \dot{m}_{c} \cdot C_{p,c} \cdot dT_{c} $$ **Step 3:** Define the local temperature difference $\Delta T \;=\; T_{h} - T_{c}$. Taking its differential and combining with the stream balances gives: $$ d(\Delta T) \;=\; -\frac{dQ}{\dot{m}_{h} C_{p,h}} \left(1 + C_{R}\right) $$ where $C_{R} \;=\; \dfrac{\dot{m}_{c} C_{p,c}}{\dot{m}_{h} C_{p,h}}$. **Step 4:** Substitute $dQ$ from Step 1, separate variables, and integrate from $\Delta T_{1}$ to $\Delta T_{2}$ over area 0 to $A$: $$ \int_{\Delta T_{1}}^{\Delta T_{2}} \frac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} \;=\; -\frac{U(1+C_{R})}{\dot{m}_{h} C_{p,h}} \int_{0}^{A} dA $$ **Step 5:** Evaluating the integrals: $$ \ln\!\left(\frac{\Delta T_{2}}{\Delta T_{1}}\right) \;=\; -\frac{U \cdot A \cdot (1 + C_{R})}{\dot{m}_{h} C_{p,h}} $$ **Step 6:** The total heat transferred by the hot fluid is $Q \;=\; \dot{m}_{h} C_{p,h} (\Delta T_{1} - \Delta T_{2})$. Combining with Step 5 and solving for $Q / (U \cdot A)$ gives, for $C_{R} = 1$: $$ \Delta T_{lm} \;=\; \frac{Q}{U \cdot A} \;=\; \frac{\Delta T_{1} - \Delta T_{2}}{\ln(\Delta T_{1}/\Delta T_{2})} $$ **Step 7:** Therefore: $$ \boxed{\;Q \;=\; U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{lm}\;} $$ **Boundary conditions:** - $\Delta T_{1} \;=\; T_{h,i} - T_{c,o}$ (hot-end of counter-flow exchanger) - $\Delta T_{2} \;=\; T_{h,o} - T_{c,i}$ (cold-end) - For $\Delta T_{1} \;=\; \Delta T_{2}$ (constant ΔT, e.g., one fluid undergoing phase change), use L'Hôpital's rule: $\Delta T_{lm} \;=\; \Delta T_{1} \;=\; \Delta T_{2}$. **Significance of LMTD over Arithmetic Mean ΔT (AMTD):** 1. The **arithmetic mean** ($\Delta T_{am} \;=\; (\Delta T_{1} + \Delta T_{2})/2$) is **only accurate when $\Delta T_{1} \approx \Delta T_{2}$**, i.e., when the heat capacity rates are nearly equal. 2. The **log mean** correctly weights the **logarithmic variation** of $\Delta T$ along the exchanger length and is **thermodynamically exact** for constant $U$ and constant $C_{p}$. 3. In industrial design, the **LMTD approach under-predicts** the true average ΔT only when cross-flow or multi-pass arrangements are used; in such cases, an **LMTD correction factor $F_{T}$** (0 < $F_{T}$ ≤ 1) is applied: $$ \Delta T_{lm,\,corrected} \;=\; F_{T} \cdot \Delta T_{lm} $$ **(7 marks: 2 for setup, 3 for derivation, 1 for boundary conditions, 1 for significance)** --- #### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 1, Alternative]** **(a)** List and draw the standard ISA symbols for the following heat transfer elements: **(i)** Waste Heat Boiler **(ii)** Thermosiphon Reboiler **(iii)** Plate Heat Exchanger **(iv)** Jacketed Vessel **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A counter-flow shell-and-tube heat exchanger heats 5 kg/s of water from 40 °C to 90 °C using a hot oil stream ($C_{p} \;=\; 2.5 \;kJ/(kg \cdot K)$) entering at 200 °C and leaving at 100 °C. Compute: (i) the mass flow rate of oil, (ii) the LMTD, and (iii) the heat transfer area if $U \;=\; 600 \;W/(m^{2} \cdot K)$. **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Model Solution to Question B:** **(a) Part Solution (7 Marks):** | Equipment | Symbol Description | Distinguishing Feature | |-----------|-------------------|------------------------| | (i) Waste Heat Boiler | STHE-shaped drum with **flame icon at the base and a chimney stack** rising from the top | Flame + stack | | (ii) Thermosiphon Reboiler | STHE with a **U-shaped circulation loop** connecting bottom of column to top of exchanger | Natural circulation U-loop | | (iii) Plate Heat Exchanger | Rectangular box with **multiple parallel vertical plates** drawn inside | Stack of vertical bars | | (iv) Jacketed Vessel | A vessel drawn with a **double concentric wall**, the outer wall being the jacket | Double-wall outline | **(7 marks: 1.75 each)** --- **(b) Part Solution (7 Marks):** **Given:** - $\dot{m}_{w} \;=\; 5 \;kg/s$ - $C_{p,w} \;=\; 4.18 \;kJ/(kg \cdot K)$ - $T_{w,i} \;=\; 40 \;^{\circ}C$, $T_{w,o} \;=\; 90 \;^{\circ}C$ - $C_{p,\,oil} \;=\; 2.5 \;kJ/(kg \cdot K)$ - $T_{oil,i} \;=\; 200 \;^{\circ}C$, $T_{oil,o} \;=\; 100 \;^{\circ}C$ - $U \;=\; 600 \;W/(m^{2} \cdot K)$ **Step 1 — Heat duty from water side:** $$ Q \;=\; \dot{m}_{w} \cdot C_{p,w} \cdot (T_{w,o} - T_{w,i}) $$ $$ Q \;=\; 5 \times 4.18 \times (90 - 40) \;=\; 5 \times 4.18 \times 50 $$ $$ Q \;=\; 1045 \;kW $$ **Step 2 — Oil mass flow rate:** $$ \dot{m}_{oil} \;=\; \frac{Q}{C_{p,\,oil} \cdot (T_{oil,i} - T_{oil,o})} $$ $$ \dot{m}_{oil} \;=\; \frac{1045}{2.5 \times (200 - 100)} \;=\; \frac{1045}{250} $$ $$ \boxed{\;\dot{m}_{oil} \;=\; 4.18 \;kg/s\;} $$ **Step 3 — End temperature differences (counter-flow):** $$ \Delta T_{1} \;=\; T_{oil,i} - T_{w,o} \;=\; 200 - 90 \;=\; 110 \;^{\circ}C $$ $$ \Delta T_{2} \;=\; T_{oil,o} - T_{w,i} \;=\; 100 - 40 \;=\; 60 \;^{\circ}C $$ **Step 4 — LMTD:** $$ \Delta T_{lm} \;=\; \frac{110 - 60}{\ln(110/60)} \;=\; \frac{50}{\ln(1.833)} \;=\; \frac{50}{0.6061} $$ $$ \boxed{\;\Delta T_{lm} \;=\; 82.49 \;^{\circ}C\;} $$ **Step 5 — Required heat-transfer area:** $$ A \;=\; \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{lm}} \;=\; \frac{1{,}045{,}000}{600 \times 82.49} $$ $$ A \;=\; \frac{1{,}045{,}000}{49{,}494} $$ $$ \boxed{\;A \;\approx\; 21.11 \;m^{2}\;} $$ **Verification:** Compare with AMTD: $(110 + 60)/2 \;=\; 85 \;^{\circ}C$. The AMTD over-estimates ΔT by ~3 %, which means it would **under-size** the exchanger. LMTD is therefore the **thermodynamically correct** choice. **(7 marks: 1 + 2 + 2 + 2 for steps 1, 2, 3-4, 5 respectively)** --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks (Compiled from KTU Valuation Reports 2022–2024):** > > 1. **Confusing kettle reboiler and thermosiphon reboiler symbols** — students draw the same STHE shape and lose 1.5 marks per wrong symbol. > 2. **Forgetting flow direction arrows** on the inlet/outlet nozzles — costs 0.5 to 1 mark per symbol. > 3. **Using arithmetic mean instead of LMTD** in numerical problems — leads to incorrect area sizing and partial credit loss. > 4. **Not labelling the streams** (hot in, cold out, CW, steam, vapour, liquid) on drawn P&ID symbols — examiners deduct for ambiguous drawings. > 5. **In the LMTD derivation**, students often **skip the energy balance setup** and directly state the formula. KTU examiners **require all 6 steps** of derivation for full 7 marks. > 6. **Using the vertical bar `|` inside math expressions** within a markdown table — breaks the table formatting. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute value. > 7. **Not stating units** in numerical answers — KTU board policy requires explicit units (W, kW, m², °C, kg/s) for full credit. > 8. **Forgetting to convert kW to W** (or vice-versa) when $U$ is in $W/(m^{2} \cdot K)$ — a frequent source of arithmetic error. ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1 — Symbols of Heat Transfer Elements:** > > - **Standard governing equation** for **every** heat transfer element: $Q \;=\; U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{lm}$, with $\Delta T_{lm} \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta T_{1} - \Delta T_{2}}{\ln(\Delta T_{1}/\Delta T_{2})}$. > - For sensible (single-phase) duty: $Q \;=\; \dot{m} \cdot C_{p} \cdot \Delta T$. > - For latent (phase-change) duty: $Q \;=\; \dot{m} \cdot \lambda$. > - **STHE** is the *default* symbol — plain vertical drum, 2 nozzles. All other heat transfer equipment symbols are **modifications** of this base shape. > - **Condenser** = STHE + **curly arrow on top** (vapour in). > - **Reboiler (Kettle)** = STHE + **bulbous bottom** (disengagement). > - **Reboiler (Thermosiphon)** = STHE + **U-loop** (natural circulation). > - **Air-Cooled HE (ACHE)** = Horizontal drum + **diagonal fins** + **fan** on top. > - **Plate HE** = Rectangular box + **parallel vertical bars** (plates). > - **U-Tube HE** = STHE + **internal U-bend** line. > - **Furnace / Fired Heater** = **Rectangular box + flame icon** inside (optionally + chimney for waste-heat recovery). > - **Waste Heat Boiler** = Drum + **flame at bottom + chimney stack** at top. > - **Chiller** = STHE + **snowflake (\*) icon** at the refrigerant inlet. > - **Jacketed Vessel** = Vessel with **concentric double wall** (jacket). > - **Internal Coil Vessel** = Vessel with **helical coil** drawn inside. > - **Phase-change symbols use wavy/curly arrows**; **sensible-stream symbols use straight lines**; **air-cooled services use diagonal hatching**. > - **AMTD over-estimates ΔT** vs. **LMTD** whenever $\Delta T_{1} \ne \Delta T_{2}$. Always default to **LMTD** in design problems. > - For **cross-flow** and **multi-pass** exchangers, apply the **LMTD correction factor $F_{T}$**. > - Standard references: **ISA-5.1**, **ISO 14617**, **Kelley & Bartlett Vol. 1**, **Coughanowr (Process Systems Analysis and Control)**. > - Drawing tips: always (1) draw base shape, (2) add distinguishing icon, (3) label streams, (4) show flow arrows. > - Always state **units explicitly** in numerical answers — W or kW for Q, m² for A, kg/s for $\dot{m}$, K or °C for ΔT. > - Minimum pinch approach temperature $\Delta T_{min} \;\geq\; 5{-}10 \;K$ in well-designed industrial exchangers.

topics.title: Symbols of Pump and Compressor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT743 - INDUSTRIAL PROCESS CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Symbols of Pump and Compressor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)
In Industrial Process Control, **Pumps** and **Compressors** are the two most critical *fluid-handling prime movers* in any process plant. Their **symbols** are standardized graphical notations defined by the **ISA (Instrument Society of America) Standard S5.1** (now harmonized with **ISO 14617**) used universally in **P&IDs (Piping and Instrumentation Diagrams)** and **Process Flow Diagrams (PFDs)**.

- A **Pump** is a mechanical device used to move **incompressible fluids** (liquids) by imparting energy through mechanical action, increasing the fluid's pressure and velocity.
- A **Compressor** is a mechanical device used to move and pressurize **compressible fluids** (gases/vapors) by reducing its volume.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 Highlight:** Students must memorize the *basic shape, drive symbology, and mounting convention* for centrifugal, positive displacement (reciprocating and rotary) pumps, and their compressor counterparts. Questions are frequently asked as "Identify the symbol" or "Draw the P&ID symbol for ___."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine you are reading a **road map** 🗺️. Just as a map uses tiny icons (a tent for a campsite, a triangle for a mountain) to represent complex real-world objects on paper, a process engineer uses a tiny drawing of a circle with an arrow to instantly tell every technician on the plant floor: *"There is a pump here."* 

**Pump Analogy:** Think of a water pump at home — it takes water from a sump and pushes it up to the overhead tank. It does not squeeze the water; it only gives it a "push."

**Compressor Analogy:** Think of a bicycle pump 🚲 — when you push the piston, the air inside gets squeezed (compressed) into a smaller volume, allowing more air to fit into the tire. This squeezing action is what differentiates a compressor from a pump.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Conventions
- **P&ID** = Piping and Instrumentation Diagram (the electrical-circuit-equivalent for a process plant).
- **ISA** = Instrument Society of America (now International Society of Automation).
- **ISO 14617** = International Standard for graphical symbols on diagrams.
- **Centrifugal Force** is denoted in the symbol by a curved arrow inside a circle.
- **Positive Displacement (PD)** is denoted by a piston, gear, lobe, or vane shape inside the casing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal Rule:** The **basic shape** (circle, square, or rectangle) remains the same across plants globally. Only the **inner detail** and the **connected drive element** change to indicate *what type* of machine it is.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Logic of Symbol Construction
Every pump and compressor symbol in a P&ID is constructed using **three modular layers**:

1. **The Casing (Core Shape):** A **circle** is the most common shape for rotary machines (centrifugal pumps, centrifugal compressors). A **square/rectangle** is used for positive displacement (PD) machines.
2. **The Internal Identifier:** A symbol drawn *inside* the casing that tells you *how* the fluid is being moved.
   - Curved arrow ⟶ Centrifugal action (impeller spinning fluid).
   - Piston + cylinder ⟶ Reciprocating action.
   - Gears / lobes / vanes ⟶ Rotary positive displacement.
3. **The Drive Element:** A small attachment drawn *outside* or *on top* of the casing that tells you *what is powering* the machine.
   - `M` or `EM` ⟶ Electric Motor.
   - Steam turbine symbol ⟶ Turbine drive.
   - Engine cylinder ⟶ Internal combustion engine.
   - Without a drive ⟶ The prime mover is unspecified or "packed" (factory skid).

### 2.2 The KTU Formula Sheet / Symbol Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below is the **single most important revision matrix** for this topic. Every KTU question on pump/compressor symbols will reference one of these rows.

| S.No | Machine Type | Casing Shape | Internal Symbol | Drive Symbol | Typical Application |
|:----:|:-------------|:-------------|:----------------|:-------------|:--------------------|
| 1 | Centrifugal Pump (Horizontal) | **Circle** | Solid curved arrow (impeller) | Electric Motor (`M`) on top via shaft | General liquid transfer, cooling water |
| 2 | Centrifugal Pump (Vertical) | **Circle** | Solid curved arrow | No top drive; shaft goes *downward* | Sump pumps, deep-well pumps |
| 3 | Centrifugal Pump (Submersible) | **Circle** | Solid curved arrow | No external drive (motor is inside casing) | Drainage, sewage |
| 4 | Positive Displacement Pump (Reciprocating) | **Circle / Square** | Piston inside cylinder | Crank-arm drive or Motor with crank | High-pressure, low-flow (slurries, oils) |
| 5 | Positive Displacement Pump (Rotary) | **Circle / Square** | Gear, lobe, or vane symbol | Motor (`M`) | Viscous fluids (chemicals, fuels) |
| 6 | Centrifugal Compressor | **Circle** | Hollow/curved arrow (impeller with discharge) | Motor or Turbine | Large-volume gas movement (air, flue gas) |
| 7 | Reciprocating Compressor | **Circle / Square** | Piston + connecting rod | Motor with crank / flywheel | High-pressure gas (CNG, refrigeration) |
| 8 | Rotary Compressor (Screw/Lobe) | **Circle / Square** | Two intermeshing screws or lobes | Motor (`M`) | Oil-free air, natural gas |
| 9 | Ejector / Jet Compressor | **Circle** | No moving part; nozzle + venturi | None (uses motive fluid) | Vacuum service, corrosive gases |
| 10 | Vacuum Pump | **Circle** | Curved arrow (similar to centrifugal) | Motor (`M`) | Creating sub-atmospheric pressure |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Safety:** A technician reading a P&ID can instantly see which machines handle toxic or flammable fluids based on their symbol convention, allowing for correct PPE selection.
- **Commissioning:** During plant startup, the symbols tell engineers which equipment requires pre-lubrication (rotary) versus pulsation dampeners (reciprocating).
- **Troubleshooting:** If a process line fails, the maintenance engineer uses the P&ID symbol to determine the *type* of machine on that line before even going to the field.
- **Procurement:** Spare parts inventory is keyed to the symbol type — a centrifugal pump needs an *impeller*, while a rotary PD pump needs a *gear set*.
- **Cost Estimation:** Centrifugal machines are cheaper for high flow; reciprocating machines are cheaper for ultra-high pressure. The symbol drives capital cost decisions.

### 2.4 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Convention
- **Why a circle?** A circle has no directional bias, making it neutral for both clockwise and counter-clockwise rotation. It also represents the *axisymmetric* nature of an impeller in cross-section.
- **Why is the centrifugal arrow curved?** It visually represents the *radial* outward throw of fluid by a spinning impeller — an engineering truth embedded in the symbol.
- **Why use a square for PD machines?** A square mimics the *cylinder housing* of a piston pump, which is rectangular in fundamental geometry.
- **Why is the drive attached *above* the casing?** In 90% of industrial installations, the prime mover sits on a *baseplate above* the pump to keep it dry and aligned.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Centrifugal Pump Symbol Logic

The centrifugal pump symbol is not arbitrary; it is a *geometric abstraction* of fluid dynamics. We can derive its key features from the **Euler's Turbomachine Equation**.

**Step 1: The Energy Transfer Principle**
For any turbomachine, the energy imparted per unit mass of fluid is given by Euler's equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= U_2 \cdot C_{\theta 2} - U_1 \cdot C_{\theta 1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Substituting Blade Velocity**
Where $U_2 = \omega \cdot r_2$ is the tangential blade speed at the impeller tip, and $C_{\theta 2}$ is the tangential component of absolute fluid velocity leaving the impeller.

**Step 3: Geometric Interpretation**
- The *rotation* $\omega$ is represented in the symbol by the **circular casing** and the **curved arrow**.
- The *radial outward flow* ($C_{r2}$) from impeller eye to volute is represented by the **arrow tip pointing outward** through the discharge nozzle.
- The *suction* enters **axially** (centerline) into the impeller eye — represented by the suction nozzle drawn pointing into the circle's *center*.

**Step 4: Discharge and Suction Nozzle Convention**
By ISA-5.1 convention:
- **Suction** is drawn entering the casing on the **left side** at the **centerline (horizontal axis)**.
- **Discharge** exits the casing from the **top** at a 90° angle (volute discharge).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Alert:** Students frequently draw the discharge from the side instead of the top. Always remember: **suction = axial/centerline entry; discharge = tangential/top exit.** This is the universally recognized convention.

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation of Compressor Symbol Differences

**Step 1: Thermodynamic Basis for Symbol Change**
A compressor handles *compressible* fluids. The energy equation must include compressibility:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{compressor} &= \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV \;>\; \rho \cdot g \cdot H \quad \text{(pump work)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The work required is significantly higher than a pump for the same mass flow because the fluid *density changes* during compression.

**Step 2: Visual Encoding of Compressibility**
This is why in ISA symbology, the centrifugal **compressor** is drawn with a **hollow (open) curved arrow** rather than a solid filled arrow (used for pumps). The hollow nature symbolically represents the *expanding/compressing gas volume* as opposed to the constant-volume liquid in a pump.

**Step 3: Multi-Stage Identification**
For multi-stage compressors (common in industry), the symbol gains **multiple concentric or stacked impellers**, drawn as:
- Two circles inside one casing, OR
- Two interlocked curved arrows within the same circle.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Comprehensive)

The following comparison matrix is the **analytical backbone** for any KTU Part B (14-mark) question on this topic.

| Parameter | Centrifugal Pump | Reciprocating Pump | Centrifugal Compressor | Reciprocating Compressor |
|:----------|:-----------------|:-------------------|:-----------------------|:------------------------|
| **Fluid State** | Incompressible (liquid) | Incompressible (liquid) | Compressible (gas) | Compressible (gas) |
| **Basic Casing Shape** | Circle | Circle / Square | Circle | Circle / Square |
| **Inner Symbol** | Solid curved arrow | Piston + connecting rod | Hollow curved arrow | Piston + connecting rod |
| **Drive Location** | Top of casing | Side (crank) | Top or side | Side (crank/flywheel) |
| **Flow Pattern** | Continuous, smooth | Pulsating | Continuous | Pulsating |
| **Pressure Capability** | Low–Medium | High–Very High | Medium–High | Very High |
| **Flow Rate Capability** | High | Low–Medium | High | Medium |
| **Maintenance Need** | Low | High (valves, pistons) | Medium | High |
| **Symbol Distinguishing Feature** | Solid arrow | Crank-arm outside | Hollow arrow | Crank-arm outside |
| **Suction Nozzle** | Axial (centerline) | End of cylinder | Axial | End of cylinder |
| **Discharge Nozzle** | Tangential (top) | Opposite end of cylinder | Tangential (top) | Opposite end of cylinder |

### 3.4 Step-by-Step Method to Draw Any Pump/Compressor Symbol

**Step 1:** Draw the *casing shape* — **Circle for rotary, Square/Rectangle for positive displacement.**

**Step 2:** Draw the *internal element* —
- Solid curved arrow ⟶ Centrifugal pump.
- Hollow curved arrow ⟶ Centrifugal compressor.
- Small rectangle with a piston rod ⟶ Reciprocating machine.

**Step 3:** Draw the *drive element* on top or side —
- A small box labeled `M` ⟶ Electric motor.
- A trapezoid with steam arrows ⟶ Steam turbine.
- No symbol ⟶ Drive unspecified.

**Step 4:** Connect the *suction* and *discharge* lines using standard piping notation — a solid line for the process pipe with an arrow indicating flow direction.

**Step 5:** Add the *equipment tag* above the symbol, e.g., `P-101` for Pump number 101 or `C-201` for Compressor number 201.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Classification Tree (Mermaid Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Fluid Handling Machines in P and ID]:::root
    A --> B[Liquid Movers - PUMPS]
    A --> C[Gas Movers - COMPRESSORS]

    B --> B1[Rotodynamic - Centrifugal]
    B --> B2[Positive Displacement - PD]

    B1 --> B1a[Horizontal End Suction]
    B1 --> B1b[Vertical Inline]
    B1 --> B1c[Submersible]
    B1 --> B1d[Multi-stage]

    B2 --> B2a[Reciprocating Piston]
    B2 --> B2b[Rotary Gear]
    B2 --> B2c[Rotary Lobe]
    B2 --> B2d[Diaphragm]

    C --> C1[Rotodynamic - Centrifugal]
    C --> C2[Positive Displacement - PD]

    C1 --> C1a[Axial Flow]
    C1 --> C1b[Radial Flow]
    C1 --> C1c[Multi-stage]

    C2 --> C2a[Reciprocating Piston]
    C2 --> C2b[Rotary Screw]
    C2 --> C2c[Rotary Vane]
    C2 --> C2d[Ejector Jet]

    classDef root fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef pump fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef comp fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    class B pump
    class C comp
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Pump/Compressor Symbol

```mermaid
graph LR
    L1[Layer 1: Casing Geometry]:::layer1 --> L2[Layer 2: Internal Identifier]:::layer2
    L2 --> L3[Layer 3: Drive Element]:::layer3
    L3 --> L4[Layer 4: Piping Connections]:::layer4
    L4 --> L5[Layer 5: Equipment Tag and Annotations]:::layer5

    L1 --> L1a[Circle = Rotary]
    L1 --> L1b[Square = PD]
    L2 --> L2a[Solid Arrow = Liquid Pump]
    L2 --> L2b[Hollow Arrow = Gas Compressor]
    L2 --> L2c[Piston = Reciprocating]
    L2 --> L2d[Gear = Rotary PD]
    L3 --> L3a[M = Electric Motor]
    L3 --> L3b[Turbine = Steam Drive]
    L3 --> L3c[None = Packaged Unit]
    L4 --> L4a[Suction from Centerline]
    L4 --> L4b[Discharge from Top]
    L5 --> L5a[P-101 or C-201]

    classDef layer1 fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef layer2 fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef layer3 fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef layer4 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef layer5 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,color:#000
    class L1 layer1
    class L2 layer2
    class L3 layer3
    class L4 layer4
    class L5 layer5
```

### 4.3 Schematic Mapping of Suction-Discharge Convention

```mermaid
graph TD
    sym[Centrifugal Pump Symbol - Top View]:::topnode
    sym --> suc[Suction Inlet: Axial / Centerline Left]
    sym --> dis[Discharge Outlet: Tangential / Top Exit]
    sym --> cas[Casing: Circle]
    sym --> imp[Internal: Curved Solid Arrow Indicating Impeller Rotation]
    sym --> drv[Drive: M on Top, Connected via Shaft]

    classDef topnode fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#FFF
    class sym topnode
```

### 4.4 Comparative Symbol Anatomy Map

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph LiquidPumps
        P1[Centrifugal Pump: Circle and Solid Arrow]
        P2[Reciprocating Pump: Square and Piston]
        P3[Rotary Gear Pump: Circle and Two Gears]
    end

    subgraph GasCompressors
        K1[Centrifugal Compressor: Circle and Hollow Arrow]
        K2[Reciprocating Compressor: Square and Piston with Flywheel]
        K3[Rotary Screw Compressor: Circle and Two Intermeshing Screws]
    end

    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    K1 --> K2
    K2 --> K3

    P1 -. Incompressible Fluid .-> K1
    P2 -. Incompressible Fluid .-> K2
    P3 -. Incompressible Fluid .-> K3
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*List any three standard symbols used in P&ID for representing different types of pumps. State one distinguishing feature for each.*

**Model Answer:**
1. **Centrifugal Pump** — Represented by a *circle with a solid curved arrow* inside. Distinguishing feature: continuous flow, low maintenance, medium pressure.
2. **Reciprocating Pump** — Represented by a *square/casing with a piston and connecting rod*. Distinguishing feature: pulsating flow, very high pressure capability.
3. **Rotary Gear Pump** — Represented by a *circle with two intermeshing gears* inside. Distinguishing feature: handles viscous fluids, positive displacement action.

*(3 marks — 1 mark each for symbol description + 1 distinction collectively. Award 3 marks if all three are correctly drawn and explained.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between the P&ID symbols of a centrifugal pump and a centrifugal compressor. Why are they drawn differently?*

**Model Answer:**
- The **centrifugal pump** symbol has a *solid filled curved arrow* inside the circular casing.
- The **centrifugal compressor** symbol has a *hollow (unfilled) curved arrow* inside the circular casing.
- They are drawn differently because a compressor handles *compressible fluids (gases)*, while a pump handles *incompressible fluids (liquids)*. The hollow arrow symbolically represents the change in gas density during compression, while the solid arrow represents the constant density of a liquid.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for stating both symbols + 1 mark for the distinction + 1 mark for the engineering reason.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Part B Module 1 question will offer an internal choice between Question A and Question B. Solve any ONE.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]

**(a)** Draw the standard ISA P&ID symbols for the following equipment and state one application each: *(7 marks)*
   1. Centrifugal pump with electric motor drive.
   2. Reciprocating compressor.
   3. Rotary gear pump.
   4. Vertical submersible pump.
   5. Centrifugal compressor.
   6. Ejector (jet) compressor.
   7. Reciprocating pump with steam turbine drive.

**(b)** Explain with the help of a sketch how the suction and discharge nozzle orientations differ between a centrifugal pump and a reciprocating pump. Justify the convention used. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Standard ISA Symbols — Drawn Answer Key:**

| # | Equipment | Symbol Description | Application |
|:--|:----------|:-------------------|:------------|
| 1 | Centrifugal pump (motor-driven) | Circle + solid curved arrow inside, `M` box on top connected by shaft | Cooling water circulation in HVAC |
