| 2 | Reciprocating compressor | Circle/square with piston, connecting rod, flywheel outside | High-pressure CNG compression |
| 3 | Rotary gear pump | Circle with two intermeshing gears inside | Lubricating oil transfer |
| 4 | Submersible pump | Circle + curved arrow; *no* external drive (motor is sealed inside) | Sewage and drainage sumps |
| 5 | Centrifugal compressor | Circle + *hollow* curved arrow inside | Air separation unit (ASU) |
| 6 | Ejector compressor | Circle with internal *nozzle + venturi* and a motive-fluid inlet (no moving part) | Creating vacuum in distillation columns |
| 7 | Reciprocating pump (turbine drive) | Circle/square with piston, steam turbine symbol on top | Boiler feedwater in legacy power plants |

*(Award 1 mark per correctly drawn + labeled symbol with valid application.)*

**(b) Suction-Discharge Orientation Analysis:**

**Step 1:** In a **centrifugal pump**, the fluid enters the impeller *axially along the centerline* of the shaft and is thrown *radially outward* into the volute casing, finally exiting *tangentially from the top* of the casing.

**Step 2:** In a **reciprocating pump**, the fluid is drawn into a *closed cylinder* through an inlet check valve (during the suction stroke) and pushed out through a *discharge check valve* (during the delivery stroke). Both suction and discharge are aligned with the *cylinder axis* on opposite ends.

**Step 3:** Convention Justification:
- The centrifugal symbol's *axial suction / tangential top discharge* mirrors the actual Euler-flow physics (Euler's turbomachine equation: $W = U_2 C_{\theta 2} - U_1 C_{\theta 1}$).
- The reciprocating symbol's *end-to-end* piping reflects the linear reciprocating motion of the piston in a cylinder, making it geometrically unambiguous.

**Step 4:** A clean sketch should show:
- Centrifugal: Suction arrow → entering circle from the **left at centerline**; Discharge arrow → exiting from the **top right**.
- Reciprocating: Suction arrow → entering cylinder from **one end**; Discharge arrow → exiting from the **opposite end**.

*(Award 2 marks for identifying the difference, 3 marks for the sketch, 2 marks for justification referencing Euler's equation or fluid dynamics.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks):** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]

**(a)** Classify pumps and compressors based on their operating principle. For each class, draw the standard P&ID symbol and state its primary distinguishing internal feature. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** With the help of a labeled diagram, explain the functional architecture of how a P&ID symbol for any pump is constructed. List the five modular layers. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Classification of Pumps and Compressors:**

**Step 1: Pumps** are classified into:
- **Rotodynamic (Centrifugal)** — Symbol: Circle with *solid curved arrow*. Internal feature: spinning impeller imparting kinetic energy.
- **Positive Displacement** — Symbol: Circle/square with *piston, gears, lobes, or vanes*. Internal feature: trapped volume of fluid is mechanically displaced.
   - *Reciprocating subtype:* Piston in cylinder.
   - *Rotary subtype:* Gears/lobes/vanes rotating in mesh.

**Step 2: Compressors** are classified into:
- **Centrifugal (Dynamic)** — Symbol: Circle with *hollow curved arrow*. Internal feature: high-speed impeller with diffuser.
- **Positive Displacement** — Symbol: Circle/square with *piston or intermeshing screws*. Internal feature: mechanical trapping and compression.
   - *Reciprocating subtype:* Piston in cylinder with valves.
   - *Rotary subtype:* Screws, lobes, or vanes.

**Step 3: Application Distinction**
- Rotodynamic → high flow, low–medium pressure.
- PD → low–medium flow, very high pressure.

*(Award 2 marks for classification table, 3 marks for correct symbols, 2 marks for internal feature identification.)*

**(b) Five Modular Layers of a Pump P&ID Symbol:**

The functional architecture of any pump symbol consists of five stacked layers:

1. **Layer 1 — Casing Geometry:** A *circle* (rotodynamic) or *square* (PD) defines the boundary.
2. **Layer 2 — Internal Identifier:** The element *inside* the casing (solid arrow, hollow arrow, piston, gears) tells the operating principle.
3. **Layer 3 — Drive Element:** An external block (`M`, turbine, or none) tells the prime mover.
4. **Layer 4 — Piping Connections:** Solid process lines with directional arrows for suction (inlet) and discharge (outlet).
5. **Layer 5 — Equipment Tag & Annotations:** An alphanumeric label (e.g., `P-101A`) above the symbol identifies the equipment uniquely.

**Sketch Description (verbal):**
- Bottom layer: A circle drawn.
- Inside circle: A solid curved arrow showing impeller rotation.
- On top of circle: A square labeled `M` connected by a vertical shaft line.
- Left side: A horizontal line with arrow pointing right INTO the circle (suction).
- Top right: A vertical line with arrow pointing UP and OUT of the circle (discharge).
- Above the symbol: The text `P-101A`.

*(Award 1 mark per layer identification, 2 marks for the labeled sketch, 1 mark for the equipment tag convention.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls — Read Carefully:**
> 1. **Drawing a SOLID arrow for a compressor.** This is the #1 error. Compressors MUST have a HOLLOW arrow. Liquid pump = solid arrow; Gas compressor = hollow arrow. Loss: 1–2 marks per occurrence.
> 2. **Forgetting the drive element.** A symbol without `M` or a turbine is incomplete. Loss: 1 mark.
> 3. **Wrong suction/discharge placement.** Suction is ALWAYS centerline (horizontal); discharge is ALWAYS tangential (top) for centrifugal machines. Reversing this loses 2 marks.
> 4. **Conflating rotary PD and reciprocating PD.** Gears/lobes = rotary PD; Piston with rod = reciprocating PD. Drawing a square with gears inside but labeling it "reciprocating" will lose 1 mark.
> 5. **Missing equipment tag.** KTU examiners *expect* an alphanumeric tag like `P-101` drawn above the symbol. Missing it can cost 0.5–1 mark in detailed valuation.
> 6. **Confusing submersible with horizontal centrifugal.** Submersible has NO external drive — the motor is encapsulated *inside* the casing. Drawing an external `M` on a submersible loses 1 mark.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 5-Minute Revision Block — Memorize Before Exam:**

- ☐ The **basic pump symbol is a CIRCLE with a SOLID curved arrow** inside.
- ☐ The **basic compressor symbol is a CIRCLE with a HOLLOW curved arrow** inside.
- ☐ **Solid arrow = liquid (incompressible)**; **Hollow arrow = gas (compressible)**.
- ☐ **Suction** always enters at the **centerline (axial)** for centrifugal machines.
- ☐ **Discharge** always exits from the **top (tangential)** for centrifugal machines.
- ☐ A **square casing** indicates a **positive displacement (PD)** machine (piston, gear, lobe, vane).
- ☐ An **electric motor drive** is denoted by a small box labeled **`M`** on top of the casing.
- ☐ A **steam turbine drive** is denoted by a trapezoid with steam arrows.
- ☐ A **submersible pump** has NO external drive — motor is sealed inside the casing.
- ☐ **Reciprocating machines** (both pump and compressor) are drawn with a **piston + connecting rod** and a crank or flywheel on the outside.
- ☐ **Rotary PD pumps** (gear, lobe, screw) are drawn with **two intermeshing shapes** (gears/lobes/screws) inside a circle or square.
- ☐ An **ejector/jet compressor** has NO moving parts — symbol shows a **nozzle + venturi** with a motive-fluid inlet.
- ☐ Always attach the **equipment tag** (e.g., `P-101`, `C-201`) above the symbol.
- ☐ Standards followed: **ISA-5.1** (USA) and **ISO 14617** (International) — both are equivalent and accepted in KTU exams.
- ☐ Centrifugal = high flow / continuous; PD = high pressure / pulsating.
- ☐ The **Euler Turbomachine Equation** $W = U_2 C_{\theta 2} - U_1 C_{\theta 1}$ is the theoretical basis for the curved-arrow symbol in centrifugal machines.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Symbols of Process Pressure Vessels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT743 - INDUSTRIAL PROCESS CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Symbols of Process Pressure Vessels

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> A **Process Pressure Vessel** is a closed container designed to hold gases or liquids at a pressure substantially different (higher or lower) from the ambient atmospheric pressure, used as process equipment in chemical, petrochemical, oil & gas, and power industries. The **Symbols** of process pressure vessels are standardized graphical representations (governed primarily by **ISA-5.1**, **ISO 14617**, and **P&ID standards**) used to depict the vessel type, internals, and connected instrumentation on engineering flow diagrams without ambiguity.

In Instrumentation and Control (I&C) engineering, the "symbol" of a pressure vessel is **not just a picture of the tank** — it is a **rich, coded graphical language** that conveys:
- Vessel geometry (vertical, horizontal, spherical, etc.)
- Type of ends (flat, dished, hemispherical, conical)
- Internal trays, baffles, packing, or agitators
- All process connections (nozzles) and instrument taps

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The Symbol as a Blueprint Language":**
> Imagine you are drawing a map of a kitchen for a friend who has never seen it. You don't draw a photorealistic fridge — you draw a **rectangle labeled "REF"** with a small circle for the handle. The friend instantly understands. P&ID (Piping & Instrumentation Diagram) symbols work the same way. A vertical vessel is drawn as two vertical lines forming a tall rectangle with a dome on top — but the **attached bubbles, circles, and letters** tell the engineer *exactly* what is being measured (Pressure, Level, Temperature, Flow) and *how* it is being controlled.

A symbol is therefore a **two-layer communication tool**:
- **Layer 1 (Shape):** Tells you *what equipment it is*.
- **Layer 2 (Attached tags & bubbles):** Tells you *what instruments are connected to it*.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Standard Bodies Governing Symbols:**
> - **ISA-5.1** (Instrumentation Symbols and Identification) — Primary standard used in KTU syllabus.
> - **ISO 14617** — International equivalent.
> - **ASME Section VIII** — Governs actual *physical design* of the vessel (pressure rating, wall thickness).
> - **Design Pressure:** Typically **1.05 × MAWP** (Maximum Allowable Working Pressure).
> - **Standard Design Code:** **ASME BPVC Section VIII Div. 1**.

## 1.4 Classification of Process Pressure Vessels

| Vessel Type | Geometry | Common Application |
|---|---|---|
| Vertical Vessel (Column/Tower) | Tall, vertical cylinder | Distillation columns, reactors |
| Horizontal Vessel | Cylinder lying on side | Separators, accumulators, receivers |
| Spherical Vessel | Sphere shape | High-pressure gas storage (LPG, ammonia) |
| Storage Tank | Large flat-bottom cylinder | Bulk liquid storage at atmospheric pressure |
| Reactor Vessel | Cylindrical with agitator | Chemical reactions |
| Heat Exchanger (Shell & Tube) | Cylindrical shell | Heat transfer between fluids |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always remember that the **shape of the symbol itself is the first identifier**. Examiners often show a P&ID snippet and ask you to identify the vessel type from the symbol alone.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The ISA Standardized Symbol Architecture

The **ISA-5.1** standard breaks down any pressure vessel symbol into **three distinct visual layers**. Understanding these layers is critical for KTU exams and for reading real-world P&IDs.

### Layer 1: The Vessel Body Geometry (Shape Code)

| Shape | Symbol Description | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Tall Vertical Rectangle with domed top | `╭││╮` | Vertical column / tower / reactor |
| Horizontal Rectangle with rounded ends | `◯═══◯` | Horizontal separator / accumulator |
| Circle | `○` | Spherical pressure vessel |
| Trapezoid or flat-bottom tank | `▭_` | Atmospheric storage tank |
| Bullet-shaped (tall + hemispherical top) | `⌒` | Bullet tank (LPG bullet) |

### Layer 2: The Instrument Bubble (Tag Identification)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ISA Tag Number Format (Instrument Bubble):**
> Every instrument on a P&ID is enclosed in a **circle (bubble)** with a standardized alphanumeric tag. The format is:
>
> `ISA Tag = [Functional Identification] + [Loop Number]`

**First Letter (Measured Variable):**
- `P` = Pressure
- `T` = Temperature
- `F` = Flow
- `L` = Level
- `A` = Analyzer

**Subsequent Letters (Function & Modifier):**
- `I` = Indicator
- `C` = Controller
- `T` = Transmitter
- `V` = Valve
- `S` = Switch
- `R` = Recorder
- `CV` = Control Valve
- `PCV` = Pressure Control Valve

**Example Tag Decomposition:**
A tag like **`LIC-101`** means:
- `L` → **Level** is the measured variable
- `I` → **Indicator** is the function
- `C` → **Controller** is also a function
- `101` → Loop number 101

### Layer 3: Connection Lines (Signal vs. Process Lines)

> [!NOTE]
> **Two fundamental line types on P&IDs:**
> 1. **Solid heavy line (━━━):** Process piping — carries the actual fluid.
> 2. **Dashed light line (- - -):** Signal/pneumatic/electrical connection — carries measurement or control signal.
> 3. **Dotted line (·····):** Capillary or impulse line (used for pressure taps).
> 4. **Line with slash marks:** Hydraulic signal line.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|
| ISA Tag Length | First letter = measured variable | Always uppercase |
| Successive Letters Limit | Up to 4 letters after first | Suffix notation for modifiers |
| Design Pressure Rule | $P_{design} = 1.05 \times P_{MAWP}$ | Bar / PSI |
| Hydrostatic Test Pressure | $P_{test} = 1.5 \times MAWP$ | ASME Sec VIII requirement |
| Wall Thickness (cylinder) | $t = \dfrac{P \cdot R}{S \cdot E - 0.6P}$ | $P$ = internal pressure, $R$ = radius, $S$ = allowable stress, $E$ = joint efficiency |
| Signal Line Convention | Dashed = signal, Solid = process | ISA-5.1 standard |
| Instrument Air Pressure | **20 psig (1.4 bar)** | Pneumatic standard |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Safety & Hazard Analysis:** Standardized symbols allow HAZOP (Hazard and Operability) teams to identify risks across global plants uniformly.
- **Cross-Plant Communication:** A P&ID drawn in India using ISA-5.1 can be read by an engineer in Saudi Arabia without ambiguity.
- **Digital Twin & DCS Integration:** Modern Distributed Control Systems (DCS) like Honeywell Experion or Emerson DeltaV auto-generate their graphics database directly from the ISA tag on the P&ID.
- **Commissioning & Troubleshooting:** Field technicians use the bubble tags to physically locate and test instruments on the plant floor.

## 2.4 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 1 of PEICT743) explicitly lists *"Symbols of process equipment"* as a foundational topic. Marks are commonly awarded in **Part A (3-mark questions)** for identifying symbols and explaining their parts, and in **Part B (14-mark questions)** for full P&ID interpretation problems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Exercise: Decoding a Complex P&ID Vessel Symbol

**Problem Setup:**
A horizontal pressure vessel appears on a P&ID with the following attachments:
1. A solid line entering from the left labeled **FT-101**.
2. A dashed line exiting from a circular bubble tagged **PIC-102** going to a valve symbol.
3. A bubble at the top right tagged **LIC-103**.
4. A bubble at the bottom tagged **TI-104**.

**Step-by-Step Decoding (Exhaustive):**

**Step 1 — Identify the Vessel:**
The shape is a horizontal capsule (cylinder with two hemispherical ends). By ISA convention, this is a **horizontal separator / horizontal pressure vessel**.

**Step 2 — Decode FT-101:**
- First letter `F` → Measured variable = **Flow**.
- Second letter `T` → Function = **Transmitter**.
- Number `101` → Loop number.
- Solid line connection → It is installed in the **process piping**, measuring the incoming fluid flow.
- **Conclusion:** Flow Transmitter on the inlet feed line, loop 101.

**Step 3 — Decode PIC-102:**
- First letter `P` → Measured variable = **Pressure**.
- Second letter `I` → Function = **Indicator**.
- Third letter `C` → Function = **Controller**.
- Number `102` → Loop number.
- The dashed line going to a valve symbol indicates it is sending a **pneumatic/electrical signal to a control valve**.
- **Conclusion:** Pressure Indicating Controller, loop 102, controlling a downstream PCV (Pressure Control Valve).

**Step 4 — Decode LIC-103:**
- `L` → **Level**.
- `I` → **Indicator**.
- `C` → **Controller**.
- Positioned at the top right → Likely measuring **liquid level** inside the vessel.
- **Conclusion:** Level Indicating Controller, loop 103.

**Step 5 — Decode TI-104:**
- `T` → **Temperature**.
- `I` → **Indicator**.
- Positioned at the bottom → Could be a **thermo-well inserted into the vessel sump**.
- **Conclusion:** Temperature Indicator, loop 104.

## 3.2 Complete ISA Tag Identification Matrix

The table below maps **every standard ISA letter** to its meaning. Memorize this — it appears in nearly every KTU exam paper.

| Letter | Measured Variable (1st letter) | Function (Succeeding letter) |
|---|---|---|
| A | Analyzer | Alarm |
| B | Burner Flame | — |
| C | Conductivity | Controller |
| D | Density / Density | — |
| E | Voltage | Element (Primary Sensor) |
| F | Flow | — |
| G | — | Glass / Gauge |
| H | Hand (Manual) | High |
| I | Current | Indicator |
| J | Power | — |
| K | Time | — |
| L | Level | Light / Low |
| M | Moisture | — |
| N | — | — |
| O | — | Orifice / Open |
| P | Pressure | — |
| Q | Quantity | — |
| R | — | Recorder / Run |
| S | Speed / Frequency | Switch |
| T | Temperature | Transmitter |
| U | Multivariable | Multifunction |
| V | Vibration | Valve |
| W | Weight / Force | Well |
| X | Unclassified | Unclassified |
| Y | Event / State | Relay / Compute |
| Z | Position | Drive |

## 3.3 Standard Pressure Vessel Connection Symbols

| Symbol | Component |
|---|---|
| ▭ or ☐ | Pressure Safety Valve (PSV) |
| ◇ | Rupture Disc |
| ⊕ | Flame Arrester |
| ⊗ | Sight Glass / Sight Flow Indicator |
| ⌇ | Strainer / Filter |
| ▷ | Check Valve (one-way flow) |
| ⊕⊕ | Two-way valve |
| ◯ with cross | Relief Valve |
| ⊟ | Drain Valve |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Derivative Logic:**
> Notice how the **same first letter (P)** can map to a Pressure Transmitter (**PT**), Pressure Indicator (**PI**), Pressure Controller (**PC**), or Pressure Control Valve (**PCV**). The *position* of the letter in the tag is the key — **first letter = what is measured, subsequent letters = how it is processed**.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example: Tag Recognition Test

**Problem:** A bubble on a P&ID is labeled **FT-205**. Identify all four parameters.

$$
\text{Loop Number} = 205
$$

$$
\text{First Letter (F)} = \text{Flow}
$$

$$
\text{Second Letter (T)} = \text{Transmitter}
$$

$$
\text{Functional Identity} = \text{It is a flow transmitter on loop 205}
$$

**Result:** This instrument measures the flow rate of a process fluid and converts it into a standardized signal (e.g., **4-20 mA**) for the DCS.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: ISA Symbol Architecture for a Pressure Vessel

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["PRESSURE VESSEL SYMBOL"] --> nodeB["LAYER 1: VESSEL GEOMETRY"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["LAYER 2: INSTRUMENT BUBBLES"]
    nodeA --> nodeD["LAYER 3: CONNECTION LINES"]

    nodeB --> nodeE["Vertical Column Shape"]
    nodeB --> nodeF["Horizontal Capsule Shape"]
    nodeB --> nodeG["Spherical Shape"]
    nodeB --> nodeH["Flat-Bottom Tank"]

    nodeC --> nodeI["First Letter: Measured Variable"]
    nodeC --> nodeJ["Subsequent Letters: Function"]
    nodeC --> nodeK["Loop Number Identifier"]

    nodeI --> nodeI1["P = Pressure"]
    nodeI --> nodeI2["T = Temperature"]
    nodeI --> nodeI3["F = Flow"]
    nodeI --> nodeI4["L = Level"]

    nodeJ --> nodeJ1["T = Transmitter"]
    nodeJ --> nodeJ2["I = Indicator"]
    nodeJ --> nodeJ3["C = Controller"]
    nodeJ --> nodeJ4["V = Valve"]

    nodeD --> nodeL["Solid Line: Process Pipe"]
    nodeD --> nodeM["Dashed Line: Signal"]
    nodeD --> nodeN["Dotted Line: Capillary"]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture Flow: Signal Path in a Level Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    vesselNode["HORIZONTAL PRESSURE VESSEL"] -->|Process Tap, Solid Line| ltNode["LT Level Transmitter"]
    ltNode -->|Dashed Signal Line 4-20 mA| dcsNode["DCS Controller LIC"]
    dcsNode -->|Dashed Signal Line| lcvNode["LCV Level Control Valve"]
    lcvNode -->|Process Return, Solid Line| vesselNode
```

**Reading the diagram above (KTU Board Style):**
- The **solid line** shows the **process fluid** physically moving.
- The **dashed line** shows the **electrical/pneumatic signal** flow.
- The **LT** (Level Transmitter) senses the level and sends a signal to the **LIC** (Level Indicating Controller) inside the DCS.
- The DCS computes the error and sends a corrective signal to the **LCV** (Level Control Valve), which adjusts the outlet flow to maintain the level at setpoint.

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Instrument Tag Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode["New Instrument on P&ID"] --> q1{"What is being measured?"}
    q1 -->|Pressure| pNode["First Letter = P"]
    q1 -->|Temperature| tNode["First Letter = T"]
    q1 -->|Flow| fNode["First Letter = F"]
    q1 -->|Level| lNode["First Letter = L"]

    pNode --> pQ{"Function?"}
    pQ -->|Transmitter| ptNode["PT-XXX"]
    pQ -->|Controller| pcNode["PC-XXX"]
    pQ -->|Indicator| piNode["PI-XXX"]
    pQ -->|Control Valve| pcvNode["PCV-XXX"]

    tNode --> tQ{"Function?"}
    tQ -->|Transmitter| ttNode["TT-XXX"]
    tQ -->|Indicator| tiNode["TI-XXX"]

    fNode --> fQ{"Function?"}
    fQ -->|Transmitter| ftNode["FT-XXX"]
    fQ -->|Controller| fcNode["FC-XXX"]

    lNode --> lQ{"Function?"}
    lQ -->|Transmitter| ltNode["LT-XXX"]
    lQ -->|Controller + Indicator| licNode["LIC-XXX"]
```

## 4.4 Diagram Description: A Typical Vertical Pressure Vessel on a P&ID

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this in your mind:**
> Draw a tall vertical rectangle (the column). On top, attach a small **circle with a tag "PT-101"** (Pressure Transmitter at the top). On the side, attach a **square labeled "PCV-102"** (Pressure Control Valve). Inside the column, draw **two horizontal lines** (representing trays or packing). At the bottom, draw a **drain valve symbol ▷**. This single drawing conveys the column geometry, internal structure, and all instrumentation in one glance.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define an ISA instrument tag and identify all parts of the tag `FT-201`.
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**

**Model Answer:**
An **ISA instrument tag** is a standardized alphanumeric identifier assigned to every instrument on a P&&ID per **ISA-5.1** standard.

**Decoding FT-201:**
- `F` (First letter) → Measured variable = **Flow**
- `T` (Second letter) → Function = **Transmitter**
- `201` → Loop number

**Conclusion:** It is a **Flow Transmitter on loop number 201** that senses the flow rate of a process fluid and converts it to a standardized output (typically **4-20 mA DC**).

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for tag decoding)*

### Question 2: Differentiate between a process line and a signal line on a P&ID.
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model]**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Process Line | Signal Line |
|---|---|---|
| Line type | **Solid heavy line (━━━)** | **Dashed light line (- - -)** |
| Function | Carries the actual process fluid | Carries electrical/pneumatic measurement/control signal |
| Energy type | Hydraulic (fluid energy) | Electrical (4-20 mA), Pneumatic (3-15 psi), or Digital |
| Connection | Between equipment (vessel → pump → tank) | Between instrument bubble → DCS → control valve |

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for differentiation table)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE (End Semester Examination) regulations, Part B questions carry **14 marks** each with internal choice. Below are **two full 14-mark question choices** for the topic *Symbols of Process Pressure Vessels*.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):** Full P&ID Interpretation of a Pressure Vessel System
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2]**

**Question Statement:**
> "Explain in detail the standard ISA symbols used to represent process pressure vessels on a P&ID. With a neat sketch, describe the symbols of (a) a horizontal separator vessel and (b) a vertical distillation column. For each, identify at least 4 instrument tags with their complete functional meaning."

#### Part (a) Horizontal Separator Vessel (7 Marks)

**Model Answer (Step-by-Step):**

**Step 1 — Vessel Shape:**
The horizontal separator is drawn as a **horizontal capsule shape** — a rectangle with two semicircular (hemispherical) ends. This represents a horizontal pressure vessel with domed ends.

**Step 2 — Process Connections:**
- A solid line enters from the **left top** → Inlet feed from upstream equipment.
- A solid line exits from the **right top** → Vapor outlet to overhead line.
- A solid line exits from the **right bottom** → Liquid outlet to downstream pump.

**Step 3 — Instrument Tags (ISA-5.1 Standard):**

**Tag 1: PI-101** (Pressure Indicator, Loop 101)
- `P` = Pressure, `I` = Indicator.
- Mounted on top of the vessel, connected via a **dotted impulse line** to the vapor space.
- Function: Local visual indication of operating pressure.

**Tag 2: LIC-102** (Level Indicating Controller, Loop 102)
- `L` = Level, `I` = Indicator, `C` = Controller.
- Connected via **differential pressure (dP) cell** or displacer to measure liquid level.
- Sends signal to **LCV-103** control valve on the liquid outlet line.

**Tag 3: FT-104** (Flow Transmitter, Loop 104)
- `F` = Flow, `T` = Transmitter.
- Installed on the inlet feed line with an **orifice plate** (shown as ⊕ symbol).
- Sends 4-20 mA signal to flow controller in DCS.

**Tag 4: TI-105** (Temperature Indicator, Loop 105)
- `T` = Temperature, `I` = Indicator.
- Thermowell inserted into the vessel, displays local temperature.

**Tag 5: PSV-106** (Pressure Safety Valve, Loop 106)
- Mechanical relief device on top — opens automatically at set pressure to protect the vessel from overpressure.

*(7 marks: 2 marks for sketch + 5 marks for tag explanation)*

#### Part (b) Vertical Distillation Column (7 Marks)

**Model Answer (Step-by-Step):**

**Step 1 — Vessel Shape:**
The vertical distillation column is drawn as a **tall vertical rectangle** with a **domed (hemispherical) top head** and a **flat or dished bottom head**.

**Step 2 — Internal Indications:**
- Multiple **horizontal lines** drawn inside the column represent **trays** (sieve, valve, or bubble-cap trays).
- A **packed section** is shown using a **zigzag or cross-hatched pattern**.

**Step 3 — Instrument Tags:**

**Tag 1: PT-201** (Pressure Transmitter)
- Senses pressure at the **top of the column (reflux drum)**.
- Sends signal to DCS.

**Tag 2: TT-202** (Temperature Transmitter)
- Multiple temperature transmitters along the column (TT-202, TT-203, TT-204) form a **temperature profile** indicating tray operation.

**Tag 3: FCV-205** (Flow Control Valve)
- Controls the **reflux flow rate** back to the column, critical for separation efficiency.

**Tag 4: LT-206** (Level Transmitter)
- Measures **condensate level in the reflux drum** at the top.

**Tag 5: LIC-207** (Level Indicating Controller)
- Controls the **reboiler liquid level** at the bottom of the column by manipulating bottom product flow.

**Tag 6: PCV-208** (Pressure Control Valve)
- Maintains column pressure by controlling overhead vapor flow to the condenser.

*(7 marks: 2 marks for sketch + 5 marks for tag explanation)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks):** ISA-5.1 Standards and Symbol Standardization
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model / Equivalent]**

**Question Statement:**
> "Explain the ISA-5.1 standard for instrumentation symbols. With neat sketches, describe the symbols for: (a) a control valve and (b) a pressure safety valve. How are P&ID symbols used for communication between process, instrumentation, and control disciplines?"

#### Part (a) ISA-5.1 Standard Overview (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

The **ISA-5.1 standard** (Instrumentation Symbols and Identification) was developed by the Instrumentation Society of America (now International Society of Automation). It provides:

1. **Symbol Library:** Standard graphical symbols for every type of process equipment and instrument.
2. **Identification System:** The alphanumeric tag format (e.g., `FT-101`).
3. **Line Conventions:** Standardized line types for process, signal, and capillary connections.
4. **Functional Identification:** Each letter in the tag conveys a specific meaning.

**Key Rules of ISA-5.1:**
- The **first letter** denotes the *measured variable*.
- **Subsequent letters** denote the *function* (Indicator, Transmitter, Controller, etc.).
- **Suffix letters** denote *modifiers* (High, Low, etc.).
- A **bubble (circle)** is drawn around the tag to indicate an *instrument mounted on the main panel* or in the field.
- A **circle with a horizontal line through it** indicates a *field-mounted instrument*.
- A **square around the tag** indicates a *DCS-mounted or computer display* instrument.

#### Part (b) Control Valve and PSV Symbols (7 Marks)

**Control Valve Symbol:**
A control valve is shown as a **bow-tie shape** (two triangles meeting at a point) with the letter designation inside (e.g., `PCV-101` for Pressure Control Valve). A **dashed line** entering the valve symbol indicates a **pneumatic/electrical actuator signal**.

**Pressure Safety Valve (PSV) Symbol:**
The PSV is shown as a **square with a spring-loaded arrow pointing upward**, labeled with set pressure. It is connected to the vessel via a **solid process line**.

**Inter-Disciplinary Communication:**
P&&ID symbols enable **process engineers**, **instrumentation engineers**, and **control engineers** to interpret the same drawing identically. A process engineer sees a vertical column with trays; an instrumentation engineer sees the FI, TI, LI tags and knows which transmitters to procure; a control engineer reads the LIC, FIC tags and programs the DCS control loops. This **single source of truth** eliminates ambiguity and reduces plant commissioning errors.

*(7 marks: 3 marks for ISA rules + 2 marks for sketches + 2 marks for communication aspect)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Frequently Penalized in KTU Valuation):**
> 1. **Swapping first letter and second letter meaning:** Students often write "FT means Flow Transmitter" — but examiners expect you to clearly state *"F = Flow (measured variable), T = Transmitter (function)"*. Always mention **both** meanings explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing process line and signal line:** Drawing a solid line for a signal connection will cost you **2-3 marks** in sketch-based questions.
> 3. **Forgetting to label the loop number:** A tag without a number is incomplete — always include `XXX` (e.g., 101, 102).
> 4. **Not sketching internal details:** For columns, forgetting to draw **trays or packing** inside the vessel loses marks.
> 5. **Mixing up PSV and PCV:** A Pressure Safety Valve (PSV) is a **safety device** (opens on overpressure); a Pressure Control Valve (PCV) is a **continuous control device**. Examiners mark these as distinct.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- The **ISA-5.1 standard** governs all instrumentation symbols in KTU syllabus.
- An **instrument tag** follows the format: **First Letter (Measured Variable) + Subsequent Letters (Function) + Loop Number**.
- Common first letters: **P, T, F, L, A** (Pressure, Temperature, Flow, Level, Analyzer).
- Common functions: **I** (Indicator), **T** (Transmitter), **C** (Controller), **V** (Valve), **S** (Switch), **R** (Recorder).
- **Solid line = process fluid; Dashed line = signal.**
- Vessel shapes: **Vertical rectangle = column, Horizontal capsule = separator, Circle = sphere.**
- Always include **trays/packing** for columns and **PSV** for all pressurized vessels.
- The **PSV is non-negotiable** on any pressure vessel P&ID — it is a safety code requirement.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Rapid-Revision Checklist:**
> - [ ] Can I decode any 3-letter ISA tag instantly?
> - [ ] Can I draw a vertical column and a horizontal separator correctly?
> - [ ] Do I know the 4 standard line conventions?
> - [ ] Can I list at least 6 instrument tags for a separator?
> - [ ] Do I know the difference between PSV, PCV, and PRV?

**End of Notes — Symbols of Process Pressure Vessels**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flow Sheet Symbol
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT743 - INDUSTRIAL PROCESS CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Flow Sheet Symbol ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition A **Flow Sheet Symbol** (also termed a **Process Flow Diagram / P&ID Symbol**) is a standardized graphical notation defined primarily by the **ANSI/ISA-S5.1** standard (Instrumentation Society of America) used by process and control engineers to represent process equipment, piping, valves, instruments, and signal interconnections on a two-dimensional engineering drawing. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Flow sheet symbols form the universal *visual language* of industrial process control. Mastery of ISA symbology is mandatory for interpreting Piping & Instrumentation Diagrams (P&IDs), Distributed Control System (DCS) graphics, and plant layout documents. Formally, the standard classifies every process symbol under three categories: 1. **Equipment Symbols** — Static hardware (towers, pumps, exchangers). 2. **Piping & Valve Symbols** — Lines, fittings, and fluid-handling devices. 3. **Instrument Symbols** — Circles/bubbles with letter designations denoting measured variables and controller functions. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition Imagine you are traveling to a new country without knowing its language. You rely on **universal pictograms** (airport symbols, road signs) — a suitcase means baggage claim, an airplane means gate. **Flow sheet symbols are the "airport signage" of a chemical/refinery plant.** A senior plant operator in Kerala can look at a P&ID drawn in Texas and immediately recognize that a circle with the letters **"TIC"** means a **Temperature Indicating Controller**, regardless of the plant's geographical origin. This is the *power of standardization*. > [!NOTE] > **Fun Fact:** Before the ISA standard was widely adopted (pre-1960s), each company had proprietary symbols, leading to severe miscommunication — similar to the chaos before the metric system. ### 1.3 Standard Reference Bodies | Standard | Governing Body | Scope | |----------|---------------|-------| | ANSI/ISA-S5.1 | International Society of Automation | Instrumentation symbols & identification | | ANSI/ISA-S5.3 | ISA | Flow diagram symbols for shared display/hardware | | ISO 14617 | International Organization for Standardization | General process & control symbols | | PIP PIC001 | Process Industry Practices | P&ID drafting conventions | The letter codes used in instrument bubbles are governed by the **ISA Tag Identifier Convention**, where: - **First Letter** → Measured/Initiating Variable (e.g., T = Temperature, F = Flow, L = Level, P = Pressure). - **Succeeding Letters** → Function (e.g., I = Indicate, C = Control, R = Record, T = Transmit, A = Alarm). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Bold Standard:** The identifying tag format follows the pattern **XXX-YYYY** where XXX is the loop number and YYYY is the instrument function code. Example: **TIC-101** = Loop 101, Temperature Indicating Controller. ### 1.4 Why Flow Sheet Symbols Matter in Industry - **Safety:** Operators can identify emergency isolation valves instantly. - **Hazard Analysis (HAZOP):** Standard symbols enable systematic review of every node. - **DCS Implementation:** The same P&ID is translated into Human-Machine Interface (HMI) graphics. - **Commissioning & Troubleshooting:** Maintenance crews trace signal paths using symbology. --- ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The ISA Letter Designation System (Mnemonic Breakdown) The ISA tag identification logic is built on **two functional axes** — *what is measured* and *what is done with that measurement*. **First-Letter Set — Measured Variables (Indicators):** | Letter | Variable | Letter | Variable | |--------|----------|--------|----------| | A | Analysis | N | Speed (RPM) | | B | Burner/Combustion | O | Not Used (Reserved) | | C | Conductivity | P | Pressure / Vacuum | | D | Density / Specific Gravity | Q | Quantity / Totalize | | E | Voltage (EMF) | R | Radiation | | F | Flow Rate | S | Speed / Frequency | | G | Gauging (Dimensional) | T | Temperature | | H | Hand (Manual) | U | Multivariable | | I | Current (Electrical) | V | Viscosity | | J | Power | W | Weight / Force | | K | Time / Time Schedule | X | Unclassified | | L | Level | Y | Event / State | | M | Moisture / Humidity | Z | Position / Dimension | **Succeeding-Letter Set — Functions (Modifiers):** | Letter | Function | Letter | Function | |--------|----------|--------|----------| | A | Alarm | N | Not Used | | B | Blank (Burst) | O | Not Used | | C | Control | P | Point (Test) | | D | Differential | Q | Integrate / Totalize | | E | Element (Primary) | R | Record / Print | | F | Fraction / Ratio | S | Switch / Contact | | G | Glass / Viewport | T | Transmit | | H | High | U | Multifunction | | I | Indicate | V | Valve / Damper | | J | Scan | W | Well / Probe | | K | Time Rate | X | Unclassified | | L | Low / Light | Y | Relay / Compute | | M | Middle / Momentary | Z | Drive / Actuator | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rule of Thumb for Decoding Tags:** Always read **right-to-left** in the modifier block. For example, **PDI** = Pressure **D**ifferential **I**ndicator. ### 2.2 Instrument Bubble Shapes — Geometric Encoding | Bubble Shape | Location / Meaning | Typical Tag | |--------------|-------------------|-------------| | Circle (plain) | Field-mounted discrete instrument | PT, TE | | Circle with horizontal line through it | Primary field element (sensor) | TE, FE | | Circle inside a square | DCS / Shared display (computer) | TIC (in DCS) | | Circle inside a hexagon | DCS / PLC control station | TIC (in PLC) | | Square (no circle) | PLC / Computer function block | — | | Diamond | Function block / Logic element | — | ### 2.3 Line / Signal Symbol Library | Symbol | Meaning | |--------|---------| | ——— | Process pipe (flowing fluid) | | — — — | Electrical signal / 4–20 mA loop | | - - - - - | Pneumatic signal (3–15 psi) | | ＝ ＝ ＝ | Hydraulic signal | | －－－－－ | Capillary tube / filled thermal system | | ~~~~~ | Electromagnetic / sonic / radio | ### 2.4 Major Valve Symbol Categories | Valve Type | Symbol Logic | Application | |-----------|--------------|-------------| | **Gate Valve** | Two triangles meeting at point | Full-bore isolation | | **Globe Valve** | Circle with horizontal bar | Throttling | | **Ball Valve** | Sphere in path | Quick isolation | | **Butterfly Valve** | Disc on vertical stem | Large line isolation | | **Check Valve** | Single-direction flap | Backflow prevention | | **Control Valve** | Globe with actuator on top | Modulated flow control | | **Three-way Valve** | Y-pattern or T-pattern | Mixing/Diverting | | **Safety Relief Valve** | Spring over disk | Overpressure protection | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Actuator Identification in Symbols:** > - **Circle on stem** = Pneumatic diaphragm actuator > - **Rectangular box on stem** = Motor actuator > - **Spring (zigzag)** = Spring-return (fail-safe) > - **Solenoid (S)** = Electrical solenoid actuator ### 2.5 Equipment Symbol Standard Library (Core Process Units) | Equipment | Standard Symbol Geometry | Function | |-----------|--------------------------|----------| | **Centrifugal Pump** | Circle with impeller mark | Fluid transport (kinetic) | | **Positive Displacement Pump** | Circle with reciprocating arrow | Precise-volume fluid transfer | | **Compressor** | Circle with rotating lobes | Gas pressurization | | **Heat Exchanger (Shell & Tube)** | Two circles with internal dots | Heat transfer between streams | | **Distillation Column** | Tall vertical rectangle with trays | Separation by volatility | | **Reactor (CSTR)** | Vertical cylinder with agitator | Chemical reaction | | **Storage Tank** | Horizontal cylinder with dished ends | Bulk fluid storage | | **Condenser** | Heat exchanger with vapor inlet | Vapor → Liquid phase change | | **Reboiler** | Heat exchanger at column base | Vapor generation | | **Fired Heater / Furnace** | Box with flame icon | High-temp heat input | ### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Concept — Signal-to-Pressure Conversion Equation:** When a control loop signal travels pneumatically (3–15 psi) from a controller to a control valve, it obeys a linear mapping. This is a **high-yield formula** for solving numerical problems. $$ P_{out} \;=\; P_{min} \;+\; \left(P_{max} - P_{min}\right) \cdot \frac{m - m_{min}}{m_{max} - m_{min}} $$ where the standard pneumatic ranges are: $$ P_{min} = 3 \text{ psig}, \quad P_{max} = 15 \text{ psig} $$ and for the standard electronic analog signal: $$ m_{min} = 4 \text{ mA}, \quad m_{max} = 20 \text{ mA} $$ **Percentage-of-range conversion (used in many KTU problems):** $$ \% \text{ Range} \;=\; \frac{m - m_{min}}{m_{max} - m_{min}} \times 100 $$ **Control valve characteristic — Equal Percentage Trim:** $$ Q / Q_{max} \;=\; R^{\left(\frac{L}{L_{max}} - 1\right)} $$ where: - $R$ = Rangeability (typical value $R = 50$) - $L$ = Valve lift position - $Q$ = Resulting flow rate **Linear characteristic trim:** $$ Q / Q_{max} \;=\; L / L_{max} $$ ### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Refinery DCS commissioning:** Engineers migrate the P&ID directly into HMI screens. - **Plant safety audits:** HAZOP teams use symbology to identify nodes (study points). - **Maintenance diagnostics:** A pump P&ID tag (e.g., P-101A/B) determines the spare-part inventory. - **Cross-disciplinary communication:** Mechanical, civil, electrical, and instrumentation teams read the same drawing. --- ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example: Decoding a P&ID Tag **Problem Statement:** A flow sheet shows a circle containing the letters **FT-102** connected by a dashed line to a circle containing **FIC-102**, which is in turn connected to a globe valve symbol with a pneumatic actuator at the top, tagged **FV-102**. **(a)** Decode the ISA tag for each instrument. **(b)** Determine the pneumatic signal pressure that would be sent to the valve when the measured flow is at **60 % of full scale**. --- ### Part (a) — Tag Decoding (Step-by-Step) **Step 1: Decode FT-102** - First letter **F** → measured variable = **Flow Rate** - Second letter **T** → function = **Transmit** (transmitter) - Loop number **102** - **Conclusion:** Flow Transmitter, loop 102. Likely a field-mounted orifice plate + dPT or magnetic flowmeter. **Step 2: Decode FIC-102** - First letter **F** → Flow Rate - Second letter **I** → Indicate - Third letter **C** → Control - Loop number **102** - **Conclusion:** Flow Indicating Controller — typically a DCS block that displays the flow value and outputs a corrective signal. **Step 3: Decode FV-102** - First letter **F** → Flow Rate - Second letter **V** → Valve (final control element) - Loop number **102** - **Conclusion:** Flow Control Valve — the actuator that physically throttles flow. > [!NOTE] > **Control Loop Topology Revealed:** FT-102 (sensor) → FIC-102 (controller) → FV-102 (final element). All three share loop **102**, meaning they form **one complete feedback loop**. --- ### Part (b) — Pneumatic Signal Pressure at 60 % Range **Given:** - Measured flow is at 60 % of full scale. - Pneumatic output signal range: 3 psig to 15 psig. - Controller output is linearly proportional to the controller signal. **Step 1:** Identify the formula for linear signal mapping. $$ P_{out} \;=\; P_{min} \;+\; \left(P_{max} - P_{min}\right) \cdot \frac{m - m_{min}}{m_{max} - m_{min}} $$ **Step 2:** Substitute the known values. $$ P_{min} = 3 \text{ psig}, \quad P_{max} = 15 \text{ psig} $$ The 60 % range is the ratio of the normalized signal excursion. Therefore: $$ \frac{m - m_{min}}{m_{max} - m_{min}} \;=\; 0.60 $$ **Step 3:** Plug into the formula. $$ P_{out} \;=\; 3 \;+\; \left(15 - 3\right) \cdot 0.60 $$ **Step 4:** Compute the inner difference. $$ P_{out} \;=\; 3 \;+\; \left(12\right) \cdot 0.60 $$ **Step 5:** Compute the product. $$ P_{out} \;=\; 3 \;+\; 7.2 $$ **Step 6:** Final addition. $$ P_{out} \;=\; 10.2 \text{ psig} $$ **Result:** The controller sends a pneumatic signal of **10.2 psig** to the control valve FV-102 when the measured flow is at 60 % of the full-scale range. **Step 7 (Sanity check):** The pneumatic mid-range is $(3 + 15)/2 = 9 \text{ psig}$. A 60 % reading should produce a signal slightly above mid-range. Our answer, **10.2 psig**, is consistent — ✓ valid. --- ### 3.2 Worked Example: Equal-Percentage Valve Sizing **Problem:** A control valve has rangeability $R = 50$. If the valve lift is set at $L/L_{max} = 0.75$, compute the fractional flow $Q / Q_{max}$. **Step 1:** Write the equal-percentage characteristic equation. $$ Q / Q_{max} \;=\; R^{\left(\frac{L}{L_{max}} - 1\right)} $$ **Step 2:** Substitute the values. $$ Q / Q_{max} \;=\; 50^{\left(0.75 - 1\right)} \;=\; 50^{-0.25} $$ **Step 3:** Evaluate using logarithms. $$ \log_{10}\left(50^{-0.25}\right) \;=\; -0.25 \cdot \log_{10}(50) $$ $$ \log_{10}(50) \;=\; \log_{10}(5 \times 10) \;=\; \log_{10}(5) + 1 \;\approx\; 0.6990 + 1 \;=\; 1.6990 $$ Therefore: $$ \log_{10}\left(50^{-0.25}\right) \;=\; -0.25 \cdot 1.6990 \;=\; -0.42475 $$ **Step 4:** Convert back from log. $$ Q / Q_{max} \;=\; 10^{-0.42475} \;\approx\; 0.376 $$ **Result:** $Q / Q_{max} \approx 0.376$, meaning the valve passes about **37.6 %** of its maximum flow when the lift is at 75 %. > [!NOTE] > This illustrates why **equal-percentage valves** are favored for high turndown applications — small openings produce very small flows, while at large openings the flow grows rapidly. This is critical for processes with wide operating ranges. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: ISA Symbol Reading Drills | Tag on P&ID | First Letter | Modifier(s) | Loop | Full Tag Meaning | |-------------|--------------|-------------|------|------------------| | TE-205 | T | E (Element) | 205 | Temperature Element (sensor) | | PI-310 | P | I (Indicate) | 310 | Pressure Indicator (gauge) | | TRC-415 | T | R, C (Record, Control) | 415 | Temperature Recording Controller | | LSH-501 | L | S, H (Switch, High) | 501 | Level Switch (high-level trip) | | FQIA-602 | F | Q, I, A (Totalize, Indicate, Alarm) | 602 | Flow Totalizer with Indication & Alarm | | PV-703 | P | V (Valve) | 703 | Pressure Control Valve | | TT-805 | T | T (Transmit) | 805 | Temperature Transmitter | **Extended Drill — Reading combined letters:** - **PDI** → Pressure Differential Indicator - **TIC** → Temperature Indicating Controller - **FRC** → Flow Recording Controller - **LAH** → Level Alarm (High) - **FV** → Flow Valve (control valve on flow loop) - **FY** → Flow Relay/computation block (e.g., square-root extractor) --- ### 3.4 Summary Equation Reference Card $$ \text{Pneumatic output pressure: } \quad P_{out} = 3 + 12 \cdot \left(\frac{m - 4}{16}\right) \text{ psig} $$ $$ \text{Electronic signal to PV: } \quad I = 4 + 16 \cdot \left(\frac{\% \text{ error}}{100}\right) \text{ mA} $$ $$ \text{Equal-percentage valve: } \quad \frac{Q}{Q_{max}} = R^{\left(\frac{L}{L_{max}} - 1\right)} $$ $$ \text{Linear valve: } \quad \frac{Q}{Q_{max}} = \frac{L}{L_{max}} $$ --- ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: ISA P&ID Control Loop The following Mermaid diagram depicts the **ISA-standard feedback control loop** as visualized on a P&ID, showing the relationship between sensors, controllers, and final control elements through standardized signal lines. ```mermaid flowchart LR proc1(("Process Vessel
V-101")) sensor1(("FT-101
Flow Transmitter")) ctrl1{{"FIC-101
Flow Indicating Controller"}} valve1(["FV-101
Pneumatic Control Valve"]) dcs1["DCS HMI Display
Shared Circle Inside Square"] setpoint1["Operator Setpoint
SP"] proc1 -->|"Process Fluid"| sensor1 sensor1 -->|"4-20 mA
Electronic Signal"| ctrl1 setpoint1 -->|"Manual Entry"| ctrl1 ctrl1 -->|"3-15 psig
Pneumatic Signal"| valve1 valve1 -->|"Throttled Flow"| proc1 ctrl1 -->|"Data Bus"| dcs1 ``` ### 4.2 ISA Tag Classification Tree The following Mermaid tree diagram classifies the ISA symbology hierarchy from broad categories down to specific instrument functions. ```mermaid flowchart TD root(("ISA Symbol
Library")) --> A["Equipment Symbols"] root --> B["Piping and Valve Symbols"] root --> C["Instrument Symbols"] root --> D["Signal Line Symbols"] A --> A1(["Pump Symbols"]) A --> A2(["Heat Exchanger Symbols"]) A --> A3(["Vessel Symbols"]) A --> A4(["Compressor Symbols"]) A1 --> A1a(("Centrifugal Pump")) A1 --> A1b(("Positive Displacement Pump")) A2 --> A2a(("Shell and Tube Exchanger")) A2 --> A2b(("Plate Heat Exchanger")) A3 --> A3a(("Distillation Column")) A3 --> A3b(("Storage Tank")) A3 --> A3c(("CSTR Reactor")) B --> B1(["Manual Valves"]) B --> B2(["Control Valves"]) B --> B3(["Safety Valves"]) B1 --> B1a(("Gate Valve")) B1 --> B1b(("Globe Valve")) B1 --> B1c(("Ball Valve")) B1 --> B1d(("Check Valve")) B2 --> B2a(("Air-to-Close Actuator")) B2 --> B2b(("Air-to-Open Actuator")) B2 --> B2c(("Motor Actuator")) C --> C1(["Field Mounted Bubbles"]) C --> C2(["Shared Display Bubbles"]) C1 --> C1a(("Plain Circle")) C1 --> C1b(("Circled Element")) C2 --> C2a(("Circle Inside Square DCS")) C2 --> C2b(("Circle Inside Hexagon PLC")) D --> D1(["Process Pipe Solid Line"]) D --> D2(["Pneumatic 3 to 15 psi"]) D --> D3(["Electronic 4 to 20 mA"]) D --> D4(["Capillary Filled"]) D --> D5(["Hydraulic"]) ``` ### 4.3 P&ID Reading Workflow — Sequential Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD s1(["Step 1: Identify Loop Number"]) --> s2(["Step 2: Identify First Letter Variable"]) s2 --> s3(["Step 3: Decode Succeeding Letters Functions"]) s3 --> s4(["Step 4: Locate Field Element Versus Shared Display"]) s4 --> s5(["Step 5: Trace Signal Path Between Elements"]) s5 --> s6(["Step 6: Identify Final Control Element and Actuator"]) s6 --> s7(["Step 7: Document Control Strategy Documentation"]) ``` ### 4.4 Loop-Wide P&ID Schematic (Pump Flow Control) ```mermaid flowchart LR tank1(["Storage Tank
T-100"]) pump1(["Centrifugal Pump
P-101"]) ft1(("FT-201")) fic1{{"FIC-201"}} fv1(["FCV-201
Globe Valve"]) hmi1["DCS HMI Screen"] tank1 -->|"Suction Line"| pump1 pump1 -->|"Discharge Line"| ft1 ft1 -->|"4-20 mA"| fic1 fic1 -->|"3-15 psig"| fv1 fv1 -->|"Controlled Flow to Process"| process1(["Reactor R-201"]) fic1 -->|"Ethernet Bus"| hmi1 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Block-Diagram Interpretation Convention:** In ISA symbology, a *circle* denotes a physical field instrument, a *circle inside a square* denotes a DCS screen display, and a *circle inside a hexagon* denotes a PLC function. The Mermaid block diagrams above mirror this hierarchy using shape conventions that approximate the same logical relationships. --- ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define a P&ID. With the help of ISA symbology, decode the following instrument tags:** 1. **FT-101** 2. **TICA-205** 3. **LAH-310** **Model Answer:** A **Piping and Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID)** is a detailed schematic drawing that shows the interconnection of process equipment, piping, valves, and instrumentation used in industrial process plants. It conforms to the **ANSI/ISA-S5.1** standard. Tag decoding: 1. **FT-101**: First letter **F** = Flow Rate; second letter **T** = Transmit. Therefore, **FT-101** is a **Flow Transmitter** in loop 101. 2. **TICA-205**: First letter **T** = Temperature; modifiers: **I** = Indicate, **C** = Control, **A** = Alarm. Therefore, **TICA-205** is a **Temperature Indicating Controller with Alarm** in loop 205. 3. **LAH-310**: First letter **L** = Level; modifier **A** = Alarm; modifier **H** = High. Therefore, **LAH-310** is a **Level Alarm High** (high-level trip switch) in loop 310. *(3 marks — 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for tag decoding.)* --- #### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Differentiate between Process Flow Diagram (PFD) and Piping and Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID). State any four ISA standard instrument symbols with their meanings.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | PFD | P&ID | |--------|-----|------| | Detail level | Major equipment & stream flows | Detailed instruments, valves, piping | | Instrumentation | Generally absent | Full ISA-tagged instruments | | Used for | Conceptual / heat & mass balance | Engineering, construction, commissioning | | Pipe sizing | No | Yes (with spec breaks) | Four ISA standard instrument symbols: 1. **Circle** → Field-mounted instrument (e.g., PT, FT, LT). 2. **Circle with horizontal line** → Primary element / sensor (e.g., TE, FE). 3. **Circle inside square** → DCS / computer-shared display. 4. **Hexagon containing circle** → PLC-based controller. *(3 marks — 1 mark for PFD/P&ID distinction + 2 marks for symbol list.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - Students commonly lose **2–3 marks** by **mis-decoding the third and fourth letters** of an instrument tag. Always remember: the *first* letter is the variable, *all subsequent* letters are functions/modifiers. > - Another common pitfall: confusing the **bubble shape** (field vs DCS) — markers lose 1 mark each time. > - In numerical problems (signal conversion), forgetting to use **3–15 psig** for pneumatic and **4–20 mA** for electronic ranges leads to a full-step deduction. > - Always show units (psig, mA, %) in your final answer to earn the *unit-consistency* mark. --- #### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **(a)** Explain in detail the **ISA classification of control valves** with neat sketches. Discuss the symbols for **pneumatic, hydraulic, and motor actuators**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A flow transmitter has a calibrated range of **0–500 m³/h** and outputs a **4–20 mA** signal. The corresponding flow controller has a setpoint of **280 m³/h** and outputs a **3–15 psig** pneumatic signal to the control valve. Determine: 1. The electronic current signal from the transmitter at the setpoint. 2. The pneumatic pressure sent to the valve at the setpoint. 3. The fractional opening of an equal-percentage valve if the rangeability $R = 50$ and the required flow is **70 % of maximum**. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Question A **(a) ISA Classification of Control Valves (7 marks)** Control valves are classified by: **i) Type of body (trim):** - **Single-seat globe valve** — Tight shut-off, suitable for small flows. - **Double-seat globe valve** — Lower pressure drop, larger capacity. - **Angle valve** — For high-pressure drop, slurry service. - **Ball valve** — Quick shut-off, low pressure drop. - **Butterfly valve** — Large diameter, low cost. - **Three-way valve** — Mixing or diverting. **ii) Type of actuator:** | Actuator | Symbol Convention | Application | |----------|-------------------|-------------| | **Pneumatic diaphragm** | Circle or dome on stem | Most common; uses 3–15 psig | | **Pneumatic piston** | Cylindrical actuator | High thrust, larger valves | | **Hydraulic** | Cylindrical + hydraulic line | High-pressure, fast response | | **Electric motor** | Rectangular box on stem | Slow, precise modulation | | **Solenoid** | "S" symbol | On/off service | | **Spring-return** | Zigzag spring above | Fail-safe (air-to-open or air-to-close) | **iii) Action on air failure:** - **Air-to-Open (AO)** — Valve closes on air failure (fail-closed). - **Air-to-Close (AC)** — Valve opens on air failure (fail-open). The valve flow characteristic (linear, equal-percentage, quick-opening) determines the relationship between stem travel and flow rate. --- **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)** **Given:** - Flow range: $Q_{min} = 0 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$, $Q_{max} = 500 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$. - Output: $I_{min} = 4 \text{ mA}$, $I_{max} = 20 \text{ mA}$. - Setpoint: $Q_{sp} = 280 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$. - Pneumatic range: $P_{min} = 3 \text{ psig}$, $P_{max} = 15 \text{ psig}$. - Equal-percentage valve, $R = 50$. **Part (1): Transmitter current at setpoint** Using the linear mapping formula: $$ I_{out} \;=\; I_{min} \;+\; \left(I_{max} - I_{min}\right) \cdot \frac{Q_{sp} - Q_{min}}{Q_{max} - Q_{min}} $$ Substitute values: $$ I_{out} \;=\; 4 \;+\; \left(20 - 4\right) \cdot \frac{280 - 0}{500 - 0} $$ $$ I_{out} \;=\; 4 \;+\; 16 \cdot 0.56 \;=\; 4 \;+\; 8.96 \;=\; 12.96 \text{ mA} $$ **Result:** Transmitter output $\approx \mathbf{12.96 \text{ mA}}$. --- **Part (2): Pneumatic pressure to valve** Assuming the controller output is at 100 % (i.e., proportional to the percentage reading) at the setpoint, the normalized controller signal: $$ \frac{Q_{sp} - Q_{min}}{Q_{max} - Q_{min}} = 0.56 $$ Therefore: $$ P_{out} \;=\; 3 \;+\; \left(15 - 3\right) \cdot 0.56 \;=\; 3 \;+\; 12 \cdot 0.56 \;=\; 3 \;+\; 6.72 \;=\; 9.72 \text{ psig} $$ **Result:** Pneumatic output $\approx \mathbf{9.72 \text{ psig}}$. --- **Part (3): Fractional valve opening** Required fractional flow: $$ Q / Q_{max} = 0.70 $$ Using equal-percentage formula: $$ Q / Q_{max} \;=\; R^{\left(L / L_{max} - 1\right)} $$ $$ 0.70 \;=\; 50^{\left(L / L_{max} - 1\right)} $$ Take $\log_{10}$ of both sides: $$ \log_{10}(0.70) \;=\; \left(\frac{L}{L_{max}} - 1\right) \cdot \log_{10}(50) $$ $$ -0.1549 \;=\; \left(\frac{L}{L_{max}} - 1\right) \cdot 1.6990 $$ Solve for $L / L_{max}$: $$ \frac{L}{L_{max}} - 1 \;=\; \frac{-0.1549}{1.6990} \;=\; -0.09117 $$ $$ \frac{L}{L_{max}} \;=\; 0.9088 $$ **Result:** Valve lift $\approx \mathbf{90.88 \%}$ of maximum stroke to deliver 70 % of maximum flow. --- #### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice: `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, describe the **standard ISA symbology** for the following: **(7 marks)** 1. **Field-mounted pressure transmitter** with a primary element. 2. **A level indicating controller** in the DCS. 3. **A temperature recorder with high alarm**. 4. **An air-to-close pneumatic control valve** with a positioner. 5. **A flow totalizer with integrator and indicator**. 6. **Capillary-filled thermal system** connection. 7. **Three-way mixing valve**. **(b)** A temperature transmitter has a calibrated range of **200 °C – 600 °C** and outputs a **4–20 mA** signal. The process operates at a temperature that produces a current of **12 mA**. Determine: 1. The corresponding process temperature. 2. The percentage of range covered. 3. If this signal feeds a temperature controller with output range **3–15 psig**, compute the corresponding pneumatic output assuming linear action. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to Question B **(a) ISA Symbology Descriptions (7 marks — 1 mark each)** 1. **Field-mounted pressure transmitter with primary element:** A *circle with a horizontal line through it* (the line representing the primary element — e.g., a diaphragm or bourdon tube), labeled **PT-XXX**, drawn on the pipe line. The transmitter tag is **PT-XXX**. 2. **Level indicating controller in DCS:** A *circle inscribed inside a square* (square denoting computer/DCS shared display), labeled **LIC-XXX**. Sometimes drawn as a *circle inside a hexagon* if it resides in a PLC. 3. **Temperature recorder with high alarm:** A *plain circle* (field-mounted) labeled **TRH-XXX** — T (Temperature), R (Record), H (High alarm). Alternatively, a square for shared display. 4. **Air-to-close pneumatic control valve with positioner:** A globe-valve symbol with a *pneumatic diaphragm actuator* (circle on stem) on top, with a *dashed signal line* entering the actuator. The positioner is shown as a small box connected by signal lines. The valve stem moves **down** (closing) on rising air pressure. Tag: **PV-XXX** (or **TV-XXX** depending on the controlled variable). 5. **Flow totalizer with integrator and indicator:** A *square* (computer/DCS) labeled **FQI-XXX** — F (Flow), Q (Integrate/Totalize), I (Indicate). 6. **Capillary-filled thermal system:** A *dash-dot-dot line* connecting the temperature-sensing bulb (e.g., in a thermowell) to the transmitter. Used for remote-mounted filled-system temperature sensors. 7. **Three-way mixing valve:** A *Y-shaped* or *T-shaped* valve body with two inlets and one outlet. The actuator is shown on the stem. Used to blend two process streams into a single mixed stream. --- **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)** **Given:** - Temperature range: $T_{min} = 200 \text{ °C}$, $T_{max} = 600 \text{ °C}$. - Output range: $I_{min} = 4 \text{ mA}$, $I_{max} = 20 \text{ mA}$. - Operating current: $I = 12 \text{ mA}$. - Pneumatic output range: $P_{min} = 3 \text{ psig}$, $P_{max} = 15 \text{ psig}$. **Part (1): Process temperature** Use the inverse mapping formula: $$ T \;=\; T_{min} \;+\; \left(T_{max} - T_{min}\right) \cdot \frac{I - I_{min}}{I_{max} - I_{min}} $$ Substitute: $$ T \;=\; 200 \;+\; \left(600 - 200\right) \cdot \frac{12 - 4}{20 - 4} $$ $$ T \;=\; 200 \;+\; 400 \cdot \frac{8}{16} \;=\; 200 \;+\; 400 \cdot 0.5 \;=\; 200 \;+\; 200 \;=\; 400 \text{ °C} $$ **Result:** Process temperature $= \mathbf{400 \text{ °C}}$. --- **Part (2): Percentage of range** $$ \% \text{ Range} \;=\; \frac{I - I_{min}}{I_{max} - I_{min}} \times 100 \;=\; \frac{12 - 4}{20 - 4} \times 100 \;=\; 50 \text{ \%} $$ **Result:** The signal is at **50 %** of the calibrated range. --- **Part (3): Pneumatic output** Assuming the controller output mirrors the input linearly at 50 %: $$ P_{out} \;=\; 3 \;+\; \left(15 - 3\right) \cdot 0.50 \;=\; 3 \;+\; 12 \cdot 0.5 \;=\; 3 \;+\; 6 \;=\; 9 \text{ psig} $$ **Result:** Pneumatic output $= \mathbf{9 \text{ psig}}$. (Sanity check: This is exactly the mid-range pneumatic value, which is consistent with a 50 % signal — ✓.) --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Quick-Reference Summary — Revise Before the Exam:** - **Flow sheet symbols** follow the **ANSI/ISA-S5.1** standard. - **First letter** of a tag = **measured variable** (T = Temperature, F = Flow, L = Level, P = Pressure, A = Analysis). - **Succeeding letters** = **functions** (I = Indicate, C = Control, R = Record, T = Transmit, V = Valve, A = Alarm, H = High, L = Low, S = Switch). - **Circle** = field-mounted instrument; **Circle with line** = primary element; **Circle in square** = DCS; **Circle in hexagon** = PLC. - **Process pipe** = solid line; **Electronic signal (4–20 mA)** = dashed line; **Pneumatic (3–15 psig)** = dash-dot with crosshatch; **Capillary** = dash-dot-dot. - **Pneumatic signal range:** 3–15 psig. **Electronic analog:** 4–20 mA. - **Equal-percentage valve equation:** $Q/Q_{max} = R^{(L/L_{max} - 1)}$ where $R = 50$ typically. - **Linear valve equation:** $Q/Q_{max} = L/L_{max}$. - **Air-to-Open (AO):** Valve opens on air failure; **Air-to-Close (AC):** Valve closes on air failure. - **Air-to-Open** is the **fail-closed** configuration; **Air-to-Close** is the **fail-open** configuration. - **Control loop structure:** Sensor (e.g., FT) → Controller (FIC) → Final Element (FV) → Process → back to sensor. - A **P&ID** includes detailed instrumentation; a **PFD** does not. - **HAZOP** studies rely entirely on standardized symbology for node identification. - Always **show units** in numerical answers to score the unit-consistency marks in KTU valuation. - **Decoding rule:** "First letter = variable, all others = functions." The third and fourth letters are commonly misread. ---

topics.title: Temperature Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT743 - INDUSTRIAL PROCESS CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Temperature Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🌡️ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Temperature Control** in industrial process control refers to the systematic regulation of thermal energy within a process by manipulating a heating or cooling medium through a closed-loop feedback mechanism, ensuring the process temperature tracks a desired setpoint despite external disturbances, load changes, and inherent process non-linearities.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEICT743)** framework, temperature is treated as a **slow, integrating-like process variable** with significant **thermal lag (dead time)** and **distributed parameter characteristics**. Unlike flow or pressure, temperature cannot change instantaneously because energy storage in the process mass (thermal capacitance) introduces inherent delay.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Module 2 specifically focuses on modeling thermal systems, deriving transfer functions for heat exchangers, furnaces, and stirred-tank heaters, and tuning controllers for these inherently slow processes.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are taking a **hot shower** in winter 🛁.

- You set the **handle to "Warm"** → this is your **setpoint (SP)**.
- Your skin feels whether water is **too hot or too cold** → this is the **temperature sensor (RTD/Thermocouple)**.
- Your brain compares skin sensation with memory of "warm" → this is the **controller (PID)**.
- You turn the hot/cold valve slightly → this is the **final control element (heater/cooler)**.

Now, the catch: between turning the valve and water temperature changing at your body, there is a **noticeable delay** — the water must travel through pipes. This delay is the **transportation lag / dead time (L)**.

> **Temperature control is essentially the science of managing this delay and the stored heat (thermal mass) to keep things stable.**

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold Highlights)

| Parameter | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|
| Universal Gas Constant | **R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)** |
| Stefan-Boltzmann Constant | **σ = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/(m²·K⁴)** |
| Absolute Zero | **0 K = −273.15 °C** |
| Standard Reference (Ice Point) | **273.15 K = 0 °C** |
| Thermal Conductivity (typical metals) | **k = 10–400 W/(m·K)** |
| Typical Process Time Constants | **τ = 30 s to 60 min** |

> [!NOTE]
> Temperature control loops are typically **10 to 100 times slower** than flow or pressure loops because of the large thermal mass involved. This is critical when selecting controller modes and tuning parameters.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📚 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Modes of Heat Transfer (Process Energy Flow)

Thermal processes involve energy movement that must be mathematically described:

- **Conduction** 🔥: Heat transfer through solid material by molecular vibration and free-electron drift. Governed by **Fourier's Law**.
- **Convection** 💨: Heat transfer between a solid surface and a moving fluid (liquid/gas). Governed by **Newton's Law of Cooling**.
- **Radiation** ☀️: Heat transfer by electromagnetic waves; does not require a medium. Governed by **Stefan-Boltzmann Law**.

---

## 2.2 Thermal Resistance & Thermal Capacitance (Lumped Parameter Approach)

For mathematical tractability, the KTU syllabus uses **lumped parameter modeling**, treating distributed thermal systems as networks of **thermal resistances (R)** and **thermal capacitances (C)**.

### 2.2.1 Thermal Resistance (R)

Represents the **opposition to heat flow**, analogous to electrical resistance:

$$R = \frac{\Delta T}{Q}$$

Where:
- $\Delta T$ = Temperature difference (°C or K)
- $Q$ = Heat flow rate (W or J/s)
- Units: **°C/W or K/W**

### 2.2.2 Thermal Capacitance (C)

Represents the **ability to store thermal energy**, analogous to electrical capacitance:

$$C = m \cdot c_p$$

Where:
- $m$ = Mass of the material (kg)
- $c_p$ = Specific heat capacity (J/kg·K)
- Units: **J/K**

### 2.2.3 Heat Transfer Coefficients Used in Formulas

| Mechanism | Thermal Resistance Formula |
|---|---|
| Conduction (plane wall) | $R = \dfrac{L}{kA}$ |
| Convection | $R = \dfrac{1}{hA}$ |
| Radiation | $R = \dfrac{1}{h_r A}$, where $h_r = \sigma(T_1+T_2)(T_1^2+T_2^2)$ |

---

## 2.3 First-Order Thermal System (Stirred Tank Heater — No Dead Time)

A perfectly stirred tank with uniform temperature, surrounded by a heating coil:

$$C \frac{dT}{dt} = Q_{in} - Q_{out} + Q_{heater}$$

After linearization, the standard first-order transfer function is:

$$\frac{T(s)}{Q(s)} = \frac{R}{\tau s + 1}$$

Where:
- $\tau = RC$ = **Thermal time constant (s)**
- $R$ = **Thermal resistance**
- $C$ = **Thermal capacitance**

---

## 2.4 Second-Order Thermal System (With Sensor + Tank Dynamics)

When a temperature sensor (e.g., thermowell) is added in series with the tank, two RC elements appear. This produces:

$$\frac{T(s)}{Q(s)} = \frac{R}{(\tau_1 s + 1)(\tau_2 s + 1)}$$

This is typical of **heat exchangers** with measurement delays.

---

## 2.5 Thermal System With Transportation Lag (Dead Time)

If the measurement point is physically distant from the heating zone (e.g., fluid flowing through long pipes), **dead time** appears:

$$\frac{T(s)}{Q(s)} = \frac{R}{(\tau s + 1)} e^{-Ls}$$

Where:
- $L$ = **Dead time (transportation lag)** in seconds
- The exponential $e^{-Ls}$ is **Padé-approximated** for controller design.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| Formula / Equation | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| $C = mc_p$ | Thermal capacitance | Energy storage |
| $R = \Delta T / Q$ | Thermal resistance | Heat flow opposition |
| $\tau = RC$ | Time constant | Process dynamics |
| $q = kA \dfrac{\Delta T}{L}$ | Fourier's Law (Conduction) | Heat through walls |
| $q = hA \Delta T$ | Newton's Law (Convection) | Heat to fluid |
| $q = \sigma A T^4$ | Stefan-Boltzmann (Radiation) | Radiative heat transfer |
| $\dfrac{T(s)}{Q(s)} = \dfrac{R}{\tau s + 1}$ | First-order thermal TF | Stirred tank |
| $\dfrac{T(s)}{Q(s)} = \dfrac{R}{(\tau_1 s+1)(\tau_2 s+1)}$ | Second-order thermal TF | Heat exchanger |
| $\dfrac{T(s)}{Q(s)} = \dfrac{R\,e^{-Ls}}{\tau s + 1}$ | First-order with dead time | Pipeline temperature |
| $T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15$ | Scale conversion | Sensor calibration |
| $\text{ITAE} = \int_0^\infty t \cdot \vert e(t) \vert \, dt$ | Performance index | PID tuning (LT) |
| $K_c = 0.586 \cdot \dfrac{\tau}{L}$ (ITAE load) | ITAE tuning for PI | Temperature loops |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pipe Trick Applied:** All absolute values in equations above use `\vert` or `\mid` instead of `|` so the KTU formula table remains readable.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

Temperature control is the **backbone** of:
- 🛢️ **Petrochemical reactors** (exothermic reactions must be tightly controlled).
- 🏭 **Furnace annealing** in metallurgy (precise thermal cycling).
- 🍞 **Food & pharmaceutical drying** (sterilization temperatures).
- ⚛️ **Nuclear reactor coolant** loops (safety-critical).
- 🧪 **Polymerization reactors** (temperature affects molecular weight distribution).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: First-Order Thermal Transfer Function (Stirred Tank Heater)

### Setup
Consider a perfectly mixed tank containing a process fluid of:
- Mass $m$ (kg)
- Specific heat $c_p$ (J/kg·K)
- Inlet temperature $T_i$, outlet temperature $T$
- Volumetric flow rate $F$ (m³/s)
- Density $\rho$ (kg/m³)
- Heat input rate $Q$ (W) from heating coil

### Energy Balance (Step-by-Step)

**Step 1 — Rate of Accumulation of Energy:**
$$\text{Accumulation} = m c_p \frac{dT}{dt}$$

**Step 2 — Energy In by Flow:**
$$E_{in} = \rho F c_p T_i$$

**Step 3 — Energy Out by Flow:**
$$E_{out} = \rho F c_p T$$

**Step 4 — Energy In by Heater:**
$$E_{heater} = Q$$

**Step 5 — Conservation of Energy:**
$$\text{Accumulation} = E_{in} - E_{out} + E_{heater}$$

Substituting:
$$m c_p \frac{dT}{dt} = \rho F c_p T_i - \rho F c_p T + Q$$

**Step 6 — Rearranging:**
$$m c_p \frac{dT}{dt} + \rho F c_p T = \rho F c_p T_i + Q$$

Dividing throughout by $\rho F c_p$:
$$\frac{m}{\rho F} \frac{dT}{dt} + T = T_i + \frac{Q}{\rho F c_p}$$

Define:
- Process time constant: $\tau = \dfrac{m}{\rho F}$
- Steady-state gain: $R = \dfrac{1}{\rho F c_p}$ (thermal resistance)

The ODE becomes:
$$\tau \frac{dT}{dt} + T = T_i + R Q$$

**Step 7 — Taking Laplace Transform (zero initial conditions):**
$$\tau s T(s) + T(s) = T_i(s) + R Q(s)$$

**Step 8 — Solving for Transfer Function** (taking $T_i$ as constant disturbance = 0):
$$T(s)(\tau s + 1) = R Q(s)$$

$$\boxed{\;\frac{T(s)}{Q(s)} = \frac{R}{\tau s + 1}\;}$$

This is the **KTU benchmark first-order thermal transfer function**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Second-Order Thermal TF (Tank + Sensor in Series)

Consider two capacitances $C_1$ (process fluid) and $C_2$ (sensor mass), connected through thermal resistance $R$.

Let $T_1$ = bulk fluid temp, $T_2$ = sensor temp, $T_a$ = ambient, $Q$ = heater input.

**Node 1 (Fluid):**
$$C_1 \frac{dT_1}{dt} = Q - \frac{T_1 - T_2}{R}$$

**Node 2 (Sensor):**
$$C_2 \frac{dT_2}{dt} = \frac{T_1 - T_2}{R}$$

Taking Laplace:
$$C_1 s T_1(s) = Q(s) - \frac{T_1(s) - T_2(s)}{R}$$

$$C_2 s T_2(s) = \frac{T_1(s) - T_2(s)}{R}$$

Solving algebraically (substituting $T_1$ into $T_2$ equation) yields:

$$\frac{T_2(s)}{Q(s)} = \frac{R}{(R C_1 s + 1)(R C_2 s + 1)}$$

Letting $\tau_1 = R C_1$ and $\tau_2 = R C_2$:

$$\boxed{\;\frac{T_2(s)}{Q(s)} = \frac{R}{(\tau_1 s + 1)(\tau_2 s + 1)}\;}$$

This is a **standard KTU second-order thermal transfer function** without zeros.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Thermal Process Models

| Model Type | Transfer Function | Physical Example | Order | Dead Time |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Bare Thermocouple | $R / (\tau s + 1)$ | Sensor alone | 1 | 0 |
| Stirred Tank Heater | $R / (\tau s + 1)$ | CSTR | 1 | 0 |
| Tank + Thermowell | $R / [(\tau_1 s+1)(\tau_2 s+1)]$ | Reactor with shielded TC | 2 | 0 |
| Long Pipeline | $R e^{-Ls} / (\tau s + 1)$ | Furnace outlet measurement | 1 | $L$ |
| Heat Exchanger (full) | $R e^{-Ls} / [(\tau_1 s+1)(\tau_2 s+1)]$ | Shell-tube HEX | 2 | $L$ |
| Distributed Furnace | Higher-order + dead time | Reheat furnace | n | $L$ |

> [!NOTE]
> KTU exam problems frequently ask students to **identify the order and dead time** of a thermal system given a step-response graph. Use the **63.2% rise time rule** to extract $\tau$ and the **tangent-line intercept** method to estimate $L$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Closed-Loop Temperature Control System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP[Setpoint SP degreesC] --> SUM[Comparator Summing Junction]
    FB[Feedback T measured] --> SUM
    SUM --> CTRL[Controller PID Modes]
    CTRL --> FCV[Final Control Element Heater Cooler Valve]
    FCV --> PROC[Thermal Process Stirred Tank Furnace HEX]
    PROC --> SENSE[Temperature Sensor RTD TC Thermistor]
    SENSE --> TR[Transmitter Signal Conditioner]
    TR --> FB
```

**Explanation of Functional Flow:**
1. The **Setpoint (SP)** is the desired temperature entered by the operator.
2. The **Summing Junction** computes the error: $e(t) = SP - T_{measured}$.
3. The **PID Controller** outputs a corrective signal based on P, I, D actions.
4. The **Final Control Element** (e.g., thyristor power controller, steam valve, Peltier module) modulates heat input.
5. The **Thermal Process** (furnace, tank, HEX) physically changes temperature.
6. The **Temperature Sensor** (RTD/TC) detects the new temperature.
7. The **Transmitter** converts sensor signal to a standardized 4–20 mA or 0–10 V signal.
8. The loop closes back to the comparator.

---

## 4.2 Thermal RC Network Diagram (Lumped Parameter Model)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q[Heat Source Q watts] --> R1[Thermal Resistance R1]
    R1 --> N1[Node T1 Fluid Temperature]
    N1 --> R2[Thermal Resistance R2 Sensor Well]
    R2 --> N2[Node T2 Sensor Tip Temperature]
    N2 --> R3[Thermal Resistance R3 to Ambient]
    R3 --> AMB[Ambient Ta]
    C1[(Thermal Capacitance C1 Fluid Mass)] --- N1
    C2[(Thermal Capacitance C2 Sensor Mass)] --- N2
```

**How to Read This:**
- Each **resistor symbol (R1, R2, R3)** represents opposition to heat flow (analogous to electrical resistance).
- Each **capacitor symbol (C1, C2)** represents stored thermal energy (analogous to electrical capacitance).
- **Nodes (N1, N2)** are temperatures at intermediate points.
- This is the **physical origin** of the second-order transfer function derived in Section 3.

---

## 4.3 Temperature Sensor Selection Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Choose Temperature Sensor] --> RANGE{Measurement Range}
    RANGE -->|minus 200 to plus 850 C| RTD[RTD Pt100 Pt1000 High Accuracy]
    RANGE -->|0 to 2300 C| TC[Thermocouple K J T Type Fast Response]
    RANGE -->|minus 55 to plus 150 C| TH[Thermistor NTC PTC Low Cost]
    RANGE -->|Above 1000 C Non Contact| PY[Pyrometer Infrared Radiation]
    RTD --> APP{Application}
    TC --> APP
    TH --> APP
    PY --> APP
    APP -->|Lab Precision| CHOICE1[Use RTD Class A or AA]
    APP -->|Industrial Furnace| CHOICE2[Use Type K or S Thermocouple]
    APP -->|Consumer Electronics| CHOICE3[Use NTC Thermistor]
    APP -->|Moving Objects Molten Metal| CHOICE4[Use Pyrometer]
```

**Engineering Interpretation:**
- **RTD** → chosen where **stability, linearity, and accuracy** matter most.
- **Thermocouple** → chosen where **wide range and ruggedness** are priorities.
- **Thermistor** → chosen for **low-cost, narrow-range** applications.
- **Pyrometer** → chosen for **non-contact** measurement of moving or extremely hot bodies.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 🎯 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define thermal resistance and thermal capacitance. State their SI units.
**Model Answer:**

> **Thermal Resistance (R):** It is the opposition offered by a body to the flow of heat. It is defined as the ratio of temperature difference to the steady-state heat flow rate.
$$R = \frac{\Delta T}{Q}$$
> **SI Unit:** Kelvin per Watt (**K/W**).
