> **Thermal Capacitance (C):** It is the ability of a body to store thermal energy. It equals the product of mass and specific heat capacity.
$$C = m \cdot c_p$$
> **SI Unit:** Joules per Kelvin (**J/K**).

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023 type]**

---

### Q2. Why is temperature considered the slowest process variable in industrial control?
**Model Answer:**

> Temperature processes are slow because of **large thermal mass (high thermal capacitance)** and **resistance to heat transfer**. Energy must accumulate in the process before temperature changes. Typical time constants range from **30 seconds to several minutes**, compared to milliseconds for pressure and seconds for flow. Also, dead time due to sensor location and transportation lag makes temperature loops inherently sluggish, requiring careful tuning with PI or PID modes (avoiding aggressive derivative action on noisy sensor signals).

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 type]**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A — Stirred Tank Heater Modeling & Tuning

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** A perfectly mixed tank contains **500 kg** of process fluid with specific heat **$c_p = 2.5$ kJ/(kg·K)**. The flow rate is **0.005 m³/s** with density **900 kg/m³**. Heat input rate $Q$ is manipulated. Derive the transfer function relating temperature $T$ to heat input $Q$. (7 marks)

**(b)** If dead time of **30 s** is introduced (sensor located 20 m downstream with fluid velocity 0.67 m/s), determine **PI tuning parameters using ITAE load-rejection criterion**. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation:**

Given:
- $m = 500$ kg
- $c_p = 2500$ J/(kg·K)
- $F = 0.005$ m³/s
- $\rho = 900$ kg/m³

Energy balance:
$$m c_p \frac{dT}{dt} = \rho F c_p (T_i - T) + Q$$

Substituting values:
$$500 \times 2500 \frac{dT}{dt} = 900 \times 0.005 \times 2500 (T_i - T) + Q$$

$$1{,}250{,}000 \frac{dT}{dt} = 11{,}250 (T_i - T) + Q$$

Dividing by 11,250:
$$111.11 \frac{dT}{dt} + T = T_i + 8.89 \times 10^{-5} Q$$

Hence:
- Time constant: $\tau = 111.11$ **s** (≈ 1.85 min)
- Steady-state gain: $R = 8.89 \times 10^{-5}$ **°C/W**

$$\boxed{\;\frac{T(s)}{Q(s)} = \frac{8.89 \times 10^{-5}}{111.11\,s + 1}\;}$$

**Part (b) — ITAE Tuning:**

Process parameters (with dead time):
- $K_p = R = 8.89 \times 10^{-5}$ °C/W
- $\tau = 111.11$ s
- $L = 30$ s

ITAE Load-Rejection (PI Mode) tuning formulas:
$$K_c = 0.586 \cdot \frac{\tau}{L \cdot K_p}$$

$$\tau_I = \frac{\tau}{0.586}$$

Calculating $K_c$:
$$K_c = 0.586 \cdot \frac{111.11}{30 \times 8.89 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$K_c = 0.586 \cdot \frac{111.11}{2.667 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$K_c = 0.586 \cdot 41{,}667$$

$$\boxed{K_c \approx 24{,}417 \text{ (dimensionless)}}$$

Calculating $\tau_I$:
$$\tau_I = \frac{111.11}{0.586}$$

$$\boxed{\tau_I \approx 189.6 \text{ s}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students commonly lose marks by **(1)** forgetting to linearize and only writing the ODE, **(2)** mixing units (using kJ in one place and J in another), and **(3)** forgetting to specify units of $K_c$ and $\tau_I$ in the final answer. Always double-check numerical magnitude — a $K_c$ in thousands is normal for slow thermal loops with low process gain.

---

### Question B — Second-Order Thermal System & Heat Exchanger

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** For a heat exchanger, the process fluid temperature $T$ is related to steam valve opening $V$ by:
$$\frac{T(s)}{V(s)} = \frac{2}{(30s + 1)(5s + 1)}$$

Determine the steady-state gain, time constants, process type, and order. Compute the new steady-state temperature if valve opening changes by **10%**. Initial temperature = **120 °C**. (7 marks)

**(b)** Discuss in detail the **On-Off** versus **PID** control strategies for temperature. Why is **PI** the most commonly recommended mode for industrial temperature loops? (7 marks)

#### Model Solution

**Part (a):**

Identifying parameters:
- Steady-state gain: $K_p = 2$ °C/%
- Time constants: $\tau_1 = 30$ s, $\tau_2 = 5$ s
- Order: **2nd order (overdamped)**
- Dead time: $L = 0$

Steady-state temperature change for $\Delta V = 10\%$:
$$\Delta T_{ss} = K_p \cdot \Delta V = 2 \times 10 = 20\ °C$$

New steady-state temperature:
$$T_{ss,new} = 120 + 20 = \boxed{140\ °C}$$

The dominant time constant is $\tau_1 = 30$ s; the system settles approximately within $5 \tau_1 = 150$ s.

**Part (b) — Control Strategy Discussion:**

| Mode | Description | Suitability for Temperature |
|---|---|---|
| **On-Off** | Output switches between fully ON and fully OFF based on error sign | Used in domestic thermostats, low-cost incubators; causes oscillation around setpoint |
| **P-only** | Output proportional to error | Leaves steady-state offset; rarely used alone |
| **PI** | Adds integral to remove offset | **Industry standard for temperature** — slow process tolerates integral windup risks |
| **PD** | Derivative damps overshoot | Derivative amplifies sensor noise — limited use |
| **PID** | Full three-mode | Used in critical temperature loops (e.g., reactor); derivative filtered to avoid noise |

**Why PI dominates temperature loops:**
1. Thermal processes are slow → integral action has ample time to eliminate offset without aggressive behaviour.
2. Sensor signals (especially thermocouples) are **noisy** → derivative action amplifies noise → undesirable.
3. Large time constants naturally suppress oscillation → integral action alone gives stable, offset-free response.
4. On-Off causes continuous cycling → undesirable for sensitive processes (e.g., pharmaceutical dryers).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students often **(1)** forget to mention derivative noise amplification as a reason for avoiding D in temperature loops, **(2)** confuse P-only offset with PI offset elimination, and **(3)** fail to state that **On-Off** is acceptable only for non-critical applications. Include at least **two** numerical values from the problem in your answer for full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 🌡️ **Temperature is the slowest process variable** — typical $\tau = 30$ s to 60 min; large thermal mass dominates dynamics.
- 📐 **Thermal resistance** $R = \Delta T / Q$ (units **K/W**); **Thermal capacitance** $C = m c_p$ (units **J/K**).
- 🧪 **First-order thermal TF**: $\dfrac{R}{\tau s + 1}$ for perfectly mixed tanks without measurement lag.
- 🔁 **Second-order thermal TF**: $\dfrac{R}{(\tau_1 s+1)(\tau_2 s+1)}$ for tank + sensor dynamics.
- ⏱️ **Dead time** $L$ arises from sensor location and transportation lag; the TF gains the factor $e^{-Ls}$.
- 🌐 **Three modes of heat transfer** — conduction (Fourier), convection (Newton), radiation (Stefan-Boltzmann).
- 📏 **Temperature scales** — Kelvin (SI), Celsius, Rankine, Fahrenheit; conversions: $T(K) = T(°C) + 273.15$.
- 🎛️ **PI controller is the industry default** for temperature — slow process + noisy sensors make derivative problematic.
- ⚙️ **ITAE tuning** for PI in temperature: $K_c = 0.586\,\tau/(L K_p)$, $\tau_I = \tau/0.586$ (load-reaction).
- 🔥 **Heat transfer coefficients**: conduction uses $k$, convection uses $h$, radiation uses $\sigma T^4$.
- 🧯 **Sensor selection rule**: RTD for accuracy, Thermocouple for range, Thermistor for cost, Pyrometer for non-contact.
- ⚠️ **Avoid pure derivative action** on thermocouple signals — noise amplification corrupts control output.
- 🎯 **Step-response identification**: 63.2% rise time = $\tau$; tangent intercept = $L$.

> **Final KTU Study Tip:** In ESE numerical problems, always **write units at every step**, **linearize non-linear terms** (especially radiation $T^4$), and **state assumptions** (e.g., perfect mixing, constant $c_p$, negligible ambient losses) for full credit.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simple Temperature Control Scheme
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT743 - INDUSTRIAL PROCESS CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Simple Temperature Control Scheme

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Simple Temperature Control Scheme** is a closed-loop feedback control architecture in which the **Process Variable (PV)** — temperature — is continuously measured, compared against a desired **Set Point (SP)**, and regulated by manipulating the flow of an energy-transfer medium (steam, hot oil, electrical current, or cooling water) through a **Final Control Element (FCE)**.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this belongs to the family of **Single-Input Single-Output (SISO)** regulatory loops and forms the foundational building block of every thermal process unit operation — jacketed reactors, distillation reboilers, heat exchangers, sterilizers, and annealing furnaces.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> A temperature control loop is treated as the canonical example to teach the **Measurement → Transmission → Decision → Actuation** chain. Mastering this scheme makes every subsequent control loop (flow, level, pressure, composition) trivially understandable, because the architecture is identical — only the sensor physics change.

### 1.2 The Five Universal Building Blocks

Every temperature control loop — no matter how simple — contains exactly five functional blocks in a ring (closed path):

| # | Block | Role | Typical Hardware |
|---|-------|------|------------------|
| 1 | **Process** | Where temperature must be held | Jacketed CSTR, furnace, oven |
| 2 | **Sensor + Transmitter** | Converts °C into a pneumatic/electronic signal | RTD (Pt100), Thermocouple (J/K), Transmitter (4–20 mA) |
| 3 | **Controller** | Compares PV vs SP, decides corrective action | PID controller (electronic / DCS block) |
| 4 | **Final Control Element** | Physically manipulates heat input | Modulating steam valve, SCR / VFD on heater |
| 5 | **Comparator (error detector)** | Computes e(t) = SP − PV | Internal to the controller card |

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The Home Geyser

Imagine the simplest object you already understand: **an automatic electric rice cooker / geyser**.

- You dial a temperature on the knob → that is the **Set Point**.
- A **bimetallic strip or thermistor** inside senses the actual water temperature → that is the **Sensor**.
- The "click" you hear (snapping of contacts) is the **Controller making a decision**.
- The **heating coil** is the **Final Control Element**.
- The **water in the tank** is the **Process**.

When the water is cold (PV < SP), the contacts close → heater ON. When water becomes hot (PV ≥ SP), contacts open → heater OFF. This is a **two-position (ON–OFF) control scheme**. An industrial scheme simply replaces the snap-action thermostat with a smooth PID controller and replaces the heater with a modulating steam valve, but the *ring of causation is identical*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why temperature is harder to control than flow or pressure:** Temperature loops exhibit the **largest dead time (transportation lag)** and the **slowest time constants** (minutes to hours) of any common process variable. Heat must physically diffuse through metal walls, accumulate in fluid mass, and overcome thermal capacitance (m·C_p). This is why the temperature loop demands more careful tuning and why KTU examiners frequently test its dynamic behaviour.

### 1.4 Standard Signals, Units & Ranges (Commit to Memory)

| Quantity | Engineering Unit | Standard Signal |
|----------|------------------|-----------------|
| Temperature (PV) | **°C** or **K** | — |
| Resistance (RTD) | **Ω** (Pt100 → 100 Ω @ 0 °C) | — |
| mV (Thermocouple) | **mV** | — |
| Transmission | — | **4–20 mA** (industry) / 3–15 psig (legacy pneumatic) |
| Valve stem position | % travel (0–100 %) | — |
| Controller output | — | **4–20 mA** or **3–15 psig** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — The Closed-Loop Reasoning Chain

The control loop executes **continuously** in the following seven logical steps. KTU examiners award marks when a student can write this chain unprompted:

1. **Set Point (SP)** is loaded into the controller by the operator (e.g., 80 °C).
2. **Sensor** (RTD or thermocouple) measures the current temperature of the process fluid.
3. **Transmitter** linearises and converts the raw mV/Ω signal into a standardised **4–20 mA** current loop.
4. **Error Detector** (inside controller) computes the deviation:
   e(t) = SP − PV(t)
5. **Controller Algorithm** (P, PI, or PID) processes the error and issues a correcting output m(t) typically 4–20 mA.
6. **I/P Converter** (if pneumatic valve) or directly an **electronic actuator** positions the **control valve** modulating the heating/cooling medium.
7. **Process** responds; new PV is sensed; the cycle repeats — typically every **250 ms to 1 s** in modern DCS systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" of Step 4:** A negative error means PV > SP (too hot) → controller must *reduce* heating → valve closes (less steam). A positive error means PV < SP (too cold) → controller must *increase* heating → valve opens. The sign convention is the single most common place where students lose marks in derivations.

### 2.2 Sensing Elements — What KTU Expects You to Know

#### (a) Resistance Temperature Detector (RTD) — Platinum (Pt100)
The resistance varies nearly linearly with temperature:

R_T = R_0 \cdot \left[ 1 + \alpha \, T + \beta \, T^2 \right]

For a **purely linear** first-order approximation (often used in textbooks):

R_T \approx R_0 \cdot (1 + \alpha \, T)

where:
- R_0 = **100 Ω** at T = 0 °C
- \alpha \approx 3.85 \times 10^{-3} \, °C^{-1} (IEC 60751 standard)

#### (b) Thermocouple
Based on the **Seebeck Effect**, generates an mV proportional to the temperature difference between hot and cold (reference) junctions:

V \approx a \, T + b \, T^2

Typical sensitivities: Type K ≈ **41 µV/°C**, Type J ≈ **50 µV/°C**.

### 2.3 The Controller — Modes of Control (Hierarchical Complexity)

| Mode | Equation m(t) | Behaviour |
|------|---------------|-----------|
| **ON–OFF** (two-position) | m = m_{max} if e > 0; m = m_{min} otherwise | Simplest, cheap, causes oscillation |
| **P (Proportional)** | m(t) = \bar{m} + K_c \, e(t) | Eliminates oscillation but causes **offset** |
| **PI** | m(t) = \bar{m} + K_c \!\left[e(t) + \frac{1}{\tau_I}\!\int e(t)\,dt\right] | Removes offset (industry default for flow/level) |
| **PID** | m(t) = \bar{m} + K_c \!\left[e(t) + \frac{1}{\tau_I}\!\int e(t)\,dt + \tau_D \frac{de(t)}{dt}\right] | Best dynamic response; used for **temperature** |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MEMORISE THIS TABLE** — KTU questions on Module 2 frequently combine two or more of these equations in a single 14-mark problem.

| # | Concept / Law | Equation | Variable Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Error signal | e(t) = SP − PV(t) | Deviation | °C |
| 2 | RTD resistance (linear) | R_T = R_0 (1 + \alpha T) | Platinum sensor | Ω |
| 3 | Current-loop linearisation | I = 4 + \dfrac{PV − PV_{min}}{PV_{max} − PV_{min}} \times 16 | Transmitter | mA |
| 4 | Heat duty (energy balance) | Q = \dot{m} \, C_p \, \Delta T | Heat transferred | W or kW |
| 5 | LMTD heat transfer | \Delta T_{LM} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 − \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)} | Log-mean temp. difference | °C |
| 6 | Newton's law of cooling/heating | Q = U \, A \, \Delta T_{LM} | Overall transfer | W |
| 7 | First-order thermal lag | \tau \dfrac{dT}{dt} + T(t) = K_p \, Q(t) | Process dynamics | s |
| 8 | PID controller output | m(t) = K_c \!\left[ e(t) + \dfrac{1}{\tau_I}\!\int e\,dt + \tau_D \dfrac{de}{dt}\right] | Controller law | % |
| 9 | Proportional band | PB = \dfrac{100}{K_c} \cdot \dfrac{MV_{range}}{PV_{range}} | Equivalent of gain | % |
| 10 | Valve flow (linear) | \dot{m} = C_v \sqrt{\Delta P / \rho} | Through FCE | kg/s |

> **Syllabus Tag:** Lines 1, 2, 7, and 8 are the *most-tested* in the KTU 2024 Scheme question papers.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Petrochemical reactors** — exothermic reactions require tight temperature control (±1 °C) to prevent runaway (a classic KTU case study).
- **Pharmaceutical batch reactors** — FDA validation requires documented, repeatable temperature profiles.
- **Power-plant boiler superheaters** — control of steam temperature (~540 °C) within ±5 °C prevents turbine blade failure.
- **Food & dairy pasteurisation** — HTST processes demand ±0.5 °C accuracy for legal compliance.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Linearisation of the RTD Characteristic

**Problem statement:** A Pt100 RTD reads 138.50 Ω. Using the IEC linear approximation, determine the measured temperature.

**Step 1 — State the governing equation** (linear form, sufficient for KTU):

R_T = R_0 (1 + \alpha \, T)

**Step 2 — Substitute the known quantities** (R_0 = 100 Ω, \alpha = 3.85 \times 10^{-3} \, °C^{-1}, R_T = 138.50 Ω):

138.50 = 100 \, (1 + 3.85 \times 10^{-3} \, T)

**Step 3 — Divide both sides by 100:**

1.385 = 1 + 3.85 \times 10^{-3} \, T

**Step 4 — Subtract 1 from both sides:**

0.385 = 3.85 \times 10^{-3} \, T

**Step 5 — Solve for T:**

T = \dfrac{0.385}{3.85 \times 10^{-3}} = 100 \, °C

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** At T = 100 °C, an ideal Pt100 should read exactly 138.5 Ω. The derivation is dimensionally and numerically consistent — a key valuation cue for KTU scripts.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — The Closed-Loop Error Equation for a First-Order Thermal Process

We now develop the *governing differential equation* of the temperature loop in symbolic form. This derivation is a KTU 2024 favourite because it tests both **mathematical maturity** and **process intuition**.

**Step 1 — Begin with the process energy balance** (single-lumped capacitance, well-mixed tank):

m \, C_p \, \dfrac{dT}{dt} = Q_{in} − Q_{out} − Q_{loss}

**Step 2 — Assume perfect mixing and constant mass flow**, with Q_{loss} negligible compared to the manipulated Q. Let the manipulated heat be **proportional** to controller output m(t):

Q_{in} = K_v \, m(t)

**Step 3 — The outgoing sensible-heat term** can be linearised about the steady-state operating point T_s as:

Q_{out} = \dot{m} \, C_p \, (T − T_s) + \text{constant}

**Step 4 — Define deviation variables** for clarity:

T'(t) = T(t) − T_s, \quad m'(t) = m(t) − \bar{m}

**Step 5 — Combine and simplify** by letting the thermal capacitance be C = m \, C_p and the process gain K_p = K_v / \dot{m} C_p:

C \, \dfrac{dT'}{dt} + \dfrac{T'}{\tau} = K_p \, m'(t)

which rearranges to the canonical first-order ODE:

\tau \dfrac{dT'}{dt} + T'(t) = K_p \, K_v \, m'(t)

where \tau = \dfrac{m C_p}{\dot{m} C_p} is the **thermal time constant** (seconds).

**Step 6 — Laplace transform** (zero initial condition):

(\tau s + 1) \, T'(s) = K_p \, K_v \, M'(s)

**Step 7 — Define the process transfer function:**

G_p(s) = \dfrac{T'(s)}{M'(s)} = \dfrac{K_p}{\tau s + 1}

**Step 8 — Sensor + transmitter** has (assumed) unity gain / negligible dynamics:

H(s) \approx 1

**Step 9 — Controller** (proportional mode first, for simplicity):

G_c(s) = K_c

**Step 10 — Write the closed-loop relation** (PV = T', SP = R):

T'(s) = G_c(s) \, G_p(s) \, \left[ R(s) − T'(s) \right]

**Step 11 — Solve for T'(s) / R(s)** (the **closed-loop transfer function**):

\dfrac{T'(s)}{R(s)} = \dfrac{G_c \, G_p}{1 + G_c \, G_p \, H} = \dfrac{K_c \, K_p / (\tau s + 1)}{1 + K_c \, K_p / (\tau s + 1)}

**Step 12 — Multiply numerator and denominator by (\tau s + 1)**:

\dfrac{T'(s)}{R(s)} = \dfrac{K_c \, K_p}{\tau s + 1 + K_c \, K_p}

**Step 13 — Identify the new (closed-loop) time constant:**

\tau_{CL} = \dfrac{\tau}{1 + K_c \, K_p}

**Step 14 — Interpretation:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Increasing controller gain K_c *reduces* the closed-loop time constant \tau_{CL}. This means **higher gain → faster response**. However, KTU examiners expect you to note the trade-off: too much gain causes instability / oscillation. This tension — *speed vs stability* — is the philosophical core of every control problem.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Steady-State Offset Under Pure P-Control

For a step set-point change R = \Delta R (constant), set s = 0 in Step 12 above:

T'_{ss} = \dfrac{K_c K_p}{1 + K_c K_p} \, \Delta R

**Offset** = SP − PV at steady state:

e_{ss} = \Delta R − T'_{ss} = \Delta R - \dfrac{K_c K_p}{1 + K_c K_p} \Delta R

e_{ss} = \dfrac{\Delta R}{1 + K_c K_p}

> This is the celebrated **proportional offset**. Only by adding the **integral mode** can e_{ss} → 0 — a classic KTU Part B question.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Control Modes for Temperature

| Performance Criterion | ON–OFF | P | PI | PID |
|-----------------------|--------|---|----|----|
| Offset (steady-state error) | Large cyclic | \neq 0 | **0** | 0 |
| Speed of response | Slow (hysteresis-bound) | Fast | Medium | **Fastest** |
| Oscillation / overshoot | Continuous (limit cycle) | Low | Medium | Tuneable |
| Sensitivity to noise | Low | Low | Medium | **High (D-action amplifies noise)** |
| Suitability for **temperature** | Small domestic heaters | Not preferred | OK | **Industry default** |
| KTU examination weightage | 3 marks (definition) | 3 marks | 7 marks | 14 marks |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram of the Simple Temperature Control Scheme

The following Mermaid diagram renders the canonical closed-loop ring (Measurement → Transmission → Decision → Actuation → Process → back to Measurement).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeSP["Set Point SP degC"]
    nodeSum{{"Comparator e(t)=SP-PV"}}
    nodeCtrl["Controller PID Kc tauI tauD"]
    nodeItoP["I to P Converter 3 to 15 psig"]
    nodeValve["Control Valve Steam or Coolant"]
    nodeProc["Process Jacketed Tank"]
    nodeSens["Temperature Sensor Pt100 RTD"]
    nodeTx["Transmitter 4 to 20 mA"]
    nodeSP --> nodeSum
    nodeSum -- error e --> nodeCtrl
    nodeCtrl -- output m --> nodeItoP
    nodeItoP -- pneumatic --> nodeValve
    nodeValve -- heat flow --> nodeProc
    nodeProc -- temperature --> nodeSens
    nodeSens -- resistance --> nodeTx
    nodeTx -- current feedback --> nodeSum
```

### 4.2 Signal-Flow Graph (Laplace-Domain Representation)

For a first-order thermal process under a PI controller, the loop algebra is:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeR["R(s) Set Point"]
    nodeE["E(s) Error"]
    nodeGc["Gc(s) Controller"]
    nodeGv["Gv(s) Valve"]
    nodeGp["Gp(s) Process Kp over tau s plus 1"]
    nodeH["H(s) Measurement unity"]
    nodeR -- plus --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeGc
    nodeGc --> nodeGv
    nodeGv --> nodeGp
    nodeGp -- PV --> nodeE
    nodeGp -- to output --> nodeH
    nodeE -- minus feedback --> nodeR
```

### 4.3 Physical Layout (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

Because a true process sketch cannot be rendered in pure Mermaid, the following block-functional layout shows where each instrument physically resides in the plant:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FIELD["Field Area"]
        fieldTank["Jacketed Reactor with Pt100 sensor"]
        fieldValve["Steam Control Valve on jacket inlet"]
        fieldTx["Temperature Transmitter in junction box"]
    end
    subgraph CONTROL_ROOM["Control Room DCS Rack"]
        crCard["PID Controller Card slot 7 of DCS"]
        crHMI["Operator HMI showing 78.6 degC"]
    end
    fieldTank -- RTD leads --> fieldTx
    fieldTx -- 4 to 20 mA pair --> crCard
    crCard -- 4 to 20 mA output --> fieldValve
    fieldValve -- manipulates steam --> fieldTank
    crCard -- Ethernet --> crHMI
```

### 4.4 Reading the Diagrams (Examiner Expectation)

When KTU asks you to *"draw the block diagram of a temperature control loop"*, you must include:
- All **five blocks** (sensor / transmitter / controller / final control element / process)
- The **direction of signal flow** with arrows
- The **error comparator** explicitly
- A clear **feedback path** returning from process to comparator
- Labelling of signal types (e.g., 4–20 mA, 3–15 psig)

A diagram missing the feedback path is **automatically penalised 2–3 marks**.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define a Simple Temperature Control Scheme and list its five essential components.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Simple Temperature Control Scheme is a closed-loop feedback arrangement in which the **Process Variable (PV) — temperature** is continuously measured, compared with a desired **Set Point (SP)**, and maintained by automatic adjustment of a heating/cooling medium through a Final Control Element.

The five essential components are:
1. **Process** — the vessel whose temperature is to be controlled (e.g., jacketed reactor).
2. **Temperature Sensor + Transmitter** — e.g., Pt100 RTD with a 4–20 mA transmitter.
3. **Controller** — computes error and outputs correcting signal (PID card / DCS block).
4. **Final Control Element** — e.g., pneumatically actuated steam valve (with I/P converter).
5. **Comparator (Error Detector)** — internal to the controller; computes e(t) = SP − PV.

*(Award 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for components.)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State the linear relationship between resistance and temperature for a Pt100 RTD and find the resistance at 150 °C.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The linear approximation for a Pt100 RTD (IEC 60751) is:

R_T = R_0 \, (1 + \alpha \, T)

where R_0 = 100 Ω and \alpha = 3.85 \times 10^{-3} \, °C^{-1}.

Substituting T = 150 °C:

R_T = 100 \, \bigl[1 + (3.85 \times 10^{-3})(150)\bigr] = 100 \, [1 + 0.5775] = 157.75 \, \Omega

*(Award 1 mark for formula + 1 mark for substitution + 1 mark for final answer.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Never** omit units — every numerical answer loses 0.5–1 mark if °C, Ω, mA are missing.
> 2. **Never** write only the formula — examiners want to see the substitution step.
> 3. **Sign convention** for the error e(t) = SP − PV must be consistent throughout, or the integral-mode derivation collapses.
> 4. **Steady-state offset** must always be addressed when discussing P-only control — forgetting this is the #1 reason students lose 3–4 marks.
> 5. **Block diagrams** must include the feedback path; omitting it is treated as an incomplete answer.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** Draw the complete block diagram of a simple temperature control scheme for a jacketed CSTR heated by steam, and explain the function of each block. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The block diagram consists of five elements arranged in a closed ring:

1. **Process (Jacketed CSTR):** The thermal mass whose temperature T is to be controlled. It has thermal capacitance C and a steady-state gain K_p and time constant \tau.
2. **Temperature Sensor (Pt100 RTD):** Senses the fluid temperature and converts it into a proportional change in electrical resistance.
3. **Transmitter:** Converts the RTD resistance (Ω) into a standard 4–20 mA current signal proportional to the measurement range.
4. **Controller (PID card in DCS):** Receives the 4–20 mA PV signal, compares it with the SP entered by the operator, computes the error e(t), and applies the PID algorithm to produce a 4–20 mA corrective output.
5. **I/P Converter + Control Valve:** Converts the electronic controller output (4–20 mA) into pneumatic pressure (3–15 psig) which positions the steam valve stem, modulating the steam flow into the jacket.

The feedback path closes from the sensor back to the comparator (within the controller). The signal chain is: SP → Error → PID → 4–20 mA → 3–15 psig → Valve → Steam → Jacket → Process → T → RTD → Transmitter → PV → Error.

*(Award 2 marks for the diagram, 3 marks for naming blocks, 2 marks for explaining functions clearly.)*

---

**(b)** A Pt100 RTD is used to measure the temperature of a chemical reactor. The RTD resistance is found to be **175.85 Ω**. Using the linear approximation, calculate the measured temperature. If the transmitter is calibrated for 0–200 °C and outputs 4–20 mA, find the current signal at this temperature. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Temperature from RTD resistance** — using R_T = R_0(1 + \alpha T):

175.85 = 100 \, (1 + 3.85 \times 10^{-3} \, T)

1.7585 = 1 + 3.85 \times 10^{-3} \, T

0.7585 = 3.85 \times 10^{-3} \, T

T = \dfrac{0.7585}{3.85 \times 10^{-3}} = 197.01 \, °C \approx 197 \, °C

**(ii) Transmitter current signal** — using the linear 4–20 mA mapping:

I = 4 + \dfrac{PV − PV_{min}}{PV_{max} − PV_{min}} \times 16

I = 4 + \dfrac{197 − 0}{200 − 0} \times 16

I = 4 + 0.985 \times 16 = 4 + 15.76 = 19.76 \, mA

**Conclusion:** The reactor is operating very close to its upper temperature limit, and the controller will receive a near-maximum PV signal, triggering appropriate steam-valve throttling.

*(Award 3 marks for T, 3 marks for I, 1 mark for physical interpretation.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** With the aid of a closed-loop transfer-function derivation, show that pure proportional control on a first-order thermal process produces a steady-state offset. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

For a first-order thermal process with transfer function:

G_p(s) = \dfrac{K_p}{\tau s + 1}

and a proportional controller G_c(s) = K_c with unity feedback H(s) = 1, the closed-loop transfer function from set point R(s) to PV T'(s) is derived as:

T'(s) = G_c \, G_p \, [R(s) − T'(s)]

\dfrac{T'(s)}{R(s)} = \dfrac{K_c \, K_p / (\tau s + 1)}{1 + K_c \, K_p / (\tau s + 1)} = \dfrac{K_c K_p}{\tau s + 1 + K_c K_p}

Applying the **Final Value Theorem** for a step set-point change R(s) = \Delta R / s:

T'_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \, \dfrac{K_c K_p}{\tau s + 1 + K_c K_p} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{s} = \dfrac{K_c K_p}{1 + K_c K_p} \, \Delta R

**Offset** is defined as the residual error at steady state:

e_{ss} = \Delta R − T'_{ss} = \Delta R - \dfrac{K_c K_p}{1 + K_c K_p} \Delta R

e_{ss} = \dfrac{\Delta R}{1 + K_c K_p}

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since K_c K_p > 0, the offset e_{ss} is **always positive and non-zero** for finite K_c. To eliminate it, the controller must contain an **integral mode**, which provides infinite DC gain, driving the denominator term to infinity and hence e_{ss} → 0.

*(Award 3 marks for transfer-function derivation, 2 marks for offset expression, 2 marks for explanation.)*

---

**(b)** A jacketed reactor has process gain K_p = 2.5 °C/% and thermal time constant \tau = 240 s. With a proportional controller of gain K_c = 0.8 %/°C, calculate (i) the closed-loop time constant, and (ii) the steady-state offset for a step set-point change of 20 °C. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** K_p = 2.5 °C/%, \tau = 240 s, K_c = 0.8 %/°C, \Delta R = 20 °C.

**(i) Closed-loop time constant:**

\tau_{CL} = \dfrac{\tau}{1 + K_c K_p} = \dfrac{240}{1 + (0.8)(2.5)} = \dfrac{240}{1 + 2.0} = \dfrac{240}{3.0} = 80 \, s

**Interpretation:** The closed-loop responds 3× faster than the open-loop process — a quantitative demonstration of how feedback accelerates dynamics.

**(ii) Steady-state offset:**

e_{ss} = \dfrac{\Delta R}{1 + K_c K_p} = \dfrac{20}{1 + (0.8)(2.5)} = \dfrac{20}{3.0} = 6.67 \, °C

**Interpretation:** Although the response is faster, the PV will settle at SP − 6.67 °C = 13.33 °C, falling short of the desired 20 °C. This persistent shortfall is the **proportional offset** — practically unacceptable for a temperature loop in a chemical reactor, which is why **PI or PID control is the industrial standard**.

*(Award 3 marks for \tau_{CL}, 3 marks for offset, 1 mark for physical interpretation.)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A **Simple Temperature Control Scheme** is a **SISO closed-loop** with exactly **five functional blocks** in a ring.
- ✅ The canonical error equation is **e(t) = SP − PV(t)** — sign convention matters.
- ✅ The industry-standard transmission signal is **4–20 mA** (live-zero allows fault detection: < 4 mA = sensor broken).
- ✅ **Pt100 RTD** is the most common temperature sensor; resistance is linear with temperature: R_T = R_0 (1 + \alpha T).
- ✅ **Thermocouples** generate mV; suitable for higher ranges than RTDs but require cold-junction compensation.
- ✅ **First-order thermal process** has transfer function G_p(s) = K_p / (\tau s + 1); typical \tau ranges from seconds (small sensors) to hours (large reactors).
- ✅ **Closed-loop time constant** shrinks by factor (1 + K_c K_p) relative to open-loop \tau — feedback makes systems faster.
- ✅ **Proportional-only control always leaves a steady-state offset** = \Delta R / (1 + K_c K_p).
- ✅ **Integral action eliminates offset** (infinite DC gain) but may introduce overshoot/oscillation; **derivative action** improves stability and speeds up response but amplifies measurement noise.
- ✅ For **temperature loops**, **PID is the industry default**; PI is acceptable if the loop has significant noise.
- ✅ The **block diagram must show the feedback path explicitly** — omitting it costs marks in KTU valuations.
- ✅ Units, units, units — every numerical answer in KTU scripts must carry units (°C, Ω, mA, s).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Valve Used in the Temperature Control Scheme
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT743 - INDUSTRIAL PROCESS CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Valve Used in the Temperature Control Scheme

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In an industrial **Temperature Control Scheme**, the **valve** functions as the **Final Control Element (FCE)** that physically modulates the flow rate of the heating or cooling medium (steam, hot oil, chilled water, or coolant) entering the process heat exchanger, jacketed vessel, or coil. The valve receives a pneumatic, hydraulic, or electrical signal from the **Temperature Controller (TIC)** and translates this low-energy command signal into a precise mechanical displacement of its plug/stem assembly, thereby regulating the heat-transfer rate to maintain the process temperature at the desired set point.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the valve used in a typical industrial temperature control loop is most commonly a **Pneumatically Actuated Diaphragm-Operated Globe-Type Control Valve**, frequently equipped with a **Valve Positioner** to eliminate stem-thrust non-linearities and dynamic lags.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEICT743 – Module 2):**
> The valves discussed in temperature control schemes fall under the broader category of *Control Valves* and include:
> 1. Pneumatic Diaphragm-Actuated Globe Valves
> 2. Three-Way Mixing / Diverting Valves
> 3. Self-Operated Temperature Regulators
> 4. On–Off (Solenoid) Valves
> 5. Electrically Actuated (Motor-Operated) Valves

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Tap with a Brain"

Imagine the **water tap** in your home kitchen. You turn the knob slightly, and a small stream flows. You turn it more, and more water flows. Now imagine a robot hand that turns the tap **automatically** based on the temperature of water in a bucket:
- If the water is too **cold**, the robot hand turns the tap **more open** to let more hot water in.
- If the water is too **hot**, the robot hand turns the tap **less open** to reduce hot-water flow.

That **robot hand + tap** combination is exactly what a **temperature control valve** does — it is a **mechanically intelligent tap** that physically opens/closes (or modulates) the heating/cooling medium flow in direct response to controller commands.

## 1.3 Key Operating Parameters (Standard KTU Metrics)

| Parameter | Standard Symbol | Typical Value (Industrial) |
|---|---|---|
| Actuator Air Supply Pressure | $P_{sup}$ | **20 psig (1.4 bar)** |
| Spring Range | $\Delta P_{spring}$ | **3–15 psig** (0.2–1.0 bar) |
| Valve Body Rating | ANSI Class | **150# / 300#** |
| Process Temperature Range | $T_{process}$ | **-50 °C to +550 °C** |
| Stem Travel | $L$ | **0.5 inch to 2 inch** |
| Trim Material Hardness | — | **316 SS, Stellite-faced** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "3–15 psig" Pneumatic Standard:**
> Almost every KTU problem on pneumatic temperature control valves assumes an air-to-open / air-to-close spring range of **3 to 15 psig** (or its SI equivalent **0.2 to 1.0 bar**). A signal of **3 psig = full closed (FC)** and **15 psig = full open (FO)** — this is non-negotiable in KTU board exams.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Valves Used in Temperature Control Schemes

Industrial temperature control valves can be broadly classified along **three orthogonal axes**:

### Axis A: By Actuation Principle
1. **Pneumatic Diaphragm-Actuated** — most common in temperature loops
2. **Electro-Pneumatic (I/P Transducer + Diaphragm)**
3. **Hydraulic Piston-Actuated** — for high-thrust, high-pressure steam
4. **Electric Motor-Actuated** — for slow, large-bore isolation
5. **Self-Operated** (no external power) — uses thermal bulb/rod expansion

### Axis B: By Body / Trim Design
1. **Globe Valve** (single-seat, double-seat, three-way)
2. **Ball Valve** (segmented V-ball for throttling)
3. **Butterfly Valve** (large flow, low pressure drop)
4. **Saunders Diaphragm Valve** (for corrosive slurries)

### Axis C: By Flow Characteristic
1. **Quick-Opening** — for on–off and two-position temperature control
2. **Linear** — for proportional temperature loops with constant pressure drop
3. **Equal Percentage** — for temperature loops with large load variations and high turndown

## 2.2 Component Anatomy of a Pneumatic Diaphragm Temperature Control Valve

The standard **pneumatically actuated globe valve** (which is the *de-facto* KTU textbook answer for "valve in temperature control") consists of the following hierarchical parts:

**I. Actuator Assembly (Upper Portion)**
- **Diaphragm Case (Top + Bottom)**: Houses the elastomer/reinforced rubber diaphragm.
- **Diaphragm**: A flexible circular disc that deflects under air pressure.
- **Actuator Spring**: Provides the opposing (fail-safe) force.
- **Stem Connector / Yoke**: Mechanically couples diaphragm to valve stem.

**II. Valve Body Assembly (Lower Portion)**
- **Body / Bonnet**: Cast steel or stainless steel pressure vessel.
- **Stem**: Connects diaphragm to plug; transmits linear motion.
- **Plug**: The throttling element shaped to give the desired flow characteristic.
- **Seat Ring**: Stationary ring that mates with the plug to form the flow restriction.
- **Packing Box**: Prevents process fluid leakage along the stem.
- **Packing Nut / Gland**: Compresses the packing.
- **Travel Indicator**: External scale showing plug position.

## 2.3 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

> [!NOTE]
> **Step-by-Step Operational Logic of a Pneumatic Diaphragm Temperature Control Valve:**

1. The **Temperature Transmitter (TT)** senses the process temperature and sends a standardized signal (e.g., **4–20 mA** or **0.2–1.0 bar** pneumatic) to the **Temperature Indicating Controller (TIC)**.
2. The TIC compares the measured temperature with the set point and computes an error.
3. The controller output (e.g., **3–15 psig**) is routed pneumatically to the **top of the diaphragm case** of the valve actuator.
4. The **air pressure on the diaphragm** creates a downward force $F_{air} = P_{air} \times A_{diaphragm}$.
5. This force is opposed by the **actuator spring force** $F_{spring} = k \times x$, where $x$ is stem displacement.
6. **Mechanical equilibrium** is reached when $F_{air} = F_{spring}$, at which point the plug has moved to a position that produces the correct flow rate.
7. The process temperature is thereby corrected, and the loop stabilises.

## 2.4 Force-Balance Equation of the Diaphragm Actuator

The heart of the pneumatic valve analysis is the **steady-state force-balance equation**:

$$F_{air} = F_{spring} + F_{friction} + F_{process}$$

In a **frictionless, process-balanced** idealisation (which is what KTU expects unless stated otherwise):

$$P_{air} \times A_{d} = k \cdot x$$

Solving for stem displacement $x$:

$$x = \frac{A_{d}}{k} \cdot P_{air}$$

This linear relationship is precisely why the pneumatic actuator is treated as an ideal **integrator-with-spring** in process-control textbooks.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains the canonical KTU-board-exam formulas. Memorise them.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $x = \dfrac{A_{d}}{k} \cdot P_{air}$ | Stem displacement from pneumatic force balance | m, m², N/m, Pa |
| 2 | $F_{net} = P_{air} \cdot A_{d} - kx$ | Net actuating force on the diaphragm-stem | N |
| 3 | $C_{v} = \dfrac{Q}{\sqrt{\Delta P / G}}$ | Valve flow coefficient (US units) | gpm/√psi |
| 4 | $C_{v} = \dfrac{Q}{N_1} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{G / G_1}{\Delta P / P_1}}$ | Generalised $C_v$ equation (SI variant) | — |
| 5 | $\dot{m} = C_{v} \sqrt{\Delta P \cdot \rho}$ | Mass flow rate through valve | kg/s |
| 6 | $Q = C_{v} \cdot N_1 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta P}{G}}$ | Volumetric flow rate | m³/h |
| 7 | $I_{valve} = \dfrac{\Delta P_{valve}}{\Delta P_{system}}$ | Valve authority (pressure drop ratio) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\tau_{actuator} = \dfrac{A_{d}^{2}}{k \cdot R_{pneumatic}}$ | Pneumatic actuator time constant | seconds |
| 9 | $A = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4}$ | Diaphragm effective area | m² |
| 10 | $q(t) = K_v \cdot x(t)$ | Linear valve flow law (linear trim) | m³/s |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall — Do not confuse $C_v$ (US) with $K_v$ (SI):**
> - US: $C_v$ = flow in **gpm** of water at 1 psi drop.
> - SI: $K_v$ = flow in **m³/h** of water at 1 bar drop.
> Conversion: $K_v \approx 0.865 \cdot C_v$

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In a real chemical plant, the temperature control valve sits at the end of a long chain: **Sensor → Transmitter → Controller → I/P Transducer → Valve Positioner → Actuator → Stem → Plug → Seat → Process.** A malfunction in **any** of these links breaks the temperature loop. A 1 % stem-position error can translate to a 5–15 °C temperature excursion, which in a polymerisation reactor can mean **off-spec product** or even a **runaway reaction**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I: Stem Displacement of a Pneumatic Diaphragm Actuator

This is the **most frequently asked 7-mark derivation** in KTU Module 2 board exams. The examiner expects every force term to be written explicitly.

### Problem Setup
A pneumatic diaphragm actuator with effective area $A_{d}$ receives a controller air pressure $P_{air}$. The actuator spring has stiffness $k$ and a pre-compression equal to a "zero-signal" force $F_0$. The valve stem moves downward by a distance $x$ against friction $F_f$ and process unbalance $F_p$.

### Step 1 — Define the Forces

The **downward force** applied by the air on the diaphragm is:

$$F_{air} = P_{air} \cdot A_{d}$$

The **upward force** of the (compressed) spring is:

$$F_{spring} = F_0 + k \cdot x$$

The **process unbalance force** on the plug is the difference between the upstream and downstream fluid forces:

$$F_{process} = (P_1 - P_2) \cdot A_{plug} = \Delta P \cdot A_{eff}$$

The **friction force** in the packing is:

$$F_{friction} = \mu \cdot N_{packing}$$

### Step 2 — Write the Equilibrium Equation

At steady state, sum of forces = 0 (Newton's 2nd law for static):

$$F_{air} = F_{spring} + F_{process} + F_{friction}$$

Substituting:

$$P_{air} \cdot A_{d} = F_0 + kx + \Delta P \cdot A_{eff} + \mu N_{packing}$$

### Step 3 — Solve for Stem Displacement $x$

Rearranging for $x$:

$$k \cdot x = P_{air} \cdot A_{d} - F_0 - \Delta P \cdot A_{eff} - \mu N_{packing}$$

Therefore:

$$x = \frac{A_{d}}{k} \cdot P_{air} - \frac{F_0}{k} - \frac{\Delta P \cdot A_{eff}}{k} - \frac{\mu N_{packing}}{k}$$

### Step 4 — Idealised Case (KTU Standard Assumption)

Assuming **frictionless packing** and **pressure-balanced trim** ($A_{eff} = 0$), and that the spring pre-load is calibrated such that $P_{air} = 3$ psig gives $x = 0$:

$$x = \frac{A_{d}}{k} \cdot (P_{air} - 3\,\text{psig})$$

For a 3–15 psig signal range with full travel $L$:

$$x = L \cdot \frac{P_{air} - 3}{15 - 3} = \frac{L}{12} \cdot (P_{air} - 3)$$

This is the **canonical KTU final answer** for stem-displacement problems.

## 3.2 Derivation II: Valve Flow Coefficient $C_v$ for Temperature Control

For a temperature control loop on a heat exchanger, we need to find the required valve size.

### Step 1 — Energy Balance on Heat Exchanger

The heat-transfer rate required to maintain temperature is:

$$\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$$

### Step 2 — Mass Flow Required

$$\dot{m} = \frac{\dot{Q}}{c_p \cdot \Delta T}$$

### Step 3 — Valve Flow Equation (Liquid Service)

For an incompressible fluid (water, glycol, hot oil):

$$\dot{m} = C_{v} \cdot \rho \cdot \sqrt{\frac{\Delta P_{valve}}{\rho}} = C_{v} \sqrt{\Delta P_{valve} \cdot \rho}$$

Solving for $C_v$:

$$C_{v} = \frac{\dot{m}}{\sqrt{\Delta P_{valve} \cdot \rho}}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute Mass Flow from Heat Duty

$$C_{v} = \frac{\dot{Q}}{c_p \cdot \Delta T \cdot \sqrt{\Delta P_{valve} \cdot \rho}}$$

This is the **standard KTU formula for sizing a temperature control valve on a liquid-heating/cooling service**.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (14-Mark Style)

**Problem:** A temperature control loop uses a pneumatic diaphragm valve to regulate steam flow into a jacketed reactor. The controller output is **3–15 psig**, the diaphragm effective area is **$A_{d} = 0.012\,m^{2}$**, the spring stiffness is **$k = 25{,}000\,N/m$**, and the spring pre-load is set so that at **3 psig** the plug is just touching the seat. Find:
(a) Stem displacement when the controller output is **9 psig**.
(b) Confirm whether the plug is fully open at **15 psig** if the rated total travel is **25 mm**.

### Solution

**Part (a) — Stem displacement at 9 psig:**

Convert 9 psig to pascals:

$$P_{air} = 9\,\text{psig} \times 6894.76 = 62{,}053\,\text{Pa}$$

Apply the force-balance equation with pre-load at 3 psig:

$$x = \frac{A_{d}}{k} \cdot (P_{air} - P_{0})$$

Convert $P_0 = 3$ psig to Pa:

$$P_{0} = 3 \times 6894.76 = 20{,}684\,\text{Pa}$$

Plug in:

$$x = \frac{0.012}{25{,}000} \times (62{,}053 - 20{,}684)$$

$$x = 4.8 \times 10^{-7} \times 41{,}369$$

$$x = 0.01986\,m = 19.86\,mm \approx 20\,mm$$

**Part (b) — Check at 15 psig:**

At 15 psig = 103,421 Pa:

$$x_{max} = \frac{0.012}{25{,}000} \times (103{,}421 - 20{,}684)$$

$$x_{max} = 4.8 \times 10^{-7} \times 82{,}737$$

$$x_{max} = 0.0397\,m = 39.7\,mm$$

Since the rated travel is only **25 mm**, the plug would hit the **mechanical stop** before reaching 15 psig. The valve is therefore **over-sized** for this spring — the KTU-correct remediation is to **increase the spring stiffness to $k' = 39.7\,mm / 25\,mm \times 25{,}000 = 39{,}700\,N/m$**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** Always state units in the final answer. "x = 19.86 mm" gets full marks; "x = 19.86" without units loses 0.5 mark.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Valve for Temperature Control

| Temperature Control Scenario | Recommended Valve | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Small lab bath, ±1 °C tolerance | **On–Off Solenoid Valve** | Simple, cheap, hysteresis tolerable |
| Fermenter jacket, ±0.5 °C, batch | **Linear Globe + Positioner** | Proportional, predictable gain |
| Distillation reflux, ±0.1 °C, large turndown | **Equal-Percentage + Positioner** | Handles 50:1 rangeability |
| Steam header, high pressure, fast response | **Pneumatic Piston-Actuated** | High thrust, short stroke time |
| Hot-oil heat exchanger, viscous, slow load | **Electric Motor-Actuated** | Slow, high torque, no air supply needed |
| District heating, no instrument air | **Self-Operated Temperature Regulator** | Thermal-bulb powered, intrinsically fail-safe |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Pneumatic Temperature Control Valve — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["Temperature Transmitter TT"] --> node2["Temperature Controller TIC"]
    node2 --> node3["I to P Converter I P"]
    node3 --> node4["Pneumatic Signal 3 to 15 psig"]
    node4 --> node5["Diaphragm Top Chamber"]
    node5 --> node6["Elastomer Diaphragm"]
    node6 --> node7["Stem Mechanical Linkage"]
    node7 --> node8["Valve Stem"]
    node8 --> node9["Plug and Seat Assembly"]
    node9 --> node10["Process Flow Heating or Cooling Medium"]
    node11["Actuator Spring Failsafe"] --> node7
    node12["Valve Positioner Optional"] --> node5
    node13["Process Temperature Feedback"] --> node1
    node14["Atmospheric Vent"] --> node5
    node15["Packing Box Seal"] --> node8
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Cross-Sectional View of Diaphragm-Actuated Globe Valve

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Controller Air Inlet 3 to 15 psig"] --> nodeB["Top Diaphragm Case"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Flexible Diaphragm"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Diaphragm Plate"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Upper Stem"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Yoke Coupling"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Lower Stem"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["Valve Plug"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["Valve Seat Ring"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["Process Inlet"]
    nodeI --> nodeK["Process Outlet"]
    nodeL["Actuator Spring Surrounds Stem"] --> nodeE
    nodeM["Bonnet Assembly Houses Stem"] --> nodeG
    nodeN["Packing Box Above Bonnet"] --> nodeE
    nodeO["Travel Indicator Scale on Stem"] --> nodeG
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Control Loop Architecture for Temperature Control Valve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Process Vessel Heated or Cooled"] --> B["Temperature Sensor RTD or Thermocouple"]
    B --> C["Transmitter 4 to 20 mA"]
    C --> D["Controller PID"]
    D --> E["Current to Pressure Converter I to P"]
    E --> F["Pneumatic Control Valve"]
    F --> A
    G["Set Point SP Input"] --> D
    H["Valve Positioner Feedback"] --> E
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Three-Way Mixing Valve in Temperature Control

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inA["Hot Water Inlet Port A"] --> mixNode["Three Way Mixing Valve"]
    inB["Cold Water Inlet Port B"] --> mixNode
    mixNode --> outC["Mixed Outlet Port C to Process"]
    ctrlP["Pneumatic Actuator"] --> mixNode
    sig["Controller Signal 3 to 15 psig"] --> ctrlP
    fb["Position Feedback to Controller"] --> sig
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why a Three-Way Valve for Temperature Control?**
> A **three-way mixing valve** combines a **hot** and a **cold** stream to produce an **intermediate-temperature** stream, which is far more energy-efficient than a two-way valve that mixes hot water with cold water *downstream* of the valve (which wastes energy and causes thermal shocks).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**List the main components of a pneumatically actuated diaphragm-type control valve used in a temperature control loop.**

**Model Answer:**
The main components are: (1) **Diaphragm case** (top and bottom housings), (2) **Flexible diaphragm** (elastomeric disc), (3) **Actuator spring** (provides fail-safe return force), (4) **Stem** (rigid rod transmitting diaphragm motion to the plug), (5) **Valve plug** (throttling element), (6) **Seat ring** (stationary mating surface), (7) **Valve body** and **bonnet** (pressure-containing envelope), (8) **Packing box and gland** (prevents process fluid leakage), and optionally (9) **Valve positioner** (eliminates stem-thrust non-linearities).

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is valve authority, and why is it important in temperature control?**

**Model Answer:**
**Valve authority** $\alpha$ is defined as the ratio of the pressure drop across the control valve to the total pressure drop in the system at maximum flow:

$$\alpha = \frac{\Delta P_{valve,\,max}}{\Delta P_{valve,\,max} + \Delta P_{piping,\,max}}$$

For temperature control loops, an authority of **0.3 to 0.5** is recommended. If $\alpha$ is too low, the valve's throttling effectiveness is overwhelmed by piping/equipment pressure drop variations, leading to poor temperature control. If $\alpha$ is too high, the system wastes pumping/boiler energy.

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 – Module 2]**

**(a)** With a neat functional diagram, explain the **construction and working of a pneumatic diaphragm-actuated globe valve** used in a temperature control scheme. (7 marks)

**(b)** Derive the **stem displacement equation** for a pneumatic diaphragm actuator and show that for a 3–15 psig signal the displacement is **linear** in the controller output pressure. (7 marks)

### Model Solution

**(a) Construction and Working (7 marks)**

**Construction:**
A pneumatic diaphragm-actuated globe valve consists of two main assemblies:

1. **Actuator (Upper Assembly):** Contains a circular flexible diaphragm clamped between upper and lower diaphragm cases. A coiled spring sits below the diaphragm and pushes upward. A pneumatic signal line enters the top case.

2. **Valve Body (Lower Assembly):** A globe-shaped body with two ports (inlet and outlet) connected by an internal seat ring. A plug attached to a vertical stem mates with the seat. The stem passes through a packing box to prevent leakage.

**Working:**
- The controller sends a **3–15 psig** pneumatic signal to the top of the diaphragm.
- The air pressure pushes the diaphragm **downward** with force $F_{air} = P_{air} \times A_{d}$.
- The diaphragm's downward motion is transmitted through the stem to the plug.
- The plug lifts off the seat, allowing process fluid (steam / chilled water) to flow.
- The **spring force** opposes the motion; equilibrium is reached when $F_{air} = F_{spring}$.
- Higher controller output → more opening → more heating/cooling medium → temperature corrected.

**Functional Diagram:**

| Top (Air In) | → Diaphragm → Stem → Plug → Seat → Process Fluid |
|---|---|---|---|---|

**(b) Derivation of Stem Displacement (7 marks)**

Force balance on the diaphragm-stem-plug assembly:

$$P_{air} \cdot A_{d} = k \cdot x + F_{0}$$

where $F_0$ is the spring pre-load. At the lower end of the signal range, $P_{air} = 3$ psig and $x = 0$, so:

$$3\,\text{psig} \times A_{d} = F_{0}$$

Subtracting the two equations:

$$(P_{air} - 3\,\text{psig}) \cdot A_{d} = k \cdot x$$

$$\boxed{x = \frac{A_{d}}{k} \cdot (P_{air} - 3\,\text{psig})}$$

Since $A_d$ and $k$ are constants, $x$ is a **linear function** of $P_{air}$. For a 3–15 psig signal range, when $P_{air} = 15$ psig, the maximum displacement is:

$$x_{max} = \frac{A_{d}}{k} \times 12\,\text{psig}$$

This is the **rated total valve travel**. The relationship $x \propto P_{air}$ makes the pneumatic actuator behave as a **pure proportional device**, which is why it is preferred for temperature control.

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 – Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain with a diagram the operation of a **three-way mixing valve** in a temperature control scheme. (7 marks)

**(b)** A pneumatic temperature control valve is used to regulate hot water flow into a heat exchanger. The valve authority is **0.5**, the maximum process flow is **20 m³/h**, and the system total pressure drop at max flow is **4 bar**. Calculate the pressure drop across the valve and the required $C_v$ for the service. (7 marks)

### Model Solution

**(a) Three-Way Mixing Valve (7 marks)**

A three-way mixing valve has **three ports**:
- Port A: Hot water inlet
- Port B: Cold water inlet
- Port C: Mixed-water outlet to process

A single **pneumatic actuator** drives a shared plug that simultaneously restricts the hot port and opens the cold port (or vice versa). When the controller senses a low temperature, the plug moves to **increase hot-water flow** and **decrease cold-water flow**, raising the mixed temperature. Conversely, when the temperature is too high, the action reverses.

The advantage of a three-way valve in temperature control is that it provides **continuous blending** without two-way valve "mixing tee" energy waste, and it inherently **fails safe** in a defined position (e.g., port A closed on air failure).

**Functional Diagram:**

| Hot In → [Plug modulates] → Mixed Out → Process |
|---|---|
| Cold In → [Plug modulates opposite] → (joins above) |

**(b) Numerical Calculation (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Pressure drop across the valve:**

Using valve authority $\alpha = \dfrac{\Delta P_{valve}}{\Delta P_{system}}$:

$$\Delta P_{valve} = \alpha \cdot \Delta P_{system} = 0.5 \times 4\,\text{bar} = 2\,\text{bar}$$

**Step 2 — Convert flow to SI-compatible units:**

Maximum flow: $Q = 20\,\text{m}^3/\text{h}$

For water ($G = 1$, $\rho = 1000\,\text{kg/m}^3$), using the SI $K_v$ formula:

$$K_v = Q \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho}{\Delta P_{valve} \cdot 1000}} \quad \text{(with $\Delta P$ in kPa, $Q$ in m³/h)}$$

Convert 2 bar to kPa: $\Delta P = 200\,\text{kPa}$.

$$K_v = 20 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{1000}{200 \times 1000}} = 20 \times \sqrt{0.005}$$

$$K_v = 20 \times 0.0707 = 1.414\,\text{m}^3/\text{h}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to US $C_v$:**

$$C_v = \frac{K_v}{0.865} = \frac{1.414}{0.865} = 1.635\,\text{gpm}/\sqrt{\text{psi}}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta P_{valve} = 2\,\text{bar}, \quad C_v \approx 1.64, \quad K_v \approx 1.41}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert bar to kPa** in the $K_v$ formula → loses 2 marks.
> 2. **Writing $C_v$ instead of $K_v$** when the problem specifies SI units → loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Using $G$ (specific gravity) = 1 for water but forgetting** to convert density in the SI formula → loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Confusing "valve authority" with "valve gain"** — they are NOT the same. Authority is a static system property; gain is a dynamic sensitivity. The KTU board examiner will deduct 1–2 marks for this confusion.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam:**

- ☐ The valve in a temperature control scheme is the **Final Control Element (FCE)**.
- ☐ The most common type is the **pneumatically actuated diaphragm globe valve**.
- ☐ The standard pneumatic signal range is **3–15 psig** (0.2–1.0 bar).
- ☐ The diaphragm force equation: $F_{air} = P_{air} \cdot A_{d}$.
- ☐ The spring opposes: $F_{spring} = kx + F_0$.
- ☐ Equilibrium gives: $x = \dfrac{A_{d}}{k}(P_{air} - 3\,\text{psig})$.
- ☐ **Valve authority** $\alpha = \dfrac{\Delta P_{valve}}{\Delta P_{system}}$ should be 0.3–0.5.
- ☐ **$C_v$** is US units (gpm/√psi), **$K_v$** is SI units (m³/h/√bar); $K_v \approx 0.865 \, C_v$.
- ☐ A **three-way mixing valve** is preferred when blending hot and cold streams.
- ☐ A **valve positioner** is used to overcome stem-thrust non-linearities and pack friction.
- ☐ **Quick-opening trim** is for on–off temperature control; **linear trim** for proportional control; **equal-percentage trim** for high-turndown loops.
- ☐ **Self-operated temperature regulators** need no external power — they use a thermal-bulb filled with a volatile liquid.
- ☐ Always write **units** in the final numerical answer.
- ☐ For liquid service, the valve-sizing formula is $C_v = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{\sqrt{\Delta P \cdot \rho}}$.
- ☐ For steam service, the formula is corrected for specific volume: $C_v = \dfrac{\dot{m}}{K \cdot P_1 \cdot y}$ (consult the KTU steam table).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Temperature Response of an Uncontrolled Chemical Reactor to Step Changes in Load
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT743 - INDUSTRIAL PROCESS CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Temperature Response of an Uncontrolled Chemical Reactor to Step Changes in Load

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In **Industrial Process Control (KTU 2024 Scheme, PEICT743)**, the **Temperature Response of an Uncontrolled Chemical Reactor to Step Changes in Load** refers to the open-loop transient behaviour of the reactor temperature (T) when one or more **load (disturbance) variables** are suddenly changed by a step amount, while no feedback controller is acting on the system. The "load" in this context means the *upstream/downstream variables that are not manipulated* by the control loop, such as:

- **Feed concentration, $C_{A_0}$** (mol/L)
- **Feed temperature, $T_0$** (°C or K)
- **Coolant inlet temperature, $T_c$** (°C or K)
- **Feed volumetric flow rate, F** (m³/s)

The analysis is performed on a classic **Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor (CSTR)** carrying out an irreversible, exothermic, liquid-phase reaction of the form $A \rightarrow B$, governed by Arrhenius kinetics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This topic is the foundation for understanding **process dynamics, self-regulation, thermal runaway, and the need for feedback control**. Every chemical reactor in industry (petrochemical, polymer, pharmaceutical) is analysed through this lens before any controller is designed.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Pressure Cooker on a Stove" Picture

Imagine a **pressure cooker** sitting on a gas stove:
- The **fire** represents the *rate of heat generation* by the chemical reaction (it grows with temperature exponentially — Arrhenius).
- The **cooling water jacket** around the cooker represents *heat removal* (roughly proportional to $T - T_c$).
- The **potato** inside is the *reactant A* — as it cooks (reacts), it produces more steam (heat) but also shrinks (concentration drops).

Now, suddenly, someone either:
1. **Pours in more potato (step in $C_{A_0}$)** — more material to react → more heat,
2. **Pours in hotter water (step in $T_0$)** — direct energy input,
3. **Turns down the cold tap (step in $T_c$ upward)** — less cooling.

Without a thermostat (i.e., *uncontrolled*), what happens to the cooker's temperature? It may:
- **Stabilise** at a new higher temperature (well-behaved case), or
- **Run away to infinity** (thermal runaway) — the fire grows faster than the cooling can remove the heat, and the cooker explodes.

This is exactly the situation in an exothermic CSTR, and studying the **temperature trajectory $T(t)$** under these step loads is the central problem of this module.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants used in this topic:**
> - Universal gas constant: $R = 8.314 \; \text{J/(mol·K)}$
> - Activation energy: $E$ (typically $5 \times 10^4$ to $1.5 \times 10^5$ J/mol for industrial reactions)
> - Liquid density $\rho \approx 1000 \; \text{kg/m}^3$, $C_p \approx 4180 \; \text{J/(kg·K)}$ for aqueous systems
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Two Coupled Balances of a CSTR

The CSTR is described by **two coupled nonlinear ordinary differential equations**:

**(a) Component (Material) Balance on Reactant A:**

$$V \frac{dC_A}{dt} = F(C_{A_0} - C_A) - V k_0 \, e^{-E/(RT)} \, C_A$$

**(b) Overall Energy (Heat) Balance:**

$$V \rho C_p \frac{dT}{dt} = F \rho C_p (T_0 - T) + (-\Delta H) V k_0 e^{-E/(RT)} C_A - UA(T - T_c)$$

**Interpretation of each term (left to right):**

| Term | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|
| $V \frac{dC_A}{dt}$ | Rate of accumulation of A in the reactor |
| $F(C_{A_0}-C_A)$ | Net convective input of A |
| $Vk_0 e^{-E/RT}C_A$ | Rate of consumption of A by reaction |
| $V\rho C_p \frac{dT}{dt}$ | Rate of accumulation of thermal energy |
| $F\rho C_p(T_0-T)$ | Net convective energy in/out with feed |
| $(-\Delta H)VkC_A$ | Rate of heat **generation** by exothermic reaction |
| $UA(T-T_c)$ | Rate of heat **removal** by coolant jacket |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why are these two equations "coupled"?** The temperature $T$ appears inside the exponential Arrhenius rate, and the concentration $C_A$ appears as a multiplicative factor of the heat-generation term. Hence changing $C_A$ affects $T$, and changing $T$ affects $C_A$. They **must be solved simultaneously**.

### 2.2 Steady-State Operating Point

At steady state, all time derivatives are zero. Let steady-state values be denoted with subscript $s$. Then:

$$0 = F(C_{A_0,s} - C_{A,s}) - V k_s C_{A,s}$$

$$0 = F \rho C_p (T_{0,s} - T_s) + (-\Delta H) V k_s C_{A,s} - UA(T_s - T_{c,s})$$

where $k_s = k_0 e^{-E/(RT_s)}$.

These algebraic equations fix the operating point $(C_{A,s}, T_s)$ for a given set of load variables.

### 2.3 Linearised Deviation Model

Define small deviation variables (a cornerstone of KTU board problems):

$$C_A' = C_A - C_{A,s}, \quad T' = T - T_s, \quad C_{A_0}' = C_{A_0} - C_{A_0,s}, \quad T_0' = T_0 - T_{0,s}, \quad T_c' = T_c - T_{c,s}$$

Linearising the two balances around the steady state (the math is shown in Section 3) yields the **standard 2×2 first-order state-space model**:

$$\tau_m \frac{dC_A'}{dt} = -C_A' + K_{m,Ca0}\, C_{A_0}' + K_{m,T}\, T'$$

$$\tau_e \frac{dT'}{dt} = -T' + K_{e,Ca0}\, C_{A_0}' + K_{e,T0}\, T_0' + K_{e,Tc}\, T_c' + K_{e,T}\, T'$$

**Definitions of the gains and time constants:**

| Symbol | Formula | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| $\tau_m$ | $V/F$ | Material-balance residence time (time constant) |
| $\tau_e$ | $V\rho C_p / (F\rho C_p + UA)$ | Energy-balance thermal time constant |
| $K_{m,T}$ | $-(-\Delta H)V C_{A,s} \frac{E}{RT_s^2} \cdot \frac{V}{F} / V$ | Sensitivity of $C_A$ to $T$ (negative — higher $T$ consumes $A$ faster) |
| $K_{e,T}$ | $\beta T_s$ where $\beta = (-\Delta H)C_{A,s}\frac{E}{RT_s^2}\cdot\frac{V}{F\rho C_p}$ | Self-heating gain (positive — exothermic positive feedback) |
| $K_{e,Ca0}$ | $(-\Delta H) V \frac{F}{V}/(F\rho C_p + UA) \cdot V$ | Gain from feed concentration to temperature |
| $K_{e,T0}$ | $F\rho C_p/(F\rho C_p + UA)$ | Gain from feed temperature to reactor temperature |
| $K_{e,Tc}$ | $UA/(F\rho C_p + UA)$ | Gain from coolant temperature to reactor temperature |

> [!WARNING]
> **Stability Criterion (Critical for KTU):** The open-loop reactor is **asymptotically stable** if and only if:
> $$1 - K_{e,T} K_{m,T} > 0 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \text{heat removal slope} > \text{heat generation slope at } T_s$$
> If this fails, the reactor is **open-loop unstable** and exhibits **thermal runaway**.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $k = k_0 e^{-E/(RT)}$ | Arrhenius rate constant | 1/s |
| 2 | $V \frac{dC_A}{dt} = F(C_{A_0}-C_A) - V k C_A$ | Component material balance | mol/s |
| 3 | $V\rho C_p \frac{dT}{dt} = F\rho C_p(T_0-T) + (-\Delta H)VkC_A - UA(T-T_c)$ | Energy balance | W |
| 4 | $\tau_m = V/F$ | Material time constant | s |
| 5 | $\tau_e = V\rho C_p / (F\rho C_p + UA)$ | Energy time constant | s |
| 6 | $Q_{gen}(T) = (-\Delta H)V k_0 e^{-E/(RT)} C_{A,s}$ | Heat generation curve | W |
| 7 | $Q_{rem}(T) = F\rho C_p(T_0-T) + UA(T-T_c)$ | Heat removal line | W |
| 8 | $T(t) = T_s + \Delta T (1 - e^{-t/\tau_e})$ | First-order step response | °C or K |
| 9 | $\frac{dQ_{gen}}{dT} = Q_{gen}\frac{E}{RT^2}$ at $T_s$ | Slope of heat generation curve | W/K |
| 10 | $\frac{dQ_{rem}}{dT} = -(F\rho C_p + UA)$ | Slope of heat removal line | W/K |

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **Petrochemical crackers** (ethylene, propylene plants): Thermal runaway can rupture furnace tubes costing millions; dynamic analysis prevents this.
- **Polymerisation reactors** (PVC, polystyrene): Hot spots degrade product molecular weight; uncontrolled step changes in monomer feed ruin batches.
- **Pharma batch reactors**: FDA requires documented open-loop dynamics before approving the control strategy.
- **Nuclear reactor coolant loops**: Same mathematical skeleton; "load" is the heat from fission, and the analysis is virtually identical.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Steady-State Heat Curves (S-Q Diagram)

The **S-Q (Sustained-Quality / Heat) diagram** is essential for KTU board questions on thermal runaway.

**Step 1:** At steady state, set $dT/dt = 0$ in the energy balance. The reaction rate $k(T)$ varies, so for a given $C_{A,s}$:

$$Q_{gen}(T) \triangleq (-\Delta H) V k_0 e^{-E/(RT)} C_{A,s}$$

**Step 2:** This is a strongly **nonlinear, monotonically increasing, convex (S-shaped) function of T** due to the exponential. It is zero at $T = 0$ and shoots up rapidly for high $T$.

**Step 3:** The heat removal term, rearranged from the energy balance at steady state, gives:

$$Q_{rem}(T) \triangleq F\rho C_p (T - T_0) + UA(T - T_c)$$

This is a **straight line in T** with slope $(F\rho C_p + UA)$ and an intercept that depends on $T_0, T_c$.

**Step 4:** Steady states occur at the **intersections** of $Q_{gen}(T)$ and $Q_{rem}(T)$. Depending on the slopes, there can be **1, 2, or 3 intersections** (the famous "S-curve and line" problem).

**Step 5:** Compute the slope of the heat generation curve at the steady state by differentiating $Q_{gen}(T)$ with respect to $T$:

$$\frac{dQ_{gen}}{dT} = (-\Delta H) V C_{A,s} k_0 e^{-E/(RT)} \cdot \frac{E}{RT^2} = Q_{gen}(T_s) \cdot \frac{E}{R T_s^2}$$

**Step 6:** Slope of the heat removal line is constant:

$$\frac{dQ_{rem}}{dT} = F\rho C_p + UA$$

**Step 7:** A steady state is **locally stable** if heat removal can catch up with a small temperature perturbation, i.e.:

$$\frac{dQ_{rem}}{dT} \Big/ \frac{dQ_{gen}}{dT} > 1 \quad \text{at } T_s$$

This is the **slope condition for thermal stability**. In the multi-steady-state case (3 intersections), the middle one is always unstable and leads to **runaway**.

### 3.2 Response to a Step Change in Feed Concentration $\Delta C_{A_0}$

Assume the reactor is initially at steady state $(C_{A,s}, T_s)$. At $t = 0$, $C_{A_0}$ is stepped to $C_{A_0,s} + \Delta C_{A_0}$.

**Step 1 — Material balance response (assuming $T$ held constant initially):**
The material balance is a first-order ODE in $C_A$ with time constant $\tau_m$:

$$\frac{dC_A'}{dt} + \frac{1}{\tau_m} C_A' = \frac{\Delta C_{A_0}}{\tau_m}$$

Solution with $C_A'(0) = 0$:

$$C_A'(t) = \Delta C_{A_0} \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_m}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Energy balance response (decoupled for the analysis):**
Substituting the new $C_A(t)$ into the energy balance gives a forced first-order ODE:

$$\tau_e \frac{dT'}{dt} + T' = K_{e,Ca0} \, C_A'(t)$$

Substituting $C_A'(t)$ from Step 1 and solving (using integrating factor or Laplace), with $T'(0) = 0$:

$$T'(t) = K_{e,Ca0} \Delta C_{A_0} \left[1 - \frac{\tau_e \, e^{-t/\tau_m} - \tau_m \, e^{-t/\tau_e}}{\tau_e - \tau_m}\right]$$

**Step 3 — Final steady state of temperature:**
As $t \to \infty$:

$$T'(\infty) = K_{e,Ca0} \Delta C_{A_0}$$

**Step 4 — Two limiting cases:**

- **If $\tau_e \gg \tau_m$** (slow thermal response): the concentration reaches its new value quickly, then temperature rises slowly on the thermal time constant — a smooth first-order rise.
- **If $\tau_m \gg \tau_e$** (slow material response): temperature responds first to the transient reaction rate and then relaxes — possible **inverse response** (initial dip then rise) if the coupling gain $K_{e,T} \cdot K_{m,T}$ is large.

### 3.3 Response to a Step Change in Coolant Temperature $\Delta T_c$

**Step 1:** A step in $T_c$ does not affect the material balance at all (since $T_c$ enters only the energy balance). So $C_A$ remains at $C_{A,s}$.

**Step 2:** The energy balance becomes (with $C_A' = 0$):

$$\tau_e \frac{dT'}{dt} + T' = K_{e,Tc} \, \Delta T_c$$

where $K_{e,Tc} = UA / (F\rho C_p + UA)$.

**Step 3:** With $T'(0) = 0$:

$$T'(t) = K_{e,Tc} \, \Delta T_c \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_e}\right)$$

This is a **clean first-order response** with time constant $\tau_e$ and steady-state change $K_{e,Tc} \Delta T_c$. Notice that $0 < K_{e,Tc} < 1$, so the temperature moves *less* than the coolant.

### 3.4 Response to a Step Change in Feed Temperature $\Delta T_0$

The energy balance again becomes a simple first-order ODE (since $C_A$ is unaffected by $T_0$ in this linearised model):

$$\tau_e \frac{dT'}{dt} + T' = K_{e,T0} \, \Delta T_0$$

where $K_{e,T0} = F\rho C_p / (F\rho C_p + UA)$, so $K_{e,T0} + K_{e,Tc} = 1$.

**Step 3 — Solution:**

$$T'(t) = K_{e,T0} \, \Delta T_0 \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_e}\right)$$

### 3.5 Response to a Step Change in Feed Flow Rate $\Delta F$

This is the **most complex** case because $F$ appears in *both* balances. Linearising and solving the coupled 2×2 system in Laplace domain yields a **second-order response** in temperature with two time constants — typically one fast ($\tau_e$) and one slow ($\tau_m$). The exact solution:

$$T'(t) = A_1 e^{-t/\tau_1} + A_2 e^{-t/\tau_2} + T'(\infty)$$

where $\tau_1, \tau_2$ are the eigenvalues of the system matrix and are functions of $\tau_m, \tau_e, K_{e,T}, K_{m,T}$. Students must compute the characteristic polynomial:

$$(\tau_m s + 1)(\tau_e s + 1) - K_{e,T} K_{m,T} = 0$$

and solve for the roots $s = -1/\tau_1, -1/\tau_2$.

### 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

> **Problem (Illustrative):** A CSTR has $V = 1$ m³, $F = 0.01$ m³/s, $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $C_p = 4180$ J/(kg·K), $UA = 5000$ W/K, $T_c = 300$ K. Steady-state: $T_s = 350$ K. Compute $\tau_e$ and the response of $T$ to a step $\Delta T_c = +10$ K.

**Solution:**

$$\tau_e = \frac{V\rho C_p}{F\rho C_p + UA} = \frac{1 \cdot 1000 \cdot 4180}{0.01 \cdot 1000 \cdot 4180 + 5000} = \frac{4.18 \times 10^6}{41800 + 5000} = \frac{4.18 \times 10^6}{46800} \approx 89.3 \; \text{s}$$

$$K_{e,Tc} = \frac{UA}{F\rho C_p + UA} = \frac{5000}{46800} \approx 0.1068$$

$$T'(\infty) = 0.1068 \times 10 = 1.068 \; \text{K}$$

$$T(t) = 350 + 1.068 \left(1 - e^{-t/89.3}\right) \; \text{K}$$

At $t = \tau_e = 89.3$ s, $T \approx 350 + 0.632 \times 1.068 \approx 350.68$ K. **Answer in 3 significant figures.**
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — CSTR with Energy and Material Flows

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    Reactor["CSTR\nV, rho, Cp"]:::reactor

    CoolOut["Coolant Out"]:::cool

    FeedIn -- "F, Ca0, T0" --> Reactor
    Reactor -- "F, Ca, T" --> ProductOut
    CoolIn -- "Tc" --> Jacket
    Jacket -- "Tc + delta" --> CoolOut
    Jacket -- "Heat Transfer Q = UA T minus Tc" --> Reactor
    Reactor -. "State Variables" .-> MassBal
    Reactor -. "State Variables" .-> EnergyBal
    MassBal --> DynResp
    EnergyBal --> DynResp
```
