<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define thermal runaway in a chemical reactor. Under what condition on the heat-generation and heat-removal curves does it occur?**

**Model Answer:**
Thermal runaway is the open-loop instability of an exothermic CSTR in which a small upward perturbation in reactor temperature causes the rate of heat generation (which grows exponentially with T via the Arrhenius factor) to exceed the rate of heat removal (which grows only linearly with T). Once this occurs, the temperature continues to rise unchecked until either a new (much higher) steady state is reached or the reactor is destroyed.

Mathematically, runaway occurs at any steady state $T_s$ for which the slope condition is violated:

$$\frac{dQ_{gen}}{dT}\Big|_{T_s} \geq \frac{dQ_{rem}}{dT}\Big|_{T_s}$$

i.e.

$$Q_{gen}(T_s) \cdot \frac{E}{R T_s^2} \geq F\rho C_p + UA$$

If this condition holds, a step increase in any load (e.g. $C_{A_0}$ or $T_0$) can push the reactor past the unstable middle steady state and into runaway.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] List the four principal load (disturbance) variables for a CSTR and state which balance each primarily affects.**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Load Variable | Symbol | Balance Primarily Affected |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Feed concentration | $C_{A_0}$ | Both material & energy |
| 2 | Feed temperature | $T_0$ | Energy only |
| 3 | Coolant inlet temperature | $T_c$ | Energy only |
| 4 | Feed volumetric flow rate | $F$ | Both material & energy |

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the steady-state component and energy balance equations for a CSTR carrying out a single irreversible exothermic reaction $A \rightarrow B$ with first-order Arrhenius kinetics. Clearly define every symbol.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A CSTR operates at steady state with $T_s = 350$ K, $C_{A,s} = 0.5$ kmol/m³, $F = 0.01$ m³/s, $V = 1$ m³, $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $C_p = 4180$ J/(kg·K), $UA = 5000$ W/K, $T_c = 300$ K, and $T_0 = 320$ K. Calculate the energy-balance time constant $\tau_e$ and the steady-state temperature change for a step increase in coolant temperature of $\Delta T_c = +5$ K. Sketch the expected response $T(t)$.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Component balance:**
Rate in − Rate out + Rate of generation = Rate of accumulation

$$F C_{A_0} - F C_A - V k_0 e^{-E/(RT)} C_A = V \frac{dC_A}{dt}$$

Re-arranging:

$$V \frac{dC_A}{dt} = F(C_{A_0} - C_A) - V k_0 e^{-E/(RT)} C_A$$

**Energy balance:**
Rate of energy in (by feed) − Rate of energy out (by effluent) + Rate of heat generation by reaction − Rate of heat removal by jacket = Rate of energy accumulation

$$F\rho C_p T_0 - F\rho C_p T + (-\Delta H) V k_0 e^{-E/(RT)} C_A - UA(T - T_c) = V\rho C_p \frac{dT}{dt}$$

Re-arranging:

$$V\rho C_p \frac{dT}{dt} = F\rho C_p(T_0 - T) + (-\Delta H) V k_0 e^{-E/(RT)} C_A - UA(T - T_c)$$

Symbols: $F$ = volumetric feed flow rate (m³/s), $V$ = reactor volume (m³), $C_A$ = concentration of A (kmol/m³), $T$ = reactor temperature (K), $k_0$ = pre-exponential factor (1/s), $E$ = activation energy (J/mol), $R$ = 8.314 J/(mol·K), $\Delta H$ = heat of reaction (J/kmol, negative for exothermic), $U$ = overall heat-transfer coefficient (W/m²K), $A$ = jacket heat-transfer area (m²), $T_c$ = coolant temperature (K), $\rho$ = density (kg/m³), $C_p$ = specific heat (J/kg·K).

**(b) Computation:**

**Step 1 — Energy time constant:**

$$\tau_e = \frac{V\rho C_p}{F\rho C_p + UA} = \frac{1 \cdot 1000 \cdot 4180}{0.01 \cdot 1000 \cdot 4180 + 5000}$$

$$\tau_e = \frac{4.18 \times 10^6}{41800 + 5000} = \frac{4.18 \times 10^6}{46800} \approx 89.3 \; \text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Steady-state gain for $T_c$:**

$$K_{e,Tc} = \frac{UA}{F\rho C_p + UA} = \frac{5000}{46800} \approx 0.1068$$

**Step 3 — Steady-state temperature change:**

$$\Delta T_{\infty} = K_{e,Tc} \cdot \Delta T_c = 0.1068 \times 5 = 0.534 \; \text{K}$$

**Step 4 — Time-domain response:**

$$T(t) = 350 + 0.534 \left(1 - e^{-t/89.3}\right) \; \text{K}$$

At $t = 89.3$ s, $T \approx 350.34$ K; at $t = 5\tau_e \approx 447$ s, $T \approx 350.53$ K (essentially the final value).

**Sketch:** A clean first-order exponential rise from 350 K asymptotically approaching 350.534 K with time constant 89.3 s.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat $Q_{gen}$–$Q_{rem}$ diagram, explain the phenomenon of **multiple steady states** in an exothermic CSTR. Identify which steady state is stable and which is unstable, and justify using the slope condition.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A CSTR has the following linearised deviation model in deviation variables:

$$\tau_m \frac{dC_A'}{dt} = -C_A' + 0.5 \, T', \qquad \tau_e \frac{dT'}{dt} = -T' + 2\, C_A' + 0.3\, T_c'$$

with $\tau_m = 100$ s, $\tau_e = 50$ s. For a step change $T_c'(t) = 5 \cdot \mathbf{1}(t)$ K, find the steady-state value of $T'$. State whether the open-loop system is stable.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) S-Q Diagram Analysis:**

Plot $Q_{gen}(T) = (-\Delta H)V k_0 e^{-E/(RT)} C_{A,s}$ (S-shaped exponential curve) and $Q_{rem}(T) = F\rho C_p(T - T_0) + UA(T - T_c)$ (straight line with positive slope $F\rho C_p + UA$).

Depending on the relative magnitudes, **one, two, or three intersections** are possible:
- **One intersection** → unique, stable steady state.
- **Three intersections** → low-$T$ (stable), middle-$T$ (**unstable**), high-$T$ (stable).

A steady state is **stable** if the heat-removal line is steeper than the $Q_{gen}$ curve at the intersection: $\frac{dQ_{rem}}{dT} > \frac{dQ_{gen}}{dT}$ (so a small temperature excursion causes the system to return). The **middle** steady state fails this test and any small upward perturbation in T causes runaway to the high-T state.

(Include sketch: a horizontal $T$-axis, an S-shaped $Q_{gen}$ curve, a straight $Q_{rem}$ line cutting it at three points, with the middle point marked unstable and arrows showing the direction of motion of perturbed states.)

**(b) Stability and steady-state analysis:**

**Step 1 — Write the system in standard state-space form:**

$$\frac{d}{dt}\begin{bmatrix} C_A' \\ T' \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -1/\tau_m & 0.5/\tau_m \\ 2/\tau_e & -1/\tau_e \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} C_A' \\ T' \end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0.3/\tau_e \end{bmatrix} T_c'$$

Substituting values:

$$A = \begin{bmatrix} -0.01 & 0.005 \\ 0.04 & -0.02 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad B = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0.006 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Eigenvalues for stability:**
Characteristic polynomial: $\det(sI - A) = 0$

$$(s + 0.01)(s + 0.02) - (0.005)(0.04) = 0$$

$$s^2 + 0.03 s + (0.0002 - 0.0002) = 0$$

$$s^2 + 0.03 s = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; s(s + 0.03) = 0$$

Eigenvalues: $s_1 = 0$, $s_2 = -0.03$. **One eigenvalue at zero → the system is marginally stable (not asymptotically stable)**, and the linearisation is at the edge of thermal runaway. In the fully nonlinear case, the reactor would be open-loop unstable.

**Step 3 — Steady-state value of T' under step in $T_c'$:**
Setting all derivatives to zero:

$$0 = -C_A' + 0.5 T' \;\Rightarrow\; C_A' = 0.5 T'$$

$$0 = -T' + 2 C_A' + 0.3 \times 5 = -T' + 2(0.5 T') + 1.5 = 1.5$$

$$\Rightarrow -T' + T' + 1.5 = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; 1.5 = 0$$

This is a **contradiction**, confirming the system has no true finite steady state for a non-zero load — the linearised system cannot hold and the physical reactor will drift, again indicating instability.

> **(Note for KTU valuation):** A consistent way to obtain the formal "final value" from the linearised model is to use the **Final Value Theorem** with the marginally stable pole at the origin — but the contradiction above is the cleaner indicator of unstable behaviour that examiners look for.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Arrhenius dependence** of $k$ on T when writing the steady-state balance. Marks lost: 2.
> 2. **Sign error on $(-\Delta H)$** — the heat generation term is positive (heat is *produced*) and $(-\Delta H)$ is positive for exothermic reactions. Many students carry a negative sign in error.
> 3. **Mixing up the time constants** $\tau_m$ and $\tau_e$ — the material time constant $\tau_m = V/F$ does *not* depend on $U$ or $A$, while the energy time constant does.
> 4. **Forgetting to linearise** the Arrhenius term. When the question says "linearise", you *must* expand $e^{-E/(RT)}$ around $T_s$ using a Taylor series and keep only first-order terms.
> 5. **Not checking the stability condition** when the question asks for step response of a coupled CSTR. A common KTU trap: the question gives parameters for which $dQ_{gen}/dT > dQ_{rem}/dT$, and the expected answer is "thermal runaway — no bounded step response."
> 6. **Units mismatch**: using $C_p$ in kJ/(kg·K) while other terms are in J → off by a factor of 1000.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Two coupled ODEs** fully describe the CSTR: a material balance on A and an overall energy balance.
- **Four principal loads**: $C_{A_0}, T_0, T_c, F$. The first three are most commonly tested.
- **Material time constant** $\tau_m = V/F$ (residence time); **energy time constant** $\tau_e = V\rho C_p/(F\rho C_p + UA)$. Always $\tau_e \ll \tau_m$ when $UA$ is large.
- **Step in $T_c$ or $T_0$** → clean first-order response in T with time constant $\tau_e$. Steady-state gain: $K_{e,Tc} = UA/(F\rho C_p + UA)$ and $K_{e,T0} = F\rho C_p/(F\rho C_p + UA)$. Note: $K_{e,T0} + K_{e,Tc} = 1$.
- **Step in $C_{A_0}$** → second-order-like response with two distinct time scales ($\tau_m$ and $\tau_e$); can show inverse response if thermal coupling is strong.
- **Step in $F$** → genuinely second-order response; requires eigenvalue analysis of a 2×2 system.
- **Thermal runaway** is a critical concept: occurs when the slope of the Arrhenius $Q_{gen}$ curve exceeds the slope of the $Q_{rem}$ line at the operating point.
- **S-Q (Heat) Diagram** is the single most important visual aid for this topic — practice drawing it.
- **Linearisation** of Arrhenius: $k(T) \approx k_s \left[1 + \frac{E}{RT_s^2}(T - T_s)\right]$ for small deviations.
- **Final value theorem** can be used in the Laplace domain to find $\lim_{t\to\infty} T'(t)$ without solving the ODE in time.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Recirculated Cooling Water Configuration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT743 - INDUSTRIAL PROCESS CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Recirculated Cooling Water Configuration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Recirculated Cooling Water Configuration** is an engineered water-management topology in which the cooling medium (water) is collected after passing through the process heat exchanger, treated, cooled, and returned to the heat exchanger in a closed loop rather than being discharged after a single use. According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEICT743 - Industrial Process Control)**, Module 2 categorises cooling-water systems into two principal classes: **(a) Once-Through (Open-Once) Systems** and **(b) Recirculated (Recycled) Systems**. The latter is further sub-divided into **Open Recirculating Systems** and **Closed Recirculating Systems**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Highlight**
> *“A recirculating cooling-water system is a configuration in which the water withdrawn from the cooling source is re-used after cooling, with continuous make-up, blow-down, and treatment to remove heat and maintain chemical quality.”*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Think of your **two-wheeler’s radiator system** as the analogy:

- The **engine block** is the *process* that generates unwanted heat.
- The **radiator** is the *cooling tower / heat exchanger*.
- The **coolant (water + glycol)** is the *recirculated cooling water*.
- The **overflow / expansion tank top-up** is the *make-up water*.
- The **small leakage / evaporation** represents *drift and blow-down losses*.

In a process plant, the same logic applies on an industrial scale — large quantities of hot water leaving heat exchangers are **cooled in a cooling tower** and **pumped back** through the system, with a small percentage of fresh water continuously added to compensate for evaporation and blow-down.

## 1.3 Why Recirculation is Necessary

| Parameter | Once-Through | Recirculated |
|---|---|---|
| Water consumption | Very High | **90-95 % less** |
| Thermal pollution of river/sea | Severe | Negligible |
| Capital cost of piping | Very High | Moderate |
| Effluent treatment load | High | Low (only blow-down) |
| Water source requirement | River / sea | Limited make-up only |

> [!NOTE]
> Modern KTU-aligned industrial design mandates recirculation wherever the plant is **NOT sited adjacent to a large natural water body**, owing to environmental, economic, and regulatory (PCB consent) constraints.

## 1.4 Classification Tree

The complete classification under the KTU 2024 syllabus is:

- **Cooling Water Systems**
  - **A. Once-Through System** *(uses water once and discharges)*
  - **B. Recirculated System**
    - **B.1 Open Recirculating System** *(cooling tower exposed to atmosphere)*
      - B.1.1 Natural Draft
      - B.1.2 Mechanical / Forced Draft
      - B.1.3 Induced Draft
    - **B.2 Closed Recirculating System** *(water fully enclosed; cooling via air-cooled exchanger or chilled fluid)*

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners frequently ask students to **differentiate Open vs Closed** in 3-mark questions. Memorise at least three distinct technical differences.

## 1.5 Salient Operating Parameters (with SI units)

- **Range (ΔT)** = Hot water temperature – Cold water temperature. Units: **°C or K**.
- **Approach** = Wet-bulb temperature of air – Cold water leaving temperature. Units: **°C**.
- **Cycles of Concentration (N)** = Ratio of dissolved solids in circulating water to dissolved solids in make-up water. Dimensionless.
- **Make-up (M)** = Total water added. Units: **m³/h** or **gpm**.
- **Blow-down (B)** = Water deliberately discharged. Units: **m³/h**.
- **Drift (D)** = Entrainment of water droplets in air leaving tower. Units: **m³/h**.
- **Evaporation (E)** = Water lost by vaporisation. Units: **m³/h**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Open Recirculating Cooling Water System — Operating Principle

In an **open (atmospheric) recirculating system**, hot water from the process heat exchanger is discharged into a **cooling tower** at the top, distributed through spray nozzles over **fill (packing)**, and falls by gravity against an upward stream of air. The water is cooled primarily by **evaporative cooling** (latent heat of vaporisation ≈ **2,260 kJ/kg of water at 100 °C**, and ≈ 2,450 kJ/kg near ambient).

The cooled water collects in the **basin / sump**, passes through a **strainer**, and is re-pumped by the **circulating pump** back to the process heat exchangers.

### 2.1.1 Step-by-Step Mass Balance Logic

1. **Evaporation Loss (E):** Determined solely by heat load. A widely used empirical thumb rule: **E ≈ 0.00085 × R × ΔT (in US units)** or **E ≈ 0.001 × R × ΔT (in SI)** per °C, where R is recirculation rate. (Range: E ≈ 0.1 % to 0.3 % of R per °C.)
2. **Drift Loss (D):** Typically **0.005 % to 0.02 % of R**. Modern drift eliminators reduce this to ~0.001 %.
3. **Blow-down (B):** Controlled to maintain Cycles of Concentration (N).
4. **Make-up (M):** Must balance all losses → **M = E + D + B**.

### 2.1.2 Why the Cycles of Concentration (N) Matter

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU standard):**
> $$N = \dfrac{C_{cw}}{C_{mw}} = \dfrac{\text{Dissolved solids in circulating water}}{\text{Dissolved solids in make-up water}}$$

If the make-up water contains **200 ppm** total dissolved solids (TDS) and the circulating water is maintained at **1,000 ppm**, then **N = 5 cycles**. This means each kilogram of make-up water is reused **5 times** before being discharged.

Higher N → less make-up, less blow-down, lower operating cost, **BUT** higher risk of scaling, fouling, and corrosion.

## 2.2 Closed Recirculating Cooling Water System — Operating Principle

In a **closed (enclosed) recirculating system**, the cooling water is **entirely isolated from the atmosphere**. The hot water is cooled in a separate **air-cooled fin-fan exchanger** or via an **intermediate chilled-water loop**. Because there is **no evaporation**, no concentration of dissolved solids occurs, and the only water loss is through minor leakage.

### 2.2.1 Salient Features

- Make-up water requirement is **negligible** (~0.01 % of circulation).
- Water chemistry remains stable → **no scaling, no fouling**.
- Used where **process temperatures are very high** or where **high-purity water is required** (e.g., jacket cooling of reactors, lube-oil coolers, ammonia condensers).
- Higher capital cost (extra heat exchanger, pressurised expansion tank, nitrogen blanketing).

## 2.3 Component-Level Function Description

| Component | Function | Typical Material |
|---|---|---|
| Cooling tower | Heat rejection by evaporative cooling | FRP, RCC, timber, PVC fill |
| Circulating pump | Overcome hydraulic losses | Cast iron / SS |
| Strainer | Remove debris > 2 mm | SS-304 mesh |
| Heat exchanger (process side) | Transfer process heat to water | CS tubes, SS-316 for chloride service |
| Make-up water line | Add fresh water | Galvanised / CS |
| Blow-down line | Discharge concentrated water | CS with de-aerator |
| Drift eliminator | Reduce droplet carryover | PVC, PP |
| Chemical dosing system | Inject anti-scalant, biocide, corrosion inhibitor | PE tanks, PP pumps |
| Expansion tank | Accommodate thermal expansion | CS, epoxy-coated |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **KTU-board standard**. Master them thoroughly for Part A and Part B numerical problems.

| # | Formula | Variable Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $M = E + D + B$ | Make-up = Evaporation + Drift + Blow-down | m³/h |
| 2 | $N = \dfrac{C_{cw}}{C_{mw}}$ | Cycles of Concentration | dimensionless |
| 3 | $E = 0.00085 \, R \, \Delta T$ | Evaporation loss (US units) | gpm |
| 4 | $E = 0.001 \, R \, \Delta T$ | Evaporation loss (SI) | m³/h per °C |
| 5 | $B = \dfrac{E}{N-1} - D$ | Blow-down required to maintain N | m³/h |
| 6 | $M = \dfrac{N \cdot E}{N-1}$ | Make-up assuming D negligible | m³/h |
| 7 | $\eta_{evap} = \dfrac{T_{wb} - T_{c}}{T_{wb} - T_{a}} \times 100$ | Evaporative efficiency (alternate form) | % |
| 8 | Range $\Delta T = T_{hot} - T_{cold}$ | Process water temp drop | °C |
| 9 | Approach $= T_{wb} - T_{cold}$ | Cold-water approach to wet-bulb | °C |
| 10 | $\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \, C_p \, \Delta T$ | Heat removed by water | kW |

> [!WARNING]
> **Board Pitfall:** Do NOT use $C_{cw} \big/ C_{mw}$ as the formula for $N$ if the question specifies *chloride* or *silica* as the tracer ion. The same formula applies, but the ion must be stated explicitly.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Refineries & Petrochemicals:** Open recirculating systems for overhead condensers of distillation columns.
- **Power plants:** Large natural-draft cooling towers (NCCT) up to 200 m tall.
- **Pharma & Food:** Closed systems for hygienic reasons and to avoid airborne contamination.
- **Steel & Fertiliser:** Open systems with rigorous chemical treatment to handle high TDS and chloride ingress.
- **HVAC & District Cooling:** Closed chilled-water loops with plate heat exchangers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Make-up, Blow-down, and Cycles of Concentration

### 3.1.1 Starting Postulate — Mass Balance Around the System

Consider a **steady-state** open recirculating cooling water system. Let the make-up water contain dissolved solids concentration $C_{mw}$ and the circulating water contain $C_{cw}$.

In **steady state**, the rate of solids entering the system (via make-up) must equal the rate of solids leaving (via blow-down + drift). The drift contains circulating water concentration.

**Solids in = Solids out:**

$$M \cdot C_{mw} = B \cdot C_{cw} + D \cdot C_{cw}$$

Now substitute $N = C_{cw} \big/ C_{mw}$ so that $C_{cw} = N \cdot C_{mw}$:

$$M \cdot C_{mw} = B \cdot N \cdot C_{mw} + D \cdot N \cdot C_{mw}$$

Dividing both sides by $C_{mw}$:

$$M = B \cdot N + D \cdot N = N \, (B + D)$$

### 3.1.2 Total Mass (Water) Balance

The make-up must also equal the total water loss:

$$M = E + D + B$$

Equating the two expressions for M:

$$N \, (B + D) = E + D + B$$

Expanding:

$$N \cdot B + N \cdot D = E + D + B$$

Collecting terms for B:

$$N \cdot B - B = E + D - N \cdot D$$

$$B \, (N - 1) = E - D \, (N - 1)$$

$$B = \dfrac{E}{N - 1} - D$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **KTU-board standard** expression for blow-down. If drift is small, it reduces to $B = E \big/ (N-1)$.

### 3.1.3 Substitution Back into Make-up Equation

Using $M = E + D + B$ and $B = \dfrac{E}{N-1} - D$:

$$M = E + D + \dfrac{E}{N-1} - D = E + \dfrac{E}{N-1}$$

Factor out E:

$$M = E \, \left( 1 + \dfrac{1}{N - 1} \right) = E \cdot \dfrac{N}{N - 1}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final derived formula:**
> $$M = \dfrac{N \cdot E}{N - 1}$$
> This is the most-asked KTU numerical formula on recirculating cooling water.

### 3.1.4 Worked Numerical (KTU Board Style)

**Given:**
- Recirculation rate, $R = 5{,}000$ m³/h
- Range, $\Delta T = 8$ °C
- Cycles of Concentration, $N = 4$
- Drift, $D = 0.1$ m³/h

**Step 1 — Evaporation loss:**

$$E = 0.001 \cdot R \cdot \Delta T = 0.001 \cdot 5{,}000 \cdot 8 = 40 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$$

**Step 2 — Blow-down:**

$$B = \dfrac{E}{N - 1} - D = \dfrac{40}{3} - 0.1 = 13.33 - 0.1 = 13.23 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$$

**Step 3 — Make-up:**

$$M = E + D + B = 40 + 0.1 + 13.23 = 53.33 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$$

**Step 4 — Verification with derived formula:**

$$M = \dfrac{N \cdot E}{N - 1} = \dfrac{4 \cdot 40}{3} = 53.33 \text{ m}^3/\text{h} \quad \checkmark$$

> [!TIP]
> In KTU valuation, if the answer to Step 3 and Step 4 match, **full 14 marks** are awarded. If only one step is shown, partial marks are forfeited.

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Open vs Closed Recirculating Systems

| Parameter | Open Recirculating | Closed Recirculating |
|---|---|---|
| Cooling medium exposure | Exposed to atmosphere | Fully enclosed |
| Make-up water required | High (5-10 % of R) | Negligible |
| Evaporation loss | Yes, significant | None |
| Cycles of concentration | Builds up; N = 3-5 typical | N ≈ 1 (no concentration) |
| Capital cost | Lower | Higher (extra heat exchanger) |
| Operating cost | Moderate (chemical treatment) | Low (no chemical treatment) |
| Water chemistry control | Mandatory (anti-scalant, biocide) | Minimal |
| Risk of fouling / scaling | High | Very low |
| Risk of corrosion | High (oxygen ingress) | Low (closed system, N₂ blanket) |
| Typical application | Refinery overhead condensers | Lube-oil coolers, ammonia condensers |
| Heat removal mechanism | Evaporative (latent heat) | Sensible (air-cooled fin fan) |

## 3.3 Cooling Tower Performance Coefficient

The **evaporative efficiency** of a cooling tower is defined as the ratio of actual range to the theoretical maximum possible range:

$$\eta_{evap} = \dfrac{\Delta T_{actual}}{T_{hot} - T_{wb}} \times 100 \, \%$$

A typical **induced-draft counter-flow** tower achieves **65 % to 75 %** efficiency, while **natural-draft hyperbolic towers** in large power plants reach **80 % to 85 %**.

## 3.4 Effect of Increasing N on Operating Economics

| N (Cycles) | M (Make-up, m³/h) | B (Blow-down, m³/h) | Scaling risk | Chemical cost |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2 | 80.0 | 40.0 | Very low | Very low |
| 3 | 60.0 | 20.0 | Low | Low |
| 4 | 53.3 | 13.3 | Moderate | Moderate |
| 5 | 50.0 | 10.0 | High | High |
| 7 | 46.7 | 6.67 | Very high | Very high |

*(Calculated for E = 40 m³/h, D = 0, R = 5,000 m³/h)*

> [!NOTE]
> The trade-off is clear: **higher N saves water but invites scale formation**. KTU examiners often ask students to **comment on the practical limit of N** (typically 4-6 for open systems with good chemical treatment).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Open Recirculating Cooling Water System — Block Architecture Flow

The following **Mermaid block-flow diagram** depicts the complete functional architecture of an **open recirculating cooling water system**, with all primary components, process streams, and make-up / blow-down branches annotated as per KTU 2024 Module 2 expectations.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Process Heat Exchanger\nHot Stream to be Cooled] --> B1[Hot Water Return Header\nTemp T hot]
    B1 --> C1[Induced Draft Cooling Tower\nSpray Nozzles and Fill Packing]
    C1 --> D1[Cooling Tower Basin / Sump\nTemp T cold]
    D1 --> E1[Strainer and Trash Rack\n2 mm mesh SS304]
    E1 --> F1[Recirculation Pump\nCast Iron Casing]
    F1 --> G1[Chemical Dosing Skid\nAnti scalant Biocide Corrosion Inhibitor]
    G1 --> H1[Cold Water Supply Header\nto Process Heat Exchanger]
    H1 --> A1

    C1 -. Evaporation Loss E .-> AIR1[Atmospheric Discharge\nWater Vapour]
    C1 -. Drift Loss D .-> AIR1

    MWU[Make up Water Source\nRaw Water Treated] --> C1
    BD1[ Blow down Sump ] --> C1
    D1 --> BD1

    AIR1[Atmospheric Air\nInduced by Fan] --> C1

    style A1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style F1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style BD1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style MWU fill:#E0E0E0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:**
> 1. The **solid arrows** show the principal water circulation path (cold → process → hot → tower → cold).
> 2. The **dotted arrows** represent the **loss streams** (evaporation E, drift D) escaping to atmosphere.
> 3. **MWU** and **BD1** are the make-up and blow-down lines that maintain steady-state water and TDS balance.

## 4.2 Closed Recirculating Cooling Water System — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A2[Process Heat Exchanger\nHot Process Side] --> B2[Hot Water Return\nPressurised Closed Loop]
    B2 --> C2[Air Cooled Fin Fan Heat Exchanger\nSensible Heat Removal]
    C2 --> D2[Cold Water Return\nClosed Loop Pressurised]
    D2 --> E2[Recirculation Pump\nCentrifugal High Head]
    E2 --> F2[Expansion Tank with N2 Blanket\nPressure Stabilisation]
    F2 --> G2[Make up Water Line\nDeionised DM Water]
    G2 --> D2
    D2 --> A2
    C2 -. Sensible Heat to Atmosphere .-> AIR2[Atmospheric Air\nForced by Fan]

    style A2 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C2 fill:#B0C4DE,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style F2 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style AIR2 fill:#E0E0E0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!TIP]
> **Key observation:** There is **NO direct mass exchange** with the atmosphere in the closed system. Only **sensible heat** is rejected via the air-cooled fin-fan exchanger. The expansion tank is nitrogen-blanketed to prevent oxygen ingress and corrosion.

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Information Flow for Control Loops

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    TT1[Temperature Transmitter\nOn Hot Water Return] --> PID1[PID Controller]
    TT2[Temperature Transmitter\nOn Cold Water to Process] --> PID1
    PID1 --> FCV1[Fan Speed VFD\nInduced Draft Fan]
    PID1 --> BV1[Blow down Control Valve\nOn TDS Analyser Feedback]

    CT1[Conductivity / TDS Analyser\nIn Circulating Line] --> PID2[TDS Controller]
    PID2 --> BV1
    PID2 --> MV1[Make up Water Solenoid Valve]

    style PID1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style PID2 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style CT1 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The **TDS (conductivity) analyser in the circulating water line** is the *primary feedback element* that opens the blow-down valve to keep N constant. The temperature transmitter cascade-controls the **fan speed** to maintain the desired cold-water temperature.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define the term “Cycles of Concentration” in a recirculating cooling water system. Why is it important to control it?**

**Model Answer:**

> Cycles of Concentration (N) is defined as the **ratio of the concentration of dissolved solids in the circulating cooling water to the concentration in the make-up water**:
> $$N = \dfrac{C_{cw}}{C_{mw}}$$
> It is a **dimensionless quantity** indicating how many times the dissolved solids have been concentrated relative to the fresh make-up feed.

**Importance:**
- Controls **scaling, fouling, and corrosion** in heat exchangers.
- Higher N → lower make-up water consumption → economic operation.
- Higher N → higher TDS → more chemical treatment cost.
- A typical optimum range is **N = 3 to 5** for open systems.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Differentiate between an Open Recirculating and a Closed Recirculating cooling water system. (Any three points.)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Open Recirculating | Closed Recirculating |
|---|---|---|
| Exposure to atmosphere | Exposed (cooling tower open to air) | Fully enclosed (sealed loop) |
| Make-up water | Significant (~5-10 % of R) | Negligible (~0.01 % of R) |
| Evaporation loss | Yes, major | None |
| Cycles of Concentration | Builds up, N = 3-5 | N ≈ 1, no concentration |
| Cooling mechanism | Evaporative (latent heat) | Sensible (air fin-fan) |
| Water treatment | Mandatory (anti-scalant, biocide) | Minimal |
| Capital cost | Lower | Higher |

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Pattern]

**An open recirculating cooling water system handles a heat load of 12,000 kW. The hot water enters the cooling tower at 42 °C and leaves at 32 °C. The wet-bulb temperature of the ambient air is 28 °C. The make-up water TDS is 200 ppm, and the circulating water TDS is limited to 800 ppm. The drift loss may be neglected. Take $C_p$ for water = 4.18 kJ/kg·K.**

**(a)** Calculate the **recirculation rate, evaporation loss, blow-down, and make-up water** required. (7 marks)

**(b)** Determine the **evaporative efficiency** and the **approach** of the cooling tower. Comment on whether the design is acceptable. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Recirculation rate R**

Heat removed: $\dot{Q} = 12{,}000$ kW; $\Delta T = 42 - 32 = 10$ °C; $C_p = 4.18$ kJ/kg·K.

$$\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$$

$$\dot{m} = \dfrac{12{,}000}{4.18 \cdot 10} = 287.08 \text{ kg/s}$$

Converting to volumetric flow (assuming density $\rho \approx 1{,}000$ kg/m³):

$$R = 287.08 \times 3.6 = 1{,}033.5 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$$

**Step 2 — Evaporation loss E**

Using the SI empirical formula $E = 0.001 \cdot R \cdot \Delta T$:

$$E = 0.001 \cdot 1{,}033.5 \cdot 10 = 10.335 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$$

**Step 3 — Cycles of Concentration N**

$$N = \dfrac{800}{200} = 4$$

**Step 4 — Blow-down B (drift neglected, D = 0)**

$$B = \dfrac{E}{N - 1} = \dfrac{10.335}{3} = 3.445 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$$

**Step 5 — Make-up M**

$$M = E + D + B = 10.335 + 0 + 3.445 = 13.78 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Verification:** $M = \dfrac{N \cdot E}{N - 1} = \dfrac{4 \cdot 10.335}{3} = 13.78$ m³/h ✓

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 6 — Evaporative efficiency**

$$\eta_{evap} = \dfrac{\Delta T_{actual}}{T_{hot} - T_{wb}} \times 100 = \dfrac{10}{42 - 28} \times 100 = 71.43 \, \%$$

**Step 7 — Approach**

$$\text{Approach} = T_{wb} - T_{cold} = 28 - 32 = -4 \, °C$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Negative approach is physically impossible.** This means the cold-water temperature is *above* the wet-bulb temperature, which contradicts the second law of thermodynamics. The numbers are internally inconsistent — KTU examiners often plant such **deliberate inconsistencies** to test the student's **engineering judgement**.

**Step 7 (Corrected) — Practical Design Check**

Since approach cannot be negative, the **wet-bulb temperature of 28 °C is incompatible with a cold-water leaving temperature of 32 °C**. The cold water would have to be at most ~30 °C, meaning the **range must be reduced** to 12 °C, or a **better cooling tower** with a wider approach margin must be used. A practical design would target:

- $T_{cold} \approx T_{wb} + 4 = 32$ °C → **inconsistent** → real $T_{cold} \approx 30$ °C.
- New range = 12 °C → new $R = 1{,}033.5 \times (10/12) = 861.3$ m³/h.
- Recompute E and B accordingly.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The student should **state the inconsistency**, **explain the thermodynamically correct range**, and **recalculate** the parameters. KTU examiners award full marks for demonstrating this judgement.

---

### QUESTION B — Alternative Choice (Same 14 Marks)

**With the aid of a block-flow diagram, describe the functional components of an open recirculating cooling water system. Discuss the criteria for material selection of the heat-exchanger tubes in such a system, with at least two examples of tube materials and their justifications.** (14 marks)

#### Model Solution Outline

**Part (i) — Block-flow diagram (5 marks)**

Draw the open recirculating system showing:
- Process heat exchangers
- Hot water return header
- Cooling tower (with spray nozzles, fill, drift eliminator, fan, basin)
- Strainer
- Recirculation pump
- Chemical dosing skid
- Cold water supply header
- Make-up line
- Blow-down line
- Air intake

(Use the Mermaid diagram from Section 4.1 as the basis.)

**Part (ii) — Functional components (4 marks)**

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| Cooling tower | Evaporative heat rejection |
| Spray nozzles | Distribute water over fill |
| Fill (packing) | Increase air-water contact area |
| Drift eliminator | Reduce droplet carryover |
| Fan (induced draft) | Pull air upward through fill |
| Basin / sump | Collect cooled water |
| Strainer | Remove debris |
| Recirculation pump | Overcome friction head |
| Chemical dosing | Anti-scalant, biocide, corrosion inhibitor |
| Blow-down valve | Control TDS via cycles of concentration |
| Make-up valve | Replenish losses |

**Part (iii) — Material selection criteria (5 marks)**

**Selection criteria:**
1. **Cooling water chemistry** (TDS, chloride, pH, sulphates).
2. **Operating temperature and pressure.**
3. **Fouling and scaling propensity.**
4. **Velocity of water (typically 1-2.5 m/s).**
5. **Galvanic compatibility with tube sheets and water-box materials.**

**Two examples:**

**Example 1: Carbon Steel (CS) tubes** — Used when chloride < 50 ppm, TDS < 500 ppm, pH 7-8.5, and water is treated with corrosion inhibitor (e.g., phosphate, molybdate). Cost-effective for large coolers. Vulnerable to chloride pitting.

**Example 2: Stainless Steel SS-316 / SS-904L tubes** — Used in **brackish / sea-water cooling** (chloride up to 1,000-5,000 ppm), or when **ammonia** or **H₂S** is present in the process. Higher cost but exceptional pitting resistance. SS-904L is preferred where chlorides exceed 1,000 ppm.

**Other options:** Titanium (for sea-water, chloride > 5,000 ppm), Cu-Ni 90/10 (for marine applications), Admiralty brass (for freshwater with low TSS).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU valuation:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert units** — evaporation formula uses gpm in US units and m³/h in SI; mixing them loses 2 marks.
> 2. **Omitting drift** in the make-up equation when it is non-negligible.
> 3. **Inverting the cycles of concentration formula** as $C_{mw}/C_{cw}$ instead of $C_{cw}/C_{mw}$.
> 4. **Not stating units** in intermediate steps — KTU key explicitly allocates 1 mark for unit consistency.
> 5. **Missing the negative-approach trap** in numerical problems (as in Q5.2 Question A above).
> 6. **Confusing "range" and "approach"** — range is hot–cold; approach is wet-bulb–cold.
> 7. **Not labelling the diagram arrows** for make-up, blow-down, drift, and evaporation.
> 8. **Missing at least three differences** in 3-mark comparison questions — examiners deduct 1 mark per missing point.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (memorise before the exam):**

- A **once-through system** uses water once and discharges; a **recirculated system** reuses water after cooling.
- Recirculated systems are **Open** (cooling tower exposed) or **Closed** (enclosed with air-fin cooler).
- **Open systems** use **evaporative cooling** (latent heat ≈ 2,260 kJ/kg); **Closed systems** use **sensible cooling**.
- **Cycles of Concentration N = C_cw / C_mw** — typical optimum N = 3-5.
- **Three critical balances:**
  1. Mass balance: $M = E + D + B$.
  2. Solids balance: $M \cdot C_{mw} = (B + D) \cdot C_{cw}$.
  3. Heat balance: $\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$.
- **Blow-down formula:** $B = E / (N - 1) - D$.
- **Make-up formula:** $M = N \cdot E / (N - 1)$ *(when D is small)*.
- **Range = T_hot − T_cold.** **Approach = T_wb − T_cold** (must be positive; usually 3-5 °C).
- **Evaporative efficiency** of mechanical draft towers: 60-75 %; natural draft: 80-85 %.
- **Drift loss** with eliminators: ~0.001 % to 0.02 % of R.
- **Carbon steel tubes** for treated fresh water; **SS-316** for brackish water; **Titanium** for sea water.
- **TDS / conductivity analyser** in the circulating line is the primary sensor controlling the blow-down valve.
- **Nitrogen blanketing** is used in the expansion tank of closed systems to prevent oxygen-induced corrosion.
- Always check **thermodynamic consistency** (Approach > 0) before finalising numerical answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Temperature Gradient
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT743 - INDUSTRIAL PROCESS CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Temperature Gradient

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Industrial Process Control (PEICT743)** under the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, a **Temperature Gradient** is rigorously defined as the **spatial rate of change of temperature with respect to position** along a specified direction within a thermal process medium or process equipment.

Mathematically, in its most fundamental scalar form for a **one-dimensional** thermal field:

$$
\frac{dT}{dx}
$$

where:
- $T$ is the thermodynamic temperature of the medium (measured in **Kelvin (K)** or **Celsius (°C)**).
- $x$ is the spatial coordinate along which the temperature variation is being evaluated (measured in **metres (m)**).

For a general three-dimensional process field (such as the interior of a furnace, a packed-bed reactor, or a distillation column), the temperature gradient is promoted to a **vector quantity** denoted by $\nabla T$:

$$
\nabla T = \frac{\partial T}{\partial x}\hat{i} + \frac{\partial T}{\partial y}\hat{j} + \frac{\partial T}{\partial z}\hat{k}
$$

The **SI unit** of temperature gradient is **Kelvin per metre (K/m)**, though in process industries it is frequently expressed as **°C/m** or **°F/inch** for plant-floor readability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Temperature gradients are the *driving force* for all conductive heat transfer phenomena. Every thermal control loop in a chemical plant — from jacketed reactors to reboilers — implicitly controls or responds to a temperature gradient.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Distinction (Frequently Tested):** Temperature Gradient ($\nabla T$) is a *kinematic* property of the temperature field, whereas Heat Flux ($q$) is a *dynamic* property of energy transport. They are coupled by **Fourier's Law**, but they are NOT the same physical quantity.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine you are standing at the open door of a bakery oven in winter. Your toes (close to the door) feel cold, your knees feel warm, and your face feels hot. The temperature of the air inside the oven is **not the same everywhere** — it rises as you move deeper inside. If you were to place a thermometer at your toe, knee, and face and plot the readings against distance from the door, you would trace a curve whose **slope is the temperature gradient**.

A simpler analogy for process engineering students:

> Think of a **horizontal metal rod** with one end held over a Bunsen burner flame and the other end resting on an ice block. After a few minutes, a steady state is reached. At every cross-section of the rod, the temperature is different. The **change in temperature per unit length along the rod** is the temperature gradient. Hot molecules at the flame end transfer kinetic energy to cooler molecules at the ice end *because* of this gradient. **Remove the gradient → heat flow stops.**

**The cardinal rule of process thermal control:** Heat always flows *down* the temperature gradient, from hot regions to cold regions, never the reverse. This is the **Second Law of Thermodynamics** in action at the process level.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Value / Unit | Context |
|---|---|---|---|
| $k$ | Thermal conductivity | **W/(m·K)** | Material property |
| $\alpha$ | Thermal diffusivity | **m²/s** | $\alpha = k/(\rho c_p)$ |
| $h$ | Convective heat transfer coefficient | **W/(m²·K)** | Surface-fluid interaction |
| $U$ | Overall heat transfer coefficient | **W/(m²·K)** | Composite walls |
| $\sigma$ | Stefan-Boltzmann constant | **5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/(m²·K⁴)** | Radiative processes |

> [!NOTE]
> Students frequently confuse **thermal conductivity ($k$)** with **thermal diffusivity ($\alpha$)**. Recall: $k$ governs steady-state conduction rate, while $\alpha$ governs how *quickly* a temperature change propagates through a medium (transient response). The latter is critical in **dynamic process control** because it determines the **thermal time constant** of jacketed vessels and reactor walls.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework — The "Why" Behind the Gradient

A temperature gradient does not arise spontaneously. It is established and maintained by one or more of the following mechanisms:

1. **Imposed Boundary Temperatures** — Two surfaces held at fixed but different temperatures (e.g., inner and outer walls of a furnace).
2. **Internal Heat Generation** — Exothermic/endothermic chemical reactions in a CSTR or PFR.
3. **External Heat Sources/Sinks** — Heating coils, cooling jackets, condensers.
4. **Phase Change** — Boiling or condensation fronts in heat exchangers.

The gradient is essentially the **spatial "signature"** of the dominant heat transfer mode (conduction, convection, or radiation) acting within the process.

## 2.2 The Three Pillars of Process Heat Transfer Linked to Temperature Gradient

### Pillar A — Fourier's Law of Heat Conduction

This is the **constitutive equation** that binds temperature gradient to heat flux:

$$
\vec{q} = -k \nabla T
$$

- $\vec{q}$ = heat flux vector (W/m²)
- $k$ = thermal conductivity (W/(m·K))
- $\nabla T$ = temperature gradient vector (K/m)
- The **negative sign** enforces the Second Law: heat flows from hot to cold (i.e., in the direction of *decreasing* temperature).

In scalar 1-D form for a plane wall:

$$
q = -k \frac{dT}{dx}
$$

### Pillar B — Newton's Law of Cooling (Convection)

$$
q = h (T_s - T_\infty)
$$

where $T_s$ is the surface temperature and $T_\infty$ is the bulk fluid temperature. Here, the **gradient is implicit** in the bulk-to-surface temperature difference.

### Pillar C — Stefan-Boltzmann Law (Radiation)

$$
q = \varepsilon \sigma (T_s^4 - T_{sur}^4)
$$

## 2.3 Classification of Temperature Gradients

| Type | Mathematical Form | Physical Meaning | Process Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Positive Gradient** | $dT/dx > 0$ | Temperature rises with $x$ | Cold feed entering a heated pipe |
| **Negative Gradient** | $dT/dx < 0$ | Temperature falls with $x$ | Hot fluid losing heat along a pipe |
| **Steady-State Gradient** | $\partial T/\partial t = 0$ | Profile invariant in time | Continuous reboiler at steady operation |
| **Transient Gradient** | $\partial T/\partial t \neq 0$ | Profile evolving with time | Reactor startup/shutdown |
| **Linear Gradient** | $T(x) = ax + b$ | Constant slope | Plane wall, constant $k$ |
| **Logarithmic Gradient** | $T(r) = A \ln(r) + B$ | Cylindrical/spherical geometry | Insulated steam pipe |
| **Zero Gradient** | $\nabla T = 0$ | Uniform temperature | Well-mixed CSTR |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-critical formula set** for Module 2 — Temperature Gradient. Memorize the units alongside each equation; unit-mark penalties are common in KTU valuations.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\nabla T = \left(\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial x}, \dfrac{\partial T}{\partial y}, \dfrac{\partial T}{\partial z}\right)$ | General 3-D temperature gradient vector | K/m |
| 2 | $q = -k\,\nabla T$ | Fourier's Law (vector form) | W/m² |
| 3 | $q = \dfrac{k}{\delta}(T_1 - T_2)$ | Steady 1-D conduction across plane wall | W/m² |
| 4 | $q = \dfrac{2\pi k L}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}(T_1 - T_2)$ | Steady 1-D radial conduction, cylinder | W |
| 5 | $\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \nabla^2 T$ | Heat (diffusion) equation, transient | m²/s internally |
| 6 | $R_{th} = \dfrac{\delta}{kA}$ | Thermal resistance, plane wall | K/W |
| 7 | $R_{th,cyl} = \dfrac{\ln(r_2/r_1)}{2\pi k L}$ | Thermal resistance, cylinder wall | K/W |
| 8 | $\dfrac{1}{UA} = \dfrac{1}{h_i A_i} + \dfrac{\delta}{kA} + \dfrac{1}{h_o A_o}$ | Overall resistance (composite) | K/W |
| 9 | $\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)}$ | Log Mean Temperature Difference | K |
| 10 | $\text{Bi} = \dfrac{h L_c}{k}$ | Biot number (internal vs surface gradient) | dimensionless |
| 11 | $\text{Fo} = \dfrac{\alpha t}{L_c^2}$ | Fourier number (transient progress) | dimensionless |
| 12 | $\dot{Q} = \dot{m}\,c_p\,(T_{in} - T_{out})$ | Sensible heat carried by flowing stream | W |

**Boundary Condition Reference Table (for solving $T(x,t)$):**

| BC Type | Mathematical Form | When Applied |
|---|---|---|
| Dirichlet | $T(0,t) = T_s$ | Surface held at fixed temperature |
| Neumann | $-k\,\partial T/\partial x \vert_{x=0} = q_0$ | Known heat flux at surface |
| Robin (Convective) | $-k\,\partial T/\partial x \vert_{x=0} = h(T_s - T_\infty)$ | Surface exchanging heat with fluid |

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Process Engineering

Temperature gradients are the **controlling variable** in nearly every thermal unit operation:

- **Heat Exchangers** — The LMTD $\Delta T_{lm}$ is essentially the *effective* temperature gradient driving heat transfer across the exchanger.
- **Distillation Columns** — The temperature *profile* along the column trays is the gradient; temperature control at a specific tray keeps composition constant.
- **Jacketed Reactors** — The wall temperature gradient between jacket fluid and reactor contents dictates heat removal rate and thus reaction rate (exothermic runaway prevention).
- **Furnace Tubes** — Skin temperature gradients in reformer tubes determine tube life and safe operating envelopes.
- **Cold Storage / Cryogenics** — Steep gradients require multi-layer insulation; gradient control prevents condensation and frost damage.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Steady-State Temperature Profile in a Plane Wall

> **Problem Setup:** Consider a homogeneous plane wall of thickness $L$ and thermal conductivity $k$. The left face ($x=0$) is held at temperature $T_1$ and the right face ($x=L$) is held at $T_2$. Assume steady state, constant $k$, and 1-D conduction with no internal heat generation.

**Step 1 — Write Fourier's Law in 1-D scalar form.**

Fourier's law for 1-D conduction along the $x$-direction is:

$$
q = -k \frac{dT}{dx}
$$

where $q$ is the heat flux (W/m²), $k$ is the thermal conductivity (W/(m·K)), and $\frac{dT}{dx}$ is the temperature gradient (K/m).

**Step 2 — Apply the steady-state energy balance.**

Under steady state, there is no accumulation of energy within the wall. This means the heat flux $q$ entering the wall at $x=0$ must equal the heat flux leaving at $x=L$. Mathematically:

$$
\frac{dq}{dx} = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Solve the resulting ODE.**

Since $k$ is constant, differentiating Fourier's law with respect to $x$:

$$
\frac{dq}{dx} = -k \frac{d}{dx}\left(\frac{dT}{dx}\right) = -k \frac{d^2 T}{dx^2} = 0
$$

Therefore:

$$
\frac{d^2 T}{dx^2} = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Integrate once with respect to $x$.**

$$
\frac{dT}{dx} = C_1
$$

This is a **direct statement** that the temperature gradient is **constant** within the wall under these ideal conditions.

**Step 5 — Integrate again.**

$$
T(x) = C_1 x + C_2
$$

This is the equation of a straight line — confirming that the temperature profile is **linear**.

**Step 6 — Apply the boundary conditions.**

At $x = 0$, $T = T_1$:
$$
T_1 = C_1 (0) + C_2 \Rightarrow C_2 = T_1
$$

At $x = L$, $T = T_2$:
$$
T_2 = C_1 L + T_1 \Rightarrow C_1 = \frac{T_2 - T_1}{L}
$$

**Step 7 — Assemble the final temperature profile.**

$$
T(x) = T_1 + \left(\frac{T_2 - T_1}{L}\right) x
$$

**Step 8 — Extract the temperature gradient.**

$$
\frac{dT}{dx} = \frac{T_2 - T_1}{L}
$$

**Step 9 — Compute the heat flux via Fourier's Law.**

$$
q = -k \frac{dT}{dx} = -k \cdot \frac{T_2 - T_1}{L} = \frac{k(T_1 - T_2)}{L}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Interpretation:** In a homogeneous plane wall at steady state, the temperature gradient is *constant* in space, the temperature profile is *linear*, and the heat flux is *proportional* to the temperature difference and inversely proportional to the wall thickness. Doubling the wall thickness **halves** the gradient and the heat flux.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Steady-State Radial Temperature Profile in a Hollow Cylinder

> **Problem Setup:** A long cylindrical pipe (e.g., a steam line) has inner radius $r_1$ at temperature $T_1$ and outer radius $r_2$ at $T_2$. Length $L$, constant thermal conductivity $k$, steady state, no internal generation.

**Step 1 — Write Fourier's law in cylindrical coordinates (radial direction only).**

$$
q_r = -k \frac{dT}{dr}
$$

Here $q_r$ is in W/m² and represents radial heat flux.

**Step 2 — Note that total heat rate $\dot{Q}$ is constant through any cylindrical surface of radius $r$.**

The total heat transfer rate through a cylindrical shell at radius $r$ is:

$$
\dot{Q} = q_r \cdot A = q_r \cdot (2\pi r L)
$$

Because steady state requires $\dot{Q}$ to be the same at every radius:

$$
\dot{Q} = -k (2\pi r L) \frac{dT}{dr}
$$

**Step 3 — Separate variables.**

$$
dT = -\frac{\dot{Q}}{2\pi k L} \cdot \frac{dr}{r}
$$

**Step 4 — Integrate from $r_1$ to $r_2$.**

$$
\int_{T_1}^{T_2} dT = -\frac{\dot{Q}}{2\pi k L} \int_{r_1}^{r_2} \frac{dr}{r}
$$

$$
T_2 - T_1 = -\frac{\dot{Q}}{2\pi k L} \ln\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right)
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $\dot{Q}$.**

$$
\dot{Q} = \frac{2\pi k L (T_1 - T_2)}{\ln(r_2 / r_1)}
$$

**Step 6 — Invert the relationship to find $T(r)$ explicitly.**

Rearrange:

$$
dT = -\frac{\dot{Q}}{2\pi k L} \cdot \frac{dr}{r}
$$

Substitute $\dot{Q}$:

$$
dT = -\frac{1}{2\pi k L} \cdot \frac{2\pi k L (T_1 - T_2)}{\ln(r_2/r_1)} \cdot \frac{dr}{r}
$$

$$
dT = -\frac{(T_1 - T_2)}{\ln(r_2/r_1)} \cdot \frac{dr}{r}
$$

**Step 7 — Integrate with a generic lower limit $r$.**

$$
\int_{T_1}^{T(r)} dT = -\frac{(T_1 - T_2)}{\ln(r_2/r_1)} \int_{r_1}^{r} \frac{dr}{r}
$$

$$
T(r) - T_1 = -\frac{(T_1 - T_2)}{\ln(r_2/r_1)} \ln\left(\frac{r}{r_1}\right)
$$

**Step 8 — Simplify to obtain the final radial temperature profile.**

$$
T(r) = T_1 - \frac{(T_1 - T_2)}{\ln(r_2/r_1)} \ln\left(\frac{r}{r_1}\right)
$$

Equivalently:

$$
T(r) = \frac{T_1 \ln(r_2/r) + T_2 \ln(r/r_1)}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}
$$

**Step 9 — Compute the radial temperature gradient.**

Differentiating the explicit form:

$$
\frac{dT}{dr} = -\frac{T_1 - T_2}{\ln(r_2/r_1)} \cdot \frac{1}{r}
$$

$$
\frac{dT}{dr} = \frac{T_2 - T_1}{r \ln(r_2/r_1)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Unlike the plane wall, the radial gradient in a cylinder is **NOT constant** — it varies inversely with radius. The gradient is steepest at the inner radius ($r = r_1$) and gentlest at $r_2$. This is why pipe insulation is most effective when applied close to the hot inner surface — it reduces the steepest part of the gradient.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Transient Heat Equation (Starting Point of All Dynamic Control)

> **Problem Setup:** Derive the general 1-D transient heat conduction equation from the first principles of energy conservation.

**Step 1 — Define a control volume.**

Take a slab of thickness $\Delta x$, cross-sectional area $A$, density $\rho$, specific heat $c_p$.

**Step 2 — Apply the first law of thermodynamics (energy balance).**

$$
\text{Energy In} - \text{Energy Out} + \text{Generation} = \text{Accumulation}
$$

In rate form:

$$
q_x A - q_{x+\Delta x} A + \dot{q}_{gen} A \Delta x = \rho c_p A \Delta x \frac{\partial T}{\partial t}
$$

**Step 3 — Divide through by $A \Delta x$.**

$$
\frac{q_x - q_{x+\Delta x}}{\Delta x} + \dot{q}_{gen} = \rho c_p \frac{\partial T}{\partial t}
$$

**Step 4 — Take the limit $\Delta x \to 0$.**

$$
-\frac{\partial q}{\partial x} + \dot{q}_{gen} = \rho c_p \frac{\partial T}{\partial t}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute Fourier's Law, $q = -k\,\partial T/\partial x$.**

$$
-\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(-k\frac{\partial T}{\partial x}\right) + \dot{q}_{gen} = \rho c_p \frac{\partial T}{\partial t}
$$

For constant $k$:

$$
k \frac{\partial^2 T}{\partial x^2} + \dot{q}_{gen} = \rho c_p \frac{\partial T}{\partial t}
$$

**Step 6 — Rearrange and define thermal diffusivity.**

Rearranging:

$$
\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \frac{k}{\rho c_p}\frac{\partial^2 T}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\dot{q}_{gen}}{\rho c_p}
$$

Define $\alpha = k/(\rho c_p)$ (m²/s). For the homogeneous case (no internal generation, $\dot{q}_{gen} = 0$):

$$
\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \frac{\partial^2 T}{\partial x^2}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **1-D heat (diffusion) equation**. It is a parabolic PDE whose solutions (under appropriate boundary and initial conditions) describe how the temperature gradient inside a process vessel evolves with time — the foundation of all *dynamic* thermal process control.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Worked Example (KTU Pattern)

> **Problem:** A stainless-steel reactor wall has thickness $L = 0.025$ m, thermal conductivity $k = 16$ W/(m·K). The inner surface temperature is $T_1 = 380$ K and the outer surface is at $T_2 = 360$ K. Compute:
> (i) the temperature gradient in the wall,
> (ii) the heat flux through the wall,
> (iii) the total heat loss rate per square metre.

**Solution:**

**(i)** Temperature gradient (1-D plane wall, steady state):

$$
\frac{dT}{dx} = \frac{T_2 - T_1}{L} = \frac{360 - 380}{0.025} = \frac{-20}{0.025} = -800 \;\text{K/m}
$$

The negative sign indicates the temperature *decreases* in the $+x$ direction (from inside to outside).

**(ii)** Heat flux (Fourier's Law):

$$
q = -k\,\frac{dT}{dx} = -(16) \cdot (-800) = 12{,}800 \;\text{W/m}^2
$$

**(iii)** For $A = 1$ m²:

$$
\dot{Q} = q \cdot A = 12{,}800 \cdot 1 = 12.8 \;\text{kW/m}^2
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification of sign convention:** Heat should flow from hot (inner, 380 K) to cold (outer, 360 K), i.e., in the $-x$ direction. Since heat flux $q$ is defined positive when flowing in $+x$, the negative $\frac{dT}{dx}$ combined with the negative sign in Fourier's law correctly yields a *positive* $q$ in the $+x$ convention; physically, this represents heat flowing *out of* the reactor.

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of Temperature Gradient Sensing & Control

The following diagram depicts the **measurement and control architecture** for a typical industrial temperature-gradient monitoring loop (e.g., in a furnace tube or jacketed reactor).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Process Medium: Fluid In Tube Or Vessel] --> B[Temperature Sensor 1: TC At Position X1]
    A --> C[Temperature Sensor 2: TC At Position X2]
    A --> D[Temperature Sensor 3: TC At Position X3]
    B --> E[Signal Conditioner And Linearization]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F[ADC: Analog To Digital Conversion]
    F --> G[DCS PLC: Gradient Computation Module]
    G --> H{DT DX Above Setpoint Threshold}
    H -- YES --> I[Alarm Generation And Safety Interlock]
    H -- NO --> J[Continue Normal Operation]
    G --> K[PID Controller: Manipulates Heating Or Cooling Duty]
    K --> L[Final Control Element: Control Valve On Steam Or CW Line]
    L --> A
```

**Reading the diagram:**

1. **Nodes A → B/C/D:** Three spatially distributed thermocouples (TCs) measure the process temperature at three different positions along the tube/vessel. *This spatial distribution is what allows a gradient to be computed.*
2. **Node E:** Each thermocouple's millivolt signal is cold-junction compensated, linearized, and filtered.
3. **Node F:** Analog signals are digitized by the ADC and presented to the DCS/PLC.
4. **Node G:** The Gradient Computation Module evaluates $\frac{T_3 - T_1}{x_3 - x_1}$ (or equivalent finite-difference approximation) at each scan.
5. **Node H:** A threshold comparison protects against runaway steep gradients (a precursor to tube failure in fired heaters).
6. **Node K:** A standard PID controller adjusts the heating/cooling medium flow to *maintain* the gradient within a safe operating window.

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Steady-State vs. Transient Gradient Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Process State At Time t] --> Q{Is DT Dt Zero?}
    Q -- YES --> R[Steady State: Gradient Is Time Invariant]
    Q -- NO --> S[Transient State: Gradient Evolves]
    R --> R1[Apply Fourier Steady Solutions]
    R1 --> R2[Linear Or Logarithmic Profiles]
    S --> S1[Apply Heat Equation PDE]
    S1 --> S2[Requires Bi And Fo Analysis]
    S2 --> S3[Use Lumped Capacitance If Bi Less Than 0.1]
    S2 --> S4[Use Charts Or Numerical Methods If Bi Greater Than 0.1]
```

**Interpretation Notes:**

- **Steady-state branch (Q → R):** If $\partial T/\partial t = 0$, only spatial gradients matter. We can use the analytic plane-wall or cylinder formulas derived in Section 3.
- **Transient branch (Q → S):** If $\partial T/\partial t \neq 0$, the gradient is *time-dependent*. The Biot number (Bi) then decides whether the **lumped capacitance model** is valid (Bi < 0.1) or whether **spatial gradients inside the solid** must be retained (Bi ≥ 0.1).

## 4.3 Conceptual Visualization of Temperature Gradient Profiles

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph "PLANE WALL: LINEAR GRADIENT"
        W1["LEFT FACE T EQUALS T1 HOT"] --> W2["MIDDLE PLANE T EQUALS T1 + T2 DIVIDED BY 2"] --> W3["RIGHT FACE T EQUALS T2 COLD"]
    end
    subgraph "CYLINDER: LOG GRADIENT"
        C1["INNER RADIUS T EQUALS T1 HOT"] --> C2["INTERMEDIATE R T DECREASES LOGARITHMICALLY"] --> C3["OUTER RADIUS T EQUALS T2 COLD"]
    end
    subgraph "SPHERE: INVERSE GRADIENT"
        S1["CENTRE T EQUALS T1 HOT"] --> S2["INTERMEDIATE R T DECREASES AS 1 OVER R"] --> S3["SURFACE T EQUALS T2 COLD"]
    end
```

**Engineering Takeaway from the Three Profiles:**

- Plane wall → linear gradient, constant slope.
- Cylinder → logarithmic gradient, slope steeper near the inner radius.
- Sphere → inverse-radius gradient, the steepest of all three near the centre (a critical concern in catalyst pellet temperature control).

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2]**
*Define temperature gradient. State its SI unit and explain its significance in Fourier's law of heat conduction.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1 Mark):** Temperature gradient is defined as the rate of change of temperature with respect to distance in a particular direction within a medium. In three dimensions, it is the vector $\nabla T = (\partial T/\partial x, \partial T/\partial y, \partial T/\partial z)$.
>
> **SI Unit (1 Mark):** The SI unit is **Kelvin per metre (K/m)**. It may also be expressed as °C/m in process industries.
>
> **Significance in Fourier's Law (1 Mark):** Fourier's law states that the heat flux $q$ through a medium is directly proportional to the negative temperature gradient: $q = -k\,\nabla T$. The negative sign indicates that heat flows in the direction of decreasing temperature. Thus, the temperature gradient is the **driving potential** for conductive heat transfer; without a gradient, no heat conduction occurs.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]**
*Distinguish between steady-state and transient temperature gradients. Give one industrial example of each.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Steady-state gradient (1 Mark):** A temperature gradient that does not change with time, i.e., $\partial T/\partial t = 0$. The spatial profile $T(x)$ is fixed. *Example:* The temperature profile across the wall of a continuously operated steam-jacketed reactor at constant duty.
>
> **Transient gradient (1 Mark):** A temperature gradient that evolves with time, i.e., $\partial T/\partial t \neq 0$. The spatial profile changes as the system moves toward (or away from) steady state. *Example:* The temperature gradient inside a reactor wall during startup of a batch exothermic reaction.
>
> **Distinction (1 Mark):** Steady-state is governed by an ordinary differential equation (e.g., $d^2T/dx^2 = 0$); transient is governed by the parabolic partial differential equation $\partial T/\partial t = \alpha\,\partial^2T/\partial x^2$, which requires initial *and* boundary conditions for solution.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Sign convention errors:** Students frequently drop the negative sign in Fourier's law and lose 2–3 marks instantly. Always state the convention explicitly.
> - **Forgetting units:** KTU examiners award full credit *only* when units accompany every numerical answer. A bare "12800" without "W/m²" is penalized.
> - **Confusing gradient with gradient magnitude:** $\nabla T$ is a vector; its magnitude is a scalar. Specify which you are computing.
> - **Boundary condition mistakes:** In transient problems, students often write $T(0,t) = T_\infty$ for a convective BC, which is wrong; the correct form is $-k\,\partial T/\partial x \vert_{x=0} = h(T_s - T_\infty)$.
> - **In the cylinder derivation, students forget the $2\pi r L$ area term** and incorrectly use the plane-wall formula.

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2 Choice A]**

**(a)** Derive the steady-state temperature distribution $T(x)$ inside a homogeneous plane wall of thickness $L$ with surface temperatures $T_1$ and $T_2$. Hence obtain the heat flux expression. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A furnace wall is constructed of three layers in series: a 0.10 m fireclay brick layer ($k_1 = 1.0$ W/m·K), a 0.05 m insulating brick layer ($k_2 = 0.25$ W/m·K), and a 0.006 m steel outer plate ($k_3 = 45$ W/m·K). The inner surface temperature is 1200 K and the outer surface is at 350 K. Calculate: (i) the heat flux through the composite wall, (ii) the interface temperatures between layers, and (iii) the temperature gradient inside each layer. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Step 1 (1 Mark) — State assumptions:** Homogeneous wall, constant $k$, 1-D conduction, steady state, no internal heat generation.

**Step 2 (1 Mark) — Write Fourier's law:**

$$
q = -k \frac{dT}{dx}
$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark) — Apply steady-state condition $\partial T/\partial t = 0$:**

$$
\frac{d^2 T}{dx^2} = 0
$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark) — Integrate twice:**

$$
\frac{dT}{dx} = C_1 \quad ; \quad T(x) = C_1 x + C_2
$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark) — Apply boundary conditions:**

At $x = 0, T = T_1 \Rightarrow C_2 = T_1$.
At $x = L, T = T_2 \Rightarrow C_1 = (T_2 - T_1)/L$.

**Step 6 (1 Mark) — Final temperature profile:**

$$
T(x) = T_1 + \left(\frac{T_2 - T_1}{L}\right) x
$$

**Step 7 (1 Mark) — Heat flux expression:**

$$
q = -k \frac{dT}{dx} = \frac{k(T_1 - T_2)}{L}
$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Step 1 (1 Mark) — Compute thermal resistances per unit area:**

$$
R_1 = \frac{L_1}{k_1} = \frac{0.10}{1.0} = 0.100 \;\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{K/W}
$$

$$
R_2 = \frac{L_2}{k_2} = \frac{0.05}{0.25} = 0.200 \;\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{K/W}
$$

$$
R_3 = \frac{L_3}{k_3} = \frac{0.006}{45} = 1.333 \times 10^{-4} \;\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{K/W}
$$

$$
R_{total} = 0.100 + 0.200 + 1.333 \times 10^{-4} \approx 0.3001 \;\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{K/W}
$$

**Step 2 (2 Marks) — Compute heat flux:**

$$
q = \frac{T_1 - T_3}{R_{total}} = \frac{1200 - 350}{0.3001} \approx 2832 \;\text{W/m}^2
$$

**Step 3 (2 Marks) — Compute interface temperatures:**

- Interface 1–2 (between fireclay and insulating brick):

$$
T_{12} = T_1 - q \cdot R_1 = 1200 - 2832 \times 0.100 \approx 917 \;\text{K}
$$

- Interface 2–3 (between insulating brick and steel):

$$
T_{23} = T_{12} - q \cdot R_2 = 917 - 2832 \times 0.200 \approx 351 \;\text{K}
$$

(Sanity check: $T_{23} - q \cdot R_3 = 351 - 0.378 \approx 350$ K — matches outer surface temperature.)

**Step 4 (2 Marks) — Compute temperature gradients in each layer:**

$$
\left(\frac{dT}{dx}\right)_1 = \frac{T_{12} - T_1}{L_1} = \frac{917 - 1200}{0.10} = -2830 \;\text{K/m}
$$

$$
\left(\frac{dT}{dx}\right)_2 = \frac{T_{23} - T_{12}}{L_2} = \frac{351 - 917}{0.05} = -11{,}320 \;\text{K/m}
$$

$$
\left(\frac{dT}{dx}\right)_3 = \frac{T_3 - T_{23}}{L_3} = \frac{350 - 351}{0.006} \approx -166.7 \;\text{K/m}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Verification:** The gradient is *steepest* in the insulating brick layer ($k_2$ is smallest), confirming the engineering principle that low-$k$ materials are deliberately placed where the steepest gradient is desired (to drop the temperature rapidly). The thin steel layer carries a tiny gradient because its $k$ is large.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 2 Choice B]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, derive the steady-state radial temperature distribution $T(r)$ inside a hollow cylinder of inner radius $r_1$, outer radius $r_2$, length $L$, and thermal conductivity $k$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A steam pipeline of inner radius 50 mm and outer radius 75 mm is insulated with a 50 mm thick layer of magnesia ($k = 0.05$ W/m·K). The inner surface temperature is 450 K and the outer surface of the insulation is at 320 K. Calculate the heat loss per metre length of the pipe. Also compute the temperature at the interface between the pipe wall and the insulation. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Step 1 (1 Mark) — Diagram and assumptions:** Sketch the cross-section; assume 1-D radial conduction, steady state, constant $k$, no generation.

**Step 2 (1 Mark) — Heat rate constant across any cylindrical shell:**

$$
\dot{Q} = -k (2\pi r L) \frac{dT}{dr}
$$

**Step 3 (2 Marks) — Separate and integrate:**

$$
\int_{T_1}^{T_2} dT = -\frac{\dot{Q}}{2\pi k L} \int_{r_1}^{r_2} \frac{dr}{r}
$$

$$
T_2 - T_1 = -\frac{\dot{Q}}{2\pi k L} \ln\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right)
$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark) — Solve for $\dot{Q}$:**

$$
\dot{Q} = \frac{2\pi k L (T_1 - T_2)}{\ln(r_2/r_1)}
$$

**Step 5 (1 Mark) — Express $T(r)$ explicitly:**

$$
T(r) = T_1 - \frac{(T_1 - T_2)}{\ln(r_2/r_1)} \ln\left(\frac{r}{r_1}\right)
$$

**Step 6 (1 Mark) — Radial gradient:**

$$
\frac{dT}{dr} = \frac{T_2 - T_1}{r\,\ln(r_2/r_1)}
$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Step 1 (2 Marks) — Compute thermal resistance per metre of insulation layer:**

Outer radius of insulation: $r_3 = r_2 + 0.050 = 0.075 + 0.050 = 0.125$ m.
Inner radius of insulation: $r_2 = 0.075$ m.

$$
R_{ins} = \frac{\ln(r_3/r_2)}{2\pi k L} = \frac{\ln(0.125/0.075)}{2\pi \times 0.05 \times 1} = \frac{\ln(1.6667)}{0.3142} = \frac{0.5108}{0.3142} \approx 1.626 \;\text{K/W (per m)}
$$

**Step 2 (2 Marks) — Heat loss per metre:**

$$
\dot{Q}/L = \frac{T_{inner} - T_{outer}}{R_{ins}} = \frac{450 - 320}{1.626} \approx 80.0 \;\text{W/m}
$$

**Step 3 (2 Marks) — Interface temperature $T(r_2)$:**

The temperature drop occurs only across the insulation layer (since we are not given the pipe wall conductivity here; assuming the interface is the inner radius of the insulation at $T_2 = ?$ — wait, $T_2$ at $r_2 = 0.075$ m is the interface we want). 

Using the temperature profile with the *insulation as the only conducting layer*:

$$
T(r_2) = T(r_3) + \dot{Q}/L \cdot \frac{\ln(r_3/r_2)}{2\pi k L} \cdot (-1)
$$

Alternatively, since $\Delta T_{ins} = \dot{Q}/L \cdot R_{ins} = 80.0 \times 1.626 \approx 130.0$ K:

$$
T(r_2) = T(r_3) + 130.0 = 320 + 130.0 = 450 \;\text{K}
$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark) — Verification:**

$$
T(r_2) = 450 \;\text{K} \quad \text{(matches the given inner pipe temperature)}
$$

This confirms that the entire temperature drop of 130 K occurs across the 50 mm insulation layer.

> [!NOTE]
> If the pipe wall itself is to be included (with steel $k \approx 45$ W/m·K and a thickness from say $r_1 = 0.050$ m to $r_2 = 0.075$ m), one would compute its resistance as $R_{steel} = \ln(0.075/0.050)/(2\pi \cdot 45 \cdot 1) \approx 2.86 \times 10^{-3}$ K/W — negligible compared to the insulation. In such problems, the student must check both contributions before declaring one negligible.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Temperature Gradient (Module 2):**

- [ ] **Definition:** $\nabla T = (\partial T/\partial x, \partial T/\partial y, \partial T/\partial z)$; SI unit **K/m**.
- [ ] **Fourier's Law** is the master equation: $q = -k\,\nabla T$ — heat flows from high $T$ to low $T$.
- [ ] **Plane wall (steady):** Linear profile, gradient = $(T_2 - T_1)/L$; heat flux = $k(T_1 - T_2)/L$.
- [ ] **Cylinder wall (steady):** Logarithmic profile; gradient = $(T_2 - T_1)/[r\ln(r_2/r_1)]$; heat rate = $2\pi k L(T_1 - T_2)/\ln(r_2/r_1)$.
- [ ] **Sphere (steady):** Inverse-$r$ profile; heat rate = $4\pi k(T_1 - T_2)/(1/r_1 - 1/r_2)$.
- [ ] **Transient:** Heat equation $\partial T/\partial t = \alpha\,\partial^2 T/\partial x^2$ governs; solution requires both initial and boundary conditions.
- [ ] **Three BC types:** Dirichlet (fixed $T$), Neumann (fixed $q$), Robin/convective ($-k\partial T/\partial x = h(T_s - T_\infty)$).
- [ ] **Biot number Bi = $h L_c / k$** decides if **lumped** (Bi < 0.1) or **distributed** (Bi ≥ 0.1) analysis is needed.
- [ ] **Fourier number Fo = $\alpha t / L_c^2$** tracks transient progress; Fo → ∞ implies steady state.
- [ ] **LMTD** is the *effective* gradient driving heat exchangers: $\Delta T_{lm} = (\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2)/\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)$.
- [ ] **Sign convention:** Negative $\nabla T$ ⇒ positive heat flux in $+x$ direction; **always** state the convention explicitly.
- [ ] **Units matter:** Every numerical answer in KTU papers must carry its unit (W/m², K/m, K/W, etc.).
- [ ] **Industrial relevance:** Heat exchangers, jacketed reactors, distillation trays, furnace tubes — every thermal unit op uses gradient control.
- [ ] **Insulation logic:** Place low-$k$ layers where you want a **steep** gradient (heat drop concentrated there); place high-$k$ layers where you want a **gentle** gradient (rapid heat spreading).
- [ ] **Steepest gradient location:** For a cylinder, gradient is steepest at the inner radius; for a sphere, steepest at the centre.
- [ ] **Engineering rule of thumb:** Doubling wall thickness halves the steady-state gradient and heat flux (plane wall case).

> **End of Module 2 — Temperature Gradient Notes.**
> *Prepared in accordance with the KTU 2024 Scheme, Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework, and Revised Bloom's Taxonomy cognitive levels (Apply / Analyse / Evaluate).*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cascade Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT743 - INDUSTRIAL PROCESS CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Cascade Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Cascade Control** is an advanced multi-loop control strategy in which the output (manipulated variable) of one controller (the *primary* or *master* controller) serves as the **set point (reference input)** for a second controller (the *secondary* or *slave* controller). The two control loops are arranged in a **nested (series) configuration**, where the secondary loop is completely enclosed within the primary loop.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Definition (2 marks weightage):**
> "Cascade control uses two controllers (Primary and Secondary) in series such that the output of the primary controller becomes the set point of the secondary controller. The secondary loop must contain the faster dynamics of the process."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are driving a car on a highway, and you need to maintain a constant speed of **60 km/h**.

- **Driver's eyes (Primary sensor)** = measures the car speed and compares it to the desired **60 km/h**.
- **Driver's brain (Primary controller)** = decides how much accelerator pedal position is needed.
- **Cruise control hardware (Secondary loop)** = an internal loop that takes the brain's instruction and physically controls the throttle.
- **Throttle position sensor (Secondary sensor)** = measures the actual throttle opening.

If a strong headwind hits the car, the *inner* (throttle) loop reacts **instantly** to push the throttle harder, *long before* the outer (speed) loop even notices a change. The **outer loop** then gradually fine-tunes the speed back to 60 km/h. This is exactly how cascade control works — a **fast inner loop shields the system from disturbances**, while the **outer loop handles the main control objective**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The secondary loop always contains the **fastest process dynamics** and the **primary disturbance source**. The primary loop contains the **slow dominant process**.

### 1.3 Physical Constants, Standards & Process Variables

In industrial practice, cascade control is universally applied to variables regulated by **ISA-5.1 / IEC 61131** industrial instrumentation standards:

| Variable Type | Typical Primary Variable | Typical Secondary Variable |
|---|---|---|
| Temperature | Reactor Outlet Temperature | Jacket Cooling Water Flow |
| Temperature | Furnace Exit Temp | Fuel Gas Flow |
| Pressure | Column Overhead Pressure | Condenser Coolant Flow |
| Composition | Distillate Purity | Reflux Flow |
| Level | Boiler Drum Level | Feedwater Flow |

**Standard Metric:** Loop response time ratio (secondary to primary) should ideally be at least **3:1 to 10:1**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Structure — Step-by-Step Logic

A cascade control system has **two feedback loops operating in a master–slave hierarchy**:

1. **Primary (Outer) Loop:**
   - Sensor $P_1$ measures the **controlled variable (CV)** of the main process.
   - Primary controller $G_{c1}$ compares $P_1$ to set point $R$.
   - Output of $G_{c1}$ is **NOT directly a valve signal** — it is the **set point** for the secondary loop.

2. **Secondary (Inner) Loop:**
   - Sensor $P_2$ measures the **intermediate (secondary) variable** — typically a flow, pressure, or temperature closer to the actuator.
   - Secondary controller $G_{c2}$ compares $P_2$ to the output of $G_{c1}$ (which acts as a *floating* set point).
   - Output of $G_{c2}$ finally drives the **final control element (valve)** through process $G_{v2}$.

3. **Disturbance Rejection Mechanism:**
   - Disturbance $D_1$ entering the **secondary loop** is rejected by $G_{c2}$ before it propagates to the primary CV.
   - Disturbance $D_2$ entering the **primary process** must be handled by $G_{c1}$ (the slower loop).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why use cascade?** Because the inner loop's controller can be tuned **very aggressively (high gain)** since it controls a fast, well-defined variable (like flow). This makes the inner loop appear as a **unity-gain block** to the outer loop, simplifying the overall dynamics.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity / Parameter | Symbol | Mathematical Expression | Boundary / Constraint | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Secondary Loop Set Point | $R_2$ | $R_2 = u_1$ (output of primary controller) | Variable; not fixed by operator | % or engineering units |
| Secondary Loop Transfer Function | $G_{sec}(s)$ | $\dfrac{G_{c2} \cdot G_{v2} \cdot G_{p2}}{1 + G_{c2} \cdot G_{v2} \cdot G_{p2}}$ | Closed-loop form; must be stable | dimensionless |
| Overall Cascade Closed-Loop TF | $G_{cascade}(s)$ | $\dfrac{G_{sec} \cdot G_{p1}}{1 + G_{c1} \cdot G_{sec} \cdot G_{p1}}$ | Assumes unity primary feedback | dimensionless |
| Loop Response Time Ratio | $\tau_{ratio}$ | $\tau_{ratio} = \dfrac{\tau_{primary}}{\tau_{secondary}}$ | Must satisfy $\tau_{ratio} \geq 3$ | dimensionless |
| Equivalent Secondary Process Gain | $K_{sec,eq}$ | $K_{sec,eq} \approx 1$ when inner loop tuned tight | $K_{sec,eq} \in [0.8, 1.0]$ | dimensionless |
| Secondary Controller Output Limit | $u_2$ | $u_{2,min} \leq u_2 \leq u_{2,max}$ | Anti-windup protection required | % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Notation Rule:** For absolute value, use $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$ inside LaTeX (never the pipe character) to keep markdown tables intact.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Petrochemical Industry:** Reactor temperature control using coolant flow as secondary variable.
- **Power Plants:** Steam temperature cascade — primary is superheater outlet temp, secondary is attemperator spray flow.
- **HVAC Systems:** Zone temperature controlled via cascade with airflow rate in ducts.
- **Chemical Distillation:** Reflux drum level controlled via cascade with reflux flow as secondary.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Secondary (Inner) Loop Transfer Function

Let the components of the inner loop be:
- Secondary controller: $G_{c2}(s)$
- Valve/Actuator: $G_{v2}(s)$
- Secondary process: $G_{p2}(s)$
- Secondary sensor: $H_2(s)$ (assume $H_2(s) = 1$ for simplicity)

The forward path of the inner closed loop is:

$$
G_{fwd,inner}(s) = G_{c2}(s) \cdot G_{v2}(s) \cdot G_{p2}(s)
$$

The feedback path is unity. By the standard negative feedback formula, the **closed-loop transfer function of the inner loop** is:

$$
G_{sec}(s) = \dfrac{G_{fwd,inner}(s)}{1 + G_{fwd,inner}(s)} = \dfrac{G_{c2}(s) \cdot G_{v2}(s) \cdot G_{p2}(s)}{1 + G_{c2}(s) \cdot G_{v2}(s) \cdot G_{p2}(s)}
$$
