## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Relative Gain Array (RGA) — The Quantitative Guideline for MV Selection

The RGA is the **single most important KTU examinable formula** for MV selection in MIMO systems. We derive it from first principles.

**Step 1 — Define the multivariable steady-state model.**
Consider an $n \times n$ plant with $n$ controlled variables and $n$ manipulated variables. The linearised steady-state model is:

$$\bar{c} = K \bar{m}$$

where $K$ is the $n \times n$ steady-state gain matrix, with elements $k_{ij} = \left( \dfrac{\partial c_i}{\partial m_j} \right)_{\text{steady state}}$.

**Step 2 — Define the relative gain $\lambda_{ij}$.**
The relative gain of the pairing $c_i \leftrightarrow m_j$ is the ratio of the open-loop gain (with all other loops open) to the closed-loop gain (with all other loops perfectly controlled):

$$\lambda_{ij} = \dfrac{\left( \dfrac{\partial c_i}{\partial m_j} \right)_{m_k = \text{const},\ k \neq j}}{\left( \dfrac{\partial c_i}{\partial m_j} \right)_{c_k = \text{const},\ k \neq i}}$$

**Step 3 — Express the open-loop gain (numerator).**
With all other $m_k$ held constant, a small change $\Delta m_j$ produces a change in $c_i$ given by the $(i, j)$ element of $K$, namely $k_{ij}$. Therefore:

$$\left( \dfrac{\partial c_i}{\partial m_j} \right)_{m_k = \text{const}} = k_{ij}$$

**Step 4 — Express the closed-loop gain (denominator).**
With all other $c_k$ held constant, the changes in $m_k$ ($k \neq j$) are dictated by the perfect controllers on the other loops. We must compute the partial of $c_i$ with respect to $m_j$ subject to the constraint $c_k = \text{const}$ for all $k \neq i$. Differentiating $\bar{c} = K \bar{m}$ with respect to $m_j$:

$$\left( \dfrac{\partial c_k}{\partial m_j} \right)_{c_k = \text{const}} = 0 \quad \text{for all } k \neq i$$

Expanding $c_k$ in terms of all $m_l$:

$$\sum_{l=1}^{n} k_{kl} \dfrac{\partial m_l}{\partial m_j} = 0 \quad (k \neq i)$$

Since $m_l$ is independent, $\dfrac{\partial m_l}{\partial m_j} = \delta_{lj}$ (Kronecker delta). Hence:

$$k_{kj} + \sum_{l \neq j} k_{kl} \dfrac{\partial m_l}{\partial m_j} = 0$$

Let $\alpha_l = \dfrac{\partial m_l}{\partial m_j}$ for $l \neq j$. The system becomes:

$$\sum_{l \neq j} k_{kl} \alpha_l = -k_{kj} \quad \text{for all } k \neq i$$

Solving this $(n-1) \times (n-1)$ linear system yields $\alpha_l$ for $l \neq j$. The denominator then becomes:

$$\left( \dfrac{\partial c_i}{\partial m_j} \right)_{c_k = \text{const}} = k_{ij} + \sum_{l \neq j} k_{il} \alpha_l$$

**Step 5 — Combine via Cramer's rule.**
Using Cramer's rule to solve the constrained system, it can be shown that:

$$\lambda_{ij} = k_{ij} \left[ K^{-1} \right]_{ji}^{T} = k_{ij} \left[ K^{-1} \right]_{ij}$$

because $\left[K^{-1}\right]^T$ has element $(j, i)$ equal to $\left[K^{-1}\right]_{ij}$ for a square matrix.

**Step 6 — RGA in matrix form.**
Stacking all $\lambda_{ij}$ into a matrix $\Lambda$, we obtain the elegant Hadamard (element-wise) product:

$$\Lambda = K \circ \left( K^{-1} \right)^T$$

where $\circ$ denotes the Hadamard product and $T$ denotes matrix transpose.

**Step 7 — Physical interpretation and KTU pairing rule.**

$$\boxed{\lambda_{ij} \approx 1 \Rightarrow \text{ideal pairing} \mid \lambda_{ij} \in (0, 1) \Rightarrow \text{acceptable} \mid \lambda_{ij} < 0 \Rightarrow \text{NEVER pair} \mid \lambda_{ij} > 1 \Rightarrow \text{caution}}$$

**Step 8 — Niederlinski Stability Index.**
The pairings selected by the RGA must additionally satisfy the Niederlinski Index:

$$NI = \dfrac{\det(K)}{\prod_{i=1}^{n} k_{ii}}$$

If $NI < 0$, the selected pairings are **closed-loop unstable** for any controller tuning, and re-pairing is mandatory.

### 3.2 Worked Example — 2×2 RGA Calculation

**Problem:** A blending tank has two feeds: hot water (controlled to give $T$) and concentrated acid (controlled to give concentration $C$). The steady-state gain matrix is:

$$K = \begin{bmatrix} k_{TT} & k_{TC} \\ k_{CT} & k_{CC} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.8 & -0.1 \\ 0.6 & 0.4 \end{bmatrix}$$

Determine the best MV pairing using RGA.

**Solution:**

Step A — Compute $\det(K)$:

$$\det(K) = (0.8)(0.4) - (-0.1)(0.6) = 0.32 + 0.06 = 0.38$$

Step B — Compute $K^{-1}$:

$$K^{-1} = \dfrac{1}{0.38} \begin{bmatrix} 0.4 & 0.1 \\ -0.6 & 0.8 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.0526 & 0.2632 \\ -1.5789 & 2.1053 \end{bmatrix}$$

Step C — Compute $\left( K^{-1} \right)^T$:

$$\left( K^{-1} \right)^T = \begin{bmatrix} 1.0526 & -1.5789 \\ 0.2632 & 2.1053 \end{bmatrix}$$

Step D — Compute Hadamard product $\Lambda = K \circ \left( K^{-1} \right)^T$:

$$\Lambda = \begin{bmatrix} (0.8)(1.0526) & (-0.1)(-1.5789) \\ (0.6)(0.2632) & (0.4)(2.1053) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.8421 & 0.1579 \\ 0.1579 & 0.8421 \end{bmatrix}$$

Step E — Verify row sums equal 1: $0.8421 + 0.1579 = 1.0$ ✓ (diagonal-dominant system).

Step F — Decision:
- $\lambda_{11} = 0.8421$ — pair $T \leftrightarrow m_1$ (hot water).
- $\lambda_{22} = 0.8421$ — pair $C \leftrightarrow m_2$ (acid).
- Both diagonal elements are positive and close to 1 ⇒ **Diagonal pairing is correct**.

Step G — Niederlinski check:

$$NI = \dfrac{\det(K)}{k_{TT} \cdot k_{CC}} = \dfrac{0.38}{(0.8)(0.4)} = \dfrac{0.38}{0.32} = 1.1875 > 0$$

Pairing is **closed-loop stable**. ✓

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Candidate Manipulating Variables

| Candidate MV | Guideline 1: Direct effect? | Guideline 2: No recycle? | Guideline 3: No inverse response? | Guideline 4: Low interaction? | Guideline 5: Linear range? | Guideline 6: Safe? | Guideline 7: No constraints? | Guideline 8: Suitable for FF? | Total Score (/8) | KTU Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Steam flow (heat exchanger) | ✅ Strong | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | **8/8** | **Best MV** |
| Process fluid flow | ⚠️ Weak (gain too low) | ✅ | ✅ | ⚠️ Cross-couples with flow loop | ⚠️ Affects ΔT (NL) | ✅ | ✅ | ⚠️ Disturbance may swamp FF | **3/8** | Not recommended |
| Bypass flow | ✅ Moderate | ⚠️ Couples to outlet T | ✅ Inverse response possible | ⚠️ Strong interaction | ⚠️ Nonlinear | ✅ | ✅ | ⚠️ Limited range | **3/8** | Conditional |
| Feed temperature | ✅ Strong | ✅ | ⚠️ Time delay from upstream | ✅ | ✅ | ⚠️ Disturbs upstream unit | ⚠️ Saturates during startup | ✅ | **5/8** | Marginal |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Decision Flowchart — Selecting the Manipulating Variable

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Process Under Design] --> B{Identify all CVs and MVs}
    B --> C[Build steady-state gain matrix K]
    C --> D{Is the system MIMO?}
    D -- No, SISO --> E[Apply Guidelines 1, 2, 3, 5, 6, 7, 8]
    E --> F{Rank MVs by speed, gain, and delay}
    F --> G[Select MV with highest score]
    G --> H[Proceed to controller tuning]
    D -- Yes, MIMO --> I[Compute RGA: Λ = K o K-inv transpose]
    I --> J{All diagonal λ_ii > 0 and near 1?}
    J -- No --> K[Reject pairing and re-examine alternatives]
    K --> I
    J -- Yes --> L[Check Niederlinski Index NI > 0]
    L -- No --> M[Re-pair even if RGA is positive]
    L -- Yes --> N[Validate against Guidelines 1, 2, 3, 6, 7]
    N --> O{All guidelines satisfied?}
    O -- No --> P[Identify failing guideline and re-design]
    P --> I
    O -- Yes --> Q[Final MV selection locked]
    Q --> H[Proceed to controller tuning]
    H --> R[End]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper 1]**

**Q1. Define the term "manipulating variable" in the context of industrial process control. Mention TWO criteria for its selection.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** The manipulating variable (MV) is the process variable that the controller is permitted to adjust through a final control element (valve, VFD, damper) in order to keep the controlled variable (CV) at the desired setpoint in the presence of load disturbances.
- **Criteria (2 marks):**
  - (i) The MV should have a **direct, fast, and large influence** on the CV (high $K_p$, low $\tau$, low $\theta_d$).
  - (ii) The MV should **not cause inverse response** or recycle back to the process (minimum-phase dynamics).

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q2. What is the Relative Gain Array (RGA)? State the KTU rule for acceptable CV–MV pairing using RGA.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** The RGA $\Lambda$ is a matrix of relative gains $\lambda_{ij}$ computed via the Hadamard product $\Lambda = K \circ \left(K^{-1}\right)^T$, where $K$ is the steady-state gain matrix. Each $\lambda_{ij}$ compares the open-loop gain of the $c_i$–$m_j$ pairing (with other loops open) to its gain when all other loops are perfectly controlled.
- **Pairing rule (1 mark):** Pair $c_i$ with $m_j$ such that $\lambda_{ij}$ is **positive and as close to 1.0 as possible**. Negative RGA values are **forbidden** (closed-loop instability). Values greater than 5 are cautioned against.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — End Semester, Module 2, Model Paper]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** List and explain **any six (6) guidelines** for selecting the manipulating variable in a chemical process control loop. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 2×2 distillation column has the following steady-state gain matrix relating the two product compositions ($x_D$, $x_B$) to the two candidate manipulated variables (reflux $L$, reboiler steam $V$):

$$K = \begin{bmatrix} 0.6 & 0.2 \\ 0.3 & 0.9 \end{bmatrix}$$

Compute the **Relative Gain Array (RGA)** and the **Niederlinski Index**, then state the recommended pairing with justification. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer for Q1A(a) — 7 marks:**

1. **Direct, large, and rapid influence (1 mark):** The MV must exhibit a high steady-state gain $K_p$, small time constant $\tau$, and short dead time $\theta_d$, so the controller can effectively counter disturbances.

2. **Avoid recycle streams (1 mark):** The MV should not be recycled within the process, as internal feedback paths create unobservable dynamics and limit-cycle oscillations.

3. **No inverse response (1 mark):** The MV–CV transfer function must be minimum-phase, i.e., all zeros in the left-half s-plane. Inverse response defeats PID action.

4. **Minimize interaction (1 mark):** In MIMO systems, the MV should have minimal steady-state and dynamic coupling with neighbouring loops; pair such that the RGA element is positive and near unity.

5. **Favourable range and linearity (1 mark):** The MV should have a wide operable range and near-linear gain over the expected operating envelope to simplify controller tuning.

6. **Safety and economy (1 mark):** The MV should be safe to manipulate, environmentally benign, and economically efficient (e.g., not a high-value reagent).

7. **No constraint violations (1 mark):** The MV must not be saturated by adjacent unit operations, as constraint activation leads to integral wind-up and post-constraint transients.

---

**Model Answer for Q1A(b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1: Compute the determinant (1 mark):**

$$\det(K) = (0.6)(0.9) - (0.2)(0.3) = 0.54 - 0.06 = 0.48$$

**Step 2: Compute the inverse (2 marks):**

$$K^{-1} = \dfrac{1}{0.48} \begin{bmatrix} 0.9 & -0.2 \\ -0.3 & 0.6 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.875 & -0.4167 \\ -0.625 & 1.25 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3: Compute the transpose of the inverse (1 mark):**

$$\left( K^{-1} \right)^T = \begin{bmatrix} 1.875 & -0.625 \\ -0.4167 & 1.25 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4: Compute the Hadamard product (2 marks):**

$$\Lambda = K \circ \left( K^{-1} \right)^T = \begin{bmatrix} (0.6)(1.875) & (0.2)(-0.625) \\ (0.3)(-0.4167) & (0.9)(1.25) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.125 & -0.125 \\ -0.125 & 1.125 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5: Niederlinski Index (1 mark):**

$$NI = \dfrac{\det(K)}{k_{11} \cdot k_{22}} = \dfrac{0.48}{(0.6)(0.9)} = \dfrac{0.48}{0.54} = 0.8889 > 0$$

**Step 6: Recommended pairing (1 mark):**
Although $NI > 0$ (stable), the diagonal RGA elements $\lambda_{11} = \lambda_{22} = 1.125$ are **positive** but **slightly above 1**, indicating mild interaction. Off-diagonal elements are **negative** ($\lambda_{12} = \lambda_{21} = -0.125$), which means the *alternative* pairing is **forbidden**. The recommended pairing is therefore:

- $x_D$ (distillate composition) $\leftrightarrow$ $L$ (reflux)
- $x_B$ (bottom composition) $\leftrightarrow$ $V$ (reboiler steam)

(Diagonal pairing — 1 mark)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Alternative**

**(a)** Explain the **concept of inverse response** in process control. Why is it a disqualifying criterion for selecting a manipulating variable? Derive the dynamic condition under which inverse response occurs. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 3×3 chemical reactor has the steady-state gain matrix relating the controlled variables $(C_1, C_2, C_3)$ to manipulated variables $(F_1, F_2, F_3)$:

$$K = \begin{bmatrix} 1.0 & 0.1 & 0.0 \\ 0.1 & 1.0 & 0.1 \\ 0.0 & 0.1 & 1.0 \end{bmatrix}$$

Verify the row-sums of the RGA, calculate the Niederlinski Index, and recommend the pairing. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer for Q1B(a) — 7 marks:**

**Definition (2 marks):** Inverse response is a non-minimum-phase dynamic behaviour in which the controlled variable initially moves in the **opposite direction** to its final steady-state value following a step change in the manipulated variable. The step response exhibits a "wrong-way" transient.

**Mathematical condition (3 marks):** Inverse response occurs when the process transfer function $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K_p (1 - \alpha s)}{(\tau_1 s + 1)(\tau_2 s + 1)}$ has a **Right-Half-Plane (RHP) zero** at $s = +1/\alpha$, i.e., the numerator coefficient of $s$ is **negative** when the constant term is positive. The initial slope of the step response is:

$$\left. \dfrac{dy(t)}{dt} \right|_{t=0^+} = -K_p \alpha$$

If $\alpha > 0$ and $K_p > 0$, the initial slope is **negative**, signalling inverse response. The condition for inverse response is therefore $\alpha > 0$ (RHP zero).

**Why disqualifying (2 marks):**
- (i) PID action applies a corrective force based on the sign of the error; the initial wrong-way motion amplifies rather than reduces the integral of the error, causing **overshoot and possible instability**.
- (ii) Classical tuning rules (Ziegler–Nichols, Cohen–Coon) assume monotonic FOPDT behaviour; they fail catastrophically for inverse-response processes, requiring **specialised Smith-predictor or internal-model-control (IMC-PID)** structures.

---

**Model Answer for Q1B(b) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1: Compute $\det(K)$ (2 marks):**
Expanding along the first row:

$$\det(K) = 1.0 \cdot [(1.0)(1.0) - (0.1)(0.1)] - 0.1 \cdot [(0.1)(1.0) - (0.1)(0.0)] + 0$$
$$= 1.0 \cdot [1.0 - 0.01] - 0.1 \cdot [0.1 - 0] = 0.99 - 0.01 = 0.98$$

**Step 2: Compute $K^{-1}$ (2 marks):**
Since $K = I + \epsilon$ where $\epsilon$ is small and tridiagonal, the inverse is approximately:

$$K^{-1} \approx \dfrac{1}{0.98} \begin{bmatrix} 0.99 & -0.099 & 0.0099 \\ -0.099 & 0.99 & -0.099 \\ 0.0099 & -0.099 & 0.99 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.0102 & -0.1010 & 0.0101 \\ -0.1010 & 1.0102 & -0.1010 \\ 0.0101 & -0.1010 & 1.0102 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3: RGA via Hadamard product (2 marks):**

$$\Lambda = K \circ \left( K^{-1} \right)^T$$

Since $K$ is symmetric, $\left( K^{-1} \right)^T = K^{-1}$. Therefore:

$$\Lambda = \begin{bmatrix} (1.0)(1.0102) & (0.1)(-0.1010) & (0.0)(0.0101) \\ (0.1)(-0.1010) & (1.0)(1.0102) & (0.1)(-0.1010) \\ (0.0)(0.0101) & (0.1)(-0.1010) & (1.0)(1.0102) \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\Lambda = \begin{bmatrix} 1.0102 & -0.0101 & 0.0000 \\ -0.0101 & 1.0102 & -0.0101 \\ 0.0000 & -0.0101 & 1.0102 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4: Verify row sums (0.5 mark):**
- Row 1: $1.0102 - 0.0101 + 0.0 = 1.0001 \approx 1.0$ ✓
- Row 2: $-0.0101 + 1.0102 - 0.0101 = 0.9900$ (rounding error) ≈ 1.0 ✓
- Row 3: $0.0 - 0.0101 + 1.0102 = 1.0001 \approx 1.0$ ✓

**Step 5: Niederlinski Index (0.5 mark):**

$$NI = \dfrac{\det(K)}{k_{11} \cdot k_{22} \cdot k_{33}} = \dfrac{0.98}{1.0 \times 1.0 \times 1.0} = 0.98 > 0$$

**Step 6: Recommended pairing (1 mark):**
All diagonal RGA elements are **positive and ≈ 1**, and all off-diagonal elements are **negative** (forbidden pairing). The Niederlinski index is positive. Therefore, the **diagonal pairing** is recommended:

- $C_1 \leftrightarrow F_1$
- $C_2 \leftrightarrow F_2$
- $C_3 \leftrightarrow F_3$

This is a **near-decoupled system** with negligible interaction — an ideal control structure.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the transpose:** $\Lambda = K \circ (K^{-1})^T$, **NOT** $K \circ K^{-1}$. This is the #1 error in board exams.
> 2. **Not verifying row sums:** Every row and column of $\Lambda$ must sum to 1.0. If it does not, a calculation error exists.
> 3. **Selecting the pairing with the highest *magnitude* $\lambda_{ij}$:** Students often pick $\lambda_{ij} = 1.125$ over $\lambda_{ij} = 0.842$ in the distillation example, not realising that values slightly above 1 are preferred over values slightly below 1 only when they are positive — **both 0.842 and 1.125 are valid** if the off-diagonal signs are correct. The key criterion is **sign first, magnitude second**.
> 4. **Ignoring the Niederlinski Index:** A positive RGA pairing can still be **closed-loop unstable** if $NI < 0$. Always compute $NI$ after RGA.
> 5. **Failing to invoke engineering guidelines alongside RGA:** RGA is necessary but not sufficient. KTU expects you to justify the pairing with **at least 2–3 of the 8 guidelines** (e.g., safety, range, no inverse response).
> 6. **Confusing CV and MV in the gain matrix convention:** The convention $c_i = \sum k_{ij} m_j$ must be stated explicitly to avoid partial-derivative errors.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — "Guidelines for Identifying the Manipulating Variable"**

- ✅ The **manipulating variable (MV)** is the controller's degree of freedom, adjusted via a final control element to maintain the controlled variable (CV) at setpoint.
- ✅ The selection of MV precedes controller tuning; it is a **structural design decision**, not a tuning decision.
- ✅ **8 Golden Guidelines** (in order of importance): (1) Direct, large, rapid influence; (2) No recycle; (3) No inverse response (minimum-phase); (4) Minimal loop interaction; (5) Favourable range and linearity; (6) Safety and economy; (7) No constraint violation; (8) Suitable for feedforward augmentation.
- ✅ The **Relative Gain Array (RGA)** is the quantitative tool for MV selection in MIMO systems: $\Lambda = K \circ \left(K^{-1}\right)^T$.
- ✅ **Pairing rule:** $\lambda_{ij} > 0$ (preferably near 1); $\lambda_{ij} < 0$ ⇒ forbidden; $\lambda_{ij} > 5$ ⇒ cautioned.
- ✅ **Row/column sums** of $\Lambda$ must equal 1.0 — use this as a calculation-verification step.
- ✅ **Niederlinski Index** $NI = \det(K) / \prod_i k_{ii}$ must be **positive** for the selected pairing to be closed-loop stable.
- ✅ **FOPDT model** $G_p(s) = K_p e^{-\theta_d s} / (\tau s + 1)$ is the standard first-cut process model used to rank candidate MVs by speed.
- ✅ **Feedforward gain** $K_{ff} = -K_d / K_p$ must be physically implementable (typically $0.1 < \vert K_{ff} \vert < 10$).
- ✅ **Inverse response** is signalled by a **Right-Half-Plane zero** in $G_p(s)$ and is a **disqualifying criterion** for MV selection.
- ✅ In the KTU 2024 ESE, expect either a **2×2 or 3×3 RGA calculation problem (7 marks)** or a **guideline-justification question (7 marks)**, or a combination (14 marks with internal choice).
- ✅ Always state the **sign convention** and **physical units** of $K_{ij}$ before plugging into formulas.
- ✅ Memorise the **Hadamard product form** of RGA; do NOT derive it from scratch in the exam — it wastes time.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Column Pressure Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT743 - INDUSTRIAL PROCESS CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Column Pressure Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# COLUMN PRESSURE CONTROL — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU-Style Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Column Pressure Control** is the automatic regulation of the operating pressure inside a distillation (or absorption) column by manipulating either the **condenser duty (heat removal rate)**, the **vapor boil-up rate (reboiler duty)**, the **vapor/fuel flow to a side reboiler**, the **vent/bleeder flow**, or a combination of these, in order to maintain a stable pressure profile that preserves product separation efficiency, energy economy, and safe operation.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, column pressure control is a **Composition/Quality-influencing CV** (controlled variable) that is tightly coupled to:
- **Tray temperature profile** (since boiling point depends on pressure via the Clausius–Clapeyron relation)
- **Relative volatility (α)** of the key components
- **Internal vapor and liquid traffic** (flooding / weeping boundaries)

## 1.2 Why Column Pressure Is Treated as a "Master" Loop

Unlike temperature, pressure inside a distillation column responds almost instantaneously (because vapors are low-density, low-residence-time phases). A **change in pressure is detected within seconds**, while a temperature change can take many minutes. This makes pressure the **fastest and most authoritative** controlled variable in the column.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEICT743 – Module 2):**
> Column pressure control is studied under the broader umbrella of *Multi-loop and Special Control Strategies*. The examiner expects students to (a) draw standard control schemes, (b) state the manipulated variable, (c) explain the interaction with temperature and level loops, and (d) recognize when **pressure-compensated temperature control** is mandatory.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Pressure Cooker in a Mountain Hotel"

Imagine a pressure cooker in a hill-station hotel. The cook's job is to keep the steam pressure **just right** — too high and the food burns, too low and it never cooks. The cook has two knobs:

1. The **flame under the cooker** (analogous to **reboiler duty** — adding vapor)
2. The **lid vent / cooling water flow** (analogous to **condenser duty** — removing vapor)

A distillation column is the same idea, but in a tall, multi-tray "cooker" where each tray is a different recipe stage. The **column pressure** is the master knob, because once it changes, *every* tray's temperature changes simultaneously — and that is exactly what we want for a stable separation.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- Standard column overhead pressure: **101.325 kPa (1 atm)** for atmospheric columns; **20–400 kPa** for vacuum; **up to ~2000 kPa** for pressure columns.
- Typical time constant of pressure loop: **seconds to a few minutes** (very fast).
- Typical time constant of temperature loop: **5 to 60 minutes** (very slow).
- Typical condenser cooling-water-to-condensate ΔT: **5 °C to 15 °C**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Pressure Control Cannot Be Ignored

Pressure inside a column influences separation in three fundamental ways:

| Effect | Physical Mechanism | Engineering Consequence |
|---|---|---|
| **Effect on Boiling Point** | Clausius–Clapeyron equation — boiling point rises with pressure | Tray temperature shifts → changes relative volatility → changes composition |
| **Effect on Relative Volatility (α)** | α generally **decreases** with increasing pressure | Separation becomes **harder** at high pressure |
| **Effect on Internal Flow Regime** | Higher P → higher vapor density → larger volumetric flow | Risk of **flooding**, **weeping**, or **entrainment** changes |

## 2.2 The Steady-State Pressure Balance

For an ideal overhead system with total condenser, the steady-state pressure at the top of the column is governed by the **vapor condensation equilibrium**:

$$Q_{C} = U \cdot A \cdot (T_{C,out} - T_{cw,in})$$

where:
- $Q_C$ = heat removed by condenser (W)
- $U$ = overall heat-transfer coefficient (W/m²·K)
- $A$ = heat-transfer area (m²)
- $T_{C,out}$ = condensate temperature (≈ overhead vapor temperature)
- $T_{cw,in}$ = cooling water inlet temperature

The overhead vapor temperature is **directly linked** to the column top pressure through the **Antoine equation**:

$$\log_{10}(P) = A - \frac{B}{C + T}$$

This is the **master link** between pressure and tray temperature that the KTU examiner loves to test.

## 2.3 Manipulated Variable (MV) Options for Pressure Control

| MV # | Manipulated Variable | Mechanism | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **MV-1** | Condenser cooling-water flow | Changes $Q_C$ | **Most common — atmospheric columns** |
| **MV-2** | Condenser flooded area / subcooling | Changes effective $A$ | Condensate-level (flooded) control |
| **MV-3** | Vapor bypass around condenser | Splits vapor between condenser & bypass | Vacuum columns |
| **MV-4** | Vent / bleed valve on accumulator | Vents non-condensables | Inert-containing systems |
| **MV-5** | Reboiler steam flow | Indirectly changes vapor generation | Bottom pressure control |
| **MV-6** | Hot-vapor bypass in vacuum systems | Adds/removes vapor load | Vacuum columns |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Rule:** For an **atmospheric column with a total condenser**, the **canonical** answer is: *manipulate cooling-water flow to control overhead pressure*. Any deviation from this (e.g., vapor bypass) is considered an *advanced* scheme and must be justified.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_{b}(P) = \dfrac{B}{A - \log_{10}P} - C$ | Inverted Antoine equation — gives tray temperature from pressure |
| 2 | $K = \exp\!\left(\dfrac{-\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T}-\dfrac{1}{T_{ref}}\right)\right)$ | Van't Hoff relation — equilibrium K-value vs. T |
| 3 | $\alpha = \dfrac{K_{LK}}{K_{HK}}$ | Relative volatility (LK = light key, HK = heavy key) |
| 4 | $N_{min} = \dfrac{\ln\!\left[\dfrac{x_{D}}{1-x_{D}}\cdot\dfrac{1-x_{B}}{x_{B}}\right]}{\ln \alpha_{avg}}$ | Fenske equation — minimum stages |
| 5 | $R_{min} = \dfrac{x_{D} - y_{q}}{y_{q} - x_{q}}$ | Underwood / Gilliland minimum reflux |
| 6 | $P_{top} = P_{accum} + \Delta P_{line} + \Delta P_{cond}$ | Pressure drop balance — overhead system |
| 7 | $\tau_{P} \approx \dfrac{V \cdot \rho_{v}}{Q_{C}}$ | Approximate pressure-loop time constant |
| 8 | $\text{PC factor} = \dfrac{dT_{top}/dP_{top}}{dT_{top}/dx_{top}}$ | Sensitivity ratio (used in RGA) |

> **Important KTU Board Notation Convention:** Distinguish $K$ (equilibrium constant) from $K_C$ (controller gain). Examiners penalize mixing these up.

## 2.5 Pressure–Temperature Interaction (The "Why" Behind Pressure-Compensated Control)

Because tray temperature $T$ is a function of **both** composition $x$ **and** pressure $P$:

$$T = f(x, P)$$

A simple temperature controller will interpret a **pressure disturbance** as a **composition disturbance** and move the reflux or boil-up wrongly. The remedy is to **measure pressure, compute a pressure-corrected temperature set point**, and use that as the controlled variable:

$$T_{corr} = T_{measured} - \left(\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_{x} \cdot (P - P_{ref})$$

This is the essence of **pressure-compensated temperature control**, frequently asked in KTU Module 2.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Clausius–Clapeyron Approximation for Boiling-Point Shift

The exact Clausius–Clapeyron equation is:

$$\dfrac{dP}{dT} = \dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{T \cdot \Delta V}$$

Assuming the vapor behaves as an ideal gas ($\Delta V \approx RT/P$) and $\Delta H_{vap}$ is constant over the small interval, separation of variables gives:

$$\int_{P_1}^{P_2} \dfrac{dP}{P} = \dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\int_{T_1}^{T_2}\dfrac{dT}{T^{2}}$$

Evaluating both integrals:

$$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2}-\dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)$$

Now, suppose pressure changes from $P_1$ to $P_2 = P_1 + \Delta P$ (small perturbation). To first order in $\Delta P$:

$$\ln\!\left(1 + \dfrac{\Delta P}{P_1}\right) \approx \dfrac{\Delta P}{P_1}$$

Similarly, expanding $1/T_2$ around $1/T_1$:

$$\dfrac{1}{T_2} = \dfrac{1}{T_1 + \Delta T} \approx \dfrac{1}{T_1}\left(1 - \dfrac{\Delta T}{T_1}\right)$$

Substituting:

$$\dfrac{\Delta P}{P_1} = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\cdot\left(-\dfrac{\Delta T}{T_1^{2}}\right)$$

Rearranging, the **pressure-induced temperature shift** is:

$$\boxed{\;\Delta T = \dfrac{R\,T_1^{2}}{\Delta H_{vap}\,P_1}\cdot \Delta P\;}$$

> **Numerical sanity check (KTU-favourite numbers):** For benzene at 1 atm, $T_1 = 353\,\text{K}$, $\Delta H_{vap} = 30{,}800\,\text{J/mol}$, $P_1 = 101{,}325\,\text{Pa}$, $R = 8.314\,\text{J/mol·K}$:
>
> $$\dfrac{\Delta T}{\Delta P} = \dfrac{8.314 \times 353^{2}}{30{,}800 \times 101{,}325} \approx 3.3 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{K/Pa}$$
>
> So a **5 kPa** swing in pressure shifts the bubble-point by roughly **1.65 K** — easily large enough to confuse a temperature controller.

## 3.2 Derivation: Minimum Number of Stages vs. Pressure (Fenske + α(P) link)

Starting with the Fenske equation:

$$N_{min} = \dfrac{\ln\!\left[\dfrac{x_{D}}{1-x_{D}}\cdot\dfrac{1-x_{B}}{x_{B}}\right]}{\ln \alpha_{avg}}$$

The relative volatility depends on temperature and pressure. For two components with latent heats $L_1, L_2$:

$$\alpha(T) = \alpha_{ref}\cdot \exp\!\left[\dfrac{(L_2 - L_1)}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T} - \dfrac{1}{T_{ref}}\right)\right]$$

Substituting $T = T_{ref} + \Delta T$ and the $\Delta T(P)$ relation derived above, the closed-form dependence of $N_{min}$ on $P$ emerges. The **directional result** (which is what KTU rewards) is:

$$\dfrac{\partial N_{min}}{\partial P} = -\dfrac{N_{min}}{\alpha\ln\alpha}\cdot\dfrac{\partial \alpha}{\partial P}$$

Since $\partial \alpha / \partial P < 0$ for nearly all common systems, we conclude:

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering conclusion:** A *rise* in column pressure *increases* $N_{min}$ (more stages required) and *decreases* the *real* reflux ratio, so the column either floods or fails purity spec. **Stable pressure is therefore essential to stable separation.**

## 3.3 Derivation: Relative Gain Array (RGA) for the Pressure–Temperature Pair

Consider a 2×2 system with controlled variables $(P,\,T)$ and manipulated variables $(Q_C,\,Q_R)$ (condenser & reboiler duties). The steady-state gain matrix (signs and magnitudes) is:

$$K = \begin{bmatrix} k_{P,C} & k_{P,R} \\ k_{T,C} & k_{T,R} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -a & +b \\ -c & +d \end{bmatrix}$$

where $a, b, c, d > 0$ and typically $a, d \gg b, c$ (pressure responds fastest to condenser, temperature responds fastest to reboiler).

The RGA pairing index is:

$$\lambda_{11} = \dfrac{k_{P,C}\,k_{T,R} - k_{P,R}\,k_{T,C}}{k_{P,C}\,k_{T,R} - k_{P,R}\,k_{T,C}} = \dfrac{(-a)(+d) - (+b)(-c)}{(-a)(+d) - (+b)(-c)} = 1$$

Hence $\lambda_{11} = 1$ exactly — meaning **no steady-state interaction** for the symmetric sign pattern, and the **diagonal pairing** $(P - Q_C,\; T - Q_R)$ is the recommended (canonical) KTU-board pairing.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — "When to Use Which Control Scheme"

| Scheme | Best For | Limitation | KTU marks (typical) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Condenser-duty pressure control** | Atmospheric columns, total condenser | Slow cooling-water response | 3 (definition) |
| **Vapor bypass pressure control** | Vacuum columns, partial condensers | Capital cost | 7 (scheme + diagram) |
| **Pressure-compensated temperature** | Tight purity specs, varying P | Requires accurate P sensor | 14 (full design) |
| **Vent/bleed control** | Inert accumulation, oxidizing systems | Product loss | 7 |
| **Bottom pressure via reboiler steam** | Reboiler-limited columns | Affects temperature loop directly | 7 |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics (Mermaid Block-Level Architecture)

## 4.1 Canonical Atmospheric Column — Overhead Pressure Control

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph COL["DISTILLATION COLUMN"]
        TR1[Feed Tray]
        TR2[Top Tray - Temperature Sensor T]
        TR3[Bottom Tray - Level Sensor L_B]
    end

    subgraph COND["TOTAL CONDENSER"]
        CW[Cooling Water In]
        CWO[Cooling Water Out]
        CONDDRUM[Reflux Drum]
    end

    subgraph LOOP1["PRESSURE LOOP - FAST"]
        PT[Pressure Transmitter P_top]
        PIC[PI Controller]
        CV1[Cooling Water Valve]
    end

    subgraph LOOP2["TEMPERATURE LOOP - SLOW"]
        TT[Temperature Transmitter T_top]
        TIC[TI Controller]
        RR[Reflux Flow Valve]
    end

    subgraph LOOP3["LEVEL LOOP"]
        LT[Level Transmitter L_drum]
        LIC[LI Controller]
        PROD[Distillate Product Valve]
    end

    TR2 --> COND
    COND --> CW
    COND --> CWO
    COND --> CONDDRUM
    CONDDRUM --> RR
    RR --> TR2
    CONDDRUM --> LT
    CONDDRUM --> PROD

    COND --> PT
    PT --> PIC
    PIC --> CV1
    CV1 --> COND

    TR2 --> TT
    TT --> TIC
    TIC --> RR

    LT --> LIC
    LIC --> PROD
```

**Interpretation for KTU Board Answer:**
- The **pressure loop** manipulates **cooling-water flow** (fastest available MV).
- The **temperature loop** manipulates **reflux flow** (slower, composition-related).
- The **level loop** manipulates **distillate product flow** (mass-balance).
- These three loops form the **canonical 3 × 3 override-free** structure.

## 4.2 Pressure-Compensated Temperature Control Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Temperature Sensor on Sensitive Tray] --> B[T_meas]
    C[Pressure Sensor at Column Top] --> D[P_meas]
    D --> E[Compute dT/dP using Antoine]
    B --> F[Adder: T_corr = T_meas - dT/dP * P_meas - P_ref]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Temperature Controller TIC]
    G --> H[Reflux Flow Valve]
    H --> I[Reflux to Column]
    I --> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> The signal processing in the **Adder block** effectively decouples the temperature CV from pressure disturbances, so the controller responds **only** to true composition changes.

## 4.3 Vacuum Column — Vapor-Bypass Pressure Control

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VAP[Overhead Vapor] --> SPLIT{Split}
    SPLIT -->|To Condenser| COND[Condenser]
    SPLIT -->|Bypass| BYPV[Bypass Leg with Control Valve]
    BYPASS_VALVE[Bypass Valve V_bp] --> BYPV
    COND --> ACC[Accumulator]
    BYPV --> ACC
    ACC --> VACC[Vacuum Pump]
    PT[Pressure Transmitter] --> PIC[PI Controller]
    PIC --> BYPASS_VALVE
    VACC -->|Discharge| OUT[Non-condensables to Vent]
```

**Why vapor bypass is needed for vacuum columns:**
The pressure is **below atmospheric**, so the driving force for condensation is small and condenser duty alone is sluggish. Opening a vapor bypass valve **adds vapor load** on the vacuum pump, **reducing the effective vacuum** (i.e., raising pressure), giving a fast MV.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]
**State any three reasons why column pressure must be tightly controlled in a distillation column.**

**Model Answer:**
1. Pressure directly affects tray temperature (via Clausius–Clapeyron / Antoine), so unstable pressure = unstable separation and off-spec product.
2. Pressure affects relative volatility $\alpha$ — higher pressure lowers $\alpha$, demanding more stages / reflux, risking flooding.
3. Pressure variations alter vapor density and internal flow, moving the column toward flooding, weeping, or entrainment limits, endangering safe operation.
*(Examiner accepts any three equivalent points.)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]
**Identify the canonical manipulated variable for overhead pressure control in an atmospheric distillation column with a total condenser. Justify your choice in one sentence.**

**Model Answer:**
The canonical MV is **cooling-water flow to the condenser**. Justification: It is the fastest, most direct means of removing overhead vapor, and the time constant of the pressure response (seconds) closely matches the cooling-water dynamics, yielding a tight, stable pressure loop.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Sample Paper Pattern]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **canonical 3-loop control structure** (pressure, temperature, level) of an atmospheric distillation column having a total condenser. State the controlled variable, manipulated variable, and the type of controller used in each loop. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the relationship between tray temperature and pressure using the Clausius–Clapeyron equation, and comment on the practical implications for temperature control. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** The canonical 3-loop structure consists of:
- **Loop 1 — Pressure control (PC):** CV = column top pressure $P_{top}$; MV = condenser cooling-water flow; Controller = PI (fast integral action); Transmitter = absolute pressure transmitter on the overhead line; Final control element = pneumatically/hydraulically actuated globe valve on cooling-water return line.
- **Loop 2 — Temperature control (TC):** CV = sensitive-tray temperature (typically 2–5 trays below the top); MV = reflux flow; Controller = PI with external reset; Transmitter = RTD / thermocouple in a thermowell.
- **Loop 3 — Reflux-drum level control (LC):** CV = reflux-drum level; MV = distillate product flow; Controller = PI; Transmitter = differential-pressure level transmitter.

The student must also draw the **block diagram** (refer to §4.1) and clearly label all three loops.

**(b)** See the full derivation in §3.1. The end result is:

$$\Delta T = \dfrac{R\,T_1^{2}}{\Delta H_{vap}\,P_1}\cdot \Delta P$$

Practical implications:
- A small pressure drift ($\Delta P \approx 5$ kPa) can shift $T$ by 1–2 K, easily comparable to a real composition-induced shift of 0.5–2 K.
- Therefore, a plain temperature controller will misread pressure disturbances as composition disturbances and **mis-actuate** the reflux valve.
- The remedy is **pressure-compensated temperature control** (§4.2) or the use of a **differential pressure (dP) controller** across a fixed section of trays as a proxy for composition.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Sample Paper Pattern]

**(a)** Compare **condenser-duty pressure control** with **vapor-bypass pressure control**. State the operating situations in which each is preferred, with a labelled sketch of the vapor-bypass scheme. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A distillation column operates at top pressure 105 kPa with benzene as the light key. The bubble-point of benzene at 101.325 kPa is 353.1 K and the latent heat of vaporization is 30,800 J/mol. Using the Clausius–Clapeyron approximation, estimate the new bubble-point temperature when the column pressure rises to 110 kPa. Comment on the implication for temperature-based composition control. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)**

| Feature | Condenser-duty control | Vapor-bypass control |
|---|---|---|
| MV | Cooling-water flow | Bypass valve opening |
| Speed | Fast for atmospheric columns | Fast for vacuum columns |
| Preferred when | Total condenser, P ≥ atmospheric | Partial condenser, vacuum service |
| Limitation | Slow response if cooling-water ΔT is small | Wastes energy; capital cost |

Vapor-bypass sketch: refer to §4.3 (Mermaid diagram).

**(b)** Use the derived relation:

$$\Delta T = \dfrac{R\,T_1^{2}}{\Delta H_{vap}\,P_1}\cdot \Delta P$$

Substituting: $R = 8.314$, $T_1 = 353.1$ K, $P_1 = 101{,}325$ Pa, $\Delta H_{vap} = 30{,}800$ J/mol, $\Delta P = 110{,}000 - 101{,}325 = 8{,}675$ Pa:

$$\dfrac{\Delta T}{\Delta P} = \dfrac{8.314 \times 353.1^{2}}{30{,}800 \times 101{,}325} = \dfrac{8.314 \times 124{,}679.6}{3.121 \times 10^{9}}$$

$$\dfrac{\Delta T}{\Delta P} = \dfrac{1.0367 \times 10^{6}}{3.121 \times 10^{9}} = 3.322 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{K/Pa}$$

Therefore:

$$\Delta T = 3.322 \times 10^{-4}\times 8{,}675 = 2.88\;\text{K}$$

New bubble-point: $T_2 = 353.1 + 2.88 = 355.98\;\text{K}$ (≈ 356 K).

**Implication for temperature-based control:** A pressure rise of just ~9 kPa causes a ~3 K shift in tray temperature — large enough to be misinterpreted as a composition change. Hence a **plain TC** will mis-actuate; the column must employ **pressure-compensated TC** or a **dP-based** composition proxy.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not** confuse the manipulated variable for *pressure* with the manipulated variable for *temperature*. The PC moves cooling water; the TC moves reflux. Mixing these up costs **2–3 marks** instantly.
> 2. **Do not** apply the Clausius–Clapeyron equation with non-consistent units. $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) demands $P$ in Pa and $\Delta H$ in J/mol — using kPa and kJ/mol is a frequent **silent error** that loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Do not** draw the P&ID without labeling transmitter, controller, and final control element distinctly (PT, PIC, PCV). Marks are split between these symbols.
> 4. **Do not** write "pressure affects temperature" without a quantitative statement — the KTU 2024 rubric awards full marks only when the **Antoine / Clausius** form is shown.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Pressure is the fastest CV in a column** — its loop must be tuned before temperature and composition loops.
- **Canonical MV for atmospheric column with total condenser = cooling-water flow.**
- **Vapor bypass is used for vacuum columns** where condensation driving force is weak.
- **Bottom pressure is usually NOT controlled directly** — it is a *result* of the top pressure plus tray pressure drops.
- **Relative volatility $\alpha$ decreases with pressure** — raising P makes separation harder.
- **Clausius–Clapeyron** gives the master link $dP/dT = \Delta H_{vap}/(T \Delta V)$; integrated form is the exam-favourite.
- **Antoine equation** $\log_{10} P = A - B/(C+T)$ is the engineer's working form of the vapor-pressure curve.
- **Pressure-compensated temperature** computes $T_{corr}$ by removing the pressure-induced component from the measured tray temperature.
- **RGA pairing** for $(P, T)$ vs. $(Q_C, Q_R)$ is exactly 1 on the diagonal — no steady-state interaction, but **dynamic interaction** exists because of the time-constant mismatch.
- **3-loop structure** (P, T, L) is the **canonical** atmospheric-column control architecture; deviations require explicit justification.
- **Inert accumulation** in the reflux drum mandates a vent/bleed valve; ignoring this leads to **pressure drift even with full cooling water**.
- **Total condenser** = single liquid phase leaving the condenser; **partial condenser** = two-phase exit — different control strategies apply.
- **Pressure-loop time constant** $\tau_P \approx V \rho_v / Q_C$ — small vapor inventory + high heat removal = fast loop.
- **Energy efficiency tip:** Pressure control by cooling water alone wastes the least energy; vapor bypass wastes the most.
- **KTU Board buzzwords to use verbatim:** "Clausius–Clapeyron", "Antoine", "relative volatility", "vapor bypass", "sensitive tray", "pressure-compensated temperature", "RGA pairing".

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Column Pressure Control with Liquid Distillate and Inert
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT743 - INDUSTRIAL PROCESS CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Column Pressure Control with Liquid Distillate and Inert

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Column Pressure Control with Liquid Distillate and Inert** is a specialized pressure regulation scheme employed in distillation columns where the overhead product is withdrawn as a **liquid** stream from the reflux accumulator (reflux drum) and where **non-condensable inert gases** (such as nitrogen, carbon dioxide, methane, or hydrogen) are liberated or entrained along with the rising vapor inside the column. In this configuration, the manipulated variable for pressure regulation is the **vent (bleed) valve on the reflux accumulator**, which releases the accumulated non-condensable gases to the atmosphere, flare, or recovery header.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The presence of inerts changes the pressure dynamics from a simple vapor–condensate balance to a coupled *partial pressure* problem governed by **Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures** and the **ideal gas law**.

The general operating equation governing the vapor–liquid equilibrium in the presence of inerts at the top of the column is expressed as:

$$P_{top} = P_{vap} + P_{inert}$$

where:
- $P_{top}$ = total column top pressure
- $P_{vap}$ = partial pressure of condensable vapor (the light key component)
- $P_{inert}$ = partial pressure of non-condensable inert gas

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Whistling Pressure Cooker"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Pump:** Imagine a **pressure cooker** on a stove.
> - The **cooking food + water vapor** is like the **condensable vapor** rising up the column.
> - The **steam vent weight (whistle)** that jiggles to release excess steam is exactly like the **vent valve** on the reflux drum.
> - If you seal the cooker completely, pressure rises uncontrollably. If you keep the whistle slightly open, the pressure stabilizes at a desired value.
> - **Air leaks** into the cooker (representing inerts like N₂) do not condense — they just accumulate and must be *purged* periodically. This is precisely why a continuous vent is required in real columns.

The role of the inert bleed is therefore to **prevent partial pressure dilution** of the condensable vapor, which would otherwise raise the bubble point of the mixture and ruin separation efficiency.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Engineering Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Values (Bolded for Recall):**
> - **Standard vent gas velocity:** **60 to 90 m/s** through the vent valve to prevent back-diffusion of moisture.
> - **Typical column top pressure range:** **1.05 to 5.0 kg/cm² (g)** in refinery distillation.
> - **Acceptable inert partial pressure in reflux drum:** **< 0.05 atm** to maintain separation.
> - **Material:** Vent piping is usually **carbon steel** for hydrocarbon inerts and **stainless steel (SS 304/316)** for corrosive gases like H₂S or CO₂.

## 1.4 Why Pressure Control Matters in a Distillation Column

| Phenomenon | Effect on Column |
|---|---|
| ↑ Pressure | ↑ Boiling point of mixture → poor separation |
| ↑ Pressure | ↑ Tray temperature profile → potential flooding |
| Inert accumulation | ↓ Partial pressure of vapor → loss of condensation driving force |
| Inert accumulation | Overhead condenser becomes gas-blanketed → loss of heat transfer |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physical Architecture of the Control Loop

The column pressure control loop with liquid distillate and inerts consists of the following instrumentation chain:

1. **Pressure Transmitter (PT)** mounted on the **reflux accumulator / overhead receiver** (not on the column top, because that location is exposed to two-phase flow).
2. **Pressure Controller (PIC)** — typically **PI mode** (Proportional + Integral), reverse acting for a fail-closed vent valve.
3. **Control Valve on the Vent Line** — usually a **small-bore globe valve** or a **double-block-and-bleed** configuration.
4. **Reflux Pump** delivers condensed liquid back to the column; the **distillate product pump** controls liquid level in the accumulator.
5. **Overhead Condenser (Total Condenser)** converts all rising vapor to liquid.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of the Pressure Dynamics

> [!NOTE]
> **Stepwise Reasoning (How pressure responds to disturbances):**

**Step 1 — Disturbance enters the column** (e.g., feed composition change, reboiler steam surge, or in-leakage of air).

**Step 2 — More vapor rises to the overhead system.** Some of this vapor is condensable, some is inert (if present).

**Step 3 — The total condenser condenses the condensable fraction** (mostly the light key) into liquid. The inert fraction cannot condense at the coolant temperature available.

**Step 4 — Inerts accumulate in the reflux drum** (headspace above the liquid), displacing condensable vapor molecules from the gas space.

**Step 5 — Per Dalton's Law**, even though the *total* pressure may be the same, the *partial pressure of condensable vapor* drops, so condensation rate falls.

**Step 6 — Vapor "backs up" in the condenser and column**, increasing column top pressure.

**Step 7 — The PT senses the rise** and the PIC modulates the **vent valve open**.

**Step 8 — Inerts are released**, partial pressure of vapor recovers, condensation resumes, and pressure returns to setpoint.

## 2.3 Inert Dynamics — Why the Vent is Mandatory

Non-condensable gases have a **dew point far below** the operating temperature of the condenser. Therefore, they will *never* liquefy under normal operation and have only one route out of the system: **the vent line**. Failure to provide this route results in:

- Inert blanketing of condenser tubes
- Loss of heat transfer coefficient (U) — can drop by **50–70%**
- Steady rise in column pressure with no ability to reject it

## 2.4 The KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they form the core of any 14-mark derivation question.**

| # | Equation | Description | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{top} = P_{vap} + P_{inert}$ | Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures | **kPa, atm, or bar** |
| 2 | $P_{inert} = y_{inert} \cdot P_{top}$ | Partial pressure of inert in vapor space | **kPa** |
| 3 | $y_{inert} = \dfrac{n_{inert}}{n_{vap} + n_{inert}}$ | Mole fraction of inert in overhead vapor | dimensionless |
| 4 | $P \cdot V = n \cdot R \cdot T$ | Ideal Gas Law applied to reflux drum headspace | $P$ in Pa, $V$ in m³, $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) |
| 5 | $\dfrac{dP}{dt} = \dfrac{R \cdot T}{V} \cdot \left(F_{in}^{vap} - F_{out}^{vent} - F_{cond}\right)$ | Dynamic mass balance on drum headspace | **Pa/s** |
| 6 | $\dfrac{dP_{inert}}{dt} = \dfrac{R \cdot T}{V} \cdot \left(F_{in}^{inert} - F_{out}^{vent}\right)$ | Inert-only dynamic balance | **Pa/s** |
| 7 | $K_p \left(1 + \dfrac{1}{\tau_I \, s}\right)$ | PI controller transfer function | dimensionless |
| 8 | $T_{boil} = T_{boil}^{\circ} + \Delta T_{pressure}$ | Effect of pressure on boiling point (qualitative) | **K or °C** |
| 9 | $\Delta H_{vap} \left(\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}\right) = R \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)$ | **Clausius–Clapeyron Equation** (advanced) | J/mol, K, Pa |
| 10 | $q_{vent} = C_v \cdot \sqrt{\Delta P_{valve}}$ | Valve flow equation (linear/equal %) | m³/h |

> **Notation Quick-Glance:**
> - $F_{in}^{vap}$ = molar flow rate of condensable vapor entering drum
> - $F_{out}^{vent}$ = molar flow rate vented (manipulated variable)
> - $F_{cond}$ = molar condensation rate
> - $V$ = effective gas volume in reflux drum headspace
> - $\tau_I$ = integral time of the controller

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where this scheme is used in industry:**
> - **Crude oil atmospheric and vacuum distillation units (ADU / VDU)**
> - **Ethylene and propylene fractionators** in petrochemical plants
> - **Air separation units (ASU)** — N₂, O₂, Ar columns
> - **Ammonia and methanol synthesis gas columns** — where N₂ and H₂ act as inerts
> - **Natural gas processing** — CO₂ and H₂S must be vented from the de-ethanizer / de-propanizer overhead systems

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Steady-State Material Balance on the Reflux Drum

We consider the reflux drum as a control volume at steady state. Let:
- $V$ = volume of vapor space in drum (m³)
- $F_{in}$ = molar flow of vapor entering drum from condenser (kmol/s)
- $F_{cond}$ = molar flow of liquid condensate leaving (kmol/s)
- $F_{vent}$ = molar flow of vent gas leaving via control valve (kmol/s)
- $F_{ref}$ = molar flow of reflux returned to column (kmol/s)

**Step 1 — Write the total molar balance:**

$$F_{in} = F_{cond} + F_{vent}$$

**Step 2 — Split the inlet into condensable and inert components:**

$$F_{in} = F_{vap} + F_{inert}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Dalton's Law to the headspace:**

$$P_{top} = y_{vap} P_{top} + y_{inert} P_{top} = P_{vap} + P_{inert}$$

**Step 4 — Express inert mole fraction in terms of flow rates:**

$$y_{inert} = \dfrac{F_{inert}}{F_{vap} + F_{inert}}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute into the partial pressure expression:**

$$P_{inert} = \dfrac{F_{inert}}{F_{vap} + F_{inert}} \cdot P_{top}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for the required vent flow to keep $P_{inert}$ below a safe limit $P_{inert}^{max}$:**

$$F_{vent}^{inert} = F_{inert} - \dfrac{P_{inert}^{max}}{P_{top}} \cdot \left(F_{vap} + F_{inert}\right)$$

This equation is the **key design equation** used to size the vent valve in KTU examination problems.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Dynamic Pressure Response (Transfer Function Approach)

For a control-engineering question, we now derive the dynamic model. Apply the ideal gas law to the drum headspace:

$$P \cdot V = n \cdot R \cdot T \quad \Rightarrow \quad n = \dfrac{P \cdot V}{R \cdot T}$$

**Step 1 — Differentiate with respect to time (assuming $T$ and $V$ constant):**

$$\dfrac{dn}{dt} = \dfrac{V}{R \cdot T} \cdot \dfrac{dP}{dt}$$

**Step 2 — Equate to net molar accumulation = inflow − outflow:**

$$\dfrac{V}{R \cdot T} \cdot \dfrac{dP}{dt} = F_{in}^{vap}(t) - F_{out}^{vent}(t) - F_{cond}(t)$$

**Step 3 — Define small deviation variables:**
- $P(t) = \bar{P} + p'(t)$
- $F_{in}^{vap}(t) = \bar{F}_{in}^{vap} + f_{in}'(t)$
- $F_{out}^{vent}(t) = \bar{F}_{out}^{vent} + f_{v}'(t)$
- $F_{cond}(t) = \bar{F}_{cond} + f_{c}'(t)$

**Step 4 — At steady state, the net balance is zero, so subtracting it yields:**

$$\dfrac{V}{R \cdot T} \cdot \dfrac{dp'(t)}{dt} = f_{in}'(t) - f_{v}'(t) - f_{c}'(t)$$

**Step 5 — Take the Laplace transform (zero initial conditions):**

$$\dfrac{V}{R \cdot T} \cdot s \cdot P'(s) = F_{in}'(s) - F_{v}'(s) - F_{c}'(s)$$

**Step 6 — Express condensation as a function of pressure (more pressure → more vapor mass → more condensation):**

$$F_{c}(t) = K_c \cdot P(t) \quad \Rightarrow \quad F_{c}'(s) = K_c \cdot P'(s)$$

**Step 7 — Substitute and rearrange to find the transfer function between vent flow and pressure:**

$$\left(\dfrac{V}{R \cdot T} \cdot s + K_c\right) P'(s) = F_{in}'(s) - F_{v}'(s)$$

$$\boxed{\;\dfrac{P'(s)}{F_{v}'(s)} = \dfrac{-1}{\dfrac{V}{R \cdot T} \cdot s + K_c} = \dfrac{-K_p}{\tau_p \, s + 1}\;}$$

where:
- $K_p = \dfrac{1}{K_c}$ = process gain
- $\tau_p = \dfrac{V}{R \cdot T \cdot K_c}$ = process time constant (seconds)

**Step 8 — With a PI controller, the closed-loop transfer function is:**

$$G_{CL}(s) = \dfrac{G_c(s) \cdot G_p(s)}{1 + G_c(s) \cdot G_p(s)} = \dfrac{K_c \, K_p \left(\tau_I s + 1\right)}{\tau_I \, s \left(\tau_p s + 1\right) + K_c \, K_p \left(\tau_I s + 1\right)}$$

This is the **first-order-plus-integral closed-loop response** the student must derive in the KTU exam.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Effect of Inert on Bubble Point (Quick Numerics)

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked example to test understanding:**

**Given:** A column operates at total top pressure of **120 kPa**. The vapor contains **95 mol% condensable light key** and **5 mol% inert N₂**.

**Step 1 — Compute partial pressure of condensable vapor:**

$$P_{vap} = y_{vap} \cdot P_{top} = 0.95 \times 120 = 114 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 2 — Compute partial pressure of inert:**

$$P_{inert} = y_{inert} \cdot P_{top} = 0.05 \times 120 = 6 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 3 — If the inert purge fails and $y_{inert}$ rises to 20%:**

$$P_{vap, new} = 0.80 \times 120 = 96 \text{ kPa}$$

This represents a **drop in condensing driving force of 15.8%** — and this is why vent valve failure is a critical alarm in the DCS.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 P&ID-Level Block Architecture (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Reboiler Steam] --> B[Distillation Column]
    B --> C[Overhead Vapor Riser]
    C --> D[Total Condenser]
    D --> E[Reflux Accumulator Drum]
    E --> F[Reflux Pump]
    F --> G[Back to Column]
    E --> H[Distillate Product Pump]
    H --> I[Distillate Storage]
    E --> J[Vent Line to Flare]
    J --> K[To Atmosphere or Recovery]

    PT[Pressure Transmitter PT-1001] --> PIC[PIC Controller]
    PIC --> VENT[Vent Control Valve PCV-1001]

    PT -. senses .-> E
    VENT -. mounted on .-> J

    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style B fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style D fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style E fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style J fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style PT fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style PIC fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style VENT fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

## 4.2 Signal-Flow Diagram of the Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP[Setpoint Pressure] --> SUM[Summing Junction]
    PTM[PT Measurement] --> SUM
    SUM --> ERR[Error Signal e t]
    ERR --> CTRL[PI Controller Kc 1 plus 1 over tauI s]
    CTRL --> CV[Control Valve Output]
    CV --> PROC[Process Drum Dynamics 1 over tauP s plus 1]
    PROC --> DP[Disturbance Inert In-leakage]
    DP --> PROC
    PROC --> OUT[Column Top Pressure]
    OUT -. feedback .-> PTM

    style SP fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333
    style CTRL fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333
    style PROC fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333
    style OUT fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333
```

## 4.3 Cause-and-Effect Matrix for the Control Loop

| Cause (Input Disturbance) | Effect on Drum | Sensor Response | Controller Action | Final Result |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| ↑ Reboiler steam | ↑ Vapor to condenser | PT rises | PIC closes reflux slightly / vents inerts | Pressure stabilizes |
| ↑ Inert in feed | Inert accumulates in drum | PT rises slowly | PIC opens vent valve wider | Inert partial pressure falls |
| ↓ Cooling water | Less condensation | PT rises fast | PIC cannot help much; alarm trips | Column trips on high pressure |
| ↓ Column feed | ↓ Vapor to condenser | PT falls | PIC closes vent valve | Pressure stabilizes at setpoint |
| Vent valve stuck open | Continuous loss of vapor | PT falls below setpoint | PIC commands valve closed (saturated) | Loss of product; high fuel cost |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Q1]**
**Explain briefly why a vent valve is mandatory on the reflux accumulator of a distillation column when inerts are present in the feed.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Inerts (non-condensable gases such as N₂, CO₂, CH₄) cannot be liquefied at the operating temperature of the overhead condenser. As they enter the reflux drum with the vapor stream, they accumulate in the headspace and by **Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures** reduce the partial pressure of the condensable vapor. This impairs condensation, raises total column pressure, and degrades separation. A vent valve on the accumulator bleeds the inerts continuously, keeping the inert partial pressure below a safe limit (typically < 5% of total pressure) and thereby maintaining stable column operation.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Q2]**
**State Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures and its significance in the context of column pressure control.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Dalton's Law states that in a mixture of non-reacting gases, the **total pressure equals the sum of the partial pressures of the individual components**:
$P_{total} = \sum_i P_i = \sum_i y_i P_{total}$.
In column pressure control, the law is significant because inert gases in the overhead vapor occupy a portion of the total pressure, reducing the partial pressure of the condensable vapor. This reduces the condensation driving force, allowing pressure to drift upward. The vent valve on the reflux drum manipulates the inert mole fraction, and hence the partial pressure distribution, to maintain the desired total pressure.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *(Internal Choice Option 1)*
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2 Full Question]**

**(a)** Derive the dynamic transfer function relating the vent valve flow to the column top pressure for a reflux accumulator with inert accumulation. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A distillation column operates with a reflux drum of vapor headspace volume **0.5 m³**, temperature **40 °C (313 K)**, and condensation rate constant $K_c$ of **0.002 kmol/(s·kPa)**. The current operating pressure is **150 kPa**. A step increase in inerts of **0.05 kmol/h** enters the drum. Calculate the **new steady-state pressure** and the **process time constant $\tau_p$**. Take R = 8.314 kJ/(kmol·K). **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

**Assumptions** (1 mark each, list first):
1. Drum headspace behaves as an ideal gas.
2. Temperature and liquid volume in the drum are constant.
3. Condensation rate is proportional to drum pressure: $F_{cond} = K_c P$.
4. The vent valve is the only outlet for inert gas.
5. Pressure is uniform throughout the headspace (well-mixed assumption).

**Derivation** (each logical step 1 mark):

**Step 1 — Ideal gas law applied to drum headspace:**

$$P V = n R T \quad \Rightarrow \quad n = \dfrac{P V}{R T}$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate w.r.t. time:**

$$\dfrac{dn}{dt} = \dfrac{V}{R T} \dfrac{dP}{dt}$$

**Step 3 — Net molar balance on the headspace (vapor + inert):**

$$\dfrac{dn}{dt} = F_{in}^{vap} - F_{out}^{vent} - F_{cond}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute:**

$$\dfrac{V}{R T} \dfrac{dP}{dt} = F_{in}^{vap} - F_{out}^{vent} - F_{cond}$$

**Step 5 — Introduce deviation variables and linearize:**

$$\dfrac{V}{R T} \dfrac{dp'}{dt} = f_{in}' - f_{v}' - f_{cond}'$$

**Step 6 — Take Laplace transform:**

$$\dfrac{V}{R T} s P'(s) = F_{in}'(s) - F_{v}'(s) - F_{cond}'(s)$$

**Step 7 — Substitute $F_{cond}'(s) = K_c P'(s)$ and solve:**

$$\dfrac{P'(s)}{F_{v}'(s)} = \dfrac{-1}{\dfrac{V}{R T} s + K_c} = \dfrac{-K_p}{\tau_p s + 1}$$

where $K_p = 1/K_c$ and $\tau_p = V/(R T K_c)$. **(1 mark)**

### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given data:** $V = 0.5$ m³, $T = 313$ K, $K_c = 0.002$ kmol/(s·kPa), $P_0 = 150$ kPa, R = 8.314 kJ/(kmol·K), inert step input $\Delta F_{inert} = 0.05$ kmol/h $= 1.389 \times 10^{-5}$ kmol/s.

**Step 1 — Convert R to consistent units:**

$$R = 8.314 \text{ kJ/(kmol·K)} = 8.314 \text{ kPa·m}^3/(\text{kmol·K})$$

**Step 2 — Compute the process time constant $\tau_p$:**

$$\tau_p = \dfrac{V}{R T K_c} = \dfrac{0.5}{8.314 \times 313 \times 0.002}$$

**Numerator:** $0.5$
**Denominator:** $8.314 \times 313 = 2602.28$; multiplied by $0.002$ gives $5.2046$

$$\tau_p = \dfrac{0.5}{5.2046} = 0.0961 \text{ s} \approx 0.096 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the process gain $K_p$:**

$$K_p = \dfrac{1}{K_c} = \dfrac{1}{0.002} = 500 \text{ kPa/(kmol/s)}$$

**Step 4 — Steady-state analysis (as $t \to \infty$, $dP/dt = 0$):**

At steady state with a step inert input of magnitude $F_{inert}^{step}$:
$$0 = F_{inert}^{step} - F_{vent}^{ss} - K_c \Delta P$$
The vent valve will open to release the extra inert, so $F_{vent}^{ss} = F_{inert}^{step}$ (the extra inerts must be vented):

$$\Delta P = 0 \text{ (if vent perfectly compensates)}$$

However, if we assume the vent cannot fully release inerts and a residual pressure rise occurs, the new pressure is given by integrating the differential equation:

$$P_{new} = P_0 + \dfrac{F_{inert}^{step}}{K_c} = 150 + \dfrac{1.389 \times 10^{-5}}{0.002}$$

$$P_{new} = 150 + 0.00695 = 150.007 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 5 — Final result interpretation:**

$$\boxed{\tau_p \approx 0.096 \text{ s}, \quad P_{new} \approx 150.007 \text{ kPa}}$$

The very small time constant indicates the drum responds quickly to disturbances — typical of small overhead receivers. **(1 mark for interpretation and final answer)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *(Internal Choice Option 2)*
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2 Alternate]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the column pressure control scheme when the distillate is withdrawn as a liquid and the system contains non-condensable inert gases. Identify all loop elements and the manipulated, controlled, and disturbance variables. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A column has overhead vapor flow **100 kmol/h** containing **2 mol% inerts** and operates at **200 kPa**. If the inerts must not exceed **4 kPa partial pressure** to maintain condensation efficiency, calculate:
   (i) The required vent flow rate in kmol/h.
   (ii) The maximum allowable inerts in the feed if the vent is limited to **3 kmol/h**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

### Part (a) — Block Diagram & Explanation (7 marks)

**Diagram (3 marks):** Draw the P&ID showing — Column → Condenser → Reflux Drum, with PT sensing drum pressure, PIC controller sending signal to vent control valve on the vent line. (Mermaid equivalent already shown in Section 4.1.)

**Identification of variables (2 marks):**

| Variable Type | Quantity |
|---|---|
| **Controlled Variable (CV)** | Column top / reflux drum pressure |
| **Manipulated Variable (MV)** | Vent valve position (inert bleed rate) |
| **Disturbance Variables (DVs)** | Feed composition (inert content), reboiler duty, cooling water temperature, ambient conditions |

**Working principle (2 marks):**

When the inerts rise, the partial pressure of the condensable vapor falls, and the pressure transmitter (PT) detects the rise in total pressure. The PIC controller (PI mode, reverse-acting) opens the vent valve PCV to release the inerts. The inert partial pressure falls, condensation driving force is restored, and total pressure returns to setpoint. If cooling water fails, the vent alone cannot help, and the high-pressure interlock must trip the column.

### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Total vapor flow $F_{in} = 100$ kmol/h
- Inert mole fraction $y_{inert} = 0.02$ (i.e., 2 mol%)
- Total pressure $P_{top} = 200$ kPa
- Max allowable inert partial pressure $P_{inert}^{max} = 4$ kPa

### Part (b)(i) — Required vent flow (3 marks)

**Step 1 — Compute actual partial pressure of inerts at current conditions:**

$$P_{inert, actual} = y_{inert} \cdot P_{top} = 0.02 \times 200 = 4 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 2 — Interpret:** The current inert partial pressure is exactly at the maximum allowable (4 kPa). Therefore, the **minimum vent flow must equal the full inert inflow**:

$$\boxed{F_{vent} = F_{inert} = y_{inert} \cdot F_{in} = 0.02 \times 100 = 2 \text{ kmol/h}}$$

### Part (b)(ii) — Maximum inerts in feed (4 marks)

**Step 1 — Set vent at its maximum capacity $F_{vent,max} = 3$ kmol/h.**

**Step 2 — At steady state, vent flow = inert inflow:**

$$F_{inert}^{max} = F_{vent}^{max} = 3 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**Step 3 — Compute maximum allowable inert mole fraction:**

$$y_{inert}^{max} = \dfrac{F_{inert}^{max}}{F_{in}} = \dfrac{3}{100} = 0.03 \text{ or } 3 \text{ mol\%}$$

**Step 4 — Verify the partial pressure constraint:**

$$P_{inert} = y_{inert}^{max} \cdot P_{top} = 0.03 \times 200 = 6 \text{ kPa}$$

This **exceeds 4 kPa**, which means the inert constraint is now violated. The **true binding constraint** is the partial pressure limit, not the vent capacity. So we must recalculate:

**Step 5 — Use the partial pressure constraint directly:**

$$y_{inert}^{max} = \dfrac{P_{inert}^{max}}{P_{top}} = \dfrac{4}{200} = 0.02 \text{ or } 2 \text{ mol\%}$$

**Step 6 — Recompute required vent flow for 2 mol% inerts:**

$$F_{vent} = 0.02 \times 100 = 2 \text{ kmol/h} \leq 3 \text{ kmol/h (vent capacity)} \;\;\checkmark$$

**Step 7 — Final answer:**

$$\boxed{y_{inert}^{max} = 2 \text{ mol\%}, \quad F_{vent}^{required} = 2 \text{ kmol/h}}$$

The vent capacity of 3 kmol/h is more than sufficient; the **partial pressure limit is the binding constraint**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert R into consistent units** (kJ vs J vs Pa·m³). Mismatched units give wrong $\tau_p$ — usually a 2-mark loss.
> 2. **Drawing the vent valve on the column top instead of the reflux drum** — the exam specifically tests *placement*. Vent on the column top is a 1–2 mark penalty.
> 3. **Ignoring inerts entirely** when asked about pressure control with a "liquid distillate" — many students default to coolant-flow manipulation. The KTU examiner will deduct up to 3 marks for this conceptual error.
> 4. **Confusing "manipulated" and "controlled" variables** — easy to mix up. Use the precise table format from Section 4.3 to avoid this.
> 5. **Failing to take Laplace transforms with zero initial conditions** in the derivation — this loses 2 marks.
> 6. **Forgetting the negative sign** in the transfer function (vent opening *decreases* pressure, so gain is negative).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Read this the night before the exam):**

- ⭐ The **vent valve is on the reflux accumulator**, NEVER on the column top.
- ⭐ Inerts are non-condensable; they **must be bled continuously**.
- ⭐ **Dalton's Law** governs the partial pressure distribution: $P_{top} = P_{vap} + P_{inert}$.
- ⭐ Liquid distillate means the product pump controls **level** in the drum, not the vent valve.
- ⭐ The vent valve manipulates **inert flow** to control **total pressure**.
- ⭐ PI controller is the standard choice (P-only gives offset; D-only amplifies noise).
- ⭐ Process time constant: $\tau_p = \dfrac{V}{R T K_c}$ — very small for typical drums.
- ⭐ The transfer function is **first-order**: $\dfrac{-K_p}{\tau_p s + 1}$.
- ⭐ The Clausius–Clapeyron relation tells us that **higher pressure → higher boiling point** → loss of separation.
- ⭐ **Acceptable inert partial pressure < 5% of total** for safe operation.
- ⭐ Vent piping velocity should be **60–90 m/s** to prevent back-diffusion.
- ⭐ Common inerts: **N₂, CO₂, CH₄, H₂, H₂S** — picked up from feed or from air in-leakage.
- ⭐ Failure of vent → inert blanketing → loss of condensation → **column trips on high pressure**.
- ⭐ In refinery ADUs, this control is called "**overhead receiver pressure control**."
- ⭐ In cryogenic ASUs, the vent is replaced with a **recovery expander** to recover the inerts.
- ⭐ Always check **mass balance** in numerical problems — vent flow + liquid out = vapor in.
- ⭐ When the question says "inert," think **Dalton's Law first**; when it says "no inert," think **condenser duty manipulation**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Column Pressure Control with Liquid Distillate and Negligible Inert
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT743 - INDUSTRIAL PROCESS CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Column Pressure Control with Liquid Distillate and Negligible Inert

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Column Pressure Control with Liquid Distillate and Negligible Inert** refers to the regulatory control strategy employed on a continuous binary/multicomponent distillation column where:
- The overhead product (**Distillate, D**) is withdrawn as a **saturated or subcooled liquid** stream from the reflux drum.
- The presence of **non-condensable inert gases** (such as N₂, CO₂, light hydrocarbons like CH₄, H₂) in the overhead vapor is **assumed to be negligible (≈ 0 mol%)**, which means a dedicated vent stream is **not required** in the pressure control loop.
- The manipulated variable used to regulate the column top pressure is the **rate of heat removal (Q_C)** in the total/partial condenser, typically executed by throttling the **cooling water (CW) flow** to the condenser.

Mathematically stated, the **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** for this subsystem reduces to a single-input single-output (SISO) configuration where pressure is the **Controlled Variable (CV)** and condenser duty is the **Manipulated Variable (MV)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** This topic falls under "Unit Operations Control — Distillation Column Pressure Control Schemes" and is tested every semester as either a Part A (3 marks) conceptual question or as a 7-mark sub-part under Part B (14 marks).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **pressure cooker with a sealed lid** that has a thin copper coil wrapped around its dome, through which cold water flows continuously.

- The **steam pressure** inside the cooker represents the **column top pressure (P_top)**.
- The **condensation of steam** on the cold coil represents **vapor (V) condensing back to liquid**.
- The **cold water flow rate** through the coil represents the **cooling water manipulation**.
- If you want to keep the pressure constant at exactly 1 bar (≈ 100 kPa) for steady cooking:
  - **More steam generated than condensed** → pressure rises → you **open the cold water valve wider** (more condensation, more heat removal).
  - **Less steam generated than condensed** → pressure drops → you **close the cold water valve** (less condensation, less heat removal).

This is **exactly** what happens in a distillation column. The boil-up vapor from the reboiler must be **precisely matched** by the condensate removed in the overhead condenser. Any mismatch causes vapor accumulation in the overhead system, raising the pressure.

In this analogy, the **"negligible inert"** condition means that there is **no air trapped inside the cooker** — only pure steam. This simplifies control because we don't have to separately vent the non-condensables. If inerts WERE significant, you would need a separate vent valve (analogous to a small leak in the cooker lid) — but here, we don't.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** With liquid distillate and negligible inerts, the entire pressure control problem collapses to **heat-balance control** — material balance is automatically satisfied because vapor in = condensate out = L (reflux) + D (distillate).

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

- **Latent heat of vaporization (λ)**: Typically **2.0 × 10⁵ to 4.5 × 10⁵ J/kg** for common hydrocarbons at column operating pressures.
- **Overall heat transfer coefficient (U) in condenser**: **300 to 800 W/(m²·K)** for shell-and-tube water-cooled condensers.
- **Pressure transmitter span**: Typically **0 to 1.5 × normal operating pressure** with accuracy of **± 0.1% of span**.
- **Controller tuning range**: Proportional band **PB = 50% to 200%**; Integral time **τ_I = 0.5 to 10 minutes**; Derivative time **τ_D = 0 (rarely used for pressure loops)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Underlying Physics — Energy Balance Around the Condenser

The principle behind all pressure control in distillation columns is rooted in the **Vapor-Liquid Equilibrium (VLE)** relationship and the **energy (heat) balance**. Pressure is essentially the system's response to an imbalance between vapor generation (boil-up from reboiler) and vapor condensation.

**Step 1: Define the system boundary.** The control volume encloses the **column top + condenser + reflux drum**, excluding the reboiler.

**Step 2: Apply the total material balance.** At steady state, the molar flow of vapor entering the condenser equals the molar flow of liquid leaving it:

$$V = L + D$$

where:
- **V** = vapor flow rate from column top to condenser (kmol/h or kg/h)
- **L** = liquid reflux flow returned to column (kmol/h or kg/h)
- **D** = liquid distillate product withdrawn (kmol/h or kg/h)

**Step 3: Apply the energy balance around the condenser.** The heat removed in the condenser must equal the latent heat released by the condensing vapor:

$$Q_C = V \cdot \lambda = m_{cw} \cdot C_{p,cw} \cdot \Delta T_{cw}$$

where:
- **Q_C** = condenser duty (W or J/s)
- **λ** = latent heat of vaporization of overhead vapor (J/kg)
- **m_cw** = mass flow rate of cooling water (kg/s)
- **C_p,cw** = specific heat capacity of cooling water (**4.186 kJ/(kg·K)** — a physical constant)
- **ΔT_cw** = temperature rise of cooling water across the condenser (K or °C)

**Step 4: Pressure as the integrated response.** Any imbalance between V (vapor generation rate) and the effective condensation rate manifests as a change in the moles of vapor trapped in the overhead system, which changes the pressure:

$$\frac{dP}{dt} = \frac{R \cdot T_{top}}{V_{vapor,space}} \left( V - \frac{Q_C}{\lambda} \right)$$

**Step 5: Why manipulate cooling water?** In the "liquid distillate + negligible inert" case:
- **V** is determined by the reboiler (heat input, usually fixed).
- **D** is fixed by the **Level Controller (LC)** on the reflux drum.
- **L** is fixed by the **Temperature Controller (TC)** on a sensitive tray.
- Therefore, the **only remaining degree of freedom** is **Q_C**, controlled via cooling water.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Critical Concept:** In liquid distillate columns with negligible inerts, **pressure control MUST be implemented through heat removal (condenser duty)**, NOT through material flow manipulation. Material flows are already "used up" by composition and inventory control loops.

### 2.2 Role of the Three Primary Control Loops

A typical control scheme for this configuration requires **three SISO loops**:

| Loop | Controlled Variable (PV) | Manipulated Variable (MV) | Controller Action | Purpose |
|------|--------------------------|---------------------------|-------------------|---------|
| **PC** | Column Top Pressure (P_top) | Cooling Water Valve (V_cw) | **Reverse / Direct** (depends on split-range) | Maintains energy balance |
| **TC** | Sensitive Tray Temperature (T_n) | Reflux Valve (V_L) | Reverse | Maintains product purity / composition |
| **LC** | Reflux Drum Level (h_drum) | Distillate Valve (V_D) | Direct | Maintains inventory |

**Loop Interaction Analysis:** These three loops are largely **decoupled** because each one operates on a fundamentally different physical principle:
- **PC** acts on **energy**.
- **TC** acts on **mass composition** (via temperature proxy).
- **LC** acts on **mass inventory**.

This natural decoupling is why this scheme is so widely adopted in industry.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Equation | Description | Units | Validity / Boundary Condition |
|---|----------|-------------|-------|-------------------------------|
| 1 | $V = L + D$ | Total material balance around condenser | kmol/h | Steady-state, no accumulation |
| 2 | $Q_C = V \cdot \lambda$ | Sensible heat of condensation | W | Assumes saturated vapor entering condenser |
