<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks)

### Q1. Define "Throughput" in a vacuum system. State its SI and CGS units. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition:** Throughput (denoted $Q$) is defined as the quantity of gas (in pressure-volume units) flowing per unit time through any cross-section of a vacuum system, measured at a specified pressure and temperature.

$$Q = P \cdot \frac{dV}{dt} = S \cdot P$$

**Units:**
- SI: **Pa·m³/s** (pascal-cubic-meter per second)
- CGS / Vacuum Engineering: **Torr·L/s** (torr-liter per second)

**Conversion:** 1 Pa·m³/s = **7.5 Torr·L/s**

*(Marks breakdown: 1 for definition, 1 for equation, 1 for units)*

### Q2. Differentiate between viscous flow and molecular flow regimes in vacuum piping. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Viscous Flow | Molecular Flow |
|-----------|--------------|----------------|
| **Dominant Collisions** | Molecule-molecule | Molecule-wall |
| **Regime** | $P \cdot D > 0.5$ Torr·cm | $P \cdot D < 0.5$ Torr·cm |
| **Conductance ∝** | $D^4$ and $P_{avg}$ (laminar) | $D^3$ (independent of $P$) |
| **Flow Type** | Continuous, compressible | Effusive, Knudsen |
| **Pressure Range** | Rough to medium vacuum | High to UHV |

*(Marks breakdown: 1 for regime condition, 1 for collision distinction, 1 for conductance scaling)*

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Descriptive Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks)

**Q A(a) [7 Marks]:** With a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of a typical two-stage industrial vacuum system. Describe the role of each component.

**Model Solution:**

A two-stage industrial vacuum system consists of two pumping stages connected in series — a **roughing (backing) pump** to evacuate from atmospheric pressure to medium vacuum, and a **high-vacuum pump** to take the system down to the final target pressure. The functional architecture is:

1. **Process Chamber:** The sealed volume where the industrial process occurs. Volume $V$ is application-specific (e.g., 100 L for a sputtering unit).
2. **High-Vacuum Gate Valve:** An isolation valve that opens only when the chamber is below the cross-over pressure (typically ~100 mTorr) to prevent damage to the HV pump from sudden pressure surges.
3. **High-Vacuum Pump (Turbomolecular/Diffusion):** Reduces pressure from $10^{-3}$ Torr to $10^{-6}$–$10^{-9}$ Torr depending on type.
4. **Foreline Valve:** Connects the HV pump outlet to the roughing pump; also acts as a roughing bypass.
5. **Roughing Pump (Rotary Vane):** Evacuates from 760 Torr to ~$10^{-3}$ Torr.
6. **Foreline Trap:** A sorbent or oil-mist filter that prevents backstreaming of hydrocarbons from the rough pump into the HV pump.
7. **Vacuum Gauges:** Pirani (rough/medium), Bayard-Alpert or Penning (HV), connected via 4–20 mA signals to a controller.
8. **Control System:** PLC-based PID controller that sequences the valves based on pressure thresholds and interlocks.

**Pressure Profile:** The pressure decreases monotonically from chamber → HV pump inlet → foreline → rough pump inlet → exhaust.

*(Marks: 2 diagram, 3 component description, 2 pressure profile)*

**Q A(b) [7 Marks]:** A vacuum chamber of volume $V = 250$ L is evacuated from atmospheric pressure (760 Torr) to $10^{-5}$ Torr using a turbomolecular pump of effective speed $S_{eff} = 200$ L/s at the chamber. Calculate:
   (i) The time required to reach $10^{-5}$ Torr.
   (ii) The throughput at the moment the pressure reaches $10^{-5}$ Torr.
   (iii) The number of gas molecules remaining in the chamber at the final pressure (at $T = 293$ K).

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Pump-down Time:**

$$t = \frac{V}{S_{eff}} \ln\left(\frac{P_i}{P_f}\right)$$

$$t = \frac{250}{200} \ln\left(\frac{760}{10^{-5}}\right)$$

$$t = 1.25 \cdot \ln(7.6 \times 10^7)$$

$$t = 1.25 \cdot 18.144$$

$$\boxed{t = 22.68 \text{ seconds}}$$

**(ii) Throughput at Final Pressure:**

$$Q = S_{eff} \cdot P_f = 200 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$\boxed{Q = 2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Torr·L/s}}$$

**(iii) Number of Molecules at $P_f$:**

Using the number density formula:

$$N = 3.27 \times 10^{19} \cdot P \text{ (Torr)} \cdot V \text{ (L)}$$

$$N = 3.27 \times 10^{19} \cdot 10^{-5} \cdot 250$$

$$\boxed{N = 8.175 \times 10^{16} \text{ molecules}}$$

*(Marks: 3 + 2 + 2 with full substitution and unit clarity)*

---

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**Q B(a) [7 Marks]:** Explain the working principle of a turbomolecular pump and a diffusion pump with suitable diagrams. Compare their performance characteristics. `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

**Turbomolecular Pump (TMP):**
- **Principle:** High-speed rotor blades (rotating at 75,000–90,000 RPM) impart downward momentum to gas molecules, pushing them from the inlet (chamber) toward the outlet (backing side). The stator blades redirect the flow.
- **Compression Ratio:** For N₂, typically $10^9$–$10^{11}$.
- **Operating Range:** $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-11}$ Torr.
- **Backing Pressure Required:** < 1 mTorr (hence paired with a dry rough pump).
- **Advantages:** No hydrocarbons, clean UHV, fast pump-down.
- **Disadvantages:** Expensive, fragile to sudden venting, requires frequent bearing maintenance.

**Diffusion Pump:**
- **Principle:** A boiler vaporizes a low-vapor-pressure oil (e.g., polyphenyl ether, silicone DC-705). High-velocity vapor jets emanating from nozzles entrain gas molecules via momentum transfer, pushing them downward to the foreline.
- **Compression Ratio:** $10^7$–$10^8$ for N₂.
- **Operating Range:** $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-10}$ Torr.
- **Backing Pressure Required:** < 100 mTorr.
- **Advantages:** Robust, high throughput, no moving mechanical parts in vacuum region.
- **Disadvantages:** Risk of oil backstreaming, slower pump-down, contamination-sensitive.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Turbomolecular | Diffusion |
|-----------|----------------|-----------|
| Ultimate Vacuum | $10^{-11}$ Torr | $10^{-10}$ Torr |
| Speed (typical) | 100–5000 L/s | 1000–50000 L/s |
| Hydrocarbon-free | Yes | No (unless trapped) |
| Vibration | High (rotor) | Low |
| Cost | Higher | Lower |

*(Marks: 2 + 2 principle explanation, 2 + 1 comparison)*

**Q B(b) [7 Marks]:** A rotary vane pump has a pumping speed of $S_p = 30$ L/s. It is connected to a chamber through a circular tube of diameter $D = 5$ cm and length $L = 100$ cm. The average pressure in the tube is $10^{-2}$ Torr. Calculate:
   (i) The conductance of the tube (molecular flow).
   (ii) The effective pumping speed at the chamber.
   (iii) The pressure difference between chamber and pump inlet.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Conductance (Molecular Flow):**

Using $C = 12.1 \cdot \frac{D^4}{L} \cdot P_{avg}$:

$$C = 12.1 \cdot \frac{(5)^4}{100} \cdot 10^{-2}$$

$$C = 12.1 \cdot \frac{625}{100} \cdot 10^{-2}$$

$$C = 12.1 \cdot 6.25 \cdot 10^{-2}$$

$$\boxed{C = 7.5625 \times 10^{-1} \text{ L/s} \approx 0.756 \text{ L/s}}$$

**(ii) Effective Pumping Speed:**

$$\frac{1}{S_{eff}} = \frac{1}{S_p} + \frac{1}{C} = \frac{1}{30} + \frac{1}{0.756}$$

$$\frac{1}{S_{eff}} = 0.0333 + 1.3228 = 1.3561$$

$$\boxed{S_{eff} = 0.737 \text{ L/s}}$$

**Observation:** The tube is the bottleneck — the conductance is so low that it dominates the effective speed.

**(iii) Pressure Difference:**

By throughput conservation: $Q = S_{eff} \cdot P_{chamber} = S_p \cdot P_{pump}$

Let $P_{chamber} = 10$ Torr (assumed). Then:

$$Q = 0.737 \times 10 = 7.37 \text{ Torr·L/s}$$

$$P_{pump} = \frac{Q}{S_p} = \frac{7.37}{30} = 0.246 \text{ Torr}$$

$$\Delta P = P_{chamber} - P_{pump} = 10 - 0.246 = 9.754 \text{ Torr}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta P \approx 9.75 \text{ Torr}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The pressure difference is significant because the tube is too small for the pump speed. This illustrates the KTU design principle: **pipes must be sized to have conductance much greater than the inverse of pump speed.**

*(Marks: 2 + 3 + 2)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Mixing Torr and Pa without conversion factor. Always show $1 \text{ Torr} = 133.3 \text{ Pa}$ explicitly.
> 2. **Forgetting the $P_{avg}$ term** in the molecular-flow conductance equation — costs 1 mark.
> 3. **Applying the wrong pump-down formula** when speed is time-dependent. The simple equation $t = (V/S)\ln(P_i/P_f)$ is valid **only for constant $S$**.
> 4. **Confusing throughput with pumping speed.** Throughput has units of $P \cdot V/t$, not $V/t$.
> 5. **Ignoring conductance limitations** of connecting pipes. KTU problems often have a "trap" where the pipe conductance, not the pump, limits performance.
> 6. **Not drawing block diagrams with labeled pressures** at each stage — diagrams without numerical pressure labels lose 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final High-Density Summary — Read This Twice Before the Exam**

- ✅ **Vacuum = Pressure Below Atmospheric.** Atmospheric pressure = **760 Torr = 101.325 kPa**.
- ✅ **Master Equation:** $Q = S \cdot P$ (throughput, speed, pressure).
- ✅ **Pump-Down Time:** $t = \frac{V}{S}\ln(P_i/P_f)$ — derived from ideal gas law + mass balance.
- ✅ **Effective Speed (series network):** $\frac{1}{S_{eff}} = \frac{1}{S_p} + \sum \frac{1}{C_i}$
- ✅ **Conductance (Molecular, $D$ in cm, $L$ in cm):** $C = 12.1 \cdot \frac{D^4}{L} \cdot P_{avg}$ (L/s).
- ✅ **Conductance (Viscous):** $C = 182 \cdot \frac{D^4}{L} \cdot P_{avg}$ (L/s).
- ✅ **Regime Boundary:** Molecular flow ↔ Viscous flow at $P \cdot D = 0.5$ Torr·cm.
- ✅ **Mean Free Path:** $\lambda = \frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{P \text{ (Torr)}}$ cm.
- ✅ **Number Density:** $N = 3.27 \times 10^{19} \cdot P \cdot V$ (in molecules, with $P$ in Torr, $V$ in L).
- ✅ **Vacuum Regimes:** Rough (760–1), Medium (1–10⁻³), HV (10⁻³–10⁻⁸), UHV (10⁻⁸–10⁻¹¹) Torr.
- ✅ **Pump Hierarchy:** Diaphragm/Rotary Vane (rough) → Roots Blower (medium-rough) → Turbomolecular/Diffusion/Cryo (HV–UHV).
- ✅ **Gauges by Range:** Bourdon (atm) → Pirani/TC (rough-medium) → Bayard-Alpert/Penning (HV) → Extractor/Magnetron (UHV).
- ✅ **Rule of Thumb:** Pipe conductance must be **≥ 4× pump speed** to avoid throughput loss.
- ✅ **Leak Detection:** Helium mass-spectrometer leak detector sensitivity = $5 \times 10^{-9}$ Pa·m³/s.
- ✅ **Industrial Applications:** Semiconductors, pharma lyophilization, steel degassing (RH/VOD), vacuum distillation, PVD/CVD coatings.
- ✅ **Interlock Sequence:** Rough pump ON → Wait for foreline pressure < 100 mTorr → Open foreline valve → Open HV valve → Engage HV pump.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Feed Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT743 - INDUSTRIAL PROCESS CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Feed Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Feed Control** in Industrial Process Control refers to a class of advanced regulatory and multivariable control strategies in which the **feed stream** (flow rate, temperature, composition, or pressure) entering a process unit is treated as the **manipulated variable (MV)** or, conversely, where the feed stream properties act as the **measured/disturbance variable (DV)** driving a feedforward compensation action.

In strict KTU 2024 Scheme nomenclature, Feed Control encompasses two complementary engineering philosophies:

1. **Feed as Manipulated Variable (Feedback Feed Control):** The feed flow $F$ or feed temperature $T_f$ is automatically adjusted by the controller to maintain a controlled variable (typically reactor temperature, product composition, or column tray temperature) at its setpoint.
2. **Feed as Disturbance Variable (Feedforward Feed Control):** The feed stream properties (flow, temperature, composition) are measured before entering the unit, and the controller proactively adjusts a downstream manipulated variable to cancel the expected process upset before it propagates.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot:** *Feed Control is the systematic use of inlet stream measurements (flow, $T_f$, $C_{Af}$) — either as the manipulated variable or as the disturbance input to a feedforward/feedback architecture — to regulate the operating point of a chemical process unit.*

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen Tap" Intuition

Imagine you are boiling water in a kettle on a gas stove and you want to keep the water at exactly **80 °C** for brewing green tea.

- **Feedback Feed Control analogy:** You watch a thermometer stuck in the kettle. If the temperature drops below 80 °C, you *increase the gas flow* (manipulated variable) — but the gas here represents the **feed of heat (energy) into the system**. Now translate this to a chemical reactor: instead of gas, you manipulate the **cold feed flow $F$** entering the reactor. A hotter reactor temperature means too much heat is being released by reaction; you increase $F$ (the cold feed), which acts as a *thermal flywheel*, absorbing heat and cooling the reactor. **This is the canonical CSTR Feed-Temperature Control loop.**

- **Feedforward Feed Control analogy:** Before pouring the water, you notice it is already quite warm from the tap (the disturbance). So you *preemptively lower the gas flame* because you know a warm inlet will heat up faster. In a chemical plant, if the upstream supplier tells you that the feed concentration $C_{Af}$ has just increased (more reactant → more reaction heat), the feedforward controller immediately commands a proportional increase in the coolant or feed flow, *before* the reactor temperature has had time to deviate from setpoint.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter in industry?** A typical ethylene cracker processes **feed flow rates of 80–150 tonnes/hour**. A 1 % uncompensated disturbance in feed temperature or composition can swing product yields by **3–7 %**, costing millions of dollars annually. Feed Control is therefore one of the highest-leverage loops on the KTU Board Exam and in real plant economics.

---

### 1.3 Scope and Boundaries of Feed Control within KTU Module 2

Within Module 2 of PEICT743, Feed Control is taught alongside its siblings — **Cascade, Ratio, Split-Range, Selective, Override, and Feedforward Control**. It is the *entry point* to advanced regulatory control. Specifically, the syllabus targets:

- Feed manipulation in a **CSTR** for exothermic reaction temperature regulation.
- Feed-temperature feedback loop using energy-balance derivation.
- Feed-forward architecture where $F$ or $T_f$ is the disturbance.
- Interaction analysis between the feed loop and the level/flow loops on the same vessel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Physical Constants used in this Module:**
> - Universal gas constant $R = 8.314 \; \text{J/(mol·K)}$
> - Molar volume at STP $V_m = 22.414 \; \text{L/mol}$
> - Acceleration due to gravity $g = 9.81 \; \text{m/s}^2$
> - Standard reference temperature $T_{ref} = 298.15 \; \text{K}$

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architectural Classification of Feed Control Schemes

Feed Control is not a single loop — it is a **family of architectures** distinguished by *what is measured*, *what is manipulated*, and *what is controlled*. The KTU 2024 Scheme expects the student to recognize each scheme from a P&ID or block diagram.

#### Scheme A — Feed-Flow Feedback (Rare, Used for Surge Tanks)
- **MV:** Feed flow valve position (downstream of flow transmitter).
- **CV:** Feed flow rate $F$ itself.
- **Purpose:** Decouples upstream disturbances from downstream unit operations.
- **Typical application:** Feed surge drum between a pipeline header and a distillation column.

#### Scheme B — Feed-Temperature Feedback (Canonical CSTR Loop)
- **MV:** Feed flow rate $F$ (or feed temperature $T_f$ via a pre-heater).
- **CV:** Reactor outlet temperature $T$.
- **Disturbance:** Inlet temperature $T_f$, coolant temperature $T_c$, feed concentration $C_{Af}$.
- **Purpose:** Stabilizes the highly nonlinear, potentially runaway exothermic reactor.

#### Scheme C — Feedforward from Feed (FF + Feedback Trim)
- **DV (measured):** Feed flow $F$, feed temperature $T_f$, feed composition $C_{Af}$.
- **MV (manipulated):** Coolant flow, steam flow, or feed flow itself.
- **CV:** Reactor temperature $T$ or product composition $C_A$.
- **Purpose:** Rejects feed disturbances *before* they affect the CV.

---

### 2.2 Underlying Theoretical Pillars

#### Pillar 1 — Conservation Laws (Steady-State Foundation)

For any process unit under Feed Control, two governing equations must hold at steady state:

1. **Total mass balance:** $\sum F_{in} = \sum F_{out}$
2. **Component mass balance:** $F_{in} C_{A,in} = F_{out} C_{A,out} + V r_k$
3. **Energy balance:** $F \rho C_p (T_{f} - T) + (-\Delta H) V r_k - Q = 0$

Where $r_k = k_0 e^{-E/RT} C_A^n$ is the reaction rate.

#### Pillar 2 — Dynamic Linearization (Deviation Variables)

Because the Arrhenius term $e^{-E/RT}$ is highly nonlinear, the KTU Board exam expects the student to linearize around the operating point $(T^*, C_A^*, F^*)$:

$$\Delta T = T - T^*, \quad \Delta C_A = C_A - C_A^*, \quad \Delta F = F - F^*$$

Substituting $T = T^* + \Delta T$ and using a first-order Taylor expansion yields the linearized state-space model that feed controllers are designed upon.

#### Pillar 3 — The "Why" of Feed Manipulation

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering "Why":** Why manipulate feed instead of coolant?
> - **Coolant** is often constrained (fouling, valve saturation, condensation vs. sensible heat regimes).
> - **Feed** has a *dual effect*: it dilutes concentration *and* carries enthalpy. This makes it an extremely powerful MV for exothermic reactors because a small $\Delta F$ produces a large $\Delta T$.
> - On the negative side, feed manipulation **changes production rate**, which is economically undesirable if the reactor is throughput-limited. The KTU examiner will test whether you recognize this trade-off.

#### Pillar 4 — Interaction (Loop Coupling)

In a CSTR with both a *feed-flow* controller and a *level* controller, both loops manipulate valves on the same stream. This creates **negative interaction** (one loop fighting the other). The correct KTU-accepted pairing uses:

| Loop | MV | CV | Pairing Logic |
|---|---|---|---|
| Level | Outlet flow $F_{out}$ | Level $h$ | Self-regulating for level |
| Temperature | Feed flow $F$ | $T$ | Fastest MV for $T$ |
| Composition | Coolant $F_c$ | $C_A$ | Final trim |

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation Name | Formula | Variables & Units | Applicability |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | CSTR Energy Steady State | $F \rho C_p (T_f - T) + (-\Delta H)V r_k - Q = 0$ | $F$ [m³/s], $T$ [K], $r_k$ [mol/m³·s] | Scheme B |
| 2 | CSTR Component Balance | $F(C_{Af} - C_A) - V r_k = V \dfrac{dC_A}{dt}$ | $C_A$ [mol/m³] | Scheme B, C |
| 3 | Linearized Feed Gain | $K_p = \left( \dfrac{\partial T}{\partial F} \right)_{ss}$ | [K·s/m³] | Tuning |
| 4 | Feedforward Controller | $G_{ff}(s) = -\dfrac{G_d(s)}{G_p(s)}$ | Dimensionless transfer function | Scheme C |
| 5 | Steady-State FF Ratio | $m_{ss} = -\dfrac{K_d}{K_p}$ | $\vert K_d / K_p \vert$ | Bias tuning |
| 6 | Feed Temperature Gain | $\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial T_f} = \dfrac{F \rho C_p}{F \rho C_p + Q/(T-T_c)}$ | Dimensionless | Sensitivity |
| 7 | Open-Loop Time Constant | $\tau_p = \dfrac{V}{F + V \partial r_k / \partial T \cdot (-\Delta H)/\rho C_p}$ | [s] | Dynamic analysis |
| 8 | Arrhenius Sensitivity | $\dfrac{\partial r_k}{\partial T} = \dfrac{E}{RT^2} r_k$ | [mol/m³·s·K] | Linearization |
| 9 | Closed-Loop Characteristic | $1 + G_c G_v G_p G_m = 0$ | Polynomial roots | Stability |
| 10 | IAE Tuning (Cohen-Coon, Feed Loop) | $K_c = \dfrac{1}{K_p} \dfrac{\tau_p}{\theta} \left( 0.9 + \dfrac{\theta}{3\tau_p} \right)$ | — | Feed loop tuning |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Reminder (KTU Examiner Pet Peeve):** When writing the energy balance, students *very frequently* drop the sign on $(-\Delta H)$. For an **exothermic** reaction, $\Delta H < 0$, so $(-\Delta H) > 0$. Always state this explicitly in the exam.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Utility of Feed Control

- **Petrochemical industry:** Steam-cracker furnaces use *feed-flow-to-firing ratio control* (a feed-aware scheme) to maintain coil outlet temperature.
- **Polymerization reactors:** $T$ runaway is the #1 safety hazard. Feed manipulation with cascade and feedforward trim is the industry-standard ISA-84 SIL architecture.
- **Pharmaceutical batch reactors:** Feed control is critical in **semi-batch** operation where one reactant is fed gradually to control exotherm.
- **Utilities:** Boiler feedwater control is *the* canonical cascade + feedforward loop (3-element drum level control).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Steady-State Gain $\partial T / \partial F$ for a CSTR Feed Loop

We will derive the **process gain** of the CSTR with respect to feed flow $F$, which is the single most important equation for tuning a Feed-Temperature controller.

**Starting point — CSTR Energy Balance (steady state):**

$$F \rho C_p (T_f - T) + (-\Delta H) V r_k(T, C_A) - Q = 0$$

**Step 1 — Expand and collect the $F$-dependent terms:**

$$F \rho C_p (T_f - T) = Q - (-\Delta H) V r_k$$

**Step 2 — Solve explicitly for the $F$-containing term on the left:**

$$F = \dfrac{Q - (-\Delta H) V r_k}{\rho C_p (T_f - T)}$$

**Step 3 — Apply implicit differentiation. At constant $Q$, $T_f$, $C_{Af}$, differentiate both sides with respect to $T$:**

Recall that $r_k = k_0 e^{-E/RT} C_A$. At constant $C_A$ (slow composition dynamics), $\dfrac{dr_k}{dT} = \dfrac{E}{RT^2} r_k$.

Differentiating $F$ with respect to $T$ (treating numerator and denominator as $T$-dependent via $r_k$ and $T$):

Numerator derivative: $\dfrac{d}{dT}\left[Q - (-\Delta H)V r_k\right] = -(-\Delta H)V \dfrac{dr_k}{dT} = -(-\Delta H) V \dfrac{E}{RT^2} r_k$

Denominator derivative: $\dfrac{d}{dT}\left[\rho C_p (T_f - T)\right] = -\rho C_p$

**Step 4 — Apply quotient rule:**

$$\dfrac{dF}{dT} = \dfrac{-\rho C_p \left[Q - (-\Delta H)V r_k\right] - \left[-\rho C_p\right]\left[-(-\Delta H)V \dfrac{E}{RT^2} r_k\right]}{\left[\rho C_p(T_f - T)\right]^2}$$

**Step 5 — Simplify the numerator. The first term contains the steady-state energy balance $F \rho C_p (T_f - T) = Q - (-\Delta H) V r_k$:**

$$-\rho C_p \left[F \rho C_p (T_f - T)\right] - \rho C_p (-\Delta H) V \dfrac{E}{RT^2} r_k$$

$$= -\rho^2 C_p^2 (T_f - T) F - \rho C_p (-\Delta H) V \dfrac{E}{RT^2} r_k$$

**Step 6 — Invert to get $\partial T / \partial F$:**

$$\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial F} = \dfrac{\left[\rho C_p (T_f - T)\right]^2}{-\rho^2 C_p^2 (T_f - T) F - \rho C_p (-\Delta H) V \dfrac{E}{RT^2} r_k}$$

**Step 7 — Divide numerator and denominator by $\rho^2 C_p^2 (T_f - T)$:**

$$\boxed{\;\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial F} = -\dfrac{(T_f - T)}{F + \dfrac{(-\Delta H) V E r_k}{\rho C_p R T^2}}\;}$$

**Physical Interpretation:**

- The **negative sign** means increasing feed flow *cools* the reactor (as expected for cold-feed exothermic cooling).
- The denominator has a positive contribution from the **Arrhenius term** — this is the *thermal feedback amplification*. As $E$ or $r_k$ grows, the denominator grows, the gain becomes *less negative* (smaller in magnitude), and the reactor becomes **less sensitive to feed disturbances** in open-loop terms, but **more sensitive to temperature perturbations** — this is the seed of thermal runaway.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Feedforward Controller $G_{ff}(s)$ for Feed Disturbance

**Architecture setup:**

- Process transfer function (MV → CV): $G_p(s)$
- Disturbance transfer function (DV → CV): $G_d(s)$
- Feedforward controller: $G_{ff}(s)$

**Block diagram algebra (load disturbance enters at process output):**

$$Y(s) = G_p(s) M(s) + G_d(s) D(s)$$

With $M(s) = G_{ff}(s) D(s)$ (feedforward-only, no feedback trim yet):

$$Y(s) = G_p(s) G_{ff}(s) D(s) + G_d(s) D(s)$$

**Set $Y(s) = 0$ for ideal disturbance rejection:**

$$G_p(s) G_{ff}(s) + G_d(s) = 0$$

**Solve:**

$$\boxed{\;G_{ff}(s) = -\dfrac{G_d(s)}{G_p(s)}\;}$$

**Step-by-step with concrete first-order forms:**

Assume $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K_p}{\tau_p s + 1}$ and $G_d(s) = \dfrac{K_d}{\tau_d s + 1}$.

Then:

$$G_{ff}(s) = -\dfrac{K_d}{K_p} \cdot \dfrac{\tau_p s + 1}{\tau_d s + 1}$$

This is a **lead-lag** controller — lead on $\tau_p$, lag on $\tau_d$. KTU examiners love asking what happens if $\tau_d < \tau_p$ (causal/non-implementable) vs. $\tau_d \geq \tau_p$ (proper lead-lag).

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Closed-Loop Characteristic Equation for Feed Loop with PI Controller

**Setup:** PI controller $G_c(s) = K_c\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{\tau_I s}\right)$, valve $G_v(s) = K_v$, process $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K_p e^{-\theta s}}{\tau_p s + 1}$, transmitter $G_m(s) = K_m$.

**Closed-loop transfer function denominator:**

$$1 + G_c G_v G_p G_m = 0$$

**Substituting and clearing the time-delay via Pade' approximation (1st order):** $e^{-\theta s} \approx \dfrac{1 - \theta s/2}{1 + \theta s/2}$.

After clearing fractions and collecting by powers of $s$, the characteristic equation is:

$$\tau_I \tau_p s^3 + \tau_I s^2 + K_c K_v K_p K_m \tau_I (1 - \theta s/2) s + K_c K_v K_p K_m = 0$$

**Final characteristic polynomial:**

$$\tau_I \tau_p s^3 + \left[\tau_I - K_c K_v K_p K_m \tau_I \theta / 2 \right] s^2 + K_c K_v K_p K_m \tau_I s + K_c K_v K_p K_m = 0$$

Apply **Routh-Hurwitz stability criterion** to find the ultimate $K_{c,u}$ at which sustained oscillations occur.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Which Feed Control Scheme

| Process Characteristic | Recommended Feed Scheme | Reason | KTU Pitfall |
|---|---|---|---|
| Large feed flow variations, slow reactor dynamics | Feedforward from $F$ | Disturbance rejection before $T$ deviates | Forget to add feedback trim |
| Exothermic with $E/RT^2 \cdot (-\Delta H)V r_k / \rho C_p \gg F$ | Cascade $T_f \to T$ | Inner loop fast, outer slow | Reverse cascade order |
| Pure feed-tank level control | Single-loop $F \to h$ | Self-regulating | Confuse with CSTR |
| Distillation feed composition swing | FF from $C_{Af}$, trim reflux | Composition is slow | Use ratio instead of FF |
| Semi-batch addition of limiting reactant | Feed-flow profile ramp | Safety & conversion | Allow runaway if profile too steep |

---

### 3.5 Detailed Numerical Example (KTU Board Exam Pattern)

**Problem:** A CSTR carries out an irreversible first-order exothermic reaction $A \to B$ with $k_0 = 7.2 \times 10^{10} \; \text{min}^{-1}$, $E/R = 10{,}000 \; \text{K}$, $V = 1 \; \text{m}^3$, $\rho C_p = 4{,}000 \; \text{kJ/(m}^3\cdot\text{K)}$, $(-\Delta H) = 50{,}000 \; \text{kJ/kmol}$. At steady state: $T = 350 \; \text{K}$, $C_A = 0.5 \; \text{kmol/m}^3$, $T_f = 300 \; \text{K}$, $F = 0.05 \; \text{m}^3/\text{min}$.

Compute (a) the steady-state $Q$ required, (b) the process gain $\partial T / \partial F$ at this operating point.

**Step 1 — Compute $r_k$:**

$$r_k = k_0 e^{-E/RT} C_A = 7.2 \times 10^{10} \cdot e^{-10000/350} \cdot 0.5$$

$$e^{-28.571} = 3.305 \times 10^{-13}$$

$$r_k = 7.2 \times 10^{10} \cdot 3.305 \times 10^{-13} \cdot 0.5 = 0.0119 \; \text{kmol/(m}^3\cdot\text{min)}$$

**Step 2 — Solve energy balance for $Q$:**

$$Q = F \rho C_p (T_f - T) + (-\Delta H) V r_k$$

$$Q = 0.05 \cdot 4000 \cdot (300 - 350) + 50000 \cdot 1 \cdot 0.0119$$

$$Q = -10{,}000 + 595 = -9{,}405 \; \text{kJ/min}$$

**The negative sign** indicates that the *reaction* generates 595 kJ/min but the *feed* removes 10,000 kJ/min (feed is acting as coolant). The net $Q$ = heat that must be *added* by the jacket to maintain 350 K. So the jacket must supply 9,405 kJ/min.

**Step 3 — Compute $\partial T / \partial F$ using the derived formula:**

Denominator Arrhenius term: $\dfrac{(-\Delta H) V E r_k}{\rho C_p R T^2}$ — note here $E/R = 10{,}000 \; \text{K}$, so $E = 10000 R$.

$$\dfrac{50000 \cdot 1 \cdot 10000 R \cdot 0.0119}{4000 \cdot R \cdot 350^2} = \dfrac{50000 \cdot 10000 \cdot 0.0119}{4000 \cdot 122500}$$

$$= \dfrac{5{,}950{,}000}{490{,}000{,}000} = 0.01214 \; \text{m}^3/\text{min}$$

Total denominator: $F + 0.01214 = 0.05 + 0.01214 = 0.06214$.

$$\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial F} = -\dfrac{(T_f - T)}{0.06214} = -\dfrac{(300 - 350)}{0.06214} = -\dfrac{-50}{0.06214} = +804.5 \; \text{K·min/m}^3$$

> [!WARNING]
> **SIGN CONVENTION PITFALL:** KTU students frequently get the sign wrong on $(T_f - T)$. Here $T_f = 300 < T = 350$, so $(T_f - T) = -50$. The *negative* sign in front flips it to $+804.5$, meaning increasing $F$ **raises** $T$! This is a cold-feed-limited case. With hotter feed, increasing $F$ would *lower* $T$. **Always draw a phase-plane sketch before tuning.**

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Figure 1 — Canonical CSTR Feed-Temperature Feedback Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP[Setpoint T_sp] --> SUM1((+))
    TT[Reactor T transmitter] --> SUM1
    SUM1 --> EC[Error e t]
    EC --> TC[Temperature Controller Gc]
    TC --> FCV[Feed Flow Valve]
    FCV --> CSTR[CSTR Reactor]
    FEED[Feed F Tf Caf] --> CSTR
    CSTR --> TT
    JACKET[Coolant Q] --> CSTR
    CC[Composition Sensor CA] -.-> CSTR
```

**Reading the diagram:** The reactor outlet temperature $T$ is measured, compared with the setpoint $T_{sp}$, the error is processed by the temperature controller $G_c(s) = K_c(1 + 1/\tau_I s)$, and the output drives the **feed flow valve** — this is the defining feature of a Feed-Temperature feedback loop.

---

### 4.2 Figure 2 — Feedforward from Feed + Feedback Trim (FF+FB)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F_FLOW[Feed Flow FT] --> FF[Feedforward Block Gff]
    FF --> SUM2((+))
    T_TX[Reactor T transmitter] --> FB[Feedback Controller Gc]
    FB --> SUM2
    SUM2 --> VALVE[Coolant Valve]
    VALVE --> CSTR2[CSTR Reactor]
    F_FLOW -.Disturbance.-> CSTR2
    T_TX --> CSTR2
```

**Reading the diagram:** The feed flow disturbance $F$ enters the FF block *before* it can affect $T$. The output of the FF block is summed with the feedback trim, producing a faster combined correction than feedback alone.

---

### 4.3 Figure 3 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Full Feed Control Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Layer1[Measurement Layer]
        A1[Feed Flow Transmitter FT-101]
        A2[Feed Temperature TT-102]
        A3[Feed Composition AT-103]
        A4[Reactor Temperature TT-201]
        A5[Reactor Level LT-202]
    end
    subgraph Layer2[Control Layer]
        B1[FIC-101 Feed Flow Controller]
        B2[TIC-201 Reactor Temperature Controller]
        B3[LIC-202 Level Controller]
        B4[AIC-203 Composition Controller]
        B5[FF-X101 Feedforward Block]
    end
    subgraph Layer3[Final Element Layer]
        C1[FCV-101 Feed Valve]
        C2[TCV-201 Coolant Valve]
        C3[LCV-202 Outlet Valve]
        C4[ACV-203 Trim Valve]
    end
    subgraph Layer4[Process Unit]
        D1[CSTR Reactor V-200]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B5
    A3 --> B5
    A4 --> B2
    A5 --> B3
    B5 --> B2
    B1 --> C1
    B2 --> C2
    B3 --> C3
    B4 --> C4
    C1 --> D1
    C2 --> D1
    C3 --> D1
    C4 --> D1
    D1 --> A4
    D1 --> A5
```

**Reading the diagram:** Four measurement tags feed four controllers; the feedforward block dynamically couples the feed measurements to the temperature controller, and the final elements drive the CSTR. This is the **complete KTU Module 2 architecture** that students must be able to draw from memory.

---

### 4.4 Figure 4 — Steady-State Operating Curve (Schematic Reasoning)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X[Feed Flow F] --> Y1[Heat Removed by Feed Q_feed]
    X --> Y2[Heat Released by Reaction Q_rxn]
    Y1 --> NET((Net Heat))
    Y2 --> NET
    NET --> T[Reactor T]
    T -.Feedback.-> Y2
```

**Reading the diagram:** Increasing $F$ removes more heat (Q_feed rises), but a *colder* reactor slows reaction rate, dropping $Q_{rxn}$. The loop closes — this is the **thermal feedback** that students must internalize to understand why CSTRs can run away.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question A.1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define Feed Control. Differentiate between feedback feed control and feedforward feed control schemes with one industrial example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Feed Control** is the class of process control strategies in which the feed stream properties (flow, temperature, composition) to a process unit are used either as the **manipulated variable** or as the **disturbance variable** to regulate a process output.
>
> **Feedback Feed Control:** The reactor outlet temperature $T$ is measured and compared with the setpoint. The controller adjusts the **feed flow valve** to bring $T$ back to setpoint. *Example:* CSTR exothermic reactor temperature control via feed flow manipulation.
>
> **Feedforward Feed Control:** Feed flow/temperature/composition is measured *before* the reactor. The feedforward block computes the corrective action on coolant (or feed) to cancel the expected disturbance. *Example:* Steam-cracker furnace firing-rate feedforward based on hydrocarbon feed flow.
>
> **Key distinction:** Feedback reacts *after* the disturbance has propagated; feedforward acts *before* the disturbance reaches the CV.

---

#### Question A.2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**State the steady-state energy balance equation for a CSTR. Why is manipulating feed flow an effective temperature-control strategy for exothermic reactions?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The steady-state energy balance for a CSTR is:
>
> $$F \rho C_p (T_f - T) + (-\Delta H) V r_k(T, C_A) - Q = 0$$
>
> Feed flow manipulation is effective because (i) the term $F \rho C_p (T_f - T)$ appears linearly in the energy balance, giving a direct thermal coupling, and (ii) for exothermic reactions, increasing $F$ when $T_f < T$ provides *sensible-heat cooling*, rapidly absorbing the excess reaction enthalpy. The feed thus acts as a *thermal flywheel* — a powerful MV with fast dynamic response.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **INSTRUCTIONS:** Answer **any ONE** full question from the two choices provided. Each choice has sub-parts (a) and (b), each worth 7 marks.

---

#### Question B-A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** For a CSTR carrying out an irreversible first-order exothermic reaction $A \to B$, derive the expression for the **steady-state process gain** $\partial T / \partial F$ in terms of feed flow rate $F$, feed temperature $T_f$, reactor temperature $T$, reaction rate $r_k$, and physical constants. Clearly state all assumptions. (7 marks)

**(b)** A CSTR has the following operating data: $V = 2 \; \text{m}^3$, $F = 0.1 \; \text{m}^3/\text{min}$, $T_f = 310 \; \text{K}$, $T = 360 \; \text{K}$, $\rho C_p = 4{,}000 \; \text{kJ/(m}^3 \cdot \text{K)}$, $(-\Delta H) = 60{,}000 \; \text{kJ/kmol}$, $E/R = 9{,}500 \; \text{K}$, $k_0 = 5 \times 10^{10} \; \text{min}^{-1}$, $C_A = 0.4 \; \text{kmol/m}^3$. Compute $r_k$ and the steady-state process gain $\partial T/\partial F$. Comment on the sign and stability implications. (7 marks)

##### Model Solution — B-A (a)

**Assumptions:**
1. Constant density $\rho$ and heat capacity $C_p$.
2. Perfect mixing (lumped CSTR).
3. Single irreversible first-order reaction $r_k = k_0 e^{-E/RT} C_A$.
4. Steady-state conditions.
5. Jacket duty $Q$ is independent of $F$ at the operating point (or $Q$ is held constant for the partial derivative).

**Starting equation (energy balance at steady state):**

$$F \rho C_p (T_f - T) + (-\Delta H) V r_k - Q = 0$$

**Solve for $F$:**

$$F = \dfrac{Q - (-\Delta H)V r_k}{\rho C_p (T_f - T)}$$

**Differentiate implicitly with respect to $T$** (treating $r_k$ as a function of $T$ via Arrhenius, and $Q$, $T_f$, $C_A$ as constants):

$$\dfrac{\partial r_k}{\partial T} = \dfrac{E}{RT^2} k_0 e^{-E/RT} C_A = \dfrac{E}{RT^2} r_k$$

After applying the quotient rule and simplifying (see Section 3.1 for the full algebra):

$$\boxed{\;\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial F} = -\dfrac{(T_f - T)}{F + \dfrac{(-\Delta H) V E r_k}{\rho C_p R T^2}}\;}$$

**Mark allocation:** Assumptions (1), Starting equation (1), Solving for $F$ (1), Implicit differentiation with Arrhenius (2), Final expression (1), Physical units (1). **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution — B-A (b)

**Step 1 — Compute $r_k$:**

$$r_k = k_0 e^{-E/RT} \cdot C_A = 5 \times 10^{10} \cdot e^{-9500/360} \cdot 0.4$$

$$e^{-26.389} = 3.499 \times 10^{-12}$$

$$r_k = 5 \times 10^{10} \cdot 3.499 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 0.4 = 0.06999 \; \text{kmol/(m}^3 \cdot \text{min)}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Arrhenius term in the denominator:**

$$\dfrac{(-\Delta H) V E r_k}{\rho C_p R T^2} = \dfrac{(-\Delta H) V (E/R) r_k}{\rho C_p T^2}$$

$$= \dfrac{60000 \cdot 2 \cdot 9500 \cdot 0.06999}{4000 \cdot 360^2}$$

$$= \dfrac{79{,}788{,}600}{518{,}400{,}000} = 0.1539 \; \text{m}^3/\text{min}$$

**Step 3 — Total denominator and process gain:**

$$F + \text{Arrhenius term} = 0.1 + 0.1539 = 0.2539$$

$$\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial F} = -\dfrac{(310 - 360)}{0.2539} = -\dfrac{-50}{0.2539} = +196.9 \; \text{K·min/m}^3$$

**Step 4 — Sign and stability comment (2 marks within 7):**

> The positive sign indicates that increasing $F$ **raises** $T$ at this operating point (because the feed $T_f = 310 \; \text{K}$ is *colder* than $T = 360 \; \text{K}$, but the strong exotherm dominates; the larger feed mass also carries more reactant in, amplifying the heat release). Operationally this means **increasing feed on a temperature rise is destabilizing** — the controller must **decrease** feed on a temperature rise. The magnitude (~197 K·min/m³) is large; the loop will be fast but sensitive.

---

#### Question B-B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **feedforward control scheme** based on feed flow disturbance. Derive the ideal feedforward controller transfer function $G_{ff}(s)$. (7 marks)

**(b)** For a distillation column feed preheater, $G_p(s) = \dfrac{2.0}{5s + 1}$ (steam valve → column feed temperature, °C/%) and $G_d(s) = \dfrac{0.8}{3s + 1}$ (feed flow disturbance → column feed temperature, °C/(m³/h)). The feed flow transmitter range is 0–100 m³/h mapped to 4–20 mA. Design the feedforward controller transfer function. If a 10 % step change occurs in feed flow transmitter signal, compute the expected change in steam valve position at steady state. (7 marks)

##### Model Solution — B-B (a)

**Block diagram (described in words for mark allocation):**

The block diagram consists of: Feed flow transmitter → summing junction (against setpoint or no setpoint for FF) → $G_{ff}(s)$ block → steam (or coolant) valve → process $G_p(s)$ → controlled variable. In parallel, the disturbance $D(s)$ feeds directly into the process via $G_d(s)$.

**Derivation:**

Closed-loop (FF-only) equation:

$$Y(s) = G_p(s) M(s) + G_d(s) D(s), \quad M(s) = G_{ff}(s) D(s)$$

Substitute:

$$Y(s) = \left[G_p(s) G_{ff}(s) + G_d(s)\right] D(s)$$

For perfect disturbance rejection, $Y(s) = 0$:

$$\boxed{\;G_{ff}(s) = -\dfrac{G_d(s)}{G_p(s)}\;}$$

**Mark split:** Block diagram (2), Closed-loop equation (2), Setting $Y=0$ (1), Final expression (1), Discussion of sign and physical realizability (1). **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution — B-B (b)

**Step 1 — Substitute the given $G_p$ and $G_d$:**

$$G_{ff}(s) = -\dfrac{0.8 / (3s + 1)}{2.0 / (5s + 1)} = -\dfrac{0.8}{2.0} \cdot \dfrac{5s + 1}{3s + 1} = -0.4 \cdot \dfrac{5s + 1}{3s + 1}$$

This is a **lead-lag** controller — lead on $\tau_p = 5$ min, lag on $\tau_d = 3$ min.

**Step 2 — Steady-state FF gain:**

$$G_{ff}(0) = -0.4 \cdot \dfrac{0 + 1}{0 + 1} = -0.4$$

**Step 3 — Compute the 10 % step in feed flow transmitter:**

A 10 % step in transmitter signal corresponds to a 10 % change in measured feed flow at the input to $G_{ff}$. For a step input $D(s) = \dfrac{10}{s}$ (% of transmitter span),

$$M(s) = G_{ff}(s) \cdot D(s) = -0.4 \cdot \dfrac{5s+1}{3s+1} \cdot \dfrac{10}{s}$$

**Step 4 — Steady-state valve output via Final Value Theorem:**

$$m_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s M(s) = -0.4 \cdot \dfrac{1}{1} \cdot 10 = -4 \; \%$$

**Step 5 — Physical interpretation:**

A 10 % increase in measured feed flow disturbance leads to a **4 % decrease** in the steam valve opening (to reduce steam as more cold feed is now arriving). This is the desired *anticipatory* correction.

> **Mark allocation:** $G_{ff}$ derivation (2), Steady-state gain (1), Step input (1), Final Value Theorem (2), Interpretation (1). **(7 marks)**

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Traps

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Examiner Pitfall Callout (Feed Control Topic):**
>
> 1. **Sign error in $(-\Delta H)$** — loses 1 mark immediately on any energy-balance derivation. Always write: *"For exothermic reactions, $\Delta H < 0$, so $(-\Delta H) > 0$."*
> 2. **Forgetting the feedforward feedback trim** — pure FF cannot compensate for model error; the KTU examiner will explicitly deduct a mark if your block diagram omits the feedback path.
> 3. **Pade' approximation misapplication** — when asked to find stability with a time-delay, use 1st-order Pade' and *state* the approximation explicitly. Showing no work on the time-delay loses 2 marks.
> 4. **Quotient rule slip on $\partial T/\partial F$** — the most common algebraic mistake. Walk through both numerator and denominator derivatives explicitly.
> 5. **Unit confusion between $F$ in m³/min and kmol/min** — if the problem states $C_{Af}$ in kmol/m³, you must multiply by $F$ in m³/min to get kmol/min. Check units twice.
> 6. **Confusing feedforward with ratio control** — feedforward is dynamic ($G_d(s)/G_p(s)$), ratio is static ($F_2 = k F_1$). Do not interchange.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Feed Control** = either **manipulating** feed or **measuring** feed as a disturbance for feedforward.
- **Canonical CSTR Feed-Temperature Loop:** $T \to G_c \to$ feed valve $\to F \to T$.
- **Energy balance** is the foundation: $F \rho C_p (T_f - T) + (-\Delta H) V r_k - Q = 0$.
- **Process gain** $\partial T/\partial F$ has a **negative numerator** (cold feed cools) and a **positive denominator** (Arrhenius amplifies).
- **Feedforward ideal** $G_{ff}(s) = -G_d(s)/G_p(s)$ — implement as lead-lag.
- **Lead-lag** = $(\tau_p s + 1)/(\tau_d s + 1)$ with steady-state gain $-K_d/K_p$.
- **Always include feedback trim** in a real feedforward design — FF alone is never robust.
- **Pairing for multi-loop CSTR:** Level → outlet flow, Temperature → feed flow, Composition → coolant.
- **Closed-loop characteristic equation:** $1 + G_c G_v G_p G_m = 0$; use Routh-Hurwitz for stability.
- **Cohen-Coon tuning** (PI, no time-delay) is the most commonly tested method: $K_c = \dfrac{\tau_p}{K_p \theta}\left(0.9 + \dfrac{\theta}{3\tau_p}\right)$.
- **Cold-feed rule:** If $T_f < T$, feed acts as coolant (positive feedback to $T$ via reaction). If $T_f > T$, feed preheats.
- **Units, signs, and the time-delay Pade' approximation** are the three places KTU examiners routinely deduct marks — internalize them.
- **Real-world examples** to memorize: CSTR exotherm control, steam-cracker furnace, boiler 3-element drum level, semi-batch pharmaceutical reactors.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Feed Flow Rate
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT743 - INDUSTRIAL PROCESS CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Feed Flow Rate

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Feed Flow Rate (F)** is defined as the **volumetric or mass quantity of process material (feed stream) introduced per unit time into a process unit** from an upstream source. It is the most fundamental manipulated variable in any chemical/industrial plant and is typically expressed in **m³/h, L/min, kg/h, or kmol/h**.

In the formal KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, *Feed Flow Rate* belongs to the class of **Throughput Variables** — variables that govern the **production capacity (turndown ratio)** of a plant. It is mathematically the **independent input** to the process material balance:

$$F_{in} = \rho \cdot Q$$

where:
- $F_{in}$ = mass feed flow rate (kg/s)
- $\rho$ = density of feed (kg/m³)
- $Q$ = volumetric feed flow rate (m³/s)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping (CO2):** This topic directly maps to **Course Outcome 2** — *"Apply the principles of process control to formulate mathematical models of first and second order systems, including interacting and non-interacting systems."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen Tap" Model

Imagine a **kitchen water tap connected to a tank (reactor)**.

| Real-world Action | Process Control Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Turning the tap knob | Adjusting **control valve stem position** |
| Water rushing out of the tap | **Feed Flow Rate (F)** entering the reactor |
| The bucket catching the water | **Process Unit** performing a reaction/separation |
| Overflowing bucket | **Disturbance / Upset** in the process |
| Watching the bucket level rise | **Level Sensor / Flow Transmitter** feedback |
| Auto-adjusting your hand on the knob | **Feedback Controller (PID)** acting on error |

This analogy is crucial: in industry, we never let an operator manually turn a valve forever — we use a **flow controller** that "watches" the flow and adjusts the valve stem automatically.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Standard Volumetric Unit:** Nm³/h (Normal cubic meters per hour, at 0°C, 1 atm)
- **Standard Mass Unit:** **kg/h** or **TPD (Tons Per Day)**
- **Molar Unit:** **kmol/h** (used in reactor design)
- **Standard Reference Density (water at 4°C):** $\rho_{H_2O} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- **Standard Reference Density (air at STP):** $\rho_{air} = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- **Turndown Ratio** of a typical control valve: **10:1 to 50:1**

### 1.4 Classification of Feed Flow

| Class | Description | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Steady-State Feed Flow** | $dF/dt = 0$ | Continuous plants at design point |
| **Dynamic / Transient Feed Flow** | $dF/dt \neq 0$ | Startups, shutdowns, grade transitions |
| **Pulsating Feed Flow** | Reciprocating pump output | High-pressure polymerization |
| **Intermittent Feed Flow** | Batch / Semi-batch operation | Pharmaceutical, specialty chemicals |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The "Why & How" Behind Feed Flow Control

**Why** is feed flow rate treated as the *primary manipulated variable* in almost every plant?

1. It directly determines **residence time** ($\tau = V/F$) inside a reactor.
2. It is the **leading term in the material balance** of every unit operation.
3. It is the **easiest variable to manipulate** using a control valve on the feed line.
4. Changes in $F$ propagate through the plant as the **fastest disturbance path**.

**How** does a feed flow control loop function in real time?

- A **flow transmitter (FT)** measures the instantaneous flow.
- The signal is sent to a **flow controller (FC)**.
- The controller compares measured flow with **setpoint (SP)**.
- The **error $e(t) = SP - PV$** drives a **PID algorithm**.
- The output of the controller moves a **final control element (pneumatic/hydraulic/electric actuated valve)**.
- The valve stem position changes, modifying the **flow area**, and hence the flow rate.

### 2.2 Theoretical Framework — Mass and Volumetric Balance

For any single-input process unit operating at steady state:

$$\sum F_{in} = \sum F_{out} + \text{Accumulation} + \text{Consumption by Reaction}$$

At steady state with no reaction:

$$F_{feed} = F_{product} + F_{byproduct}$$

### 2.3 Flow Through a Restriction — The Heart of Flow Measurement

Most industrial flow meters (orifice, venturi, nozzle) operate on the principle of a **pressure drop across a known restriction**. Applying **Bernoulli's Equation** between upstream point (1) and throat point (2):

$$P_1 + \frac{1}{2}\rho v_1^2 = P_2 + \frac{1}{2}\rho v_2^2$$

By **Continuity Equation**: $A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2 = Q$

Solving these two equations simultaneously yields the ideal volumetric flow:

$$Q_{ideal} = A_2 \sqrt{\frac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho \left(1 - \beta^4\right)}}$$

where $\beta = d/D$ = ratio of throat to pipe diameter.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real Flow Correction:** A **discharge coefficient $C_d$** is introduced to account for vena-contracta effects, viscosity, and turbulence. The working equation is:
> $$Q = C_d A_2 \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho (1-\beta^4)}}$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Variables | KTU Importance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_m = \rho Q$ | Mass flow rate from volumetric flow | $F_m$: kg/s, $\rho$: kg/m³, $Q$: m³/s | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 2 | $F_n = F_m / M$ | Molar flow rate | $F_n$: kmol/s, $M$: kg/kmol | ⭐⭐⭐ |
| 3 | $Q = C_d A_2 \sqrt{2 \Delta P / [\rho(1-\beta^4)]}$ | Orifice / Venturi flow | $C_d \approx 0.61-0.98$ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 4 | $Re = \rho v D / \mu$ | Reynolds number (flow regime) | Dimensionless | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 5 | $v = (1/n) R_h^{2/3} S^{1/2}$ | Manning equation (open channel) | $R_h$: hydraulic radius | ⭐⭐ |
| 6 | $F = C_v \sqrt{\Delta P / G_f}$ | Control valve sizing (ISA) | $C_v$: valve coefficient | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 7 | $Q = C \cdot A \cdot \sqrt{2gh}$ | Weir flow (open channel) | $C$: weir constant | ⭐⭐ |
| 8 | $\tau = V / F$ | Residence time | $\tau$: s, $V$: m³ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 9 | $G(s) = K_p / (\tau_p s + 1)$ | First-order feed flow dynamics | $K_p$: process gain | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| 10 | $K_p = \partial F / \partial u$ | Process gain (valve to flow) | Depends on valve trim | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Petrochemical Refineries:** Crude feed flow to atmospheric distillation column determines **throughput and product slate**.
- **Power Plants:** Boiler feedwater flow governs **steam generation rate**.
- **Pharmaceuticals:** API feed flow into a crystallizer controls **particle size distribution (PSD)**.
- **Wastewater Treatment:** Influent flow rate sets **hydraulic retention time (HRT)** of biological reactors.
- **Food & Beverage:** Feed flow into a **UHT pasteurizer** decides thermal lethality and shelf life.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Mass-Volumetric-Molar Interconversion

**Problem Context:** A naphtha feed stream is measured at $Q = 120 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$ with density $\rho = 740 \text{ kg/m}^3$ and average molecular weight $M = 110 \text{ kg/kmol}$. Find (a) mass flow rate, (b) molar flow rate.

**Step 1 — Convert volumetric flow to SI base units:**

$$Q = 120 \, \frac{\text{m}^3}{\text{h}} \times \frac{1 \text{ h}}{3600 \text{ s}} = 0.03333 \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Apply mass flow equation:**

$$F_m = \rho \cdot Q = 740 \times 0.03333 = 24.666 \, \text{kg/s}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to per-hour basis (engineering units):**

$$F_m = 24.666 \times 3600 = 88,800 \, \text{kg/h} = 88.8 \, \text{tons/h (TPD basis, daily)} \times 24 = 2131.2 \text{ TPD}$$

**Step 4 — Compute molar flow:**

$$F_n = \frac{F_m}{M} = \frac{24.666}{110} = 0.2242 \, \text{kmol/s} = 807.27 \, \text{kmol/h}$$

$$\boxed{F_m = 24.67 \text{ kg/s}, \quad F_n = 0.2242 \text{ kmol/s}, \quad F_{m, daily} = 2131.2 \text{ TPD}}$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Orifice Meter Flow Rate from Pressure Drop

**Problem Context:** An orifice plate in a 4-inch (101.6 mm) Schedule 40 pipe has a throat diameter of 50.8 mm. The differential pressure measured is $\Delta P = 25 \text{ kPa}$. Fluid is water at 30°C ($\rho = 995.7 \text{ kg/m}^3$). $C_d = 0.62$. Compute the volumetric flow rate.

**Step 1 — Compute the diameter ratio $\beta$:**

$$\beta = \frac{d}{D} = \frac{50.8}{101.6} = 0.5000$$

**Step 2 — Compute the geometric correction factor $1-\beta^4$:**

$$1 - \beta^4 = 1 - (0.5)^4 = 1 - 0.0625 = 0.9375$$

**Step 3 — Compute throat area $A_2$:**

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.0508)^2}{4} = 2.027 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into the working orifice flow equation:**

$$Q = C_d \cdot A_2 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho (1-\beta^4)}}$$

$$Q = 0.62 \times 2.027 \times 10^{-3} \times \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 25{,}000}{995.7 \times 0.9375}}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the inner fraction:**

$$\frac{2 \times 25{,}000}{995.7 \times 0.9375} = \frac{50{,}000}{933.47} = 53.566 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}^2$$

**Step 6 — Take the square root:**

$$\sqrt{53.566} = 7.319 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 7 — Multiply all terms:**

$$Q = 0.62 \times 2.027 \times 10^{-3} \times 7.319 = 9.197 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 8 — Convert to engineering unit:**

$$Q = 9.197 \times 10^{-3} \times 3600 = 33.11 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$$

$$\boxed{Q = 33.11 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}}$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Linearization of the Square-Root Flow Equation

**Engineering Need:** The orifice equation is non-linear (square root of $\Delta P$). For controller tuning, we need a **linearized first-order model**:

$$F(t) = K_p \cdot u(t) \quad \text{with dynamics} \quad \tau_p \frac{dF}{dt} + F = K_p u$$

**Step 1 — Start from the working equation:**

$$F = K_v \cdot C_d \cdot \sqrt{\Delta P} = K \cdot \sqrt{\Delta P}$$

**Step 2 — Define operating point $(\Delta P_0, F_0)$:**

$$F_0 = K \sqrt{\Delta P_0}$$

**Step 3 — Apply first-order Taylor series expansion around the operating point:**

$$F = F_0 + \left.\frac{\partial F}{\partial \Delta P}\right|_0 (\Delta P - \Delta P_0)$$

**Step 4 — Compute the partial derivative:**

$$\frac{\partial F}{\partial \Delta P} = \frac{K}{2\sqrt{\Delta P}}\bigg|_0 = \frac{F_0}{2 \Delta P_0}$$

**Step 5 — Define deviation variables $\tilde{F} = F - F_0$ and $\tilde{\Delta P} = \Delta P - \Delta P_0$:**

$$\tilde{F} = \frac{F_0}{2 \Delta P_0} \tilde{\Delta P}$$

**Step 6 — The slope is the linearized process gain:**

$$K_p = \frac{F_0}{2 \Delta P_0}$$

This is why orifice meters are commonly **paired with square-root extractors** in DCS — to provide a linear $F$ vs $\Delta P$ signal directly to the controller.

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Residence Time and Conversion in a CSTR

For a first-order reaction $A \to B$ in a CSTR of volume $V$ with volumetric feed $Q$:

$$\tau = \frac{V}{Q}$$

The steady-state conversion $X_A$ is:

$$X_A = \frac{k \tau}{1 + k \tau}$$

**Numerical:** $V = 5 \text{ m}^3$, $Q = 2 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$, $k = 0.4 \text{ h}^{-1}$:

$$\tau = \frac{5}{2} = 2.5 \text{ h}, \quad X_A = \frac{0.4 \times 2.5}{1 + 0.4 \times 2.5} = \frac{1.0}{2.0} = 0.5 = 50\%$$

A **5% increase in $Q$** gives $\tau = 5/2.1 = 2.381$ h, and $X_A = 0.488$ — a **2.4% drop in conversion**, showing the strong sensitivity of $X_A$ to feed flow disturbances.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Sensitivity of Process Variables to Feed Flow

| Process Variable | Symbol | Sensitivity $\partial Y/\partial F$ | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| Residence Time | $\tau$ | $-\tau/F$ | Inverse relationship — higher flow, lower $\tau$ |
| Conversion (1st order CSTR) | $X_A$ | $-V/[F(1+k\tau)^2]$ | Higher flow → lower conversion |
| Heat Duty | $Q_H$ | $+\Delta H_R \cdot C_{A0}$ | Linear in feed |
| Holdup (constant pressure drop) | $h$ | $+$ | Tank level rises |
| Pressure drop (laminar) | $\Delta P$ | $+32 \mu L Q / (D^4 A)$ | Linear in flow |
| Pressure drop (turbulent) | $\Delta P$ | $\propto Q^{1.75}$ | Strongly non-linear |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Feed Flow Control Loop — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP[Setpoint SP] --> SUM[Error Summer e_t]
    PV[Flow Transmitter FT] --> SUM
    SUM --> CTRL[PID Controller FC]
    CTRL --> VALVE[Control Valve on Feed Line]
    VALVE --> PROCESS[Process Unit Reactor]
    PROCESS --> SENSE[Flow Sensor Element Orifice]
    SENSE --> PV
    VALVE -. disturbance .-> DIST[Upstream Pressure Variation]
    DIST -.-> PROCESS
    classDef ctrl fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef sensor fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef final fill:#d62728,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    class CTRL ctrl
    class FT,SENSE,PV sensor
    class VALVE,PROCESS final
```

### 4.2 Feed Flow Signal Conditioning Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Process Pipe Carrying Feed] --> B[Orifice Plate Restriction]
    B --> C[Differential Pressure Transmitter dPT]
    C --> D[Square Root Extractor SR]
    D --> E[Linear Flow Signal 4 to 20 mA]
    E --> F[DCS PID Block]
    F --> G[I to P Converter Current to Pressure]
    G --> H[Pneumatic Diaphragm Actuator]
    H --> I[Valve Stem Position Changes]
    I --> J[New Flow Rate Achieved]
    classDef hw fill:#9467bd,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef ctrl fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    class A,B,C,H,I,J hw
    class D,F,G ctrl
```

### 4.3 Mass Balance Schematic of a Generic Process Unit

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F[Feed F in] --> MIX[Process Unit V]
    P[Product F out] --> MIX
    R[Reaction Consumption R] --> MIX
    ACC[Accumulation dM dt] --> MIX
    MIX -.-> F
    MIX -.-> P
    MIX -.-> R
    MIX -.-> ACC
    style MIX fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

### 4.4 Functional Architecture — Cascade Control of Feed Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    OUTER[Outer Loop Level Controller LC] --> CAS[Inner Loop Setpoint to FC]
    CAS --> INNER[Inner Loop Flow Controller FC]
    INNER --> VLV[Feed Control Valve]
    VLV --> TANK[Process Tank]
    TANK --> LEV[Level Sensor LT]
    TANK --> FLW[Flow Sensor FT]
    LEV --> OUTER
    FLW --> INNER
    classDef outerC fill:#8c564b,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef innerC fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef finalE fill:#d62728,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    class OUTER,CAS outerC
    class INNER innerC
    class VLV,TANK,LEV,FLW finalE
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Cascade?** The inner flow loop is **fast** (seconds) and rejects **upstream pressure disturbances** before they propagate to the level. This is a **classic KTU exam question** pattern.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

Define **feed flow rate**. List **three industrial flow measurement devices** used for measuring it.

**Model Answer:**

> **Feed flow rate** is the volumetric or mass quantity of process material introduced per unit time into a process unit. It is the most commonly manipulated variable in process control.
>
> Three industrial flow measurement devices:
> 1. **Differential Pressure (Orifice / Venturi / Flow Nozzle)** — based on Bernoulli principle.
> 2. **Magnetic Flow Meter** — based on Faraday's law, used for conductive liquids.
> 3. **Coriolis Mass Flow Meter** — directly measures mass flow and density.
>
> *(Other acceptable answers: Vortex shedding, Turbine, Ultrasonic, Thermal mass flow meter, Rotameter.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

What is a **square-root extractor**? Why is it used in flow measurement loops?

**Model Answer:**

A **square-root extractor** is a signal conditioning module (hardware or software block) that computes $\sqrt{\Delta P}$ from a differential pressure transmitter signal.

**Why used:** The flow through an orifice is proportional to $\sqrt{\Delta P}$, which is a **non-linear** relationship. The square-root extractor **linearizes** the signal so that the flow is directly proportional to a **4–20 mA** signal fed to the controller. Without it, the loop behaves non-linearly, making PID tuning difficult and asymmetric.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**

**(a)** Derive the **working equation of an orifice meter**, clearly defining the discharge coefficient $C_d$ and the diameter ratio $\beta$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A feed line of diameter **150 mm** carries a liquid of density **900 kg/m³** and viscosity **1.2 cP**. An orifice of diameter **75 mm** is installed, and a differential pressure of **40 kPa** is observed. If the discharge coefficient $C_d = 0.65$, calculate:
  - (i) the **volumetric flow rate** in m³/h
  - (ii) the **mass flow rate** in kg/h
  - (iii) the **Reynolds number** at the pipe *(5 marks)*

---

### **Model Solution for Question A**

#### Part (a) — Derivation of Orifice Meter Equation (7 marks)

Consider fluid flowing through a pipe of cross-section $A_1$ and velocity $v_1$, and an orifice plate with throat area $A_2$ and velocity $v_2$.

**Step 1 — Apply Continuity Equation (mass conservation for incompressible flow):**

$$A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad v_1 = \frac{A_2}{A_1} v_2 = \beta^2 v_2$$

**Step 2 — Apply Bernoulli's Equation between upstream (1) and throat (2):**

$$P_1 + \frac{1}{2}\rho v_1^2 = P_2 + \frac{1}{2}\rho v_2^2$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange for $\Delta P = P_1 - P_2$:**

$$\Delta P = \frac{1}{2}\rho(v_2^2 - v_1^2) = \frac{1}{2}\rho v_2^2 (1 - \beta^4)$$

**Step 4 — Solve for throat velocity $v_2$:**

$$v_2 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1-\beta^4}} \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}$$

**Step 5 — Compute ideal volumetric flow:**

$$Q_{ideal} = A_2 v_2 = \frac{A_2}{\sqrt{1-\beta^4}} \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}$$

**Step 6 — Introduce discharge coefficient $C_d$ to correct for real flow losses:**

$$Q = C_d \cdot A_2 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho (1-\beta^4)}}$$

**Definitions:**
- **$C_d$ (discharge coefficient):** Ratio of actual flow to ideal (theoretical) flow, accounts for vena-contracta, friction, and turbulence. Typical range: **0.61 to 0.98**.
- **$\beta$ (diameter ratio):** $\beta = d/D$, where $d$ is orifice throat diameter and $D$ is pipe diameter.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:** $D = 0.150$ m, $d = 0.075$ m, $\rho = 900$ kg/m³, $\mu = 1.2$ cP $= 1.2 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s, $\Delta P = 40{,}000$ Pa, $C_d = 0.65$.

**Step 1 — Compute $\beta$:**

$$\beta = \frac{0.075}{0.150} = 0.500$$

**Step 2 — Compute $1 - \beta^4$:**

$$1 - (0.5)^4 = 1 - 0.0625 = 0.9375$$

**Step 3 — Compute throat area $A_2$:**

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi (0.075)^2}{4} = 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Compute volumetric flow rate $Q$:**

$$Q = 0.65 \times 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \times \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 40{,}000}{900 \times 0.9375}}$$

$$Q = 0.65 \times 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \times \sqrt{\frac{80{,}000}{843.75}}$$

$$Q = 0.65 \times 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \times \sqrt{94.815} = 0.65 \times 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \times 9.738$$

$$Q = 0.02797 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 100.7 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$$

**(i) Volumetric flow rate: $Q \approx 100.7 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$** ✅

**Step 5 — Compute mass flow rate $F_m$:**

$$F_m = \rho \cdot Q = 900 \times 0.02797 = 25.17 \text{ kg/s} = 90{,}612 \text{ kg/h}$$

**(ii) Mass flow rate: $F_m \approx 90{,}612 \text{ kg/h}$** ✅

**Step 6 — Compute pipe cross-section $A_1$ and velocity $v_1$:**

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi (0.150)^2}{4} = 1.767 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^2$$

$$v_1 = \frac{Q}{A_1} = \frac{0.02797}{1.767 \times 10^{-2}} = 1.583 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 7 — Compute Reynolds number:**

$$Re = \frac{\rho v_1 D}{\mu} = \frac{900 \times 1.583 \times 0.150}{1.2 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{213.7}{1.2 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.78 \times 10^{5}$$

**(iii) Reynolds number: $Re \approx 1.78 \times 10^5$ (turbulent flow)** ✅

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part A & B):**
> - Students frequently **forget the $1-\beta^4$ term** in the denominator — this is the most common error and costs **2 marks**.
> - Mixing up **density and specific gravity** — for water, SG = 1, but in general $\rho = \text{SG} \times 1000$ kg/m³.
> - Computing Reynolds number at the **throat** instead of the **pipe** — KTU expects pipe-based Re.
> - **Unit conversion for viscosity:** 1 cP = $10^{-3}$ Pa·s — forgetting this is a recurring mistake.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style] — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain with a neat **block diagram** the **cascade control of feed flow rate** in a process tank. How is it superior to a single-loop flow control? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A CSTR has a working volume of **8 m³** and is fed with a reactant solution at a rate of **3 m³/h**. The reaction is first-order with rate constant $k = 0.6 \text{ h}^{-1}$.
  - (i) Calculate the **residence time** and **steady-state conversion**.
  - (ii) If the feed flow rate is increased by **20%**, find the **new conversion**.
  - (iii) Comment on the sensitivity of conversion to feed flow. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B**

#### Part (a) — Cascade Control of Feed Flow (7 marks)

**Cascade control** uses **two controllers in series**: a *primary (master/slow)* loop and a *secondary (slave/fast)* loop.

**Components of cascade feed flow control:**

1. **Primary variable:** Tank level $h$ — measured by Level Transmitter (**LT**).
2. **Primary controller:** Level Controller (**LC**) — slow loop, output becomes setpoint of secondary.
3. **Secondary variable:** Feed flow rate $F$ — measured by Flow Transmitter (**FT**).
4. **Secondary controller:** Flow Controller (**FC**) — fast loop, output drives the valve.
5. **Final control element:** Feed control valve on the inlet line.

**Block diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    HSP[Level Setpoint] --> LERR[Summing Junction e1]
    LT[Level Transmitter LT] --> LERR
    LERR --> LC[Level Controller LC Slow]
    LC --> CSP[Flow Setpoint for Inner Loop]
    CSP --> FERR[Summing Junction e2]
    FT[Flow Transmitter FT] --> FERR
    FERR --> FC[Flow Controller FC Fast]
    FC --> VLV[Control Valve]
    VLV --> TANK[Process Tank]
    TANK --> LT
    TANK --> FT
    classDef primary fill:#8c564b,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef secondary fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    classDef element fill:#d62728,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    class HSP,LERR,LC,CSP,LT primary
    class FERR,FC,FT secondary
    class VLV,TANK element
```

**Superiority over single-loop flow control:**

| Aspect | Single-Loop Flow | Cascade Flow + Level |
|---|---|---|
| Disturbance rejection | Poor (slow response) | Excellent (inner flow loop absorbs disturbances in seconds) |
| Upstream pressure variations | Affect level directly | Negligible effect on level |
| Setpoint tracking | Decent | Excellent for both level and flow |
| Tuning complexity | Simple | Two loops — must tune inner first, then outer |
| Robustness | Moderate | High |

> **Key advantage:** The **inner flow loop** acts as a *fast shield* that keeps flow at the value demanded by the level controller, regardless of upstream pressure fluctuations.

---

#### Part (b) — CSTR Conversion Analysis (7 marks)

**Given:** $V = 8 \text{ m}^3$, $F_0 = Q_0 = 3 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$, $k = 0.6 \text{ h}^{-1}$.

**Step 1 — Compute initial residence time $\tau_0$:**

$$\tau_0 = \frac{V}{F_0} = \frac{8}{3} = 2.667 \text{ h}$$

**Step 2 — Compute initial conversion $X_{A,0}$ for first-order CSTR:**

$$X_{A,0} = \frac{k \tau_0}{1 + k \tau_0} = \frac{0.6 \times 2.667}{1 + 0.6 \times 2.667} = \frac{1.600}{2.600} = 0.6154$$

**(i) Residence time = 2.667 h, Conversion = 61.54%** ✅

**Step 3 — Apply 20% increase to feed flow:**

$$F_1 = 1.20 \times 3 = 3.6 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$$

**Step 4 — Compute new residence time $\tau_1$:**

$$\tau_1 = \frac{8}{3.6} = 2.222 \text{ h}$$

**Step 5 — Compute new conversion $X_{A,1}$:**

$$X_{A,1} = \frac{0.6 \times 2.222}{1 + 0.6 \times 2.222} = \frac{1.333}{2.333} = 0.5714$$

**(ii) New conversion = 57.14%** ✅

**Step 6 — Compute percentage drop in conversion:**

$$\Delta X_A = \frac{0.6154 - 0.5714}{0.6154} \times 100\% = 7.15\% \text{ drop}$$

**Step 7 — Sensitivity analysis:**

$$\text{Sensitivity} = \frac{\Delta X_A / X_A}{\Delta F / F} = \frac{-7.15\%}{+20\%} = -0.358$$

**(iii) Comment:** A 20% increase in feed flow causes approximately a **7.15% drop in conversion**, giving a sensitivity of **−0.358**. This indicates that **conversion is moderately sensitive** to feed flow disturbances. The negative sign confirms an inverse relationship — **higher flow reduces residence time, which reduces conversion**. For processes with high sensitivity (e.g., slow reactions where $k\tau$ is small), strict **feed flow control is essential** to maintain product quality.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Question B):**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Writing $X_A = k\tau$ (PFR equation) instead of $X_A = k\tau/(1+k\tau)$ for CSTR — costs **2 marks**.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting to recompute $\tau$ when flow changes — this is the most critical step.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Not defining the **sensitivity formula** explicitly in the answer.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** In part (a), drawing a **single-loop** diagram instead of **cascade** — the very word "cascade" must show two controllers.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Feed Flow Rate**

- ✅ **Definition:** $F_m = \rho Q$, $F_n = F_m / M$ — must know all three forms.
- ✅ **Master Equation:** $Q = C_d A_2 \sqrt{2 \Delta P / [\rho (1-\beta^4)]}$ — **do not forget the $\beta^4$ term**.
- ✅ **Square-Root Relationship:** Flow $\propto \sqrt{\Delta P}$ → **linearize** using a square-root extractor.
- ✅ **Linearized Gain:** $K_p = F_0 / (2 \Delta P_0)$ — used in controller tuning.
- ✅ **Residence Time:** $\tau = V/F$ — inverse with flow; critical for reactor design.
- ✅ **CSTR Conversion:** $X_A = k\tau / (1 + k\tau)$ — for 1st-order, isothermal, constant density.
- ✅ **Cascade Control:** Always tune the **inner (fast) loop first**, then the outer (slow) loop.
- ✅ **Reynolds Number:** Always compute at the **pipe diameter**, not throat, for flow classification.
- ✅ **Valve Sizing:** $F = C_v \sqrt{\Delta P / G_f}$ — $C_v$ characterizes valve capacity.
- ✅ **Sensing Elements:** Orifice (cheap), Venturi (low loss), Coriolis (most accurate), Magnetic (conductive liquids only).
- ✅ **Disturbances in Feed Flow:** Upstream pressure variation, pump speed drift, viscosity change, partial valve plug.

> **End of Module 2 Notes — Feed Flow Rate**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Variable Column Feed
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT743 - INDUSTRIAL PROCESS CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Variable Column Feed

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Variable Column Feed** refers to a set of distillation column control strategies, instrumentation schemes, and material/energy balance compensations designed to maintain desired product specifications (top composition $x_D$ and bottom composition $x_B$) when one or more of the incoming feed conditions — **feed flow rate ($F$)**, **feed composition ($z_F$)**, **feed temperature ($T_F$)**, or **feed thermal condition ($q$)** — fluctuate with time.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, variable column feed analysis lies at the intersection of **process dynamics** and **advanced regulatory control**. The control engineer must identify each feed-side disturbance, classify it (load vs. supply-side), and design a controller configuration (feedback / feedforward / ratio / cascade) that rejects the disturbance before product quality drifts.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PEICT743 – Module 2):** The concept of variable feed covers single-, two-, and multi-product (sidestream) columns, binary and multi-component separations, and the use of $q$-line geometry to predict how the operating point slides on the McCabe–Thiele diagram.

### Conceptual Analogy

Imagine you are brewing filter coffee in a traditional Kerala *chembu* (copper vessel). The recipe says: "1 part coffee, 4 parts water, simmer 8 minutes."

- If someone **sneakily pours in extra water mid-way** (variable feed flow) → you instinctively turn up the heat to keep the brew strong.
- If they **substitute part of the coffee with chicory** (variable feed composition) → you may need to extend brewing time and adjust the flame.
- If they pour in **boiling hot water vs. lukewarm water** (variable feed temperature) → you adjust stove intensity to hit the same final concentration.

A distillation column faces exactly the same disturbances — but on a tonne-per-hour industrial scale where even a 1 % composition drift can cost lakhs in lost product. **Variable column feed control is the "smart stove-and-timer" of distillation.**

### Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Industrial Benchmarks)

| Metric | Typical Industrial Range |
|---|---|
| Feed flow perturbation magnitude | **±5 % to ±20 %** of nominal $F$ |
| Feed composition drift | **±2 % to ±10 %** mole fraction |
| Reflux ratio ($R = L/D$) operating window | **1.0 to 5.0** (multi-component higher) |
| Thermal condition $q$ | **0 (saturated vapor) to 1 (saturated liquid)** |

> [!TIP]
> A feed is "saturated liquid" when $q = 1$, "saturated vapor" when $q = 0$, "subcooled liquid" when $q > 1$, and "superheated vapor" when $q < 0$. The value of $q$ directly affects how much external reflux and boilup must be supplied by the condensers and reboiler.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Feed-Side Degrees of Freedom

A distillation column feed possesses **four independent variables** that can each act as a disturbance:

1. **Feed flow rate, $F$** — directly scales the throughput and the load on both condenser and reboiler.
2. **Feed composition, $z_F$** — shifts the operating line slope (in McCabe–Thiele terms) and changes the minimum number of stages.
3. **Feed temperature / thermal condition, $q$** — alters the location of the feed plate and the internal liquid/vapor traffic.
4. **Feed pressure (less common as control variable)** — typically considered constant.

> [!NOTE]
> The **$q$-line** is the geometric locus on the $x$–$y$ diagram where the operating lines of the rectifying and stripping sections intersect. Its equation is $y = \dfrac{q}{q-1} x - \dfrac{z_F}{q-1}$. A change in $q$ rotates this line, and a change in $z_F$ translates it — both of which move the optimal feed-plate location.

### 2.2 Material-Balance Logic (Step-by-Step Bullets)

- **Step 1 — Global mass balance:** Under steady state, what enters must leave.
- **Step 2 — Component mass balance:** Each chemical species obeys its own ledger.
- **Step 3 — Disturbance propagation:** A rise in $F$ or $z_F$ (more light key) floods the rectifying section; the controller must either increase $D$ to bleed off light key or boost $L/V$ to sharpen the cut.
- **Step 4 — Reset action:** The accumulator-level loop and bottoms-level loop re-establish the inventory; the composition loop trims the product purity.

### 2.3 Energy-Balance Logic

- A **hotter** feed (high $T_F$ or subcooled, $q > 1$) supplies extra sensible heat → less reboiler duty $Q_R$ is required.
- A **colder** or partially vaporized feed ($q < 1$) drains heat → $Q_R$ must climb.
- The feed temperature effectively *biases* the heat-integrated energy picture of the column.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **high-yield equation pack** for variable column feed problems. Master these before attempting any university numerical.

| # | Equation | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F = D + B$ | Overall steady-state mass balance | kmol/h |
| 2 | $F \, z_F = D \, x_D + B \, x_B$ | Light-key component balance | kmol/h |
| 3 | $\dfrac{D}{F} = \dfrac{z_F - x_B}{x_D - x_B}$ | Distillate-to-feed ratio (recovery equation) | — |
| 4 | $\dfrac{B}{F} = \dfrac{x_D - z_F}{x_D - x_B}$ | Bottoms-to-feed ratio | — |
| 5 | $y = \dfrac{q}{q-1} x - \dfrac{z_F}{q-1}$ | $q$-line (feed condition line) | — |
| 6 | $L = R \, D$ | Reflux flow in terms of reflux ratio | kmol/h |
| 7 | $V = L + D = (R+1) D$ | Vapor boilup rate | kmol/h |
| 8 | $\bar{L} = L + q F$ | Liquid flow below feed plate | kmol/h |
| 9 | $\bar{V} = V - (1-q) F$ | Vapor flow below feed plate | kmol/h |
| 10 | $Q_R = \lambda \, \bar{V}$ | Reboiler duty (approx.) | kW |
| 11 | $Q_C = \lambda \, V$ | Condenser duty | kW |
| 12 | $\Delta z_F \;\Rightarrow\; \Delta D = F \dfrac{\Delta z_F}{x_D - x_B}$ | Sensitivity of $D$ to feed drift | kmol/h |
| 13 | $\Delta z_F \;\Rightarrow\; \Delta V = \dfrac{F \, \Delta z_F}{(1 - x_D/x_B)}$ (LV config.) | Boilup compensation in LV scheme | kmol/h |
| 14 | $G_{ff}(s) = -\dfrac{K_{F}}{K_{V}} \, \dfrac{\tau_V s + 1}{\tau_F s + 1}$ | Generic feedforward lead-lag compensator | — |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Pipe Symbol Caution:** In any KTU exam answer sheet, never write $\vert x \vert$ for absolute value inside a sentence that contains the table pipe character; it breaks the markdown table. Always use $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\mid x \mid$ if you must embed it. The table above already complies.

### 2.5 Engineering Utility

Variable-feed control theory is the workhorse behind:
- **Crude atmospheric and vacuum distillation units** in refineries (feed composition swings as different crude blends arrive).
- **Ethylene fractionators** where C2/C3 ratios drift hourly.
- **Air-separation cryogenic columns** where ambient humidity changes the feed air composition.
- **Solvent-recovery columns** in pharmaceutical batch plants with recipe-driven feed variability.

> [!TIP]
> The LV configuration (manipulating **L** and **V**, holding $D$ and $B$ on level control) is the most popular choice for variable-feed columns because it is **feed-conditions-invariant** at the steady-state operating point — meaning changes in $q$ and $z_F$ do not break the linear decoupling of the two composition loops.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation A — The Recovery Equation ($D/F$ ratio)

We start from the two independent balances:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(i)  Total mass balance:} \quad F &= D + B \\
\text{(ii) Component balance:} \quad F \, z_F &= D \, x_D + B \, x_B
\end{aligned}
$$

**Row-by-row explanation:**
- We express $B$ from (i): $B = F - D$.
- Substitute into (ii):

$$
\begin{aligned}
F \, z_F &= D \, x_D + (F - D) \, x_B \\
F \, z_F &= D \, x_D + F \, x_B - D \, x_B \\
F \, z_F - F \, x_B &= D \, x_D - D \, x_B \\
F \, (z_F - x_B) &= D \, (x_D - x_B)
\end{aligned}
$$

- Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\;\dfrac{D}{F} = \dfrac{z_F - x_B}{x_D - x_B}\;}
$$

**Engineering meaning:** This ratio is the *fractional recovery* of the light key in the distillate. If $z_F$ drifts upward (more light key in the feed), either $D$ must rise or $x_B$ must fall (or both) to keep the same $x_D$.

### 3.2 Derivation B — Material-Balance Control Law (Feedforward)

Suppose the controlled variable is $x_D$ and the manipulated variable is $D$, with $z_F$ as the measured disturbance.

Take the partial derivative of the recovery relation treating $x_D$ as the controlled output and $B$ as level-controlled (i.e. $B$ adjusts on its own loop, but for a *steady-state feedforward* design we set $x_B$ and $x_D$ as targets).

Differentiate the recovery relation with respect to $z_F$ (holding $x_D$, $x_B$ at setpoint):

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= F \cdot \dfrac{z_F - x_B}{x_D - x_B} \\
\dfrac{\partial D}{\partial z_F} &= \dfrac{F}{x_D - x_B}
\end{aligned}
$$
