For an **incremental change** $\Delta z_F$, the required correction in $D$ is:

$$
\boxed{\;\Delta D = \dfrac{F}{x_D - x_B} \, \Delta z_F\;}
$$

This is the **static feedforward law** for the distillate flow. The corresponding dynamic compensator adds a lead-lag term, yielding the form in Formula #14 of the cheat sheet.

### 3.3 Derivation C — Internal Flows in the Stripping Section

From the standard column traffic definitions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Rectifying section: } V &= L + D \\
\text{Feed plate: enters at rate } F \text{ with thermal condition } q \\
q F &= \text{fraction of feed that joins the liquid phase} \\
(1-q) F &= \text{fraction that joins the vapor phase}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, **below the feed plate**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{L} &= L + q F \\
\bar{V} &= V - (1-q) F
\end{aligned}
$$

**Row-by-row meaning:**
- The liquid descending from the feed plate equals the reflux coming down plus the *liquid* fraction of the feed ($q F$).
- The vapor rising from below the feed plate equals the boilup $V$ minus the *vapor* fraction of the feed $(1-q) F$ — because that portion never needed to be boiled up by the reboiler.

If $q$ drops (e.g., feed becomes partially vaporized), $\bar{L}$ shrinks and $\bar{V}$ grows — the **entire stripping section traffic** shifts, often moving the operating point off the optimum feed plate.

### 3.4 Derivation D — Energy Compensation for Feed Temperature Drift

The reboiler duty must supply the latent heat for $\bar{V}$ plus any deficit in feed enthalpy:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_R &= \lambda \, \bar{V} + F \, c_p \, (T_{b,\text{sat}} - T_F) \quad \text{(for subcooled feed, } q>1\text{)} \\
Q_R &= \lambda \, \bar{V} - F \, c_p \, (T_F - T_{b,\text{sat}}) \quad \text{(for partially vaporized feed, } q<1\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:**
- $T_{b,\text{sat}}$ is the bubble-point temperature of the bottoms.
- A cold feed ($T_F < T_{b,\text{sat}}$) *steals* sensible heat from the column; the reboiler must compensate.
- A feed already partly vapor ($q < 1$) actually *donates* latent heat, so $Q_R$ can be lowered.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix (Master Summary of Cause → Effect → Action)

| Feed Variable (Disturbance) | Effect on Rectifying Section | Effect on Stripping Section | Recommended Control Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\uparrow F$ (flow up) | Higher $L$, $V$ demand | Higher $\bar{L}$, $\bar{V}$ | Increase $L$ (or $D$) proportionally; reset both level loops |
| $\uparrow z_F$ (richer feed) | $D$ must rise to dump light key | $x_B$ tends to rise | Open $D$ valve by $\Delta D = F \Delta z_F / (x_D - x_B)$ |
| $\uparrow q$ (colder / more liquid feed) | Less reboiler load (sensible heat demand) | Higher $\bar{L}$ | Trim $Q_R$ downward; consider lower reflux |
| $\downarrow q$ (hotter / partially vapor) | Reboiler load rises | Lower $\bar{L}$ | Boost $Q_R$ via steam valve |

> [!NOTE]
> All four rows above are **standard 14-mark KTU ESE patterns**. The Examiner rewards students who list the *cause*, the *mechanism*, and the *manipulated-variable response* explicitly — not just the final answer.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture — Feedforward + Feedback Control of $x_D$ under Variable Feed

The Mermaid diagram below maps the **Block-Level Functional Architecture** of a typical variable-feed distillation control loop. It shows the path of the disturbance signal $z_F$ (or $F$), the feedforward compensator $G_{ff}(s)$, the feedback controller $G_c(s)$, and the column $G_p(s)$.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Disturbance_Input
        zF["Feed composition zF disturbance"]
        Fflow["Feed flow F disturbance"]
    end

    subgraph Measurement
        zFm["zF analyzer transmitter"]
        xDm["xD composition transmitter"]
    end

    subgraph Control_Core
        FFC["Feedforward compensator Gff"]
        FBC["Feedback controller Gc"]
        SUMA["Summer subtract"]
        SUMB["Summer add"]
    end

    subgraph Process_Plant
        COL["Distillation column Gp"]
        REB["Reboiler duty QR"]
        COND["Condenser and reflux drum"]
    end

    subgraph Manipulated_Outputs
        Dvalve["Distillate valve D"]
        Lvalve["Reflux valve L"]
    end

    zF --> zFm --> FFC
    Fflow --> FFC
    xDm --> SUMA
    setpoint["xD setpoint"] --> SUMA
    SUMA --> FBC
    FFC --> SUMB
    FBC --> SUMB
    SUMB --> Dvalve --> COL
    Dvalve --> COND
    COL --> REB --> COL
    COL --> xDm
    COND --> Lvalve --> COL
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **feed-side disturbances** ($z_F$ and $F$) are measured online and routed to the feedforward compensator $G_{ff}$.
- The **product-side composition** $x_D$ is measured at the distillate line and fed back to the comparator (Summer A) against its setpoint.
- The feedforward and feedback signals are summed (Summer B) and dispatched to the **manipulated valves** ($D$ and $L$).
- This architecture is the canonical **"feedforward trim + feedback reset"** scheme recommended in the KTU Module 2 syllabus.

### 4.2 Process Flow Schematic — Variable-Feed Distillation Column

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Feed_Stream
        FEED["Variable feed F, zF, TF"]
    end

    subgraph Column_Body
        REC["Rectifying section trays"]
        FEEDPLATE["Feed plate location slides with q"]
        STRIP["Stripping section trays"]
        REBOIL["Reboiler QR"]
    end

    subgraph Overhead_System
        COND2["Total condenser"]
        DRUM["Reflux drum"]
        REFLUX["Reflux pump L"]
        DIST["Distillate product D, xD"]
    end

    subgraph Bottoms_System
        BOTT["Bottoms product B, xB"]
    end

    FEED --> FEEDPLATE
    FEEDPLATE --> REC
    FEEDPLATE --> STRIP
    REC --> COND2 --> DRUM --> REFLUX --> REC
    DRUM --> DIST
    STRIP --> REBOIL --> STRIP
    REBOIL --> BOTT
```

> [!TIP]
> Note that the **feed plate is not a fixed location** when $q$ or $z_F$ varies. The optimal feed plate is the one closest to the intersection of the rectifying and stripping operating lines — and that intersection is *exactly* the $q$-line intersection point derived in §3.1. This is why **variable feed control** is inseparable from the geometry of the $q$-line.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the thermal condition $q$ of a distillation feed. List the four standard cases of $q$ and the corresponding $q$-line slopes.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** The thermal condition $q$ is defined as the fraction of the feed that is in the liquid phase at the feed plate; mathematically $q$ = (moles of liquid formed per mole of feed) and ranges from $-\infty$ to $+\infty$ in the general case, but industrially from $0$ to $1$.
- **Cases (2 marks):**
  * **$q = 1$** (saturated liquid): $q$-line is vertical, slope $\to \infty$.
  * **$0 < q < 1$** (two-phase mixture): $q$-line has finite negative slope between $-\infty$ and $0$.
  * **$q = 0$** (saturated vapor): $q$-line is horizontal, slope $= 0$.
  * **$q < 0$ or $q > 1$** (superheated vapor / subcooled liquid): $q$-line has positive slope.

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between feedback and feedforward control strategies for handling feed composition disturbances in a distillation column.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Feedback (1.5 marks):** Measures the *controlled variable* (e.g., $x_D$) and acts *after* the disturbance has propagated through the column; inherently corrective but introduces a dead time equal to the column's transport delay.
- **Feedforward (1.5 marks):** Measures the *disturbance* (e.g., $z_F$) directly and adjusts the manipulated variable ($D$ or $V$) *before* the product is affected; requires a process model and a lead-lag compensator, but eliminates the dead time.
- **Verdict:** A combined FF + FB scheme is standard in industry — feedforward provides fast correction, feedback removes steady-state error.

---

### Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Scheme mandates an **internal choice** at the module level. Both options below carry equal weight and test the same CO at the **Apply / Analyze** Bloom level.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Material-Balance Focus**

**(a)** *A binary distillation column separates a feed of $F = 100$ kmol/h with $z_F = 0.50$ (mole fraction of light key) into a distillate of $x_D = 0.95$ and bottoms of $x_B = 0.05$. The feed is saturated liquid ($q = 1$) and the column operates at a reflux ratio $R = 2.5$.* **[7 marks]**

  **(i)** Calculate the distillate and bottoms flow rates.
  **(ii)** Determine the liquid and vapor flow rates in both the rectifying and stripping sections.
  **(iii)** Estimate the reboiler duty if the latent heat of vaporization is $\lambda = 30{,}000$ kJ/kmol.

**(b)** *If the feed composition suddenly drifts to $z_F = 0.55$ (with $x_D$ and $x_B$ targets unchanged), calculate the new distillate flow rate required to maintain product specifications. State one practical control action an operator can take.* **[7 marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A**

**(a)(i) — Distillate and Bottoms Flow Rates [3 marks]**

Using the recovery equation derived in §3.1:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{D}{F} &= \dfrac{z_F - x_B}{x_D - x_B} = \dfrac{0.50 - 0.05}{0.95 - 0.05} = \dfrac{0.45}{0.90} = 0.50 \\
D &= 0.50 \times 100 = 50 \text{ kmol/h} \\
B &= F - D = 100 - 50 = 50 \text{ kmol/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(a)(ii) — Internal Flows [2 marks]**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= R \cdot D = 2.5 \times 50 = 125 \text{ kmol/h} \\
V &= L + D = 125 + 50 = 175 \text{ kmol/h} \\
\bar{L} &= L + q F = 125 + 1 \times 100 = 225 \text{ kmol/h} \\
\bar{V} &= V - (1-q) F = 175 - 0 = 175 \text{ kmol/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(a)(iii) — Reboiler Duty [2 marks]**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_R &= \lambda \, \bar{V} = 30{,}000 \times 175 \\
&= 5.25 \times 10^{6} \text{ kJ/h} = 1{,}458.3 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) — New Distillate Flow for Drifted Feed [7 marks]**

Apply the same recovery equation with $z_F = 0.55$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{D_{\text{new}}}{F} &= \dfrac{0.55 - 0.05}{0.95 - 0.05} = \dfrac{0.50}{0.90} = 0.5556 \\
D_{\text{new}} &= 0.5556 \times 100 = 55.56 \text{ kmol/h} \\
\Delta D &= 55.56 - 50 = +5.56 \text{ kmol/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

Cross-check using the feedforward law from §3.2:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta D &= \dfrac{F}{x_D - x_B} \cdot \Delta z_F = \dfrac{100}{0.95 - 0.05} \times 0.05 = \dfrac{100 \times 0.05}{0.90} = 5.56 \text{ kmol/h} \;\checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Practical control action (1 mark):** Open the distillate control valve by approximately $\dfrac{5.56}{50} \times 100 \approx 11.1\%$ of its operating stroke (or implement a feedforward trim on the reflux-to-distillate ratio controller).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Feed-Condition & Energy Focus**

**(a)** *With reference to the $q$-line on the McCabe–Thiele diagram, explain how a change in (i) feed composition $z_F$ and (ii) thermal condition $q$ affects the location of the feed plate. Support your answer with the $q$-line equation.* **[7 marks]**

**(b)** *A distillation column receives a saturated-liquid feed ($F = 80$ kmol/h, $z_F = 0.40$). The distillate must be $x_D = 0.92$ and the bottoms $x_B = 0.04$. The feed enters at $T_F = 80\,°C$, but the bubble-point of the feed is $T_{b,\text{sat}} = 95\,°C$. Average heat capacity $c_p = 90$ kJ/(kmol·K). If the latent heat is $\lambda = 32{,}000$ kJ/kmol, calculate:* **[7 marks]**

  **(i)** The required vapor boilup $V$ in the rectifying section (assume $R = 2.0$).
  **(ii)** The additional reboiler duty needed to preheat the subcooled feed to saturation.

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B**

**(a) — $q$-line Geometry and Feed-Plate Location [7 marks]**

The $q$-line equation is:

$$
y = \dfrac{q}{q-1} \, x - \dfrac{z_F}{q-1}
$$

**(i) Effect of $z_F$ (3 marks):** A change in $z_F$ shifts the $q$-line *horizontally* (translation). The intersection of the $q$-line with the rectifying operating line moves along that operating line, so the **optimal feed plate slides toward the top** if $z_F$ rises (richer feed → sharper cut → need more rectifying stages).

**(ii) Effect of $q$ (4 marks):** A change in $q$ rotates the $q$-line about the point $(z_F, z_F)$ on the $45°$ diagonal. As $q \to 1$ (cold liquid feed), the line becomes steeper, the intersection moves to a *lower* $x$-value, and the **feed plate migrates downward** into the stripping section. Conversely, $q \to 0$ (vapor feed) flattens the line and pushes the feed plate upward into the rectifying section.

> **Concluding remark (½ mark):** In practice, a column with multiple feed nozzles is used so the operator can switch the active feed plate to track the geometrically optimal position.

**(b)(i) — Rectifying-Section Vapor Flow [3 marks]**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= F \cdot \dfrac{z_F - x_B}{x_D - x_B} = 80 \times \dfrac{0.40 - 0.04}{0.92 - 0.04} = 80 \times \dfrac{0.36}{0.88} = 32.73 \text{ kmol/h} \\
L &= R \cdot D = 2.0 \times 32.73 = 65.45 \text{ kmol/h} \\
V &= L + D = 65.45 + 32.73 = 98.18 \text{ kmol/h}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b)(ii) — Feed Preheat Reboiler Duty [4 marks]**

The feed is subcooled by:

$$
\Delta T = T_{b,\text{sat}} - T_F = 95 - 80 = 15 \text{ K}
$$

Since $T_F < T_{b,\text{sat}}$, the feed acts like a *saturated liquid with extra subcooling*. The required extra sensible heat to bring it to bubble point:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{preheat}} &= F \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T \\
&= 80 \times 90 \times 15 = 1{,}08{,}000 \text{ kJ/h} = 30 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total reboiler duty** (latent + sensible):

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{R,\text{total}} &= \lambda \cdot \bar{V} + Q_{\text{preheat}} \\
\bar{V} &= V - (1-q) F = 98.18 - 0 = 98.18 \text{ kmol/h (saturated liquid feed, } q=1\text{)} \\
Q_{R,\text{total}} &= 32{,}000 \times 98.18 + 1{,}08{,}000 \\
&= 3.142 \times 10^{6} + 0.108 \times 10^{6} = 3.250 \times 10^{6} \text{ kJ/h} \\
&\approx 902.8 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls in Variable-Feed Problems**
> 1. **Forgetting the $qF$ and $(1-q)F$ terms** in the internal-flow derivations: students often write $\bar{V} = V$, which is correct *only* for $q=1$. This costs **2 full marks** in ESE.
> 2. **Mixing up $\bar{L}$ and $\bar{V}$:** remember — below the feed, $\bar{L} = L + qF$ (gets *bigger*) and $\bar{V} = V - (1-q)F$ (gets *smaller*).
> 3. **Using volumetric flow instead of molar flow** in $Q_R = \lambda \bar{V}$: always confirm $\lambda$ is in kJ/kmol and $\bar{V}$ in kmol/h, never m³/h.
> 4. **Ignoring the $\Delta T$ preheat term** when $T_F \neq T_{b,\text{sat}}$: it is worth **at least 1 mark** in any 14-mark question that mentions feed temperature.
> 5. **Drawing the $q$-line as the $45°$ line:** the $q$-line is *never* the diagonal (except for $q=1$, where it is vertical). Examiners deduct a mark for this geometric error.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ Variable column feed = controlling a distillation column when $F$, $z_F$, $T_F$, or $q$ changes.
- ✅ The **four feed-side degrees of freedom** are $F$, $z_F$, $T_F$ (or $q$), and feed pressure (assumed constant).
- ✅ **Recovery equation:** $\dfrac{D}{F} = \dfrac{z_F - x_B}{x_D - x_B}$ — the single most-tested formula in this module.
- ✅ **$q$-line equation:** $y = \dfrac{q}{q-1} x - \dfrac{z_F}{q-1}$ — geometry is key to feed-plate location.
- ✅ **Internal flows:** $V = L + D$; $\bar{V} = V - (1-q)F$; $\bar{L} = L + qF$.
- ✅ **Reboiler duty:** $Q_R = \lambda \bar{V} + F c_p (T_{b,\text{sat}} - T_F)$ (subcooled case).
- ✅ **LV configuration** is the most feed-conditions-invariant scheme; the standard "go-to" choice in ESE answers.
- ✅ **Feedforward control law:** $\Delta D = \dfrac{F}{x_D - x_B} \Delta z_F$ — the static trim; add a lead-lag for dynamics.
- ✅ Always **show the recovery-equation substitution step-by-step** for full marks; examiners reward the *method*, not just the number.
- ✅ A **two-product multi-component** column reduces (in the LV sense) to the same material-balance framework — only the light-key recovery matters.
- ✅ **Mermaid-rendered block diagrams** carry weight in design-type questions — use them to summarize FF + FB architecture.
- ✅ Industrial benchmarks: feed drift $\pm 5{-}20\%$, $z_F$ drift $\pm 2{-}10\%$, $R$ window $1.0{-}5.0$.
- ✅ **Examiner mantra:** "If you mention $q$ and $q$-line, you earn the geometry marks."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Feed Temperature
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT743 - INDUSTRIAL PROCESS CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Feed Temperature**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of Industrial Process Control, **Feed Temperature** ($T_i$ or $\theta_i$) is rigorously defined as the temperature of the input (inlet) process stream entering a process unit at any given instant of time. It is treated as one of the most important **load (disturbance) variables** in thermal process operations such as stirred-tank heaters, heat exchangers, reboilers, and chemical reactors.

Mathematically, in deviation variable form, it is expressed as:
$$T_i'(t) = T_i(t) - T_{i,s}$$

where $T_{i,s}$ is the nominal steady-state feed temperature and $T_i'(t)$ represents the perturbation from steady state.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU 2024 Scheme places Feed Temperature under the umbrella of *Process Variables & Their Dynamics* in Module 2. Students must clearly distinguish between **manipulated variables** (e.g., steam flow) and **load variables** (e.g., feed temperature, feed composition).

## 1.2 Real-World Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine a household **geyser (water heater)** connected to a cold-water pipeline. The temperature of the water entering the geyser (the *feed temperature*) is not constant — in winter it might be 15 °C, while in summer it could be 25 °C. Even if the heating element delivers exactly the same power:

- When the incoming water is **colder**, the geyser must work harder, and the outlet temperature drops momentarily before the thermostat compensates.
- When the incoming water is **warmer**, the outlet water becomes hotter for a short time.

This is precisely what happens in industrial thermal process units. The **feed temperature is an uncontrolled upstream disturbance** that the control system must reject to maintain the desired outlet (process) temperature. The same idea applies to a **preheat furnace** in a steel plant — if the steel slab entering is colder, more fuel is needed to achieve the target exit temperature.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Units & Constants:** Temperature is universally measured in **°C (Celsius)** or **K (Kelvin)**. Specific heat capacity of water is **$C_p = 4186 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$**, and of most organic liquids lies in the range **1500 – 3000 J/(kg·K)**. Density of water is **$\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$** at 4 °C.

## 1.3 Significance in Process Industries

Feed temperature is significant because:

- It is a **primary disturbance** in nearly every thermal separation / heating process.
- It directly influences the **steady-state energy demand** of the process.
- It determines the **dynamic response** of the outlet temperature to upstream fluctuations.
- It is the most common reason for the installation of **feedforward control** schemes.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Role of Feed Temperature as a Disturbance

In a typical controlled thermal process (e.g., a stirred-tank heater), three classes of variables are present:

| **Variable Class** | **Example** | **Controllability** |
|---|---|---|
| Output (Controlled Variable) | Outlet Temperature $T$ | Measured, controlled |
| Manipulated Variable | Steam flow rate $w_s$ | Adjusted by controller |
| **Load / Disturbance** | **Feed Temperature $T_i$** | **Uncontrolled upstream** |

A change in feed temperature constitutes a **load disturbance** that propagates through the process and perturbs the controlled variable. The control loop must counteract this perturbation by adjusting the manipulated variable (steam flow).

## 2.2 Transfer Function Modeling — Conceptual Flow

The general modeling procedure for feed temperature dynamics is:

1. **Write the unsteady-state mass balance** (for liquid systems, flow in = flow out).
2. **Write the unsteady-state energy balance** over the control volume.
3. **Linearize** the equation around the steady-state operating point.
4. **Convert to deviation variables** to eliminate constants.
5. **Apply the Laplace Transform** to obtain the transfer function.

## 2.3 The Two Standard Transfer Functions

For a perfectly mixed stirred-tank heater, with steam flow held **constant** (so any change in outlet temperature is purely due to feed temperature):

$$\frac{T'(s)}{T_i'(s)} = \frac{K_1}{\tau_1 s + 1}$$

When steam flow is the manipulated variable and feed temperature is held constant:

$$\frac{T'(s)}{W_s'(s)} = \frac{K_2}{\tau_2 s + 1}$$

By the **Principle of Superposition**, the total response is:

$$T'(s) = \frac{K_1}{\tau_1 s + 1}\,T_i'(s) + \frac{K_2}{\tau_2 s + 1}\,W_s'(s)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For a constant-holdup, constant-density stirred tank heater, both transfer functions share the same time constant:** $\tau_1 = \tau_2 = \tau_p = V/w$ (residence time). This is a high-yield KTU result.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Symbol** | **Formula** | **Units** |
|---:|:---:|:---|:---:|
| Process Time Constant | $\tau_p$ | $\tau_p = \dfrac{V}{w} = \dfrac{V \rho}{W}$ | s or min |
| Steady-State Gain (Feed Temp.) | $K_1$ | $K_1 = 1$ | dimensionless |
| Steady-State Gain (Steam Flow) | $K_2$ | $K_2 = \dfrac{1}{w \rho C_p}$ | $\dfrac{\text{K}}{\text{kg/s}}$ |
| Step Response (First Order) | $T'(t)$ | $T'(t) = K \cdot \Delta T_i \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_p}\right)$ | K or °C |
| 63.2 % Response Time | $t_{0.632}$ | $t_{0.632} = \tau_p$ | s or min |
| 95 % Response Time | $t_{0.95}$ | $t_{0.95} \approx 3\,\tau_p$ | s or min |
| 99.3 % Response Time | $t_{0.993}$ | $t_{0.993} \approx 5\,\tau_p$ | s or min |
| Energy Balance (Deviation Form) | — | $V \rho C_p \dfrac{dT'}{dt} = w \rho C_p (T_i' - T') + Q'$ | W |
| Residence Time | $\theta$ | $\theta = \dfrac{V}{w}$ | s |

> **Note on Notation:** The vertical bar symbol has been deliberately replaced with LaTeX `\vert`-style notation to preserve the markdown table structure. In handwritten exams, use plain `|x|`.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Disturbance Rejection Tuning:** Understanding feed temperature dynamics lets engineers tune PID controllers (especially Integral action) to reject such disturbances faster.
- **Feedforward Control Design:** $T_i$ is the most common **measured feedforward variable** — it is transmitted to a feedforward controller to take corrective action *before* the disturbance reaches the process outlet.
- **Energy Auditing:** Knowing how $T_i$ fluctuations affect $Q$ (steam demand) allows plants to size boilers and heat exchangers correctly.
- **Safety:** A sudden drop in feed temperature can cause thermal shock in reactors and glass-lined vessels; dynamic modeling predicts the severity.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Schematic of the System

We analyze a **continuously stirred tank heater (CSTH)** with the following assumptions:

- Constant liquid volume $V$ (perfect level control)
- Constant density $\rho$ and specific heat $C_p$
- Perfect mixing (uniform outlet temperature $T$)
- Volumetric flow rate $w$ is constant in = out
- Steam flow rate $w_s$ delivers heat $Q$ (caloric content $H_s$)
- Feed enters at temperature $T_i$, leaves at temperature $T$

## 3.2 Total Steady-State Energy Balance

At steady state, with no accumulation:

$$0 = w \rho C_p (T_{i,s} - T_s) + Q_s$$

Solving for the steady-state heat input:

$$Q_s = w \rho C_p (T_s - T_{i,s})$$

## 3.3 Unsteady-State (Dynamic) Energy Balance

Applying the first law of thermodynamics to the control volume:

$$V \rho C_p \frac{dT}{dt} = w \rho C_p (T_i - T) + Q$$

where $Q = w_s \cdot H_s$ is the heating rate provided by steam.

## 3.4 Linearization & Deviation Variables

Subtract the steady-state equation from the dynamic equation. Note that $V$, $\rho$, $C_p$, and $w$ are all constant, so they survive the subtraction unaffected:

$$V \rho C_p \frac{d(T - T_s)}{dt} = w \rho C_p (T_i - T_{i,s}) - w \rho C_p (T - T_s) + (Q - Q_s)$$

Define deviation variables:

$$T' = T - T_s, \quad T_i' = T_i - T_{i,s}, \quad Q' = Q - Q_s$$

The linearized dynamic energy balance becomes:

$$V \rho C_p \frac{dT'}{dt} = w \rho C_p (T_i' - T') + Q'$$

Rearranging into standard first-order ODE form:

$$V \rho C_p \frac{dT'}{dt} + w \rho C_p \, T' = w \rho C_p \, T_i' + Q'$$

## 3.5 Case 1 — Feed Temperature as the Only Input (Steam Held Constant)

If $Q' = 0$ (i.e., steam flow is unchanged), the equation reduces to:

$$V \rho C_p \frac{dT'}{dt} + w \rho C_p \, T' = w \rho C_p \, T_i'$$

Dividing throughout by $w \rho C_p$:

$$\frac{V}{w} \frac{dT'}{dt} + T' = T_i'$$

Taking the Laplace transform (zero initial conditions):

$$\tau_p \, s \, T'(s) + T'(s) = T_i'(s)$$

where $\tau_p = V/w$ is the **residence time** of the tank.

Therefore, the **transfer function relating outlet temperature to feed temperature** is:

$$\boxed{\;\frac{T'(s)}{T_i'(s)} = \frac{1}{\tau_p \, s + 1}\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Physical Insight:** The steady-state gain $K_1 = 1$ means that *every 1 °C change in feed temperature ultimately produces a 1 °C change in outlet temperature* at steady state (when heat input is held fixed). This is intuitively correct: in a perfectly mixed tank with constant heat, the outlet temperature must follow the inlet temperature with zero offset.

## 3.6 Case 2 — Steam Flow as the Only Input (Feed Temperature Held Constant)

If $T_i' = 0$, the dynamic equation becomes:

$$V \rho C_p \frac{dT'}{dt} + w \rho C_p \, T' = Q'$$

Substituting $Q' = H_s \, W_s'$ (steam flow deviation times latent heat):

$$\tau_p \frac{dT'}{dt} + T' = \frac{H_s}{w \rho C_p} \, W_s'$$

Taking the Laplace transform:

$$\boxed{\;\frac{T'(s)}{W_s'(s)} = \frac{K_2}{\tau_p \, s + 1}, \qquad K_2 = \frac{H_s}{w \rho C_p}\;}$$

## 3.7 Complete Two-Input Transfer Function

By linear superposition, the full dynamic model of the CSTH is:

$$T'(s) = \frac{1}{\tau_p s + 1}\,T_i'(s) + \frac{K_2}{\tau_p s + 1}\,W_s'(s)$$

## 3.8 Step Response to a Sudden Change in Feed Temperature

Let the feed temperature undergo a step change of magnitude $\Delta T_i$ at $t = 0$:

$$T_i'(s) = \frac{\Delta T_i}{s}$$

Substituting into the transfer function:

$$T'(s) = \frac{1}{\tau_p s + 1} \cdot \frac{\Delta T_i}{s} = \frac{\Delta T_i}{s(\tau_p s + 1)}$$

Performing partial fraction expansion:

$$\frac{1}{s(\tau_p s + 1)} = \frac{1}{s} - \frac{\tau_p}{\tau_p s + 1} = \frac{1}{s} - \frac{1}{s + 1/\tau_p}$$

Taking the inverse Laplace transform:

$$\boxed{\;T'(t) = \Delta T_i \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_p}\right)\;}$$

### Numerical Worked Example

**Given:**
- Volume: $V = 2 \text{ m}^3$
- Volumetric flow rate: $w = 0.5 \text{ m}^3/\text{min}$
- Initial outlet temperature: $T_s = 80 \text{ °C}$
- Initial feed temperature: $T_{i,s} = 30 \text{ °C}$
- Step change in feed temperature: $\Delta T_i = +10 \text{ °C}$ (feed suddenly enters at 40 °C)

**Step 1 — Compute the time constant:**

$$\tau_p = \frac{V}{w} = \frac{2 \text{ m}^3}{0.5 \text{ m}^3/\text{min}} = 4 \text{ min}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the step response equation:**

$$T'(t) = 10 \left(1 - e^{-t/4}\right) \text{ °C}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate at key time instants:**

| $t$ (min) | $e^{-t/4}$ | $T'(t)$ (°C) | $T(t)$ (°C) | % of Final |
|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| 0 | 1.000 | 0.00 | 80.00 | 0.0 % |
| 1 | 0.779 | 2.21 | 82.21 | 22.1 % |
| 2 | 0.607 | 3.93 | 83.93 | 39.3 % |
| 4 = $\tau_p$ | 0.368 | 6.32 | 86.32 | 63.2 % |
| 8 = $2\tau_p$ | 0.135 | 8.65 | 88.65 | 86.5 % |
| 12 = $3\tau_p$ | 0.050 | 9.50 | 89.50 | 95.0 % |
| 20 = $5\tau_p$ | 0.007 | 9.93 | 89.93 | 99.3 % |

**Step 4 — Steady-state outlet temperature:**

$$T_{\infty} = T_s + \Delta T_i = 80 + 10 = 90 \text{ °C}$$

This matches the physical expectation: with no controller action, the outlet temperature eventually rises by exactly 10 °C.

## 3.9 Time to Reach a Specified Outlet Temperature

Setting $T'(t) = \alpha \cdot \Delta T_i$ and solving for $t$:

$$t = -\tau_p \ln(1 - \alpha)$$

For example, to reach 90 % of the final change ($\alpha = 0.90$):

$$t_{0.90} = -\tau_p \ln(0.10) = 2.303 \, \tau_p$$

For our example: $t_{0.90} = 2.303 \times 4 = 9.21 \text{ min}$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Feed Temperature as a Disturbance in a Feedback Loop

The figure below shows the complete functional block diagram of a stirred-tank heater under feedback control, with **feed temperature acting as a load disturbance** that the controller must reject.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP[Set Point Tsp] --> SUM1[Summing Junction]
    T1[Temperature Transmitter TT] --> FB[Feedback Signal]
    FB --> SUM1
    SUM1 --> ERR[Error Signal e]
    ERR --> TC[Temperature Controller TC PID]
    TC --> CV[Control Valve on Steam Line]
    CV --> Q[Steam Heat Input Q]
    Q --> PROC[Stirred Tank Heater Process Gp1]
    TI[Feed Temperature Ti Disturbance] --> PROC
    WF[Feed Flow Rate w] --> PROC
    PROC --> TOUT[Outlet Temperature T]
    TOUT --> T1
    T1 -. Feedback .-> FB
    TI -. Disturbance Path .-> PROC
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Two-Input Transfer Function Representation

The same process may be redrawn with explicit disturbance and manipulated-variable paths, which is the format expected in KTU 14-mark derivations.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    TI[Feed Temperature Deviation Ti prime] --> GP1[Disturbance Transfer Function K1 over tau s plus 1]
    QS[Steam Flow Deviation Ws prime] --> GP2[Manipulated Transfer Function K2 over tau s plus 1]
    GP1 --> SUM2[Summing Junction]
    GP2 --> SUM2
    SUM2 --> TDEV[Outlet Temperature Deviation T prime]
    TDEV --> TACT[Actual Outlet Temperature T equals Ts plus T prime]
```

## 4.3 Feedforward Control — Compensating for Feed Temperature

To achieve faster disturbance rejection, modern plants use **feedforward control** based on the measured feed temperature.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TI[Feed Temperature Sensor on Inlet Line] --> FFC[Feedforward Controller FFC]
    FFC --> CVFF[Steam Control Valve]
    SP[Set Point] --> SUM1
    TOUTS[Outlet Temperature] --> SUM1
    SUM1 --> TC[Feedback Controller TC]
    TC --> CVFF
    CVFF --> PROC[Stirred Tank Heater]
    TI --> PROC
    PROC --> TOUTS
```

> **Engineering Note:** In feedforward control, the FFC transfer function is designed as $G_{ff} = -G_{d}/G_{p}$, where $G_d$ is the disturbance transfer function (feed temperature) and $G_p$ is the manipulated-variable transfer function (steam). For the CSTH: $G_{ff} = -1/K_2 = -w \rho C_p / H_s$.

## 4.4 Physical Schematic (As-Built in a Plant)

```
   ┌──────────────┐                           ┌──────────────┐
   │  Cold Feed   │                           │   Steam In   │
   │   Ti (°C)    │                           │   Ws (kg/s)  │
   └──────┬───────┘                           └──────┬───────┘
          │                                          │
          │  Pipe with Flowmeter                    Steam Valve
          │      & TT (Ti sensor)                       │
          ▼                                          ▼
   ╔══════════════════════════════════════════════════╗
   ║   STIRRED TANK HEATER  (Volume V, Density ρ)     ║
   ║   ────────────────────                           ║
   ║   Energy Balance:                                ║
   ║   VρCp dT/dt = wρCp(Ti − T) + Q                  ║
   ║                                                  ║
   ║   [Impeller ──► Perfect Mixing Assumed]          ║
   ╚══════════════════════════════════════════════════╝
          │ Outlet Temperature T (°C)
          │  (Sensor TT to Controller TC)
          ▼
   ──────────  To Downstream Process
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define feed temperature. Why is it classified as a load (disturbance) variable in process control?**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Feed temperature is the temperature of the input process stream entering a process unit such as a heat exchanger, reactor, or distillation column.
- **Disturbance classification (1 mark):** It is a load variable because it is an *uncontrolled, upstream* variable that enters the process from the environment and cannot be set by the control system.
- **Engineering impact (1 mark):** Variations in feed temperature directly disturb the controlled variable (e.g., outlet temperature), forcing the controller to take corrective action via the manipulated variable (e.g., steam flow).

---

### **Q2. For a perfectly mixed constant-holdup stirred tank heater, the steady-state gain relating outlet temperature to feed temperature is unity. Justify.**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Energy balance (1 mark):** At steady state, $Q_s = w \rho C_p (T_s - T_{i,s})$. When $T_i$ changes by $\Delta T_i$ and $Q$ is held constant, the only way to satisfy the steady-state balance is for $T$ to also change by the same amount $\Delta T_i$.
- **Transfer function (1 mark):** From the deviation energy balance, $\dfrac{T'(s)}{T_i'(s)} = \dfrac{1}{\tau_p s + 1}$, so the steady-state gain is $\lim_{s \to 0} \dfrac{1}{\tau_p s + 1} = 1$.
- **Physical interpretation (1 mark):** A perfectly mixed tank with constant heat input acts as a thermal "follower" — the outlet temperature tracks the inlet temperature with unity gain at steady state, regardless of the flow rate or volume.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### **Question A — Feed Temperature Dynamics of a Stirred Tank Heater**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2 — 14 Marks]**

A continuously stirred tank heater is used to heat a process liquid. The following data are given:

- Volume of liquid in tank: $V = 3.0 \text{ m}^3$
- Volumetric flow rate: $w = 1.0 \text{ m}^3/\text{min}$
- Density: $\rho = 950 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Specific heat: $C_p = 3000 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$
- Steady-state outlet temperature: $T_s = 75 \text{ °C}$
- Steady-state feed temperature: $T_{i,s} = 25 \text{ °C}$

#### **Part (a) — Derive the transfer function relating the deviation in outlet temperature to the deviation in feed temperature, assuming constant heat input. Find the time constant and the steady-state gain. (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Energy balance (2 marks):**
The unsteady-state energy balance for the tank is:
$$V \rho C_p \frac{dT}{dt} = w \rho C_p (T_i - T) + Q$$

**Step 2 — Subtract steady state (1 mark):**
Defining deviation variables $T' = T - T_s$, $T_i' = T_i - T_{i,s}$, $Q' = Q - Q_s$ and subtracting the steady-state equation:
$$V \rho C_p \frac{dT'}{dt} = w \rho C_p (T_i' - T') + Q'$$

**Step 3 — Set $Q' = 0$ and rearrange (1 mark):**
With steam flow held constant, $Q' = 0$:
$$V \rho C_p \frac{dT'}{dt} + w \rho C_p \, T' = w \rho C_p \, T_i'$$
$$\frac{V}{w} \frac{dT'}{dt} + T' = T_i'$$

**Step 4 — Laplace transform and time constant (2 marks):**
Taking the Laplace transform:
$$\left(\tau_p s + 1\right) T'(s) = T_i'(s) \quad \text{where } \tau_p = \frac{V}{w}$$
$$\boxed{\frac{T'(s)}{T_i'(s)} = \frac{1}{\tau_p s + 1}}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical values (1 mark):**
$$\tau_p = \frac{3.0}{1.0} = 3.0 \text{ min}, \quad K_1 = 1$$
The process is a first-order lag with unity gain and a time constant of 3 minutes.

#### **Part (b) — The feed temperature suddenly drops by 8 °C. Find the outlet temperature at $t = 3$ min, $t = 6$ min, and at steady state. (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Step response equation (2 marks):**
For a step change $\Delta T_i = -8 \text{ °C}$:
$$T'(t) = \Delta T_i \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_p}\right) = -8 \left(1 - e^{-t/3}\right) \text{ °C}$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate at $t = 3$ min (one time constant) (2 marks):**
$$T'(3) = -8 (1 - e^{-1}) = -8 (1 - 0.368) = -8 \times 0.632 = -5.06 \text{ °C}$$
$$T(3) = 75 - 5.06 = 69.94 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate at $t = 6$ min (two time constants) (1 mark):**
$$T'(6) = -8 (1 - e^{-2}) = -8 (1 - 0.135) = -8 \times 0.865 = -6.92 \text{ °C}$$
$$T(6) = 75 - 6.92 = 68.08 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 4 — Steady-state value (2 marks):**
$$T'(\infty) = -8 (1 - 0) = -8 \text{ °C}$$
$$T_{\infty} = 75 - 8 = 67 \text{ °C}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question A:**
> - Students frequently **forget to convert** volumetric flow rate $w$ (m³/min) into consistent SI units (m³/s) when the volume is given in m³. Marks are lost if $\tau_p$ is calculated with mixed time units.
> - Many students write the transfer function as $\dfrac{K_1}{\tau_p s - 1}$ (with a *minus* sign) — the correct sign is **plus** for a stable, non-oscillatory thermal process.
> - Do not include a $-1$ in the numerator: the gain is exactly $+1$, *not* $-1$.

---

### **Question B — Combined Feed-Temperature and Steam-Flow Response**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2 — 14 Marks]**

For the same stirred tank heater in Question A, suppose now that the heat input $Q$ is supplied by saturated steam with latent heat $H_s = 2200 \text{ kJ/kg}$, and the steam flow rate is $W_s$ (kg/s).

#### **Part (a) — Derive the complete two-input transfer function model relating $T'$ to $T_i'$ and $W_s'$. (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Energy balance with steam (2 marks):**
The dynamic energy balance is:
$$V \rho C_p \frac{dT}{dt} = w \rho C_p (T_i - T) + W_s H_s$$

**Step 2 — Steady-state equation (1 mark):**
$$0 = w \rho C_p (T_{i,s} - T_s) + W_{s,s} H_s$$

**Step 3 — Subtract and rearrange (2 marks):**
$$V \rho C_p \frac{dT'}{dt} + w \rho C_p T' = w \rho C_p T_i' + H_s W_s'$$
Dividing by $w \rho C_p$:
$$\tau_p \frac{dT'}{dt} + T' = T_i' + \frac{H_s}{w \rho C_p} W_s'$$

**Step 4 — Laplace transform and superposition (2 marks):**
$$\boxed{T'(s) = \frac{1}{\tau_p s + 1}\,T_i'(s) + \frac{K_2}{\tau_p s + 1}\,W_s'(s)}$$
where $\tau_p = V/w = 3$ min and
$$K_2 = \frac{H_s}{w \rho C_p} = \frac{2.2 \times 10^6}{1.0 \times 950 \times 3000} = 0.772 \text{ K/(kg/s)}$$

#### **Part (b) — If the feed temperature drops by 5 °C and simultaneously the steam flow decreases by 0.3 kg/s, find the steady-state change in outlet temperature. (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Steady-state gain (steam) (1 mark):**
At steady state, the process acts as a pure gain block:
$$T'(\infty) = (1) \cdot T_i'(\infty) + K_2 \cdot W_s'(\infty)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values (2 marks):**
$$T'(\infty) = (1)(-5) + (0.772)(-0.3)$$
$$T'(\infty) = -5 - 0.232 = -5.232 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 3 — Physical interpretation (2 marks):**
The feed-temperature disturbance dominates (causing 5 °C drop) while the steam-flow reduction contributes an additional 0.23 °C drop. Total steady-state drop in outlet temperature is approximately 5.23 °C.

**Step 4 — Final outlet temperature (2 marks):**
$$T_{\infty} = 75 - 5.23 = 69.77 \text{ °C}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question B:**
> - When the problem gives $H_s$ in kJ/kg, students often forget to convert to **J/kg** before computing $K_2$. Unit consistency is the single biggest marking trap in this question.
> - Some students attempt to **add the gains incorrectly** by trying to combine the two transfer functions into one — the correct method is the **Principle of Superposition**, treating each input independently.
> - Watch the sign conventions: a *drop* in feed temperature is *negative* $T_i'$, a *drop* in steam flow is *negative* $W_s'$ — both contribute to a *decrease* in outlet temperature.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Feed Temperature (Module 2)**

- **Definition:** Feed temperature $T_i$ is the temperature of the inlet process stream; it is a **load (disturbance) variable**, not a manipulated one.
- **Canonical Process:** The *stirred-tank heater* is the standard textbook example used by KTU for thermal-process modeling.
- **Key Assumption:** Perfect mixing → lumped-parameter model → **first-order ODE** → first-order transfer function.
- **Master Transfer Function (Feed Temperature):** $\dfrac{T'(s)}{T_i'(s)} = \dfrac{1}{\tau_p s + 1}$.
- **Master Transfer Function (Steam Flow):** $\dfrac{T'(s)}{W_s'(s)} = \dfrac{K_2}{\tau_p s + 1}$ with $K_2 = \dfrac{H_s}{w \rho C_p}$.
- **Shared Time Constant:** $\tau_p = V/w = V\rho/W$ (residence time of the tank).
- **Unity Gain Insight:** $K_1 = 1$ — a 1 °C feed-temperature step eventually produces a 1 °C outlet-temperature change when heat input is constant.
- **Step Response:** $T'(t) = \Delta T_i \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_p}\right)$ — 63.2 % at $t = \tau_p$, 95 % at $t = 3\tau_p$, 99.3 % at $t = 5\tau_p$.
- **Superposition Principle:** With multiple inputs, total response is the algebraic sum of individual responses.
- **Control Implication:** Feed temperature is the **#1 reason for installing feedforward control** in thermal processes.
- **Pitfall to Avoid:** Mixed SI units (m³ vs. m³/min, kJ vs. J) — always normalize *before* computing.
- **Pitfall to Avoid:** Sign errors in the transfer-function denominator (must be $\tau_p s + 1$, *not* $-1$).
- **Pitfall to Avoid:** Forgetting the linearization step — KTU explicitly tests whether students subtracted the steady-state equation.

> **End of Module 2 — Feed Temperature Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Boil-Up Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT743 - INDUSTRIAL PROCESS CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Boil-Up Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Boil-Up Control** is a fundamental regulatory control strategy employed in continuous distillation columns wherein the rate of vapor generation in the reboiler is dynamically manipulated to maintain a desired product specification, tray temperature profile, or composition profile on a designated sensitive tray. Mathematically, the boil-up rate $V_B$ (in $\text{kmol/hr}$ or $\text{kg/hr}$) is defined as the molar flow rate of vapor leaving the reboiler and entering the bottom-most tray of the column.

The reboiler duty $Q_R$ (in $\text{kW}$ or $\text{kJ/hr}$) is directly related to the boil-up rate through the latent heat of vaporization of the bottoms mixture:

$$Q_R = V_B \cdot \lambda_{bottoms}$$

where $\lambda_{bottoms}$ is the average latent heat of vaporization of the bottom product in $\text{kJ/kmol}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Boil-up control is classified under *Energy/Manipulated Variable Control Schemes* in distillation column instrumentation. It is one of the five canonical control degrees of freedom — the others being reflux flow, reflux ratio, distillate flow, and bottoms flow.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Smart Pressure Cooker" Concept

Imagine you are cooking biryani in a heavy-bottomed pressure cooker on an induction stove:

| Cooking Element | Distillation Equivalent | Control Function |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Burner heat input (stove knob) | Steam flow to reboiler | **Manipulated Variable (MV)** |
| Steam coming out of rice | Vapour rising through trays | **Boil-up rate ($V_B$)** |
| Aroma / doneness of rice layer | Tray temperature / composition | **Controlled Variable (CV)** |
| The pot's pressure | Column overhead pressure | **Disturbance** |
| Smart stove with temperature probe | Cascade controller (TC → FC) | **Boil-Up Controller** |

The chef (control engineer) wants the rice to be perfectly cooked (specification purity). Instead of guessing the flame, a smart stove uses a temperature probe (Temperature Controller) to continuously sense how the cooking is progressing and adjust the gas flow (Flow Controller on steam line) accordingly. This **two-loop hierarchy** is exactly what cascade boil-up control does in a real column.

> [!NOTE]
> **Energy Insight:** In a typical petrochemical distillation column, the reboiler consumes **60–70 %** of the total column energy. Hence, controlling boil-up efficiently is not just a product quality issue — it is a major **plant economy** and **sustainability** issue.

## 1.3 Standard Industrial Metrics

The following key performance indicators are universally tracked in KTU-aligned process control problems:

- **Boil-up ratio** $V_B / B$ (dimensionless) — usually between **1.2 and 2.5** for binary distillation.
- **Reflux-to-boil-up ratio** $L / V_B$ — defines the operating line slope.
- **Reboiler duty** $Q_R$ in $\text{MW}$ for large columns (typically **1–50 MW**).
- **Steam economy** $\text{kg distillate} / \text{kg steam}$ — benchmark **≈ 0.8–1.2**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physical Logic Chain

Boil-up control operates on a clear *cause → effect* chain that students must internalize for the KTU ESE:

1. **Cause (Manipulation):** The flow controller (FC) modulates the steam control valve position, altering steam mass flow $m_s$ entering the reboiler shell side.
2. **Energy Transfer:** Steam condenses, releasing its latent heat $\lambda_s$, transferring energy $Q_R = m_s \cdot \lambda_s$ to the process fluid in the tubes.
3. **Phase Change:** A fraction of the liquid bottoms $L_B$ is vaporized to produce vapour $V_B = Q_R / \lambda_{bottoms}$.
4. **Vapour Traffic:** Vapour $V_B$ ascends through the trays, contacting downcoming liquid $L$, effecting mass transfer.
5. **Effect (Measurement):** The temperature $T_i$ on a sensitive tray $i$ (or composition $x_i$) shifts in response.
6. **Feedback:** The temperature transmitter (TT) sends $T_i$ to the TC, completing the loop.

## 2.2 Why Boil-Up Control Is Preferred Over Direct Steam Manipulation

In KTU-style problem statements, examiners often ask: *"Why use a temperature controller to manipulate steam, instead of directly manipulating steam at a fixed rate?"* The answer lies in **disturbance rejection**:

- Feed composition changes $z_F$ shift the column profile.
- Feed temperature $T_F$ varies with ambient and upstream unit upsets.
- Ambient column pressure $P$ changes with weather/condenser fouling.
- Reflux subcooling changes with cooling water temperature.

A **direct steam valve** cannot reject these. A **cascade TC → FC** automatically re-positions the steam valve to maintain tray temperature despite these disturbances, because *tray temperature is a true indicator of internal composition*.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Symbol Meaning | Units | Typical KTU Use |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | $Q_R = V_B \cdot \lambda_{bottoms}$ | Reboiler duty = boil-up × latent heat | $\text{kJ/hr}$ | Energy balance |
| 2 | $Q_R = m_s \cdot \lambda_s$ | Steam-side energy balance | $\text{kJ/hr}$ | Sizing steam valve |
| 3 | $V_B / B$ | Boil-up ratio (bottom product) | dimensionless | Operating point |
| 4 | $L / V_B$ | Reflux-to-boil-up ratio (slope of rectifying op. line) | dimensionless | McCabe–Thiele |
| 5 | $y_{n+1} = \frac{L}{V_B} x_n + \frac{D \cdot x_D}{V_B}$ | Rectifying section operating line | — | Tray-by-tray calc |
| 6 | $T_{tray} \approx T_{bub}(x_i, P)$ | Tray temp ≈ bubble point of liquid at tray composition & pressure | $\text{°C}$ | TC selection |
| 7 | $\Delta T_{corrected} = T_{tray} - T_{ref}(P_{ref})$ | Pressure-compensated temperature | $\text{°C}$ | Eliminate $P$-disturbance |
| 8 | $\text{CT} = V_B \cdot \lambda_{bottoms} / (m_s \cdot \lambda_s)$ | Condensate test / energy ratio | — | Validation |
| 9 | $G_{TC}(s) = K_{TC} \left(1 + \frac{1}{\tau_I s} + \tau_D s\right)$ | PID controller transfer function | — | Tuning |
| 10 | $G_{loop}(s) = \frac{G_c G_v G_p}{1 + G_c G_v G_p}$ | Closed-loop transfer function | — | Stability analysis |

> [!NOTE]
> Symbols used: $L$ = liquid flow, $V_B$ = boil-up vapour, $B$ = bottoms flow, $D$ = distillate, $x_D$ = distillate composition, $y_{n+1}$ = vapour from tray below, $x_n$ = liquid on tray $n$, $K_{TC}$ = controller gain, $\tau_I$ = integral time, $\tau_D$ = derivative time, $G_c, G_v, G_p$ = controller, valve, process transfer functions.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility Beyond the Exam

- **Ethylene Plants:** Boil-up control on the demethanizer and deethanizer determines polymer-grade ethylene purity.
- **Petroleum Refining:** Atmospheric and vacuum crude distillation columns rely on boil-up to control diesel and VGO cuts.
- **Pharmaceuticals:** High-purity solvent recovery columns (methanol, ethanol) use boil-up control for GMP-grade output.
- **Cryogenic Air Separation:** Boil-up in the low-pressure column of an ASU directly sets argon and oxygen purity.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Steam Flow Required for a Given Boil-Up

This is the **most frequently asked 7-mark derivation** in KTU ESE Module 2 questions. We will derive the steam flow $m_s$ needed in the reboiler to sustain a boil-up rate $V_B$.

### Step 1 — Energy Released by Steam Condensation
Steam at saturation condenses on the shell side, releasing its latent heat. The energy transfer rate is:

$$\dot{Q}_{steam} = m_s \cdot \lambda_s$$

where $m_s$ is the mass flow rate of steam ($\text{kg/hr}$) and $\lambda_s$ is the latent heat of vaporization of steam ($\text{kJ/kg}$).

### Step 2 — Energy Absorbed by Boil-Up
On the tube side, a fraction of the bottom liquid is vaporized. The energy absorbed equals the vapor mass flow times its latent heat:

$$\dot{Q}_{process} = m_{vapour} \cdot \lambda_{b}$$

Here, the molar boil-up rate is $V_B$ ($\text{kmol/hr}$) and the molar mass of bottoms is $M_B$ ($\text{kg/kmol}$), so:

$$m_{vapour} = V_B \cdot M_B$$

Substituting:

$$\dot{Q}_{process} = V_B \cdot M_B \cdot \lambda_{b}$$

### Step 3 — Steady-State Energy Balance
At steady state, neglecting sensible heat changes (a standard KTU simplification for short-time dynamics):

$$\dot{Q}_{steam} = \dot{Q}_{process} + Q_{loss}$$

Assuming negligible heat loss $Q_{loss} \approx 0$ for an insulated reboiler:

$$m_s \cdot \lambda_s = V_B \cdot M_B \cdot \lambda_{b}$$

### Step 4 — Solve for Steam Flow

$$m_s = \frac{V_B \cdot M_B \cdot \lambda_{b}}{\lambda_s}$$

> **Sanity check:** With $V_B = 100 \, \text{kmol/hr}$, $M_B = 80 \, \text{kg/kmol}$, $\lambda_b = 360 \, \text{kJ/kg}$, $\lambda_s = 2200 \, \text{kJ/kg}$:

$$m_s = \frac{100 \times 80 \times 360}{2200} = \frac{2\,880\,000}{2200} = 1309.09 \, \text{kg/hr}$$

This is the required saturated steam mass flow.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cascade Boil-Up Control Transfer Function

We model the cascade TC–FC structure shown later in SECTION_4. The block diagram yields two nested loops.

### Inner (Secondary) Loop — Flow Control
Let:
- $G_v(s) = \frac{K_v}{\tau_v s + 1}$ — steam valve
- $G_f(s) = \frac{K_f}{\tau_f s + 1}$ — flow sensor and transmitter

The inner closed-loop transfer function from setpoint to actual flow is:

$$G_{inner}(s) = \frac{G_c^{FC}(s) \cdot G_v(s) \cdot G_f(s)}{1 + G_c^{FC}(s) \cdot G_v(s) \cdot G_f(s)}$$

If $G_c^{FC}(s) = K_{c,f}$ (pure P-control, typical for flow loops) and we make the standard KTU assumption that the inner loop is much faster than the outer loop, then $G_{inner}(s) \approx 1$ for frequencies of interest in the outer loop. This is the **Inner-Loop Bandwidth Justification**.

### Outer (Primary) Loop — Temperature Control
The temperature process model (tray $T$ to reboiler heat input) is approximately first-order with dead time:

$$G_p(s) = \frac{K_p \, e^{-\theta s}}{\tau_p s + 1}$$

where $\theta$ is the transport delay (typically 1–5 minutes for tray dynamics).

The overall open-loop transfer function for the outer TC is:

$$G_{OL}(s) = G_c^{TC}(s) \cdot G_{inner}(s) \cdot G_p(s)$$

For a PI temperature controller $G_c^{TC}(s) = K_{c,T}\left(1 + \frac{1}{\tau_{I,T} s}\right)$:

$$G_{OL}(s) = K_{c,T}\left(1 + \frac{1}{\tau_{I,T} s}\right) \cdot \frac{K_p \, e^{-\theta s}}{\tau_p s + 1}$$

### Tuning Guidance (Cohen–Coon / ITAE KTU-Standard)
Using the **ITAE criterion** for setpoint tracking on a FOPTD model with $\theta / \tau_p \in [0.1, 1.0]$, the recommended PI settings are:

$$K_{c,T} = \frac{0.586}{K_p} \left(\frac{\theta}{\tau_p}\right)^{-0.916}$$

$$\tau_{I,T} = \frac{\tau_p}{1.03 - 0.165 \left(\frac{\theta}{\tau_p}\right)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked to "design a boil-up controller", you must (1) justify the cascade structure, (2) state the ITAE/IMC tuning values, and (3) comment on the inner-loop being at least 3–5× faster than the outer loop.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Pressure-Compensated Temperature Setpoint

Tray temperature is affected by both composition $x$ *and* pressure $P$. To decouple them, define a **pressure-compensated temperature**:

$$T_{PC} = T_{tray} - T_{sat}(P_{ref}) \cdot \left(\frac{P}{P_{ref}}\right)^{\alpha}$$

where $\alpha \approx 0.07$ for hydrocarbon systems (a thermodynamic correction factor). For binary mixture at tray $i$ with light key mole fraction $x_i$:

$$T_{tray} \approx T_{bub}(x_i, P)$$

A change in $P$ alone shifts $T_{tray}$ by approximately $\frac{\partial T_{bub}}{\partial P}$, biasing the TC. The compensation $T_{PC}$ removes this bias so that the controller truly "sees" only composition changes.

The corrected error sent to the TC is:

$$e_{TC}(s) = T_{sp,PC}(s) - T_{PC}(s)$$

This is implemented in modern DCS systems as a calculation block fed by both $T$ and $P$ transmitters.

## 3.4 Summary of Analytical Matrix

| Control Layer | Sensor | Manipulated Variable | Tuning Type | Time Constant |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Inner (FC) | Orifice / vortex flowmeter | Steam control valve | P-only, high gain | **5–15 s** |
| Outer (TC) | RTD / thermocouple on tray $i$ | Setpoint of FC | PI or PID | **2–10 min** |
| Override (LC) | Differential pressure / level | Bottoms pump speed | PI | **1–3 min** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cascade Boil-Up Control Architecture

The Mermaid diagram below depicts the **canonical cascade temperature-flow control structure** used for boil-up regulation in a KTU-exam-style distillation column. Both control loops are clearly indicated, along with the major physical components and signal paths.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP["Temperature Setpoint SP_T"]
    TC["Temperature Controller TC"]
    OUTTC["TC Output 4 to 20 mA"]
    FCSP["Flow Setpoint SP_F"]
    FC["Flow Controller FC"]
    VALVE["Steam Control Valve FCV"]
    STEAM["Steam Supply Line"]
    REBOIL["Reboiler Heat Exchanger"]
    VAP["Boilup Vapour V_B"]
    COL["Distillation Column Trays"]
    TRAYT["Sensitive Tray Temperature TT"]
    TRAYP["Column Pressure Transmitter PT"]
    COMP["Pressure Compensation Block"]
    TTFB["Temperature Feedback 4 to 20 mA"]
    FBFC["Steam Flow Feedback 4 to 20 mA"]
    SP --> TC
    TC --> OUTTC
    OUTTC --> FCSP
    FCSP --> FC
    FC --> VALVE
    VALVE --> STEAM
    STEAM --> REBOIL
    REBOIL --> VAP
    VAP --> COL
    COL --> TRAYT
    COL --> TRAYP
    TRAYP --> COMP
    TRAYT --> COMP
    COMP --> TTFB
    TTFB --> TC
    VALVE --> FBFC
    FBFC --> FC
```

## 4.2 Interpretation of the Diagram

- **Outer Loop (Slow, Quality Loop):** TT measures tray temperature → TC compares with SP_T → TC outputs a new flow setpoint to the FC. This loop directly controls product quality / composition.
- **Inner Loop (Fast, Hardware Loop):** FC compares the setpoint with the actual steam flow from FT → FC adjusts the pneumatic / electric steam valve FCV. This loop protects against steam pressure disturbances.
- **Compensation Path:** The PT (pressure transmitter) and TT signals are mathematically combined in the **Pressure Compensation Block** before reaching the TC. This eliminates pressure-induced temperature bias.

## 4.3 Physical Component Mapping (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

| Block in Diagram | Real Equipment | Standard Tag in P&ID |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Temperature Setpoint | Operator entry in DCS | SP on HMI |
| TC | PID function block in DCS | TIC-100 |
| FC | PID function block in DCS | FIC-101 |
| Steam Control Valve | Pneumatic globe valve w/ positioner | FCV-101 |
| Reboiler | Kettle or thermosyphon reboiler | E-101 |
| TT | RTD Pt100 in thermowell | TT-100 |
| FT | Orifice plate + DPT, or vortex meter | FE-101 / FT-101 |
| PT | Capacitance or piezoresistive sensor | PT-100 |
| Compensation Block | DCS math block (CTBR or custom) | TY-100 |

> [!NOTE]
> The architecture above is universally accepted in **ISA-5.1** tag conventions, which is the standard referenced in KTU Process Control exam questions on P&ID interpretation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`
**Define boil-up in the context of distillation column control. Why is it considered a critical manipulated variable?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Boil-up is the rate at which liquid in the reboiler is vaporized to generate the upward vapour stream in a distillation column, denoted $V_B$ in $\text{kmol/hr}$. It is critical because (i) it directly determines the vapour-liquid traffic that drives mass transfer on the trays, (ii) it consumes 60–70 % of the column's total energy, and (iii) manipulating boil-up is the most energy-efficient way to control bottoms purity and the rectifying-section composition profile. **(3 marks)**

### Question A2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`
**Differentiate between direct steam manipulation and cascade TC–FC boil-up control.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Direct steam manipulation** uses only a single controller (often a hand valve or simple FC) on the steam line. It cannot reject disturbances in feed temperature, ambient pressure, or steam header pressure. (1 mark)
- **Cascade TC–FC** uses an inner flow loop (fast, FC) and an outer temperature loop (slower, TC). The TC adjusts the FC's setpoint, so any steam-supply disturbance is corrected by the FC before it affects tray temperature. (2 marks)
- The cascade structure provides faster disturbance rejection, decouples the steam header pressure disturbance, and yields more stable tray temperature and product purity.

## 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question B-A. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]`

**(a)** Draw and explain the cascade control configuration used for boil-up rate control in a distillation column. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** A distillation column has a bottom product flow $B = 50 \, \text{kmol/hr}$ and a desired boil-up ratio $V_B / B = 1.8$. The molar mass of the bottoms is $M_B = 78 \, \text{kg/kmol}$ and its latent heat of vaporization is $\lambda_b = 380 \, \text{kJ/kg}$. Saturated steam at $\lambda_s = 2100 \, \text{kJ/kg}$ is used. Calculate (i) the boil-up rate, (ii) the reboiler duty, and (iii) the steam mass flow rate required. Assume 5 % heat loss. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Cascade Boil-Up Control — Explanation (7 marks)**

- **Diagram & tag identification (2 marks):** P&ID showing TT → TIC → FIC → FCV → Reboiler → column → TT feedback. List the major blocks.
- **Outer (master) loop: TC** (2 marks) — measures tray temperature, compares with setpoint, outputs a flow setpoint. PI/PID control. Slower (time constant 2–10 min).
- **Inner (slave) loop: FC** (2 marks) — measures steam flow, manipulates steam valve. P or PI control. Faster (5–15 s).
- **Justification (1 mark):** Inner loop rejects steam header pressure disturbances; outer loop controls product quality; cascade is robust against multiple disturbances.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**(i) Boil-up rate** $V_B$:

$$V_B = 1.8 \times B = 1.8 \times 50 = 90 \, \text{kmol/hr} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

**(ii) Reboiler duty** $Q_R$:

$$m_{vapour} = V_B \cdot M_B = 90 \times 78 = 7020 \, \text{kg/hr} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

$$Q_R = m_{vapour} \cdot \lambda_b = 7020 \times 380 = 2\,667\,600 \, \text{kJ/hr} = 740.6 \, \text{kW} \quad \textbf{(1.5 marks)}$$

**(iii) Steam flow rate** $m_s$ (with 5 % heat loss, i.e. efficiency $\eta = 0.95$):

$$\text{Required steam energy} = \frac{Q_R}{\eta} = \frac{2\,667\,600}{0.95} = 2\,808\,000 \, \text{kJ/hr} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

$$m_s = \frac{2\,808\,000}{\lambda_s} = \frac{2\,808\,000}{2100} = 1337.14 \, \text{kg/hr} \quad \textbf{(1.5 marks)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Valuation Pitfall — 3 Most Common Mistakes:**
> 1. Forgetting to convert kmol to kg using $M_B$ when computing $\dot{Q}_{process}$ (loses 1–2 marks).
> 2. Ignoring the 5 % heat loss, i.e. using $Q_R$ instead of $Q_R / \eta$ when computing $m_s$ (loses 1 mark).
> 3. Failing to clearly draw the cascade block diagram with feedback arrows — examiners allocate dedicated marks for the diagram, not just the description.

---

### Question B-B. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2 — Alternative Choice]`

**(a)** Discuss the problem of **inverse response** in reboiler-based boil-up control. With a neat sketch of the reboiler dynamics, explain why it occurs and how a cascade controller mitigates it. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Explain the concept of **pressure-compensated temperature control** of a distillation tray. Derive the expression for pressure-corrected temperature and state two industrial benefits. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Inverse Response in Reboiler Dynamics (7 marks)**

- **Definition (1 mark):** Inverse response is a non-minimum phase behavior where the controlled variable (e.g. tray temperature) initially moves in the *opposite* direction to its final steady-state value after a step change in manipulated variable (steam flow).
- **Physical cause — subcooled liquid effect (3 marks):** When steam flow suddenly increases, the metal tube wall heats up first, vaporizing the thin liquid film on the inner tube surface rapidly. This sudden evaporation **expands** the liquid–vapour mixture at the bottom of the column, pushing the tray above *up* and causing an initial *drop* in tray temperature (because colder liquid is pushed onto the tray). Only after this transient subsides does the higher steady-state boil-up increase tray temperature.
- **Mollier/sketch justification (1.5 marks):** Two parallel first-order paths with opposite gains — a fast path (negative gain, tube-wall vaporization) and a slow path (positive gain, bulk liquid heating) — superimpose to give the characteristic initial-dip response.
- **Cascade mitigation (1.5 marks):** The inner FC keeps steam flow tightly regulated at its setpoint, reducing the magnitude of the step into the reboiler, thereby smoothing the inverse-response bump. Additionally, the TC's derivative action $K_d$ can be used to "anticipate" the initial dip.

**(b) Pressure-Compensated Temperature Control (7 marks)**

- **Motivation (1.5 marks):** Tray temperature $T_{tray}$ depends on both composition $x_i$ (signal of interest) and column pressure $P$ (a disturbance). A change in $P$ of 10 kPa can shift $T_{tray}$ by 1–3 °C, masquerading as a composition change.
- **Derivation (3.5 marks):** The bubble-point relation gives $T_{tray} = T_{bub}(x_i, P)$. Taylor expansion around the reference pressure $P_{ref}$:

$$T_{tray} \approx T_{bub}(x_i, P_{ref}) + \left.\frac{\partial T_{bub}}{\partial P}\right|_{x_i} (P - P_{ref})$$

Define the pressure-corrected temperature as the value the tray *would* have at $P_{ref}$:

$$T_{PC} = T_{tray} - \left.\frac{\partial T_{bub}}{\partial P}\right|_{x_i} (P - P_{ref})$$

Empirically, for hydrocarbon systems, $\left.\frac{\partial T_{bub}}{\partial P}\right|_{x_i} \approx \alpha \cdot T_{bub} / P_{ref}$ with $\alpha \approx 0.07$, leading to the engineering approximation used in SECTION 3.3:

$$T_{PC} \approx T_{tray} - T_{bub}(x_i, P_{ref}) \cdot \left[\left(\frac{P}{P_{ref}}\right)^{\alpha} - 1\right]$$

- **Two industrial benefits (2 marks):**
  1. Eliminates false alarms on composition excursions caused by barometric pressure changes.
  2. Allows the same controller tuning to remain valid across day–night pressure cycles, reducing operator interventions.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Valuation Pitfall — Common Errors in Question B-B:**
> 1. Stating "inverse response occurs due to nonlinearity" — examiners expect the *two-path physical mechanism* (tube-wall vaporization vs. bulk liquid heating). Generic answers lose 2–3 marks.
> 2. Confusing *pressure-compensated temperature* with *differential temperature* (between two trays). The latter measures *composition gradient*, not pressure effects. Misconception loses 2 marks.
> 3. Skipping the partial derivative in the derivation and jumping to the final formula — full marks require the Taylor expansion step.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Boil-up rate** $V_B$ is the molar vapour flow leaving the reboiler — it is the single most important energy-side manipulated variable in distillation.
- **Standard configuration:** Cascade TC (outer, slow) → FC (inner, fast) → steam control valve. Always draw the block diagram with two feedback arrows.
- **Energy balance** $m_s \cdot \lambda_s = V_B \cdot M_B \cdot \lambda_b$ is the foundation of every numerical question — memorize it.
- **Inner loop bandwidth** must be at least **3–5× faster** than the outer loop. This is the *justification* for using cascade rather than single-loop.
- **Pressure compensation** removes the false-temperature signal from ambient / condenser pressure swings. Use $T_{PC} = T_{tray} - T_{ref} \cdot \left[(P/P_{ref})^{\alpha} - 1\right]$.
- **Inverse response** arises from the dual-path mechanism in the reboiler; cascade control + derivative action mitigate it.
- **ITAE tuning** for the outer TC: $K_c = 0.586 / K_p \cdot (\theta/\tau_p)^{-0.916}$; $\tau_I = \tau_p / (1.03 - 0.165 (\theta/\tau_p))$.
- **Energy economics:** Reboiler = 60–70 % of column energy → efficient boil-up control directly cuts plant operating cost.
- **Tag conventions:** Use **TIC** (temperature indicating controller), **FIC** (flow indicating controller), **FCV** (flow control valve), **TT/FT/PT** (transmitters).
- **Sensitive tray selection** (Stage 4 in KTU syllabus): place the TC sensor on the tray where temperature is most sensitive to the manipulated variable — usually 4–8 trays above the feed for high-purity columns.
- **Exam technique:** Always state (a) the loop pairings, (b) the tuning type, (c) the rationale, and (d) any compensation/override logic. KTU evaluators mark these four items explicitly.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reflux Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT743 - INDUSTRIAL PROCESS CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Reflux Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reflux** is the portion of condensed overhead vapor from a distillation column that is returned to the top of the column to enhance the separation of components by providing liquid-vapor contact on the trays or packing.

In the context of **Industrial Process Control (PEICT743)**, *reflux control* refers to the set of instrumentation, control loops, and control strategies employed to regulate the flow rate of this returned condensate. Reflux is one of the **two primary manipulated variables** (the other being reboiler steam/vapor flow) used to control the **distillate composition** and to maintain the column's internal material/energy balance.

Mathematically, the reflux ratio is expressed as:

$$R = \frac{L}{D}$$

where:
- $R$ = **Reflux Ratio** (dimensionless)
- $L$ = Reflux flow rate returned to the column (kmol/h or kg/h)
- $D$ = Distillate product flow rate withdrawn (kmol/h or kg/h)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The course explicitly requires the study of "Reflux Control" as a sub-topic of distillation column control under Module 2 (Control of Multi-variable & Multi-loop Processes). Students are expected to know the four classical reflux control schemes by name, configuration, and selection criteria.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are brewing a strong cup of coffee using a pour-over method. The hot water (vapor equivalent) rises through the ground coffee and collects as a weak, watery coffee at the bottom (distillate equivalent). To make the coffee stronger, you would *re-pour* some of that weak coffee back over the grounds to extract more flavor. This re-poured weak coffee is the **reflux**.

Now, the speed and amount at which you re-pour determines how strong the final coffee will be. If you pour too fast (high reflux), the column gets "flooded" and the operation becomes inefficient. If you pour too slowly (low reflux), the coffee (distillate) will be too weak. **Reflux control is essentially the "pouring rate controller" of a distillation column.**

> [!TIP]
> Think of the reflux pump and its control valve as a **"liquid thermostat"** for the column — it keeps the top of the column in the optimal operating "sweet spot" to deliver a product of the desired purity.

### 1.3 Key Industrial Metrics

The following metrics are critical when discussing reflux control:

- **Reflux Ratio (R)**: A dimensionless number typically ranging from **0.5 to 25** depending on relative volatility and product specification.
- **Internal Reflux**: The actual liquid flowing down the top tray, which is **always greater than or equal to the external (measured) reflux** due to vapor condensation in the top section.
- **External Reflux**: The condensed liquid measured and controlled by the flow transmitter on the reflux line.
- **Reflux Drum**: A horizontal vessel that stores the condensed overhead vapor, providing surge capacity and allowing vapor-liquid equilibrium (VLE) at a sub-cooled condition.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Material Balance Foundation of Reflux Control

The top of a distillation column is governed by three streams:

1. **Vapor leaving the top tray** ($V_{N}$), which enters the condenser.
2. **Reflux liquid** ($L$) returning to the column.
3. **Distillate product** ($D$) leaving the system.

The **overall material balance** at the top of the column is:

$$V_{N} = L + D$$

The **component material balance** for the more volatile component is:

$$V_{N} \cdot y_{N} = L \cdot x_{N+1} + D \cdot x_{D}$$

where $y_N$ and $x_D$ are vapor and liquid compositions respectively.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for control:** The composition of the distillate ($x_D$) is sensitive to the ratio $L/D$, not to $L$ alone. This is why most practical reflux control schemes use a **flow-ratio controller** (often called the "reflux ratio controller") rather than just a simple flow controller on the reflux line.

### 2.2 Why Reflux is the Preferred Manipulated Variable

For controlling distillate composition, an engineer can manipulate any of three streams:
- Distillate flow ($D$)
- Reflux flow ($L$)
- Reboiler steam/vapor flow

Reflux is preferred over distillate flow for the following reasons:
- **Indirect control** of $D$ keeps the inventory in the reflux drum stable.
- Reflux is a **fast-acting** variable compared to reboiler steam (which has large thermal lag).
- The **internal liquid dynamics** are directly affected by reflux, which in turn influences tray hydraulics and Murphree efficiency.

### 2.3 The Four Standard Reflux Control Schemes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Topic:** The four classical schemes below appear frequently in KTU University Examinations.

#### **Scheme 1: Fixed Reflux (Constant L) — "Open Loop" Reflux**

- Reflux flow is held constant by a **flow controller (FC)** with a fixed setpoint.
- Distillate flow $D$ is allowed to float.
- **Use Case:** Rare in practice; used in laboratory columns or when the feed is very stable.

#### **Scheme 2: Constant Reflux Ratio (L/D Ratio Control)**

- A ratio relay or computed setpoint adjusts $L$ as a function of $D$.
- Implementation: $L_{SP} = k \cdot D$, where $k$ is the desired reflux ratio.
- **Use Case:** Most common scheme; preserves composition during feed disturbances.

#### **Scheme 3: Reflux as a Manipulated Variable (Composition Control via Reflux)**

- A composition controller (AC) measures distillate composition via a chromatograph or analyzer.
- The AC output resets the setpoint of the reflux flow controller (FC).
- This is a **cascade control** structure: AC → FC → Control Valve → Reflux Flow.
- **Use Case:** Tighter composition control; high-purity columns.

#### **Scheme 4: Internal Reflux Control**

- Uses a **temperature sensor in the top tray** or a differential pressure sensor across the top section to estimate internal reflux.
- The control loop compensates for the difference between external and internal reflux caused by sub-cooling in the overhead system.
- **Use Case:** Columns with significant sub-cooled reflux or where ambient heat losses are non-negligible.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Symbol** | **Equation** | **Units** | **Remarks** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Reflux Ratio | $R$ | $R = L / D$ | dimensionless | Most important controlled ratio |
| Total Material Balance (Top) | — | $V_N = L + D$ | kmol/h | Steady-state assumption |
| Component Balance (Top) | — | $V_N y_N = L x_{N+1} + D x_D$ | kmol/h | Used for tray-by-tray design |
| Internal Reflux | $L_{int}$ | $L_{int} = L \left[1 + \frac{C_p \Delta T}{\lambda}\right]$ | kmol/h | $\lambda$ = latent heat of vaporization |
| Condenser Heat Duty | $Q_C$ | $Q_C = V_N \cdot \lambda$ | kW | Determines cooling water demand |
| Sub-cooling Correction | $\Delta T$ | $T_{dew} - T_{reflux\ drum}$ | °C or K | For internal reflux estimation |
| Slope of $y$-$x$ curve at pinch | $m$ | $dy/dx$ at operating point | dimensionless | Determines controllability |
| Relative Volatility | $\alpha$ | $\alpha = (K_A / K_B)$ | dimensionless | Feed property |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown LaTeX Note:** In the table above, all absolute value bars and division slashes have been rendered as inline LaTeX expressions to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Reflux control is the cornerstone of **petrochemical, refinery, and pharmaceutical** process operations. It is encountered in:
- **Crude oil atmospheric and vacuum distillation units (CDU/VDU)**
- **Aromatics plants** (benzene-toluene-xylene separation)
- **Ethylene plants** (demethanizer, deethanizer, C2 splitter)
- **Ethanol-water** separation columns
- **Air separation units (ASUs)** for cryogenic oxygen/nitrogen/argon production

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Insight:** A poorly tuned reflux loop can cause **"weeping," "flooding," or "dumping"** on the top trays, leading to off-spec product and unscheduled shutdowns. Reflux control failure is among the top three causes of column trips in refinery operations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The Internal Reflux Equation

The derivation of the internal reflux equation is one of the most analytically important derivations in this module. It is a **favourite of KTU board examiners** and tests the student's understanding of energy balances in the overhead system.

**Problem Setup:**
A condenser operating at $T_{dew}$ produces a saturated liquid. The reflux drum operates at temperature $T_{reflux}$. The condensate may be sub-cooled by an amount:

$$\Delta T_{sub} = T_{dew} - T_{reflux}$$

This sub-cooling occurs because the vapor does extra work condensing and then cooling the liquid below its bubble point before it is pumped back to the column.

**Step 1: Apply an Energy Balance Around the Reflux Drum**

The hot vapor entering the drum carries an enthalpy equal to its latent heat of vaporization $\lambda$. The sub-cooled reflux liquid leaving the drum carries enthalpy $C_p \cdot T_{reflux}$ (per unit mass), and the "extra" heat that was removed corresponds to the sub-cooling load.

**Step 2: Equate the Sensible Heat Gain to the Sub-cooling Load**

Per unit mass of reflux:

$$L_{int} = L \left[ 1 + \frac{C_p \cdot (T_{dew} - T_{reflux})}{\lambda} \right]$$

**Step 3: Rewrite Using the Sub-cooling Temperature**

$$\Delta T_{sub} = T_{dew} - T_{reflux}$$

Therefore:

$$L_{int} = L \left[ 1 + \frac{C_p \cdot \Delta T_{sub}}{\lambda} \right]$$

**Step 4: Expand and Simplify**

The "extra" internal reflux generated by sub-cooling is:

$$L_{int} - L = L \cdot \frac{C_p \cdot \Delta T_{sub}}{\lambda}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Interpretation:** When the reflux is sub-cooled, the liquid reaching the top tray is denser and colder than the saturated liquid. As it absorbs heat from the rising vapor on the top tray, *additional* vapor condenses. This extra condensation manifests as **additional internal reflux** that the external flowmeter cannot "see."

**Step 5: Numerical Example**

Given:
- External reflux $L = 100$ kmol/h
- $C_p = 2.5$ kJ/(kg·K) (approx. for light hydrocarbons)
- $\lambda = 350$ kJ/kg
- $\Delta T_{sub} = 5$ K

Then:

$$L_{int} = 100 \left[ 1 + \frac{2.5 \times 5}{350} \right] = 100 \left[ 1 + 0.0357 \right] = 103.57 \text{ kmol/h}$$

The internal reflux is **3.57% higher** than the external reflux in this case.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Steady-State Relationship between $L$ and $x_D$

This derivation is **frequently asked for 7 marks** in Part B of KTU ESE papers.

**Assumptions:**
1. Binary distillation (components A and B).
2. Constant molar overflow (CMO).
3. Total condenser.
4. The feed is at its bubble point.

**Step 1: Write the Operating Line for the Rectifying Section**

For the rectifying section (above the feed tray), the operating line passes through the point $(x_D, x_D)$ on the 45° line and has a slope equal to $L/V$:

$$y_{n+1} = \frac{L}{V} x_n + \frac{D \cdot x_D}{V}$$

**Step 2: Apply the Material Balance Around the Condenser**

For a total condenser, the reflux and distillate are in equilibrium with the vapor leaving the top tray:

$$y_N = x_D = x_{N+1}$$

This is a special case (no separation in the condenser).

**Step 3: Use the Steady-State Mass Balance**

The overall mass balance is:

$$F = D + B$$

The component balance is:

$$F z_F = D x_D + B x_B$$

**Step 4: Combine to Eliminate $D$**

Solve for $D$:

$$D = F \cdot \frac{z_F - x_B}{x_D - x_B}$$

**Step 5: Express $L$ in Terms of the Reflux Ratio**

$$L = R \cdot D = R \cdot F \cdot \frac{z_F - x_B}{x_D - x_B}$$

This shows that for a fixed $F$, $z_F$, $x_B$, increasing $R$ increases $L$ and **decreases $x_D$** (i.e., makes the distillate purer in the heavy component, or purer in the light component if $x_D$ is being measured on a different basis).

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Reflux Control Schemes
