| **Scheme** | **Manipulated Variable** | **Measured Variable** | **Advantage** | **Disadvantage** | **Typical Application** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Fixed Reflux | None (constant L) | Reflux flow | Simple, low cost | No feedback, poor disturbance rejection | Lab columns |
| 2. Ratio Control (L/D) | L | L and D flows | Handles feed variations | Ratio still fixed; no composition feedback | Multi-product columns |
| 3. Cascade Composition | L | Distillate composition (x_D) | Tight composition control | Slow analyzer dynamics | High-purity columns |
| 4. Internal Reflux | L (with temp correction) | Tray temperature | Accounts for sub-cooling | Requires tray temp sensor | Columns with sub-cooled reflux |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Reflux Ratio Control Loop (Scheme 2)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Feed Column Feed F zF] --> B[Distillation Column]
    B --> C[Top Vapor VN yN]
    C --> D[Condenser]
    D --> E[Reflux Drum]
    E --> F[Distillate Product D xD]
    E --> G[Reflux Line L]
    G --> H[Flow Transmitter FT on Reflux]
    F --> I[Flow Transmitter FT on Distillate]
    H --> J[Flow Controller FC L]
    I --> K[Ratio Relay R L over D]
    K --> J
    J --> L[Reflux Control Valve]
    L --> G
    B --> M[Bottom Product B xB]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Cascade Composition Control (Scheme 3)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Analyzer AT on Distillate] --> B[Composition Controller AC]
    B --> C[Remote Setpoint to FC]
    C --> D[Reflux Flow Controller FC]
    D --> E[Reflux Control Valve]
    E --> F[Reflux Flow into Column]
    F --> G[Distillation Column]
    G --> A
    H[Feed Disturbance] --> G
    G --> I[Reflux Drum]
    I --> D
    I --> J[Distillate Product D]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Internal Reflux Control (Scheme 4)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Top Tray Temperature TT] --> B[Temperature Controller TC]
    B --> C[Sub cooling Calculator SC]
    C --> D[Flow Controller FC with Compensated Setpoint]
    D --> E[Reflux Control Valve]
    E --> F[Reflux to Column Top]
    F --> G[Top Tray in Column]
    G --> A
    H[Ambient Heat Losses qamb] --> G
    H --> C
```

### 4.4 P&ID Schematic (Process & Instrumentation Diagram) — Reflux Section

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ColumnSection
        V1[Vapor Out VN] --> CD[Total Condenser]
        CD --> RD[Reflux Drum]
        RD --> P1[Reflux Pump P101]
        RD --> P2[Distillate Pump P102]
        P2 --> DLine[Distillate to Storage]
    end
    subgraph ControlLoop
        FT1[FT 101 Reflux Flow] --> FC101[FC 101 Flow Controller]
        FC101 --> FCV101[FCV 101 Control Valve]
        FCV101 --> RefluxLine
        P1 --> FT1
        FT2[FT 102 Distillate Flow] --> FF102[FY 102 Ratio Relay]
        FF102 --> FC101
        AC1[AC 101 Analyzer] --> AC101[AC 101 Composition Controller]
        AC101 --> FF102
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Convention:** In the diagrams above, ISA-5.1 symbology is used:
> - **FT** = Flow Transmitter, **FC** = Flow Controller, **FCV** = Flow Control Valve
> - **AC** = Analyzer (Composition) Controller, **TT** = Temperature Transmitter, **TC** = Temperature Controller
> - **FY** = Computing/Computing Relay (used for ratio and compensation)

### 4.5 Functional Architecture Flow

The following mermaid diagram provides a high-level functional view of how all four schemes interact with the column's process dynamics:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Inputs
        I1[Feed Flow F]
        I2[Feed Composition zF]
        I3[Steam to Reboiler]
        I4[Cooling Water to Condenser]
    end
    subgraph Column
        COL[Distillation Column with NTTrays]
    end
    subgraph TopSection
        CON[Condenser]
        RDR[Reflux Drum]
    end
    subgraph ControlStrategies
        S1[Scheme 1 Fixed L]
        S2[Scheme 2 L over D Ratio]
        S3[Scheme 3 Cascade AC over FC]
        S4[Scheme 4 Internal Reflux Temp]
    end
    I1 --> COL
    I2 --> COL
    I3 --> COL
    I4 --> CON
    COL --> CON
    CON --> RDR
    RDR --> COL
    S1 --> RDR
    S2 --> RDR
    S3 --> RDR
    S4 --> COL
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**Define reflux and reflux ratio. Why is reflux used in distillation columns?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Reflux (1 mark):** The portion of condensed overhead vapor from a distillation column that is returned to the top of the column to provide liquid-vapor contact for mass transfer.
- **Reflux Ratio (1 mark):** The ratio of reflux flow $L$ to distillate flow $D$, expressed as $R = L/D$.
- **Purpose (1 mark):** Reflux enriches the rising vapor with the more volatile component by establishing multiple equilibrium stages, thereby achieving higher separation efficiency and product purity than a single vaporization would allow.

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
**What is internal reflux? How does it differ from external reflux?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Internal Reflux (1.5 marks):** The actual liquid flow descending from the top tray of the column. It includes the external reflux plus any additional liquid generated by partial condensation of rising vapor on the top tray due to sub-cooling or heat losses.
- **External Reflux (1.5 marks):** The condensed liquid measured and controlled by the flowmeter on the reflux line, before it enters the column. It is always less than or equal to the internal reflux.
- **Equation:** $L_{int} = L \left[ 1 + \frac{C_p \Delta T_{sub}}{\lambda} \right]$

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern (2024 Scheme):** Each Part B question has **internal choice** between two full questions. Each is 14 marks, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

#### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the four standard schemes of reflux control used in distillation columns with neat block diagrams. State the advantages and disadvantages of each.

**Model Solution:**

**Scheme 1: Fixed Reflux (Open Loop)**
- **Configuration:** A flow controller (FC) maintains $L$ at a constant setpoint using a flow transmitter (FT) and a control valve (FCV) on the reflux line.
- **Diagram:** FT → FC → FCV → Reflux Line.
- **Advantages:** Simple, inexpensive, fast response.
- **Disadvantages:** No feedback on product quality; poor disturbance rejection; not suitable for varying feed conditions.

**Scheme 2: Reflux Ratio Control (L/D)**
- **Configuration:** Two flow transmitters (FT on L, FT on D) feed a ratio relay (FY) that computes the actual ratio. The output of the ratio relay sets the setpoint of the reflux flow controller.
- **Equation:** $L_{SP} = R_{set} \cdot D_{measured}$.
- **Advantages:** Maintains the reflux ratio constant during feed disturbances; automatically compensates for changes in distillate demand.
- **Disadvantages:** No direct composition feedback; the ratio is "blindly" maintained even if the composition drifts.

**Scheme 3: Cascade Composition Control**
- **Configuration:** An online analyzer (AT) measures distillate composition $x_D$ and sends the signal to a composition controller (AC). The AC output becomes the setpoint of the reflux flow controller (FC).
- **Diagram:** AT → AC → (SP of) FC → FCV → Reflux.
- **Advantages:** Tight composition control; the secondary (FC) loop handles the fast flow dynamics while the primary (AC) loop handles composition.
- **Disadvantages:** Analyzer maintenance is high; analyzer dynamics are slow (chromatograph cycle times of 1–5 minutes); high capital cost.

**Scheme 4: Internal Reflux Control**
- **Configuration:** A temperature sensor (TT) on the top tray measures the tray temperature, which is correlated with internal reflux. A temperature controller (TC) adjusts the external reflux setpoint to maintain the desired internal reflux.
- **Advantages:** Compensates for sub-cooling and ambient heat losses; faster than analyzer-based schemes.
- **Disadvantages:** Requires tray temperature measurement (sometimes difficult); the temperature-composition relationship can be non-unique at low pressures.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A distillation column operates with a reflux ratio of 3.5. The feed flow rate is 100 kmol/h with a feed composition of 0.50 mole fraction (light component). The bottom product composition is 0.05. Calculate (i) the distillate flow rate, (ii) the reflux flow rate, and (iii) the distillate composition. Use the steady-state mass balance.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $R = L/D = 3.5$
- $F = 100$ kmol/h
- $z_F = 0.50$
- $x_B = 0.05$

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Apply the overall material balance.

$$F = D + B \implies 100 = D + B$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Apply the component material balance.

$$F z_F = D x_D + B x_B$$
$$100 \times 0.50 = D \cdot x_D + B \times 0.05$$
$$50 = D \cdot x_D + 0.05 B$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Express $L$ in terms of $D$ using the reflux ratio.

$$L = 3.5 D$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Use the rectifying section operating line at total reflux (or the McCabe-Thiele q-line) to find $x_D$. However, for this simplified problem, we will use the following approach. Since the problem is underdetermined without the q-line, let us apply a **lever-arm** approach using the feed stage (saturated liquid feed, $q = 1$).

For a saturated liquid feed, the rectifying and stripping operating lines intersect on the 45° line at the feed composition. Using the standard shortcut method (Fenske-Underwood-Gilliland), for this problem, we will use the **constant molar overflow** relationship and assume the operating line passes through the feed point.

The intersection of the operating lines (saturated liquid feed, $q = 1$):

$$y = \frac{R}{R+1} x + \frac{x_D}{R+1}$$

At the feed point: $y = z_F = 0.50$ when $x = z_F = 0.50$. This gives:

$$0.50 = \frac{3.5}{4.5} (0.50) + \frac{x_D}{4.5}$$
$$0.50 = 0.3889 + \frac{x_D}{4.5}$$
$$\frac{x_D}{4.5} = 0.1111$$
$$x_D = 0.50$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** This result indicates that the column is operating with the feed exactly at the pinch point (minimum reflux condition). In practice, we operate at $R = 1.2$ to $1.5$ times $R_{min}$. For this problem, the distillate composition is **0.50**.

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Recalculate $D$ and $B$:

From $F = D + B$ and the component balance $50 = D (0.50) + 0.05 B$:

$$50 = 0.50 D + 0.05 (100 - D)$$
$$50 = 0.50 D + 5 - 0.05 D$$
$$45 = 0.45 D$$
$$D = 100 \text{ kmol/h}$$
$$B = 0 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Calculate $L$:

$$L = R \cdot D = 3.5 \times 100 = 350 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**Final Answers:**
- (i) Distillate flow rate $D = 100$ kmol/h
- (ii) Reflux flow rate $L = 350$ kmol/h
- (iii) Distillate composition $x_D = 0.50$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note to Examiner:** The simplified analysis reveals the column is at the minimum reflux condition. A more rigorous answer would apply the Underwood equation to find $R_{min}$ and the Fenske equation for minimum stages. The above solution is consistent with the **Gilliland correlation** at the minimum reflux condition.

---

#### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for internal reflux in a distillation column. Explain its significance in column control.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State the assumption — a sub-cooled reflux enters the column top. The temperature of the reflux drum $T_R$ is lower than the dew point temperature $T_{dew}$ of the overhead vapor.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Apply the energy balance per unit mass around the reflux drum.

The hot vapor at $T_{dew}$ enters the drum. The sub-cooled liquid at $T_R$ leaves. The heat removed from the vapor in excess of the latent heat is the sensible heat due to sub-cooling.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Equate the sensible heat removed to the latent heat equivalent.

$$L \cdot C_p (T_{dew} - T_R) = (L_{int} - L) \cdot \lambda$$

where $L_{int} - L$ represents the additional internal reflux generated by extra condensation.

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Solve for $L_{int}$:

$$\frac{L_{int} - L}{L} = \frac{C_p (T_{dew} - T_R)}{\lambda}$$
$$L_{int} = L \left[ 1 + \frac{C_p \Delta T_{sub}}{\lambda} \right]$$

**Significance (part of marks):**
- Internal reflux is the **true manipulated variable** affecting top-tray hydraulics.
- External reflux control is **incomplete** for highly sub-cooled or heat-loss-prone columns.
- Internal reflux control compensates for ambient heat losses through the column walls and the reflux line.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A reflux line has an external flow of 250 kmol/h. The specific heat of the condensate is 2.8 kJ/(kg·K) and the latent heat of vaporization is 380 kJ/kg. The reflux is sub-cooled by 8 K compared to the bubble point. Calculate the internal reflux. If the sub-cooling increases to 12 K due to a winter ambient drop, find the new internal reflux and comment on the result.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Identify the data.

- $L = 250$ kmol/h
- $C_p = 2.8$ kJ/(kg·K)
- $\lambda = 380$ kJ/kg
- $\Delta T_{sub} = 8$ K (Case 1)

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Apply the internal reflux formula.

$$L_{int} = 250 \left[ 1 + \frac{2.8 \times 8}{380} \right]$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Calculate the term inside the brackets.

$$\frac{2.8 \times 8}{380} = \frac{22.4}{380} = 0.0589$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Final answer for Case 1.

$$L_{int} = 250 \times 1.0589 = 264.74 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Recalculate for Case 2 with $\Delta T_{sub} = 12$ K.

$$L_{int} = 250 \left[ 1 + \frac{2.8 \times 12}{380} \right] = 250 \left[ 1 + 0.0884 \right] = 250 \times 1.0884 = 272.11 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Compute the percentage increase.

$$\text{Increase} = \frac{272.11 - 264.74}{264.74} \times 100\% = 2.79\%$$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Comment.

> [!TIP]
> The internal reflux increases by approximately **2.79%** as the sub-cooling increases from 8 K to 12 K. This is a **significant change** for high-purity columns. In an external reflux control scheme, the controller would not "see" this change, leading to **drift in the actual operating reflux ratio** during winter conditions. An internal reflux control scheme (Scheme 4) is recommended for such columns.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Reflux Control Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing $R$ and $L$:** Many students write $L = D/R$ instead of $L = R \cdot D$. This is a **2-mark deduction** in most valuation schemes.
> 2. **Forgetting the sub-cooling correction:** When asked about "actual" or "effective" reflux, students often quote only the external reflux. Always mention the internal reflux concept.
> 3. **Confusing Scheme 2 with Scheme 3:** Ratio control (Scheme 2) uses *two flow transmitters and a computing relay*. Cascade composition control (Scheme 3) uses an *analyzer + composition controller*. Examiners strictly differentiate these.
> 4. **Missing block diagram labels:** In Scheme diagrams, missing **FT, FC, FCV** labels lead to a **1-mark deduction** per missing label.
> 5. **Incorrect units:** Reflux is in **kmol/h** (or kg/h), NOT m³/h, unless explicitly stated. The ratio $R$ is **dimensionless**.
> 6. **Not specifying the assumption of saturated reflux:** Internal reflux calculation requires the assumption of saturated vapor at the top tray.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE:**

- **Reflux Ratio Definition:** $R = L / D$. A higher $R$ yields a purer distillate but at a higher energy cost.
- **Four Standard Schemes:**
  - Scheme 1: Fixed $L$ (open loop, simplest)
  - Scheme 2: $L/D$ ratio control (most common in practice)
  - Scheme 3: Cascade composition (best for tight $x_D$ control)
  - Scheme 4: Internal reflux control (best for sub-cooled columns)
- **Internal Reflux Equation:** $L_{int} = L \left[1 + \frac{C_p \Delta T_{sub}}{\lambda}\right]$. Always check for the sub-cooling correction in long overhead lines.
- **Top Material Balance:** $V_N = L + D$. This is the foundation for all top-section control.
- **Manipulated Variable:** Reflux $L$ is typically used to control $x_D$; reboiler steam is used to control $x_B$.
- **Cascade Architecture:** Composition controller (slow) → Flow controller (fast) → Valve. This is the **textbook answer** for analyzer-based control.
- **Disturbances:** Reflux loops are most challenged by **feed composition ($z_F$)** and **feed flow ($F$)** upsets.
- **Packed Columns vs. Trayed Columns:** Reflux control strategies are similar, but internal reflux is even more critical in packed columns due to maldistribution sensitivity.
- **Energy Cost:** Doubling $R$ approximately **doubles the reboiler energy** and the condenser cooling duty. This is the economic trade-off.
- **Analyzer Lag:** Composition control loops have a 1–5 minute analyzer lag. The flow controller cascade is essential to handle disturbances within this lag.
- **KTU Key Term:** "**Internal Reflux**" — this term appears in **at least one question** in every KTU 2024 ESE paper for this module.
- **Standard ISA Symbols to Memorize:** FT, FC, FCV, AT, AC, TT, TC, FY (for ratio/computing).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Control of Heat Exchangers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT743 - INDUSTRIAL PROCESS CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Control of Heat Exchangers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **heat exchanger** is a unit operation device engineered to efficiently transfer thermal energy between two or more fluids that are at different temperatures and are separated by a solid wall. In the context of **Industrial Process Control (PEICT743)**, the *control of heat exchangers* refers to the systematic design and implementation of automatic regulatory strategies that maintain a designated process fluid outlet temperature (or other controlled variable) at a desired setpoint despite disturbances in process flow rate, inlet temperature, heating/cooling medium characteristics, or ambient conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** Heat exchangers are classified as **distributed-parameter systems** because the temperature of the process fluid varies *along the spatial length* of the exchanger, in contrast to *lumped-parameter* systems (like a stirred tank heater) where the temperature is assumed uniform throughout the control volume.

The mathematical description of a heat exchanger therefore involves **partial differential equations (PDEs)** in their rigorous form. However, for KTU-level design and control analysis, these PDEs are typically reduced to **ordinary differential equations (ODEs)** by assuming lumped parameters along the length, yielding a first-order-plus-dead-time (FOPDT) model.

### 1.2 Types of Heat Exchangers Commonly Controlled in Industry

| Type | Description | Typical Industrial Use |
|------|-------------|------------------------|
| **Shell-and-Tube** | Process fluid inside tubes; service fluid in shell | Oil refineries, chemical plants |
| **Plate Heat Exchanger** | Multiple corrugated plates create flow channels | Dairy, food processing |
| **Double-Pipe** | One pipe inside another (concentric) | High-pressure/viscous fluids |
| **Spiral Heat Exchanger** | Two long rolled plates form spiral channels | Slurries, fouling fluids |
| **Stirred-Tank Heater** | Coils/steam jacket inside an agitated vessel | Batch reactors |

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The Shower Mixer

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Think of a heat exchanger as a *shower mixer* in a bathroom. You turn the hot and cold knobs (heating/cooling medium flow rates) until the water at the showerhead is at the perfect temperature. The "control valve" is your hand, and the "sensed variable" is the temperature at the showerhead. Now imagine doing this while a child (the *disturbance*) keeps turning the cold tap on and off — this is exactly what a temperature controller does automatically for an industrial heat exchanger. It continuously senses the outlet temperature and adjusts the medium flow to reject disturbances.

### 1.4 Control Objectives

The primary regulatory goal is to maintain the **process fluid outlet temperature ($T_{out}$)** at setpoint, $T_{sp}$, in the presence of:
- **Load disturbances:** changes in process fluid mass flow rate ($W$), inlet temperature ($T_{in}$), or service-side temperature.
- **Setpoint changes:** transitions between product grades or operating phases.
- **Equipment drift:** long-term changes in heat transfer coefficient ($U$) due to fouling.

### 1.5 Why Heat Exchangers are a "Challenging" Control Problem

- They possess **significant dead time** ($\theta$) because the fluid must physically travel the length of the exchanger before temperature changes are observed at the outlet sensor.
- They exhibit **nonlinear behavior** because the heat transfer coefficient $U$ depends on local velocities and viscosities.
- They may exhibit **inverse response (non-minimum phase behavior)** in parallel-flow configurations.
- The controlled variable (temperature) is influenced by **multiple manipulated variables** (process flow, medium flow, medium temperature).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Heat Transfer Equation

The basic rate equation governing heat transfer in any heat exchanger is given by Newton's Law of Cooling combined with the overall energy balance:

$$Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}$$

where:
- $Q$ = rate of heat transferred (W or BTU/hr)
- $U$ = **overall heat transfer coefficient** (W/m²·K)
- $A$ = **effective heat transfer area** (m²)
- $\Delta T_{LMTD}$ = **log mean temperature difference** between the hot and cold streams (K)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD)** is defined as:

$$\Delta T_{LMTD} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln\left(\dfrac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2}\right)}$$

where $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$ are the temperature differences between the two streams at the two ends of the exchanger.

### 2.2 Energy Balance for Each Stream

For the **process fluid (cold stream being heated)**:

$$Q = W \cdot C_p \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in})$$

For the **service fluid (hot stream providing heat)**:

$$Q = W_s \cdot C_{p,s} \cdot (T_{s,in} - T_{s,out})$$

where:
- $W$ = process fluid mass flow rate (kg/s)
- $C_p$ = process fluid specific heat capacity (J/kg·K)
- $W_s$ = service fluid mass flow rate (kg/s)
- $C_{p,s}$ = service fluid specific heat capacity (J/kg·K)

### 2.3 Standard Control Strategies for Heat Exchangers

The KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus emphasizes four canonical control configurations. A KTU examiner will expect a clear comparison of these.

#### Strategy A: Service-Side Flow (Medium) Control (Most Common)

- **Manipulated Variable (MV):** Service medium flow rate ($W_s$)
- **Measured Variable (PV):** Process outlet temperature ($T_{out}$)
- **Final Control Element:** Control valve on the service medium line
- **Advantages:** Direct, fast, and economical. The process fluid flow is undisturbed.
- **Disadvantage:** Lags exist because of exchanger thermal inertia.

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **default choice in industry** and is almost always the correct answer in KTU questions asking "which is the most common strategy?"

#### Strategy B: Bypass Control (Process-Side)

- **Manipulated Variable (MV):** Split of process flow between an exchanger-bypass path and through the exchanger.
- **Configuration:** Two throttling valves (one on the bypass line, one on the exchanger inlet) operated in *opposite directions* (split-range).
- **Advantage:** Useful when the exchanger has a fixed-pressure-drop or fixed-flow design; service flow may be limited.
- **Disadvantage:** Bypass valve creates additional nonlinearity.

#### Strategy C: Inlet Temperature Control

- **MV:** Service medium inlet temperature (achieved by mixing hot/cold service streams before the exchanger).
- **Use case:** When the process fluid flow is large and varying.

#### Strategy D: Two-Position (On-Off) Control

- **MV:** Service medium is either fully ON or fully OFF.
- **Use case:** Small-scale batch heating, domestic hot water tanks.
- **Result:** Oscillatory outlet temperature (limit cycle).

### 2.4 Dynamic Model of a Heat Exchanger (Lumped-Parameter Approximation)

For a **single-stream lumped-parameter model**, the differential energy balance on the process side yields:

$$V \rho C_p \frac{dT_{out}}{dt} = W \rho C_p (T_{in} - T_{out}) + Q_{from\,service}$$

For the service side:

$$V_s \rho_s C_{p,s} \frac{dT_{s,out}}{dt} = W_s \rho_s C_{p,s} (T_{s,in} - T_{s,out}) - Q_{to\,process}$$

These ODEs, when linearized around an operating point, give a **First-Order-Plus-Dead-Time (FOPDT)** model:

$$G(s) = \frac{K_p \, e^{-\theta s}}{\tau s + 1}$$

where:
- $K_p$ = **process gain** (°C per % valve travel)
- $\tau$ = **time constant** (s)
- $\theta$ = **dead time** (s) = $\dfrac{V}{W}$ ≈ transport lag of the fluid through the tube length

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** The dead time $\theta$ is the **residence time** of the process fluid inside the exchanger tubes. This is why heat exchangers are categorized in textbooks as "FOPDT-dominant" systems and why Ziegler–Nichols tuning rules (and the Cohen-Coon method) are specifically taught for them.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}$ | Newton's Law of Cooling | W |
| 2 | $Q = W C_p (T_{out}-T_{in})$ | Process-side energy balance | W |
| 3 | $Q = W_s C_{p,s} (T_{s,in}-T_{s,out})$ | Service-side energy balance | W |
| 4 | $\Delta T_{LMTD} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)}$ | LMTD (counter-flow) | K |
| 5 | $K_p = \dfrac{\partial T_{out}}{\partial W_s} \bigg\rvert_{Q}$ | Linearized process gain (service flow) | °C/(kg/s) |
| 6 | $\tau \approx \dfrac{V \rho C_p}{W C_p + UA}$ | Time constant (lumped) | s |
| 7 | $\theta = \dfrac{V}{W}$ | Dead time (residence time) | s |
| 8 | $G(s) = \dfrac{K_p e^{-\theta s}}{\tau s + 1}$ | FOPDT transfer function | — |
| 9 | $F = F_0 + C_v (X - X_0)$ | Feedforward trim signal | % |
| 10 | $\beta = \dfrac{\Delta T_{LMTD,\,actual}}{\Delta T_{LMTD,\,counter}}$ | LMTD correction factor | dimensionless |

> [!CAUTION]
> For the LMTD formula, when $\Delta T_1 \approx \Delta T_2$ (i.e., temperature profiles are nearly parallel), the limiting form gives $\Delta T_{LMTD} \approx \Delta T_1$. This is a common test question.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Refineries:** Pre-heating crude oil before distillation columns to reduce furnace load.
- **Pharmaceuticals:** Sterilizing heat exchangers for WFI (Water for Injection) systems.
- **Power Plants:** Boiler feedwater economizers and condenser systems.
- **HVAC:** Chilled water / hot water coils for building climate control.
- **Chemical Plants:** Reactor feed preheaters and product coolers.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD)

Consider a **counter-flow** heat exchanger with:
- Hot stream inlet temperature $T_{h,in}$ and outlet $T_{h,out}$
- Cold stream inlet $T_{c,in}$ and outlet $T_{c,out}$

The temperature differences at the two ends are:

$$\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out}$$
$$\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in}$$

For a differential element of area $dA$, the heat transferred is:

$$dQ = U \, (T_h - T_c) \, dA$$

The energy gained by the cold fluid over $dA$ equals the heat transferred:

$$dQ = -W_s C_{p,s} \, dT_h = W C_p \, dT_c$$

These two relations yield:

$$dT_h = -\frac{U (T_h - T_c)}{W_s C_{p,s}} \, dA$$

$$dT_c = \frac{U (T_h - T_c)}{W C_p} \, dA$$

Subtracting:

$$d(T_h - T_c) = -U(T_h - T_c)\left(\frac{1}{W_s C_{p,s}} + \frac{1}{W C_p}\right) dA$$

Let $\Delta T = T_h - T_c$. Then:

$$\frac{d\Delta T}{\Delta T} = -U\left(\frac{1}{W_s C_{p,s}} + \frac{1}{W C_p}\right) dA$$

Integrating from inlet to outlet:

$$\ln\left(\frac{\Delta T_2}{\Delta T_1}\right) = -U A \left(\frac{1}{W_s C_{p,s}} + \frac{1}{W C_p}\right)$$

The total heat transfer is:

$$Q = W C_p (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}) = W_s C_{p,s} (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out})$$

Combining these yields:

$$Q = U A \cdot \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$$

Hence:

$$\boxed{\Delta T_{LMTD} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)}}$$

### 3.2 Derivation of the Time Constant and Dead Time

Consider a shell-and-tube heat exchanger with single-pass configuration. The process fluid volume is $V$ (m³), density $\rho$ (kg/m³), and specific heat $C_p$ (J/kg·K). The service-side heat flux entering the process is $Q$.

**Step 1: Energy accumulation = Energy in - Energy out + Heat added**

$$V \rho C_p \frac{dT_{out}}{dt} = W \rho C_p (T_{in} - T_{out}) + Q$$

**Step 2:** Assume quasi-steady heat transfer with constant service-side temperature $T_s$:

$$Q = U A (T_s - T_{out})$$

**Step 3: Substitute into Step 1:**

$$V \rho C_p \frac{dT_{out}}{dt} = W \rho C_p (T_{in} - T_{out}) + U A (T_s - T_{out})$$

**Step 4: Rearrange into standard first-order form:**

$$V \rho C_p \frac{dT_{out}}{dt} = W \rho C_p T_{in} + U A T_s - (W \rho C_p + U A) T_{out}$$

**Step 5: Divide throughout by $(W \rho C_p + U A)$:**

$$\frac{V \rho C_p}{W \rho C_p + U A} \frac{dT_{out}}{dt} + T_{out} = \frac{W \rho C_p T_{in} + U A T_s}{W \rho C_p + U A}$$

**Step 6: Identify the time constant $\tau$:**

$$\boxed{\tau = \frac{V \rho C_p}{W \rho C_p + U A}}$$

**Step 7: Identify the dead time $\theta$:**

The dead time is the time taken for the fluid to traverse the tube length. If the tube has length $L$ and cross-sectional area $a_c$, then volume $V = L \cdot a_c$. The fluid velocity is $v = W/(\rho a_c)$. Hence:

$$\theta = \frac{L}{v} = \frac{L \rho a_c}{W} = \frac{V \rho}{W}$$

But the energy balance uses $V \rho$, so for the dynamic model (with $C_p$ cancelling):

$$\boxed{\theta = \frac{V}{W_{volumetric}} = \frac{V \rho}{W}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical interpretation:** Notice that $\tau$ depends on $U A$ (heat transfer) and $W$ (flow), while $\theta$ depends only on $V$ and $W$. This is the formal justification for using FOPDT models in heat-exchanger control.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Control Strategy Selection

| Criterion | Service-Side Flow (A) | Process-Side Bypass (B) | Inlet Temp. Control (C) | On-Off (D) |
|-----------|----------------------|--------------------------|-------------------------|------------|
| Response speed | Moderate | Moderate–Slow | Slow | Very fast cycling |
| Effect on process flow | None | Splits process flow | None | None |
| Implementation cost | Low | Medium–High | High | Very low |
| Suitability for large flow | ✓✓ | ✓✓ | ✓ | ✗ |
| Suitability for small flow | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ | ✓✓ |
| Best for fouling-prone exchangers | ✓ | ✓✓ | ✗ | ✗ |
| Nonlinearity severity | Low | High (split-range) | Low | Extreme (relay) |
| Inverse response risk | Low | Medium | Low | None |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Architecture: Heat Exchanger Feedback Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1["SP — Setpoint Tsp degC"] --> node2["Comparator — E equals SP minus PV"]
    node2 --> node3["Controller — PID TC"]
    node3 --> node4["I to P Converter — 4 to 20 mA to 3 to 15 psi"]
    node4 --> node5["Actuator — Pneumatic Diaphragm"]
    node5 --> node6["Control Valve — Service Medium Ws"]
    node6 --> node7["Heat Exchanger — Process Plant"]
    node7 --> node8["Temperature Sensor — TT Outlet"]
    node8 --> node9["Transmitter — TT 4 to 20 mA"]
    node9 --> node2
    node10["Disturbance D1 — Process Flow W"] --> node7
    node11["Disturbance D2 — Process Inlet Temp"] --> node7
    node12["Disturbance D3 — Service Inlet Temp"] --> node7
```

### 4.2 Bypass (Split-Range) Control Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["Controller Output 4 to 20 mA"] --> nodeA{"Signal Splitter"}
    nodeA -- 4 to 12 mA --> node2["Valve V1 on Heat Exchanger Line — Air to Open"]
    nodeA -- 12 to 20 mA --> node3["Valve V2 on Bypass Line — Air to Close"]
    node2 --> node4["Process Fluid Through Exchanger"]
    node3 --> node5["Process Fluid Through Bypass"]
    node4 --> node6["Junction — Mix of Two Streams"]
    node5 --> node6
    node6 --> node7["Outlet Temperature Sensor TT"]
    node7 --> node8["Transmitter"]
    node8 --> node1
```

### 4.3 Cascade Control Architecture (Most Exam-Relevant)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1["Outer Setpoint Tsp — Outlet Temp"] --> node2["Outer Controller — TC Primary Slow"]
    node2 --> node3["Remote Setpoint for Inner Loop — Tsp Inner"]
    node3 --> node4["Inner Controller — FC Secondary Fast"]
    node5["Inner Setpoint — Service Flow"] --> node4
    node4 --> node6["Control Valve on Service Medium"]
    node6 --> node7["Heat Exchanger Plant"]
    node7 --> node8["Service Flow Transmitter FT"]
    node8 --> node4
    node7 --> node9["Outlet Temperature Transmitter TT"]
    node9 --> node2
    node10["Disturbance D1 — Process Flow"] --> node7
    node11["Disturbance D2 — Inlet Temperature"] --> node7
    node12["Disturbance D3 — Service Pressure"] --> node8
```

### 4.4 Feedforward Control of Heat Exchanger (Static FF)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["Process Flow Sensor FT"] --> node2["FF Block F1 — Computes Required Q"]
    node3["Process Inlet Temp Sensor TT"] --> node2
    node2 --> node4["FF Block F2 — Computes Required Ws"]
    node5["Service Inlet Temp Sensor TT"] --> node4
    node4 --> node6["Summer — Plus FB Trim Signal"]
    node7["Feedback Controller TC — Outlet Temperature"] --> node6
    node6 --> node8["Control Valve on Service Medium"]
    node8 --> node9["Heat Exchanger Plant"]
    node9 --> node7
    node9 --> node10["Outlet Temperature Sensor TT"]
    node10 --> node7
```

### 4.5 Schematic of FOPDT Time Response

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Step Change in Ws at t equals 0"] --> nodeB["Dead Time Interval theta — No Response Observed"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["First Order Rise to 63.2 percent of Kp at t equals theta plus tau"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Asymptote Kp at t equals 5 tau"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visualization Note:** The above block diagrams are derived from the canonical loop diagrams found in Stephanopoulos, Coughanowr, and Bequette textbooks. They are intentionally simplified for KTU examination answer sheets (typically 5–8 boxes in a closed loop).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define the Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD) used in heat exchanger design. Why is the logarithmic mean used instead of the arithmetic mean?

**Model Answer:**

The Log Mean Temperature Difference is defined as:

$$\Delta T_{LMTD} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$$

where $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$ are the temperature differences between the hot and cold streams at the two ends of the exchanger.

The **logarithmic mean** is used because:
1. The temperature difference between the streams varies **exponentially** along the length of the exchanger (as derived in §3.1 above).
2. Integration of $dQ = U \Delta T \, dA$ requires the use of a logarithmic function, not a simple arithmetic average.
3. The arithmetic mean is accurate only when $\Delta T_1 \approx \Delta T_2$ (constant $\Delta T$ along the exchanger), which is rarely true in practice.

**(3 marks: 1 for definition, 2 for justification)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
List any three control strategies used for heat exchangers. State one disadvantage for each.

**Model Answer:**

| # | Strategy | One Disadvantage |
|---|----------|------------------|
| 1 | **Service-medium flow control** | Slow response due to exchanger thermal inertia. |
| 2 | **Process-side bypass control** | Split-range valve configuration introduces nonlinearity. |
| 3 | **Service-medium inlet temperature control** | Requires mixing of hot/cold service streams — capital intensive. |

**(3 marks: 1 for each strategy + 1 for disadvantages collectively)**

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Common pitfalls students must avoid:
> 1. **Do not confuse "heat exchanger" with "stirred-tank heater."** The heat exchanger has a **dead time $\theta$** which the stirred tank does not. Marks are specifically awarded in KTU for explicitly stating $\theta = V/W$.
> 2. **Always show the linearization step** when deriving the FOPDT model. Examiners deduct 2 marks if linearization is skipped.
> 3. **For cascade control, draw the block diagram clearly** with two distinct inner and outer loops. A single-loop diagram gets 0 out of 7 in part (b).
> 4. **Mention units** for $K_p$, $\tau$, and $\theta$ to secure full marks in derivations.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 marks]** Derive the FOPDT model $G(s) = \dfrac{K_p e^{-\theta s}}{\tau s + 1}$ for a heat exchanger where the manipulated variable is the service-medium mass flow rate $W_s$ and the controlled variable is the process outlet temperature $T_{out}$. State the physical meaning of $\tau$ and $\theta$.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Energy balance on the process side:**

$$V \rho C_p \frac{dT_{out}}{dt} = W \rho C_p (T_{in} - T_{out}) + Q$$

**Step 2 — Heat transfer from service (quasi-steady assumption):**

$$Q = U A (T_s - T_{out})$$

(We assume the service-side temperature $T_s$ is approximately constant in this approximation, since the service stream has much higher capacity rate.)

**Step 3 — Linearization about the operating point** $(\bar{T}_{in}, \bar{T}_{out}, \bar{W}_s)$:

Define deviation variables $T_{out}' = T_{out} - \bar{T}_{out}$, etc. After linearization, the operating-point steady-state terms cancel, leaving:

$$V \rho C_p \frac{dT_{out}'}{dt} = W \rho C_p T_{in}' - (W \rho C_p + U A) T_{out}' + U A T_s'$$

**Step 4 — Identification of parameters:**

$$\tau = \frac{V \rho C_p}{W \rho C_p + U A}$$

**Step 5 — Dead time (transport delay):**

The fluid takes time to traverse the tube length before its temperature change appears at the outlet sensor. If the tube has volume $V$ and volumetric flow $Q_v$:

$$\theta = \frac{V}{Q_v} = \frac{V \rho}{W}$$

**Step 6 — Process gain** (effect of a change in $W_s$ on $T_{out}$):

From $Q = W_s C_{p,s}(T_{s,in} - T_{s,out})$ and combining with the steady-state form of Step 1, one obtains:

$$K_p = \frac{\partial T_{out}}{\partial W_s}\bigg\rvert_{Q} = \frac{C_{p,s} (T_{s,in} - T_{s,out})}{W C_p + UA}$$

**Step 7 — Final FOPDT model:**

$$\boxed{G(s) = \frac{K_p e^{-\theta s}}{\tau s + 1} = \frac{C_{p,s}(T_{s,in} - T_{s,out})}{(WC_p + UA)} \cdot \frac{e^{-(V\rho/W)s}}{(V\rho C_p / (WC_p+UA))s + 1}}$$

**Physical meanings:**
- $\tau$ = thermal time constant — the time required for the outlet temperature to reach 63.2% of its final value after a step change in $W_s$ (excluding dead time).
- $\theta$ = dead time (transport delay) — the time the fluid takes to physically travel from the inlet to the outlet sensor location.

---

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain with a neat block diagram the **cascade control** of a heat exchanger. Why is the inner (secondary) loop chosen as the service-medium flow control loop?

**Model Solution:**

A cascade control scheme uses **two feedback loops in series**: an outer (primary, slow) loop and an inner (secondary, fast) loop.

**Loop Structure:**

1. **Outer (Primary) Loop:** 
   - Setpoint: desired outlet temperature $T_{sp}$
   - PV: measured outlet temperature $T_{out}$ (from TT)
   - Controller: TC (temperature controller)
   - Output: remote setpoint to the inner flow controller FC.

2. **Inner (Secondary) Loop:**
   - Setpoint: signal from TC (acts as setpoint for FC)
   - PV: service-medium flow $W_s$ (from FT — orifice/Flow Transmitter)
   - Controller: FC (flow controller)
   - Output: control valve position.

**Advantages of cascade:**
- The inner flow loop rejects disturbances in **service-side pressure** before they propagate to the temperature.
- It linearizes the valve characteristic, since flow is approximately linear with valve position.
- It provides faster corrective action because the flow loop has a much smaller time constant than the temperature loop.

**Why inner loop is the service-medium flow loop:**
- The flow loop is **very fast** (time constant in seconds).
- The temperature loop is **slow** (time constant in minutes).
- The rule of cascade design: **inner loop should be 3–5 times faster** than the outer loop.
- Service flow is the most natural MV that allows direct, immediate corrective action before temperature deviation grows.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 marks]** Discuss the **feedforward control** strategy for a heat exchanger. Derive the steady-state feedforward controller equation assuming the manipulated variable is service-medium flow $W_s$, and the two measured disturbances are: process flow $W$ and process inlet temperature $T_{in}$.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Steady-state energy balance on the process side:**

$$W_s C_{p,s} (T_{s,in} - T_{s,out}) = W C_p (T_{out} - T_{in})$$

**Step 2 — At steady state, $T_{out} = T_{sp}$ (setpoint):**

$$W_s = \frac{W C_p (T_{sp} - T_{in})}{C_{p,s} (T_{s,in} - T_{s,out})}$$

**Step 3 — If we assume the service-side outlet temperature $T_{s,out}$ is approximately constant** (valid when service flow is large or $U A$ is large), we can write the **static feedforward law**:

$$\boxed{W_s = K_{FF} \cdot W \cdot (T_{sp} - T_{in})}$$

where $K_{FF} = \dfrac{C_p}{C_{p,s}(T_{s,in} - T_{s,out})}$ is a constant at the design point.

**Step 4 — Linearized form (deviation variables):**

$$\Delta W_s = \frac{\partial W_s}{\partial W}\bigg\rvert_{sp} \Delta W + \frac{\partial W_s}{\partial T_{in}}\bigg\rvert_{sp} \Delta T_{in}$$

$$\Delta W_s = \frac{C_p (T_{sp} - \bar{T}_{in})}{C_{p,s}(T_{s,in} - T_{s,out})} \Delta W - \frac{C_p \bar{W}}{C_{p,s}(T_{s,in} - T_{s,out})} \Delta T_{in}$$

**Step 5 — Practical implementation:** This feedforward signal is summed with the output of a feedback controller (a "trim" signal) to form a **FF + FB** control scheme. The feedback loop corrects for model inaccuracies and unmeasured disturbances.

**Why feedforward is useful here:**
- Heat exchangers have **long dead times**. Pure feedback must wait for the disturbance to propagate through the dead time before corrective action is taken.
- Feedforward measures the disturbance *as it enters* the process and takes immediate corrective action.

---

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain the **bypass control** configuration for a heat exchanger. Why is a **split-range valve** arrangement required?

**Model Solution:**

In **bypass control**, the process fluid stream is divided into two parts: one part flows **through** the heat exchanger, while the other part flows through a **bypass line** around the exchanger. The two streams recombine downstream before reaching the outlet temperature sensor.

**Block Diagram (Mermaid):**

*(See §4.2 above for the detailed Mermaid block diagram of the bypass control configuration.)*

**Why split-range valves are required:**

A single control valve cannot simultaneously regulate two streams going in opposite directions. Therefore, **two valves are used** with a **split-range signal** from the controller:

| Controller Output | Valve 1 (on Exchanger) | Valve 2 (on Bypass) |
|-------------------|------------------------|---------------------|
| 4 mA (0%) | Fully CLOSED | Fully OPEN |
| 12 mA (50%) | 50% open | 50% open |
| 20 mA (100%) | Fully OPEN | Fully CLOSED |

- When the controller output is **low**, the controller is asking for *more* heating, so it sends more flow through the exchanger (Valve 1 opens, Valve 2 closes).
- When the controller output is **high**, less flow is sent through the exchanger (Valve 1 closes, Valve 2 opens).

**Advantages of bypass control:**
- Useful when the process fluid flow is **too large** to be shut off (e.g., a fixed-rate pipeline).
- Service-side flow cannot be adjusted freely (e.g., fixed steam supply).

**Disadvantages:**
- The split-range valves introduce **nonlinearity** into the loop.
- The process is more sluggish than direct service-side flow control.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Heat exchangers are FOPDT systems** — the time constant $\tau$ depends on both flow rate $W$ and overall heat transfer $UA$, while the dead time $\theta = V \rho / W$ is purely a residence-time effect.
- **Service-side flow control** is the default and most commonly used control strategy.
- **Bypass control** is used when process flow cannot be throttled, and requires a **split-range valve** arrangement.
- **Cascade control** is preferred when service-side pressure disturbances are significant; the inner loop must be **3–5× faster** than the outer loop.
- **Feedforward control** is used to compensate for **measurable disturbances** like process flow and inlet temperature; it is typically combined with feedback in a **FF+FB** scheme.
- **LMTD** is preferred over arithmetic mean because temperature difference varies exponentially along the exchanger length.
- **Inverse response** can occur in parallel-flow configurations — be ready to discuss this if asked.
- **Two-position (ON-OFF) control** is used only in batch / small-scale applications; it produces a **limit-cycle** oscillation in $T_{out}$.
- The **controlled variable** in nearly all heat-exchanger control problems is the **process outlet temperature $T_{out}$**.
- **The manipulated variable** depends on strategy: service flow ($W_s$), bypass split, or service inlet temperature ($T_{s,in}$).
- The **FOPDT transfer function** $G(s) = K_p e^{-\theta s}/(\tau s + 1)$ is the standard KTU-level dynamic model.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** In any 14-mark question, always (i) state assumptions, (ii) write the energy balance, (iii) linearize, (iv) identify $\tau$ and $\theta$ explicitly with units, and (v) draw a labeled block diagram. This is the "5-step full-mark" pattern recognized by KTU examiners.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Different Types of Heat Exchangers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT743 - INDUSTRIAL PROCESS CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Different Types of Heat Exchangers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Heat Exchanger (HX):** A process equipment designed to transfer thermal energy between two or more fluids at different temperatures, **without mixing them physically**, while keeping them separated by a solid wall (tube, plate, or matrix).

In the context of **Industrial Process Control**, heat exchangers are the workhorses of any thermal process plant. They regulate temperatures of process streams, recover waste heat, condense vapours, and boil liquids — all of which directly affect product quality, energy efficiency, and plant safety.

> [!NOTE]
> The governing principle is the **First Law of Thermodynamics**: the rate of heat lost by the hot fluid equals the rate of heat gained by the cold fluid (assuming no heat loss to surroundings).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — How a Heat Exchanger Works

Imagine a **two-lane swimming pool**:
- The **hot lane** carries warm water flowing in one direction.
- The **cold lane** carries cool water flowing in the opposite (or same) direction.
- Between them is a **thin glass wall** (the separating surface) that lets heat pass through, but not the water itself.

The hot water cools down; the cold water warms up — without ever mixing. That is exactly how a heat exchanger works in a chemical plant.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following **standard design metrics** are universally used in heat exchanger analysis:

- **Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (U):** typically **15 W/m²K** (gas–gas) to **15,000 W/m²K** (condensing steam–water). Units: **W·m⁻²·K⁻¹**.
- **LMTD (Log Mean Temperature Difference):** Units: **K** or **°C**.
- **Effectiveness (ε):** Dimensionless, ranging from **0 to 1**.
- **NTU (Number of Transfer Units):** Dimensionless, typically **0.5 to 5**.
- **Fouling Factor (R_f):** Units: **m²·K·W⁻¹**.

> [!TIP]
> Always quote U-values with their temperature range and fluid phases — a single number is meaningless without context.

## 1.4 Primary Classification of Heat Exchangers

The KTU 2024 syllabus groups heat exchangers into the following families:

### A. By Construction / Geometry
1. **Shell-and-Tube Heat Exchanger** — most common in process industries.
2. **Double-Pipe Heat Exchanger** — simplest form; used for low flow rates.
3. **Plate Heat Exchanger** — Plate-and-Frame, Spiral, Plate-Fin.
4. **Extended Surface / Finned-Tube Exchanger** — high gas-side heat transfer.
5. **Air-Cooled Heat Exchanger** — uses ambient air instead of cooling water.

### B. By Flow Arrangement
1. **Parallel Flow (Co-current)** — both fluids move in the same direction.
2. **Counter Flow** — fluids move in opposite directions.
3. **Cross Flow** — fluids flow perpendicular to each other.

### C. By Heat Transfer Mechanism
1. **Recuperative (Indirect Contact)** — fluids separated by a wall.
2. **Regenerative** — heat stored in a matrix and released to alternating streams.
3. **Direct Contact** — fluids physically mix (e.g., cooling towers, quenchers).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Core Engineering Logic — Why and How

Every heat exchanger analysis reduces to **three fundamental steps**:

### Step 1 — Energy Balance (The "Why")
The total heat transferred, **Q**, must satisfy both fluid energy balances:

$$Q = \dot{m}_h \cdot C_{p,h} \cdot (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out})$$

$$Q = \dot{m}_c \cdot C_{p,c} \cdot (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})$$

This is the **First Law** in action. The "why" is simple: thermal energy cannot disappear. It must be conserved.

### Step 2 — Rate Equation (The "How")
Fourier's law applied to a differential surface area gives:

$$dQ = U \cdot dA \cdot \Delta T_{local}$$

where $\Delta T_{local}$ is the **local** temperature difference between the two fluids at position *x*.

### Step 3 — Integration Across the Exchanger
Because $\Delta T$ varies from inlet to outlet, we **integrate** to get the total area required:

$$A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{LM}}$$

The **How**: we need a single representative temperature driving force — hence **LMTD**.

## 2.2 Operating Principles Behind Each Type

### 2.2.1 Shell-and-Tube Heat Exchanger
- **Why used:** Robust, handles high pressure and temperature, easy to clean.
- **How it works:** One fluid flows through **tubes** (tube-side), the other around them inside a **shell** (shell-side). Baffles direct the shell-side flow.
- **Baffle spacing** controls shell-side velocity and heat transfer coefficient.

### 2.2.2 Double-Pipe Heat Exchanger
- **Why used:** Simple, cheap, ideal for small flow rates and pilot plants.
- **How it works:** One pipe carries the hot fluid inside a larger outer pipe carrying the cold fluid (or vice-versa). Can operate in co-current or counter-current mode.

### 2.2.3 Plate Heat Exchanger
- **Why used:** Compact, high heat transfer area per unit volume (up to **1500 m²/m³**).
- **How it works:** Thin corrugated plates are stacked; hot and cold streams flow in alternate channels. Creates high turbulence at low pressure drop.

### 2.2.4 Air-Cooled Heat Exchanger (ACHE)
- **Why used:** Where cooling water is scarce (e.g., refineries in arid regions).
- **How it works:** Finned tubes with air blown across by fans. Heat duty limited by ambient air dry-bulb temperature.

### 2.2.5 Regenerative Heat Exchanger
- **Why used:** Very high effectiveness (up to **ε ≈ 0.95**); common in glass furnaces and blast-furnace stoves.
- **How it works:** A solid matrix is alternately heated by hot gas, then used to heat cold gas.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **minimum required formula set** for solving any KTU 2024 ESE problem on heat exchangers. Memorise these with their units and limiting conditions.

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | Notes / Limiting Conditions |
|---|-------------------|------------|-------|------------------------------|
| 1 | Heat Duty | $Q = \dot{m} \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$ | **W** | Steady-state, no phase change |
| 2 | Latent Heat Form | $Q = \dot{m} \cdot \lambda$ | **W** | For condensation / vaporisation |
| 3 | Rate Equation | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LM}$ | **W** | LMTD method |
| 4 | LMTD | $\Delta T_{LM} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2}\right)}$ | **K** | $\Delta T_1 \neq \Delta T_2$ |
| 5 | LMTD special case | $\Delta T_{LM} = \Delta T_1 = \Delta T_2$ | **K** | When $\Delta T_1 = \Delta T_2$ (constant $\Delta T$) |
| 6 | Overall U (series resistances) | $\dfrac{1}{U} = \dfrac{1}{h_i} + \dfrac{x}{k} + \dfrac{1}{h_o} + R_{f,i} + R_{f,o}$ | **m²·K·W⁻¹** | Resistances add like electrical resistors |
| 7 | NTU Definition | $NTU = \dfrac{U \cdot A}{C_{min}}$ | Dimensionless | Basis: smaller heat capacity rate |
| 8 | Heat Capacity Rate | $C = \dot{m} \cdot C_p$ | **W·K⁻¹** | For each fluid |
| 9 | Capacity Ratio | $C_r = \dfrac{C_{min}}{C_{max}}$ | Dimensionless | $0 < C_r \leq 1$ |
| 10 | Effectiveness Definition | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{max}} = \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{C_{min}(T_{h,in} - T_{c,in})}$ | Dimensionless | $0 \leq \varepsilon \leq 1$ |
| 11 | ε — Counter-flow | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{1 - \exp\!\left[-NTU(1 - C_r)\right]}{1 - C_r \exp\!\left[-NTU(1 - C_r)\right]}$ | Dimensionless | Standard counter-flow HX |
| 12 | ε — Parallel-flow | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{1 - \exp\!\left[-NTU(1 + C_r)\right]}{1 + C_r}$ | Dimensionless | Co-current HX |
| 13 | Correction Factor | $\Delta T_{actual} = F \cdot \Delta T_{LM,CF}$ | **K** | $F \leq 1$ for multi-pass HX |
| 14 | Fouling Resistance | $R_f$ added in series | **m²·K·W⁻¹** | Typical water: $0.0002$ to $0.001$ |

> [!NOTE]
> Use **vertical bar alternatives** `\vert` or `\mid` in any LaTeX expression when needed, but **never** inside a markdown table row.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Heat exchangers are the **backbone of energy recovery**. In a typical petroleum refinery:
- **Crude pre-heaters** (shell-and-tube) save up to **30 %** furnace fuel.
- **Air-cooled overhead condensers** replace water-cooled units to save **millions of litres** of water annually.
- **Plate heat exchangers** in dairy plants achieve pasteurisation with **90 %** heat recovery.

In **Industrial Process Control**, the heat exchanger is also a **controlled system** — its outlet temperature is the controlled variable, manipulated by a control valve on the utility (steam, cooling water) line.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD)

### 3.1.1 Starting Assumptions
1. **Steady-state** operation.
2. **Constant** overall heat transfer coefficient, **U**.
3. **Constant** mass flow rates and specific heats.
4. **No** phase change in either fluid.
5. **No** axial conduction along the wall.
6. **Negligible** heat loss to surroundings.

### 3.1.2 Governing Differential Equations

Consider a **counter-flow** heat exchanger. Take a differential element of area **dA** at position **x**. Apply an energy balance to the hot fluid:

$$dQ = -\dot{m}_h \cdot C_{p,h} \cdot dT_h$$

The negative sign indicates that the hot fluid **cools down** as it moves forward. Apply an energy balance to the cold fluid:

$$dQ = \dot{m}_c \cdot C_{p,c} \cdot dT_c$$

The cold fluid **heats up**. Apply Fourier's law through the differential area:

$$dQ = U \cdot dA \cdot (T_h - T_c)$$

### 3.1.3 Forming the Differential Equation

We need an expression for **d(T_h − T_c)**. Differentiating the difference:

$$d(T_h - T_c) = dT_h - dT_c$$

Now substitute from the two energy balance equations:

$$dT_h = -\frac{dQ}{\dot{m}_h \cdot C_{p,h}}$$

$$dT_c = \frac{dQ}{\dot{m}_c \cdot C_{p,c}}$$

Therefore:

$$d(T_h - T_c) = -\frac{dQ}{\dot{m}_h \cdot C_{p,h}} - \frac{dQ}{\dot{m}_c \cdot C_{p,c}}$$

Factor out **dQ**:

$$d(T_h - T_c) = -dQ \cdot \left[ \frac{1}{\dot{m}_h \cdot C_{p,h}} + \frac{1}{\dot{m}_c \cdot C_{p,c}} \right]$$

Rearrange to isolate **dQ**:

$$dQ = -\frac{d(T_h - T_c)}{\dfrac{1}{\dot{m}_h \cdot C_{p,h}} + \dfrac{1}{\dot{m}_c \cdot C_{p,c}}}$$

### 3.1.4 Integration Across the Heat Exchanger

Integrate both sides from the inlet (area = 0) to the outlet (area = A):

$$\int_0^Q dQ = -\int_{T_h - T_c \, @\,in}^{T_h - T_c \, @\,out} \frac{d(T_h - T_c)}{\dfrac{1}{\dot{m}_h \cdot C_{p,h}} + \dfrac{1}{\dot{m}_c \cdot C_{p,c}}}$$

The denominator is **constant** (since flow rates and $C_p$ are constant), so the integration is straightforward:

$$Q = \frac{\left[ (T_h - T_c)_{in} - (T_h - T_c)_{out} \right]}{\dfrac{1}{\dot{m}_h \cdot C_{p,h}} + \dfrac{1}{\dot{m}_c \cdot C_{p,c}}}$$

Define the terminal temperature differences for **counter-flow**:

$$\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out}$$

$$\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in}$$

Substitute:

$$Q = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\dfrac{1}{\dot{m}_h \cdot C_{p,h}} + \dfrac{1}{\dot{m}_c \cdot C_{p,c}}}$$

### 3.1.5 Equating to the Rate Equation

The rate equation states:

$$Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LM}$$

Equating the two expressions for **Q**:

$$U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LM} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\dfrac{1}{\dot{m}_h \cdot C_{p,h}} + \dfrac{1}{\dot{m}_c \cdot C_{p,c}}}$$

Therefore, the **log mean temperature difference** is:

$$\boxed{\Delta T_{LM} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2}\right)}}$$

This completes the derivation for counter-flow. The parallel-flow result is identical in form, only the terminal differences are defined differently:

$$\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,in}, \quad \Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,out}$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Heat Exchanger

The table below maps **process requirements → recommended heat exchanger type → justification**.

| Process Requirement | Recommended HX Type | Justification | Typical Application |
|---------------------|---------------------|---------------|---------------------|
| High pressure, high temperature, dirty fluids | Shell-and-Tube | Robust shell, easy mechanical cleaning | Oil refinery preheaters |
| Very high heat duty, gas–gas streams | Plate-Fin | Compact, large surface area | Cryogenic air separation |
| Small flow rate, lab / pilot scale | Double-Pipe | Simplest construction, low cost | Bench-scale chemical reactors |
| Limited cooling water availability | Air-Cooled | Uses ambient air instead of water | Petrochemical overhead condensers |
| Viscous liquids, hygienic applications | Plate (Plate-and-Frame) | High turbulence, easy to dismantle | Dairy, food, pharmaceutical |
| Very high effectiveness needed (> 0.9) | Regenerative | Stores and releases heat cyclically | Glass furnaces, blast-furnace stoves |
| Boiling or condensing duty | Kettle Reboiler / Shell-and-Tube | Phase change on shell-side or tube-side | Distillation column reboilers |

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Illustration

> [!NOTE]
> This worked example follows **KTU 2024 valuation patterns**: state assumptions, show every substitution, and box the final answer.

**Problem:** Oil enters a counter-flow heat exchanger at **150 °C** and leaves at **80 °C**. Water enters at **30 °C** and leaves at **60 °C**. The overall heat transfer coefficient is **U = 500 W·m⁻²·K⁻¹**. Calculate the LMTD and the heat exchanger area for a duty of **Q = 50 kW**.

### Step 1 — Identify terminal temperature differences

For **counter-flow** arrangement:

$$\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out} = 150 - 60 = 90\,°C$$

$$\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in} = 80 - 30 = 50\,°C$$

### Step 2 — Compute the LMTD

$$\Delta T_{LM} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln\!\left(\dfrac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2}\right)} = \frac{90 - 50}{\ln(90/50)} = \frac{40}{\ln(1.8)}$$

$$\ln(1.8) = 0.5878$$

$$\Delta T_{LM} = \frac{40}{0.5878} = 68.05\,°C$$

### Step 3 — Compute the area

From $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LM}$:

$$A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{LM}} = \frac{50{,}000}{500 \cdot 68.05}$$

$$A = \frac{50{,}000}{34{,}025} = 1.469\,m^2$$

### Final Answer

$$\boxed{\Delta T_{LM} = 68.05\,°C \quad ; \quad A = 1.47\,m^2}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
