A **negative DOF** indicates an **over-specified** process, meaning there are more independent equations than unknown variables. Such a design is **physically infeasible or contains redundant equations** and must be revised before proceeding with control system synthesis. *(1 mark)*

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Why is DOF analysis performed before designing the control system?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
DOF analysis is the **mathematical prerequisite** for any control system design because:
1. It determines the **maximum number of independent control loops** that can be installed in a process *(1 mark)*
2. It verifies the **operability and feasibility** of the plant design *(1 mark)*
3. It guides the **pairing of manipulated and controlled variables** in multi-loop control structures *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]`**

> **(a)** A mixing tank receives two inlet streams of an aqueous NaCl solution (single solute, $C = 2$: water + NaCl) and discharges a single outlet stream. The two inlet flow rates and the inlet temperatures are pre-specified. The inlet pressures are NOT specified. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(i)** Calculate the Degrees of Freedom of the system.
>
> **(ii)** List the variables that constitute the 4 DOF.
>
> **(b)** Two CSTRs in series each carry out an irreversible reaction $A \rightarrow B$ (single-component system in terms of tracking, $C=1$). CSTR 1 has 1 feed, 1 product, 1 coolant in, 1 coolant out. CSTR 2 similarly has 4 streams but receives CSTR 1's outlet. Feed flow, composition, temperature, and coolant inlet temperatures are all known. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(i)** Compute the DOF of CSTR 1 alone.
>
> **(ii)** Compute the DOF of the integrated two-CSTR system.

#### **Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a)(i) – 4 marks:**
- $S = 3$ (Stream 1, Stream 2, Stream 3 outlet)
- $C = 2$ (water + NaCl)
- $N_V = S(C+2) = 3 \times 4 = 12$
- $N_E$ = 1 mass balance (overall) + 1 component balance (NaCl) + 1 energy balance = **3**
- $N_{knowns}$ = 2 inlet flow rates + 2 inlet temperatures = **4**
- $N_D = 12 - 3 - 4 = \boxed{5}$ DOF

*(Note: Correction — since NaCl + water is a 2-component system, the DOF is 5. The 5 DOFs are: 2 inlet pressures, 2 inlet NaCl compositions, 1 outlet pressure. Temperatures and flow rates are all determined by balances.)*

**Part (a)(ii) – 3 marks:**
The 5 DOF correspond to:
1. Pressure of Stream 1
2. Pressure of Stream 2
3. NaCl mass fraction of Stream 1
4. NaCl mass fraction of Stream 2
5. Pressure of Stream 3 (outlet)

**Part (b)(i) – 3 marks:**
For CSTR 1 alone:
- $S = 4$
- $C = 1$
- $N_V = 4(1+2) = 12$
- $N_E$ = 1 mass balance + 1 energy balance = **2**
- $N_{knowns}$ = feed F, T, P, $x_A$ + coolant $T_{in}$, $F_{in}$ = **6**
- $N_D = 12 - 2 - 6 = \boxed{4}$ DOF

**Part (b)(ii) – 4 marks:**
For the integrated system:
- $S_{total} = 4 + 4 - 1 = 7$ (subtract the shared stream counted twice)
- $N_V = 7 \times 3 = 21$
- $N_E$ = 2 mass + 2 energy = **4**
- $N_{knowns}$ = 6 (CSTR 1 feed) + 4 (CSTR 2 coolant in) = **10**
- $N_D = 21 - 4 - 10 = \boxed{7}$ DOF

### **Question B `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`**

> **(a)** Derive the general expression for Degrees of Freedom of a process stream containing $C$ components. Clearly identify each term. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** A distillation column separates a binary mixture (benzene-toluene, $C = 2$). It has 4 streams: feed, distillate, bottoms, and a reboiler steam supply. The feed flow rate, composition, temperature, and pressure are all specified. The bottoms composition is specified as a product specification. The reflux ratio is also fixed. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(i)** Compute the DOF.
>
> **(ii)** Based on the result, suggest the number of controllers required for full regulatory control.

#### **Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a) – 7 marks:**

**Derivation (full marks):**

A stream with $C$ components carries the following intensive variables:
- Total molar (or mass) flow rate: 1 variable
- Temperature: 1 variable
- Pressure: 1 variable
- Composition: $(C - 1)$ independent mole/mass fractions (the last is fixed by $\sum x_i = 1$)

Total per stream:

$$
N_{var/stream} = 1 + 1 + 1 + (C-1) = C + 2
$$

For $S$ streams:

$$
N_V = S(C+2)
$$

Independent equations consist of:
- $C$ material balance equations (one per component)
- 1 energy balance (per unit)
- $N_{eq}$ equilibrium/summation/spec equations

$$
N_E = C + 1 + N_{eq}
$$

Accounting for known variables:

$$
\boxed{N_D = S(C+2) - (C + 1 + N_{eq}) - N_{knowns}}
$$

**Part (b)(i) – 4 marks:**
- $S = 4$, $C = 2$
- $N_V = 4 \times 4 = 16$
- $N_E$ = 2 (mass balances) + 1 (energy) + 1 (summation $\sum y_i = 1$) + 1 (equilibrium on each stage — for a simple analysis, take 1 overall) = **5**
- $N_{knowns}$ = Feed $F, z, T_f, P_f$ + bottoms $x_B$ + reflux ratio $R$ = **6**
- $N_D = 16 - 5 - 6 = \boxed{5}$ DOF

**Part (b)(ii) – 3 marks:**
The 5 DOF correspond to the following independent variables that can be set as controlled variables:
1. Distillate composition $x_D$
2. Bottoms flow rate $B$
3. Distillate flow rate $D$
4. Reboiler steam flow rate
5. Condenser cooling water flow rate (or column pressure)

**5 single-loop controllers** are required for full regulatory control. Advanced control (e.g., dual composition control with temperature inferentials) can further enhance performance.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> **Top 3 places students lose marks on DOF problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting the "+2" for T and P** when counting stream variables. This alone causes a 2–4 mark penalty.
> 2. **Counting the shared stream twice** in multi-unit problems. Always subtract 1 stream per interconnection.
> 3. **Confusing DOF with number of control loops after pairing.** DOF is an *upper bound* on the number of loops; some DOF may be left as design specs without dedicated controllers.
> 4. **Ignoring equilibrium relations** in separation processes (VLE adds $C$ equations per equilibrium stage).
> 5. **Forgetting summation constraints** ($\sum x_i = 1$ gives 1 extra equation per stream).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Degrees of Freedom**

- **DOF** = minimum number of independent variables to fully specify a process.
- General formula: $N_D = S(C+2) - N_E - N_{knowns}$
- Each stream has **$C + 2$** intensive variables: $F$, $T$, $P$, and $(C-1)$ compositions.
- Per-unit equations include: $C$ mass balances, 1 energy balance, equilibrium relations, summation constraints.
- **$N_D > 0$** → Under-specified; add controllers or specs.
- **$N_D = 0$** → Exactly specified; ideal design.
- **$N_D < 0$** → Over-specified; physically infeasible; must redesign.
- **Number of controllers $\leq N_D$** (one controller per DOF maximum for single-loop control).
- **Multi-unit rule:** $N_{D,total} = \sum N_{D,i} - N_{links}$ where $N_{links}$ is the variables of shared streams.
- DOF must be computed **before** any control system synthesis or P&ID development.
- Always **start the problem with a clear stream and equation count** — examiners reward structured solutions.
- In reactive systems, include a **mole balance per species**; in separation systems, include **VLE relations**.
- Distinguish between **regulatory DOF** (setpoints) and **optimization DOF** (decision variables for economic operation).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Liquid-to-Liquid Heat Exchanger
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT743 - INDUSTRIAL PROCESS CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Liquid-to-Liquid Heat Exchanger**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Liquid-to-Liquid Heat Exchanger (LLHE):** A thermal process device specifically engineered to transfer sensible heat between two liquid process streams — a **hot liquid** and a **cold liquid** — without allowing the fluids to come into direct contact (i.e., without mixing). Heat transfer occurs through a separating wall (typically metallic), driven purely by a temperature gradient between the streams.

In the context of **PEICT743 – Industrial Process Control**, a liquid-to-liquid heat exchanger is treated as a **distributed-parameter, nonlinear, multivariable process unit** whose controlled variable is most commonly the **outlet temperature of one of the two liquids**, while manipulated variables include the **flow rate of the hot/cold stream** or the **bypass fraction**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping:** This topic appears under *Module 3 – Typical Industrial Processes and their Modelling*, where students are expected to derive dynamic models, steady-state models, and control-loop structures for shell-and-tube and double-pipe liquid-to-liquid heat exchangers.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are holding a cup of **hot tea** (≈ 80 °C) in your right hand and a cup of **cold milk** (≈ 10 °C) in your left hand. If you pour them into a single large vessel, they mix and reach a lukewarm equilibrium — that is **direct-contact mixing** and is *not* a heat exchanger.

Now imagine a **stainless steel double-walled thermos flask**: the inner wall holds the hot tea, the outer cavity holds the cold milk, and heat crosses the steel wall but the liquids never touch. The tea cools down, the milk warms up — that is exactly what a **liquid-to-liquid heat exchanger** does, just at an industrial scale (often handling thousands of kilograms per hour).

> [!TIP]
> **Student Intuition Trick:** Think of the metal wall as a **"heat bridge"** — it does not store heat (well, almost), it merely **conducts** it from the hot side to the cold side, exactly like a slide in a playground lets the child on top come down but the slide itself does not move.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following constants and dimensional groups are routinely used:

- **Stefan's constant** (not used in LLHE, but related): $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \, \mathrm{W/m^2K^4}$
- **Overall heat transfer coefficient (U) for liquid-liquid exchangers:** typically in the range **$150$ to $12{,}000 \, \mathrm{W/m^2 \cdot K}$**
- **Standard flow configurations:** Counter-flow and Parallel-flow
- **Design standard for shell-and-tube LLHE:** **TEMA** (Tubular Exchanger Manufacturers Association)

## 1.4 Why Liquid-to-Liquid Exchangers are Central to Process Control

Liquid streams dominate chemical, petrochemical, refinery, and HVAC plants. Controlling the outlet temperature of an LLHE is a canonical **Single-Input Single-Output (SISO)** control problem — yet it exhibits **inverse response**, **nonlinear gain**, and **transport delay**, making it a preferred topic in KTU 2024 evaluation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Liquid-to-Liquid Heat Exchangers

For KTU 2024, the following classification is considered high-yield:

1. **Double-Pipe (Concentric Pipe) LLHE** — used for small flow rates; easiest to model.
2. **Shell-and-Tube LLHE** — the industrial workhorse; shell side + tube side.
3. **Plate-Type LLHE** — gasketed plates; high U-value; compact.
4. **Spiral LLHE** — used for slurries and fouling fluids.

## 2.2 Operating Modes (Flow Arrangement)

- **Parallel Flow (Co-current):** Both liquids enter at the same end, flow in the same direction.
- **Counter Flow (Counter-current):** Liquids enter at opposite ends — **most efficient** arrangement and the one most often analyzed in KTU problems.
- **Cross Flow:** One fluid flows perpendicular to the other (plate or shell-and-tube multi-pass).

## 2.3 The Two Fundamental Governing Principles

### Principle 1 — Energy Balance (1st Law of Thermodynamics)

For steady state, no heat loss to the surroundings, and no phase change:

$$Q = \dot{m}_h \, c_{p,h} \, (T_{h,\text{in}} - T_{h,\text{out}})$$

$$Q = \dot{m}_c \, c_{p,c} \, (T_{c,\text{out}} - T_{c,\text{in}})$$

where:

- $Q$ = **rate of heat transfer** (W or kW)
- $\dot{m}$ = mass flow rate (kg/s)
- $c_p$ = specific heat capacity (J/kg·K)
- Subscripts $h$ and $c$ denote the **hot** and **cold** streams respectively.

### Principle 2 — Rate Equation (Fourier's Law applied to the wall)

$$Q = U \, A \, \Delta T_{lm}$$

where:

- $U$ = **overall heat transfer coefficient** (W/m²·K)
- $A$ = effective heat transfer area (m²)
- $\Delta T_{lm}$ = **log mean temperature difference** (K)

## 2.4 Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD) — Counter-Flow

$$\Delta T_{lm, \text{CF}} = \frac{(T_{h,\text{in}} - T_{c,\text{out}}) - (T_{h,\text{out}} - T_{c,\text{in}})}{\ln \left( \dfrac{T_{h,\text{in}} - T_{c,\text{out}}}{T_{h,\text{out}} - T_{c,\text{in}}} \right)}$$

## 2.5 LMTD — Parallel-Flow

$$\Delta T_{lm, \text{PF}} = \frac{(T_{h,\text{in}} - T_{c,\text{in}}) - (T_{h,\text{out}} - T_{c,\text{out}})}{\ln \left( \dfrac{T_{h,\text{in}} - T_{c,\text{in}}}{T_{h,\text{out}} - T_{c,\text{out}}} \right)}$$

## 2.6 Effectiveness–NTU Method

When outlet temperatures are unknown (rating problem), the **ε–NTU** method is preferred.

- **Heat capacity rate ratio:** $C_r = \dfrac{C_{\min}}{C_{\max}}$
- **Number of Transfer Units:** $\text{NTU} = \dfrac{U A}{C_{\min}}$
- **Maximum possible heat transfer:** $Q_{\max} = C_{\min} \, (T_{h,\text{in}} - T_{c,\text{in}})$

**Effectiveness equations (high-yield for KTU):**

Counter-flow:

$$\varepsilon_{\text{CF}} = \frac{1 - \exp\!\left[-\text{NTU}\,(1 - C_r)\right]}{1 - C_r \, \exp\!\left[-\text{NTU}\,(1 - C_r)\right]}$$

Parallel-flow:

$$\varepsilon_{\text{PF}} = \frac{1 - \exp\!\left[-\text{NTU}\,(1 + C_r)\right]}{1 + C_r}$$

Then:

$$Q = \varepsilon \, Q_{\max} = \varepsilon \, C_{\min} \, (T_{h,\text{in}} - T_{c,\text{in}})$$

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use the following reference table for fast revision. Note the use of `\vert` instead of vertical bars to protect Markdown table integrity.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Heat duty (hot side) | $Q = \dot{m}_h c_{p,h} (T_{h,\text{in}} - T_{h,\text{out}})$ | W | Sensible heat, no phase change |
| 2 | Heat duty (cold side) | $Q = \dot{m}_c c_{p,c} (T_{c,\text{out}} - T_{c,\text{in}})$ | W | Energy balance check |
| 3 | Overall rate | $Q = U A \Delta T_{lm}$ | W | Steady-state design equation |
| 4 | LMTD Counter-Flow | $\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | K | $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,\text{in}} - T_{c,\text{out}}$ |
| 5 | LMTD Parallel-Flow | $\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | K | $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,\text{in}} - T_{c,\text{in}}$ |
| 6 | Capacity rate ratio | $C_r = C_{\min} / C_{\max}$ | dimensionless | Range $0 \le C_r \le 1$ |
| 7 | NTU | $\text{NTU} = U A / C_{\min}$ | dimensionless | $> 0$ |
| 8 | Effectiveness counter-flow | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{1 - \exp[-\text{NTU}(1 - C_r)]}{1 - C_r \exp[-\text{NTU}(1 - C_r)]}$ | dimensionless | As $C_r \to 0$, $\varepsilon \to 1 - e^{-\text{NTU}}$ |
| 9 | Effectiveness parallel-flow | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{1 - \exp[-\text{NTU}(1 + C_r)]}{1 + C_r}$ | dimensionless | Always $< 1$ |
| 10 | $\Delta T_m$ correction | $\Delta T_m = F_T \cdot \Delta T_{lm,\text{CF}}$ | K | $F_T$ correction factor for multi-pass |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Refinery pre-heat trains** — recovering heat from crude oil hot streams to warm incoming crude before the furnace → saves millions of rupees in fuel cost.
- **District cooling/heating networks** — chilled water/glycol loops are LLHE systems.
- **Food and dairy industry** — pasteurization of milk uses plate LLHE.
- **Power plant condensers** — though typically condensing steam, the heating of cooling water is essentially liquid-to-liquid.
- **Jacketed reactors** — modeled as a 1-D distributed parameter LLHE.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the LMTD Expression (Counter-Flow)

Consider an elemental area $dA$ along the exchanger length. The hot stream temperature drops by $dT_h$ and the cold stream temperature rises by $dT_c$.

**Step 1 — Energy balance on the element**

Heat transferred across element $dA$:

$$dQ = -\dot{m}_h c_{p,h} \, dT_h = \dot{m}_c c_{p,c} \, dT_c$$

> The negative sign on $dT_h$ indicates the hot stream is losing energy (cooling down).

**Step 2 — Rate equation for the element**

$$dQ = U \, (T_h - T_c) \, dA$$

**Step 3 — Forming the differential relation**

$$U \, (T_h - T_c) \, dA = -\dot{m}_h c_{p,h} \, dT_h$$

**Step 4 — Substituting the energy balance for the cold side**

The temperature difference:

$$\Delta T = T_h - T_c$$

$$d(\Delta T) = dT_h - dT_c$$

From the energy balances:

$$dT_h = -\frac{dQ}{\dot{m}_h c_{p,h}}$$

$$dT_c = \frac{dQ}{\dot{m}_c c_{p,c}}$$

Therefore:

$$d(\Delta T) = -\frac{dQ}{\dot{m}_h c_{p,h}} - \frac{dQ}{\dot{m}_c c_{p,c}} = -dQ \left( \frac{1}{C_h} + \frac{1}{C_c} \right)$$

Where $C_h = \dot{m}_h c_{p,h}$ and $C_c = \dot{m}_c c_{p,c}$.

**Step 5 — Combining with rate equation**

Using $dQ = U \Delta T \, dA$:

$$\frac{d(\Delta T)}{-dQ} = \frac{d(\Delta T)}{-U \Delta T \, dA} = \frac{1}{C_h} + \frac{1}{C_c}$$

$$-\frac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} = U \left( \frac{1}{C_h} + \frac{1}{C_c} \right) dA$$

**Step 6 — Integrating over the full exchanger**

Integrating from $0$ to $A$, and from $\Delta T_1$ (inlet) to $\Delta T_2$ (outlet):

$$-\int_{\Delta T_1}^{\Delta T_2} \frac{d(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} = U \left( \frac{1}{C_h} + \frac{1}{C_c} \right) \int_0^A dA$$

$$\ln \left( \frac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2} \right) = U A \left( \frac{1}{C_h} + \frac{1}{C_c} \right)$$

**Step 7 — Solving for Q**

$$Q = C_h (T_{h,\text{in}} - T_{h,\text{out}}) = C_c (T_{c,\text{out}} - T_{c,\text{in}})$$

After algebraic rearrangement (eliminating $C_h$ and $C_c$ from the energy balance):

$$Q = U A \cdot \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)} = U A \, \Delta T_{lm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** The LMTD formula arises naturally from the assumption of **constant U** and **constant $c_p$** along the length.

## 3.2 Derivation of Effectiveness for Counter-Flow Exchanger

We define:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{Q}{Q_{\max}} = \frac{C_h (T_{h,\text{in}} - T_{h,\text{out}})}{C_{\min} (T_{h,\text{in}} - T_{c,\text{in}})}$$

**Step 1 — Case A:** $C_h = C_{\min}$ (hot stream is the minimum)

Then $C_c = C_{\max}$ and $C_r = C_{\min} / C_{\max}$.

**Step 2 — Energy balance rewritten in terms of $\varepsilon$**

$$T_{h,\text{out}} = T_{h,\text{in}} - \varepsilon (T_{h,\text{in}} - T_{c,\text{in}})$$

$$T_{c,\text{out}} = T_{c,\text{in}} + \varepsilon \frac{C_{\min}}{C_{\max}} (T_{h,\text{in}} - T_{c,\text{in}})$$

**Step 3 — Plug into LMTD-based rate equation**

$$Q = U A \, \Delta T_{lm}$$

$$C_{\min} (T_{h,\text{in}} - T_{c,\text{in}}) \varepsilon = U A \, \Delta T_{lm}$$

After substituting $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,\text{in}} - T_{c,\text{out}}$ and $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,\text{out}} - T_{c,\text{in}}$ in terms of $\varepsilon$ and simplifying, one obtains:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{1 - \exp\!\left[-\text{NTU}\,(1 - C_r)\right]}{1 - C_r \, \exp\!\left[-\text{NTU}\,(1 - C_r)\right]}$$

**Step 4 — Special case $C_r \to 0$**

When $C_r = 0$, applying L'Hôpital's rule:

$$\varepsilon = 1 - e^{-\text{NTU}}$$

This corresponds to the well-known formula for a condenser or an exchanger with one stream undergoing a very large flow rate (constant temperature).

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Counter-Flow)

**Given:**

- Hot oil: $\dot{m}_h = 2 \, \mathrm{kg/s}$, $c_{p,h} = 2100 \, \mathrm{J/kg \cdot K}$, $T_{h,\text{in}} = 150 \, ^\circ\mathrm{C}$
- Cold water: $\dot{m}_c = 1 \, \mathrm{kg/s}$, $c_{p,c} = 4180 \, \mathrm{J/kg \cdot K}$, $T_{c,\text{in}} = 30 \, ^\circ\mathrm{C}$
- $U A = 12{,}000 \, \mathrm{W/K}$

**Step 1 — Compute capacity rates**

$$C_h = 2 \times 2100 = 4200 \, \mathrm{W/K}$$

$$C_c = 1 \times 4180 = 4180 \, \mathrm{W/K}$$

Therefore $C_{\min} = 4180$, $C_{\max} = 4200$, $C_r = 4180/4200 = 0.9952$.

**Step 2 — Compute NTU**

$$\text{NTU} = \frac{U A}{C_{\min}} = \frac{12000}{4180} = 2.871$$

**Step 3 — Compute effectiveness**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{1 - \exp[-2.871(1 - 0.9952)]}{1 - 0.9952 \cdot \exp[-2.871(1 - 0.9952)]}$$

Calculate $1 - C_r = 0.00476$ and $\text{NTU}(1 - C_r) = 2.871 \times 0.00476 = 0.01367$:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{1 - e^{-0.01367}}{1 - 0.9952 \cdot e^{-0.01367}}$$

$$\varepsilon = \frac{1 - 0.98642}{1 - 0.9952 \times 0.98642} = \frac{0.01358}{1 - 0.98178} = \frac{0.01358}{0.01822}$$

$$\varepsilon = 0.7453$$

**Step 4 — Compute actual heat duty**

$$Q = \varepsilon \, C_{\min} (T_{h,\text{in}} - T_{c,\text{in}}) = 0.7453 \times 4180 \times (150 - 30)$$

$$Q = 0.7453 \times 4180 \times 120 = 373{,}843 \, \mathrm{W} \approx 373.8 \, \mathrm{kW}$$

**Step 5 — Compute outlet temperatures**

$$T_{h,\text{out}} = 150 - \frac{Q}{C_h} = 150 - \frac{373843}{4200} = 150 - 89.01 = 60.99 \, ^\circ\mathrm{C}$$

$$T_{c,\text{out}} = 30 + \frac{Q}{C_c} = 30 + \frac{373843}{4180} = 30 + 89.44 = 119.44 \, ^\circ\mathrm{C}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Observation:** The cold water leaves at $119.4 \, ^\circ\mathrm{C}$ — a crossover-like scenario! In a true counter-flow this is permissible because the two streams are never in contact.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — How to Identify the KTU Problem Type

| KTU Clue in Question | Recommended Method | Reason |
|----------------------|--------------------|--------|
| "Design the area" or "find A" | LMTD method | Outlet temperatures given |
| "Rate the exchanger" (UA known, find Q) | ε–NTU method | Outlet temperatures not yet known |
| "Compare counter vs parallel" | Both LMTDs, same Q | ΔT_lm counter > ΔT_lm parallel |
| "Process control" / "dynamic" | First-order + dead-time model | $G(s) = K e^{-\theta s}/(\tau s + 1)$ |
| "C_r = 1" or "balanced flow" | Limit case formula | $\varepsilon = \text{NTU}/(1+\text{NTU})$ counter-flow |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Counter-Flow vs Parallel-Flow — Temperature Profile

The following Mermaid diagram shows the **Block-Level Functional Architecture** of the heat exchanger as a process control object — including the input, output, disturbances, and control loop.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Hot Liquid Stream Inlet]:::hot
    B[Cold Liquid Stream Inlet]:::cold
    C[Heat Exchanger Vessel]:::vessel
    D[Hot Liquid Stream Outlet]:::hot
    E[Cold Liquid Stream Outlet]:::cold
    F[Temperature Sensor TC]:::sensor
    G[PID Controller]:::controller
    H[Control Valve on Cold Bypass]:::valve
    I[Disturbance: Hot Inlet Temp Variation]:::dist

    A --> C
    B --> C
    C --> D
    C --> E
    E --> F
    F --> G
    G --> H
    H --> B
    I --> A
    F --> G

    classDef hot fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#b30000,color:#000
    classDef cold fill:#cce6ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef vessel fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806000,color:#000
    classDef sensor fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366,color:#000
    classDef controller fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf,color:#000
    classDef valve fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#9673a6,color:#000
    classDef dist fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Counter-Flow Schematic (Block-Level View)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1[Hot In at T_hi] -->|Flow direction| X1[Element dA]
    A2[Cold In at T_ci] -->|Opposite direction| X1
    X1 --> A3[Hot Out at T_ho]
    X1 --> A4[Cold Out at T_co]

    B1[Energy Balance Hot] -.-> X1
    B2[Energy Balance Cold] -.-> X1
    B3[Rate Equation Q = U dA dT] -.-> X1

    classDef inlet fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#990000
    classDef outlet fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#003366
    classDef logic fill:#fff5cc,stroke:#806600
    class A1,A2 inlet
    class A3,A4 outlet
    class B1,B2,B3 logic
```

## 4.3 Process Control Loop for Outlet Temperature

```mermaid
graph TD
    SP[Setpoint T_sp]:::set --> SUM{Summing Junction}:::sum
    PV[Process Variable T_c_out measured]:::pv --> SUM
    SUM -->|Error e t| CTRL[PID Controller]:::ctrl
    CTRL -->|Output u t| VALVE[Control Valve on Cold Flow]:::valve
    VALVE -->|Manipulated m_c| PLANT[Liquid-to-Liquid Heat Exchanger]:::plant
    PLANT --> PV
    DIST[Disturbance: T_h_in variation]:::dist --> PLANT

    classDef set fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806000
    classDef sum fill:#f5f5f5,stroke:#333333
    classDef pv fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#6c8ebf
    classDef ctrl fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#82b366
    classDef valve fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#9673a6
    classDef plant fill:#ffe6cc,stroke:#d79b00
    classDef dist fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#b85450
```

## 4.4 Heat Transfer Path (Wall-Conduction View)

```mermaid
graph LR
    H1[Hot Bulk Fluid] -->|Convective h_h| W1[Tube Wall Inner Surface]
    W1 -->|Conduction k| W2[Tube Wall Outer Surface]
    W2 -->|Convective h_c| C1[Cold Bulk Fluid]

    classDef fluid fill:#cce6ff,stroke:#003366
    classDef wall fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806000
    class H1,C1 fluid
    class W1,W2 wall
```

The wall-resistance diagram translates into the standard overall coefficient:

$$\frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_h} + \frac{\delta}{k} + \frac{1}{h_c} + R_{f,h} + R_{f,c}$$

where $\delta$ is the wall thickness, $k$ is wall thermal conductivity, and $R_f$ are the fouling resistances.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]

**Q:** Define **log mean temperature difference (LMTD)** and state why counter-flow heat exchangers generally yield a higher LMTD than parallel-flow exchangers for the same inlet and outlet temperatures.

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **LMTD Definition:** The Log Mean Temperature Difference is the *effective* constant temperature difference that, when multiplied by $U A$, gives the actual heat transfer rate:
> $$\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$$

**Why counter-flow is higher:** In counter-flow, the hot outlet meets the cold inlet (the smaller terminal $\Delta T$), and the hot inlet meets the cold outlet (the larger terminal $\Delta T$). For identical terminal values, the logarithmic mean is always larger in counter-flow. The physical intuition is that **counter-flow maintains a more uniform driving force** along the length, whereas parallel-flow has a "pinch" near the outlet where both temperatures approach the same value.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]

**Q:** What is meant by **heat exchanger effectiveness (ε)**? For a counter-flow exchanger with $C_r = 0$, derive the effectiveness expression.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Effectiveness is the ratio of actual heat transferred to the **maximum possible** heat transfer:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{Q_{\text{actual}}}{Q_{\max}} = \frac{Q_{\text{actual}}}{C_{\min}(T_{h,\text{in}} - T_{c,\text{in}})}$$

**Derivation for $C_r = 0$:** As $C_{\min} \ll C_{\max}$, the maximum stream's outlet temperature is approximately its inlet, so $\Delta T$ varies from $(T_{h,\text{in}} - T_{c,\text{in}})$ at one end to $(T_{h,\text{out}} - T_{c,\text{in}})$ at the other. Substituting into $Q = U A \Delta T_{lm}$ and simplifying:

$$\varepsilon = 1 - e^{-\text{NTU}}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper]**

A counter-flow shell-and-tube heat exchanger is used to cool oil using water.

**Data:**

- Hot oil: $\dot{m}_h = 1.5 \, \mathrm{kg/s}$, $c_{p,h} = 2000 \, \mathrm{J/kg \cdot K}$, $T_{h,\text{in}} = 130 \, ^\circ\mathrm{C}$
- Cold water: $\dot{m}_c = 1.0 \, \mathrm{kg/s}$, $c_{p,c} = 4180 \, \mathrm{J/kg \cdot K}$, $T_{c,\text{in}} = 25 \, ^\circ\mathrm{C}$
- Overall coefficient: $U = 850 \, \mathrm{W/m^2 \cdot K}$
- Heat exchanger area: $A = 8 \, \mathrm{m^2}$

**(a) [7 Marks]** Determine the rate of heat transfer, the outlet temperatures of both streams, and the LMTD.

**(b) [7 Marks]** If the hot oil inlet temperature suddenly rises by **10 °C** (disturbance), find the new steady-state outlet temperature of the water. Comment briefly on the dynamic response expected in practice.

---

### **Model Solution for Question A**

**Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Capacity rates**

$$C_h = 1.5 \times 2000 = 3000 \, \mathrm{W/K}$$

$$C_c = 1.0 \times 4180 = 4180 \, \mathrm{W/K}$$

$$C_{\min} = 3000, \quad C_{\max} = 4180, \quad C_r = 3000/4180 = 0.7177$$

**Step 2 — NTU**

$$\text{NTU} = \frac{U A}{C_{\min}} = \frac{850 \times 8}{3000} = 2.2667$$

**Step 3 — Effectiveness**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{1 - \exp\!\left[-2.2667(1 - 0.7177)\right]}{1 - 0.7177 \exp\!\left[-2.2667(1 - 0.7177)\right]}$$

Calculate $1 - C_r = 0.2823$ and $\text{NTU}(1 - C_r) = 2.2667 \times 0.2823 = 0.6400$:

$$e^{-0.64} = 0.5273$$

$$\varepsilon = \frac{1 - 0.5273}{1 - 0.7177 \times 0.5273} = \frac{0.4727}{1 - 0.3785} = \frac{0.4727}{0.6215}$$

$$\varepsilon = 0.7605$$

**Step 4 — Heat duty**

$$Q = \varepsilon \cdot C_{\min} \cdot (T_{h,\text{in}} - T_{c,\text{in}}) = 0.7605 \times 3000 \times (130 - 25)$$

$$Q = 0.7605 \times 3000 \times 105 = 239{,}558 \, \mathrm{W} \approx 239.6 \, \mathrm{kW}$$

**Step 5 — Outlet temperatures**

$$T_{h,\text{out}} = 130 - \frac{Q}{C_h} = 130 - \frac{239558}{3000} = 130 - 79.85 = 50.15 \, ^\circ\mathrm{C}$$

$$T_{c,\text{out}} = 25 + \frac{Q}{C_c} = 25 + \frac{239558}{4180} = 25 + 57.31 = 82.31 \, ^\circ\mathrm{C}$$

**Step 6 — LMTD verification**

$$\Delta T_1 = T_{h,\text{in}} - T_{c,\text{out}} = 130 - 82.31 = 47.69 \, ^\circ\mathrm{C}$$

$$\Delta T_2 = T_{h,\text{out}} - T_{c,\text{in}} = 50.15 - 25 = 25.15 \, ^\circ\mathrm{C}$$

$$\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{47.69 - 25.15}{\ln(47.69 / 25.15)} = \frac{22.54}{\ln(1.8963)} = \frac{22.54}{0.6399} = 35.22 \, ^\circ\mathrm{C}$$

**Check:** $U A \Delta T_{lm} = 850 \times 8 \times 35.22 = 239{,}496 \approx 239.6 \, \mathrm{kW}$ ✓

---

**Part (b):**

The hot oil inlet now becomes $T_{h,\text{in}}' = 140 \, ^\circ\mathrm{C}$, all other data unchanged. $C_r$ and NTU are unchanged, so $\varepsilon = 0.7605$ remains the same.

$$Q' = 0.7605 \times 3000 \times (140 - 25) = 0.7605 \times 3000 \times 115 = 262{,}373 \, \mathrm{W}$$

$$T_{c,\text{out}}' = 25 + \frac{262373}{4180} = 25 + 62.77 = 87.77 \, ^\circ\mathrm{C}$$

**Increase in water outlet temperature:** $\Delta T_{c,\text{out}} = 87.77 - 82.31 = 5.46 \, ^\circ\mathrm{C}$.

**Comment on dynamic response:** The disturbance is a **step** change in inlet hot stream temperature. In practice, the cold outlet temperature does not respond instantly — it exhibits:

1. A **transport delay (dead time) θ** equal to the cold-stream residence time inside the exchanger.
2. A **first-order lag** with time constant $\tau \approx \frac{\rho_c V}{C_c}$ where $V$ is the cold-side hold-up.

The transfer function between disturbance and controlled output is therefore of the form:

$$G_d(s) = \frac{K_d \, e^{-\theta s}}{\tau s + 1}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Question A:** Students often confuse the **counter-flow terminal assignment** of $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$. Always remember:
> - In counter-flow: $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,\text{in}} - T_{c,\text{out}}$ and $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,\text{out}} - T_{c,\text{in}}$.
> - In parallel-flow: $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,\text{in}} - T_{c,\text{in}}$ and $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,\text{out}} - T_{c,\text{out}}$.
> Mixing these up costs **2 marks** routinely. Also, do not forget to convert the natural log argument — common slip.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

A liquid-to-liquid heat exchanger is used to heat a process stream. The following data are given:

- Cold stream inlet: $T_{c,\text{in}} = 40 \, ^\circ\mathrm{C}$, $\dot{m}_c c_{p,c} = 5000 \, \mathrm{W/K}$
- Hot stream inlet: $T_{h,\text{in}} = 200 \, ^\circ\mathrm{C}$, $C_h = 3500 \, \mathrm{W/K}$
- Required cold stream outlet: $T_{c,\text{out}} = 120 \, ^\circ\mathrm{C}$
- Overall heat transfer coefficient: $U = 1000 \, \mathrm{W/m^2 \cdot K}$

**(a) [7 Marks]** Using the LMTD method, calculate the **required heat transfer area** for both (i) a counter-flow arrangement and (ii) a parallel-flow arrangement.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Comment on the control strategy if the disturbance is the **hot-stream inlet temperature** $T_{h,\text{in}}$. Suggest a suitable manipulated variable, a controlled variable, and the type of controller.

---

### **Model Solution for Question B**

**Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Heat duty from cold stream energy balance**

$$Q = C_c (T_{c,\text{out}} - T_{c,\text{in}}) = 5000 \times (120 - 40) = 400{,}000 \, \mathrm{W} = 400 \, \mathrm{kW}$$

**Step 2 — Hot stream outlet temperature**

$$T_{h,\text{out}} = T_{h,\text{in}} - \frac{Q}{C_h} = 200 - \frac{400000}{3500} = 200 - 114.29 = 85.71 \, ^\circ\mathrm{C}$$

**Step 3 — Counter-flow LMTD**

$$\Delta T_1^{\text{CF}} = T_{h,\text{in}} - T_{c,\text{out}} = 200 - 120 = 80 \, ^\circ\mathrm{C}$$

$$\Delta T_2^{\text{CF}} = T_{h,\text{out}} - T_{c,\text{in}} = 85.71 - 40 = 45.71 \, ^\circ\mathrm{C}$$

$$\Delta T_{lm}^{\text{CF}} = \frac{80 - 45.71}{\ln(80/45.71)} = \frac{34.29}{\ln(1.7501)} = \frac{34.29}{0.5596} = 61.27 \, ^\circ\mathrm{C}$$

**Step 4 — Counter-flow area**

$$A_{\text{CF}} = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{lm}^{\text{CF}}} = \frac{400000}{1000 \times 61.27} = 6.53 \, \mathrm{m^2}$$

**Step 5 — Parallel-flow LMTD**

$$\Delta T_1^{\text{PF}} = T_{h,\text{in}} - T_{c,\text{in}} = 200 - 40 = 160 \, ^\circ\mathrm{C}$$

$$\Delta T_2^{\text{PF}} = T_{h,\text{out}} - T_{c,\text{out}} = 85.71 - 120 = -34.29 \, ^\circ\mathrm{C}$$

> [!WARNING]
> A **negative** $\Delta T_2$ in parallel-flow signals that the streams have **crossed over** — physically permissible but indicates that parallel-flow is unsuitable for this duty.

The absolute value must be used in the formula (in fact, parallel-flow is not feasible here because the cold outlet exceeds the hot outlet):

$$A_{\text{PF}} \to \text{Infeasible (crossover condition)}$$

This **demonstrates a key advantage of counter-flow**: it can achieve outlet temperatures that cross over the inlet temperatures, whereas parallel-flow cannot.

**Step 6 — If we hypothetically constrained the hot stream so that $T_{h,\text{out}} = 150 \, ^\circ\mathrm{C}$ (i.e., Q = $3500 \times 50 = 175,000$ W), then:**

$$\Delta T_1^{\text{PF}} = 160, \quad \Delta T_2^{\text{PF}} = 150 - 120 = 30$$

$$\Delta T_{lm}^{\text{PF}} = \frac{160 - 30}{\ln(160/30)} = \frac{130}{\ln(5.333)} = \frac{130}{1.6740} = 77.66 \, ^\circ\mathrm{C}$$

$$A_{\text{PF}} = \frac{175000}{1000 \times 77.66} = 2.253 \, \mathrm{m^2}$$

But this does not meet the $T_{c,\text{out}} = 120$ requirement! So in practice counter-flow with $A = 6.53 \, \mathrm{m^2}$ is the only feasible solution.

---

**Part (b):**

- **Disturbance:** Hot-stream inlet temperature $T_{h,\text{in}}$
- **Controlled Variable (CV):** Cold-stream outlet temperature $T_{c,\text{out}}$ (the most economically important variable, since the heated stream goes to the next process).
- **Manipulated Variable (MV):** Hot-stream flow rate $\dot{m}_h$ — increasing $\dot{m}_h$ adds more heat input.
- **Alternative MV:** Cold-stream bypass fraction around the exchanger (used in cascade or split-range schemes).
- **Type of Controller:** **PID** with the following typical tuning (Ziegler–Nichols or IMC-based):
  - Proportional band: ~50–100 °C
  - Integral time: ~30–60 s
  - Derivative time: small or zero (because of measurement noise and transport delay)

**Control structure:** A single-loop feedback structure is adequate for moderate disturbances. For large dead times, a **cascade control** (master: $T_{c,\text{out}}$; slave: $T_{h,\text{out}}$) or a **feedforward action** based on $T_{h,\text{in}}$ is preferred.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Question B:** Students often incorrectly include **bypassing fraction as a CV** or use the wrong side for the LMTD terminals. The LMTD formula is valid only for **pure counter-flow** and **pure parallel-flow**; for multi-pass shell-and-tube, a **correction factor $F_T$** (always $\le 1$) must multiply the counter-flow LMTD. Forgetting $F_T$ loses **3 marks** in the design part.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-density rapid-revision checklist for KTU 2024 LLHE problems:**

- **Two equations that solve 90% of LLHE problems:**
  - Energy balance: $Q = \dot{m} c_p \Delta T$
  - Rate equation: $Q = U A \Delta T_{lm}$

- **Three master formulas to memorize verbatim:**
  - LMTD: $\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$
  - NTU: $\text{NTU} = U A / C_{\min}$
  - Effectiveness counter-flow: $\varepsilon = \dfrac{1 - \exp[-\text{NTU}(1 - C_r)]}{1 - C_r \exp[-\text{NTU}(1 - C_r)]}$

- **Counter-flow always gives a higher (or equal) LMTD than parallel-flow** for the same terminal temperatures.

- **Parallel-flow cannot achieve a temperature cross** (i.e., $T_{c,\text{out}} > T_{h,\text{out}}$ is impossible). Counter-flow can.

- **The two streams never mix** — only the wall conducts heat. This is the defining feature of an LLHE versus a direct-contact heat exchanger.

- **Fouling resistance $R_f$** reduces $U$ over time. The design $U$ is the **clean** value divided by a fouling factor (typically 0.7–0.9).

- **Common KTU trick:** Whenever $C_r = 1$ (balanced flow), the counter-flow formula reduces to $\varepsilon = \text{NTU}/(1 + \text{NTU})$.

- **Dynamic model in Laplace domain:**
  $$G(s) = \frac{K \, e^{-\theta s}}{\tau s + 1}$$
  where $K = \frac{\partial T_{c,\text{out}}}{\partial \dot{m}_h}$ (process gain) and $\theta$ is the transport delay.

- **Units check:** All temperatures must be in **K** or all in **°C** — but differences must be in **K** for $U$ in W/m²·K. Never mix.

- **Two methods compared:**
  - LMTD → use when **outlet temperatures are known** (design problem).
  - ε–NTU → use when **UA is known** and you must find $Q$ (rating problem).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Control of Liquid-to-Liquid Heat Exchanger Using Two-Way Valve
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT743 - INDUSTRIAL PROCESS CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Control of Liquid-to-Liquid Heat Exchanger Using Two-Way Valve

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Liquid-to-Liquid Heat Exchanger:** A unit operation device that transfers thermal energy between two liquid process streams (or a process liquid and a utility liquid) without mixing them, operating under either **co-current** or **counter-current flow** configurations.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, the control of such a heat exchanger using a **Two-Way Valve (2-Way Control Valve)** refers to a **single-input single-output (SISO) feedback control loop** where the manipulated variable is the **mass flow rate of the utility stream** (cooling water, glycol, hot oil, etc.) entering the exchanger, regulated by a pneumatically, electrically, or hydraulically actuated throttling valve having exactly **two ports** (one inlet + one outlet).

The **Controlled Variable (CV)** is the **outlet temperature of the process stream**, the **Manipulated Variable (MV)** is the **utility flow rate** (set by valve stem position), and the **Load Variables (Disturbances)** are the **inlet temperature of process fluid, utility inlet temperature, and process flow rate**.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Domestic Geyser / Shower Mixer" Analogy** 🚿

Imagine a domestic instant water heater: cold water enters, and you want a **constant warm temperature** at the shower outlet. The heating element is fixed, but if someone opens a tap downstairs (disturbance), your flow surges and the water turns cold. To compensate, you turn a **knob** (the two-way valve) that lets **more hot utility water in** to maintain the temperature. The knob is the **two-way valve**, the shower temperature is the **controlled variable**, and the downstairs tap opening is the **load disturbance**. The control loop in an industrial heat exchanger works on **exactly this principle**, scaled up by a factor of thousands, with a **PID controller** replacing your hand.

### 1.3 Classification & Physical Constants

| Classification Axis | Category (this topic) |
|---|---|
| Heat Exchanger Type | **Shell-and-Tube (utility on tube side)** |
| Valve Type | **Equal Percentage / Linear Globe Valve, 2 ports** |
| Control Action | **Reverse-Acting Feedback (T-CV → u-MV)** |
| Control Mode | **PID (typically PI in practice)** |

Key physical constants used throughout this module:
- **Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient U:** typically **200 – 1500 W/m²K** for water-water exchangers
- **Specific Heat of Water:** **C_p = 4.186 kJ/(kg·K)**
- **Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD):** geometric mean driving force for counter-current flow

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic Decomposition (Bulleted Step Logic)

The control of a liquid-to-liquid heat exchanger using a two-way valve involves the following **seven-step process logic** which you must internalize:

- **Step 1 — Process Objective:** Maintain the process outlet temperature $T_{p,out}$ at setpoint despite upstream disturbances.
- **Step 2 — Sensor Measurement:** A **RTD / Thermocouple (TT)** in a thermowell measures $T_{p,out}$ and transmits a **4–20 mA / Fieldbus signal** to the controller.
- **Step 3 — Error Generation:** The controller computes error $e(t) = T_{sp} - T_{p,out}$.
- **Step 4 — PID Computation:** The PID algorithm produces controller output $u(t)$ (0–100%).
- **Step 5 — Valve Actuation:** $u(t)$ commands the **two-way valve stem position** $v(t)$, modulating the **flow area** $A_v$ and hence the utility flow $w_u(t)$.
- **Step 6 — Heat Transfer Realization:** The new utility flow changes the **log mean temperature difference (LMTD)** and the **overall heat transfer rate** $Q(t) = U A \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}$.
- **Step 7 — Process Response:** $T_{p,out}$ moves towards setpoint. The **negative feedback** closes the loop.

### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" — Engineering Rationale

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a two-way valve (and not a three-way)?** A two-way valve is preferred when the utility supply pressure is adequate, and the exchanger has a **fixed design flow** for the process side. It is **cheaper, has lower leakage, smaller dead band, and produces less pumping cost variation** compared to a three-way valve. A three-way valve is used when the utility flow must be **diverted** (split-range) or when the heat exchanger requires **constant flow on the process side** to prevent fouling.

**How it works physically:** A two-way valve is essentially a **variable orifice**. As the stem moves down, the plug lifts off the seat, increasing the **flow coefficient C_v**. The relationship between stem travel and flow is governed by the **installed characteristic** of the valve, which is typically chosen as **equal percentage** to compensate for the nonlinear process gain of the heat exchanger.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **exam-day bible** for this topic. Memorize the symbols, units, and conditions.

| # | Equation | Symbol Meaning | Units | KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = U A \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}$ | Heat transfer rate (steady) | **W** | Energy balance |
| 2 | $Q = m_p C_{pp} (T_{p,in} - T_{p,out})$ | Process side enthalpy drop | **W** | Process stream |
| 3 | $Q = w_u C_{pu} (T_{u,out} - T_{u,in})$ | Utility side enthalpy gain | **W** | Utility stream |
| 4 | $\Delta T_{LMTD} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | Log Mean Temp Diff | **K** | Counter-current |
| 5 | $C_v = \dfrac{w_u}{\sqrt{\Delta P_v / G_f}}$ | Valve flow coefficient | **gpm/psi½** (US) | Sizing |
| 6 | $w_u = K_v \cdot f(v) \sqrt{\Delta P_v}$ | Flow–valve relation | **kg/s** | Nonlinearity |
| 7 | $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K_p e^{-\theta s}}{(\tau_1 s + 1)(\tau_2 s + 1)}$ | FOPDT / SOPDT model | — | Controller tuning |
| 8 | $K_c = \dfrac{1}{K_p} \cdot \dfrac{\tau}{\theta}$ (IMC-PID) | Controller gain | — | Tuning |
| 9 | $f(x) = 1 / \vert \Delta T \vert$ | Process gain non-linearity | — | Compensator |
| 10 | $T_{u,out} = T_{p,out} + (T_{u,in} - T_{p,out}) e^{-UA/(w_u C_{pu})}$ | Counter-current utility out | **K** | Steady-state check |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** This control configuration is the **backbone of refinery preheat trains, dairy pasteurizers, pharma WFI (Water-For-Injection) loops, and HVAC chilled-water systems**. The same block diagram underpins thousands of installed loops in Kerala's industrial corridors (Cochin Petrochemicals, BPCL Kochi, MRF Kottayam).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Steady-State Energy Balance (Counter-Current Exchanger)

**Given:** A counter-current shell-and-tube heat exchanger. Process fluid flows on the shell side, utility on the tube side. Two-way valve controls utility flow $w_u$.

**Find:** The steady-state relationship between utility flow and process outlet temperature.

**Step 1:** Apply the **First Law of Thermodynamics** to the process side (no phase change, negligible KE/PE):

The rate of heat lost by the process fluid equals the rate of heat gained by the utility.

$$Q = m_p C_{pp} (T_{p,in} - T_{p,out}) = w_u C_{pu} (T_{u,out} - T_{u,in})$$

**Step 2:** Use the overall heat transfer rate equation through the exchanger wall:

$$Q = U A \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}$$

For counter-current flow, the LMTD is:

$$\Delta T_{LMTD} = \dfrac{(T_{p,in} - T_{u,out}) - (T_{p,out} - T_{u,in})}{\ln\left[\dfrac{T_{p,in} - T_{u,out}}{T_{p,out} - T_{u,in}}\right]}$$

**Step 3:** Introduce the **Number of Transfer Units (NTU)** as $\mathcal{N} = \dfrac{UA}{w_u C_{pu}}$ to simplify the algebra.

Using the standard effectiveness–NTU relation for counter-current flow with $C_r = \dfrac{w_u C_{pu}}{m_p C_{pp}} \neq 1$:

$$\varepsilon = \dfrac{1 - \exp[-\mathcal{N}(1 - C_r)]}{1 - C_r \exp[-\mathcal{N}(1 - C_r)]}$$

where $\varepsilon = \dfrac{Q}{Q_{max}} = \dfrac{T_{p,in} - T_{p,out}}{T_{p,in} - T_{u,in}}$.

**Step 4:** Solve for $T_{p,out}$ in terms of the manipulated variable $w_u$:

$$T_{p,out} = T_{p,in} - \varepsilon \cdot (T_{p,in} - T_{u,in})$$

Substituting $\varepsilon$:

$$T_{p,out} = T_{p,in} - \left(\dfrac{1 - \exp[-\mathcal{N}(1 - C_r)]}{1 - C_r \exp[-\mathcal{N}(1 - C_r)]}\right)(T_{p,in} - T_{u,in})$$

**Step 5 — Engineering Interpretation:** As $w_u \to \infty$, $\mathcal{N} \to \infty$, $\varepsilon \to 1$, and $T_{p,out} \to T_{u,in}$ (the lower bound on cooling). As $w_u \to 0$, $\mathcal{N} \to 0$, $\varepsilon \to 0$, and $T_{p,out} \to T_{p,in}$ (no cooling). This confirms the **monotonic but nonlinear** relationship, which justifies using an **equal-percentage valve characteristic** for linearization.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Linearized Dynamic Transfer Function

**Given:** A small perturbation $\Delta w_u$ around the steady-state operating point.

**Step 1:** Write the **dynamic** process-side energy balance, including the thermal capacitance of the process fluid, the wall, and the utility:

For the process fluid lumped capacitance:

$$m_p C_{pp} \dfrac{dT_{p,out}}{dt} = m_p C_{pp}(T_{p,in} - T_{p,out}) - Q(t)$$

For the metal wall (lumped):

$$M_w C_{pw} \dfrac{dT_w}{dt} = Q_{process} - Q_{utility}$$

For the utility side (no axial mixing assumption):

$$w_u C_{pu} \dfrac{dT_{u,out}}{dt} = w_u C_{pu}(T_{u,in} - T_{u,out}) + Q(t)$$

**Step 2:** Combine the wall equation and linearize the heat transfer term around operating point:

$$Q(t) = U A \left[T_{p,out} - T_{u,out}\right] \quad \text{(simplified linear driving force)}$$

Taking the Laplace transform (zero initial conditions) and defining deviations $\tilde{T}_{p,out}(s)$, $\tilde{T}_{u,out}(s)$, $\tilde{w}_u(s)$:

$$(m_p C_{pp} s) \tilde{T}_{p,out} = -(U A)[\tilde{T}_{p,out} - \tilde{T}_{u,out}]$$

$$(M_w C_{pw} s) \tilde{T}_w = U A[\tilde{T}_{p,out} - \tilde{T}_{u,out}] - U A[\tilde{T}_w - \tilde{T}_{u,out}]$$

$$(w_u C_{pu} s) \tilde{T}_{u,out} = (C_{pu} \Delta T_{u,ss}) \tilde{w}_u - w_u C_{pu}[\tilde{T}_{u,out}] + U A[\tilde{T}_w - \tilde{T}_{u,out}]$$

**Step 3:** Eliminate $\tilde{T}_w$ and $\tilde{T}_{u,out}$ to obtain a single-input single-output relation $\dfrac{\tilde{T}_{p,out}(s)}{\tilde{w}_u(s)}$.

After algebra (you may skip intermediate algebra in exam and quote the result), the SOPDT model is:

$$G_p(s) = \dfrac{\tilde{T}_{p,out}(s)}{\tilde{w}_u(s)} = \dfrac{K_p \, e^{-\theta s}}{(\tau_1 s + 1)(\tau_2 s + 1)}$$

where:

$$\tau_1, \tau_2 = \text{eigenvalues of the 2nd-order denominator} = \dfrac{a \pm \sqrt{a^2 - 4b}}{2}$$

with $a = \dfrac{1}{\tau_p} + \dfrac{1}{\tau_w} + \dfrac{1}{\tau_u}$ and $b = \dfrac{1}{\tau_p \tau_w} + \dfrac{1}{\tau_w \tau_u} + \dfrac{1}{\tau_p \tau_u} - \dfrac{1}{\tau_p \tau_u}$, where each $\tau_i$ is the individual time constant. The process gain $K_p = \dfrac{\partial T_{p,out}}{\partial w_u} \big\vert_{ss}$ is **negative** (more utility flow → lower process outlet temperature).

**Step 4 — Final Tuned Form (KTU exam-ready):**

$$\boxed{G_p(s) = \dfrac{K_p \, e^{-\theta s}}{(\tau_1 s + 1)(\tau_2 s + 1)}}$$

This is the **SOPDT with dead time** model used to apply **Ziegler-Nichols, Cohen-Coon, or IMC-PID tuning** rules. A two-way valve control loop on a heat exchanger is almost always tuned using **Lambda Tuning (IMC)** because the dead time is significant.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Operating Regimes

| Operating Regime | $w_u$ (kg/s) | $\mathcal{N}$ | $\varepsilon$ | $T_{p,out}$ Behavior | Control Loop Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Low Flow** | 0.5 | 0.8 | 0.42 | Slow drift, large dead time | **Sluggish**, high $\theta$ |
| **Design Point** | 1.2 | 2.0 | 0.71 | Setpoint achievable | **Best dynamics**, moderate $\theta$ |
| **High Flow** | 3.0 | 5.0 | 0.92 | Near $T_{u,in}$ (low bound) | **Fast, low gain**, valve saturates |
| **Saturation** | >5.0 | >8.0 | →1 | Asymptotic to $T_{u,in}$ | **Valve wide open**, no control |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Tip:** The "low-gain at high flow" regime is why **equal-percentage valves** are mandatory for heat-exchanger control. A linear valve would give too little sensitivity in the low-flow region (where most operation occurs).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Two-Way Valve Control Loop for Heat Exchanger

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the **functional feedback loop** (Setpoint → Controller → Valve → Process → Sensor → back to Controller), which is the architecture the KTU examiner expects you to draw in a 14-mark question.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP[Setpoint Tsp] --> SUM
    PV[Process Outlet Tp_out] --> SUM
    SUM{Error e} --> CTRL[PID Controller TC]
    CTRL --> I2P[I to P Converter]
    I2P --> VALVE2W[Two Way Valve Positioner]
    VALVE2W --> VLV[Two Way Throttling Valve]
    VLV --> HEX[Liquid to Liquid Heat Exchanger]
    HEX --> UTIL[Utility Flow wu]
    HEX --> PROCOUT[Process Outlet Tp_out]
    PROCOUT --> TT[TT Temperature Transmitter]
    TT --> PV
    HEX -. Disturbance .-> D1[Process Inlet Tp_in]
    HEX -. Disturbance .-> D2[Utility Inlet Tu_in]
    HEX -. Disturbance .-> D3[Process Flow mp]
    style HEX fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style VALVE2W fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style CTRL fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style TT fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 Process Flow Diagram (P&ID Style Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PROCESS_SIDE[Process Side]
        PROCIN[Process Inlet Tp_in mp] --> PROC1[Process Flow Nozzle]
        PROC1 --> HEX1[Shell Side of Heat Exchanger]
    end
    subgraph UTILITY_SIDE[Utility Side via 2 Way Valve]
        UTILIN[Utility Inlet Tu_in] --> PUMP[Pump]
        PUMP --> VALVE2WAY[2 Way Control Valve TV]
        VALVE2WAY --> HEX2[Tube Side of Heat Exchanger]
    end
    HEX1 --> PROCOUT[Process Outlet Tp_out to next unit]
    HEX2 --> UTILOUT[Utility Outlet Tu_out]
    TT1[TT on PROCOUT] -.-> CTRL1[TC Controller]
    CTRL1 -.-> VALVE2WAY
    style VALVE2WAY fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#1a5277,stroke-width:3px
    style TT1 fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#7b241c,stroke-width:2px
    style CTRL1 fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#196f3d,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.3 Signal Flow Graph (Laplace-Domain)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    WS[W_s Setpoint] --> N1((+))
    PVFB[T_p_out measured] --> N2((-))
    N1 --> N2
    N2 --> Gc[PID Gc s]
    Gc --> Gv[Valve Gv s]
    Gv --> Gp[Process Gp s]
    Gp --> Gd[Disturbance Gd s]
    Gp --> TPV[Output T_p_out]
    TPV --> H[Sensor H s]
    H --> PVFB
    style Gc fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Gv fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Gp fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Closed-Loop Transfer Function** (for 14-mark derivations):
> $$\dfrac{T_{p,out}(s)}{T_{sp}(s)} = \dfrac{G_c(s) G_v(s) G_p(s)}{1 + G_c(s) G_v(s) G_p(s) H(s)}$$
> where $H(s) = 1$ for an ideal transmitter. The characteristic equation $1 + G_c G_v G_p = 0$ defines **stability** via the Routh-Hurwitz or Bode stability criterion.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]**
**State the manipulated, controlled, and disturbance variables for a liquid-to-liquid heat exchanger with the utility flow controlled by a two-way valve.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Manipulated Variable (MV):** Utility flow rate $w_u$ (or equivalently valve stem position $v$)
- **Controlled Variable (CV):** Outlet temperature of the process stream $T_{p,out}$
- **Disturbances (DVs):** Process inlet temperature $T_{p,in}$, utility inlet temperature $T_{u,in}$, process flow rate $m_p$, ambient heat losses
*(1 mark for each correct identification with symbol.)*

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Why is an equal-percentage trim preferred over a linear trim for a two-way valve controlling heat-exchanger utility flow?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The process gain $K_p = \partial T_{p,out} / \partial w_u$ is **highly nonlinear** — it is large in magnitude at low utility flows (low $\mathcal{N}$) and small at high utility flows (high $\mathcal{N}$). A linear valve has constant $C_v$ vs. stem travel, which compounds the nonlinearity. An **equal-percentage trim** delivers an **exponentially increasing $C_v$**, which when combined with the inverse process characteristic gives a **near-constant installed loop gain** across 10–90% valve travel. This improves **turndown ratio** and tunability. *(1 mark process nonlinearity, 1 mark equal-percentage compensates, 1 mark resulting linearization.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3 Q4]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the closed-loop feedback control of a liquid-to-liquid heat exchanger using a two-way control valve. Identify all elements and signal types. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the steady-state relationship between utility flow $w_u$ and process outlet temperature $T_{p,out}$ for a counter-current shell-and-tube heat exchanger. Show the energy balance, LMTD definition, and the resulting nonlinear equation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block Diagram Explanation (7 marks):**
- Draw the **closed-loop block diagram** as in SECTION 4.1 (3 marks for correct elements).
- Describe each element: Setpoint, Error detector, **PID controller (TC)**, I/P converter, Valve positioner, **Two-way valve**, **Heat exchanger**, **RTD transmitter (TT)**, feedback path. (2 marks)
- Explain the action: **Reverse acting** — if $T_{p,out}$ rises above setpoint, controller output decreases, valve closes partially, utility flow drops, less heat removed, $T_{p,out}$ comes back down. (1 mark)
- Mention signal types: 4–20 mA current loop, 3–15 psi pneumatic, or HART/Fieldbus digital. (1 mark)

**(b) Steady-State Derivation (7 marks):**
- Write process-side energy balance: $Q = m_p C_{pp} (T_{p,in} - T_{p,out})$ (1 mark)
- Write utility-side energy balance: $Q = w_u C_{pu} (T_{u,out} - T_{u,in})$ (1 mark)
- Write overall heat transfer: $Q = U A \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}$ (1 mark)
- Define LMTD for counter-current: $\Delta T_{LMTD} = \dfrac{(T_{p,in} - T_{u,out}) - (T_{p,out} - T_{u,in})}{\ln\left[(T_{p,in} - T_{u,out})/(T_{p,out} - T_{u,in})\right]}$ (1 mark)
- Introduce $\mathcal{N} = UA/(w_u C_{pu})$ and $C_r = w_u C_{pu}/(m_p C_{pp})$ (1 mark)
- Write the effectiveness equation: $\varepsilon = \dfrac{1 - \exp[-\mathcal{N}(1 - C_r)]}{1 - C_r \exp[-\mathcal{N}(1 - C_r)]}$ (1 mark)
- Combine to get the final nonlinear relation $T_{p,out} = f(w_u)$ and discuss that as $w_u$ increases, $T_{p,out}$ decreases monotonically with **diminishing sensitivity** at high flow. (1 mark)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3 Q5]`

**(a)** Develop a **first-order-plus-dead-time (FOPDT)** dynamic model for the heat-exchanger loop, and explain how you would tune a PID controller using the **IMC (Lambda) tuning rule** for this loop. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A counter-current heat exchanger has $UA = 5000$ W/K, $m_p = 1.0$ kg/s, $C_{pp} = 4.0$ kJ/(kg·K), utility (water) inlet at 25 °C, process inlet at 90 °C, and design utility flow $w_u = 1.5$ kg/s. Compute the steady-state process outlet temperature. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) FOPDT + IMC Tuning (7 marks):**
- The dynamic model is: $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K_p e^{-\theta s}}{\tau s + 1}$ (approximation of SOPDT by dominant pole + dead time). (2 marks)
- Identify $K_p$ from step test (ΔT/Δw_u), $\tau$ from 63% response time, $\theta$ from initial dead time. (1 mark)
- The IMC-PID tuning rule: 
  - $K_c = \dfrac{\tau}{K_p(\theta + \lambda)}$
  - $\tau_I = \tau$
  - $\tau_D = 0$ (for FOPDT)
  - where $\lambda$ is the desired closed-loop time constant, typically $\lambda \geq 0.8 \theta$. (3 marks)
- Discussion: choose $\lambda = \theta$ for tight control, $\lambda = 3\theta$ for smooth control. The heat exchanger being a "sluggish" process benefits from a larger $\lambda$. (1 mark)

**(b) Numerical Steady-State Calculation (7 marks):**
- Compute $\mathcal{N} = \dfrac{UA}{w_u C_{pu}} = \dfrac{5000}{1.5 \times 4186} = 0.797$ (1 mark)
- Compute $C_r = \dfrac{w_u C_{pu}}{m_p C_{pp}} = \dfrac{1.5 \times 4.186}{1.0 \times 4.0} = 1.570$ (1 mark)
- Since $C_r > 1$, utility capacity > process capacity, and the **utility limits** the heat transfer. (1 mark)
- Effectiveness: 
  $$\varepsilon = \dfrac{1 - \exp[-\mathcal{N}(1 - C_r)]}{1 - C_r \exp[-\mathcal{N}(1 - C_r)]}$$
- Substituting $1 - C_r = -0.570$ and $\mathcal{N}(1 - C_r) = 0.797 \times (-0.570) = -0.454$: (1 mark)
  $$\varepsilon = \dfrac{1 - e^{0.454}}{1 - 1.570 \cdot e^{0.454}} = \dfrac{1 - 1.575}{1 - 1.570 \times 1.575} = \dfrac{-0.575}{-1.474} = 0.390$$ (1 mark)
- Therefore: $T_{p,out} = T_{p,in} - \varepsilon (T_{p,in} - T_{u,in}) = 90 - 0.390 \times (90 - 25) = 90 - 25.35 = 64.65 \,°C$ (2 marks)
- **Engineering check:** The outlet is between utility inlet (25 °C) and process inlet (90 °C). ✓ (negative $\varepsilon$ would indicate a sign error). (Bonus, optional)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (Avoid these to gain the full 14 marks!):**
> 1. **Confusing the two-way vs three-way valve block diagram** — A two-way valve has **only one inlet + one outlet** and varies flow, while a three-way has **three ports** and varies flow *direction/split*. Many students draw a three-way by mistake. **Memorize:** two-way = throttle; three-way = divert.
> 2. **Forgetting the negative sign in process gain** — $K_p$ is **negative** because increasing utility flow **decreases** process outlet temperature. A controller must be **reverse-acting**.
> 3. **Skipping the LMTD definition** — Examiners allocate at least 1–2 marks purely for writing the LMTD formula correctly with the **natural log**, not base-10.
> 4. **Forgetting the dead time in the dynamic model** — Heat-exchanger loops have **transport + measurement lag** which appears as dead time $\theta$. Omitting $e^{-\theta s}$ will lose 2 marks in any "derive the model" question.
> 5. **Units mismatch in numerical problems** — $UA$ must be in W/K, $C_p$ in J/(kg·K) — **not** kJ/(kg·K) — when $Q$ is in Watts. Convert carefully or you will get the wrong answer.
> 6. **Sign error in effectiveness equation** — When $C_r > 1$, the formula has $1 - C_r$ in the denominator, which is negative; do not panic. Just compute carefully.
> 7. **Drawing a P&ID instead of a block diagram** — The 7-mark question specifically asks for a **block diagram** (control-loop topology), not a piping drawing. Save the P&ID for the process-instrumentation elective.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this checklist 5 minutes before entering the exam hall:**

- ☐ **Heat exchanger type:** Shell-and-tube, utility on **tube side** (most common in KTU problems).
- ☐ **Two-way valve** = **throttling**, single inlet + single outlet, modulates flow $w_u$.
- ☐ **CV** = $T_{p,out}$ ; **MV** = $w_u$ (or $v$ = valve position); **DVs** = $T_{p,in}, T_{u,in}, m_p$.
- ☐ **Three energy balance equations:** process side, utility side, overall $Q = UA \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}$.
- ☐ **LMTD formula** uses the **natural log (ln)** of the ratio of end-temperature differences.
- ☐ **Effectiveness** $\varepsilon = Q/Q_{max} = (T_{p,in} - T_{p,out})/(T_{p,in} - T_{u,in})$.
- ☐ **NTU** $\mathcal{N} = UA/(w_u C_{pu})$ is the key dimensionless group.
- ☐ **Process gain $K_p < 0$** (reverse acting control, controller must be reverse-acting).
- ☐ **SOPDT model** $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K_p e^{-\theta s}}{(\tau_1 s + 1)(\tau_2 s + 1)}$ is the standard exam model.
- ☐ **Equal-percentage valve** is preferred for the **nonlinear** process gain.
- ☐ **IMC-PID tuning:** $K_c = \tau / [K_p (\theta + \lambda)]$, $\tau_I = \tau$, $\lambda \approx \theta$ to $3\theta$.
- ☐ **Disturbance rejection** in the two-way valve arrangement is good for $T_{u,in}$ changes; **less good** for $m_p$ changes (because the controller does not directly see $m_p$).
- ☐ **Closed-loop TF:** $\dfrac{G_c G_v G_p}{1 + G_c G_v G_p H}$; characteristic equation governs **stability** via Routh-Hurwitz.
- ☐ **Unit conversion:** $1$ kW = 1000 W; $C_p$(water) = 4186 J/(kg·K) = 4.186 kJ/(kg·K).
- ☐ **Sign convention:** Heat *removed* from process = heat *gained* by utility → $Q$ same magnitude both sides.
- ☐ **Counter-current gives higher LMTD** than co-current → preferred for sensitive control.
- ☐ **Safety:** If utility flow is lost (pump trip, valve failure), process outlet temperature will **shoot up** — this is a **critical loop** requiring low-low and high-high temperature interlocks (KSIL & KSSH).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Control of Liquid-to-Liquid Heat Exchanger Using Bypass Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT743 - INDUSTRIAL PROCESS CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Control of Liquid-to-Liquid Heat Exchanger Using Bypass Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition
**Bypass Control** of a liquid-to-liquid heat exchanger is a single-loop temperature control strategy in which the controlled (process) stream is split into two fractions — one fraction passes through the heat exchanger where heat exchange takes place with the utility liquid, while the other fraction is diverted through a **bypass line** that circumvents the exchanger. The two streams are re-mixed downstream, and a temperature transmitter (TT) measures the mixed outlet temperature, which is fed to a PID controller. The controller's output drives a control valve on the bypass line, adjusting the fraction of flow that bypasses the exchanger to maintain the desired outlet temperature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Controlled Variable (CV):** Outlet temperature of the process stream $T_o$ (after the mixing point)
> **Manipulated Variable (MV):** Bypass flow rate $F_b$ (or bypass fraction $f = F_b/F$)
> **Disturbance Variables (DVs):** Process inlet temperature $T_i$, Process inlet flow rate $F$, Utility inlet temperature $T_{u,in}$, Utility flow rate $F_u$

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine you are a chef preparing a large pot of soup that is too hot, and you want to bring it to serving temperature. You have a cooling bath (cold water). You have two options:
- **Option A (Throttling):** Slowly dip the whole pot into the cold bath — slow and uneven.
- **Option B (Bypass Control):** Split the soup into two streams: send one stream through the cold bath (it cools), keep the other stream as-is (still hot), then mix both streams back together. By adjusting how much soup you divert through the bath, you can precisely control the final temperature.

The **bypass line** is the path where soup "skips" the bath — analogous to our process stream skipping the heat exchanger. The **mixing point** is where the cooled and uncooled streams combine. This gives much faster, smoother control than trying to cool the whole stream at once.

> [!NOTE]
> In a **liquid-to-liquid** heat exchanger, both streams are liquids (no phase change). The heat exchange is purely **sensible heating or cooling**. This makes bypass control particularly effective because mixing two liquid streams is straightforward and the dynamics are reasonably linear.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Operational Concept Breakdown
The bypass control scheme operates on a few core principles:

1. **Flow Splitting:** The total process flow $F$ is divided at a tee-junction:
   - $F_e$ flows through the heat exchanger
   - $F_b$ flows through the bypass line
   - $F = F_e + F_b$ (mass conservation)

2. **Heat Transfer in the Exchanger:** Only $F_e$ experiences the temperature change from $T_i$ (inlet) to $T_e$ (exchanger outlet).

3. **Recombination & Mixing:** Downstream, the two streams mix perfectly. The mixed outlet temperature $T_o$ is a flow-weighted average of $T_e$ and $T_i$.

4. **Feedback Loop:** $T_o$ is measured, compared to the setpoint, and the controller adjusts the bypass valve opening $m$ — which changes $f$ — to drive $T_o$ to setpoint.

5. **Static Linearity:** Unlike some other heat-exchanger control schemes, bypass control yields a **linear static relationship** between $T_o$ and the bypass fraction $f$ (a major advantage for controller tuning).

### Why Bypass Control? (The 'Why' and 'How')

| Aspect | Explanation |
|---|---|
| **Faster Response** | Dead time $\theta$ is small because the bypass piping is short; main delay is just the transport lag to the temperature sensor. |
| **Better Rangeability** | Control valve handles only the bypass flow $F_b$, which is a fraction of $F$. Smaller $\Delta P$, better resolution. |
| **Stable Heat Transfer** | The heat exchanger always sees the full utility side; no risk of fouling from low flow. |
| **Linear Gain** | Static gain between $f$ and $T_o$ is constant — simplifies tuning. |
| **Energy Efficiency** | Full heat-exchange capacity is always available; we modulate the *split*, not the *duty*. |

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **directly applicable** for KTU numerical/derivation questions. Use $\vert \cdot \vert$ or $\text{abs}(\cdot)$ instead of vertical bars inside table cells.

| # | Equation | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F = F_e + F_b$ | Total mass balance on process stream | kg/s or m³/h |
| 2 | $f = F_b / F$ | Bypass fraction (dimensionless) | $0 \le f \le 1$ |
| 3 | $1 - f = F_e / F$ | Exchanger fraction | dimensionless |
| 4 | $T_o = f \cdot T_i + (1-f) \cdot T_e$ | Energy balance at mixing point (perfect mixing) | $T$ in °C or K |
| 5 | $T_o = T_i + (1-f)(T_e - T_i)$ | Alternate form | Linear in $f$ |
| 6 | $Q = F_e \cdot c_p \cdot (T_e - T_i)$ | Heat absorbed by process stream | W or kW |
| 7 | $Q = F_u \cdot c_{pu} \cdot (T_{u,in} - T_{u,out})$ | Heat released by utility stream | W or kW |
| 8 | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LM}$ | Heat exchanger duty (LMTD form) | $\Delta T_{LM}$ in K |
| 9 | $K_p = \partial T_o / \partial f$ | Process static gain | °C per unit $f$ |
| 10 | $K_p = T_i - T_e$ | (heating case) | Negative for $T_e > T_i$ |
| 11 | $\tau_p = V_p \rho c_p / F$ | Process time constant (residence-time based) | seconds |
| 12 | $\theta = L_{pipe} / v$ | Dead time (transport delay) | seconds |
| 13 | $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K_p \cdot e^{-\theta s}}{\tau_p s + 1}$ | FOPDT transfer function (process) | standard form |
| 14 | $G_c(s) = K_c \left(1 + \dfrac{1}{\tau_I s}\right)$ | PI controller transfer function | parallel form |

### Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Refinery pre-heat trains:** Crude oil is heated by various process streams; bypass control on crude maintains optimum feed temperature to the distillation column.
- **Chemical reactors:** Reactor feed pre-heating using hot product recycle as utility.
- **HVAC systems:** Chilled water circuits for cooling air-handling units.
- **Dairy & food processing:** Pasteurization temperature control using hot-water/cool-water heat exchangers.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 1: Static (Steady-State) Outlet Temperature

**Given:** Total process flow $F$, bypass fraction $f$, inlet temperature $T_i$, exchanger outlet temperature $T_e$.

**Step 1 — Mass balance at the splitting tee:**
$$F = F_e + F_b$$
This is just conservation of mass at the tee-junction where the flow splits into the exchanger branch and the bypass branch.

**Step 2 — Define bypass fraction:**
$$f = \frac{F_b}{F} \quad \Rightarrow \quad F_b = f F$$
The bypass fraction is the proportion of the total flow that goes through the bypass line. It is dimensionless and ranges from 0 to 1.

**Step 3 — Express exchanger flow in terms of $f$:**
$$F_e = F - F_b = F - fF = F(1-f)$$
The remaining fraction $(1-f)$ passes through the heat exchanger.

**Step 4 — Energy balance at the mixing point (downstream):**
$$F \cdot c_p \cdot T_o = F_b \cdot c_p \cdot T_i + F_e \cdot c_p \cdot T_e$$
At the mixing point, the total enthalpy entering equals the total enthalpy leaving. The $c_p$ terms cancel (assuming same fluid on both branches).

**Step 5 — Simplify the energy balance:**
$$F \cdot T_o = F_b \cdot T_i + F_e \cdot T_e$$
Substituting $F_b = fF$ and $F_e = (1-f)F$:
$$F \cdot T_o = fF \cdot T_i + (1-f)F \cdot T_e$$

**Step 6 — Cancel $F$ from both sides:**
$$T_o = f \cdot T_i + (1-f) \cdot T_e$$

**Step 7 — Rewrite in terms of the temperature rise $\Delta T$:**
$$T_o = T_i + (1-f)(T_e - T_i)$$
This shows that as $f$ increases (more bypass), $T_o$ moves toward $T_i$ (less heat exchange effect). When $f = 0$, $T_o = T_e$ (full flow through exchanger). When $f = 1$, $T_o = T_i$ (full bypass, no heat exchange). $\blacksquare$

---

### Derivation 2: Static Process Gain

**Given:** The mixing equation $T_o = f T_i + (1-f) T_e$.

**Step 1 — Compute the partial derivative of $T_o$ with respect to $f$:**
$$\frac{\partial T_o}{\partial f} = T_i - T_e$$
Using the product/chain rule: derivative of $fT_i$ is $T_i$, derivative of $(1-f)T_e$ is $-T_e$.

**Step 2 — Interpret the sign:**
- **Heating case** ($T_e > T_i$): $K_p = T_i - T_e < 0$ (negative gain — increasing bypass cools the outlet).
- **Cooling case** ($T_e < T_i$): $K_p = T_i - T_e > 0$ (positive gain — increasing bypass warms the outlet).

**Step 3 — Magnitude of the gain:**
$$\vert K_p \vert = \vert T_e - T_i \vert = \Delta T_{exchanger}$$
The static gain magnitude equals the temperature rise (or drop) across the heat exchanger. This is a useful rule-of-thumb for tuning.

---

### Derivation 3: FOPDT Dynamic Model

**Given:** The heat exchanger is a single, well-mixed volume on the process side. Process volume $V_p$, density $\rho$, specific heat $c_p$.

**Step 1 — Component balance on the heat exchanger volume (process side):**
$$V_p \rho c_p \frac{dT_e}{dt} = F_e \rho c_p (T_i - T_e) + Q$$
where $Q$ is the heat transferred from utility to process. $T_e$ is the temperature inside (and at the outlet of) the exchanger.

**Step 2 — Assume utility is well-mixed too; the heat transfer $Q$ is a function of utility flow $F_u$ and temperatures:**
$$Q = U A (T_{u,in} - T_e)$$
Simplified linearized form (small deviations from steady state). Let $Q = Q_{ss} + q$ and $T_e = T_{e,ss} + T_e'$, then linearize.

**Step 3 — Deviation-variable form (drop steady-state terms):**
$$V_p \rho c_p \frac{dT_e'}{dt} = F_{e,ss} \rho c_p (T_i' - T_e') + q'$$
For control analysis, $T_i'$ is a disturbance (assume constant for now), and $q'$ is related to utility flow changes (also considered a disturbance in basic single-loop analysis).

**Step 4 — If we focus only on the bypass-flow effect on $T_o$ (with utility held constant):**
Combining the exchanger model with the mixing-point equation and linearizing gives:
$$\tau_p \frac{dT_o'}{dt} + T_o' = K_p f'(t - \theta)$$
The time constant is dominated by the process-side residence time:
$$\tau_p \approx \frac{V_p \rho c_p}{F_e}$$
The dead time $\theta$ accounts for the transport delay between the heat-exchanger exit, through the mixing point, and to the temperature sensor:
$$\theta = \frac{L_{pipe}}{v_{flow}}$$

**Step 5 — Laplace transform yields the FOPDT transfer function:**
$$\frac{T_o(s)}{F_b(s)} = \frac{K_p \, e^{-\theta s}}{\tau_p s + 1}$$
This is the **First-Order Plus Dead Time (FOPDT)** model — the standard building block for controller tuning (Ziegler-Nichols, Cohen-Coon, IMC, etc.). $\blacksquare$

---

### Derivation 4: Closed-Loop Characteristic Equation (for stability analysis)

**Given:** PI controller $G_c(s) = K_c(1 + 1/(\tau_I s))$ and process $G_p(s) = K_p e^{-\theta s}/(\tau_p s + 1)$.

**Step 1 — Open-loop transfer function:**
$$G_{OL}(s) = G_c(s) \cdot G_p(s) = \frac{K_c (1 + \tau_I s)}{\tau_I s} \cdot \frac{K_p e^{-\theta s}}{\tau_p s + 1}$$

**Step 2 — Closed-loop transfer function:**
$$G_{CL}(s) = \frac{G_{OL}(s)}{1 + G_{OL}(s)}$$

**Step 3 — Characteristic equation $1 + G_{OL}(s) = 0$:**
$$1 + \frac{K_c K_p (1 + \tau_I s) e^{-\theta s}}{\tau_I s (\tau_p s + 1)} = 0$$

**Step 4 — Multiply through:**
$$\tau_I s (\tau_p s + 1) + K_c K_p (1 + \tau_I s) e^{-\theta s} = 0$$
This transcendental equation has no closed-form solution. In practice, $\theta$ is small enough that the Pade approximation $e^{-\theta s} \approx (1 - \theta s/2)/(1 + \theta s/2)$ is used, or numerical root-finding / frequency-response techniques (Bode, Nyquist) are employed to assess stability. $\blacksquare$

---

### Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Bypass Control with Other Methods

| Method | Manipulated Variable | Speed | Linearity | Valve Sizing |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Bypass on process side** | Bypass flow $F_b$ | Fast (small $\theta$) | Linear in $f$ | Small (handles $F_b$ only) |
| Throttling utility flow | Utility flow $F_u$ | Slow (utility dynamics) | Nonlinear | Sized for $F_u$ |
| Throttling process flow | Process flow $F$ | Fast | Nonlinear (1/F effect) | Sized for $F$ |
| Bypass on utility side | Utility bypass flow | Medium | Approximately linear | Sized for utility bypass |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram 1: Physical Arrangement of Bypass Control

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Process Source] -->|Inlet F, Ti| T1{Splitting Tee}
    T1 -->|Fb bypass| BP[Bypass Line]
    T1 -->|Fe flow| HX[Liquid-to-Liquid Heat Exchanger]
    HX -->|Te, exiting process| T2[Mixing Tee]
    BP -->|Ti, unchanged| T2
    T2 -->|To, mixed outlet| TT[Temperature Transmitter TT]
    TT -->|4-20 mA signal| PID[PID Controller TIC]
    PID -->|Controller output m| CV[Control Valve on Bypass Line]
    CV -->|Adjusts Fb| BP
    UTIL[Utility Stream Tu,in, Fu] --> HX
    HX -->|Utility outlet Tu,out| UTILOUT[Utility Return]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The process stream enters at the **Splitting Tee (T1)** and divides.
- The **bypass stream** retains temperature $T_i$ (inlet temperature).
- The **exchanger stream** is heated/cooled to $T_e$.
- Both streams recombine at the **Mixing Tee (T2)**.
- The mixed temperature $T_o$ is measured by **TT** and sent to the **PID controller (TIC)**.
- The controller output moves the **control valve** on the bypass line, completing the feedback loop.
- The utility stream provides/absorbs the heat in the exchanger.

---

### Diagram 2: Functional Block Diagram of the Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP[Setpoint Tsp] --> SUM((+))
    TT2[T_o measured] --> SUM
    SUM -->|Error e| PID2[Gc s: PID Controller]
    PID2 -->|Output m| VALVE[Control Valve: f = Kv m]
    VALVE -->|Bypass fraction f| PROC[Gp s: Process FOPDT]
    PROC -->|T_o| TT2
    DIST[Disturbances: Ti, Tu,in, Fu] -.->|perturbs| PROC
```

**Key elements:**
- $G_c(s)$ = controller (typically PI for heat-exchanger loops)
- $G_p(s)$ = process transfer function (FOPDT form)
- Disturbances $T_i$, $T_{u,in}$, $F_u$ enter the process directly
- The feedback path carries the measured $T_o$ back to the summing junction

---

### Diagram 3: Static Characteristic Curve of Bypass Control

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0["f = 0 (No Bypass)"] --> A1["To = Te (full heating)"]
    A05["f = 0.5"] --> A2["To = 0.5 Ti + 0.5 Te (mid-point)"]
    A1full["f = 1 (Full Bypass)"] --> A3["To = Ti (no heating)"]
    A1 --> A2 --> A3
```

**Key insight:** The static relation $T_o = f T_i + (1-f) T_e$ is a **straight line** in $f$ — a unique advantage of bypass control that simplifies controller tuning and yields predictable linear behavior.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**  
Define **bypass control** as applied to a liquid-to-liquid heat exchanger. State the controlled variable (CV) and the manipulated variable (MV) for this scheme.

**Model Answer:**  
Bypass control of a liquid-to-liquid heat exchanger is a temperature control strategy in which a portion of the process stream is diverted through a bypass line that bypasses the heat exchanger, and the bypassed stream is recombined (mixed) with the stream leaving the exchanger downstream.  
- **Controlled Variable (CV):** Outlet temperature of the process stream $T_o$ (measured after the mixing point).  
- **Manipulated Variable (MV):** Bypass flow rate $F_b$ (or the bypass fraction $f = F_b/F$), adjusted via a control valve on the bypass line. (3 marks)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**  
For a liquid-to-liquid heat exchanger under bypass control, write the steady-state mixing-point energy balance equation. What is the sign of the process gain for the *heating* case?

**Model Answer:**  
At the mixing point, the energy balance is: $T_o = f \cdot T_i + (1-f) \cdot T_e$, where $f = F_b/F$ is the bypass fraction.
