The control loop uses a **temperature sensor (TT) → controller (TC) → control valve** chain to manipulate $W_s$ and hold $T$ at set-point despite disturbances in $F$ and $T_i$. *(7 marks: 2 for diagram, 2 for variable identification, 3 for explanation)* ##### Model Solution — Q A(b) **Assumptions (state explicitly in exam):** 1. Perfect mixing — single temperature $T$. 2. Constant $\rho$, $C_p$, $\lambda$. 3. Steam at constant $T_{ss}$ (saturated, fixed pressure). 4. Negligible steam-side capacitance. 5. No heat loss. **Step 1 — Energy balance on process fluid:** $$V \rho C_p \frac{dT}{dt} = F C_p (T_i - T) + W_s \lambda$$ **Step 2 — Steady-state condition:** $$0 = F_s C_p (T_{is} - T_s) + W_{ss}\lambda$$ **Step 3 — Linearization** (substitute $X = X_s + X'$, drop product of deviations): $$V \rho C_p \frac{dT'}{dt} = C_p(T_{is}-T_s)F' + F_s C_p T_i' - F_s C_p T' + \lambda W_s'$$ **Step 4 — Divide by $F_s C_p$:** $$\boxed{\;\frac{V\rho}{F_s}\frac{dT'}{dt} + T' = T_i' + \frac{T_{is}-T_s}{F_s}F' + \frac{\lambda}{F_s C_p}W_s'\;}$$ **Step 5 — Laplace transform:** $$(\tau_p s + 1) T(s) = T_i(s) + K_f F(s) + K_s W_s(s)$$ where $\tau_p = V\rho/F_s$, $K_f = (T_{is}-T_s)/F_s$, $K_s = \lambda/(F_s C_p)$. **Step 6 — Required transfer functions:** $$\frac{T(s)}{W_s(s)} = \frac{K_s}{\tau_p s + 1}, \qquad \frac{T(s)}{F(s)} = \frac{K_f}{\tau_p s + 1}$$ > **Key observation:** Both transfer functions share the *same* time constant $\tau_p$ but have different gains; $K_s > 0$ and $K_f < 0$. *(7 marks: 2 assumptions + 2 derivation + 3 final TF + interpretation)* --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **(a)** A steam heater processes **water** at steady-state values $F_s = 1.5$ kg/s, $T_{is} = 25\,°C$, $T_s = 75\,°C$. Saturated steam at $T_{ss} = 130\,°C$ ($\lambda = 2174$ kJ/kg) is used. The hold-up volume is $V = 0.3$ m³, $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $C_p = 4.186$ kJ/(kg·K). Compute: (i) The steady-state steam flow rate $W_{ss}$. *(2 marks)* (ii) The process time constant $\tau_p$ and the gain $K_s$. *(3 marks)* (iii) The transfer function $T(s)/W_s(s)$. *(2 marks)* **(b)** If a **step increase** of 10 % is made in the steam flow rate, calculate the *new steady-state* outlet temperature of water using the Final Value Theorem. State one limitation of this first-order model. *(7 marks)* ##### Model Solution — Q B(a) **(i)** From $F_s C_p (T_s - T_{is}) = W_{ss}\lambda$: $$W_{ss} = \frac{1.5 \times 4.186 \times (75-25)}{2174} = \frac{313.95}{2174} \approx \mathbf{0.1444 \;\text{kg/s}}$$ **(ii)** $$\tau_p = \frac{V\rho}{F_s} = \frac{0.3 \times 1000}{1.5} = \mathbf{200 \;s}$$ $$K_s = \frac{\lambda}{F_s C_p} = \frac{2174}{1.5 \times 4.186} = \frac{2174}{6.279} \approx \mathbf{346.2 \;°C/(kg/s)}$$ **(iii)** $$\boxed{\;G_p(s) = \frac{T(s)}{W_s(s)} = \frac{346.2}{200 s + 1}\;}$$ ##### Model Solution — Q B(b) Step magnitude: $\Delta W_s = 0.10 \times 0.1444 = 0.01444$ kg/s. Final-value theorem: $$\Delta T_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot G_p(s) \cdot \frac{0.01444}{s} = G_p(0) \times 0.01444 = 346.2 \times 0.01444 \approx 5.0 \;°C$$ New outlet temperature: $$T_{\text{new}} = 75 + 5.0 = \mathbf{80.0 \;°C}$$ **Limitation of the first-order model (any one):** - It ignores the **transport delay (dead time)** $\theta = V/F$ between the heater inlet and the temperature sensor. - It assumes **constant $U$**; in reality $U$ varies with steam flow rate (boiling/condensing curve). - It assumes **perfect mixing**; real heaters have a temperature profile along the tubes. - It ignores the **steam-side capacitance** (which would add a second pole). *(7 marks: 2 SS calculation + 3 FVT + 2 limitation)* --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU students typically lose marks on Steam Heater problems:** > 1. **Forgetting the steady-state equation** (SS-1) before linearizing — *without it the gains $K_s$ and $K_f$ cannot be derived.* > 2. **Sign error on $K_f$** — students often write $K_f = (T_s - T_{is})/F_s$ instead of $(T_{is}-T_s)/F_s$. Remember: $T_i < T$, so $K_f$ must be **negative**. > 3. **Dropping the deviation variables** — never write $W_s$ instead of $W_s'$ in the linearized equation; KTU examiners *will* deduct a mark. > 4. **Mixing units** — $\lambda$ in kJ/kg but $C_p$ in J/(kg·K). Convert consistently or carry kJ throughout. > 5. **Skipping the FVT justification** — always state "by the Final Value Theorem, $\lim_{s\to 0} sX(s)$" before computing the numerical answer. > 6. **No block diagram for the closed-loop question** — Part (a) of Question A *requires* a labelled diagram; a textual description alone will lose at least 2 marks. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - ✅ A **steam heater** is a *first-order thermal process* — one energy balance, one pole, no zeros. - ✅ Master energy equation: $V\rho C_p \dfrac{dT}{dt} = F C_p (T_i - T) + W_s \lambda$. - ✅ Steam-side relation: $Q = W_s \lambda$ (latent-heat dominated, no sensible-heat term). - ✅ **DOF = 3** → 1 MV ($W_s$) + 1 CV ($T$) + 1 DV ($F$ or $T_i$). - ✅ Process time constant: $\tau_p = \dfrac{V \rho}{F_s}$ (depends only on hold-up and flow, *not* on steam). - ✅ Steam-flow gain: $K_s = \dfrac{\lambda}{F_s C_p} > 0$. - ✅ Process-flow gain: $K_f = \dfrac{T_{is} - T_s}{F_s} < 0$ (more flow → cooler outlet). - ✅ Inlet-temperature gain: $K_{T_i} = 1$ (a 1 °C rise in $T_i$ raises $T$ by 1 °C at SS). - ✅ Complete TF model: $T(s) = \dfrac{T_i(s) + K_f F(s) + K_s W_s(s)}{\tau_p s + 1}$. - ✅ Standard assumptions: perfect mixing, constant properties, saturated steam, no wall capacitance, no heat loss. - ✅ Use the **Final Value Theorem** for step-response steady-state calculations. - ✅ Latent heat at 1 atm ≈ **2257 kJ/kg**, at higher pressures it *decreases*. - ✅ The same FOPDT model underpins PI/PID tuning for reboilers, jacketed reactors, and feed preheaters. - ✅ Watch out for **sign conventions** in $K_f$ and consistent **unit conversions** in numerical problems.

topics.title: Steam Throttling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT743 - INDUSTRIAL PROCESS CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Steam Throttling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Steam throttling** is defined as the **irreversible, adiabatic (isenthalpic) expansion of a fluid** — typically steam — through a **porous plug, partially closed valve, orifice, or any other flow restriction** that causes a measurable drop in upstream-to-downstream pressure while no work is extracted and heat transfer to the surroundings is negligible.

Mathematically, the defining condition of a throttling process is:

$$h_1 = h_2 = \text{constant}$$

where $h_1$ and $h_2$ are the **specific enthalpies (kJ/kg)** of the steam at the inlet and outlet of the restriction, respectively.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Memorize Verbatim):** *"Throttling is the flow of a fluid through a restriction such that there is a drop in pressure, and the process is essentially isenthalpic (constant enthalpy) and irreversible."*

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Garden Hose Pinch**
> Imagine pinching the opening of a garden hose while water is flowing through it. The water pressure **upstream** (before the pinch) is high, but **downstream** (after the pinch) it is low. The water may even feel slightly **colder** at the outlet. The water did no shaft work, no heat was added from outside, and yet the pressure dropped. The internal energy reorganized itself, and the enthalpy at the two ends of the restriction is **virtually the same**.
> 
> *Steam throttling is the exact same phenomenon, but with high-pressure steam flowing through a partially closed globe valve or an orifice plate inside a pipeline.*

### 1.3 Physical Process Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value (Steam Plant) |
|---|---|
| Upstream Pressure $P_1$ | **40 – 100 bar** |
| Downstream Pressure $P_2$ | **2 – 10 bar** |
| Velocity change | Negligible (often assumed $V_1 \approx V_2$) |
| Heat transfer $Q$ | **0** (assumed adiabatic) |
| Work done $W$ | **0** (no shaft work) |
| Enthalpy $h$ | **Constant** |

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Intuition Rule for Steam Throttling:**
> - The steam **does not "lose" energy** — it just converts some of its pressure-based energy into velocity (turbulence), which is then dissipated as internal friction (heat) within the fluid itself.
> - Since the fluid is the system, this self-generated heat is **retained** by the steam. Hence, enthalpy stays constant.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Breakdown of the Throttling Process

The throttling process can be understood through a structured, step-by-step logic:

1. **Restriction Exists** — A valve, orifice, or porous plug partially blocks the flow path.
2. **Pressure Drops** — As the fluid squeezes through, the upstream pressure $P_1$ is significantly higher than the downstream pressure $P_2$.
3. **No Heat Transfer** — The throttling device is small relative to the pipeline, and the process happens rapidly → $Q = 0$.
4. **No Shaft Work** — The fluid only moves through a fixed geometry; no turbine or compressor is involved → $W_s = 0$.
5. **Negligible Kinetic Energy Change** — The cross-section before and after is comparable → $\Delta KE \approx 0$.
6. **Negligible Potential Energy Change** — The device is small in height → $\Delta PE \approx 0$.
7. **Enthalpy is Conserved** — Applying the Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE), all those terms vanish, leaving $h_1 = h_2$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why $h_1 = h_2$? — The 'Why' Behind the Formula**
> 
> The **Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE)** per unit mass is:
> 
> $$h_1 + \frac{V_1^2}{2} + gZ_1 + q = h_2 + \frac{V_2^2}{2} + gZ_2 + w_s$$
> 
> Applying throttling constraints ($q = 0$, $w_s = 0$, $\Delta KE \approx 0$, $\Delta PE \approx 0$) gives $h_1 = h_2$.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / When to Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $h_1 = h_2$ | Isenthalpic condition — **master equation** for all throttling problems | kJ/kg |
| 2 | $h_1 + \frac{V_1^2}{2} = h_2 + \frac{V_2^2}{2}$ | Full SFEE (when velocities are given) | kJ/kg |
| 3 | $h_{f1} + x_1 \cdot h_{fg1} = h_{f2} + x_2 \cdot h_{fg2}$ | For **wet steam** entering throttling (quality change calculation) | kJ/kg |
| 4 | $h_1 = h_{f2} + x_2 \cdot h_{fg2}$ | For **superheated** steam entering throttling to saturated mixture | kJ/kg |
| 5 | $x_2 = \dfrac{h_1 - h_{f2}}{h_{fg2}}$ | Quality of steam after throttling | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\mu_{JT} = \left(\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_h$ | Joule-Thomson Coefficient — sign indicates heating/cooling | K/Pa |
| 7 | $T_2 - T_1 = \mu_{JT}(P_2 - P_1)$ | Temperature change across throttle | K |
| 8 | $h_{fg} = h_g - h_f$ | Latent heat at given pressure | kJ/kg |
| 9 | $h_{sup} = h_{g\,@P} + C_p(T_{sup} - T_{sat})$ | Approx. enthalpy of superheated steam | kJ/kg |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Markdown Table Warning — Pipe Symbol:** All absolute value bars in equations are rendered using `\vert` or `\mid` (e.g., $\vert x_2 \vert$) to prevent breaking the table structure.

### 2.3 Joule-Thomson Effect — The Key Physical Insight

> [!NOTE]
> When a real gas (including steam) is throttled, the **temperature changes** even though enthalpy is constant. This is the **Joule-Thomson Effect**.

- **For Ideal Gases:** $\mu_{JT} = 0$ → No temperature change. Throttling is purely an academic exercise.
- **For Real Gases & Steam (at typical plant conditions):** $\mu_{JT} > 0$ → **Cooling occurs** when pressure drops.
- **For Steam (high temperature superheat):** $\mu_{JT} < 0$ → **Heating occurs** (a unique case KTU loves to test).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Application |
|---|---|
| **Refrigeration** | Throttling valve is the **expansion device** in vapor-compression cycles |
| **Power Plants** | HP turbine → HP heater → reheat, but throttling used in feedwater control valves |
| **Dew Point / Steam Quality** | Throttling calorimeter measures dryness fraction $x$ |
| **Petrochemical** | Pressure let-down valves, Joule-Thomson cooling for gas liquefaction |
| **Process Control** | Control valves act as **inherent throttling devices** — PEICT743 context |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Isenthalpic Condition $h_1 = h_2$

**Step 1 — Write the Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE)** for a control volume around the throttling device:

$$h_1 + \frac{V_1^2}{2} + gZ_1 + q = h_2 + \frac{V_2^2}{2} + gZ_2 + w_s$$

**Step 2 — Apply Boundary Conditions for Throttling:**

- The throttling device is **small**, so potential energy change $gZ_1 \approx gZ_2$.
- The process is **adiabatic** because of rapid flow and small surface area → $q = 0$.
- The fluid passes through a fixed restriction, **no shaft work** is extracted → $w_s = 0$.
- The cross-sectional area does not change drastically → $\frac{V_1^2}{2} \approx \frac{V_2^2}{2}$.

**Step 3 — Substitute the conditions into the SFEE:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_1 + \cancel{\frac{V_1^2}{2}} + \cancel{gZ_1} + \cancel{0} &= h_2 + \cancel{\frac{V_2^2}{2}} + \cancel{gZ_2} + \cancel{0} \\
h_1 &= h_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion:**

$$\boxed{h_1 = h_2}$$

This is the **fundamental governing equation** for all steam throttling problems.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Throttling Calorimeter Equation

A **throttling calorimeter** is used to measure the **dryness fraction (quality) of wet steam** by throttling it to a low-pressure superheated state where quality can be easily obtained.

**Given:**
- Inlet: Wet steam at $P_1$ with quality $x_1$.
- Outlet: Superheated steam at $P_2$.

**Step 1 — Write the isenthalpic condition:**

$$h_1 = h_2$$

**Step 2 — Expand the inlet enthalpy (wet steam at $P_1$):**

$$h_1 = h_{f1} + x_1 \cdot h_{fg1}$$

**Step 3 — Expand the outlet enthalpy (superheated steam at $P_2$):**

$$h_2 = h_{g2} + C_{p2}(T_{2,\,actual} - T_{2,\,sat})$$

**Step 4 — Equate and solve for $x_1$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_{f1} + x_1 \cdot h_{fg1} &= h_{g2} + C_{p2}(T_{2,\,actual} - T_{2,\,sat}) \\
x_1 \cdot h_{fg1} &= h_{g2} + C_{p2}(T_{2,\,actual} - T_{2,\,sat}) - h_{f1} \\
x_1 &= \frac{h_{g2} + C_{p2}(T_{2,\,actual} - T_{2,\,sat}) - h_{f1}}{h_{fg1}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Boxed Result:**

$$\boxed{x_1 = \frac{h_{g2} + C_{p2}(T_{2,\,actual} - T_{2,\,sat}) - h_{f1}}{h_{fg1}}}$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Model KTU-Style)

**Problem:** Steam at **15 bar** with a quality of **0.92** is throttled to **1.5 bar**. Determine the **final state** and **final enthalpy**.

**Step 1 — Inlet Enthalpy Calculation:**

At $P_1 = 15$ bar, from steam tables: $h_{f1} = 844.84$ kJ/kg, $h_{fg1} = 1947.3$ kJ/kg.

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_1 &= h_{f1} + x_1 \cdot h_{fg1} \\
h_1 &= 844.84 + 0.92 \times 1947.3 \\
h_1 &= 844.84 + 1791.516 \\
h_1 &= 2636.36 \text{ kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Throttling Condition:**

$$h_2 = h_1 = 2636.36 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 3 — Determine Final State at $P_2 = 1.5$ bar:**

From steam tables at 1.5 bar: $h_{f2} = 467.11$ kJ/kg, $h_{g2} = 2693.6$ kJ/kg.

Since $h_{f2} < h_2 < h_{g2}$, the steam is **wet at outlet**.

**Step 4 — Find Outlet Quality $x_2$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_2 &= h_{f2} + x_2 \cdot h_{fg2} \\
2636.36 &= 467.11 + x_2 \times (2693.6 - 467.11) \\
2636.36 - 467.11 &= x_2 \times 2226.49 \\
x_2 &= \frac{2169.25}{2226.49} \\
x_2 &= 0.9742
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{h_2 = 2636.36 \text{ kJ/kg}, \quad x_2 = 0.9742 \text{ (wet steam)}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Insight:** The dryness fraction **increased** ($0.92 \rightarrow 0.9742$). This is a common KTU trick — students often assume moisture "reappears" but the state must be read directly from the steam tables at $P_2$.

---

### 3.4 Derivation of the Joule-Thomson Coefficient (for steam)

**Step 1 — Start with the definition:**

$$\mu_{JT} = \left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_h$$

**Step 2 — Use cyclic relation from thermodynamics:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
dh &= C_p \, dT - \mu_{JT} \, C_p \, dP \quad \text{(for real gases)} \\
\left(\frac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_h &= \frac{1}{C_p}\left[T\left(\frac{\partial V}{\partial T}\right)_P - V\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Sign Convention Summary:**

| Sign of $\mu_{JT}$ | Temperature Behavior |
|---|---|
| $\mu_{JT} > 0$ | Steam **cools** on throttling (typical sub-cooled/wet steam) |
| $\mu_{JT} < 0$ | Steam **heats** on throttling (high-temp superheat) |
| $\mu_{JT} = 0$ | No temperature change (ideal gas / inversion point) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Throttling Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[HP Steam Reservoir] --> B[Upstream Piping P1, T1]
    B --> C[Throttling Valve Restriction]
    C --> D[Downstream Piping P2, T2]
    D --> E[LP Process or Calorimeter]

    subgraph Throttling Zone
    C
    end

    F[SFEE Conditions] --> C
    F --> G[Q equals 0]
    F --> H[Ws equals 0]
    F --> I[Delta KE equals 0]
    F --> J[Delta PE equals 0]

    C --> K[Result: h1 equals h2]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Throttling Calorimeter Setup

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[Boiler Steam Line] --> A2[Steam Main P1, x1]
    A2 --> A3[Sampling Tube Inside Main]
    A3 --> A4[Throttling Orifice Valve]
    A4 --> A5[Exit Chamber P2 superheated]
    A5 --> A6[Thermometer T2 reading]
    A6 --> A7[Calculate x1 from formula]

    P1[Pressure Gauge 1] --- A3
    P2[Pressure Gauge 2] --- A5

    A4 -.->|adiabatic| A5
    A5 -.->|T2 measured| A6
```

### 4.3 State Change Diagram on P-h and T-s Coordinates

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[State 1 Wet Steam at P1] -->|Throttling h constant| B[State 2 Superheated at P2]

    A -. h1 .-> B
    B -. h2 equals h1 .-> A
```

| State | Pressure | Enthalpy | Quality/Phase |
|---|---|---|---|
| **State 1 (Inlet)** | $P_1$ (high) | $h_1$ | $x_1$ (wet) |
| **State 2 (Outlet)** | $P_2$ (low) | $h_2 = h_1$ | $x_2$ or superheated |

> [!NOTE]
> **On a Mollier (h-s) diagram**, the throttling process is represented by a **horizontal line** moving from right to left (since $h = \text{constant}$ and entropy increases due to irreversibility).

### 4.4 P-h Chart Movement (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Mollier_hs_Diagram
    direction TB
    P1pt[State 1: Wet steam, high P, lower s] -->|Horizontal line h constant| P2pt[State 2: Superheated, low P, higher s]
    end
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]**

**Q1. Define steam throttling. Why is it called an isenthalpic process?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Definition (1 Mark):** Steam throttling is the irreversible, adiabatic expansion of steam through a restriction (valve/orifice) that causes a pressure drop without any work extraction.
- **Isenthalpic Reasoning (2 Marks):** Applying the SFEE with $Q = 0$, $W_s = 0$, $\Delta KE = 0$, $\Delta PE = 0$ yields $h_1 = h_2$. Hence, the process is called *isenthalpic*.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]**

**Q2. What is a throttling calorimeter? Mention its limitation.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Definition (2 Marks):** A throttling calorimeter is a device used to determine the **dryness fraction of wet steam** by throttling the steam from a high-pressure wet state to a low-pressure superheated state, then applying $h_1 = h_2$.
- **Limitation (1 Mark):** It cannot be used if the steam after throttling remains wet (i.e., does not reach the superheated region) — quality cannot be back-calculated accurately.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Pattern with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Derive the steady flow energy equation and show that throttling is an isenthalpic process. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Steam at **20 bar** with **85% quality** is throttled to a pressure of **1.0 bar**. Determine: **(i)** Final enthalpy, **(ii)** Final state, **(iii)** Change in entropy. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 (2 Marks):** State the SFEE for a control volume:

$$h_1 + \frac{V_1^2}{2} + gZ_1 + q = h_2 + \frac{V_2^2}{2} + gZ_2 + w_s$$

**Step 2 (2 Marks):** List the four throttling conditions: $Q = 0$, $W_s = 0$, $\Delta KE = 0$, $\Delta PE = 0$.

**Step 3 (2 Marks):** Substitute and cancel terms to derive $h_1 = h_2$.

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Conclude that the process is isenthalpic.

#### **Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** From steam tables at $P_1 = 20$ bar: $h_{f1} = 908.79$ kJ/kg, $h_{g1} = 2799.5$ kJ/kg, $h_{fg1} = 1890.7$ kJ/kg.

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** Calculate inlet enthalpy:

$$h_1 = 908.79 + 0.85 \times 1890.7 = 908.79 + 1607.10 = 2515.89 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** Apply isenthalpic condition:

$$h_2 = h_1 = 2515.89 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 4 (2 Marks):** At $P_2 = 1.0$ bar: $h_{f2} = 417.46$ kJ/kg, $h_{g2} = 2675.5$ kJ/kg, $h_{fg2} = 2258.0$ kJ/kg.

Since $h_{f2} < h_2 < h_{g2}$, the steam is **wet at the outlet**.

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** Find $x_2$:

$$x_2 = \frac{2515.89 - 417.46}{2258.0} = \frac{2098.43}{2258.0} = 0.929$$

**Step 6 (1 Mark):** Final State: Wet steam at 1.0 bar with $x_2 = 0.929$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the working of a throttling calorimeter with a neat sketch and derive the formula for finding the dryness fraction of steam. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Saturated steam at **10 bar** is throttled to **0.2 bar** and the temperature at exit is measured as **130 °C**. If the actual exit temperature corresponds to **120 °C** in the superheated region at 0.2 bar, determine the **dryness fraction** at inlet. Take $C_p = 2.1$ kJ/kg·K. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 (2 Marks):** Describe the throttling calorimeter setup (see Section 4.2 diagram) — sampling tube, throttle, pressure gauges, thermometer.

**Step 2 (2 Marks):** State the principle: $h_1 = h_2$.

**Step 3 (2 Marks):** Derive the equation by equating wet steam enthalpy to superheated steam enthalpy.

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Box the final equation:

$$x_1 = \frac{h_{g2} + C_{p2}(T_{2,\,actual} - T_{2,\,sat}) - h_{f1}}{h_{fg1}}$$

#### **Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** From steam tables at 10 bar: $h_{f1} = 762.81$ kJ/kg, $h_{fg1} = 2015.3$ kJ/kg, $h_{g1} = 2778.1$ kJ/kg.

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** At 0.2 bar: $h_{g2} = 2706.7$ kJ/kg, $T_{sat} = 60.06$ °C, $T_{2,actual} = 120$ °C.

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** Calculate $h_2$ (superheated):

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_2 &= h_{g2} + C_p(T_{actual} - T_{sat}) \\
h_2 &= 2706.7 + 2.1 \times (120 - 60.06) \\
h_2 &= 2706.7 + 125.874 \\
h_2 &= 2832.57 \text{ kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Apply $h_1 = h_2$:

$$h_{f1} + x_1 h_{fg1} = h_2$$
$$762.81 + x_1 \times 2015.3 = 2832.57$$

**Step 5 (2 Marks):** Solve for $x_1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_1 \times 2015.3 &= 2832.57 - 762.81 \\
x_1 &= \frac{2069.76}{2015.3} \\
x_1 &= 1.027
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 (1 Mark):** Since $x_1 > 1$, the inlet steam is **slightly superheated** — KTU examiners often use this trick. State that the assumption of saturated inlet must be revised; actual inlet is superheated by a small margin.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do not forget to state boundary conditions** (Q=0, Ws=0, ΔKE=0, ΔPE=0) before canceling terms. Most students lose 2 marks here.
> - **Always re-check** if the outlet state is wet, saturated, or superheated — a wrong phase assumption leads to zero marks.
> - **Sign of $\mu_{JT}$** must be mentioned when asked about heating/cooling — examiners specifically test this.
> - **Steam table values** must be quoted with units (kJ/kg, °C) for full credit.
> - In throttling calorimeter problems, if $x_1 > 1$, **do not silently cap it at 1** — explicitly state the implication.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Steam Throttling (Module 3)**

- ✅ Throttling = **irreversible + adiabatic + isenthalpic** ($h_1 = h_2$).
- ✅ **No work, no heat transfer, no significant KE/PE change.**
- ✅ **Joule-Thomson Effect:** Real gases cool on throttling; steam may heat if highly superheated.
- ✅ **Throttling Calorimeter Equation:**
$$x_1 = \frac{h_{g2} + C_p(T_2 - T_{sat}) - h_{f1}}{h_{fg1}}$$
- ✅ On a **Mollier (h-s) diagram**, throttling is a **horizontal line** (right → left, entropy increases).
- ✅ **Dryness fraction** can **increase** across a throttle (moisture evaporates due to pressure drop).
- ✅ **Velocity change** is *negligible* across the restriction (this is what makes the derivation valid).
- ✅ Typical industrial uses: **refrigeration expansion valve, control valves, dew-point measurement, steam quality measurement.**
- ✅ Common KTU mistakes: forgetting boundary conditions, misapplying the phase assumption at exit, ignoring units in steam table lookups.
- ✅ Master equation to memorize: **SFEE → $h_1 = h_2$** (know the derivation cold).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Condensate Throttling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT743 - INDUSTRIAL PROCESS CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Condensate Throttling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Condensate Throttling** is a unit operation in process industries in which high-pressure hot condensate (saturated or sub-cooled liquid) is passed through a **throttling valve** (a restriction device such as a globe valve, orifice, or control valve) to a lower pressure zone. The sudden pressure reduction causes a portion of the liquid to vaporize spontaneously — a phenomenon known as **flashing**. The energy for this vaporization is drawn from the sensible heat of the remaining liquid itself (the liquid self-cools). This technique is heavily exploited in **distillation column reboilers, multi-effect evaporators, steam distribution headers, heat recovery networks, and refrigeration circuits** as a passive energy-recovery and pressure-stage device.

In KTU-2024 Scheme terminology, condensate throttling sits at the intersection of:
1. **Process Energy Integration** (pinch analysis).
2. **Distillation Column Auxiliary Control** (reboiler steam cascade).
3. **Steam System Optimization** (cascading boiler pressure levels).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine opening the lid of a pressure cooker immediately after the whistle.**
> The instant the pressure drops from ~1.5 bar to atmospheric, a violent jet of steam bursts out. Where did that steam come from? From the hot water inside, which was at a temperature *higher* than 100 °C (because it was pressurized). The drop in pressure lowers the local boiling point, and the excess sensible heat converts a fraction of the water into steam. **Condensate throttling is the engineered, controlled version of exactly this phenomenon.**

A second everyday analogy: think of a **CNG cylinder vent**. When you crack open a high-pressure gas cylinder to atmospheric pressure, the gas cools sharply (Joule–Thomson effect). For liquids, this cooling is even more dramatic because the latent heat extraction produces a measurable two-phase mixture.

## 1.3 Key Physical Metrics (Standard Values in Steam Tables)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> | Metric | Symbol | Typical Range |
> |---|---|---|
> | High-pressure side | $P_{H}$ | **4 – 42 bar** |
> | Low-pressure side | $P_{L}$ | **1.03 – 10 bar** |
> | Flash steam quality | $x$ | **0.05 – 0.25** |
> | Latent heat at $P_{L}$ | $h_{fg,L}$ | **2257 kJ/kg @ 1 bar → 2014 kJ/kg @ 10 bar** |
> | Throttling (J–T) enthalpy | $\Delta H_{JT}$ | ≈ 0 (isenthalpic) |

The throttling process is assumed **isenthalpic** (constant enthalpy), because the valve is treated as an adiabatic, no-work, no-significant-KE-change device. This is the *linchpin* assumption for all flash calculations.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Thermodynamic Chain of Events

When saturated liquid condensate at $P_H$ enters the throttling valve, the following sequence occurs (state points labelled **1 → 2 → 3**):

1. **State 1 — Upstream of valve:** Saturated liquid at $P_H$, temperature $T_{sat,H}$, enthalpy $h_{f1}$.
2. **Inside the valve — Joule–Thomson expansion:** Pressure falls almost instantaneously to $P_L$. The process is *isenthalpic*: $h_1 = h_2$.
3. **State 2 — Immediately downstream:** The fluid is a **two-phase mixture** (quality $x$). Temperature drops to $T_{sat,L}$. A fraction has flashed to vapor.
4. **State 3 — After phase separation (in flash drum):** Vapor (flash steam) exits the top; sub-cooled or saturated liquid at $P_L$ exits the bottom.

## 2.2 Why Does the Liquid Flash?

The fluid leaving the valve must simultaneously satisfy:
- The same **enthalpy** it entered with (no work, no heat transfer at the valve).
- The **thermodynamic state** corresponding to the new lower pressure.

A saturated liquid at $P_H$ carries enthalpy $h_{f1}$. At the lower pressure $P_L$, the saturated liquid enthalpy is $h_{f2}$ and saturated vapor enthalpy is $h_{g2}$. Since $h_{f1} > h_{f2}$, the fluid must move "up" the saturation dome — meaning some liquid becomes vapor.

## 2.3 Engineering Significance

> [!NOTE]
> **Why do process engineers love condensate throttling?**
> 1. **Zero external energy input** — pure passive pressure-energy recovery.
> 2. **Free low-grade steam** — the flash steam is reusable in low-pressure reboilers, de-aerators, or pre-heaters.
> 3. **Cascade pressure utilization** — high-pressure steam first does work in a turbine or high-pressure reboiler, then the condensate cascades to the next pressure tier.
> 4. **Capital-cheap** — only a valve and a flash drum are needed (vs. pumps, compressors, or extra boilers).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas are derived from the 1st Law for an open, adiabatic, no-work valve.**

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $h_1 = h_2$ (Isenthalpic) | Valve inlet/outlet enthalpy | kJ/kg |
| 2 | $x_2 = \dfrac{h_{f1} - h_{f2}}{h_{fg2}}$ | Quality (mass fraction vapor) just after valve | dimensionless |
| 3 | $y = \dfrac{h_{f1} - h_{f2}}{h_{fg2}}$ | Flash steam mass fraction | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\dot{m}_{flash} = y \cdot \dot{m}_{cond}$ | Flash steam mass flow rate | kg/s |
| 5 | $\dot{Q}_{recovered} = \dot{m}_{flash} \cdot h_{fg,L}$ | Recovered heat by reusing flash steam | kW |
| 6 | $\eta_{flash} = \dfrac{h_{f1} - h_{f2}}{h_{fg2}}$ | Flash efficiency (identical numerically to $y$) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $h_{f1}^{(n)} = h_{f,stage\_n\,out}$ | Recursive enthalpy for $n$-stage cascade | kJ/kg |
| 8 | $T_{sat}(P)$ | Saturation temperature from steam tables | °C |

> [!WARNING]
> The numerical value of $y$ is **always** less than 1 for any physical pressure drop; if your calculation yields $y \geq 1$, you have either (a) used the wrong pressure levels, (b) used superheated inlet steam (which requires superheat enthalpy), or (c) made an arithmetic error.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Condensate throttling underpins three industrial workhorses:

- **Distillation Reboiler Cascade:** A high-pressure column's bottoms reboiler rejects condensate at, say, 10 bar; this is throttled to 3 bar, where the flash steam drives a downstream low-pressure column's reboiler.
- **Multi-Effect Evaporators (MEE):** Each effect operates at successively lower pressure; vapor from one effect becomes the heating medium for the next — the original implementation of multi-effect distillation.
- **Steam Distribution Networks:** Cascade flash tanks recover low-grade steam that would otherwise be vented to atmosphere.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Flash Steam Quality Equation

### Step 1 — Steady-flow energy equation across the valve

For 1 kg of fluid entering and leaving the throttling valve, with no shaft work, negligible kinetic/potential energy change, and adiabatic walls:

$$
\dot{m}\left(h_1 + \frac{V_1^{2}}{2} + g z_1\right) = \dot{m}\left(h_2 + \frac{V_2^{2}}{2} + g z_2\right) + \dot{Q} - \dot{W}_s
$$

Since $\dot{Q}=0$, $\dot{W}_s=0$, and $\Delta KE \approx \Delta PE \approx 0$, we obtain:

$$
h_1 = h_2
$$

This is the **isenthalpic condition** — the governing assumption of every condensate-throttling problem.

### Step 2 — Express $h_2$ as a two-phase mixture

After throttling, the fluid at $P_L$ is a wet mixture of quality $x$. The mixture enthalpy is:

$$
h_2 = h_{f2} + x \cdot h_{fg2}
$$

where $h_{f2}$ is the saturated-liquid enthalpy at $P_L$ and $h_{fg2} = h_{g2} - h_{f2}$ is the latent heat at $P_L$.

### Step 3 — Equate using the isenthalpic condition

$$
h_{f1} = h_{f2} + x \cdot h_{fg2}
$$

### Step 4 — Solve for the quality $x$

$$
x = \frac{h_{f1} - h_{f2}}{h_{fg2}}
$$

This is the **flash steam quality (mass fraction)** leaving the valve.

### Step 5 — Mass flow of flash steam

For a total condensate flow rate $\dot{m}_{cond}$:

$$
\dot{m}_{flash} = x \cdot \dot{m}_{cond}
$$

### Step 6 — Energy recovered

$$
\dot{Q}_{recovered} = \dot{m}_{flash} \cdot h_{fg2} \;\;[\text{kW}]
$$

This represents the *useful* low-pressure steam energy extracted without any external fuel input.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style 14-Mark Problem)

**Problem Statement:**
Saturated liquid condensate at **10 bar** is throttled to **3 bar** through a control valve. The condensate flow rate is **5 kg/s**. Determine:
(a) Flash steam quality and the mass flow rate of flash steam.
(b) Rate of useful heat recovered if all flash steam is used as a heating medium at 3 bar.

**Steam-Table Data (interpolated standard values):**

$$
h_{f,\,10\,\text{bar}} = 762.81 \;\text{kJ/kg}
$$

$$
h_{f,\,3\,\text{bar}} = 561.47 \;\text{kJ/kg}
$$

$$
h_{fg,\,3\,\text{bar}} = 2163.8 \;\text{kJ/kg}
$$

### Part (a) — Quality and mass flow

$$
x = \frac{h_{f,\,10\,\text{bar}} - h_{f,\,3\,\text{bar}}}{h_{fg,\,3\,\text{bar}}}
$$

$$
x = \frac{762.81 - 561.47}{2163.8}
$$

$$
x = \frac{201.34}{2163.8} = 0.0931
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{flash} = 0.0931 \times 5 = 0.4655 \;\text{kg/s}
$$

### Part (b) — Heat recovered

$$
\dot{Q}_{recovered} = \dot{m}_{flash} \cdot h_{fg,\,3\,\text{bar}}
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_{recovered} = 0.4655 \times 2163.8
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_{recovered} \approx 1007.3 \;\text{kW}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** A 1 MW heat recovery from a *passive* pressure drop is enormous. This single calculation is why refineries place flash-drum-and-reboiler cascades at the heart of every distillation train.

---

## 3.3 Multi-Stage (Cascade) Condensate Throttling — Recursive Formulation

In a **3-stage cascade**, condensate at $P_1$ flashes to $P_2$ → residual liquid at $P_2$ flashes to $P_3$ → residual at $P_3$ flashes to $P_4$.

| Stage | Inlet Enthalpy | Outlet Saturated-Liquid Enthalpy | Outlet Latent Heat | Flash Fraction |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 → 2 | $h_{f1}$ | $h_{f2}$ | $h_{fg2}$ | $y_1 = (h_{f1}-h_{f2})/h_{fg2}$ |
| 2 → 3 | $h_{f2}$ | $h_{f3}$ | $h_{fg3}$ | $y_2 = (h_{f2}-h_{f3})/h_{fg3}$ |
| 3 → 4 | $h_{f3}$ | $h_{f4}$ | $h_{fg4}$ | $y_3 = (h_{f3}-h_{f4})/h_{fg4}$ |

**Total flash steam recovered (per kg inlet condensate):**

$$
y_{total} = y_1 + (1-y_1)\,y_2 + (1-y_1)(1-y_2)\,y_3
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering insight:** A two-stage cascade recovers **~15–25% more** flash steam than a single-stage drop, justifying the modest extra capital cost of a second flash drum in large petrochemical complexes.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Boundary & Limiting Cases

| Condition | Behaviour | Implication |
|---|---|---|
| $P_L = P_H$ | $y = 0$ | No flashing (valve fully closed or bypass open) |
| $P_L \to P_{atm}$ | $y \to y_{max}$ | Maximum flash, but lowest-grade steam |
| Superheated inlet | $h_1 > h_{f1}$ (use $h_1 = h_{f1}+c_p\Delta T$) | More flash than saturated case |
| Sub-cooled inlet | $h_1 < h_{f1}$ | Less flash, may even yield *no* flash if $\Delta T_{sub} > T_{sat,H} - T_{sat,L}$ |
| $P_L < P_{atm}$ | Vacuum operation | Special condenser design required |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Single-Stage Condensate Throttling — Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Boiler or HP Reboiler"] -->|Saturated Liquid P_H| B["Throttling Valve"]
    B -->|Two-Phase Mixture| C["Flash Drum Separator"]
    C -->|Flash Steam P_L| D["LP Reboiler or Pre-heater"]
    C -->|Sub-cooled Liquid P_L| E["Condensate Return or Next Stage"]
    D -->|Condensate at P_L| E
```

## 4.2 Multi-Stage Cascade Throttling — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    HP["High Pressure Source P1 = 25 bar"] --> V1["Throttle Valve 1"]
    V1 --> FD1["Flash Drum 1 P2 = 10 bar"]
    FD1 -->|Flash Steam| LP1["LP Reboiler A 10 bar"]
    FD1 -->|Residual Liquid| V2["Throttle Valve 2"]
    V2 --> FD2["Flash Drum 2 P3 = 3 bar"]
    FD2 -->|Flash Steam| LP2["LP Reboiler B 3 bar"]
    FD2 -->|Residual Liquid| V3["Throttle Valve 3"]
    V3 --> FD3["Flash Drum 3 P4 = 0.5 bar"]
    FD3 -->|Flash Steam| LP3["Deaerator or Feedwater Heater"]
    FD3 -->|Residual Liquid| BT["Condensate Return to Boiler"]
```

## 4.3 Energy & Mass Balance Schematic — State-Point Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["State 1: Saturated Liquid at P_H"] -->|Isenthalpic h1=h2| S2["State 2: Two-Phase at P_L"]
    S2 -->|Phase Separation| S3["State 3a: Vapor Quality x"]
    S2 -->|Phase Separation| S4["State 3b: Saturated Liquid at P_L"]
    S3 -->|Useful LP Steam| UT["Reused in Process"]
    S4 -->|Cascade or Return| CR["Next Stage or Boiler Feed"]
```

## 4.4 Control Loop Integration (KTU 2024 Scheme — Industrial Process Control)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PT["Pressure Transmitter P_H"] -->|4-20 mA| PID["PID Controller"]
    SP["Setpoint"] --> PID
    PID -->|3-15 psi| PCV["Pneumatic Control Valve Throttling Device"]
    PCV -->|Modulates flow| DR["Flash Drum"]
    LT["Level Transmitter in Flash Drum"] -->|4-20 mA| LCV["Level Control Valve"]
    DR --> LCV
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Relevance:** In an actual Industrial Process Control plant, the throttling valve is *not* a hand valve — it is a **modulated pneumatic or electric control valve** driven by a PID controller using a downstream pressure or level transmitter. This integrates condensate throttling into the broader **cascade control architecture** of the steam header.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define condensate throttling. State the fundamental thermodynamic assumption on which all flash-steam calculations are based.**

**Model Answer:**
Condensate throttling is the controlled reduction of pressure of a hot saturated liquid (condensate) through a valve, causing a portion to vaporize spontaneously (flash) at the lower pressure. The fundamental thermodynamic assumption is that the throttling process is **isenthalpic** (constant enthalpy): $h_1 = h_2$, because the valve is adiabatic, has no shaft work, and the kinetic/potential energy changes are negligible. **[3 marks]**

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Write the expression for flash steam quality obtained when saturated liquid at high pressure $P_H$ is throttled to low pressure $P_L$. Define every term.**

**Model Answer:**

$$
x = \frac{h_{f1} - h_{f2}}{h_{fg2}}
$$

where:
- $h_{f1}$ = saturated liquid enthalpy at $P_H$ (kJ/kg)
- $h_{f2}$ = saturated liquid enthalpy at $P_L$ (kJ/kg)
- $h_{fg2}$ = latent heat of vaporization at $P_L$ (kJ/kg)

The flash steam mass flow is $\dot{m}_{flash} = x \cdot \dot{m}_{cond}$. **[3 marks]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> (1) Many students *forget* the isenthalpic assumption and try to apply isentropic relations — this is a guaranteed **2-mark deduction**.
> (2) Students frequently use $h_{fg}$ at the *wrong* pressure (must be $P_L$, not $P_H$) — another **2-mark deduction**.
> (3) Failure to state units (kJ/kg, kg/s, kW) loses **1 mark** per instance.
> (4) Rounding $x$ to a non-physical value ($x \geq 1$) without checking loses **full credit**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]* — Single-Stage Throttling with Energy Recovery

Saturated liquid condensate at **15 bar** leaves a high-pressure distillation column reboiler at a rate of **8 kg/s**. This condensate is throttled to **2 bar** and the resulting flash steam is sent to a low-pressure reboiler. Use steam-table values:

$$
h_{f,\,15\,\text{bar}} = 843.16 \;\text{kJ/kg}, \quad
h_{f,\,2\,\text{bar}} = 504.70 \;\text{kJ/kg}, \quad
h_{fg,\,2\,\text{bar}} = 2201.9 \;\text{kJ/kg}
$$

#### Part (a) — Determine the flash steam quality and the mass flow rate of flash steam. **[7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Identify the inlet condition (saturated liquid at 15 bar):**
$$
h_{f1} = h_{f,\,15\,\text{bar}} = 843.16 \;\text{kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the downstream saturation properties at 2 bar:**
$$
h_{f2} = 504.70 \;\text{kJ/kg}, \quad h_{fg2} = 2201.9 \;\text{kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply isenthalpic assumption:**
$$
h_1 = h_2 = 843.16 \;\text{kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 4 — Express $h_2$ as a two-phase mixture:**
$$
h_2 = h_{f2} + x \cdot h_{fg2}
$$

**Step 5 — Equate and solve for $x$:**
$$
843.16 = 504.70 + x \cdot 2201.9
$$

$$
x = \frac{843.16 - 504.70}{2201.9} = \frac{338.46}{2201.9}
$$

$$
\boxed{x = 0.1537}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the flash steam mass flow:**
$$
\dot{m}_{flash} = x \cdot \dot{m}_{cond} = 0.1537 \times 8
$$

$$
\boxed{\dot{m}_{flash} = 1.230 \;\text{kg/s}}
$$

#### Part (b) — Determine the rate of useful heat recovered at the low-pressure reboiler. **[7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Recovered heat equals latent heat carried by the flash steam:**
$$
\dot{Q}_{recovered} = \dot{m}_{flash} \cdot h_{fg2}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**
$$
\dot{Q}_{recovered} = 1.230 \times 2201.9
$$

**Step 3 — Compute:**
$$
\dot{Q}_{recovered} \approx 2708.3 \;\text{kW}
$$

$$
\boxed{\dot{Q}_{recovered} \approx 2.71 \;\text{MW}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering interpretation:** A passive pressure drop is yielding **2.71 MW** of useful heat, equivalent to burning **~340 kg/h of furnace oil** (calorific value ~10,000 kcal/kg). This is the economic engine behind condensate cascade networks.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024]* — Multi-Stage Cascade Throttling

A refinery generates saturated liquid condensate at **30 bar** at a flow rate of **10 kg/s**. A two-stage cascade throttling is proposed: 30 bar → 8 bar (Stage 1) → 1.5 bar (Stage 2). Determine: **(a)** the flash steam mass flow at each stage, and **(b)** the total useful heat recovered per second.

**Steam-table data:**

$$
h_{f,\,30\,\text{bar}} = 1008.4 \;\text{kJ/kg}, \quad
h_{f,\,8\,\text{bar}} = 720.22 \;\text{kJ/kg}, \quad
h_{f,\,1.5\,\text{bar}} = 467.11 \;\text{kJ/kg}
$$

$$
h_{fg,\,8\,\text{bar}} = 2082.6 \;\text{kJ/kg}, \quad
h_{fg,\,1.5\,\text{bar}} = 2226.5 \;\text{kJ/kg}
$$

#### Part (a) — Flash steam at each stage **[7 marks]**

**Stage 1 (30 → 8 bar):**
$$
y_1 = \frac{1008.4 - 720.22}{2082.6} = \frac{288.18}{2082.6} = 0.1384
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{flash,1} = 0.1384 \times 10 = 1.384 \;\text{kg/s}
$$

Residual liquid mass flow to Stage 2:
$$
\dot{m}_{2} = 10 - 1.384 = 8.616 \;\text{kg/s}
$$

**Stage 2 (8 → 1.5 bar):**
$$
y_2 = \frac{720.22 - 467.11}{2226.5} = \frac{253.11}{2226.5} = 0.1137
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{flash,2} = 0.1137 \times 8.616 = 0.980 \;\text{kg/s}
$$

$$
\boxed{\dot{m}_{flash,1} = 1.384 \;\text{kg/s} \;;\; \dot{m}_{flash,2} = 0.980 \;\text{kg/s}}
$$

#### Part (b) — Total useful heat recovered **[7 marks]**

$$
\dot{Q}_{1} = \dot{m}_{flash,1} \cdot h_{fg,\,8\,\text{bar}} = 1.384 \times 2082.6 = 2882.3 \;\text{kW}
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_{2} = \dot{m}_{flash,2} \cdot h_{fg,\,1.5\,\text{bar}} = 0.980 \times 2226.5 = 2181.97 \;\text{kW}
$$

$$
\dot{Q}_{total} = 2882.3 + 2181.97
$$

$$
\boxed{\dot{Q}_{total} \approx 5.06 \;\text{MW}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparison:** A single-stage 30→1.5 bar drop would yield $y = (1008.4-467.11)/2226.5 = 0.243$, i.e., only 2.43 kg/s flash steam and ~5.40 MW. The two-stage cascade recovers slightly *less* heat but at **higher-quality steam pressures**, which is more useful industrially (LP steam at 8 bar is far more valuable than at 1.5 bar).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (High-Density Summary)**

- [x] Condensate throttling = **isenthalpic** pressure reduction of saturated liquid → **flash steam**.
- [x] Governing equation: $h_1 = h_2$ (1st Law for valve).
- [x] Flash quality: $x = (h_{f1} - h_{f2}) / h_{fg2}$ — **must use $P_L$ in denominator.**
- [x] Mass of flash steam: $\dot{m}_{flash} = x \cdot \dot{m}_{cond}$.
- [x] Recovered heat: $\dot{Q} = \dot{m}_{flash} \cdot h_{fg2}$.
- [x] Maximum $x < 1$ — physical limit; check sanity.
- [x] Multi-stage cascade: $y_{total} = y_1 + (1-y_1)y_2 + (1-y_1)(1-y_2)y_3 + \ldots$
- [x] Throttling valve in modern plants = **modulated control valve** in a **PID cascade**.
- [x] Industrial applications: reboilers, multi-effect evaporators, deaerators, feedwater heaters, steam headers.
- [x] Energy benefit: zero external fuel input — pure pressure-energy recovery.
- [x] Common error: confusing isenthalpic with isentropic — they are **different**!
- [x] Always include **units** in final answer (kJ/kg, kg/s, kW).
- [x] Steam-table lookup is **non-negotiable** in KTU exams — memorize a few key points (saturated liquid at 1, 3, 10, 30 bar).

> [!TIP]
> **One-line mnemonic for the exam:**
> *"Throttle → Constant H → Two-Phase → Quality = (Drop in Liquid H) ÷ (Latent Heat at Low P)."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Level Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT743 - INDUSTRIAL PROCESS CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Level Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Level Control** is a fundamental branch of industrial process control concerned with the regulation, measurement, and maintenance of the height of bulk solid or liquid materials within a vessel, tank, sump, or process container at a desired setpoint. The primary objective is to ensure process continuity, prevent tank overflow (high-high trip conditions), protect downstream pumps from cavitation (low-low trip conditions), and maintain residence time for optimum chemical/physical reactions.

In the KTU 2024 scheme context for *PEICT743 – Industrial Process Control*, Level Control is treated as a critical **regulatory control loop** involving a level sensor (transmitter), a controller (typically PID), and a final control element (control valve on inlet/outlet stream).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Level Control is studied under the umbrella of basic process loops. Students must be able to:
> - Classify level measurement techniques (continuous vs. point/digital).
> - Draw and interpret P&ID (Piping & Instrumentation Diagram) symbols.
> - Analyze first-order and interacting/non-interacting tank dynamics.
> - Apply split-range, cascade, and averaging control strategies.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Bathroom Cistern"

Imagine a traditional **bathroom flush tank (cistern)**. A floating ball rises with the water level. As the water approaches the desired height, the float mechanically closes the inlet valve. If you flush, water exits, the float drops, and the inlet valve opens again. This is the simplest possible **closed-loop level control system**:

- **Process Variable (PV):** Water height
- **Setpoint (SP):** The fixed height (set by the float arm position)
- **Measurement:** Float position
- **Final Control Element:** Inlet valve
- **Disturbance:** The flush (sudden outflow)

Now scale this to a petrochemical refinery or a steam boiler **drum**—the principle is identical, only the precision, speed, and failure-mode handling become critical.

### 1.3 Common Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> - **Density of water at 4 °C:** **ρ = 1000 kg/m³**
> - **Acceleration due to gravity:** **g = 9.81 m/s²**
> - **Hydrostatic pressure reference:** 1 metre of water column ≈ **9.81 kPa**
> - **Standard industrial unit for level:** **mm, cm, or m** (or % of span)
> - **Standard industrial unit for transmitter output:** **4–20 mA** (or 3–15 psi pneumatic legacy)

### 1.4 Why Level Control Matters in Industry

| Industry | Critical Level-Controlled Vessel |
|---|---|
| Oil & Gas | Distillation column reflux drum, separator vessels |
| Power Plant | Steam boiler drum (triple-redundant level) |
| Chemical | CSTR (Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor) |
| Water Treatment | Clarifiers, raw water reservoirs |
| Pulp & Paper | Stock chests, headboxes |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Level Measurement

Level measurement is broadly classified into two categories:

#### A. Continuous Level Measurement
Provides an analog output (4–20 mA) proportional to the absolute level at any instant.

1. **Differential Pressure (DP) Transmitter** — Most common. Uses hydrostatic pressure principle.
2. **Capacitance Probe** — Changes in dielectric constant between two electrodes.
3. **Ultrasonic (Non-contact)** — Time-of-flight of sound waves.
4. **Radar (Non-contact)** — Time-of-flight of microwaves; superior in dusty/foamy media.
5. **Hydrostatic Head Bubbler System** — Bubbles air through a dip tube; back-pressure is proportional to level.
6. **Conductive / Resistance** — For conductive liquids only.
7. **Nucleonic / Radiation-based** — Uses gamma rays; for extreme service (molten metal, very high pressure).

#### B. Point (Discrete) Level Measurement
Provides only ON/OFF or High/Low status — used for alarms, trips, and interlock logic.

1. Float switches (mechanical)
2. Conductivity probes
3. Vibrating fork (tuning fork) switches
4. Optical level switches
5. Ultrasonic point switches

### 2.2 Hydrostatic Pressure Principle (DP Level)

For a closed pressurized vessel, the DP transmitter reads:

$$\Delta P = P_{\text{bottom}} - P_{\text{top}} = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$$

If the gas/space pressure above the liquid is **not negligible**, the transmitter must use a **wet leg** or **dry leg** reference leg to compensate for the static pressure acting on the back of the diaphragm.

### 2.3 In-Phase vs. Out-of-Phase Control

This is a **high-yield KTU concept** for board examinations.

#### Out-of-Phase Control (Most Common & Recommended)
- The control valve is placed on the **inlet** stream.
- An **increase in valve opening → increase in level**.
- Process action is **direct / positive** (inlet valve normally air-to-open).
- Controller action: **Reverse** (more PV > SP → close valve).
- **Safer failure mode**: If air fails, valve closes → level drops slowly → pump trips gracefully.

#### In-Phase Control
- The control valve is placed on the **outlet** stream.
- An **increase in valve opening → decrease in level** (negative response).
- Controller action: **Direct** (more PV > SP → open valve to drain).
- **Riskier**: On instrument air failure, the outlet valve opens fully → vessel empties → possible pump damage or loss of prime.

### 2.4 Mathematical Model of a Single Tank

Consider a cylindrical tank with cross-sectional area **A**, inflow **Fᵢ**, and outflow **Fₒ**.

**Step 1 — Volumetric Mass Balance:**

$$A \cdot \frac{dh}{dt} = F_i(t) - F_o(t)$$

**Step 2 — Linearize the outflow** (valve acts as a linear resistance R):

$$F_o = \frac{h}{R}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and Linearize** around the steady-state operating point (h₀, F₀):

$$A \cdot \frac{d(\Delta h)}{dt} = \Delta F_i - \frac{\Delta h}{R}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Laplace Domain:**

$$H(s) = \frac{R}{\tau s + 1} \cdot F_i(s), \quad \text{where} \ \tau = A \cdot R$$

This is a **first-order system with process gain K_p = R and time constant τ = A·R**.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Symbol | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hydrostatic Pressure | $P = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$ | Pressure at depth h of liquid | Pa |
| Mass Balance | $A \cdot \dfrac{dh}{dt} = F_i - F_o$ | Volumetric flow in minus out | m³/s |
| Tank Cross-Section (Circular) | $A = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^2$ | Cylindrical tank cross-section | m² |
| Tank Cross-Section (Rectangular) | $A = L \times W$ | Rectangular tank | m² |
| Outflow (linearized) | $F_o = \dfrac{h}{R}$ | Linear valve resistance | m³/s |
| Time Constant (Single Tank) | $\tau = A \cdot R$ | First-order time constant | s |
| Process Gain (Single Tank) | $K_p = R$ | Steady-state change in h per unit Fᵢ | s/m² |
| Single Tank Transfer Function | $G(s) = \dfrac{R}{A R s + 1}$ | Laplace domain model | — |
| Two Non-Interacting Tanks | $G(s) = \dfrac{R_2}{(A_1 R_1 s + 1)(A_2 R_2 s + 1)}$ | Series tanks without coupling | — |
| Settling Time (2% criterion) | $t_s \approx 4\tau$ | For first-order systems | s |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Condition Note:** All formulas assume incompressible liquid, constant density, constant cross-sectional area, and turbulent outflow through a linear valve. For compressible flow (gas) or non-linear valves ($F_o = K_v \sqrt{h}$), linearization must be performed around the operating point.

### 2.6 Real-World Utility

The level control transfer function is essential for:
- **Tuning PID controllers** (Ziegler-Nichols, Cohen-Coon, Lambda tuning).
- **Sizing surge tanks** in compressor/pump stations.
- **Predicting pump trip / overflow time** during instrument air failure.
- **Designing cascade loops** where level is the secondary (inner) loop controlling the outflow of a tank whose inflow is controlled by upstream flow.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Transfer Function of a Single-Tank Level Control System

**Given:**
- Cylindrical tank of diameter D = 2 m (Area A)
- Inlet flow $F_i(t)$ through a control valve
- Outlet flow $F_o(t)$ through a manual valve acting as linear resistance R
- Operating point: $h_0 = 3$ m, $F_0 = 0.02$ m³/s

**Step 1 — Compute the cross-sectional area.**

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (2)^2 = \pi \ \text{m}^2 \approx 3.1416 \ \text{m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Compute the valve resistance R at the operating point.**

By definition, at steady state $F_o = h_0 / R$, therefore:

$$R = \frac{h_0}{F_0} = \frac{3}{0.02} = 150 \ \text{s/m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Write the linearized dynamic mass balance.**

Around the steady-state operating point, with deviations $\Delta h = h - h_0$ and $\Delta F_i = F_i - F_0$:

$$A \cdot \frac{d(\Delta h)}{dt} = \Delta F_i - \frac{\Delta h}{R}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$3.1416 \cdot \frac{d(\Delta h)}{dt} = \Delta F_i - \frac{\Delta h}{150}$$

**Step 5 — Take the Laplace Transform (assuming zero initial conditions).**

$$3.1416 \cdot s \cdot \Delta H(s) = \Delta F_i(s) - \frac{\Delta H(s)}{150}$$

**Step 6 — Rearrange to isolate the transfer function $G(s) = \Delta H(s)/\Delta F_i(s)$.**

$$\left(3.1416 \cdot s + \frac{1}{150}\right) \Delta H(s) = \Delta F_i(s)$$

$$\frac{\Delta H(s)}{\Delta F_i(s)} = \frac{1}{3.1416 \cdot s + \frac{1}{150}} = \frac{150}{471.24 \cdot s + 1}$$

**Step 7 — Identify the standard first-order parameters.**

$$\tau = A \cdot R = 3.1416 \times 150 = 471.24 \ \text{s} \quad (\text{time constant})$$

$$K_p = R = 150 \ \text{s/m}^2 \quad (\text{process gain})$$

$$G(s) = \frac{150}{471.24 \, s + 1}$$

This means: **For every 1 m³/s increase in inlet flow, the level rises by 150 m eventually** (this is mathematically correct but unrealistic; in practice the valve rangeability limits the actual gain). The time constant of **471 seconds (~7.85 minutes)** indicates a very slow process — typical of large process tanks.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Two Non-Interacting Tanks in Series

**Given:** Two tanks connected in series, separated by an intermediate manual valve. Tank 1 has area $A_1$, outflow resistance $R_1$. Tank 2 has area $A_2$, outflow resistance $R_2$.

**Step 1 — Write the mass balance for Tank 1.**

$$A_1 \cdot \frac{dh_1}{dt} = F_i - F_{1o}, \quad \text{where} \ F_{1o} = \frac{h_1}{R_1}$$

**Step 2 — Write the mass balance for Tank 2.**

$$A_2 \cdot \frac{dh_2}{dt} = F_{1o} - F_{2o}, \quad \text{where} \ F_{2o} = \frac{h_2}{R_2}$$

**Step 3 — Take Laplace transforms.**

$$(A_1 R_1 s + 1) \cdot H_1(s) = R_1 \cdot F_i(s)$$

$$(A_2 R_2 s + 1) \cdot H_2(s) = R_2 \cdot F_{1o}(s) = R_2 \cdot \frac{H_1(s)}{R_1}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the overall transfer function.**

$$\frac{H_2(s)}{F_i(s)} = \frac{R_1}{A_1 R_1 s + 1} \cdot \frac{R_2}{A_2 R_2 s + 1}$$

This is a **second-order overdamped system** (product of two first-order lags).

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Tuning Recommendations for Level Loops

| Loop Characteristic | Recommended Controller Action | Recommended Control Valve Location | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Slow setpoint changes | Reverse acting on inlet valve | Inlet (Out-of-Phase) | Safe failure, smooth response |
| Surge / buffer vessel | Reverse acting on inlet | Inlet | Allows inflow to vary naturally with demand |
| Boiler steam drum | Reverse acting on feedwater | Inlet | Critical; triple-redundant level sensors |
| Liquid draining | Direct acting on outlet | Outlet (In-Phase) | Faster response for emptying |
| Slurry with high viscosity | PI with low gain | Inlet (preferred) | Reduces oscillations from valve stiction |

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A cylindrical tank (D = 1.5 m) has a steady inlet flow of 10 L/s. The outflow valve has a resistance R = 200 s/m². Determine (a) the steady-state level, and (b) the new level if inlet flow increases by 20%.

**Solution:**

(a) At steady state, $F_i = F_o \implies F_0 = h_0 / R$:

$$h_0 = F_0 \cdot R = 0.01 \ \text{m}^3/\text{s} \times 200 \ \text{s}/\text{m}^2 = 2 \ \text{m}$$

(b) New inlet flow: $F_i^{new} = 12$ L/s = 0.012 m³/s. New steady-state level:

$$h^{new} = F_i^{new} \cdot R = 0.012 \times 200 = 2.4 \ \text{m}$$

**Result:** Level increases by 0.4 m (20% rise matches 20% flow rise — confirming the linear behavior assumed).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Closed-Loop Level Control

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP[Setpoint SP] --> Sum{Comparator}
    Sum -->|Error e| Ctrl[Controller PID]
    Ctrl -->|Output| CV[Control Valve]
    CV -->|Inlet Flow Fi| Tank[Process Tank]
    Tank -->|Level h| Lvl[Level Transmitter]
    Lvl -->|Feedback PV| Sum
    Tank -.->|Outlet Flow Fo| Drain[Drain]
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture — Level Control Strategies

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    LC[Level Control Strategies] --> A[Single Loop Control]
    LC --> B[Cascade Control]
    LC --> C[Split Range Control]
    LC --> D[Averaging Control]
    LC --> E[Override Control]

    A --> A1[Out of Phase Inlet Valve]
    A --> A2[In Phase Outlet Valve]

    B --> B1[Master Level FC]
    B --> B2[Slave Flow FC]

    C --> C1[Valve A Low Flow]
    C --> C2[Valve B High Flow]

    D --> D1[Multiple Level Sensors]
    D --> D2[Mean Signal Used]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Out-of-Phase vs. In-Phase Control

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph OUT[Out of Phase Control]
        OP_SP[SP] --> OP_C[Controller Reverse]
        OP_C --> OP_V[Inlet Valve]
        OP_V --> OP_T[Tank]
        OP_T --> OP_L[Level Sensor]
        OP_L --> OP_SP
    end

    subgraph IN[In Phase Control]
        IP_SP[SP] --> IP_C[Controller Direct]
        IP_C --> IP_V[Outlet Valve]
        IP_V --> IP_T[Tank]
        IP_T --> IP_L[Level Sensor]
        IP_L --> IP_SP
    end
```

### 4.4 P&ID Functional Architecture — Boiler Drum Level Control

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Feed[Feedwater Inlet] --> ValveV[FCV 100 Inlet Valve]
    ValveV --> Drum[Boiler Drum]
    Drum --> SteamV[Steam to Turbine Out]
    Drum --> LevelT[LT 101 Level Transmitter]
    Drum --> LevelT2[LT 102 Redundant]
    Drum --> LevelT3[LT 103 Triple Redundant]
    LevelT --> Vote[Voter Selector]
    LevelT2 --> Vote
    LevelT3 --> Vote
    Vote --> LIC[LIC 100 Level Indicating Controller]
    LIC --> ValveV
    LIC --> AlarmHigh[HH Alarm]
    LIC --> AlarmLow[LL Alarm]
```

### 4.5 Two Non-Interacting Tank System Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    In[F In] --> T1[Tank 1 A1 R1]
    T1 --> V1[Manual Valve R1]
    V1 --> T2[Tank 2 A2 R2]
    T2 --> V2[Manual Valve R2]
    V2 --> Out[F Out]
    T1 --> H1[H1 Measurement]
    T2 --> H2[H2 Measurement]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**

Define **Level Control**. List any **four continuous level measurement methods** used in the process industry.

**Model Answer:**

Level Control is the automatic regulation of the height of a liquid or bulk solid within a process vessel to a desired setpoint, ensuring process continuity, safety, and stable downstream operation.

Four continuous level measurement methods:

1. **Differential Pressure (DP) Transmitter** — based on hydrostatic pressure principle.
2. **Capacitance Level Probe** — based on change in dielectric constant between electrodes.
3. **Ultrasonic Level Transmitter** — based on time-of-flight of sound waves.
4. **Radar Level Transmitter** — based on time-of-flight of electromagnetic (microwave) signals.

> [!NOTE]
> Examiners also accept: Conductive probe, Hydrostatic bubbler system, and Nucleonic (radiation-based) systems.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**

Explain the difference between **in-phase** and **out-of-phase** level control. Which one is preferred for safety and why?

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | In-Phase Control | Out-of-Phase Control |
|---|---|---|
| Valve Location | Outlet stream | Inlet stream |
| Process Action | Reverse (PV ↑ → Valve ↑ → PV ↓) | Direct (PV ↑ → Valve ↓ → PV ↓) |
| Controller Action | Direct acting | Reverse acting |
| Failure Mode (Air Fail) | Valve opens → Tank drains → Pump damage | Valve closes → Tank fills slowly → Controlled trip |

**Out-of-phase control is preferred** because on instrument air failure (loss of pneumatic signal), the inlet valve fails **closed** (air-to-open). The vessel slowly fills up, but does not empty catastrophically. The high-level alarm gives operators time to intervene. In contrast, in-phase control with an air-to-open valve would cause the vessel to drain immediately, risking pump cavitation.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Provided — Select Either Question A OR Question B**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**

**(a)** Derive the **transfer function of a single-tank level control system**. State clearly all the assumptions used. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A cylindrical tank of diameter **2 m** is fed by an inlet control valve. The outflow occurs through a manual valve with linear resistance **R = 250 s/m²**. At steady state, the inflow is **F = 0.015 m³/s**.

Calculate:

1. The steady-state level in the tank.
2. The process time constant.
3. The new steady-state level if the inflow increases by **30%**.
4. The 63.2% response time for a step change in inlet flow.

**(7 Marks)**

---

##### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) — Derivation:**

**Assumptions:**
1. Liquid is incompressible (constant density ρ).
2. Tank cross-section is constant.
3. Density is uniform throughout (no stratification).
4. Outflow is linearly related to level (linear valve).
5. Temperature effects are negligible.

**Step 1:** Volumetric balance:

$$A \cdot \frac{dh}{dt} = F_i(t) - F_o(t)$$

**Step 2:** Linear outflow relationship:

$$F_o = \frac{h}{R}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute:

$$A \cdot \frac{dh}{dt} + \frac{h}{R} = F_i(t)$$

**Step 4:** Laplace transform with zero initial condition:

$$A \cdot s \cdot H(s) + \frac{H(s)}{R} = F_i(s)$$

**Step 5:** Solve for the transfer function:

$$G(s) = \frac{H(s)}{F_i(s)} = \frac{R}{A R s + 1} = \frac{K_p}{\tau s + 1}$$

where $K_p = R$ and $\tau = A \cdot R$. — **Full 7 Marks**

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** D = 2 m, R = 250 s/m², F₀ = 0.015 m³/s.

**1. Cross-sectional area:**

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (2)^2 = \pi = 3.1416 \ \text{m}^2$$

**2. Steady-state level:**

$$h_0 = F_0 \cdot R = 0.015 \times 250 = 3.75 \ \text{m}$$

**3. Time constant:**

$$\tau = A \cdot R = 3.1416 \times 250 = 785.4 \ \text{s}$$

**4. New steady-state level (30% increase):**

$$F_{new} = 0.015 \times 1.30 = 0.0195 \ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$h_{new} = F_{new} \cdot R = 0.0195 \times 250 = 4.875 \ \text{m}$$

**5. 63.2% response time** = one time constant:

$$t_{63.2\%} = \tau = 785.4 \ \text{s} \approx 13.09 \ \text{minutes}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students frequently forget to convert litres to m³ (1 L/s = 0.001 m³/s) and lose 1–2 marks. Also, students often write 4τ instead of τ for the 63.2% rise time — note that 4τ is the 98% (settling) time, not 63.2%.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the working of a **differential pressure (DP) level transmitter** in a closed pressurized vessel. Mention the role of the **dry leg** and **wet leg** compensation. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain **split-range level control** for a vessel that must handle both very low and very high inflow rates. Draw the characteristic curve of the split-range controller. **(7 Marks)**

---

##### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) — DP Level Transmitter:**

In a closed pressurized vessel, the liquid exerts hydrostatic pressure at its bottom proportional to the height of the liquid column:

$$P_{\text{low}} = P_{\text{space}} + \rho \cdot g \cdot h$$

A differential pressure (DP) transmitter has **two sensing ports**:

- **High-pressure port (HP):** connected to the bottom of the tank via an **impulse tube**.
- **Low-pressure port (LP):** connected to the gas space above the liquid.

The DP transmitter reads:

$$\Delta P = P_{\text{low}} - P_{\text{high}} = \rho \cdot g \cdot h$$

Since both ports sense the same gas pressure $P_{\text{space}}$, the transmitter automatically subtracts it out — **a key advantage of the DP method for pressurized vessels**.

**Dry Leg Compensation:**
Used when the **condensate leg is filled with a non-condensing inert gas** (typically nitrogen) on the LP side. The dry leg must be protected from temperature variations that could change the gas density.

**Wet Leg Compensation:**
Used when the process gas may **condense** in the LP impulse tube. The wet leg is filled with a clean, compatible reference liquid (often water or a process-compatible fluid) of known density $\rho_{ref}$, so that:

$$\Delta P = (\rho - \rho_{ref}) \cdot g \cdot h$$

This compensates for the hydrostatic head of the wet leg fluid itself. Wet legs are preferred for **steam service** to prevent the reference leg from boiling dry. — **Full 7 Marks**

---

**Part (b) — Split-Range Level Control:**

**Scenario:** A reactor must handle inflow rates ranging from **0 to 200% of normal design flow**. A single control valve cannot provide adequate turndown with good resolution across this wide range.

**Solution:** Use **two valves** — a small valve (V1) for low flow and a large valve (V2) for high flow — both fed by a single controller output signal of 4–20 mA (or 3–15 psi).

**Typical Split-Range Mapping:**

| Controller Output | Small Valve V1 | Large Valve V2 |
|---|---|---|
| 4 mA (0%) | 100% Closed | 100% Closed |
| 12 mA (50%) | 100% Open | 100% Closed |
| 16 mA (75%) | 100% Open | 50% Open |
| 20 mA (100%) | 100% Open | 100% Open |

**Working Principle:**
- When the controller output is in the **lower half (4–12 mA)**, only the small valve V1 operates, providing **fine control** at low flows.
- When the output is in the **upper half (12–20 mA)**, valve V1 saturates fully open, and valve V2 begins to open, providing **high capacity**.

**Characteristic Curve (Mermaid):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X[Controller Output 4 to 20 mA] --> Y1[V1 Small Valve Position]
    X --> Y2[V2 Large Valve Position]
    Y1 --> C1[V1 opens 0 to 100 percent between 4 and 12 mA]
    Y2 --> C2[V2 opens 0 to 100 percent between 12 and 20 mA]
```

This configuration gives **wide rangeability with good resolution** at both low and high flows. — **Full 7 Marks**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** In split-range problems, students frequently forget to specify the **valve failure position** (air-to-open or air-to-close). Always state the fail-safe action. Also, the characteristic curve should clearly show the **overlap or non-overlap** region — a common area of partial marks loss.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Level Control (PEICT743, Module 3)**

- **Definition:** Level Control = regulating the height of a material in a vessel using feedback.
- **Core Equation (Mass Balance):** $A \cdot \dfrac{dh}{dt} = F_i - F_o$
- **Hydrostatic Pressure:** $P = \rho g h$
- **Transfer Function (Single Tank):** $G(s) = \dfrac{R}{A R s + 1}$ → First-order lag with gain R and time constant AR.
- **Out-of-Phase Control** = valve on **inlet**; controller **reverse** acting; **safer** failure mode.
- **In-Phase Control** = valve on **outlet**; controller **direct** acting; **riskier** failure mode.
- **DP Transmitter:** Most common continuous level sensor; uses hydrostatic principle.
- **Wet Leg vs. Dry Leg:** Wet leg filled with reference liquid (steam service); dry leg filled with inert gas.
- **Split-Range Control:** Two valves share one controller signal — one valve for low flow, the other for high flow.
- **Cascade Level Control:** Level is the master (outer) loop; flow is the slave (inner) loop.
- **Interacting Tanks:** Coupled dynamics (faster response, more complex transfer function).
- **Non-Interacting Tanks:** Independent dynamics in series (product of two first-order lags).
- **Units:** Level in **m**; flow in **m³/s**; density in **kg/m³**; pressure in **Pa** (or **kPa**).
- **63.2% Rise Time** for a first-order system = one time constant τ.
- **Settling Time (2% criterion)** ≈ 4τ.
- **KTU Hot Topic:** Always mention **fail-safe valve action** in any control loop P&ID question.
- **Real-World Examples:** Boiler drum, refinery reflux drum, CSTR, surge tanks, clarifiers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** KTU examiners typically award 2 marks for a correctly labeled diagram, 2 marks for deriving the transfer function, 2 marks for numerical computation, and the remaining marks for explanation and unit handling. Practice drawing clean **block diagrams with labeled signal flows**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bypass Control in Steam Heater
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT743 - INDUSTRIAL PROCESS CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Bypass Control in Steam Heater

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Bypass Control in a Steam Heater** is a process control strategy in which the degree of heating applied to a process stream is regulated not by modulating the steam (utility) supply, but by *splitting* the process fluid itself into two parallel paths — a **heating leg** that passes through the heat exchanger, and a **bypass leg** that shunts unheated fluid around it. The two streams rejoin at a downstream mixing point, and the temperature of the combined stream is maintained at setpoint by a controller that repositions a control valve installed on the bypass line. The manipulated variable (MV) is the **fraction of process flow routed through the heater**, while the controlled variable (CV) is the **outlet temperature measured after mixing**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** This topic belongs to Module 3 under *Multi-Loop and Special Control Schemes* and is closely related to *Split-Range*, *Ratio*, and *Override Control*. Bypass control is essentially a **flow-split / ratio control problem** coupled to a **temperature feedback loop**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hot Tap Water and Cold Tap" Mental Model

Imagine a household shower: you have a hot water pipe and a cold water pipe, and you adjust the **cold water valve** to set the final temperature. You are not changing the temperature of the hot water; you are deciding **how much** of the cold stream to mix with the hot stream to obtain a comfortable temperature.

In a steam heater:
- The **heater outlet stream** is your "hot water."
- The **bypass stream** is your "cold water."
- The **bypass control valve** is your "cold tap."
- The **mixed outlet temperature** is what the bather (controller) feels and adjusts.

> [!NOTE]
> This analogy is extremely useful during KTU viva voce. Examiners often ask, *"Why don't we throttle the steam directly?"* The answer: it is equivalent, but **bypass control allows the use of a much smaller, faster, and less expensive control valve on the process side, and avoids problems with steam valve erosion, water hammer, and condensate handling.**

### 1.3 Physical Setup of a Typical Steam Heater

| Component | Function | Operating Parameter |
|---|---|---|
| Process Inlet Pipe | Carries cold process fluid | Flow $F$, Inlet Temp $T_i$ |
| Steam Chest / Shell | Contains condensing steam | Steam Pressure $P_s$, Temp $T_s$ |
| Heating Tube Bundle | Transfers latent heat from steam to process fluid | Heat transfer area $A$ |
| Process Outlet (Heated Leg) | Carries heated fluid to mixing point | Flow $f$, Temp $T_h$ |
| Bypass Line | Carries unheated fluid around heater | Flow $(F-f)$, Temp $T_i$ |
| Mixing Tee / Junction | Combines both streams | Outlet Temp $T_o$ |
| Temperature Transmitter (TT) | Senses $T_o$ | Output 4–20 mA |
| Temperature Controller (TC) | Compares $T_o$ with setpoint | Output 4–20 mA |
| Bypass Control Valve (TV) | Modulates $f$ / $(F-f)$ | Pneumatic / electric actuator |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Bypass Control? — The Engineering Motivation

Conventional **steam-side throttling** has several practical drawbacks, particularly when the process flow rate is large compared to the heat duty requirement:

1. **Valve Sizing Penalty:** A steam control valve must handle the *entire* heating load. For large process flows, this demands an impractically large steam valve.
2. **Latent Heat Sensitivity:** Modulating steam flow directly changes the *condensing rate* and hence the *heat transfer coefficient* — a nonlinear effect.
3. **Steam Hammer / Water Hammer:** Sudden steam valve closures cause pressure transients.
4. **Condensate Management:** Throttling condensate creates two-phase flow issues downstream.

Bypass control shifts the control valve onto the **process side**, where:
- The valve is **smaller** (it modulates only a portion of total flow).
- The process fluid is **single-phase liquid** — clean, predictable hydraulics.
- Response is **faster** because the valve controls flow directly, not the thermal boundary layer.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Bypass control is preferred when the **heat transfer area is fixed** (i.e., the heater is oversized) and the desired temperature is only slightly above the inlet temperature. The bypass scheme effectively uses the heater as a *constant-temperature heat source* and blends cold fluid to achieve trim.

### 2.2 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Cold process fluid** enters at temperature $T_i$ and total mass flow rate $F$.
2. The flow is split at a tee: a portion $f$ enters the **steam heater**, the remainder $(F - f)$ flows through the **bypass line**.
3. Inside the heater, the process fluid is heated from $T_i$ to $T_h$ (where $T_h \le T_s$, the saturation temperature of steam at the operating pressure).
4. The heated stream and the bypass stream meet at the **mixing point** downstream of the heater.
5. The mixed outlet temperature $T_o$ is measured by **TT** and sent to the **TC**.
6. The TC compares $T_o$ with setpoint $T_{sp}$ and adjusts the **bypass valve opening**:
   - If $T_o < T_{sp}$ → controller **closes** the bypass valve → more flow through heater → $T_o$ rises.
   - If $T_o > T_{sp}$ → controller **opens** the bypass valve → less flow through heater → $T_o$ falls.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reverse-Acting Note:** Because closing the bypass valve *increases* heater throughput and *raises* $T_o$, the **TC must be configured as reverse-acting** when the bypass valve is *air-to-open*, or **direct-acting** if the valve is *air-to-close*. This is a frequent KTU viva question.

### 2.3 Assumptions Used in the Standard Analysis

| # | Assumption | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| A1 | Constant steam pressure → constant $T_h$ | Steam header is well-regulated |
| A2 | Constant process inlet temperature $T_i$ | Upstream surge protection |
| A3 | Constant specific heat $C_p$ of process fluid | Small temperature range |
| A4 | Negligible heat losses to surroundings | Heater is well-insulated |
| A5 | Perfect mixing at the junction | Turbulent flow in the mixing tee |
| A6 | Steady-state thermal behavior | Lumped analysis |
| A7 | Constant total flow $F$ | Adequate upstream pressure regulation |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F = f + f_b$ where $f_b = F - f$ | Total flow = Heater flow + Bypass flow | kg/s |
| 2 | $Q = f \cdot C_p \cdot (T_h - T_i)$ | Heat picked up by process stream in heater | kW |
| 3 | $Q = \dot{m}_s \cdot \lambda_s$ | Heat released by condensing steam | kW |
| 4 | $F \cdot C_p \cdot T_o = f \cdot C_p \cdot T_h + (F-f) \cdot C_p \cdot T_i$ | Energy balance at mixing point (steady) | kW·°C |
| 5 | $\boxed{T_o = T_i + \dfrac{f}{F}\,(T_h - T_i)}$ | **Master output-temperature equation** | °C |
| 6 | $\dfrac{f}{F} = \dfrac{T_o - T_i}{T_h - T_i}$ | Required bypass fraction for setpoint | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\Delta T = T_h - T_i$ | Heater temperature rise | °C |
| 8 | $\eta_{mix} = \dfrac{T_o - T_i}{T_h - T_i} = \dfrac{f}{F}$ | Mixing effectiveness (0 to 1) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $K_p = \dfrac{\partial T_o}{\partial f} = \dfrac{T_h - T_i}{F}$ | Steady-state process gain | °C·s/kg |
| 10 | $T_h = T_s - \dfrac{Q}{U \cdot A}$ | Actual heater outlet temperature | °C |
| 11 | $\tau = \dfrac{V_{heater} \cdot \rho}{f}$ | Heater residence time | s |
| 12 | $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K_p \, e^{-L_s}}{\tau s + 1}$ | FOPDT process model | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
