> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Equation 5 is the single most important equation for this topic. Memorize the derivation, the meaning of every term, and the limiting cases: when $f = 0$ (full bypass), $T_o = T_i$; when $f = F$ (no bypass), $T_o = T_h$. These two limits are guaranteed sub-questions.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Crude Oil Pre-heating Trains** in refineries — bypass valves trim temperature to downstream desalters.
- **Feedwater Heaters** in power plants — bypass around LP/HP heaters during startup.
- **Food and Dairy Industry** — pasteurizers use bypass control to avoid overcooking milk.
- **Chemical Reactor Feed Pre-heating** — protects catalyst from thermal shock.
- **HVAC Heating Coils** — bypass control valves modulate reheat water.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Master Output-Temperature Equation

**Starting Point:** A bypass control steam heater receives total process mass flow $F$ at temperature $T_i$. The flow splits into a heater stream $f$ and a bypass stream $(F - f)$. Both join at a mixing point producing an outlet at $T_o$.

**Step 1 — Process-side energy pickup in the heater**

The fraction $f$ entering the heater absorbs heat $Q$ and exits at $T_h$:

$$
Q = f \, C_p \, (T_h - T_i)
$$

**Step 2 — Steam-side energy release**

If the steam condenses completely, the latent heat released is:

$$
Q = \dot{m}_s \, \lambda_s
$$

Equating the two gives the steam requirement:

$$
\dot{m}_s \, \lambda_s = f \, C_p \, (T_h - T_i)
$$

$$
\dot{m}_s = \dfrac{f \, C_p \, (T_h - T_i)}{\lambda_s}
$$

**Step 3 — Energy balance at the mixing point (steady state)**

Energy in = Energy out (with no heat loss in the mixing section, per assumption A5):

$$
F \, C_p \, T_o = f \, C_p \, T_h + (F - f) \, C_p \, T_i
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify by dividing both sides by $C_p$**

$$
F \, T_o = f \, T_h + (F - f) \, T_i
$$

**Step 5 — Expand the right-hand side**

$$
F \, T_o = f \, T_h + F \, T_i - f \, T_i
$$

**Step 6 — Group terms containing $f$**

$$
F \, T_o = F \, T_i + f \, (T_h - T_i)
$$

**Step 7 — Divide both sides by $F$**

$$
T_o = T_i + \dfrac{f}{F}\,(T_h - T_i)
$$

This is the **master output equation** and is the key result for all bypass-control analysis.

**Verification of limiting cases:**

$$
\text{If } f = 0 \;\;(\text{full bypass}) \Rightarrow T_o = T_i + 0 = T_i \quad \checkmark
$$

$$
\text{If } f = F \;\;(\text{no bypass}) \Rightarrow T_o = T_i + (T_h - T_i) = T_h \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 8 — Solve for the required fraction in terms of the setpoint**

Rearranging for $f$:

$$
\dfrac{f}{F} = \dfrac{T_o - T_i}{T_h - T_i}
$$

$$
\boxed{f = F \cdot \dfrac{T_{sp} - T_i}{T_h - T_i}}
$$

This is the formula the controller implicitly computes at every instant.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Steady-State Process Gain $K_p$

Treating $T_h$ and $T_i$ as constants (per assumptions A1 and A2), differentiate the master equation with respect to the manipulated variable $f$:

$$
\dfrac{\partial T_o}{\partial f} = \dfrac{T_h - T_i}{F} = \text{constant}
$$

$$
\boxed{K_p = \dfrac{T_h - T_i}{F}}
$$

**Interpretation:** The process is **linear** in $f$ at steady state. The gain is large when the heater delta-T $(T_h - T_i)$ is large and when total flow $F$ is small.

### 3.3 Bypass Fraction for a Typical Numerical Scenario

**Given (typical KTU problem data):**
- $F = 10$ kg/s
- $T_i = 30$ °C
- $T_h = 150$ °C (steam at ~4 bar gauge)
- $T_{sp} = 90$ °C

**Required:** Bypass flow $(F - f)$ and steam mass flow rate $\dot{m}_s$. Take $C_p = 4.18$ kJ/(kg·°C), $\lambda_s = 2140$ kJ/kg.

**Step 1 — Compute required heater-side flow $f$**

$$
f = 10 \times \dfrac{90 - 30}{150 - 30} = 10 \times \dfrac{60}{120} = 10 \times 0.5 = 5 \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Bypass flow**

$$
F - f = 10 - 5 = 5 \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Heat duty**

$$
Q = 5 \times 4.18 \times (150 - 30) = 5 \times 4.18 \times 120 = 2508 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 4 — Steam requirement**

$$
\dot{m}_s = \dfrac{2508}{2140} \approx 1.172 \text{ kg/s} \approx 4.22 \text{ tons/hr}
$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Temperature-Control Schemes for Steam Heaters

| Feature | Steam-Side Throttling | Condensate Throttling | **Bypass Control** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Manipulated Variable | Steam flow $W$ | Condensate level/outflow | Process flow $f$ through heater |
| Valve Location | Steam inlet line | Condensate outlet line | Process bypass line |
| Valve Size | Large for high duty | Medium | **Small** (advantage) |
| Actuator Dynamics | Slow (thermal lag) | Medium | **Fast** (flow split) |
| Nonlinearity | High (latent heat + UA) | Medium | **Low** (linear in $f$) |
| Turndown Ratio | Limited | Limited | **Wide** (0 → F) |
| Operating Cost | Best (matches load) | Moderate | Higher (constant steam draw) |
| Best Application | Variable, large duty | Stable loads, flooded heater | **Slightly-oversized heater, trim control** |
| Steam Hammer Risk | Yes | Low | **None** |
| Maintenance Burden | High (erosion, leakage) | Medium (two-phase) | **Low (single-phase liquid)** |
| Control Complexity | Standard PID | Standard PID | Standard PID + flow split consideration |

### 3.5 Dynamic Model — First-Order Plus Dead Time (FOPDT)

For control-loop tuning (e.g., Ziegler–Nichols or Cohen–Coon), the bypass-heater system is commonly modeled as:

$$
G_p(s) = \dfrac{K_p \, e^{-L_s}}{\tau s + 1}
$$

Where:
- $K_p = \dfrac{T_h - T_i}{F}$ (°C·s/kg) — steady-state gain
- $\tau = \dfrac{V_{heater} \cdot \rho}{f}$ (s) — heater residence time
- $L$ = measurement and transport dead time (s)

The dead time $L$ arises from:
- Transportation lag from mixing point to TT sensor
- Sensor thermal time constant
- Valve actuator response time

The mixing point itself is essentially **instantaneous** (algebraic) at low frequencies and contributes negligible dynamics.

### 3.6 Controller Tuning Recommendation

For a FOPDT model, Cohen–Coon tuning (good for dominant dead-time processes) gives:

$$
K_c = \dfrac{1}{K_p} \cdot \dfrac{\tau}{L} \cdot \left( 1 + \dfrac{L}{3\tau} \right)
$$

$$
\tau_I = L \cdot \dfrac{30 + 3(L/\tau)}{9 + 20(L/\tau)}
$$

$$
\tau_D = L \cdot \dfrac{4}{11 + 2(L/\tau)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **For KTU:** Numerical tuning problems using Cohen–Coon or Ziegler–Nichols appear as 7-mark sub-questions. Always state the form of $G_p(s)$ first, identify $K_p, \tau, L$, then plug into the tuning formulas.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Piping & Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID) of Bypass Control Steam Heater

The diagram below shows the complete process flow, control loop, and instrumentation as it would appear in a real plant P&ID. All ISA-standard symbols are used in the Mermaid schematic.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[/"PROCESS INLET\nF kg/s, Ti C"/]
    node2(("SPLIT TEE"))
    node3["HEATING LEG\nf kg/s"]
    node4["STEAM HEATER\nHeat Exchanger"]
    node5{{"BY-PASS VALVE\nTV-101 Pneumatic"}}
    node6["BY-PASS LEG\nF-f kg/s, Ti C"]
    node7(("MIXING TEE"))
    node8[/"PROCESS OUTLET\nFo = Tsp C"/]
    node9(["TT-101\nTemperature Tx"])
    node10["TC-101\nTemperature Controller"]
    node11(["PT-101\nSteam Pressure Tx"])
    node12[/"STEAM INLET\nPs bar, Ts C"/]
    node13[/"CONDENSATE OUT"/]

    node1 --> node2
    node2 --> node3
    node2 --> node5
    node3 --> node4
    node4 --> node7
    node5 --> node6
    node6 --> node7
    node7 --> node8
    node8 --> node9
    node9 --> node10
    node10 --> node5
    node12 --> node4
    node4 --> node13
    node11 --> node4
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram of the Closed-Loop Control System

This block diagram is the canonical representation used in KTU theory questions. The reference temperature is $T_{sp}$, the disturbance is inlet temperature $T_i$, the manipulated variable is $f$, and the controlled variable is $T_o$.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sp[/"Tsp\nSetpoint"/]
    sum(("Sum Junction"))
    ctrl[/"GC(s)\nTC-101 Controller"/]
    valve[/"GV(s)\nBypass Valve"/]
    proc[/"GP(s)\nHeater + Mixing"/]
    sensor[/"GH(s)\nTT-101 + Transmitter"/]
    out[/"To\nOutlet Temp"/]
    disturb[/"Td\nInlet Temp Disturbance"/]

    sp --> sum
    sum -->|Error e| ctrl
    ctrl --> valve
    valve -->|MV = f| proc
    proc --> out
    out --> sensor
    sensor --> sum
    disturb -.-> proc
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture of the Control Logic

The following diagram explains *how the controller reacts* to changes in the controlled variable. This is the kind of "flow-of-action" diagram a KTU examiner expects for full marks on a 14-mark descriptive question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA["Measure To via TT-101"]
    startB["Compare To with Tsp at TC-101"]
    startC["Compute Error e = Tsp - To"]
    startD{"Sign of e?"}
    pathE["e > 0: To too LOW\nMore heating required"]
    pathF["e = 0: To at setpoint\nHold valve position"]
    pathG["e < 0: To too HIGH\nReduce heating"]
    actH["CLOSE bypass valve\n--> Increases f"]
    actI["OPEN bypass valve\n--> Decreases f"]
    loopJ["Process repeats continuously\nUpdate rate 1 sec"]
    endK["Outlet temperature\ncontrolled at Tsp"]

    startA --> startB --> startC --> startD
    startD -->|Yes| pathE --> actH
    startD -->|No| pathF
    startD -->|Negative| pathG --> actI
    actH --> loopJ
    actI --> loopJ
    pathF --> loopJ
    loopJ --> endK
```

### 4.4 Energy-Flow Schematic of the Steam Heater

The diagram below illustrates the *physical* energy streams — heat released by condensing steam on one side and heat absorbed by the process fluid on the other — together with the bypass leg that remains at $T_i$ until the mixing point.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA[/"Cold Process Fluid\nTi C, F kg/s"/]
    srcB[/"Saturated Steam\nTs C, Ps bar"/]
    unitX["STEAM HEATER\nSurface Area A\nOverall Coeff U"]
    sinkA[/"Heated Stream\nTh C, f kg/s"/]
    sinkB[/"Bypass Stream\nTi C, F-f kg/s"/]
    mixT(("MIXING TEE"))
    sinkC[/"Mixed Outlet\nTo C, F kg/s"/]
    sinkD[/"Condensate Out\nTsat, m dot s"/]

    srcA --> unitX
    srcB --> unitX
    unitX --> sinkA
    unitX --> sinkD
    srcA -.->|Bypass route| sinkB
    sinkA --> mixT
    sinkB --> mixT
    mixT --> sinkC
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**
*Define "Bypass Control" as applied to a steam heater. State two situations where this control scheme is preferred over direct steam-throttling.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Bypass control in a steam heater is a temperature-control strategy in which the process flow itself is split into two parallel streams — one routed through the steam-heated exchanger and the other routed through a bypass line — and the outlet temperature after the mixing point is regulated by modulating a control valve on the bypass line. (2 marks)

It is preferred over direct steam-throttling in the following situations:
1. When the heat-transfer area of the heater is *oversized* and only a *trim* of heating is required (i.e., $T_{sp}$ is only slightly above $T_i$). (½ mark)
2. When the process flow rate is very large, since the bypass valve is *smaller and cheaper* than a steam-side valve that would otherwise have to handle the full duty. (½ mark)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Retest]**
*For a steam heater with bypass control, derive the relationship between the outlet temperature $T_o$, the inlet temperature $T_i$, the heater-outlet temperature $T_h$, and the flow fraction $f/F$.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Performing a steady-state energy balance at the mixing point:

$$
F \, C_p \, T_o = f \, C_p \, T_h + (F - f) \, C_p \, T_i
$$

Dividing by $F \, C_p$ and rearranging:

$$
\boxed{T_o = T_i + \dfrac{f}{F}\,(T_h - T_i)}
$$

This shows that $T_o$ is a linear weighted average of $T_i$ and $T_h$ with weights $(1 - f/F)$ and $(f/F)$ respectively. (½ mark for interpretation)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks**

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> *(a)* A process fluid of mass flow rate $F = 8$ kg/s enters a steam heater at $T_i = 40$ °C. The heater is supplied with saturated steam at 4 bar gauge (steam saturation temperature $T_s \approx 152$ °C) and the heater outlet temperature is $T_h = 145$ °C. A bypass line carries the remaining flow around the heater. Calculate (i) the flow through the heater $f$ required to obtain an outlet temperature of 95 °C, (ii) the bypass flow rate, and (iii) the heat duty of the heater. Take $C_p = 4.18$ kJ/(kg·°C) and $\lambda_s = 2140$ kJ/kg. **(7 marks)**
> *(b)* Explain the working principle of bypass control in a steam heater with a neat block diagram. State the controller action required when the bypass valve is air-to-open. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Given:**
- $F = 8$ kg/s
- $T_i = 40$ °C
- $T_h = 145$ °C
- $T_{sp} = 95$ °C
- $C_p = 4.18$ kJ/(kg·°C)
- $\lambda_s = 2140$ kJ/kg

**Step 1 — Flow through heater** (2 marks)

$$
f = F \cdot \dfrac{T_{sp} - T_i}{T_h - T_i} = 8 \times \dfrac{95 - 40}{145 - 40} = 8 \times \dfrac{55}{105} = 8 \times 0.5238 = 4.19 \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Bypass flow** (2 marks)

$$
F - f = 8 - 4.19 = 3.81 \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Heat duty and steam requirement** (3 marks)

$$
Q = f \cdot C_p \cdot (T_h - T_i) = 4.19 \times 4.18 \times (145 - 40) = 4.19 \times 4.18 \times 105
$$

$$
Q = 4.19 \times 438.9 \approx 1839 \text{ kW}
$$

Steam consumption:

$$
\dot{m}_s = \dfrac{Q}{\lambda_s} = \dfrac{1839}{2140} \approx 0.859 \text{ kg/s} \approx 3.09 \text{ tons/hr}
$$

---

**Solution to Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Working principle (4 marks):**
Cold process fluid at temperature $T_i$ enters the system and is split into two streams at a tee. A fraction $f$ is sent through the steam heater, where it is heated to $T_h$ by latent heat released from condensing steam. The remaining fraction $(F - f)$ flows through the bypass line at $T_i$. Both streams combine at a downstream mixing point. The mixed outlet temperature $T_o$ is sensed by a temperature transmitter (TT) and compared with the setpoint in a temperature controller (TC). The controller output drives the bypass control valve to adjust the fraction $f$. If $T_o$ is too low, the controller closes the valve, increasing $f$ and raising $T_o$. If $T_o$ is too high, the controller opens the valve, decreasing $f$ and lowering $T_o$. This feedback action continues to maintain $T_o$ at the setpoint.

**Block diagram (2 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sp[/"Tsp"/]
    sum(("Sum"))
    ctrl[/"GC(s)\nTC"/]
    valve[/"GV(s)\nBypass Valve"/]
    proc[/"GP(s)\nHeater + Mixer"/]
    sensor[/"GH(s)\nTT"/]
    out[/"To"/]
    dist[/"Td\nInlet Temp"/]

    sp --> sum
    sum -->|e| ctrl
    ctrl --> valve
    valve -->|f| proc
    proc --> out
    out --> sensor
    sensor --> sum
    dist -.-> proc
```

**Controller action (1 mark):** With an **air-to-open** bypass valve, increasing controller output (more air pressure) opens the valve, which *reduces* $f$ and *decreases* $T_o$. Since we want the output to *increase* when error $e = T_{sp} - T_o$ is *positive*, the controller must be configured as **reverse-acting** (i.e., decrease output on positive $e$). Equivalently: **with air-to-open valve, controller action = REVERSE**.

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> *(a)* A steam heater is operated with bypass control. The process fluid has $C_p = 2.0$ kJ/(kg·°C), $F = 20$ kg/s, $T_i = 50$ °C. Steam is supplied at 8 bar gauge ($T_s \approx 170$ °C). The desired outlet temperature is 110 °C. If the heater outlet temperature is $T_h = 160$ °C, determine (i) the bypass fraction, (ii) the heat transferred per second, and (iii) the steam flow rate required in kg/hr. Use $\lambda_s = 2050$ kJ/kg. **(7 marks)**
> *(b)* Compare bypass control with direct steam-throttling control in steam heaters. List four advantages of bypass control. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Step 1 — Bypass fraction** (3 marks)

$$
\dfrac{f}{F} = \dfrac{T_{sp} - T_i}{T_h - T_i} = \dfrac{110 - 50}{160 - 50} = \dfrac{60}{110} = 0.5455
$$

So $f = 20 \times 0.5455 = 10.91$ kg/s and the bypass flow is $20 - 10.91 = 9.09$ kg/s.
The **bypass fraction** $= (F - f)/F = 0.4545$ or **45.45%**.

**Step 2 — Heat transferred per second** (2 marks)

$$
Q = f \cdot C_p \cdot (T_h - T_i) = 10.91 \times 2.0 \times 110 = 2400.2 \text{ kW} \approx 2.4 \text{ MW}
$$

**Step 3 — Steam flow rate** (2 marks)

$$
\dot{m}_s = \dfrac{Q}{\lambda_s} = \dfrac{2400.2}{2050} = 1.171 \text{ kg/s} = 1.171 \times 3600 = 4215 \text{ kg/hr}
$$

---

**Solution to Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Comparison table-style answer (4 marks):**

| Aspect | Steam-Throttling | Bypass Control |
|---|---|---|
| Manipulated variable | Steam flow $W$ | Process flow $f$ |
| Valve type and size | Large, expensive, two-phase risk | Small, single-phase liquid |
| Speed of response | Slow (thermal lag) | Fast (flow control) |
| Nonlinearity | Significant | Nearly linear in $f$ |

**Four advantages of bypass control (3 marks — ¾ mark each, with brief justification):**

1. **Smaller control valve required** — the bypass valve modulates only a fraction of the total process flow, so its $\mathrm{C_v}$ rating can be much smaller.
2. **Faster dynamic response** — the valve controls fluid flow directly, avoiding the slow thermal time constants associated with modulating condensing steam.
3. **Linear process gain** — at steady state, $T_o$ varies linearly with $f$, simplifying controller tuning.
4. **No steam-hammer or two-phase issues** — the valve handles single-phase liquid process fluid, eliminating erosion and water-hammer problems.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in Bypass-Control Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting the mixing-point energy balance:** Many students write the heat balance across the heater and then jump directly to $T_o = T_h$. The mixing step is **mandatory** — examiners specifically look for the $T_o = T_i + (f/F)(T_h - T_i)$ derivation. (Lose 2–3 marks if missing.)
> 2. **Confusing reverse vs. direct controller action:** A wrong sign here costs full marks on the "controller action" sub-question. Always justify: *closing the air-to-open bypass valve* means *more heating* and *higher $T_o$*; with $e = T_{sp} - T_o$, positive $e$ requires *more heating*, so output must *decrease* → **reverse action**.
> 3. **Using the wrong reference for $T_h$:** Students often use $T_s$ (saturation temperature) instead of $T_h$ (the actual heater-outlet temperature). Always use the **heater-outlet** temperature in the bypass equation; the steam saturation temperature is an upper bound.
> 4. **Wrong unit for $\lambda_s$:** Ensure $\lambda_s$ and $C_p$ are in **consistent units** (e.g., both kJ-based or both J-based) before computing $Q$ and $\dot{m}_s$.
> 5. **Mixing up the direction of the bypass fraction:** $f/F$ is the *heated* fraction, while $(F-f)/F$ is the *bypass* fraction. Confusing these gives a numerically wrong answer even if the equation is correctly written.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Core Idea:** Bypass control = *split the process stream, then mix* to control outlet temperature. The valve lives on the **process side**, not the steam side.
- **Master Equation:** $\;T_o = T_i + \dfrac{f}{F}\,(T_h - T_i)\;$ — memorize and practice its derivation in both directions.
- **Limiting Cases:**
  - $f = 0$ → full bypass → $T_o = T_i$
  - $f = F$ → no bypass → $T_o = T_h$
- **Process Gain:** $K_p = (T_h - T_i)/F$ — a constant under the standard assumptions, meaning the steady-state behavior is **linear**.
- **Process Model:** FOPDT, $G_p(s) = K_p e^{-Ls} / (\tau s + 1)$, where $\tau = V_{heater} \cdot \rho / f$ is the residence time.
- **Controller Action (Air-to-Open valve):** **Reverse-Acting** — required to maintain negative feedback.
- **Mixing-Point Energy Balance:** is the heart of the analysis; never skip it.
- **When to Choose Bypass:** (i) Heater is oversized; (ii) Large process flow rate; (iii) Small temperature trim required; (iv) Steam valves cause maintenance issues.
- **Comparison Triangle:** Steam-throttling vs. Condensate-throttling vs. Bypass — know at least two advantages of bypass for each of the other two schemes.
- **Typical KTU Problem Data Pattern:** $F$, $T_i$, $T_h$, $T_{sp}$, $C_p$, $\lambda_s$ → compute $f$, bypass flow, $Q$, $\dot{m}_s$.
- **Steam Mass-Flow Conversion:** $\dot{m}_s$ in kg/s × 3600 = kg/hr; divide by 1000 to get **tons/hr** (industry-standard unit).
- **Units Consistency Check:** $C_p$ [kJ/kg·°C] × $\Delta T$ [°C] × $f$ [kg/s] = $Q$ [kW]; $\lambda_s$ [kJ/kg] × $\dot{m}_s$ [kg/s] = $Q$ [kW]. Both sides in kW.
- **Block-Diagram Components:** Setpoint → Comparator → Controller → Valve → Process (heater + mixer) → Sensor → back to comparator; disturbance enters at process.
- **P&ID Symbols to Recognize:** TT (temperature transmitter), TC (temperature controller), TV (temperature/control valve); the valve is on the **bypass line**, not on the steam line.
- **Practical Engineering Caveat:** Bypass control is **less energy-efficient** than direct steam-throttling because the heater always operates at full heat duty (full $T_h$), even when only partial heating is required. The excess heat is "wasted" by mixing with cold bypass stream. Use it when **control quality** matters more than **fuel economy**.
- **KTU Viva Quickfire Question:** *"Why is the bypass valve often smaller than the steam valve it replaces?"* — Because it handles only a *portion* of the total process flow, not the full latent-heat duty of the steam.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cascade Loop on Steam Heater
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT743 - INDUSTRIAL PROCESS CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cascade Loop on Steam Heater

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Cascade Control** is a multi-loop advanced control strategy in which the output of **one controller (the primary/master controller)** is used to dynamically adjust the **setpoint of another controller (the secondary/slave controller)**. The two controllers are nested, forming a hierarchical control structure with a *slower outer loop* regulating the primary process variable and a *faster inner loop* regulating a secondary (intermediate) variable.

In the specific context of a **Steam Heater**, cascade control is applied where:
- The **Primary Variable (PV₁)** = Outlet temperature of the process fluid
- The **Secondary Variable (PV₂)** = Steam flow rate (or steam pressure)
- The manipulated variable for the inner loop is the **steam control valve position**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Cruise Control with Fuel Flow"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine you are driving a car and want to maintain a constant *speed* (the primary variable). A single-loop controller would be like a driver who only watches the speedometer and presses/releases the accelerator. However, the car is going uphill — the engine struggles, fuel flow drops, and by the time the speed drops and the driver reacts, the car has already slowed significantly.

> Now imagine a **second driver** in the back seat who is *only* watching the **fuel flow meter** and constantly adjusting the accelerator to maintain a *target* fuel flow. The first driver (primary) now sets the *target fuel flow* dynamically — if speed drops, he commands *more* fuel flow; if speed overshoots, he commands *less* fuel flow. The second driver (secondary) reacts **instantly** to engine/road disturbances before the car's speed is even affected.

> This is **exactly** how cascade control works on a steam heater — the *temperature controller* is the primary driver setting a *steam flow* target, and the *flow controller* is the secondary driver ensuring that steam flow is precisely maintained despite steam pressure disturbances.

### 1.3 Physical Process Overview

A **Steam Heater** (commonly a shell-and-tube heat exchanger) uses saturated steam to heat a process fluid. The fundamental energy balance is governed by:

$$Q = \dot{m}_s \cdot \lambda_s = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LM}$$

where the symbols have their standard SI units (**W**, **kg/s**, **J/kg**, **W/m²·K**, **m²**, **K**).

**Disturbances** affecting outlet temperature:
1. **Inlet process fluid temperature** (slow, affects primary loop)
2. **Steam supply pressure** (fast, transient) — *handled by the secondary loop*
3. **Process fluid flow rate** variations
4. **Fouling** of heat transfer surfaces

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The defining hallmark of a cascade scheme is that it has *one manipulated variable* (steam valve) but *two measured variables* and *two controllers*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Cascade Control? — The Underlying Philosophy

A conventional single-loop feedback controller on a steam heater suffers from a critical limitation: disturbances in the **steam supply pressure** propagate through the steam flow, then through the heat exchanger dynamics, and only then affect the outlet temperature. By the time the temperature controller reacts, the disturbance has already caused a long transient deviation.

**Cascade control introduces an inner loop that "wraps" the fastest disturbance source**, effectively reducing the order and time constant of the inner process seen by the outer controller.

### 2.2 Operational Logic — Step by Step

1. **The primary transmitter (H₁)** continuously senses the **outlet temperature** *T* of the process fluid.
2. **The primary controller (T₁, "Temperature Controller")** compares *T* to the desired setpoint *T_sp* and produces a control output *u₁(t)*.
3. **This output *u₁(t) is NOT sent directly to the valve.** Instead, it dynamically becomes the **setpoint** for the secondary controller (i.e., the desired steam flow rate *F_sp*).
4. **The secondary transmitter (H₂)** continuously senses the **steam flow rate** *F* via an orifice plate / flow meter.
5. **The secondary controller (T₂, "Flow Controller")** compares *F* to *F_sp* and outputs a signal *u₂(t)* that drives the **steam control valve (Gv)**.
6. The valve adjusts steam flow, completing the inner (fast) loop in seconds, while the outer (slower) temperature loop completes in minutes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Thumb:** The secondary loop must be at least **3 to 5 times faster** than the primary loop, otherwise the cascade becomes unstable or ineffective.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity / Transfer Function | Mathematical Form | Units / Notes |
|---|------------------------------|-------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Steam-side heat duty | $Q = \dot{m}_s \cdot \lambda_s$ | **W** (J/s) |
| 2 | LMTD (Counter-flow) | $\Delta T_{LM} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2)}$ | **K** |
| 3 | Overall heat transfer | $Q = U A \Delta T_{LM}$ | **W** |
| 4 | First-order lag (steam flow to valve) | $G_{p2}(s) = \dfrac{K_{p2}}{\tau_2 s + 1}$ | $\tau_2 \approx 1\text{–}5$ **s** |
| 5 | First-order lag (steam flow to outlet T) | $G_{p1}(s) = \dfrac{K_{p1}}{\tau_1 s + 1}$ | $\tau_1 \approx 30\text{–}300$ **s** |
| 6 | Valve transfer function | $G_v(s) = K_v$ (or first-order) | dimensionless gain |
| 7 | Transmitter | $H_1(s), H_2(s) = K_{H1}, K_{H2}$ | **scaled** to controller range |
| 8 | Inner closed-loop TF | $G_{inner}(s) = \dfrac{T_2 G_v G_{p2}}{1 + T_2 G_v G_{p2} H_2}$ | Effective plant seen by T₁ |
| 9 | Inner-loop characteristic time | $\tau_{inner} \ll \tau_1$ | "Speed-up" effect |
| 10 | Master controller (PI typical) | $T_1(s) = K_{c1}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{\tau_{I1} s}\right)$ | Most common choice |
| 11 | Slave controller (PI / P-only) | $T_2(s) = K_{c2}\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{\tau_{I2} s}\right)$ | Often **P-only** is sufficient |
| 12 | Disturbance rejection ratio (qualitative) | $\dfrac{Y(s)}{D_2(s)}\bigg\vert_{cascade} \ll \dfrac{Y(s)}{D_2(s)}\bigg\vert_{single}$ | Cascade suppresses secondary D |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** In all rendered math above, any symbol resembling "absolute value" is expressed via the LaTeX command `\vert` or `\mid` to maintain the integrity of the markdown table structure. **Do not use the vertical pipe `\|` character inside table cells.**

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Cascade control on steam heaters is ubiquitous in:
- **Refineries** — preheating crude oil before distillation columns.
- **Power plants** — feedwater heating in regenerative cycles.
- **Chemical plants** — reactor feed preheating, jacketed reactor steam coils.
- **Food and pharma industries** — precise temperature control in batch sterilizers and pasteurizers.

The economic value lies in **reducing off-spec product, energy waste, and thermal stress** on process equipment.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Full Block-Diagram Transfer Function Derivation

We define the four-corner process model with all standard KTU conventions:

- $G_v(s)$ — Pneumatic / electric control valve
- $G_{p2}(s)$ — Steam flow response to valve position (fast inner process)
- $G_{p1}(s)$ — Outlet temperature response to steam flow (slow outer process)
- $H_2(s)$, $H_1(s)$ — Flow and temperature transmitters
- $T_1(s)$, $T_2(s)$ — Primary and secondary controllers
- $D_2(s)$ — Steam pressure disturbance (enters the inner process)
- $D_1(s)$ — Process fluid inlet temperature disturbance (enters the outer process)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exhaustive Derivation Policy:** Every algebraic transition is written out explicitly. **No steps are skipped.**

#### Step 1 — Inner Loop (Slave / Secondary) Closed-Loop Transfer Function

The error driving controller $T_2$ is:

$$
E_2(s) = F_{sp}(s) - H_2(s)\cdot W_s(s)
$$

The valve position, steam flow, and controller output are related by:

$$
W_s(s) = G_v(s)\,G_{p2}(s)\,u_2(s)
$$

$$
u_2(s) = T_2(s)\,E_2(s) = T_2(s)\left[F_{sp}(s) - H_2(s)\,W_s(s)\right]
$$

Substituting the controller output into the process equation:

$$
W_s(s) = G_v(s)\,G_{p2}(s)\,T_2(s)\left[F_{sp}(s) - H_2(s)\,W_s(s)\right]
$$

Now expand the right-hand side:

$$
W_s(s) = G_v G_{p2} T_2 \, F_{sp}(s) - G_v G_{p2} T_2 H_2 \, W_s(s)
$$

Collect the $W_s(s)$ terms on the left-hand side:

$$
W_s(s) + G_v G_{p2} T_2 H_2 \, W_s(s) = G_v G_{p2} T_2 \, F_{sp}(s)
$$

Factor $W_s(s)$ from the left side:

$$
W_s(s)\left[1 + T_2 G_v G_{p2} H_2\right] = G_v G_{p2} T_2 \, F_{sp}(s)
$$

Solve explicitly for the closed-loop inner transfer function:

$$
\boxed{\;\dfrac{W_s(s)}{F_{sp}(s)} \;=\; \dfrac{T_2(s)\,G_v(s)\,G_{p2}(s)}{1 + T_2(s)\,G_v(s)\,G_{p2}(s)\,H_2(s)}\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This derived block, denoted $G_{inner}(s)$, replaces the "valve + inner process" combination as seen by the primary controller. **This is the key simplification that cascade control provides.**

---

#### Step 2 — Outer Loop (Master / Primary) Closed-Loop Transfer Function

The process fluid outlet temperature $T(s)$ has two contributions — one from the manipulated steam flow and one from the inlet temperature disturbance $D_1(s)$:

$$
T(s) = G_{p1}(s)\,W_s(s) + G_{d1}(s)\,D_1(s)
$$

Substitute the inner closed-loop relation with $F_{sp}(s) = u_1(s)$ (the primary controller output acts as the secondary setpoint):

$$
T(s) = G_{p1}(s)\,G_{inner}(s)\,u_1(s) + G_{d1}(s)\,D_1(s)
$$

The primary controller computes its output as:

$$
u_1(s) = T_1(s)\left[T_{sp}(s) - H_1(s)\,T(s)\right]
$$

Substitute $u_1$ into the temperature expression:

$$
T(s) = G_{p1} G_{inner}\,T_1\left[T_{sp} - H_1 T\right] + G_{d1} D_1
$$

Expand the right-hand side:

$$
T(s) = G_{p1} G_{inner} T_1 T_{sp} - G_{p1} G_{inner} T_1 H_1 T + G_{d1} D_1
$$

Collect the $T(s)$ terms on the left:

$$
T(s) + G_{p1} G_{inner} T_1 H_1 T = G_{p1} G_{inner} T_1 T_{sp} + G_{d1} D_1
$$

Factor $T(s)$:

$$
T(s)\left[1 + T_1 G_{p1} G_{inner} H_1\right] = G_{p1} G_{inner} T_1 T_{sp} + G_{d1} D_1
$$

Divide through to obtain the final closed-loop transfer function:

$$
\boxed{\;\dfrac{T(s)}{T_{sp}(s)}\bigg\vert_{D_1=0} \;=\; \dfrac{T_1 G_{p1} G_{inner}}{1 + T_1 G_{p1} G_{inner} H_1}\;}
$$

And the disturbance transfer function:

$$
\boxed{\;\dfrac{T(s)}{D_1(s)}\bigg\vert_{T_{sp}=0} \;=\; \dfrac{G_{d1}}{1 + T_1 G_{p1} G_{inner} H_1}\;}
$$

---

#### Step 3 — Numerical Substitution (KTU Typical Values)

Take representative first-order parameters commonly assumed in KTU textbook problems:

$$
G_{p1}(s) = \frac{2}{60s+1}, \qquad G_{p2}(s) = \frac{1}{3s+1}
$$

$$
G_v(s) = 1, \qquad H_1 = H_2 = 1
$$

$$
T_1(s) = 1.5\!\left(1+\dfrac{1}{30s}\right), \qquad T_2(s) = 0.8\!\left(1+\dfrac{1}{5s}\right)
$$

**Step 3a — Compute the inner closed loop:**

$$
G_{inner}(s) = \frac{0.8\!\left(1+\tfrac{1}{5s}\right)\cdot 1 \cdot \tfrac{1}{3s+1}}{1 + 0.8\!\left(1+\tfrac{1}{5s}\right)\cdot 1 \cdot \tfrac{1}{3s+1}\cdot 1}
$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $5s(3s+1)$:

$$
G_{inner}(s) = \frac{0.8(5s+1)}{5s(3s+1) + 0.8(5s+1)}
$$

Expand denominator:

$$
5s(3s+1) + 0.8(5s+1) = 15s^2 + 5s + 4s + 0.8 = 15s^2 + 9s + 0.8
$$

So:

$$
G_{inner}(s) = \frac{0.8(5s+1)}{15s^2 + 9s + 0.8} = \frac{4s + 0.8}{15s^2 + 9s + 0.8}
$$

**Step 3b — The new effective plant** seen by the primary controller is:

$$
G_{p,\text{eff}}(s) = G_{p1}(s) \cdot G_{inner}(s) = \frac{2}{60s+1} \cdot \frac{4s+0.8}{15s^2 + 9s + 0.8}
$$

$$
G_{p,\text{eff}}(s) = \frac{2(4s+0.8)}{(60s+1)(15s^2+9s+0.8)} = \frac{8s+1.6}{900s^3 + 549s^2 + 69.8s + 0.8}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** The effective plant is now a *third-order* system, but because the inner loop is fast, the dominant slow pole is still close to $s = -1/60$, preserving stable behavior while *adding speed of response and disturbance rejection in the steam supply.*

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Cascade vs. Single-Loop Comparison

| Property | Single-Loop Feedback | Cascade Control |
|----------|----------------------|-----------------|
| Number of controllers | 1 | 2 |
| Number of measurements | 1 (outlet T) | 2 (outlet T + steam flow) |
| Response to steam pressure disturbance | Slow (entire $\tau_1$) | Fast (only $\tau_2$) |
| Effective loop time constant | $\tau_1$ | $\tau_{inner} \ll \tau_1$ |
| Tuning aggressiveness of master | Limited | Can be much more aggressive |
| Implementation complexity | Low | Medium |
| Hardware cost | Lower | Higher (extra transmitter + controller) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Cascade Steam Heater Control Loop

```mermaid
graph TD
    TSP[T_sp — Temperature Setpoint] --> SUM1[+]
    SUM1 --> ERR1[Error e1 = T_sp - H1·T]
    ERR1 --> T1C[Primary Controller T1 s — Temperature PI]
    T1C --> FSP[F_sp — Steam Flow Setpoint]
    FSP --> SUM2[+]
    SUM2 --> ERR2[Error e2 = F_sp - H2·Ws]
    ERR2 --> T2C[Secondary Controller T2 s — Flow PI]
    T2C --> GVV[Control Valve Gv s]
    GVV --> GPV[Inner Process Gp2 s — Steam Flow Dynamics]
    GPV --> WSF[Steam Flow Ws — Measured Variable 2]
    WSF --> SHELL[Steam Heater — Heat Exchanger Gp1 s]
    SHELL --> TPV[Outlet Temperature T — Measured Variable 1]
    D1PR[D1 — Inlet Process Fluid Temp Disturbance] --> SHELL
    D2PR[D2 — Steam Pressure Disturbance] --> GPV
    TPV --> HX1[H1 — Temperature Transmitter]
    HX1 --> SUM1
    WSF --> HX2[H2 — Flow Transmitter]
    HX2 --> SUM2
```

### 4.2 Mermaid — Functional Architecture Flow (Control Hierarchy View)

```mermaid
graph LR
    L1[LEVEL 1 — Plant / Process]:::plant
    L2[LEVEL 2 — Field Instrumentation]:::field
    L3[LEVEL 3 — Control Hardware]:::ctrl
    L4[LEVEL 4 — Operator / SCADA]:::op

    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4
    L4 --> L3
    L3 --> L2
    L2 --> L1

    subgraph PROC [STEAM HEATER PROCESS]
      P1[Shell Side — Saturated Steam]:::plant
      P2[Tube Side — Process Fluid]:::plant
      P3[Outlet Temperature T]:::plant
    end

    subgraph INST [FIELD DEVICES]
      F1[TT — Temperature Transmitter H1]:::field
      F2[FT — Orifice Flow Transmitter H2]:::field
      F3[PCV — Pneumatic Control Valve Gv]:::field
    end

    subgraph CTRL [CONTROL LAYER]
      C1[TC — Temperature Master Controller T1]:::ctrl
      C2[FC — Flow Slave Controller T2]:::ctrl
    end

    P3 --> F1 --> C1
    P1 --> F2 --> C2
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> F3 --> P1
    C1 --> OP[Operator HMI — Setpoint Entry]:::op

    classDef plant fill:#E8F4F8,stroke:#1B4F72,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef field fill:#FEF9E7,stroke:#7D6608,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef ctrl fill:#FDEDEC,stroke:#922B21,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef op fill:#E8F8F5,stroke:#117A65,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** Notice the *signal flow direction* — the operator sets *T_sp* on the temperature controller, the temperature controller outputs a *flow setpoint* to the flow controller, the flow controller positions the valve, the valve admits steam, the steam heats the process fluid, and the temperature is fed back to the master. The **two transmitters, two controllers, one valve** signature of cascade is clearly visible.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**State the merits of cascade control with reference to a steam-heated heat exchanger.**

**Model Answer (≈ 3-mark depth):**

Cascade control on a steam heater provides the following key merits:
1. **Improved disturbance rejection:** The inner (flow) loop suppresses steam-pressure and steam-supply disturbances *before* they can propagate to the outlet temperature.
2. **Faster dynamic response:** The dominant process lag seen by the primary controller is reduced, allowing more aggressive tuning of the master temperature controller.
3. **Reduced steady-state error** and tighter temperature regulation in the presence of secondary-loop disturbances.
4. **Linearity improvement:** The flow controller linearizes the valve characteristic, making the overall plant easier to control.

*(1 mark each, total 3)*

---

#### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is the role of the secondary (slave) controller in a cascade loop?**

**Model Answer:**

The secondary (slave) controller:
1. Maintains the **intermediate variable** (steam flow) at the setpoint dynamically commanded by the primary controller.
2. Forms a **fast inner feedback loop** that compensates for disturbances entering the secondary process (e.g., steam pressure fluctuations).
3. Provides a **linearized, well-conditioned** input to the steam control valve, isolating the primary loop from valve nonlinearities.

*(1 mark each, total 3)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse cascade with ratio, override, or feedforward control.** Cascade always has *one valve, two controllers, two measurements, and the output of one is the setpoint of the other*.
> 2. **Sign errors in summing junctions** are the single most common cause of mark loss — examiners deduct up to 2 marks for a wrong sign in $E_1$ or $E_2$.
> 3. **Forgetting the inner-loop denominator** $(1 + T_2 G_v G_{p2} H_2)$ when writing $G_{inner}(s)$ is a frequent mistake. Always state *and* simplify it explicitly.
> 4. **Do not write the secondary controller output as a function of the steam flow only** — the inner loop must be reduced algebraically to its closed-loop form first.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Draw the block diagram of a cascade control system applied to a **steam heater** where the **outlet temperature of the process fluid is the primary variable** and **steam flow rate is the secondary variable**. Clearly mark all transfer functions, summing junctions, disturbances, and signal directions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **closed-loop transfer function** of the secondary (inner) loop. Show that the inner loop simplifies the plant seen by the primary controller. Using representative first-order process models with $\tau_1 = 60$ s and $\tau_2 = 3$ s, show numerically that the inner loop is much faster than the outer loop. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block Diagram (7 marks):**
The student should produce a diagram equivalent to the **Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1** of these notes, with the following mandatory labelled blocks (1 mark for each correctly placed and labelled element, plus 2 marks for arrows and signal flow clarity):

1. **Temperature setpoint** $T_{sp}$
2. **Summing junction 1** computing $E_1 = T_{sp} - H_1 T$
3. **Primary controller** $T_1(s)$
4. **Output of $T_1$ as the setpoint** $F_{sp}$ of secondary loop — *this is the defining signature of cascade*
5. **Summing junction 2** computing $E_2 = F_{sp} - H_2 W_s$
6. **Secondary controller** $T_2(s)$
7. **Control valve** $G_v(s)$
8. **Inner process** $G_{p2}(s)$
9. **Outer process (heat exchanger)** $G_{p1}(s)$
10. **Disturbance 1** $D_1$ (inlet temperature of process fluid) entering the outer process
11. **Disturbance 2** $D_2$ (steam pressure) entering the inner process
12. **Transmitter feedback paths** $H_1$ and $H_2$ returning to the summing junctions

*(Up to 7 marks based on completeness, correctness, and clarity of the diagram.)*

**(b) Derivation (7 marks):**

Following the **exhaustive derivation in Section 3.1, Steps 1 and 3** of these notes:

1. Write the inner-loop equations explicitly: $E_2 = F_{sp} - H_2 W_s$, $u_2 = T_2 E_2$, $W_s = G_v G_{p2} u_2$. **(1 mark)**
2. Eliminate $u_2$ and $W_s$ to obtain:

$$
\frac{W_s(s)}{F_{sp}(s)} = \frac{T_2 G_v G_{p2}}{1 + T_2 G_v G_{p2} H_2}
$$

**(2 marks)**

3. Substitute numerical values $G_{p2} = 1/(3s+1)$, $G_v = 1$, $H_2 = 1$, $T_2 = 0.8(1 + 1/5s)$. Show algebraic simplification (multiplying top and bottom by $5s(3s+1)$) to get:

$$
G_{inner}(s) = \frac{4s + 0.8}{15s^2 + 9s + 0.8}
$$

**(2 marks)**

4. Identify the effective time constant from the denominator: the dominant pole is the smaller root of $15s^2 + 9s + 0.8 = 0$, which is approximately $s \approx -0.097$, i.e., $\tau_{inner} \approx 10$ s. Compare with $\tau_1 = 60$ s and conclude $\tau_{inner} \ll \tau_1$. **(2 marks)**

**Conclusion:** The inner loop executes roughly **6 times faster** than the outer loop, satisfying the cascade rule of thumb and confirming that the primary controller "sees" a faster, simplified plant.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With reference to a steam heater, explain the concept of **cascade control**. Identify clearly the **primary variable, secondary variable, primary controller, secondary controller, and the manipulated variable**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A steam heater has the following transfer functions (deviation variables):

$$
G_v(s) = 1, \quad G_{p2}(s) = \frac{0.8}{2s+1}, \quad G_{p1}(s) = \frac{1.5}{50s+1}, \quad H_1 = H_2 = 1
$$

The controllers are both proportional: $T_1 = 4$ and $T_2 = 2$. Calculate the **steady-state error** of the outer loop for a unit step change in the setpoint $T_{sp}$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Conceptual Explanation (7 marks):**

1. **Definition of cascade control** in the context of a steam heater — multi-loop strategy with nested controllers. **(1 mark)**
2. **Primary variable** = outlet process fluid temperature. **(1 mark)**
3. **Secondary variable** = steam flow rate (intermediate variable). **(1 mark)**
4. **Primary controller** = temperature controller (slower, PI typical). **(1 mark)**
5. **Secondary controller** = flow controller (faster, P or PI). **(1 mark)**
6. **Manipulated variable** = steam control valve stem position (single manipulated variable for both loops). **(1 mark)**
7. **Explanation** of how the secondary loop "wraps" the steam pressure disturbance and the resulting benefit. **(1 mark)**

**(b) Steady-State Error Calculation (7 marks):**

Step 1 — Compute the inner closed-loop TF (as derived in Section 3.1):

$$
G_{inner}(s) = \frac{T_2 G_v G_{p2}}{1 + T_2 G_v G_{p2} H_2} = \frac{2 \cdot 1 \cdot \dfrac{0.8}{2s+1}}{1 + 2 \cdot 1 \cdot \dfrac{0.8}{2s+1} \cdot 1}
$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by $(2s+1)$:

$$
G_{inner}(s) = \frac{1.6}{2s+1 + 1.6} = \frac{1.6}{2s + 2.6} = \frac{1.6}{2(s+1.3)} = \frac{0.8}{s+1.3}
$$

**(2 marks)**

Step 2 — The effective plant seen by $T_1$ is:

$$
G_{p,\text{eff}}(s) = G_{p1}(s) \cdot G_{inner}(s) = \frac{1.5}{50s+1} \cdot \frac{0.8}{s+1.3} = \frac{1.2}{(50s+1)(s+1.3)}
$$

**(1 mark)**

Step 3 — Forward-path TF of the outer loop:

$$
G_{fwd}(s) = T_1 \cdot G_{p,\text{eff}} = \frac{4 \cdot 1.2}{(50s+1)(s+1.3)} = \frac{4.8}{(50s+1)(s+1.3)}
$$

**(1 mark)**

Step 4 — Closed-loop TF for the outer loop:

$$
\frac{T(s)}{T_{sp}(s)} = \frac{G_{fwd}}{1 + G_{fwd}} = \frac{4.8}{(50s+1)(s+1.3) + 4.8}
$$

**(1 mark)**

Step 5 — Apply the **Final Value Theorem** to find the steady-state response to a unit step $T_{sp}(s) = 1/s$:

$$
T(\infty) = \lim_{s \to 0}\, s \cdot \frac{1}{s} \cdot \frac{4.8}{(50s+1)(s+1.3) + 4.8}
$$

Evaluate at $s = 0$:

$$
T(\infty) = \frac{4.8}{(1)(1.3) + 4.8} = \frac{4.8}{1.3 + 4.8} = \frac{4.8}{6.1} \approx 0.7869
$$

**(1 mark)**

Step 6 — Steady-state error:

$$
e_{ss} = T_{sp}(\infty) - T(\infty) = 1 - 0.7869 = \dfrac{1.3}{6.1} \approx 0.213
$$

**(1 mark)**

> **Final Answer:** $e_{ss} \approx 0.213$ (≈ 21.3 % of the setpoint change). The presence of steady-state error confirms the requirement of adding **integral action** in the primary controller (PI instead of P-only) for zero steady-state error in cascade temperature control.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Cascade Control on a Steam Heater**

- ✅ **One valve, two controllers, two transmitters** — the unmistakable signature of cascade.
- ✅ **Primary variable:** outlet process fluid temperature. **Secondary variable:** steam flow rate.
- ✅ The output of the **master controller IS the setpoint of the slave controller** — never confuse this with direct valve actuation.
- ✅ The **inner loop must be 3–5× faster** than the outer loop; otherwise, cascade is destabilizing.
- ✅ Derive the **inner closed-loop TF first**: $G_{inner} = T_2 G_v G_{p2} / (1 + T_2 G_v G_{p2} H_2)$ — this becomes the *effective plant* of the outer loop.
- ✅ Cascade **does NOT eliminate outer-loop disturbances** (e.g., inlet process-fluid temperature changes) — for those, **feedforward control** is needed.
- ✅ For **zero steady-state error**, the **master controller must have integral action (PI or PID)**. The slave controller can often be **P-only**.
- ✅ Cascade dramatically improves **steam-pressure disturbance rejection** — its primary real-world motivation.
- ✅ When drawing the block diagram, **label every transfer function, every summing junction sign, and every disturbance entry point** — examiners award full marks only on complete diagrams.
- ✅ When deriving transfer functions, **state the equations, substitute, and simplify** — do not skip algebraic steps; this is where most marks are lost.
- ✅ **Tuning order:** Tune the *inner* loop first with the master in manual, then tune the *outer* loop with both in automatic.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Condenser
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT743 - INDUSTRIAL PROCESS CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Condenser

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Condenser** in industrial process control is a specialized heat exchanger unit operation designed to convert a working fluid from its **vapor (gaseous) phase into its liquid phase** by rejecting latent heat of vaporization to a cooling medium (typically cooling water, air, or another process fluid). In the context of a **distillation column**, the condenser performs the dual role of (i) generating liquid **reflux** returned to the column to drive mass-transfer separation, and (ii) producing liquid **distillate (overhead product)** withdrawn as the top product.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, a condenser is treated as a **process-side controlled element** whose manipulated variables (cooling water flow, coolant inlet temperature) directly influence the column pressure, reflux drum level, and ultimately the overhead composition — making it a critical node in any **multi-loop distillation control strategy**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The condenser is a closed-loop dependent unit — it is **never controlled in isolation**. Its dynamics are tightly coupled to the reflux drum (accumulator), the reflux pump, and the reboiler. KTU board questions almost always test this *interaction* rather than the condenser as a standalone device.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Sweating Cold Drink Bottle"

Take a cold soft-drink bottle on a hot, humid day. The bottle is cold, the air around it is warm and saturated with water vapor. Water vapor from the air touches the cold surface and **loses energy**, transforming from invisible gas back into visible **droplets of liquid water** on the bottle's surface.

That is condensation. Now scale this up:
- The **cold bottle** = the cooling tubes of a shell-and-tube condenser.
- The **warm humid air** = the hot vapor rising from the top of a distillation column.
- The **water droplets** = the liquid reflux + distillate flowing down.
- The **"sweat" (heat) leaving the bottle** = the heat absorbed by the cooling water and carried away.

Just as you can change how fast the bottle "sweats" by adjusting the bottle's temperature, an engineer can change condensation rate by adjusting **cooling water flow rate** or **cooling water inlet temperature** — these are the primary *manipulated variables* in condenser control.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following quantities must be memorized for KTU derivations:

- **Latent heat of vaporization, $h_{fg}$** (J/kg) — energy released per kg of vapor condensed.
- **Overall heat transfer coefficient, $U$** (W/m²·K) — typically **500–1500 W/m²·K** for shell-and-tube condensers with cooling water.
- **LMTD** (°C or K) — logarithmic mean temperature difference driving force.
- **Condensation heat transfer coefficient, $h_c$** (W/m²·K) — for film condensation, usually **5000–15000 W/m²·K**.
- **Subcooling margin** (°C) — degree of liquid cooled below its bubble point at column pressure.
- **Reflux ratio, $R = L/D$** (dimensionless) — moles of liquid returned per mole of distillate withdrawn.

> [!NOTE]
> **Distinction KTU examiners love to test:**
> - **Total Condenser** → all overhead vapor is condensed; vapor product = 0.
> - **Partial Condenser** → only a fraction is condensed; vapor is taken as a *second product* (e.g., in a deethanizer overhead where ethane leaves as vapor and heavier components drop as liquid).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Why and How a Condenser Works

A condenser operates on the **first law of thermodynamics** applied across a control volume:

- **Energy In (hot vapor + any subcooled reflux) = Energy Out (condensed liquid + vapor product + heat rejected to coolant).**
- The driving force is a **temperature gradient** between the condensing vapor (saturated at column top temperature $T_{top}$) and the cooling medium.
- Condensation occurs at **essentially constant temperature** (assuming a pure component or narrow-boiling mixture) — this is an **isothermal** phase change. *This is why the LMTD formula simplifies nicely.*

The mechanical sequence of events is:

1. Superheated vapor enters the condenser shell/tube entry nozzle.
2. Vapor contacts cold tube surfaces → **desuperheating** (sensible cooling to saturation).
3. At saturation temperature, **phase change begins** → release of latent heat $h_{fg}$.
4. If designed for subcooling, condensed liquid is cooled below $T_{sat}$.
5. Liquid exits to the **reflux drum**; cooling water exits warmer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Phase-change vs. sensible heat:** Roughly 80–90% of the total heat duty in a typical condenser is the **latent load**; only 10–20% is desuperheating + subcooling. This is why condenser design is dominated by **two-phase heat transfer correlations** (Nusselt theory), not by single-phase correlations.

## 2.2 Classification of Condensers (KTU high-yield)

| Type | Description | Industrial Use |
|------|-------------|----------------|
| **Total Condenser** | All overhead vapor is condensed to liquid | Most refinery and chemical distillation columns |
| **Partial Condenser** | Only partial condensation; vapor taken as a side product | Cryogenic air separation, deethanizer/Depropanizer overheads |
| **Shell-and-Tube Condenser** | Vapor on shell side, cooling water in tubes | Most common in petrochemical industries |
| **Air-Cooled (Fin-Fan) Condenser** | Ambient air blown over finned tubes by fans | Power plants, arid regions, water-scarce plants |
| **Direct-Contact (Barometric) Condenser** | Vapor mixed directly with cold liquid | High-vacuum distillation, steam ejector systems |
| **Surface Condenser** | Vapor and coolant separated by a wall (most common type) | Turbines, process columns |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $Q = \dot{m}_{v} \cdot h_{fg}$ | Heat duty from mass flow × latent heat | W (J/s) |
| 2 | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{lm}$ | Standard heat exchanger design equation | W |
| 3 | $\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | Log Mean Temperature Difference | K or °C |
| 4 | $\dfrac{1}{U} = \dfrac{1}{h_i} + \dfrac{1}{h_{c}} + \dfrac{x}{k_w} + R_d$ | Overall heat transfer coefficient (resistances in series) | m²·K/W |
| 5 | $h_c = 0.943 \left[ \dfrac{\rho^2 \, g \, k^3 \, h_{fg}}{\mu \, L \, \Delta T} \right]^{1/4}$ | **Nusselt** — film condensation on **vertical** plate/surface | W/m²·K |
| 6 | $h_c = 0.725 \left[ \dfrac{\rho^2 \, g \, k^3 \, h_{fg}}{N \, \mu \, D \, \Delta T} \right]^{1/4}$ | **Nusselt** — film condensation on **N horizontal tubes** (stacked) | W/m²·K |
| 7 | $V = L + D$ | Component material balance (vapor = reflux + distillate) | kmol/h |
| 8 | $R = L/D$ | Reflux ratio (dimensionless) | — |
| 9 | $Q_c = V \cdot \lambda$ | Condenser heat duty (V = vapor rate, $\lambda$ = latent heat) | W |
| 10 | $F_t = \dfrac{\sqrt{\Delta T_1^2 + \Delta T_2^2}}{\Delta T_{lm}}$ | LMTD correction factor for non-ideal configurations | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No vertical pipes used inside table cells** (per the KTU formatting safeguard). All absolute value / magnitude bars use the dedicated macros shown above.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Petroleum Refineries:** Condensers atop crude and vacuum distillation columns — total condensers with reflux ratios typically 1.5–4.0.
- **Cryogenic Air Separation Units (ASUs):** Partial condensers separate N₂ from O₂ at very low temperatures.
- **Power Generation:** Surface condensers condense turbine exhaust steam at ~0.05 bar to maximize cycle efficiency.
- **Refrigeration & HVAC:** Condensers reject heat absorbed in the evaporator to ambient.
- **Pharmaceutical & Fine Chemicals:** Glass-lined or stainless condensers recover solvents (e.g., ethanol, acetone) — energy cost reduction is the primary driver.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD) for a Condenser

**Physical Premise:** In a condenser, the process fluid (vapor) undergoes phase change at essentially **constant temperature** $T_{sat}$. The cooling water enters at $T_{c,in}$ and leaves at $T_{c,out}$. This is a **true counter-flow** arrangement (or we can treat it as such for design purposes).

**Step 1 — Define end-temperature differences.**

For a counter-flow configuration:

$$\Delta T_1 = T_{sat} - T_{c,out}$$

$$\Delta T_2 = T_{sat} - T_{c,in}$$

Note: $\Delta T_1 < \Delta T_2$ (since the cooling water enters colder than it leaves).

**Step 2 — Energy balance on coolant side to find $T_{c,out}$.**

$$Q = \dot{m}_c \, c_{p,c} \, (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})$$

Therefore:

$$T_{c,out} = T_{c,in} + \dfrac{Q}{\dot{m}_c \, c_{p,c}}$$

**Step 3 — Differential heat transfer across element $dA$.**

Across an infinitesimal area $dA$, heat transferred is $dQ = U \cdot (T_{sat} - T_c) \cdot dA$. The coolant temperature changes by:

$$dQ = -\dot{m}_c \, c_{p,c} \, dT_c$$

**Step 4 — Eliminate $dQ$ and form differential equation.**

$$U \cdot (T_{sat} - T_c) \, dA = -\dot{m}_c \, c_{p,c} \, dT_c$$

Rearranging:

$$dA = -\dfrac{\dot{m}_c \, c_{p,c}}{U} \cdot \dfrac{dT_c}{T_{sat} - T_c}$$

**Step 5 — Integrate from inlet ($T_{c,in}$, area 0) to outlet ($T_{c,out}$, area $A$).**

$$\int_{0}^{A} dA = -\dfrac{\dot{m}_c \, c_{p,c}}{U} \int_{T_{c,in}}^{T_{c,out}} \dfrac{dT_c}{T_{sat} - T_c}$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate integral.**

Let $u = T_{sat} - T_c$, then $du = -dT_c$. Limits: $u_{in} = T_{sat} - T_{c,in} = \Delta T_2$; $u_{out} = T_{sat} - T_{c,out} = \Delta T_1$.

$$A = \dfrac{\dot{m}_c \, c_{p,c}}{U} \int_{\Delta T_2}^{\Delta T_1} \dfrac{du}{u} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_c \, c_{p,c}}{U} \ln \left( \dfrac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2} \right)$$

**Step 7 — Solve for $Q$ and equate to UA-LMTD form.**

$$Q = \dot{m}_c \, c_{p,c} \, (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}) = \dot{m}_c \, c_{p,c} \, (\Delta T_2 - \Delta T_1)$$

Substituting back:

$$A = \dfrac{Q}{U} \cdot \dfrac{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}{\Delta T_2 - \Delta T_1} = \dfrac{Q}{U} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\Delta T_{lm}}$$

**Final compact form:**

$$\boxed{\,Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{lm}, \quad \text{where} \quad \Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}\,}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Nusselt Film Condensation on a Vertical Plate

**Premise:** Vapor condenses on a cold vertical plate of height $L$. A thin liquid film of thickness $\delta(x)$ flows downward under gravity. The heat transfer resistance is the film.

**Step 1 — Velocity profile (laminar, gravity-driven, falling film).**

The shear stress balance on a film element gives a parabolic velocity profile:

$$v_x(y) = \dfrac{\rho \, g}{\mu} \left( \delta \, y - \dfrac{y^2}{2} \right)$$

**Step 2 — Mass flow rate per unit width of plate.**

$$\Gamma = \rho \int_{0}^{\delta} v_x \, dy = \dfrac{\rho^2 \, g \, \delta^3}{3 \mu}$$

**Step 3 — Heat transfer across the film by conduction (quasi-steady).**

The heat flux through the film:

$$q = \dfrac{k \, \Delta T}{\delta}$$

Since $\delta$ varies with $x$, we have local flux $q(x) = k \, \Delta T / \delta(x)$.

**Step 4 — Elemental energy balance over $dx$.**

Latent heat released by condensation on area $dx \cdot 1$ must equal conduction through film at that $x$:

$$h_{fg} \, d\Gamma = \dfrac{k \, \Delta T}{\delta(x)} \, dx$$

Substituting $\Gamma = \rho^2 g \delta^3 / (3 \mu)$:

$$h_{fg} \cdot \dfrac{\rho^2 g}{\mu} \delta^2 \, d\delta = k \, \Delta T \, dx$$

**Step 5 — Integrate from $x = 0$ ($\delta = 0$) to $x = L$ ($\delta = \delta_L$).**

$$h_{fg} \cdot \dfrac{\rho^2 g}{\mu} \cdot \dfrac{\delta_L^3}{3} = k \, \Delta T \, L$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $\delta_L$.**

$$\delta_L = \left[ \dfrac{3 \, \mu \, k \, \Delta T \, L}{\rho^2 \, g \, h_{fg}} \right]^{1/3}$$

**Step 7 — Average heat transfer coefficient.**

$$\bar{h}_c = \dfrac{k}{\delta_L} = \left[ \dfrac{\rho^2 \, g \, k^3 \, h_{fg}}{3 \, \mu \, L \, \Delta T} \right]^{1/3}$$

The empirical constant **0.943** (replacing the $3^{-1/3} \approx 0.693$ from ideal laminar analysis) is the **Nusselt correlation constant** that corrects for vapor shear, variable properties, and entrance effects:

$$\boxed{\,\bar{h}_c = 0.943 \left[ \dfrac{\rho^2 \, g \, k^3 \, h_{fg}}{\mu \, L \, \Delta T} \right]^{1/4}\,}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Component Material Balance Around Condenser + Reflux Drum

**Premise:** Steady state, single feed component. Vapor $V$ enters the condenser from the column top, and produces reflux $L$ (returned to column) and distillate $D$ (product).

**Step 1 — Total mass balance.**

$$\boxed{\,V = L + D\,}$$

**Step 2 — Component mass balance (light key).**

$$V \cdot y_D = L \cdot x_D + D \cdot x_D$$

Since the total condenser produces liquid of uniform composition $x_D = y_D$:

$$V \cdot x_D = (L + D) \cdot x_D \quad \text{(trivially satisfied)}$$

**Step 3 — Reflux ratio definition and substitution.**

$$R = \dfrac{L}{D} \quad \Rightarrow \quad L = R \cdot D$$

Substitute into mass balance:

$$V = R \cdot D + D = D(R + 1)$$

$$\boxed{\,D = \dfrac{V}{R + 1}, \qquad L = \dfrac{R \cdot V}{R + 1}\,}$$

This pair of equations is **frequently asked in KTU 2018, 2019, 2022, 2023 papers** — a 7-mark derivation.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — How Condenser Variables Affect Column Operation

| Perturbation | First-Order Effect on Condenser | Knock-on Effect on Column |
|--------------|-------------------------------|---------------------------|
| ↑ Cooling water flow | ↑ $U$, ↑ $Q$ rejected | ↓ Column pressure, ↓ $T_{top}$ |
| ↑ Cooling water inlet $T$ | ↓ $\Delta T_{lm}$ | ↑ Column pressure, ↑ $T_{top}$ |
| ↑ Reflux flow $L$ | ↓ Reflux drum level | ↓ Column top vapor load |
| ↑ Distillate draw $D$ | ↓ Reflux drum level | ↑ Vapor boil-up required |
| ↓ Feed preheat | ↑ Reboiler duty | ↑ Vapor → ↑ condenser load |
| ↓ Reflux ratio $R$ | ↓ Reflux flow | ↓ Separation efficiency, ↑ $D$ purity loss |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Distillation Column with Total Condenser — Process Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Feed F at stage N_F] --> B[Distillation Column]
    B -- "Vapor V rises" --> C[Column Top Tray NT]
    C --> D[Total Condenser]
    D -- "Hot cooling water out" --> E[CW Out to Cooling Tower]
    D -- "Condensed liquid L+D" --> F[Reflux Drum]
    F -- "Distillate D" --> G[Distillate Product Storage]
    F -- "Reflux L" --> H[Reflux Pump]
    H --> I[Reflux Returned to Top Tray]
    B -- "Liquid falls to reboiler" --> J[Reboiler]
    J -- "Steam in" --> K[Steam Supply]
    J -- "Vapor boil-up V rises" --> B
    B --> L[Bottom Product B]

    style A fill:#e0f2fe,stroke:#0369a1
    style B fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#b45309
    style D fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#b91c1c
    style F fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#15803d
    style H fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#4338ca
    style J fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#9d174d
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Condenser Control Loops

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Process[Process Variables]
        P1[Column Top Pressure P_top]
        P2[Reflux Drum Level L_drum]
        P3[Condenser Outlet T_co]
        P4[Distillate Composition x_D]
    end

    subgraph Controllers[Three Independent Controllers]
        C1[Pressure Controller PC]
        C2[Level Controller LC]
        C3[Temperature Controller TC]
    end

    subgraph Actuators[Final Control Elements]
        V1[Cooling Water Control Valve V1]
        V2[Distillate Control Valve V2]
        V3[Reflux Flow Valve V3]
    end

    subgraph CondenserUnit[Condenser and Reflux Drum]
        COND[Shell and Tube Condenser]
        DRUM[Reflux Accumulator]
    end

    P1 --> C1 --> V1 --> COND
    P2 --> C2 --> V2 --> DRUM
    P3 --> C3 --> V3 --> DRUM
    COND --> DRUM

    style Process fill:#fff7ed,stroke:#c2410c
    style Controllers fill:#f0f9ff,stroke:#0c4a6e
    style Actuators fill:#fafafa,stroke:#525252
    style CondenserUnit fill:#f0fdf4,stroke:#166534
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Observation:** The above block diagram captures the **dual composition control** strategy. In the alternative "Ryskamp" or "温差" (differential temperature) control, TC3 is replaced by a **differential temperature controller** acting on two column trays — this is also a popular exam topic.

## 4.3 Heat Transfer Resistance Network (Inside the Condenser)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Vapor Film h_v] --> B[Tube Wall k_w]
    B --> C[Cooling Water Film h_cw]
    A -- "1/h_v" --> B
    B -- "x/k_w" --> C
    C -- "1/h_cw" --> D[Combined U value]
    A -- "R_d (fouling)" --> D

    style A fill:#fecaca,stroke:#991b1b
    style B fill:#fed7aa,stroke:#9a3412
    style C fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#1e40af
    style D fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#166534
```

The total resistance is:

$$\dfrac{1}{U} = \dfrac{1}{h_v} + \dfrac{x}{k_w} + R_d + \dfrac{1}{h_{cw}}$$

For condensation, $h_v$ (vapor-side) is *much smaller* than $h_{cw}$ (cooling water) — typically $h_v \approx 5000\text{–}15000$ W/m²·K while $h_{cw} \approx 3000\text{–}8000$ W/m²·K. The **cooling water film is the controlling resistance** in well-designed condensers.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022] Differentiate between a total condenser and a partial condenser. Give one industrial example of each.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Total Condenser | Partial Condenser |
|---------|----------------|-------------------|
| Condensate fraction | 100% of overhead vapor | Only a portion (vapor product exists) |
| Products | Liquid distillate only | Liquid + vapor (two product streams) |
| Composition control | Reflux composition = distillate composition | Vapor product composition ≠ liquid composition |
| Industrial example | Crude distillation overhead condenser | Cryogenic air separation ASU overhead |
| KTU advantage | Simpler control (single composition loop) | Allows mid-boiling side-draw as vapor |

A *total condenser* is preferred when the overhead product is to be withdrawn as a single liquid stream. A *partial condenser* is chosen when the lightest component (e.g., ethane) must leave as a vapor to avoid re-evaporation losses, or when using the condenser as an additional separation stage. **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2023] Define "Reflux Ratio" and state its effect on condenser heat duty and column separation efficiency.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Reflux ratio $R$ is the ratio of liquid returned to the column ($L$) to the liquid product withdrawn as distillate ($D$):

$$R = \dfrac{L}{D}$$

**Effect on condenser heat duty:**

$$Q_c = V \cdot h_{fg} = D(R + 1) \, h_{fg}$$

As $R$ increases, $V$ (and hence $Q_c$) increases linearly. Higher reflux means more vapor must be boiled up in the reboiler and condensed at the top.

**Effect on separation efficiency:** Higher $R$ approaches **total reflux** (the theoretical limit of infinite separation stages), but the operating cost (steam + cooling water) rises steeply. The **optimum reflux ratio** is typically 1.2–1.5 times the **minimum reflux ratio** $R_m$ determined by the Underwood equation. **(3 marks)**

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### ⭐ QUESTION A — Option I (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q. (a) [7 Marks]** Derive an expression for the **Nusselt average heat transfer coefficient** for film condensation on a vertical surface. List the assumptions clearly.

**Q. (b) [7 Marks]** A refinery distillation column produces **V = 100 kmol/h** of overhead vapor, which is totally condensed. The overhead composition is $y_D = 0.95$ (light key mole fraction). The reflux ratio is maintained at $R = 2.5$. Calculate:
   (i) Distillate flow rate $D$
   (ii) Reflux flow rate $L$
   (iii) Condenser heat duty if the latent heat of vaporization of the mixture is $h_{fg} = 320$ kJ/kg and the average molecular weight in the overhead is **M = 78 kg/kmol**.

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Nusselt Derivation (7 marks)

**Assumptions (2 marks):**
1. Laminar, smooth, wavy-free condensate film.
2. Constant wall temperature $T_w$ and constant vapor temperature $T_{sat}$.
3. Liquid properties ($\rho, \mu, k$) evaluated at film mean temperature.
4. Negligible vapor shear on the liquid surface.
5. Heat transfer across the film is by conduction only.
6. Sensible cooling of the liquid and subcooling are negligible.

**Derivation (5 marks):** See Section 3.2 of these notes. Final result:

$$\bar{h}_c = 0.943 \left[ \dfrac{\rho^2 \, g \, k^3 \, h_{fg}}{\mu \, L \, \Delta T} \right]^{1/4}$$

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Given:** $V = 100$ kmol/h, $R = 2.5$, $h_{fg} = 320$ kJ/kg, $M = 78$ kg/kmol.

**(i) Distillate flow rate $D$ (2 marks):**

$$D = \dfrac{V}{R + 1} = \dfrac{100}{2.5 + 1} = \dfrac{100}{3.5} = 28.57 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**(ii) Reflux flow rate $L$ (2 marks):**

$$L = R \cdot D = 2.5 \times 28.57 = 71.43 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**Check:** $L + D = 71.43 + 28.57 = 100$ kmol/h = $V$ ✓

**(iii) Condenser heat duty $Q_c$ (3 marks):**

Mass flow of vapor:

$$\dot{m}_v = V \cdot M = 100 \times 78 = 7800 \text{ kg/h}$$

Convert to kg/s:

$$\dot{m}_v = \dfrac{7800}{3600} = 2.1667 \text{ kg/s}$$

Condenser heat duty:

$$Q_c = \dot{m}_v \cdot h_{fg} = 2.1667 \times 320{,}000 = 693{,}333 \text{ W} \approx 693.3 \text{ kW}$$

**Final answers:** $D = 28.57$ kmol/h, $L = 71.43$ kmol/h, $Q_c = 693.3$ kW. **(7 marks)**

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B — Option II (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**Q. (a) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, explain the working of a **shell-and-tube total condenser** used in a distillation column. Show the material and energy balance streams clearly.

**Q. (b) [7 Marks]** A condenser is to be designed to condense **2500 kg/h** of saturated hydrocarbon vapor with $h_{fg} = 420$ kJ/kg. Cooling water enters at **30 °C** and leaves at **45 °C**, with $c_{p,cw} = 4.18$ kJ/kg·K. The condensation temperature is **80 °C** and the overall heat transfer coefficient is $U = 800$ W/m²·K. Calculate:
   (i) The cooling water mass flow rate.
   (ii) The log mean temperature difference (LMTD).
   (iii) The required heat transfer area.

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Working of Shell-and-Tube Condenser (7 marks)

**Sketch (3 marks):** Vapor from column top enters the **shell side** at nozzle (1). It contacts the outside of the tubes, which carry cooling water inside. Latent heat is transferred through the tube wall; vapor condenses and drips down to the **shell outlet (2)**, entering the reflux drum. Cooling water enters at tube-side inlet (3), flows through the tubes, absorbs heat, and exits at outlet (4) hotter.

**Material balance (2 marks):** $V_{\text{in}} = L + D$ (all vapor condenses in a total condenser).

**Energy balance (2 marks):**

$$Q = V \cdot h_{fg} = \dot{m}_{cw} \, c_{p,cw} \, (T_{cw,out} - T_{cw,in})$$

**Control strategy:** Cooling water flow manipulates column pressure; distillate draw manipulates reflux drum level; reflux flow manipulates distillate composition.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Given:** $\dot{m}_v = 2500$ kg/h, $h_{fg} = 420$ kJ/kg, $T_{cw,in} = 30$ °C, $T_{cw,out} = 45$ °C, $c_{p,cw} = 4.18$ kJ/kg·K, $T_{sat} = 80$ °C, $U = 800$ W/m²·K.

**(i) Cooling water mass flow rate (2 marks):**

Heat duty:

$$Q = \dot{m}_v \cdot h_{fg} = \dfrac{2500}{3600} \times 420 = 291.67 \text{ kW} = 291{,}667 \text{ W}$$

Cooling water:

$$\dot{m}_{cw} = \dfrac{Q}{c_{p,cw} \cdot \Delta T_{cw}} = \dfrac{291{,}667}{4180 \times 15} = \dfrac{291{,}667}{62{,}700} = 4.652 \text{ kg/s}$$

**≈ 16,750 kg/h**

**(ii) LMTD (2 marks):**

$$\Delta T_1 = T_{sat} - T_{cw,out} = 80 - 45 = 35 \text{ °C}$$

$$\Delta T_2 = T_{sat} - T_{cw,in} = 80 - 30 = 50 \text{ °C}$$

$$\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)} = \dfrac{35 - 50}{\ln(35/50)} = \dfrac{-15}{\ln(0.7)} = \dfrac{-15}{-0.3567} = 42.06 \text{ °C}$$

**(iii) Heat transfer area (3 marks):**

$$A = \dfrac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{lm}} = \dfrac{291{,}667}{800 \times 42.06} = \dfrac{291{,}667}{33{,}648} = 8.67 \text{ m}^2$$

**Final answers:** $\dot{m}_{cw} = 4.65$ kg/s, $\Delta T_{lm} = 42.06$ °C, $A = 8.67$ m². **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversions** between kg/h and kg/s when calculating $Q$ — this alone costs 1–2 marks per KTU paper.
> 2. **Inverting $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$** in the LMTD formula. The larger $\Delta T$ should be $\Delta T_2$ (denominator in log) — otherwise the log becomes negative. KTU examiners award **partial marks** only if the absolute value is correctly taken.
> 3. **Mixing total and partial condenser logic** — a partial condenser has *two* overhead streams and the material balance becomes $V = L + D + V_{product}$.
> 4. **Not writing down Nusselt assumptions** in derivation questions — KTU explicitly awards **2 of 7 marks** for listing assumptions.
> 5. **Sign error in $D = V/(R+1)$** when students confuse "external reflux ratio" $L/D$ with "internal reflux ratio" $L/V$.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **condenser** converts vapor to liquid by removing latent heat $h_{fg}$ to a cooling medium.
- **Total condenser** → all vapor condensed, single liquid product. **Partial condenser** → vapor + liquid products.
- **Reflux ratio** $R = L/D$ is the most influential operating parameter; it appears in nearly every column calculation.
- Material balance: $V = L + D$ and $D = V/(R+1)$, $L = RV/(R+1)$ — must be memorized.
- **Condenser heat duty** scales linearly with $V$ and $h_{fg}$: $Q_c = V \cdot h_{fg}$.
- **LMTD formula** for a condenser (constant process temperature $T_{sat}$): use the two end-differences $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$ as defined; result is always positive.
- **Nusselt correlation** for film condensation (vertical surface): $h_c = 0.943 [\rho^2 g k^3 h_{fg} / (\mu L \Delta T)]^{1/4}$ — note the $1/4$ exponent, not $1/3$.
- For **horizontal tubes (N in vertical stack)**: $h_c = 0.725 [\rho^2 g k^3 h_{fg} / (N \mu D \Delta T)]^{1/4}$.
- The **cooling water film resistance** (tube-side $1/h_{cw}$) typically controls the overall $U$ — not the condensing-side film.
- **Fouling factor** $R_d$ must be added to the resistance network; a clean condenser rarely exists in service.
- **Condenser control is not isolated** — it interacts with the reflux drum, reflux pump, and column pressure. KTU questions on this topic almost always probe the *interaction*.
- **Pressure control strategy** uses condenser cooling water as the manipulated variable in the most common scheme.
- The **liquid distributor** and **reflux pump NPSH** are practical design points beyond the scope of KTU theory — but awareness of them earns full marks in design questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Control of Condenser
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT743 - INDUSTRIAL PROCESS CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Control of Condenser # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition In the context of **Industrial Process Control**, a **condenser** is a specialized shell-and-tube (or air-cooled) heat exchanger whose primary function is to remove latent heat from a process vapor, causing a phase change from the **vapor phase to the liquid phase**. The heat removed is rejected to a utility cooling medium (cooling water, refrigerant, or ambient air). **Control of a condenser** refers to the integrated set of feedback, cascade, feedforward, and split-range control strategies deployed to regulate the **overhead vapor condensation rate, reflux subcooling, condenser pressure, and condensate (reflux) level**, ensuring product purity, energy efficiency, and equipment safety within a distillation column or refluxed reactor system. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** > "Condenser control encompasses the manipulation of coolant flow rate, condensate level, and overhead pressure to maintain a stable heat-removal profile, ensuring the column operates at its designed reflux ratio and at the desired top-product specification." --- ## 1.2 Physical Process Variables in a Condenser System A typical refluxed condenser system has **five primary controlled variables (CVs)** and **four primary manipulated variables (MVs)**: | Controlled Variable (CV) | Engineering Unit | Purpose | |---|---|---| | Condenser Pressure | **kPa (gauge)** | Sets column boiling point | | Condensate (Reflux) Temperature | **°C** | Ensures no vapor flashing | | Reflux Accumulator Level | **% (or m)** | Mass balance stability | | Top-Product Composition | **mol fraction** | Purity specification | | Coolant Outlet Temperature | **°C** | Heat-duty indicator | | Manipulated Variable (MV) | Actuator Type | |---|---| | Coolant Flow Rate | Pneumatic Control Valve (PCV) | | Reflux Flow Rate | Reflux Control Valve | | Vapor By-pass to Vent | Pressure-Relief Valve | | Condensate Product Draw | Level-Control Valve | --- ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Pressure Cooker with a Cold Lid:** > Imagine a pressure cooker on a stove. Steam rises and hits a cold lid placed on top — droplets (condensate) form and drip back into the pot. Now, if the stove becomes too hot, more steam rises, and the lid gets warmer → less steam condenses → pressure inside rises dangerously. > To control this, you can: > - (a) Pour more cold water on the lid (manipulating coolant flow), > - (b) Open a small vent to release excess pressure (manipulating vent flow), > - (c) Drain the condensate faster so it doesn't flood (manipulating reflux level). > > These three knobs correspond exactly to the **temperature, pressure, and level control loops** of an industrial condenser! In an industrial distillation column, the condenser acts as the **"thermal sink"** of the entire separation process. If the condenser cannot remove heat at the same rate the reboiler supplies it, the column's internal inventory of vapor grows, pressure surges, and product purity collapses. Hence, condenser control is not an isolated loop — it is a **network of interacting loops** that, when properly tuned, govern the entire column's dynamic behavior. --- ## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics The following constants are repeatedly used in condenser modeling: - **Latent heat of vaporization of water at 100 °C: λ = 2257 kJ/kg** - **Specific heat of cooling water: Cpw ≈ 4.18 kJ/(kg·K)** - **Overall heat-transfer coefficient (U) for shell-and-tube condensers: 500 – 1500 W/(m²·K)** - **Sub-cooling margin: typically 5 – 10 °C** below saturation temperature to prevent vapor bubbles in reflux > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Highlight:** > The condenser duty equation **Q = m·λ** (latent-heat dominated) is the single most tested formula in this topic.
