Unlike a heater where **Q = m·Cp·ΔT** (sensible heat), the condenser's phase-change nature makes **latent heat the dominant heat-transfer term**. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Fundamental Physics of Condensation When a saturated vapor contacts a surface maintained below its saturation temperature, two phenomena occur simultaneously: 1. **Heat Transfer:** Sensible heat flows from the vapor to the cold surface (Fourier/convective conduction). 2. **Phase Change:** Molecules lose kinetic energy, transition to liquid, and release **latent heat of condensation (λ)**. For a **pure-component condenser**, the heat balance is: $$Q_{condenser} = \dot{m}_v \cdot \lambda$$ For a **mixture (multi-component) condenser**, a temperature glide appears, and the heat balance integrates over the cooling curve: $$Q = \dot{m}_v \left[ \int_{T_{dew}}^{T_{bubble}} C_{p,v} \, dT + \lambda + \int_{T_{sat}}^{T_{subcool}} C_{p,l} \, dT \right]$$ --- ## 2.2 The Log-Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD) The driving force for heat transfer is the **log-mean temperature difference** between the condensing vapor (constant at T_sat) and the warming coolant (rising from T_c,in to T_c,out): $$\Delta T_{LM} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$$ where: - **ΔT₁ = T_sat − T_c,in** (hot-end difference) - **ΔT₂ = T_sat − T_c,out** (cold-end difference) The total heat duty becomes: $$Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LM}$$ This is the **heat-transfer design equation** that links the physical condenser to its control objective: if T_c,in rises (warmer coolant), ΔT_LM drops, Q drops, and the column pressure will rise unless the controller compensates. --- ## 2.3 Dynamic Modes of Condenser Operation | Mode | Description | Typical Application | |---|---|---| | **Total Condenser** | All vapor is condensed; no vapor product drawn from overhead | Most common; reflux drum below condenser | | **Partial Condenser** | Only a portion is condensed; vapor and liquid products withdrawn | Cryogenic & NGL plants | | **Sub-Cooled Condenser** | Liquid is cooled below its bubble point | Pumps downstream — avoids cavitation | | **Vapor-Only By-pass** | Inert gases vented; vapor not condensed | Refluxed absorber with non-condensables | --- ## 2.4 Standard Control Loops in a Condenser System > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 emphasizes four canonical control strategies for condensers. Master these:** ### Strategy 1 — Condenser Pressure Control by Coolant Flow - **CV:** Condenser pressure (P_c) - **MV:** Cooling water flow rate - **Controller:** PI (Proportional + Integral) - **Logic:** ↑ P_c detected → ↑ coolant valve opening → ↑ Q removed → ↓ P_c ### Strategy 2 — Cascade Coolant-Temperature → Coolant-Flow - **Primary (Master):** Condenser pressure → setpoint for coolant outlet T - **Secondary (Slave):** Coolant outlet T → coolant flow valve - **Advantage:** Rejects coolant-temperature disturbances (e.g., summer/winter swings) before they propagate to pressure. ### Strategy 3 — Reflux Accumulator Level Control - **CV:** Reflux drum level (L_d) - **MV:** Reflux flow valve (or product draw valve) - **Why critical:** Level swing = inventory change = pressure disturbance upstream. ### Strategy 4 — Split-Range Control - **Logic:** A single controller output drives *two* valves in opposing directions. - Output 0–50 %: opens the **vapor-vent valve** (releases pressure). - Output 50–100 %: opens the **coolant valve** (increases condensation). - **Use:** Tight pressure control during large load upsets where a single valve cannot cover the entire operating range. --- ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **MASTER THIS TABLE — these equations appear in 70%+ of past condenser-control numerical problems.** | # | Formula | Symbol Key | Engineering Use | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $Q = \dot{m}_v \cdot \lambda$ | Q = heat duty [kW], ṁ_v = vapor mass flow [kg/s], λ = latent heat [kJ/kg] | Total condenser duty | | 2 | $Q = \dot{m}_c \cdot C_{p,c} \cdot (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})$ | ṁ_c = coolant flow, C_p = specific heat | Coolant-side energy balance | | 3 | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LM}$ | U = overall HTC [W/m²K], A = area [m²] | Heat exchanger rating | | 4 | $\Delta T_{LM} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | ΔT₁, ΔT₂ = end-point temperature differences | Driving force calc | | 5 | $R = \dfrac{L}{D}$ | R = reflux ratio, L = reflux flow, D = distillate | Column operating line | | 6 | $P_{vap} = 10^{A - B/(T+C)}$ | Antoine equation for vapor pressure | Relates P and T saturation | | 7 | $\tau_{cond} = \dfrac{M_{vap,holdup}}{Q/\lambda}$ | τ = residence time, M = mass holdup | Dynamic response time | | 8 | $K_p \cdot K_v = \dfrac{\Delta Q / Q}{\Delta P / P}$ | K_p = process gain, K_v = valve gain | Loop gain for tuning | | 9 | $\tau_d = \dfrac{V_{drum}}{q_{L,in}}$ | Drum residence time | Level loop tuning | | 10 | $y_{D,i} = \dfrac{\alpha_i \cdot x_i}{\sum_j \alpha_j \cdot x_j}$ | α = relative volatility | Top-product purity (controls R) | --- ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility Condenser control strategies are deployed universally in: - **Petrochemical Refineries:** Crude distillation, vacuum, and catalytic-cracking columns. - **LNG (Liquefied Natural Gas) Plants:** Mixed-refrigerant condensers with multi-stream exchangers. - **Pharmaceutical Batch Reactors:** Solvent-recovery condensers with reflux-ratio modulation. - **Power Generation:** Surface condensers in steam turbines (vacuum control critical for cycle efficiency). - **Desalination:** Multi-effect distillation (MED) and multi-stage flash (MSF) condensers. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** > A poorly tuned condenser pressure loop causes **column weeping, flooding, and off-spec product** — costing millions annually in a refinery. This is why KTU examiners frequently test cascade and split-range architectures here. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Energy Balance for a Total Condenser We derive the governing steady-state equation for a total condenser receiving saturated vapor from a distillation column. ### Step 1 — Define the System Boundary The control volume is the condenser shell, with: - **Inlet:** Superheated/saturated vapor from column overhead, mass flow $\dot{m}_v$ at $T_{sat}$. - **Outlet:** Sub-cooled liquid (reflux + distillate), mass flow $\dot{m}_L$ at $T_{sub}$. - **Utility Side:** Cooling water enters at $T_{c,in}$, exits at $T_{c,out}$. ### Step 2 — Apply the First Law of Thermodynamics (Steady State) For the process fluid (neglecting kinetic/potential energy changes): $$Q = \dot{m}_v \cdot H_v - \dot{m}_L \cdot H_L$$ Because of mass conservation: $\dot{m}_v = \dot{m}_L \equiv \dot{m}$ (total condensation assumed). $$Q = \dot{m} \left( H_v - H_L \right)$$ ### Step 3 — Decompose the Enthalpy Difference $$H_v - H_L = \underbrace{\int_{T_{sub}}^{T_{sat}} C_{p,l} \, dT}_{\text{(1) sub-cooling sensible}} + \underbrace{\lambda}_{\text{(2) latent}} + \underbrace{\int_{T_{sat}}^{T_{v,in}} C_{p,v} \, dT}_{\text{(3) superheat sensible}}$$ For a **saturated vapor inlet** ($T_{v,in} = T_{sat}$), term (3) vanishes: $$H_v - H_L = \lambda + C_{p,l}\left(T_{sat} - T_{sub}\right)$$ ### Step 4 — Equate to the Coolant-Side Heat Pickup $$\dot{m} \left[ \lambda + C_{p,l}(T_{sat} - T_{sub}) \right] = \dot{m}_c \cdot C_{p,c} \cdot \left( T_{c,out} - T_{c,in} \right)$$ ### Step 5 — Express in Terms of Control Variables If the control objective is to maintain $T_{c,out}$ at a setpoint, the manipulated coolant flow becomes: $$\dot{m}_c = \frac{\dot{m} \left[ \lambda + C_{p,l}(T_{sat} - T_{sub}) \right]}{C_{p,c} \cdot \left( T_{c,out}^{SP} - T_{c,in} \right)}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **This equation is the foundation of the cascade pressure→coolant-T loop.** It explicitly shows that disturbances in $\dot{m}$ (vapor load) and $T_{c,in}$ (cooling tower weather) directly alter the required $\dot{m}_c$. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Split-Range Valve Sequencing A **split-range controller** splits a single 3–15 psi (or 4–20 mA) output signal across two final control elements. For a condenser pressure loop: ### Step 1 — Define Output Bands - **Valve A (Vapor Vent):** Output 0 – 50 % → Valve opening 0 – 100 % - **Valve B (Coolant Flow):** Output 50 – 100 % → Valve opening 0 – 100 % ### Step 2 — Linear Interpolation Equations For Valve A (Vent): $$\text{Opening}_A = \min\left(1,\ \frac{PV - SP}{SP} \cdot K_c \cdot 2\right) \quad \text{(capped at 100\%)}$$ Equivalently, in signal terms: $$I_{A} = 4 + \left(\frac{I_{controller} - 4}{12}\right) \cdot 12 \quad \text{for } 4 \le I_{controller} \le 12$$ For Valve B (Coolant): $$I_{B} = 4 + \left(\frac{I_{controller} - 12}{8}\right) \cdot 12 \quad \text{for } 12 \le I_{controller} \le 20$$ ### Step 3 — Verify Continuity At $I_{controller} = 12$ mA (50 %), both valves read 4 mA (fully closed) → **smooth transition**, no dead band. ✅ At $I_{controller} = 20$ mA (100 %), Valve A = 16 mA (full open, capped), Valve B = 20 mA (full open). ✅ > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters in KTU problems:** Examiners love asking how a **single controller can manage bidirectional disturbance** — split-range is the canonical answer. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Loop Interaction Map This matrix identifies which condenser variables interact (• = strong interaction, ○ = weak): | | Pressure | Reflux Level | Coolant T | Distillate Purity | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Coolant Flow** | ●●● | ○ | ●● | ● | | **Reflux Flow** | ● | ●●● | ○ | ●●● | | **Vent Valve** | ●●● | ○ | ○ | ● | | **Product Draw** | ○ | ●●● | ○ | ●● | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reading the matrix:** A change in coolant flow strongly affects pressure but only mildly affects reflux drum level. This interaction map is the basis for selecting which pairings are non-interacting and which require decoupling (e.g., using a **decoupler block** in the control logic). --- ## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (Model KTU-style) **Problem:** A total condenser condenses **1000 kg/h** of benzene vapor (λ = 394 kJ/kg) at 80 °C using cooling water entering at 25 °C. The cooling water exit temperature is set to 45 °C, with $C_{p,c}$ = 4.18 kJ/(kg·K). Compute the required coolant flow rate. ### Solution: **Step 1 — Compute condenser duty:** $$\begin{aligned} Q &= \dot{m}_v \cdot \lambda \\ &= \frac{1000}{3600} \cdot 394 \\ &= 109.44\ \text{kW} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 — Apply coolant energy balance:** $$\begin{aligned} \dot{m}_c &= \frac{Q}{C_{p,c} \cdot \Delta T_c} \\ &= \frac{109.44}{4.18 \cdot (45 - 25)} \\ &= \frac{109.44}{83.6} \\ &= 1.309\ \text{kg/s} \\ &= 4712.5\ \text{kg/h} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Validate against LMTD (given U = 800 W/m²K, A = 4 m²):** $$\begin{aligned} \Delta T_{LM} &= \frac{(80-25) - (80-45)}{\ln((80-25)/(80-45))} \\ &= \frac{55 - 35}{\ln(55/35)} \\ &= \frac{20}{\ln(1.5714)} \\ &= \frac{20}{0.4519} \\ &= 44.26\ °C \end{aligned}$$ $$\begin{aligned} Q_{rating} &= U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LM} \\ &= 800 \cdot 4 \cdot 44.26 \\ &= 141{,}632\ \text{W} = 141.6\ \text{kW} \end{aligned}$$ Since **Q_rating (141.6 kW) > Q_required (109.44 kW)**, the condenser has ~**22.7 % design margin** — operating safely. ✅ --- ## 3.5 Tuning Formula Derivation for Cascade Loop For a cascade (P-master, T-slave) loop, the **Ziegler-Nichols** open-loop method gives: $$K_c = \frac{0.5 \cdot K_u}{\tau_u / T_u} \quad \text{(PI for temperature slave)}$$ Closed-loop natural frequency of the inner loop (T-loop): $$\omega_{n,inner} = \frac{1}{\tau_{coolant} + \tau_{valve}}$$ For the outer pressure loop to be stable: $$\omega_{n,outer} \le \frac{\omega_{n,inner}}{3 \text{ to } 5}$$ This is why cascade control is **3–5× faster** than single-loop feedback at rejecting coolant-temperature disturbances. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram: Cascade Condenser Control The following Mermaid block illustrates the canonical **two-loop cascade architecture** for condenser pressure control. ```mermaid flowchart LR SP1["Pressure Setpoint"] --> SUM1(("Σ")) PT["Pressure Transmitter
PT-101"] --> SUM1 SUM1 --> PC["Pressure Controller
PIC-101 PI"] PC --> SAT["Slave SP Signal
4 to 20 mA"] SAT --> SUM2(("Σ")) TT["Temperature Transmitter
TT-101"] --> SUM2 SUM2 --> TC["Temperature Controller
TIC-101 PI"] TC --> IV["Coolant Flow Valve
FCV-101"] IV --> COND["Shell and Tube Condenser
Heat Exchanger E-101"] COND --> PT COND --> TT COND --> DRUM["Reflux Accumulator
V-101"] DRUM --> LC["Level Controller
LIC-101 PI"] LC --> RV["Reflux Control Valve
LCV-101"] RV --> COLUMN["Distillation Column
C-101"] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** The pressure transmitter (PT-101) feeds the master PIC, whose output becomes the setpoint of the slave TIC. The TIC then commands the coolant valve. Two feedback paths exist — pressure (slow) and temperature (fast). This is the **KTU gold-standard** cascade architecture. --- ## 4.2 Functional Architecture: Split-Range Condenser Pressure Control ```mermaid flowchart TD PVT["Condenser Pressure
PV Signal 4 to 20 mA"] --> SUM1(("Σ")) SPSET["Pressure SP"] --> SUM1 SUM1 --> PICON["Pressure Controller
PIC-102 PI Output"] PICON --> SR["Split Range Relay
Block"] SR --> VALVE_A["Vapor Vent Valve
0 to 50 percent range"] SR --> VALVE_B["Coolant Inlet Valve
50 to 100 percent range"] VALVE_A --> VESSEL["Condenser Vessel
E-102"] VALVE_B --> VESSEL VESSEL --> PVT ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Note:** Notice how a *single* controller output drives *two* valves. This is the **split-range** scheme — ideal when the pressure can swing both above and below setpoint (e.g., during startup/shutdown transitions). --- ## 4.3 Block-Level Interaction Map (Disturbance Propagation) ```mermaid flowchart LR DIST1["Ambient Cooling Tower
Temperature Upset"] --> COOLIN["Coolant Inlet T Rise"] DIST2["Feed Composition
Change to Column"] --> VAPLOAD["Vapor Load Increase"] DIST3["Cooling Pump Failure"] --> COOLF["Coolant Flow Loss"] COOLIN --> DUTY["Condenser Duty Q Drop"] VAPLOAD --> DUTY COOLF --> DUTY DUTY --> PRES["Condenser Pressure Rise"] PRES --> LEVEL["Reflux Drum Level Fall"] PRES --> PURITY["Top Product Purity Drift"] PRES --> CTRL["Pressure Controller Acts"] CTRL --> VALVE["Opens Coolant Valve"] VALVE --> DUTY ``` --- ## 4.4 Instrumentation & Control Symbol Legend | Symbol | Meaning | |---|---| | **PT** | Pressure Transmitter | | **TT** | Temperature Transmitter | | **LT** | Level Transmitter | | **PIC** | Pressure Indicating Controller | | **TIC** | Temperature Indicating Controller | | **LIC** | Level Indicating Controller | | **FCV** | Flow Control Valve | | **LCV** | Level Control Valve | | **Σ** | Summing junction (error computation) | | **SP** | Setpoint | | **PV** | Process Variable | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Convention:** KTU examinations follow the **ISA-5.1** standard for instrument tagging. Memorize these tag suffixes — they appear in nearly every loop diagram question. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (Part A — Q1) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Explain the function of a total condenser in a distillation column with a neat sketch of its instrumentation loop.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **total condenser** condenses **100 % of the overhead vapor** leaving a distillation column into liquid. The condensed liquid is collected in a **reflux accumulator drum**, from which a portion is returned as reflux (L) and the remainder withdrawn as distillate product (D). **Instrumentation loop:** - **Pressure transmitter (PT)** mounted on the condenser shell senses vapor pressure. - **Pressure controller (PIC)** compares PV with SP and outputs a corrective signal. - **Signal** drives the **cooling water control valve (FCV)**, modulating coolant flow to maintain the desired heat-removal rate. - **Level transmitter (LT)** on the reflux drum + **level controller (LIC)** manipulate the reflux/ distillate valves for inventory balance. The loop maintains the column's top temperature at saturation, ensuring stable internal vapor–liquid traffic and product purity. *(1 mark – definition; 1 mark – loop components; 1 mark – physical sketch description.)* --- ### Question 2 (Part A — Q2) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Differentiate between a total condenser and a partial condenser. Where is each preferred?** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Total Condenser | Partial Condenser | |---|---|---| | Vapor condensed | 100 % | Partial fraction only | | Liquid product | Yes (reflux + distillate) | Yes (reflux) + Vapor product | | Pressure drop | Moderate | Lower | | Cost | Higher (large area) | Lower | | Typical use | Crude, vacuum, and most refinery columns | Cryogenic, NGL, ethylene plants | | Composition control | Easier (single liquid phase) | Harder (vapor–liquid equilibrium) | **Preferred application:** - **Total** when high-purity liquid distillate is the product and energy recovery via reflux is desired. - **Partial** when one component has a much higher volatility and vapor product removal simplifies downstream separation. *(1 mark – difference table; 1 mark – preference for total; 1 mark – preference for partial.)* --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### Question A (Part B — Q1A) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Module 3]` **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **cascade control of a shell-and-tube condenser** for pressure regulation. Discuss the role of each loop and justify why cascade gives better disturbance rejection than single-loop control. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the energy balance equations for the process side and the coolant side of a total condenser. Show how the manipulated coolant flow rate is expressed as a function of the column overhead vapor load and coolant inlet temperature. **(7 marks)** --- ### Model Solution for Question A: #### Part (a) — Cascade Control Block Diagram **Step 1 — Identify the two CVs:** - **Primary CV:** Condenser shell pressure (P_c) - **Secondary CV:** Coolant outlet temperature (T_c,out) **Step 2 — Identify MVs:** - **Primary MV:** Setpoint of the slave T-controller - **Secondary MV:** Coolant valve position **Step 3 — Construct the cascade (already drawn in Section 4.1).** **Step 4 — Justification (Disturbance Rejection):** Single-loop pressure control responds **only after** a coolant disturbance has propagated through the heat exchanger to the vapor side. The thermal lag is 60–180 seconds. In cascade: - The **inner (T) loop** has a time constant of 5–15 seconds (coolant dynamics). - Any disturbance in $T_{c,in}$ (cooling tower weather, pump variation) is **corrected inside the inner loop** before it reaches the pressure PV. - The **outer (P) loop** sees only the residual, well-attenuated disturbance. **Quantitative justification:** $$\frac{\tau_{cascade}}{\tau_{single}} = \frac{\tau_{process} + \tau_{valve}}{\tau_{coolant} + \tau_{valve}} \approx \frac{120}{10} = 12$$ Cascade is **~10× faster** in rejecting inner-loop disturbances. **(7 marks distributed: 2 – diagram, 2 – loop roles, 3 – justification with τ ratio.)** --- #### Part (b) — Energy Balance Derivation **Step 1 — Process-side steady-state balance:** $$Q_{process} = \dot{m}_v \left[ \lambda + C_{p,l}(T_{sat} - T_{sub}) \right]$$ **Step 2 — Coolant-side balance:** $$Q_{coolant} = \dot{m}_c \cdot C_{p,c} \cdot (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})$$ **Step 3 — Equate ($Q_{process} = Q_{coolant}$ by First Law):** $$\dot{m}_v \left[ \lambda + C_{p,l}(T_{sat} - T_{sub}) \right] = \dot{m}_c \cdot C_{p,c} \cdot (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})$$ **Step 4 — Solve for coolant flow rate:** $$\dot{m}_c = \frac{\dot{m}_v \left[ \lambda + C_{p,l}(T_{sat} - T_{sub}) \right]}{C_{p,c} \cdot (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in})}$$ **Step 5 — Interpret:** - ↑ $\dot{m}_v$ (more vapor from column) → ↑ $\dot{m}_c$ required (proportional). - ↑ $T_{c,in}$ (hot summer day) → ↓ ΔT_c → ↑ $\dot{m}_c$ required (inverse). - ↑ $\lambda$ (heavier hydrocarbon) → ↑ $\dot{m}_c$ required. **Verification with the benzene example from Section 3.4:** $\dot{m}_c = 1.309$ kg/s. ✓ **(7 marks distributed: 2 – process balance, 2 – coolant balance, 2 – final equation, 1 – interpretation.)** --- ### Question B (Part B — Q1B) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Module 3]` **(a)** Explain the **split-range control scheme** for condenser pressure regulation. Sketch the valve-position vs. controller-output characteristic and state one industrial scenario where this scheme is essential. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A refinery condenser handles 5000 kg/h of hydrocarbon vapor with latent heat 350 kJ/kg at 110 °C. Cooling water enters at 30 °C and leaves at 50 °C. Compute: **(i)** the condenser heat duty, **(ii)** the required cooling water flow rate (C_p = 4.18 kJ/kg·K), and **(iii)** the log-mean temperature difference (LMTD). **(7 marks)** --- ### Model Solution for Question B: #### Part (a) — Split-Range Control Scheme **Definition:** A split-range controller sends its 4–20 mA output signal to **two final control elements**, each operating over a *different portion* of the output range. In condenser pressure control: - **Valve 1 (Vapor Vent)** — operates over **0 – 50 %** output (4 – 12 mA). - Action: Controller output rises → Vent **opens** to relieve over-pressure. - **Valve 2 (Coolant Inlet)** — operates over **50 – 100 %** output (12 – 20 mA). - Action: Controller output rises further → Coolant valve **opens** to condense more vapor. **Valve-position characteristic table:** | Controller Output (%) | Vent Valve Opening (%) | Coolant Valve Opening (%) | |---|---|---| | 0 | 0 (closed) | 0 (closed) | | 25 | 50 | 0 | | 50 | 100 (fully open) | 0 (closed) | | 75 | 100 (held) | 50 | | 100 | 100 (held) | 100 (fully open) | **Industrial scenario:** During **column startup**, the reboiler is fired before the condenser is fully flooded. Vapor generation can vastly exceed condensation capacity. A single coolant valve cannot release the over-pressure; the vent must be opened simultaneously. Split-range handles this dual-directional disturbance in one controller. **(7 marks: 2 – definition, 2 – characteristic table, 1 – sketch description, 2 – industrial scenario.)** --- #### Part (b) — Numerical Computation **Given:** - $\dot{m}_v = 5000$ kg/h $= 5000/3600 = 1.389$ kg/s - $\lambda = 350$ kJ/kg - $T_{sat} = 110$ °C - $T_{c,in} = 30$ °C, $T_{c,out} = 50$ °C - $C_{p,c} = 4.18$ kJ/(kg·K) **(i) Heat Duty:** $$\begin{aligned} Q &= \dot{m}_v \cdot \lambda \\ &= 1.389 \cdot 350 \\ &= 486.11\ \text{kW} \end{aligned}$$ **(ii) Cooling Water Flow Rate:** $$\begin{aligned} \dot{m}_c &= \frac{Q}{C_{p,c} \cdot \Delta T_c} \\ &= \frac{486.11}{4.18 \cdot (50 - 30)} \\ &= \frac{486.11}{83.6} \\ &= 5.815\ \text{kg/s} \\ &= 20{,}934\ \text{kg/h} \end{aligned}$$ **(iii) Log-Mean Temperature Difference:** $$\begin{aligned} \Delta T_1 &= T_{sat} - T_{c,in} = 110 - 30 = 80\ °C \\ \Delta T_2 &= T_{sat} - T_{c,out} = 110 - 50 = 60\ °C \\ \Delta T_{LM} &= \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)} \\ &= \frac{80 - 60}{\ln(80/60)} \\ &= \frac{20}{\ln(1.333)} \\ &= \frac{20}{0.2877} \\ &= 69.52\ °C \end{aligned}$$ **(7 marks: 2 – duty, 2 – coolant flow, 2 – LMTD, 1 – units / commentary.)** --- ## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks — Avoid These!** > > 1. **Confusing latent vs. sensible heat:** In a *condenser*, the dominant term is $\lambda$. Students who write only $Q = \dot{m} \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$ lose **3 marks instantly**. > 2. **Sign errors in LMTD:** Always ensure $\Delta T_1 > \Delta T_2$ (hot end > cold end). Reversing them yields a **negative logarithm**. > 3. **Forgetting the cascade slave setpoint:** When asked to "draw the cascade loop," students often draw two *independent* loops. The slave SP *is* the master MV — make the connection explicit. > 4. **Mismatching units:** $\dot{m}_v$ in kg/h but $\lambda$ in kJ/kg and Q demanded in kW → divide by 3600. Examiners *specifically* test this conversion. > 5. **Split-range without a sketch:** A verbal description alone is incomplete — always include the output-vs-opening table or graph. > 6. **Ignoring sub-cooling:** If the problem says "sub-cooled by 5 °C," include $C_{p,l}(T_{sat} - T_{sub})$ in the energy balance. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3 / Control of Condenser** - **Condenser types:** Total (100 % condensed) vs. Partial (only a fraction condensed). Total is overwhelmingly more common. - **Primary controlled variables:** Condenser pressure, reflux-drum level, coolant outlet temperature, top-product purity. - **Primary manipulated variables:** Cooling-water flow, reflux flow, vent valve, distillate draw. - **Core duty equation:** $Q = \dot{m}_v \cdot \lambda$ (latent-heat dominated). - **Coolant-side equation:** $Q = \dot{m}_c \cdot C_{p,c} \cdot \Delta T_c$. - **Heat exchanger rating:** $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LM}$, where $\Delta T_{LM} = (\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2) / \ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)$. - **Cascade scheme:** Master = pressure, Slave = coolant temperature. **3–10× faster disturbance rejection** than single-loop. - **Split-range scheme:** Single controller output → two valves (vent + coolant). Used during startup/shutdown and large load swings. - **Reflux-drum level control:** Manipulates reflux (or distillate) flow; protects against pump cavitation and ensures reflux-ratio stability. - **Antoine equation:** $P_{vap} = 10^{A - B/(T+C)}$ links saturation temperature and pressure — fundamental to pressure-control logic. - **Dynamic residence time of vapor in condenser:** $\tau_{cond} = M_{holdup} / (\dot{m}_v)$ — sets the achievable loop speed. - **Sub-cooling margin:** Always cool liquid 5–10 °C below $T_{sat}$ to prevent vapor locking in reflux pumps. - **Interaction:** Coolant flow ↔ Pressure (strong); Reflux flow ↔ Purity (strong); Level ↔ Pressure (weak via inventory). - **ISA tag prefixes to memorize:** PT, TT, LT, PIC, TIC, LIC, FCV, LCV. - **Design margin check:** Always verify $Q_{rating} > Q_{required}$ by 15–25 % for turndown safety.

topics.title: Reboiler
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT743 - INDUSTRIAL PROCESS CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Reboiler # 1. Reboiler — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Reboiler (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology):** A **reboiler** is a shell-and-tube or plate-type **heat exchanger** installed at the bottom of a fractionating (distillation) column whose primary function is to supply the **latent heat of vaporization** ($\lambda$, in kJ/kmol) to the bottom liquid, generating **vapor boil-up** ($V_B$, in kmol/h) that is recycled back into the column as the **driving vapor stream** for the separation process. In the formal language of **Process Control Engineering**, the reboiler is treated as a **thermal energy input actuator** — it acts as the principal **manipulated energy source** whose duty ($Q_R$, in kW) directly governs the **internal vapor flow** and hence the **reflux-to-vapor ratio** inside the column. Therefore, the reboiler is a **critical control element** in any distillation control strategy (e.g., *Material Balance Control*, *Energy Balance Control*, *Dual Composition Control*). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (PEICT743, Module 3):** The reboiler is studied as a **distributed/lumped thermal system** exhibiting **first-order-plus-dead-time (FOPDT)** or **second-order lag** dynamics, and forms the **manipulated variable loop** in distillation column control. Students must master its **energy balance**, **transfer function**, and **interaction with column hydraulics**. ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Imagine you are boiling water in a pressure cooker to make rice. The **burner underneath** is your **reboiler**. The amount of **steam you supply** to the burner decides **how vigorously** the water boils. In a distillation column: - The **liquid mixture** at the bottom of the column = water + rice grains (different volatilities). - The **boiling action** = vaporization of the more volatile component. - The **steam flow** to the reboiler = the *knob* you turn to control how fast the separation happens. - The **condenser at the top** = the lid of the cooker that captures and returns the steam (reflux). Just as a cook adjusts the burner flame to control boiling intensity, a **process control engineer adjusts the reboiler steam valve** to control **product purity**, **column temperature profile**, and **bottom composition**. ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics > [!NOTE] > The following **standard engineering metrics** must be used in all reboiler calculations (KTU board examinations expect SI units): > - **Latent heat of vaporization of water at 1 atm:** $\lambda_{H_2O} = 2257$ **kJ/kg** > - **Steam enthalpy (saturated, 5 bar):** $H_s \approx 2746$ **kJ/kg** > - **Feedwater enthalpy (saturated liquid, 5 bar):** $H_w \approx 640$ **kJ/kg** > - **Specific heat of water:** $C_p = 4.186$ **kJ/(kg·K)** > - **Standard reboiler residence time:** $\tau = 5$ to **15 minutes** > - **Standard reboiler holdup:** $M_L = 50$ to **5000 kg** ## 1.4 Industrial Significance Reboilers are ubiquitous in: - **Petroleum refineries** (crude distillation, vacuum columns) - **Petrochemical plants** (ethylene, propylene, aromatics) - **Pharmaceutical industry** (solvent recovery) - **Air separation units** (cryogenic distillation) - **Chemical processing** (ethanol-water, acid-water separations) The reboiler contributes approximately **60–70 %** of the total operating cost of a distillation column, making its **efficient control** a key economic concern. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Types of Reboilers — Classification Logic The reboiler family is classified based on the **circulation mechanism** of the process liquid: ### 2.1.1 Kettle Reboiler - **Mechanism:** Natural convection; process liquid boils in an externally heated shell. - **Geometry:** Large vapor space, shell-side liquid. - **Advantages:** Easy to clean, good for fouling service, simple construction. - **Disadvantages:** High residence time, large holdup, slow dynamics. - **Typical Use:** Viscous, fouling, or corrosive services. ### 2.1.2 Vertical Thermosyphon Reboiler - **Mechanism:** Natural circulation driven by **density difference** between the two-phase mixture inside the tubes and the subcooled liquid in the column sump. - **Geometry:** Tube bundle installed vertically; liquid from column bottom enters the bottom, vapor-liquid mixture exits from the top. - **Advantages:** Compact, high heat transfer coefficients, **fast dynamics**. - **Disadvantages:** Sensitive to column level, requires careful hydraulic design. - **Typical Use:** Most common in modern refineries. ### 2.1.3 Horizontal Thermosyphon Reboiler - **Mechanism:** Same as vertical thermosyphon but with horizontal tube orientation. - **Advantages:** Handles larger heat duties, lower elevation, suitable for fouling. - **Disadvantages:** Lower heat transfer coefficients than vertical, less stable circulation. ### 2.1.4 Forced Circulation Reboiler - **Mechanism:** **Mechanical pump** forces liquid circulation through the exchanger. - **Advantages:** Highest heat duty capacity, decouples circulation from column hydraulics. - **Disadvantages:** High capital and operating cost, mechanical reliability issues. ## 2.2 Why the Reboiler Dynamics Matter > [!IMPORTANT] > The reboiler is **the slowest thermal element** in a distillation loop. Its dynamics govern: > - **Settling time** of composition control loops > - **Stability** of cascade and ratio control schemes > - **Tuning constants** ($K_c$, $\tau_I$, $\tau_D$) of the master composition controller > - **Anti-windup** strategy for the steam valve A wrong assumption of **instantaneous energy transfer** leads to **oscillatory or unstable** column control. ## 2.3 The Two-Energy-Domain Modeling Logic A reboiler, regardless of type, is modeled as an **interacting two-domain thermal system**: 1. **Heating-medium (Steam) Domain:** Energy carried by condensing steam. 2. **Process (Liquid/Vapor) Domain:** Energy absorbed by the process fluid to drive vaporization. The two domains are coupled through a **heat transfer resistance** represented by the **overall heat transfer coefficient $U$** (in kW/(m²·K)) and the **heat transfer area $A$** (in m²). > **Heat Transfer Rate Equation (Newton's Law of Cooling):** > $$Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}$$ > where $\Delta T_{LMTD}$ is the log-mean temperature difference between the heating medium and the process fluid. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet The following table contains **all mandatory equations** that a KTU examiner expects students to reproduce verbatim in a 14-mark question on reboiler modeling. | **S. No.** | **Quantity / Parameter** | **Formula** | **Physical Meaning** | **Unit** | |:----------:|:------------------------|:-----------:|:--------------------|:--------:| | 1 | Heat duty | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}$ | Energy transfer rate | kW | | 2 | Steam-side energy balance | $M_s C_{ps} \dfrac{dT_s}{dt} = F_s \rho_s C_{ps}(T_{si} - T_s) - Q$ | Steam condensation cooling | kW | | 3 | Process-side energy balance (Kettle) | $M_L C_{pL} \dfrac{dT_L}{dt} = F_L \rho_L C_{pL}(T_{Li} - T_L) + Q - \lambda V_B$ | Liquid heating + vaporization | kW | | 4 | Vapor boil-up rate | $V_B = \dfrac{Q}{\lambda}$ | Molar rate of vapor produced | kmol/s | | 5 | Reboiler duty (compositional form) | $Q = D \cdot \lambda \cdot (R+1) \cdot y_D$ | Energy required for given separation | kW | | 6 | FOPDT transfer function | $G(s) = \dfrac{K \cdot e^{-\theta s}}{\tau s + 1}$ | Approximate dynamic model | — | | 7 | Process gain (steam → boil-up) | $K = \dfrac{\Delta V_B}{\Delta F_s}\bigg\vert_{ss}$ | Sensitivity of boil-up to steam | (kmol/s)/(kg/s) | | 8 | Thermal time constant | $\tau = \dfrac{M_L C_{pL}}{F_L \rho_L C_{pL} + U A}$ | First-order lag | s | | 9 | Dead time (transport) | $\theta = \dfrac{L_{pipe}}{v_{flow}}$ | Transport delay in piping | s | | 10 | Log-mean temperature difference | $\Delta T_{LMTD} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | Mean driving force | K | | 11 | Reboiler energy efficiency | $\eta_R = \dfrac{\lambda V_B}{F_s (H_s - H_w)}$ | Fraction of steam energy used | dimensionless | | 12 | Thermosyphon driving head | $\Delta P_{driving} = (\rho_L - \rho_{TP}) g H$ | Natural circulation pressure | Pa | > [!NOTE] > **Common Student Mistake in KTU Board Exams:** Writing **$F_s \cdot (H_s - H_w)$** in the energy balance **without** the **$C_{ps}$** correction when using temperature instead of enthalpy. Always use **enthalpy** form $F_s \cdot \rho_s \cdot C_{ps} \cdot (T_{si} - T_s)$ for *transient* analysis, and $F_s \cdot (H_s - H_w)$ for *steady-state* duty calculation. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility | **Engineering Field** | **Application of Reboiler Modeling** | |:---------------------|:--------------------------------------| | **Distillation Control** | Tuning cascade, ratio, and override controllers | | **Process Safety** | Predicting thermal runaway, relief valve sizing | | **Plant Design** | Sizing heat transfer area, selecting tube bundle | | **Energy Optimization** | Pinch analysis, heat integration, steam header design | | **Digital Twin / APC** | Soft-sensor development, model-predictive control (MPC) | | **Operator Training Simulators (OTS)** | Realistic dynamic response for trainee practice | --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > **Derivation #1 — FOPDT Transfer Function of a Kettle Reboiler** > This derivation is the **most frequently asked 14-mark derivation** in KTU PEICT743. Students are expected to write it **line-by-line** with **explicit physical justification** for each step. ## 3.1 System Description Consider a **kettle reboiler** heated by **saturated steam** at the shell side, with **subcooled process liquid** boiling on the tube side. The model assumes: - Constant liquid holdup $M_L$ (kg) - Constant steam-side holdup $M_s$ (kg) - Constant physical properties ($\rho$, $C_p$, $\lambda$, $U$) - Negligible vapor-phase accumulation in the kettle ## 3.2 Step 1 — Steam-Side Energy Balance The rate of energy storage in the steam (condensing) side equals the **incoming energy minus the heat transferred to the process fluid**. > **Step 1.1: General form of energy balance** > $$\text{Rate of energy storage} = \text{Energy in} - \text{Energy transferred out}$$ > **Step 1.2: Substitute physical quantities** > $$M_s \cdot C_{ps} \cdot \frac{dT_s}{dt} = F_s \cdot \rho_s \cdot C_{ps} \cdot (T_{si} - T_s) - Q$$ > **Step 1.3: Express heat transfer in terms of temperatures** > $$Q = U \cdot A \cdot (T_s - T_L)$$ > **Step 1.4: Substituting (3) into (2)** > $$M_s \cdot C_{ps} \cdot \frac{dT_s}{dt} = F_s \cdot \rho_s \cdot C_{ps} \cdot (T_{si} - T_s) - U \cdot A \cdot (T_s - T_L)$$ ## 3.3 Step 2 — Process-Side Energy Balance The rate of energy storage in the process liquid equals the **incoming sensible heat plus the heat received from steam minus the vaporization duty**. > **Step 2.1: General form** > $$M_L \cdot C_{pL} \cdot \frac{dT_L}{dt} = F_L \cdot \rho_L \cdot C_{pL} \cdot (T_{Li} - T_L) + Q - \lambda \cdot V_B$$ > **Step 2.2: Substitute Q from (3)** > $$M_L \cdot C_{pL} \cdot \frac{dT_L}{dt} = F_L \cdot \rho_L \cdot C_{pL} \cdot (T_{Li} - T_L) + U \cdot A \cdot (T_s - T_L) - \lambda \cdot V_B$$ > **Step 2.3: Express vapor boil-up in terms of Q** > Since the reboiler duty is the dominant term driving vaporization, and for a *lumped* control model we assume $V_B \approx Q / \lambda$ (instantaneous vaporization): > $$\lambda \cdot V_B = Q = U \cdot A \cdot (T_s - T_L)$$ > **Step 2.4: Combine (2.2) and (2.3) — the vaporization term dominates** > For control purposes, the *sensible heating* of liquid is small compared to the *latent heat* of vaporization. Therefore: > $$M_L \cdot C_{pL} \cdot \frac{dT_L}{dt} \approx U \cdot A \cdot (T_s - T_L) - \lambda \cdot V_B$$ ## 3.4 Step 3 — Combine the Two Balances and Linearize > **Step 3.1: Take Laplace transform of the steam-side equation (assuming constant $T_{Li}$ and $F_L$)** > $$M_s \cdot C_{ps} \cdot s \cdot T_s(s) = F_s \cdot \rho_s \cdot C_{ps} \cdot T_{si}(s) - F_s \cdot \rho_s \cdot C_{ps} \cdot T_s(s) - U \cdot A \cdot [T_s(s) - T_L(s)]$$ > **Step 3.2: Rearrange to group $T_s$ terms** > $$T_s(s) \cdot [M_s \cdot C_{ps} \cdot s + F_s \cdot \rho_s \cdot C_{ps} + U \cdot A] = F_s \cdot \rho_s \cdot C_{ps} \cdot T_{si}(s) + U \cdot A \cdot T_L(s)$$ > **Step 3.3: Solve for $T_s(s)$ as a function of $T_{si}(s)$ and $T_L(s)$** > $$T_s(s) = \frac{F_s \rho_s C_{ps} \cdot T_{si}(s) + U A \cdot T_L(s)}{M_s C_{ps} s + F_s \rho_s C_{ps} + U A}$$ > **Step 3.4: Define the steam-side time constant $\tau_s$ and gain $K_s$** > $$\tau_s = \frac{M_s C_{ps}}{F_s \rho_s C_{ps} + U A}, \quad K_s = \frac{F_s \rho_s C_{ps}}{F_s \rho_s C_{ps} + U A}$$ > **Step 3.5: Steam temperature transfer function** > $$T_s(s) = \frac{K_s \cdot T_{si}(s) + (1 - K_s) \cdot T_L(s) / \text{ratio}}{\tau_s s + 1}$$ > **Step 3.6: Take Laplace transform of the process-side (dominant) equation** > Assuming the *sensible heating* of the bulk liquid $M_L C_{pL} dT_L/dt$ is **small** (because the latent heat dominates), the process side reduces to a **pseudo-algebraic relation**: > $$\lambda \cdot V_B(s) \approx U \cdot A \cdot [T_s(s) - T_L(s)]$$ ## 3.5 Step 4 — Assemble the Overall Transfer Function > **Step 4.1: Substitute $T_s(s)$ into the boil-up equation** > $$V_B(s) = \frac{U A}{\lambda} \cdot \left[\frac{K_s \cdot T_{si}(s) + (1 - K_s) T_L(s)}{\tau_s s + 1} - T_L(s)\right]$$ > **Step 4.2: Simplify — assume $T_{si}$ is the manipulated variable (steam temperature)** > If $T_L$ is approximately constant (or treated as a measured disturbance), the transfer function from steam temperature $T_{si}$ to vapor boil-up $V_B$ is: > $$\frac{V_B(s)}{T_{si}(s)} = \frac{(U A / \lambda) \cdot K_s}{\tau_s s + 1}$$ > **Step 4.3: Final FOPDT form (with transport dead time $\theta$)** > $$\boxed{\;G_{reboiler}(s) = \frac{V_B(s)}{T_{si}(s)} = \frac{K_{eff} \cdot e^{-\theta s}}{\tau_{eff} \, s + 1}\;}$$ > where: > $$K_{eff} = \frac{U A K_s}{\lambda}, \quad \tau_{eff} = \tau_s = \frac{M_s C_{ps}}{F_s \rho_s C_{ps} + U A}, \quad \theta \approx \frac{L_{pipe}}{v_{steam}}$$ ## 3.6 Numerical Example (Worked Out) > **Problem:** A kettle reboiler uses saturated steam at $T_{si} = 150°$ C. Steam holdup $M_s = 50$ kg, $C_{ps} = 4.2$ kJ/(kg·K), steam mass flow $F_s \rho_s = 0.5$ kg/s, $UA = 20$ kW/K, latent heat $\lambda = 2200$ kJ/kg. Determine the **process gain** and **time constant** of the FOPDT model. > **Step 1: Compute steam-side time constant** > $$\tau_{eff} = \frac{M_s \cdot C_{ps}}{F_s \rho_s \cdot C_{ps} + U A} = \frac{50 \times 4.2}{0.5 \times 4.2 + 20}$$ > **Step 2: Numerical evaluation** > $$\tau_{eff} = \frac{210}{2.1 + 20} = \frac{210}{22.1} \approx 9.50 \text{ s}$$ > **Step 3: Compute steam-side dimensionless gain** > $$K_s = \frac{F_s \rho_s C_{ps}}{F_s \rho_s C_{ps} + U A} = \frac{2.1}{22.1} \approx 0.0950$$ > **Step 4: Compute effective overall gain (steam temperature → boil-up)** > $$K_{eff} = \frac{U A \cdot K_s}{\lambda} = \frac{20 \times 0.0950}{2200} \approx 8.64 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/s/K}$$ > **Final FOPDT Model:** > $$G_{reboiler}(s) = \frac{8.64 \times 10^{-4} \cdot e^{-\theta s}}{9.50 \, s + 1}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Physical interpretation:** The reboiler has a **time constant of 9.5 seconds** — slow compared to flow loops (~1 s) but fast compared to composition loops (~minutes). This justifies placing the **temperature loop** as a *secondary* (inner) loop in a **cascade control** structure. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rendering Note:** A physically accurate reboiler drawing requires isometric geometry. The following Mermaid block renders a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** mapping all control interactions, energy paths, and signal flows — a KTU-approved representation for control engineering questions. ## 4.1 Reboiler Functional Architecture Flow ```mermaid flowchart TB node1["Steam Supply Header
High Pressure Steam 5 to 40 bar"] node2["Steam Control Valve SVCV
Manipulated Variable MV"] node3["Steam Flow Transmitter SFT
FT Signal 4 to 20 mA"] node4["Kettle Reboiler Unit
Two Domain Heat Exchanger"] node5["Process Liquid Inlet PLI
Subcooled Bottom Liquid"] node6["Vapor Boil-up Outlet VBO
Recycled to Column"] node7["Bottom Liquid Return BLR
To Column Sump"] node8["Steam Temperature Transmitter STT
TT Signal 4 to 20 mA"] node9["Process Temperature Transmitter PTT
Tray Temperature Measurement"] nodeA["Reboiler Level Controller RLC
LIC Loop"] nodeB["Steam Flow Controller SFC
FIC Loop Slave"] nodeC["Temperature Master Controller TMC
TIC Loop Master"] nodeD["Composition Analyzer AC
Bottom Product Xb Measurement"] nodeE["Composition Master Controller CMC
AIC Loop Master"] nodeF["DCS Distributed Control System
Signal Conditioning and Alarms"] nodeG["Safety Interlock SIS
High Temperature Trip and Low Level Trip"] node1 --> node2 node2 --> node4 node3 --> nodeB nodeB --> node2 node5 --> node4 node4 --> node6 node4 --> node7 node4 --> node8 node4 --> nodeA node9 --> nodeC node8 --> nodeB nodeC --> nodeB nodeA --> nodeF nodeB --> nodeF nodeC --> nodeF nodeD --> nodeE nodeE --> nodeC nodeF --> nodeG ``` ## 4.2 Signal Flow and Control Hierarchy Description | **Stage** | **Element** | **Role in Control** | **Signal Type** | |:---------:|:------------|:--------------------|:---------------:| | 1 | Steam Supply Header | Energy source, primary disturbance | Mass flow | | 2 | Steam Control Valve (SVCV) | **MV** — final control element | Valve position (%) | | 3 | Steam Flow Transmitter (SFT) | Feedback for slave loop | 4–20 mA | | 4 | Kettle Reboiler | **Process** — energy transfer | Thermal dynamics | | 5 | Process Liquid Inlet (PLI) | Disturbance — feed subcooling | Temperature | | 6 | Vapor Boil-up Outlet (VBO) | **Controlled output** (indirect) | Mass flow | | 7 | Steam Temperature (STT) | **CV** of master cascade loop | 4–20 mA | | 8 | Process Temperature (PTT) | Tray temperature indicator | 4–20 mA | | 9 | Level Controller (RLC) | Override protection loop | 4–20 mA | | 10 | Composition Master (CMC) | **Ultimate CV** — product purity | mA / digital | | 11 | Safety Interlock (SIS) | **Trip** — independent protection layer | Relay / digital | > [!NOTE] > **Cascade Structure Adopted:** The diagram shows a **three-level cascade**: > 1. **Inner (Slave):** Steam Flow (FIC) — fastest loop, ~1 s. > 2. **Middle (Master):** Steam Temperature (TIC) — moderate loop, ~10 s. > 3. **Outer (Master-of-Master):** Composition (AIC) — slow loop, ~minutes. > This **series cascade** is the *industry standard* for distillation control and is a frequent 7-mark sub-question in KTU 14-mark answers. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` **Differentiate between a kettle reboiler and a vertical thermosyphon reboiler in terms of construction, circulation mechanism, holdup, and dynamic response.** > **Model Answer (3 marks — exact key points expected):** > - **Construction:** Kettle has a large external shell with a tube bundle submerged in liquid; thermosyphon has tubes installed as an integral part of the column. > - **Circulation:** Kettle uses natural convection in a pool; thermosyphon uses natural circulation driven by **density difference** between the two-phase mixture inside the tubes and the subcooled liquid in the column. > - **Holdup:** Kettle has **large holdup** (slow response, $\tau \approx 5$–15 min); thermosyphon has **small holdup** (fast response, $\tau \approx 30$ s–2 min). > - **Dynamic Response:** Kettle exhibits **large time constant** and is suited for **smooth, stable operation**; thermosyphon is **fast but sensitive** to column level and pressure drop. ### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **What is the significance of the reboiler duty $Q_R$ in distillation column control? Explain how it acts as the manipulated variable in a typical composition control scheme.** > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > - The reboiler duty $Q_R$ is the **primary energy input** to the column and directly controls the **vapor boil-up rate $V_B$** through $V_B = Q_R / \lambda$. > - Manipulating $Q_R$ (by adjusting the **steam flow control valve**) changes the **internal reflux ratio**, which in turn shifts the **composition profile** on the trays. > - In a **dual composition control scheme**, the reboiler duty is cascaded with the **bottom product composition controller** (AIC), while the reflux controls the top composition. This decouples the top and bottom control actions and improves regulatory performance. --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Long Answer Questions (With Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > - Students **commonly lose 2–3 marks** by skipping the **physical justification** of each modeling step (e.g., "why we assume negligible sensible heating"). > - **Do not** confuse **steam temperature** $T_s$ with **steam saturation temperature** — clarify the difference. > - Always **state assumptions** explicitly before writing the differential equations. > - **Unit consistency** (kJ vs. kW, kg vs. kmol) is checked strictly; a missing unit can cost **half a mark** per equation. > - In the **cascade control** part, students often forget to draw the **block diagram**; this is mandatory for full marks. ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` **(a)** Derive the **dynamic mathematical model** of a **kettle reboiler**, clearly stating the assumptions, and obtain its **first-order-plus-dead-time (FOPDT) transfer function** between the **manipulated steam temperature** and the **vapor boil-up rate**. **(7 marks)** > **Model Solution:** > > **Assumptions (1 mark):** > 1. Constant holdups $M_s$ and $M_L$. > 2. Constant physical properties ($C_{ps}$, $C_{pL}$, $\lambda$, $U$). > 3. Negligible vapor-phase accumulation. > 4. Constant inlet temperatures $T_{si}$, $T_{Li}$. > 5. Vapor boil-up $V_B = Q / \lambda$. > > **Steam-Side Energy Balance (2 marks):** > $$M_s C_{ps} \frac{dT_s}{dt} = F_s \rho_s C_{ps} (T_{si} - T_s) - U A (T_s - T_L)$$ > > **Process-Side Energy Balance (2 marks):** > $$M_L C_{pL} \frac{dT_L}{dt} = F_L \rho_L C_{pL} (T_{Li} - T_L) + U A (T_s - T_L) - \lambda V_B$$ > > **Laplace Domain and Simplification (1 mark):** > Assuming $M_L C_{pL}$ contribution is small (latent heat dominates), and after Laplace transform: > $$T_s(s) = \frac{K_s T_{si}(s) + (1-K_s) T_L(s)}{\tau_s s + 1}$$ > $$\tau_s = \frac{M_s C_{ps}}{F_s \rho_s C_{ps} + UA}, \quad K_s = \frac{F_s \rho_s C_{ps}}{F_s \rho_s C_{ps} + UA}$$ > > **FOPDT Result (1 mark):** > $$\boxed{\;\frac{V_B(s)}{T_{si}(s)} = \frac{(UA/\lambda) \cdot K_s \cdot e^{-\theta s}}{\tau_s s + 1} = \frac{K_{eff} e^{-\theta s}}{\tau_{eff} s + 1}\;}$$ **(b)** With a **neat block diagram**, explain the **cascade control scheme** for a reboiler, identifying the **primary (master)**, **secondary (slave)**, and **manipulated** variables. Discuss how this scheme improves disturbance rejection. **(7 marks)** > **Model Solution:** > > **Block Diagram (3 marks):** > > ``` > [Composition Set Point] --> (AIC Master) > ↑ | > | v > [Bottom Xb] [Steam Temperature Set Point] > | > v > (TIC Slave) > | > v > [Steam Flow SP] > | > v > (FIC Inner Slave) > | > v > [Steam Control Valve SVCV] > | > v > [Kettle Reboiler] > | > v > [Vapor Boil-up V_B] > ``` > > **Variables Identification (2 marks):** > - **Primary CV:** Bottom product composition $X_B$ (controlled by master AIC). > - **Secondary CV:** Steam temperature $T_s$ (controlled by slave TIC). > - **Inner CV:** Steam flow $F_s$ (controlled by slave FIC). > - **MV:** Steam control valve position. > > **Disturbance Rejection (2 marks):** > 1. **Steam header pressure disturbance** is rejected by the **inner FIC** before it propagates — *fast* correction. > 2. **Feed composition disturbance** is rejected by the **TIC** through temperature feedback. > 3. **Slow composition drift** is corrected by the **outer AIC** master. > 4. Overall: **reduced integral error**, **faster settling**, **decoupled tuning** (slave tuned first, then master). ### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` **(a)** With a **neat sketch**, explain the construction and working of a **vertical thermosyphon reboiler**. Derive an expression for the **driving head** for natural circulation. **(7 marks)** > **Model Solution:** > > **Construction and Working (3 marks):** > - A vertical thermosyphon consists of a **tube bundle** installed vertically, connected at the bottom to the **column sump** and at the top to the **column interior**. > - **Subcooled liquid** from the column bottom enters the tubes at the bottom, **absorbs heat** from the shell-side condensing steam, **partially vaporizes**, and the **two-phase mixture** rises to the top due to **buoyancy**. > - The **vapor-liquid mixture** disengages at the top; vapor joins the column's internal vapor flow, and the unvaporized liquid returns to the sump. > > **Driving Head Derivation (4 marks):** > - Let $H$ = vertical height of the tube bundle (m). > - $\rho_L$ = density of subcooled liquid in the column sump. > - $\rho_{TP}$ = density of the two-phase mixture inside the tubes. > - The **hydrostatic pressure at the tube bottom (column side)** is $P_{col} = P_{top} + \rho_L g H$. > - The **hydrostatic pressure at the tube bottom (tube side)** is $P_{tube} = P_{top} + \rho_{TP} g H$. > - The **driving head** is the difference: > $$\Delta P_{driving} = (\rho_L - \rho_{TP}) g H$$ > - For natural circulation, this head must **exceed the total frictional pressure drop** $\Delta P_{friction}$ through the tubes: > $$\boxed{\;\Delta P_{driving} = (\rho_L - \rho_{TP}) g H \geq \Delta P_{friction}\;}$$ **(b)** Discuss the **interaction between the reboiler and the distillation column**, with specific reference to (i) **vapor boil-up effects on tray hydraulics**, (ii) **reflux-to-boil-up ratio**, and (iii) **bottoms level dynamics**. **(7 marks)** > **Model Solution:** > > **(i) Vapor Boil-up Effects on Tray Hydraulics (2 marks):** > - Increasing reboiler duty increases $V_B$, which raises the **vapor velocity** $v_v$ on each tray. > - If $v_v$ approaches the **flooding velocity**, the tray **jets** and the column efficiency collapses. > - The reboiler thus has an **upper constraint** on duty imposed by **column flooding**. > > **(ii) Reflux-to-Boil-up Ratio (2 marks):** > - The internal operating line slope is determined by $L / V = R / (R+1)$ at the top, but inside the column, the **local** $L / V$ depends on $V_B$. > - Higher $V_B$ (without changing $L$) decreases $L / V$, pushing compositions toward the **bottom product** specification and away from the **top**. > - Therefore, $V_B$ is the **primary handle** for bottom composition; reflux $L$ handles the top. > > **(iii) Bottoms Level Dynamics (3 marks):** > - The bottoms level is governed by the **liquid inventory balance**: > $$\frac{dM_B}{dt} = L_{N+1} - V_B - B$$ > - A sudden increase in $V_B$ drops the level **faster** than the level controller can compensate, creating a **sump transient** that disturbs the thermosyphon head. > - This is why **thermosyphon reboilers** require **tight level control** (typically $\pm 5$% of span) to prevent **flow instabilities** and **tube vibration**. > - A **kettle reboiler** is decoupled from column hydraulics and is preferred when level control is poor. > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfall:** Students often treat the reboiler as an **isolated** unit and forget its **two-way coupling** with the column. Always discuss both the **column's effect on the reboiler** (level, pressure) and the **reboiler's effect on the column** (vapor flow, composition). --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU PEICT743 | Module 3 | Reboiler** - [x] A **reboiler** is a **heat exchanger** at the **bottom of a distillation column** that supplies **latent heat** for vaporization. - [x] The **vapor boil-up rate** $V_B$ is given by $V_B = Q_R / \lambda$. - [x] **Four major types:** Kettle, Vertical Thermosyphon, Horizontal Thermosyphon, Forced Circulation. - [x] **Kettle** = large holdup, slow dynamics, easy maintenance. - [x] **Thermosyphon** = small holdup, fast dynamics, **density-driven circulation**. - [x] **Forced circulation** = pump-driven, used for viscous or heat-sensitive fluids. - [x] The dynamic model is **FOPDT**: $G(s) = K_{eff} e^{-\theta s} / (\tau_{eff} s + 1)$. - [x] **Steam-side time constant:** $\tau_s = M_s C_{ps} / (F_s \rho_s C_{ps} + U A)$. - [x] **Process gain:** $K_{eff} = U A K_s / \lambda$. - [x] **Thermosyphon driving head:** $\Delta P_{driving} = (\rho_L - \rho_{TP}) g H$. - [x] Reboiler duty is the **primary MV** for **bottom composition** control. - [x] **Cascade structure:** Inner FIC (steam flow) → Middle TIC (steam temp) → Outer AIC (composition). - [x] **Reboiler-Column interactions:** Vapor boil-up affects **tray hydraulics**, **reflux-to-boil-up ratio**, and **bottoms level dynamics**. - [x] **Steam-side disturbance** (header pressure) is rejected by the **inner slave loop** — never by the master. - [x] Always **state assumptions** before deriving the model (1 mark guaranteed). - [x] **Energy efficiency** $\eta_R = \lambda V_B / [F_s (H_s - H_w)]$ is a key performance indicator. - [x] Standard units in KTU exams: **kW** for duty, **K** for temperature, **Pa** for pressure, **kmol/h** for molar flow. ---

topics.title: Control of Reboiler
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT743 - INDUSTRIAL PROCESS CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Control of Reboiler

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 : Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Reboiler** is a heat exchanger located at the bottom of a distillation column whose primary function is to provide the **vapor boil-up** required to drive the separation of liquid mixtures into their constituent components. In the context of Industrial Process Control, the **Control of a Reboiler** refers to the integrated set of feedback and feedforward control strategies used to manipulate the heat input (typically steam flow rate, duty, or heating medium temperature) and/or product withdrawal rates so as to maintain desired process variables such as bottoms composition, bottoms level, and column temperature profile.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3 – Distillation Column Control):**
> The control of reboilers is examined jointly with the control of condensers, reflux drums, and the column itself. Students must master the **single-end (temperature/dual-composition) control strategies**, **material balance control**, and **energy balance control** configurations.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine boiling water in a pressure cooker on a gas stove:
- The **fire (steam/heating medium)** is the manipulated variable.
- The **level of water inside (bottoms level)** must be maintained — too little and you damage the pot; too much and your soup dilutes.
- The **concentration of dissolved salt (bottoms composition)** is your final quality — controlled indirectly by how vigorously you boil.

In a distillation column, the reboiler plays the role of this stove + pot:
- It receives **liquid bottoms** from the column.
- It transfers heat from a utility (steam, hot oil, or a fired furnace).
- It returns **vapor** back to the column as stripping vapor, while **liquid bottoms product** is withdrawn.

The control challenge is to ensure that:
1. The correct **amount of heat** is supplied (energy balance).
2. The correct **amount of bottoms liquid** is withdrawn (material balance).
3. The reboiler **does not run dry** (flooding / tube damage).

---

### 1.3 Types of Reboilers (Foundation Knowledge)

| Reboiler Type | Driving Force | Typical Application |
|---------------|---------------|---------------------|
| **Kettle Reboiler** | Natural convection, thermosiphon | Small to medium columns |
| **Thermosiphon Reboiler** | Density difference between column and reboiler | Most common in petrochemicals |
| **Forced-Circulation Reboiler** | External pump | Viscous, fouling, or heat-sensitive services |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Metric:** For KTU numerical problems, the latent heat of condensation of saturated steam at **3 bar (g) ≈ 2.14 MJ/kg** and at **10 bar (g) ≈ 2.02 MJ/kg** are commonly referenced. Always cite utility pressure when stating latent heat.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 : Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Control Objectives of a Reboiler

The control system must simultaneously satisfy **three** primary objectives:

1. **Maintain Product Quality (Bottoms Composition, $x_B$):**  
   The heavy key component concentration in the bottoms product must meet specification (e.g., ≤ 0.5% impurity).

2. **Maintain Material Balance (Bottoms Level, $L_B$):**  
   The liquid level in the reboiler (or column sump) must be kept between safe operating limits (typically 40%–70% of span).

3. **Maintain Energy Balance (Steam Flow / Heat Duty, $Q_R$):**  
   The steam (or heating medium) flow must be controlled to deliver the necessary vapor boil-up.

---

### 2.2 The Fundamental Energy Balance Equation

The reboiler is, at its core, a heat exchanger. The rate of heat transfer is governed by:

$$Q_R = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}$$

Where:
- $Q_R$ = heat duty of the reboiler (W or kW)
- $U$ = overall heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K)
- $A$ = heat transfer area (m²)
- $\Delta T_{LMTD}$ = log-mean temperature difference (K)

For a steam-driven reboiler, this heat duty equals the latent heat released by condensing steam:

$$Q_R = F_s \cdot \lambda_s$$

Where:
- $F_s$ = mass flow rate of steam (kg/s)
- $\lambda_s$ = latent heat of vaporization of steam (J/kg)

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the **core formulas** that must be memorized for KTU ESE numerical problems on reboiler control. All equations are written without vertical pipes to maintain table integrity.

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $Q_R = F_s \cdot \lambda_s$ | Heat from condensing steam | W (J/s) |
| 2 | $Q_R = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}$ | Heat transfer rate | W |
| 3 | $V_B = Q_R / \lambda_{mix}$ | Vapor boil-up rate | kmol/s |
| 4 | $L_B = F - D - V_B + L_{reflux}$ | Bottoms liquid flow (component balance) | kmol/s |
| 5 | $R = \dfrac{L}{D}$ | External reflux ratio | dimensionless |
| 6 | $RR = \dfrac{L}{D + L}$ | Internal reflux ratio | dimensionless |
| 7 | $RR = \dfrac{1}{1 + \dfrac{C_p (T_c - T_r)}{\lambda}}$ | Internal reflux ratio (Gilliland-type) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\dot{n}_{steam} = \dfrac{Q_R}{\lambda_s \cdot 3600}$ | Steam consumption | kg/h |
| 9 | $S/F = \dfrac{(x_D - x_F)}{(x_D - x_B^*)}$ | Minimum steam-to-feed ratio (Fenske-Underwood approximation) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\Delta T_{LMTD} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | LMTD for counter-current flow | K |

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In oil refineries, ethylene plants, and LNG fractionation trains, the reboiler often consumes **40%–70%** of the total column energy. Even a **1% improvement** in reboiler control efficiency translates into annual savings worth several million rupees for a medium-scale plant. KTU therefore places strong emphasis on **cascade control of reboiler temperature → steam flow**, and on the integration of the reboiler with the **bottom level and bottoms composition loops**.

---

### 2.5 Standard Control Strategies (3 Configurations)

#### Configuration A : Material Balance Control (Direct Material Balance)
- **Manipulated Variable (MV):** Bottoms flow rate $B$
- **Controlled Variable (CV):** Bottoms level $L_B$
- **Set Point of CV:** Held constant → bottoms flow is fixed.
- **Result:** Bottoms composition is **not directly controlled**; relies on tray temperature.

#### Configuration B : Energy Balance / Vapor Boil-Up Control
- **MV:** Steam flow $F_s$
- **CV:** Tray temperature $T_{tray}$ (or differential pressure $\Delta P$)
- **Set Point of CV:** Held constant → boil-up is fixed.
- **Result:** Stable separation but **slow response** to feed disturbances.

#### Configuration C : Dual Composition / Advanced Control
- **MV:** Steam flow $F_s$ and Bottoms flow $B$
- **CV:** Bottoms composition $x_B$ and Level $L_B$
- **Implementation:** Composition analyzer → controller → steam valve (cascade).
- **Result:** Tightest control but requires **analyzer with 5–15 min dead time**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Insight:** Examiners frequently draw a **P&ID of a distillation column with reboiler** and ask students to identify the control loops. Always remember — *the level loop must always close (for inventory control), while composition/temperature loops close the energy balance.*

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 : Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation : Heat Duty and Steam Consumption of a Reboiler

**Problem Statement (Modeled from typical KTU 14-mark question):**  
A kettle reboiler is used to supply vapor to the bottom of a distillation column. The column processes **$F = 100 \text{ kmol/h}$** of a benzene-toluene mixture with feed composition $z_F = 0.40$ (mole fraction benzene). Distillate composition $x_D = 0.95$, bottoms composition $x_B = 0.05$. The molar latent heat of vaporization of the mixture is $\lambda_{mix} = 30000 \text{ kJ/kmol}$. Saturated steam at 3 bar (g) is used with latent heat $\lambda_s = 2140 \text{ kJ/kg}$.

Calculate:
1. Vapor boil-up rate from the reboiler.
2. Heat duty of the reboiler in kW.
3. Steam flow rate required (kg/h).
4. Steam-to-feed ratio on mass basis.

---

**Step 1 : Overall Material Balance**

$$F = D + B$$

Where $F$ = feed, $D$ = distillate, $B$ = bottoms (all in kmol/h).

**Step 2 : Component Balance for Benzene**

$$F \cdot z_F = D \cdot x_D + B \cdot x_B$$

$$100 \times 0.40 = D \times 0.95 + B \times 0.05$$

$$40 = 0.95 D + 0.05 B$$

**Step 3 : Solve the Two-Equation System**

From material balance:
$$D = F - B = 100 - B$$

Substitute into component balance:
$$40 = 0.95(100 - B) + 0.05 B$$

$$40 = 95 - 0.95 B + 0.05 B$$

$$40 = 95 - 0.90 B$$

$$0.90 B = 55$$

$$B = 61.11 \text{ kmol/h}$$

Then:
$$D = 100 - 61.11 = 38.89 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**Step 4 : Vapor Boil-Up Rate, $V$**

For a typical kettle reboiler providing vapor to the column, assume all bottoms are vaporized then re-contacted (simplified approximation where the column operates with total boil-up at the bottom):

$$V = \frac{D \cdot (R + 1) - F \cdot (q - 1)}{(1 - q) + (R + 1)\frac{x_D}{x_B}}$$

However, the **most common KTU-level approximation** uses the simple latent-heat relation:

$$V_B = \frac{Q_R}{\lambda_{mix}}$$

We will use the energy balance directly. First compute the heat duty using a **rule-of-thumb shortcut**: heat duty equals the latent heat required to vaporize the distillate plus the heat needed to provide reflux plus sensible heat effects. For KTU problems we typically take:

$$Q_R = D \cdot \lambda_{mix} \cdot (R + 1)$$

Assume external reflux ratio $R = L/D = 1.5$ (a reasonable value for benzene-toluene separation):

$$Q_R = 38.89 \times 30000 \times (1.5 + 1)$$

$$Q_R = 38.89 \times 30000 \times 2.5$$

$$Q_R = 2{,}916{,}750 \text{ kJ/h}$$

**Step 5 : Convert to kW**

$$Q_R = \frac{2{,}916{,}750}{3600} = 810.21 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 6 : Steam Flow Rate, $F_s$**

$$F_s = \frac{Q_R}{\lambda_s} = \frac{2{,}916{,}750 \text{ kJ/h}}{2140 \text{ kJ/kg}}$$

$$F_s = 1362.97 \text{ kg/h} \approx 1363 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 7 : Steam-to-Feed Mass Ratio**

Convert feed to mass. Molecular weight of feed (benzene + toluene mix): $M_{mix} \approx (0.40 \times 78) + (0.60 \times 92) = 31.2 + 55.2 = 86.4 \text{ kg/kmol}$.

$$\dot{m}_F = 100 \times 86.4 = 8640 \text{ kg/h}$$

$$\frac{F_s}{\dot{m}_F} = \frac{1363}{8640} = 0.158$$

$$\boxed{\frac{F_s}{\dot{m}_F} \approx 0.158 \text{ kg steam per kg feed}}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation : Internal Reflux Ratio Sensitivity (for Tray Temperature Cascade)

The internal reflux ratio $RR$ inside the column is related to the external reflux ratio by:

$$RR = R \left[1 + \frac{C_p \, (T_c - T_r)}{\lambda}\right]^{-1}$$

For a typical benzene-toluene system: $C_p = 150 \text{ kJ/kmol·K}$, $\lambda = 30000 \text{ kJ/kmol}$, $T_c - T_r = 5 \text{ K}$ (subcooling), and external $R = 1.5$:

$$RR = \frac{1.5}{1 + \dfrac{150 \times 5}{30000}} = \frac{1.5}{1 + 0.025} = \frac{1.5}{1.025} = 1.463$$

This small difference (about 2.5%) demonstrates why **reboiler temperature feedback** is sometimes preferred over external reflux measurement for tight composition control.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix : Selection of Reboiler Control Strategy

| Disturbance | Best MV to Use | Why? |
|-------------|----------------|------|
| Feed composition change | Steam flow ($F_s$) | Energy balance responds fastest to composition shifts. |
| Feed flow rate change | Both $F_s$ and $B$ | Material + energy balance adjustments needed. |
| Feed temperature change | Steam flow ($F_s$) | Direct energy compensation required. |
| Steam pressure fluctuation | Steam flow (using cascade) | Cascade compensates before it affects tray T. |
| Bottoms level drift | Bottoms flow ($B$) | Inventory control is mandatory. |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 : Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart : Reboiler Control Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Feed F enters column] --> B[Distillation column trays]
    B --> C[Bottom liquid L to reboiler]
    C --> D[Reboiler heat exchanger]
    D --> E[Vapor V back to column]
    D --> F[Bottoms product B withdrawn]
    G[Steam supply at utility header] --> H[Steam control valve TC]
    H --> D
    D --> I[Temperature transmitter TT on reboiler outlet]
    I --> J[Temperature controller TC]
    J --> H
    D --> K[Level transmitter LT on reboiler shell]
    K --> L[Level controller LC]
    L --> M[Bottoms product control valve]
    M --> F
    N[Composition analyzer AT on bottoms line] --> O[Composition controller AC]
    O --> J
    P[Pressure transmitter PT on column top] --> Q[Pressure controller PC]
    Q --> R[Condenser]
```

**Reading Guide:**
- **Path 1 (Energy Balance):** `I → J → H` is the **temperature cascade loop** controlling steam flow.
- **Path 2 (Material Balance):** `K → L → M` is the **level control loop** controlling bottoms withdrawal.
- **Path 3 (Quality):** `N → O → J` is the **composition override** that resets the temperature setpoint.
- **Path 4 (Top Pressure):** `P → Q → R` stabilizes column pressure, indirectly stabilizing reboiler operation.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram : Cascade Control Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Bottoms composition xB] --> A2[Master controller AC]
    A2 -->|SP| A3[Slave controller TC]
    A3 -->|MV| A4[Steam valve FCV]
    A4 --> A5[Reboiler heat duty]
    A5 --> A6[Tray temperature T]
    A6 -->|feedback| A3
    A1 -->|feedback| A2
    B1[Steam flow Fs] --> B2[FT transmitter]
    B2 -->|PV| A3
    C1[Reboiler level LB] --> C2[Level controller LC]
    C2 --> C3[Bottoms valve BCV]
```

---

### 4.3 P&ID Style Schematic (Block Representation)

> [!NOTE]
> A physical P&ID with valves, instruments, and piping tags is shown below in **functional block form** suitable for KTU board answers.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ColumnSystem[Distillation Column System]
        direction TB
        Col[Column shell with trays] --> Reb[Kettle Reboiler]
        Reb -->|Vapor| Col
        Reb -->|Liquid bottoms| BottOut[Bottoms product line]
        Col -->|Overhead vapor| Cond[Total Condenser]
        Cond -->|Distillate| RefDrum[Reflux Drum]
        RefDrum -->|Reflux L| Col
        RefDrum -->|Distillate D| DistOut[Distillate product line]
    end
    SteamHdr[Steam Header] -->|FCV 100| Reb
    LC[LC 100] -->|BV 101| BottOut
    TC[TC 102] --> FCV100[FCV 100]
    AC[AC 103] -->|Reset SP| TC
```

**Tag Legend (ISA 5.1 Standard):**
- `FCV 100` — Flow Control Valve (steam to reboiler)
- `TC 102` — Temperature Controller (reboiler outlet)
- `AC 103` — Analyzer Controller (bottoms composition)
- `LC 100` — Level Controller (reboiler shell)
- `BV 101` — Bottoms Valve (level manipulated)

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 : KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A : Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
Define a reboiler and state **two** essential functions it performs in a distillation column.

**Model Answer:**

A reboiler is a heat exchanger installed at the bottom of a distillation column that supplies the necessary **vapor boil-up** to drive the mass-transfer separation of a liquid mixture.

Its two essential functions are:
1. To provide **thermal energy** (via condensing steam, hot oil, or a fired furnace) to partially vaporize the bottom liquid, generating stripping vapor that contacts the descending liquid on the trays.
2. To generate the **liquid bottoms product stream**, which is the heavy-key-rich residue withdrawn from the column.

It serves as both the **energy input point** and the **bottom product withdrawal point** of the column.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**  
List any **three** types of reboilers commonly used in the chemical process industry and mention one distinguishing feature of each.

**Model Answer:**

1. **Kettle Reboiler:** Has a separate vapor space above the boiling liquid; natural circulation; suitable for small-to-medium duty.
2. **Thermosiphon Reboiler:** Circulation is driven by density difference between the column and reboiler; no mechanical pump; most common in petrochemical plants.
3. **Forced-Circulation Reboiler:** Uses an external pump to circulate liquid through the heat exchanger; ideal for viscous or fouling services and where strict tube-side velocity control is needed.

---

### PART B : Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]

**a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **cascade control scheme** used to regulate the heat duty of a distillation column reboiler. Discuss why cascade control is preferred over a single-loop control strategy. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A kettle reboiler receives liquid at its boiling point. Steam at **4 bar (g)** ($\lambda_s = 2137$ kJ/kg) supplies heat. The reboiler delivers $V = 50$ kmol/h of vapor to a column. Molar latent heat of the mixture is $\lambda_{mix} = 32000$ kJ/kmol. Calculate **(i)** the heat duty in kW and **(ii)** the steam consumption in kg/h. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

A **cascade control scheme** for reboiler heat duty uses two controllers in series:

- **Primary (Master) Loop:** Measures the **reboiler outlet temperature** (or tray temperature near the bottom). The temperature controller (TC) compares the measured temperature $T$ with its setpoint $T_{sp}$ and outputs a signal that becomes the setpoint for the secondary loop.

- **Secondary (Slave) Loop:** Measures the **steam flow rate** $F_s$ using a flow transmitter (FT). The flow controller (FC) compares the steam flow with the setpoint issued by the TC and adjusts the steam control valve (FCV).

**Block Diagram (textual):**

$$T_{sp} \rightarrow \text{TC} \rightarrow F_{s,sp} \rightarrow \text{FC} \rightarrow \text{FCV} \rightarrow Q_R \rightarrow T$$

**Why cascade is preferred:**
1. **Disturbance rejection:** Steam pressure fluctuations are corrected by the **inner (flow) loop** before they propagate to the temperature. The temperature loop never sees the disturbance.
2. **Nonlinearity compensation:** The flow loop linearizes the steam valve characteristic.
3. **Faster response:** Inner flow loop has a time constant of seconds; outer temperature loop of minutes. The cascade effectively speeds up the disturbance rejection by an order of magnitude.
4. **Protection against valve stiction:** The flow controller continuously trims the valve position.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**(i) Heat Duty:**

The reboiler delivers $V = 50$ kmol/h of vapor. Heat required = sensible + latent heat. Assuming the liquid enters at boiling point, sensible heat is zero, so:

$$Q_R = V \cdot \lambda_{mix}$$

$$Q_R = 50 \text{ kmol/h} \times 32000 \text{ kJ/kmol}$$

$$Q_R = 1{,}600{,}000 \text{ kJ/h}$$

Converting to kW:

$$Q_R = \frac{1{,}600{,}000}{3600} = 444.44 \text{ kW}$$

$$\boxed{Q_R \approx 444.44 \text{ kW}}$$

**(ii) Steam Consumption:**

$$F_s = \frac{Q_R}{\lambda_s} = \frac{1{,}600{,}000 \text{ kJ/h}}{2137 \text{ kJ/kg}}$$

$$F_s = 748.71 \text{ kg/h}$$

$$\boxed{F_s \approx 748.7 \text{ kg/h}}$$

---

#### **QUESTION B** [Alternative Choice — Internal Choice]

**a)** Explain the **three standard control configurations** for a distillation column reboiler. State which configuration provides the tightest bottoms composition control and justify your answer. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A distillation column separates a binary mixture with the following data:  
- Feed flow $F = 200$ kmol/h, $z_F = 0.50$  
- Distillate $x_D = 0.98$, Bottoms $x_B = 0.02$  
- External reflux ratio $R = 2.0$, Molar latent heat of mixture $= 29000$ kJ/kmol  
- Saturated steam latent heat $= 2200$ kJ/kg  

Calculate: **(i)** Distillate and bottoms flow rates, **(ii)** Vapor boil-up rate from the reboiler (kmol/h), **(iii)** Heat duty in kW, and **(iv)** Steam consumption in kg/h. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Configuration 1 — Material Balance (Level) Control:**  
The bottoms level controller adjusts the bottoms flow rate. This fixes the material balance but does **not** directly control composition. Used in columns where bottoms composition is loosely specified.

**Configuration 2 — Energy Balance (Vapor Boil-Up / Temperature) Control:**  
A temperature controller on a sensitive tray (or reboiler outlet) adjusts the steam flow to the reboiler. This stabilizes the energy balance, which indirectly stabilizes composition.

**Configuration 3 — Dual Composition Control:**  
Both bottoms flow and steam flow are manipulated using two composition analyzers. Bottoms composition controller resets the steam setpoint; level controller adjusts bottoms flow. **This configuration provides the tightest composition control** because both material and energy balances are simultaneously trimmed by direct composition feedback. It does, however, require reliable online analyzers with acceptable lag.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**(i) Distillate and Bottoms Flow Rates:**

Material balance:
$$F = D + B \Rightarrow 200 = D + B$$

Component balance:
$$F \cdot z_F = D \cdot x_D + B \cdot x_B$$

$$200 \times 0.50 = D \times 0.98 + B \times 0.02$$

$$100 = 0.98 D + 0.02 B$$

Substituting $D = 200 - B$:
$$100 = 0.98(200 - B) + 0.02 B$$

$$100 = 196 - 0.98 B + 0.02 B$$

$$100 = 196 - 0.96 B$$

$$0.96 B = 96$$

$$B = 100 \text{ kmol/h}$$

$$D = 200 - 100 = 100 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**(ii) Vapor Boil-Up Rate:**

$$V_B = D \cdot (R + 1) = 100 \times (2.0 + 1) = 300 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**(iii) Heat Duty:**

$$Q_R = V_B \cdot \lambda_{mix} = 300 \times 29000 = 8{,}700{,}000 \text{ kJ/h}$$

$$Q_R = \frac{8{,}700{,}000}{3600} = 2416.67 \text{ kW}$$

**(iv) Steam Consumption:**

$$F_s = \frac{8{,}700{,}000}{2200} = 3954.55 \text{ kg/h}$$

$$\boxed{F_s \approx 3954.5 \text{ kg/h}}$$

---

### ⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Reboiler Control Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert kJ/h to kW** — Students often leave the answer in kJ/h when the question asks for kW. This alone costs 1 mark.
> 2. **Using mass latent heat instead of molar latent heat** — Always check the units of $\lambda$. If given as kJ/kg, multiply by molar flow (kg/h) directly; if kJ/kmol, multiply by kmol/h.
> 3. **Ignoring subcooling / sensible heat** — In real reboilers, the entering liquid may be subcooled by 5–10 K. KTU sometimes tests this by adding a sensible heat term $F \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$.
> 4. **Confusing $R$ and $RR$** — External reflux ratio $R = L/D$, Internal reflux ratio $RR = L/(D+L)$. Using the wrong one in $V_B = D(R+1)$ gives wrong vapor rates.
> 5. **Not labelling the cascade loop correctly** — Many students write "TC controls steam" without specifying that the **slave** is the flow controller. Examiners deduct 2 marks for this.
> 6. **Drawing the reboiler loop upside-down** — The MV of the **level loop** is the bottoms valve (downstream); the MV of the **temperature loop** is the steam valve (upstream). Mixing these up loses 1–2 marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Control of Reboiler" (Module 3):**
> - A reboiler is the **energy input** and **bottoms withdrawal** point of a distillation column.
> - **Heat duty formula:** $Q_R = F_s \cdot \lambda_s = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}$.
> - **Vapor boil-up:** $V_B = D(R + 1)$ (assuming saturated liquid feed, $q = 1$).
> - **Three control configurations:** Material balance (level) → Energy balance (temperature) → Dual composition (analyzer-driven).
