> - **Cascade control** uses TC (master) on temperature and FC (slave) on steam flow for fast disturbance rejection.
> - **Level control is always mandatory** for inventory stability.
> - **Composition control is the ultimate objective** but is limited by analyzer dead time (5–15 min).
> - **Steam pressure fluctuations** must be rejected by the inner flow loop, not by the temperature loop.
> - **Internal reflux ratio** $RR$ is slightly less than external $R$ due to subcooling — usually a 2–5% correction.
> - **Steam-to-feed mass ratio** in a typical distillation column is between **0.1 and 0.4**; benzene-toluene columns sit near 0.15.
> - **Standard KTU latent heats to memorize:** Steam at 3 bar (g) → 2140 kJ/kg; Steam at 10 bar (g) → 2020 kJ/kg.
> - **Kettle reboilers** are most common at small scale; **Thermosiphon** dominates in petrochemicals; **Forced-circulation** is for viscous/fouling service.
> - **Pitfall to avoid:** Do not assume the reboiler outlet temperature equals the bottom tray temperature; they differ by the boiling-point elevation (BPE) of the mixture.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: System Curves
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT743 - INDUSTRIAL PROCESS CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** System Curves # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of fluid transport systems (pumps, compressors, blowers, and piping networks), a **System Curve** is a graphical/analytical representation of the total head (or energy) that must be supplied by a pump to a fluid in order to sustain a given volumetric flow rate through a specific piping network. It mathematically maps the energy-demand profile of the **process (load)** as a function of flow rate. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology for Process Control: > [!IMPORTANT] > **System Curve (Process Curve / Load Curve):** The relationship between the head $H_{sys}$ (in metres of fluid, or kJ/kg) required to push a liquid through a piping system and the volumetric flow rate $Q$ (m³/h or m³/s) at which it flows. It represents the **"demand"** side of the pump–system pair. The general form is: $$H_{sys} = H_{static} + H_{friction}(Q) + H_{minor}(Q) + H_{pressure} + H_{velocity}$$ For a typical turbulent liquid system, the dominant terms collapse into a **parabolic shape**: $$H_{sys} = H_{static} + K \cdot Q^{2}$$ where: - $H_{static}$ is the **static (elevation) head** — a constant independent of flow. - $K$ is the **system resistance coefficient** (depends on pipe roughness, length, diameter, fittings, valves, etc.). - $Q$ is the volumetric flow rate. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Straw & Balloon" Analogy Imagine you are blowing up a balloon through a long, narrow straw. - The **straw + balloon combination** = the **system**. - The **balloon's back-pressure** (stretching resistance) = the **static head** (a constant push-back even at zero flow). - The **narrow straw's friction** = the **friction head** (the harder you blow, the more friction you create — like pumping more water through a pipe means more wall rubbing). - The **relationship between how hard you blow (flow) and the back-pressure you feel (head)** = the **System Curve**. So when you try to "pump" air faster through the straw, you don't just fight the balloon's stretch — you *additionally* fight the straw's friction, which grows quadratically with how fast you blow. ## 1.3 Components of a System Curve — A First Look > [!NOTE] > A system curve is built from **five physical head components**. Mastering these is critical for KTU exam derivations. | # | Head Component | Symbol | Depends on Q? | Real-world cause | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Static / Elevation head | $H_{static}$ | ❌ No | Height difference between source and destination tanks | | 2 | Friction head (pipe wall) | $H_{friction}$ | ✅ Yes (∝ Q²) | Viscous drag on pipe walls | | 3 | Minor (fitting) losses | $H_{minor}$ | ✅ Yes (∝ Q²) | Bends, valves, tees, contractions, expansions | | 4 | Pressure head (downstream) | $H_{pressure}$ | ❌ No (usually) | Pressurised receiver tank | | 5 | Velocity / Kinetic head | $H_{velocity}$ | ✅ Yes (∝ Q²) | Change in kinetic energy (often neglected for liquids in pipes) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 High-Yield Fact:** In nearly all KTU problems, $H_{velocity}$ is **negligible** (since pipe diameter is constant) and the curve simplifies to $H_{sys} = H_{static} + K \cdot Q^{2}$ — a **parabola** that intersects the **y-axis at $H_{static}$**. ## 1.4 Why System Curves Matter in Process Control The **System Curve is one half of the "Pump–System Pair."** The other half is the **Pump (Performance) Curve**, which shows what the pump *can deliver*. Where the two curves intersect is the **Operating Point** — the actual flow and head at which the system will settle. Control engineers manipulate the system curve (via **throttling valves**), the pump curve (via **speed control** or **impeller trimming**), or both to achieve the desired operating point. # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Decomposition of the System Curve ### Step 1 — Static (Elevation) Head The vertical distance between the **suction liquid level** and the **discharge liquid level** creates a constant gravitational pressure differential. Whether you pump 1 m³/h or 1000 m³/h, gravity still demands the same lift. $$H_{static} = \Delta z = z_{discharge} - z_{suction}$$ **KTU Trap:** Even when flow is **zero**, the pump must still generate $H_{static}$ to merely hold the fluid in place against gravity. This is why the system curve *does not* pass through the origin — it starts at $H_{static}$ on the y-axis. ### Step 2 — Friction Head in the Pipe For **turbulent flow** (Reynolds number $Re > 4000$, which is the KTU-default assumption), the **Darcy–Weisbach equation** gives: $$H_{friction} = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{v^{2}}{2g}$$ where: - $f$ = Darcy friction factor (dimensionless, ≈ 0.02 for smooth commercial steel pipe in fully turbulent flow). - $L$ = total straight-pipe length (m). - $D$ = internal pipe diameter (m). - $v$ = average fluid velocity (m/s). - $g$ = 9.81 m/s². Since $v = Q / A_{pipe}$ and $A_{pipe} = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4}$, we have $v \propto Q$, and therefore: $$H_{friction} \propto v^{2} \propto Q^{2}$$ So **friction head is parabolic in Q**. ### Step 3 — Minor Losses (Fittings, Valves, etc.) Each elbow, tee, valve, sudden contraction, or sudden expansion dissipates energy according to: $$H_{minor,i} = K_{i} \cdot \frac{v^{2}}{2g}$$ The standard KTU practice is to convert all minor losses into **equivalent length** $L_{eq}$ using: $$L_{eq,i} = \frac{K_{i} \cdot D}{f}$$ Summing the equivalent lengths: $$L_{total} = L_{straight} + \sum_{i} L_{eq,i}$$ This unifies the system equation into a single friction term. ### Step 4 — Pressure Head (Process Restriction) If the **destination tank is pressurised** (a common scenario in refineries, boilers, reactors), the system must overcome the gauge pressure $P_g$: $$H_{pressure} = \frac{P_{g,discharge} - P_{g,suction}}{\rho \cdot g}$$ For most KTU problems, both tanks are open to atmosphere, so $H_{pressure} = 0$. ### Step 5 — Velocity Head (Usually Neglected) When the pipe diameter is constant, the kinetic energy $\frac{v^{2}}{2g}$ is the same at inlet and outlet, so they cancel. Only when there is a change in diameter (e.g., a nozzle or an orifice) does this term contribute. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > Memorise this table — it represents 70% of the marks in any System Curve / Pump Curve numerical. | # | Formula | Meaning | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $H_{sys} = H_{static} + K \cdot Q^{2}$ | Compact system curve | m | | 2 | $H_{static} = \Delta z$ | Elevation difference | m | | 3 | $H_{friction} = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{v^{2}}{2g}$ | Darcy–Weisbach friction loss | m | | 4 | $H_{minor} = \sum K_{i} \cdot \frac{v^{2}}{2g}$ | Sum of minor losses | m | | 5 | $K = \frac{8 f L_{total}}{\pi^{2} g D^{5}}$ | System resistance coefficient (per Darcy form) | m/(m³/s)² | | 6 | $L_{eq} = \frac{K_{i} \cdot D}{f}$ | Equivalent length for a fitting | m | | 7 | $v = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{4Q}{\pi D^{2}}$ | Average fluid velocity | m/s | | 8 | $Re = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \frac{v D}{\nu}$ | Reynolds number | dimensionless | | 9 | $H_{pump} = H_{sys}$ (at operating point) | Energy balance at steady state | m | | 10 | $Q_{new} = Q_{old} \cdot \left(\frac{N_{new}}{N_{old}}\right)$ | Affinity law — flow | m³/s | | 11 | $H_{new} = H_{old} \cdot \left(\frac{N_{new}}{N_{old}}\right)^{2}$ | Affinity law — head | m | | 12 | $P_{new} = P_{old} \cdot \left(\frac{N_{new}}{N_{old}}\right)^{3}$ | Affinity law — power | W | > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL LaTeX Escape Rule:** When writing absolute values inside a table, use `\vert x \vert` (not `|x|`), otherwise the pipe character will break the markdown table rendering. ## 2.3 Engineering Utility of System Curves System curves are not just an academic exercise. In real KTU-tied industries (Kerala Fertilisers, BPCL Kochi, HLL, NTPC Kayamkulam), they are used for: - **Pump sizing and selection** during plant design. - **Energy audits** — if the static head is small and friction dominates, energy is wasted on flow; throttling becomes essential. - **Diagnosing underperformance** — if the field-measured flow is less than the design operating point, the system curve has likely drifted (valve closed, pipe scaled up, partial blockage). - **Optimising control valves** — choosing between a hand-throttled valve and a variable-speed drive (VSD) is essentially a system-curve manipulation decision. - **Cavitation prediction (NPSH analysis)** — system curves help determine the available NPSH at the pump suction, ensuring cavitation-free operation. ## 2.4 The Operating Point — Where It All Comes Together The **operating point** is the unique combination $(Q^*, H^*)$ where: $$H_{pump}(Q^*) = H_{sys}(Q^*)$$ Visually, it is the **intersection** of the pump curve (decreasing parabola) and the system curve (increasing parabola). In a well-designed system, this point should lie in the **Best Efficiency Point (BEP)** region of the pump curve — typically within 80–110% of the design flow. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the General System Curve We start from **first principles** — an energy balance between the suction reservoir (point 1) and the discharge reservoir (point 2), using the **Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE)**: $$\frac{P_{1}}{\rho g} + \frac{v_{1}^{2}}{2g} + z_{1} + H_{pump} = \frac{P_{2}}{\rho g} + \frac{v_{2}^{2}}{2g} + z_{2} + h_{L}$$ where $h_{L}$ is the total head loss. Rearranging to solve for the head the **pump must supply**: $$H_{pump} = \left(z_{2} - z_{1}\right) + \frac{P_{2} - P_{1}}{\rho g} + \frac{v_{2}^{2} - v_{1}^{2}}{2g} + h_{L}$$ Recognising each term on the right-hand side as a system demand: $$\underbrace{H_{pump}}_{\text{supply}} = \underbrace{\Delta z}_{H_{static}} + \underbrace{H_{pressure}}_{P_{diff}/\rho g} + \underbrace{\Delta H_{velocity}}_{kinetic\ change} + \underbrace{h_{L}}_{H_{friction} + H_{minor}}$$ Since both reservoirs are large (tank surfaces), $v_1 \approx v_2 \approx 0$, eliminating the velocity term. And in the KTU standard problem, both tanks are open, so $P_1 = P_2 = P_{atm}$, eliminating the pressure term. Thus: $$H_{pump} = \Delta z + h_{L}$$ Substituting the Darcy–Weisbach form for $h_{L}$ (with equivalent length absorbing minor losses): $$h_{L} = f \cdot \frac{L_{total}}{D} \cdot \frac{v^{2}}{2g}$$ Substituting $v = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^{2}}$: $$h_{L} = f \cdot \frac{L_{total}}{D} \cdot \frac{1}{2g} \cdot \left(\frac{4Q}{\pi D^{2}}\right)^{2}$$ Simplifying the square of the velocity term: $$h_{L} = f \cdot \frac{L_{total}}{D} \cdot \frac{1}{2g} \cdot \frac{16 Q^{2}}{\pi^{2} D^{4}} = \frac{8 f L_{total}}{\pi^{2} g D^{5}} \cdot Q^{2}$$ Defining the **system resistance coefficient**: $$K \equiv \frac{8 f L_{total}}{\pi^{2} g D^{5}}$$ we obtain the **canonical system curve equation**: $$H_{sys} = H_{static} + K \cdot Q^{2}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Note:** This derivation is the **exact form** examiners expect. Showing the explicit substitution of $v$ in terms of $Q$ is what earns full marks. ## 3.2 Derivation of the Operating Point Given a pump curve (often a quadratic fit): $$H_{pump} = H_{0} - a Q^{2}$$ (where $H_{0}$ is the **shut-off head** at $Q=0$ and $a$ is the pump's own resistance coefficient), and a system curve: $$H_{sys} = H_{static} + K Q^{2}$$ At the operating point, $H_{pump} = H_{sys}$: $$H_{0} - a Q^{*2} = H_{static} + K Q^{*2}$$ Collecting $Q^{*2}$ on one side: $$H_{0} - H_{static} = (K + a) Q^{*2}$$ Solving for $Q^{*}$: $$Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{H_{0} - H_{static}}{K + a}}$$ The corresponding head: $$H^{*} = H_{static} + K \cdot Q^{*2}$$ ## 3.3 Derivation of the Affinity Laws (Pump Speed Change) Affinity laws relate the performance of a geometrically similar pump at two different speeds. They are derived from **dimensional analysis / similarity of flow fields**. **Flow affinity law:** Flow rate is proportional to the product of blade-tip area and tip speed: $$\frac{Q_{new}}{Q_{old}} = \frac{N_{new}}{N_{old}} \cdot \frac{D_{new}^{3}}{D_{old}^{3}}$$ For the same impeller diameter $D$: $$Q \propto N \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q_{new} = Q_{old} \cdot \frac{N_{new}}{N_{old}}$$ **Head affinity law:** Head is proportional to the square of tip speed: $$\frac{H_{new}}{H_{old}} = \left(\frac{N_{new}}{N_{old}}\right)^{2} \cdot \left(\frac{D_{new}}{D_{old}}\right)^{2}$$ For the same impeller: $$H \propto N^{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad H_{new} = H_{old} \cdot \left(\frac{N_{new}}{N_{old}}\right)^{2}$$ **Power affinity law:** Power is proportional to the cube of tip speed: $$P \propto N^{3} \quad \Rightarrow \quad P_{new} = P_{old} \cdot \left(\frac{N_{new}}{N_{old}}\right)^{3}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Energy-saving insight:** Reducing pump speed by 10% cuts power consumption by **27.1%** (since $1 - 0.9^{3} = 0.271$). This is why VSDs are preferred over throttling valves for flow control in modern process plants. ## 3.4 Worked Numerical — KTU Style **Problem:** Water at 30 °C is pumped from a lower tank to an upper tank. The vertical separation is **12 m**. The pipe is commercial steel, **150 m long**, internal diameter **50 mm**, with **Darcy friction factor f = 0.025**. There are **four standard elbows** ($K = 0.9$ each) and **one fully open gate valve** ($K = 0.15$). The pump curve is approximated as $H_{pump} = 35 - 0.0002 Q^{2}$ with $Q$ in m³/h. Find the **operating point**. **Step 1 — Convert to SI units.** Use $Q$ in m³/s, but for clarity, let's keep $Q$ in m³/h and use the consistency check. Let's set $Q$ in L/s first: 1 m³/h = 1/3.6 L/s. For consistency, express the system curve in the form $H_{sys} = H_{static} + K \cdot q^{2}$ where $q$ is in **L/s**. **Step 2 — Compute the equivalent length for fittings:** $$L_{eq,\ elbow} = \frac{0.9 \cdot 0.050}{0.025} = 1.8 \text{ m each}$$ Total for 4 elbows: $4 \times 1.8 = 7.2$ m. $$L_{eq,\ valve} = \frac{0.15 \cdot 0.050}{0.025} = 0.30 \text{ m}$$ $$L_{total} = 150 + 7.2 + 0.30 = 157.5 \text{ m}$$ **Step 3 — Compute system resistance coefficient $K$ in SI units (using $Q$ in m³/s):** $$K = \frac{8 \cdot 0.025 \cdot 157.5}{\pi^{2} \cdot 9.81 \cdot (0.050)^{5}}$$ Numerator: $8 \times 0.025 \times 157.5 = 31.5$ Denominator step-by-step: $$\pi^{2} \approx 9.8696$$ $$9.8696 \times 9.81 \approx 96.83$$ $$(0.050)^{5} = 0.05^{5} = 3.125 \times 10^{-7}$$ $$96.83 \times 3.125 \times 10^{-7} \approx 3.026 \times 10^{-5}$$ $$K = \frac{31.5}{3.026 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 1.041 \times 10^{6} \text{ m/(m³/s)}^{2}$$ So the system curve in SI units is: $$H_{sys} = 12 + 1.041 \times 10^{6} \cdot Q^{2} \quad (Q \text{ in m}^3/\text{s})$$ **Step 4 — Convert the pump curve to SI units.** The pump curve was given as $H_{pump} = 35 - 0.0002 Q^{2}$ with $Q$ in m³/h. Convert coefficient: $Q_{m3/s} = Q_{m3/h} / 3600$, so $Q_{m3/h}^{2} = (3600 \cdot Q_{m3/s})^{2} = 12.96 \times 10^{6} \cdot Q_{m3/s}^{2}$. Pump curve in SI: $$H_{pump} = 35 - 0.0002 \times 12.96 \times 10^{6} \cdot Q^{2} = 35 - 2592 \cdot Q^{2}$$ **Step 5 — Equate pump and system curves:** $$35 - 2592 \cdot Q^{*2} = 12 + 1.041 \times 10^{6} \cdot Q^{*2}$$ $$23 = (1.041 \times 10^{6} + 2592) \cdot Q^{*2}$$ $$Q^{*2} = \frac{23}{1.0436 \times 10^{6}} \approx 2.204 \times 10^{-5}$$ $$Q^{*} \approx 4.695 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 4.695 \text{ L/s} \approx 16.9 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$$ **Step 6 — Operating head:** $$H^{*} = 12 + 1.041 \times 10^{6} \times 2.204 \times 10^{-5} = 12 + 22.95 \approx 34.95 \text{ m}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sanity check:** At $Q=0$, the pump produces 35 m, just 3 m above the static head of 12 m. The remaining 23 m goes to friction. The head loss at operating point is 22.95 m, which matches the small spare head — this is realistic for a moderately efficient system. # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Pump–System Pair — Concept Map This diagram shows the conceptual relationship between the two curves and the operating point. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Process Demand]:::demand --> B[System Curve
Increasing Parabola] A1[Pump Capability]:::pump --> B1[Pump Curve
Decreasing Parabola] B --> C[Graphical Plot
Head vs Flow] B1 --> C C --> D{Operating Point} D --> E[Flow Rate Q*] D --> F[Head H*] D --> G[Efficiency Check] G --> H{Near BEP?} H -- Yes --> I[Healthy Operation] H -- No --> J[Control Action Needed] J --> K[Throttle Valve] J --> L[Change Pump Speed] J --> M[Trim Impeller] classDef demand fill:#FFB6B6,stroke:#B22222,color:#000 classDef pump fill:#B6D7FF,stroke:#1E5A99,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 The Operating Point — Curve Intersection This is the **canonical** diagram examiners expect. The system curve starts at $H_{static}$ on the y-axis; the pump curve starts at $H_{shutoff}$ on the y-axis; they intersect at the operating point. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Axes [H vs Q Plot] direction TB O[Origin O at 0,0]:::origin HA[Q on X axis
Flow Rate]:::axis VA[Head H on Y axis]:::axis end SC[System Curve
H_sys = H_static + K Q squared
Starts at H_static]:::sys PC[Pump Curve
H_pump = H_shutoff - a Q squared
Starts at H_shutoff]:::pump OP((Operating Point
Q star and H star)):::op SC --- OP PC --- OP HA --- SC HA --- PC VA --- SC VA --- PC classDef origin fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000000,color:#000 classDef axis fill:#EFEFEF,stroke:#555555,color:#000 classDef sys fill:#FFD9B3,stroke:#CC6600,color:#000 classDef pump fill:#B3D9FF,stroke:#0055AA,color:#000 classDef op fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Effect of Valve Throttling on the System Curve When a control valve is partially closed, its $K$-value increases, **rotating the system curve upward** about the point $(0, H_{static})$ and shifting the operating point **leftward** (lower flow). ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Original System Curve
K original]:::sys1 B[Throttled System Curve
K larger, K equal to 2K original]:::sys2 C[Pump Curve unchanged]:::pump OP1((Original Operating Point
Higher Flow)):::op1 OP2((New Operating Point
Lower Flow)):::op2 A --- OP1 B --- OP2 C --- OP1 C --- OP2 A -- "Valve Closing Increases K" --> B OP1 -- "Flow Decreases" --> OP2 classDef sys1 fill:#FFD9B3,stroke:#CC6600,color:#000 classDef sys2 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#B22222,color:#000 classDef pump fill:#B3D9FF,stroke:#0055AA,color:#000 classDef op1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000 classDef op2 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000 ``` ## 4.4 Effect of Pump Speed Change on the Operating Point According to the **Affinity Laws**, reducing the pump speed $N$ shifts the pump curve **down and inward**, reducing both flow and head. Unlike throttling, this method **saves power** cubically. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Original Pump Curve
Speed N1]:::pump1 B[Reduced Speed Curve
Speed N2 less than N1]:::pump2 C[System Curve unchanged]:::sys OP1((Original OP
Q1 H1)):::op1 OP2((New OP
Q2 less than Q1
H2 less than H1]):::op2 A --- OP1 B --- OP2 C --- OP1 C --- OP2 A -- "Speed Reduction Affinity Laws" --> B OP1 -- "Q reduces linearly with N
H reduces as N squared
P reduces as N cubed" --> OP2 classDef pump1 fill:#B3D9FF,stroke:#0055AA,color:#000 classDef pump2 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#003F7F,color:#000 classDef sys fill:#FFD9B3,stroke:#CC6600,color:#000 classDef op1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000 classDef op2 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000 ``` ## 4.5 Block Diagram — How the System Curve Fits Into a Control Loop ```mermaid flowchart TD SP[Setpoint Desired Flow]:::ctrl --> CTRL[Controller
PID]:::ctrl CTRL --> CV[Control Valve]:::actuator CV --> PROC[Pumping System
Pipe network and fluid]:::process PROC --> FT[Flow Transmitter]:::sensor FT --> CTRL PROC -. "System Curve
H_sys = H_static + K Q squared" .- PROC classDef ctrl fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082,color:#000 classDef actuator fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000 classDef process fill:#FFD9B3,stroke:#CC6600,color:#000 classDef sensor fill:#B3D9FF,stroke:#0055AA,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the block diagram:** The controller reads the actual flow (from the transmitter), compares it to the setpoint, and adjusts the control valve opening. Each new valve setting corresponds to a **different system curve**, and therefore a **different operating point** on the same pump curve. # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Define a "System Curve" in the context of fluid pumping. What is its general mathematical form for turbulent flow, and what does each term represent?** **Model Answer:** A **System Curve** is a plot of the total head that a pump must supply to a fluid (in metres of fluid) versus the volumetric flow rate $Q$ (in m³/s) for a given piping network. It characterises the *energy demand* of the process. For fully turbulent flow, the general form is: $$H_{sys} = H_{static} + K \cdot Q^{2}$$ where: - $H_{static}$ is the elevation/pressure difference between source and destination — a constant, flow-independent term. - $K$ is the system resistance coefficient (depends on pipe length, diameter, roughness, fittings). - $Q^{2}$ reflects the quadratic dependence of friction head on velocity for turbulent flow. > [!NOTE] > **Marking tip:** Mentioning *both* the physical meaning and the quadratic dependence of the friction term secures full marks. --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **What is an "Operating Point" of a pump? How is it obtained graphically?** **Model Answer:** The **Operating Point** is the unique combination of flow rate $Q^*$ and head $H^*$ at which the pump's actual delivery exactly matches the system's demand — i.e., where the **pump curve** and the **system curve** intersect. Graphically, it is obtained by plotting both curves on the same $H$–vs–$Q$ axes and finding their point of intersection: $$H_{pump}(Q^*) = H_{sys}(Q^*)$$ For the typical KTU problem: $$H_{0} - a Q^{*2} = H_{static} + K Q^{*2}$$ $$\Rightarrow Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{H_{0} - H_{static}}{K + a}}$$ The ideal operating point lies close to the **Best Efficiency Point (BEP)** of the pump for energy-efficient, vibration-free operation. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Module Internal Choice Rule:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**. Skipping a sub-part is the most common cause of losing 7 marks. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** With the help of the Steady Flow Energy Equation, **derive the general form of the system curve** for a piping network carrying an incompressible fluid between two open reservoirs. Clearly define every term that appears. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A pump with the characteristic equation $H_{pump} = 40 - 0.30 Q^{2}$ (with $H$ in m and $Q$ in L/s) is used to deliver water through a piping system. The static head is **8 m** and the system resistance coefficient is **$K = 0.15$ m per L/s squared**. Determine: (i) The operating flow rate $Q^*$. (ii) The operating head $H^*$. (iii) The friction head loss at the operating point. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution for (a):** Apply the **Steady Flow Energy Equation (SFEE)** between the suction reservoir surface (1) and the discharge reservoir surface (2): $$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} + z_1 + H_{pump} = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_L$$ For two large open reservoirs: - $P_1 = P_2 = P_{atm}$ → pressure terms cancel. - $v_1 \approx v_2 \approx 0$ → kinetic terms vanish. Therefore: $$H_{pump} = (z_2 - z_1) + h_L = H_{static} + h_L$$ The total head loss $h_L$ includes pipe friction and minor losses: $$h_L = f \cdot \frac{L_{total}}{D} \cdot \frac{v^2}{2g} + \sum K_i \cdot \frac{v^2}{2g}$$ Substituting $v = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$ and absorbing minor losses into equivalent length: $$h_L = \frac{8 f L_{total}}{\pi^2 g D^5} \cdot Q^2 = K \cdot Q^2$$ where $K = \dfrac{8 f L_{total}}{\pi^2 g D^5}$. Therefore the system curve is: $$\boxed{H_{sys} = H_{static} + K \cdot Q^2}$$ **Model Solution for (b):** **(i) Operating flow rate:** Equate $H_{pump}$ and $H_{sys}$: $$40 - 0.30 Q^{*2} = 8 + 0.15 Q^{*2}$$ $$32 = 0.45 Q^{*2}$$ $$Q^{*2} = \frac{32}{0.45} = 71.11$$ $$Q^{*} = \sqrt{71.11} \approx 8.43 \text{ L/s}$$ **(ii) Operating head:** Substituting back into the system curve: $$H^* = 8 + 0.15 \times 71.11 = 8 + 10.67 = 18.67 \text{ m}$$ (Or, using the pump curve: $H^* = 40 - 0.30 \times 71.11 = 40 - 21.33 = 18.67$ m ✓) **(iii) Friction head loss:** This is the $K Q^2$ term alone: $$h_L^* = 0.15 \times 71.11 = 10.67 \text{ m}$$ So the 8 m static head + 10.67 m friction head = 18.67 m total head delivered. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **(a)** Explain the **Affinity Laws** for centrifugal pumps. A pump operating at 1450 rpm delivers 30 L/s against a head of 25 m and consumes 12 kW. If the speed is changed to 1750 rpm, calculate the new flow, head, and power. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the **effect of throttling a control valve on the system curve and the operating point**. Why is speed control more energy-efficient than throttling for flow regulation? Support with equations. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution for (a):** The **Affinity Laws** are three equations relating pump performance at two speeds, assuming geometric similarity and the same impeller diameter: $$Q \propto N, \quad H \propto N^2, \quad P \propto N^3$$ **Speed ratio:** $$\frac{N_{new}}{N_{old}} = \frac{1750}{1450} = 1.2069$$ **New flow:** $$Q_{new} = 30 \times 1.2069 \approx 36.21 \text{ L/s}$$ **New head:** $$H_{new} = 25 \times (1.2069)^2 = 25 \times 1.4566 \approx 36.42 \text{ m}$$ **New power:** $$P_{new} = 12 \times (1.2069)^3 = 12 \times 1.7580 \approx 21.10 \text{ kW}$$ > [!NOTE] > Note that a 20.7% speed increase yields a 20.7% flow increase, 45.7% head increase, and 75.8% power increase — the cubic dependence is dramatic. **Model Solution for (b):** **Effect of throttling on the system curve:** When a control valve is partially closed, the local loss coefficient $K_{valve}$ increases. Since the total $K$ of the system is the sum of all component $K$'s, increasing the valve resistance **steepens the system curve**, rotating it upward about the static-head intercept $(0, H_{static})$ on the $H$–$Q$ plot. Graphically, this moves the operating point **leftward and upward** along the (fixed) pump curve — flow decreases, but head delivered by the pump actually increases slightly. **Energy efficiency comparison:** For throttling, the additional friction loss appears as **wasted heat** in the fluid and pipe walls. The pump continues to operate near its original best-efficiency point but the *useful* flow is reduced by dissipation. For speed control, by contrast, the **pump curve itself** shifts to a new (lower) position via the affinity laws. Both flow and head decrease at the new operating point, and the **pump's power draw drops cubically**: $$\frac{P_{new}}{P_{old}} = \left(\frac{N_{new}}{N_{old}}\right)^{3}$$ **Quantitative example:** A 20% speed reduction cuts power by: $$1 - (0.8)^3 = 1 - 0.512 = 0.488 = 48.8\%$$ Whereas a 20% flow reduction by throttling typically saves only **5–15%** of the power, because the pump keeps running at near-full design speed. This is why modern KTU-affiliated industries (e.g., BPCL Kochi Refinery) prefer **Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs)** over throttling valves for flow control on large pumps. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing pump curve and system curve:** The pump curve *decreases* with $Q$, the system curve *increases* with $Q$. Reversing this will fail the question. > 2. **Forgetting the $H_{static}$ intercept:** The system curve does not pass through the origin. Drawing it from $(0,0)$ is a 2-mark penalty. > 3. **Mixing units:** A $K$ computed with $Q$ in m³/s cannot be used with $Q$ in m³/h. Always state and use consistent units. > 4. **Ignoring the equivalent length method:** Many KTU problems require summing $L_{eq}$ for fittings before computing $K$. Skipping this yields a wrong answer. > 5. **Forgetting the cubic law of power:** Students often compute $P \propto N^2$ instead of $P \propto N^3$ in affinity law problems. This is the single most common error in Module 4 questions. > 6. **Omitting the energy-efficiency conclusion:** In a "compare throttling vs. speed control" question, *not* stating "speed control saves power cubically" loses you the 2-mark conclusion. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — System Curves** - [x] **System Curve:** $H_{sys} = H_{static} + K \cdot Q^{2}$ — a **parabola starting at $H_{static}$**, increasing with $Q$. - [x] **Pump Curve:** $H_{pump} = H_{0} - a \cdot Q^{2}$ — a **decreasing parabola** from the shut-off head $H_0$. - [x] **Operating Point:** Algebraic intersection — solve $H_{pump} = H_{sys}$ simultaneously. - [x] **Five head components:** static, friction, minor, pressure, velocity — know the formula and the $Q$-dependence of each. - [x] **Darcy–Weisbach:** $h_{f} = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{v^{2}}{2g}$ — applied to straight pipes. - [x] **Equivalent length method:** $L_{eq} = \dfrac{K_i \cdot D}{f}$ — used to convert fittings into equivalent pipe length. - [x] **System resistance coefficient:** $K = \dfrac{8 f L_{total}}{\pi^{2} g D^{5}}$ — units are m per (m³/s)². - [x] **Affinity Laws (same impeller):** $Q \propto N$, $H \propto N^{2}$, $P \propto N^{3}$ — memorise the cubes. - [x] **Throttling** rotates system curve upward, lowers flow, wastes power as heat. - [x] **Speed control** shifts the pump curve itself, saving power **cubically** — preferred for energy efficiency. - [x] **Static head** is **constant** — independent of $Q$ — and forms the y-intercept of the system curve. - [x] **Friction head** is **proportional to $Q^{2}$** in fully turbulent flow. - [x] **Operating point must lie near the Best Efficiency Point (BEP)** for long pump life and minimum energy cost. - [x] **NPSH consideration:** System curve helps compute available NPSH at the pump suction to avoid cavitation. - [x] **Always** state and maintain **consistent SI units** in numerical problems. - [x] **Always** show full substitution steps in derivations — KTU examiners award method marks generously.

topics.title: Constant Static Head
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT743 - INDUSTRIAL PROCESS CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Constant Static Head # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Constant Static Head** is a specialized operating condition in process control systems, particularly relevant to the analysis of **control valve characteristics**. It describes a scenario in which the **pressure drop across the control valve remains essentially constant** (i.e., does not vary appreciably with changes in flow rate) regardless of the position of the valve stem or the magnitude of fluid flow through the system. Mathematically, the constant static head condition is expressed as: $$\Delta P_v = \text{constant} \quad \forall \, Q \in [Q_{min}, Q_{max}]$$ Where: - $\Delta P_v$ = Pressure drop across the control valve - $Q$ = Volumetric flow rate through the system > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Under this condition, the **Installed Valve Characteristic** becomes **identical** to the **Inherent Valve Characteristic**. This is a critical observation frequently tested in KTU examinations as it forms the foundation of control valve sizing and selection. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy Imagine a **water tap connected directly to a large overhead water tank** (with negligible pipe friction loss). As you open or close the tap, the pressure at the tap inlet remains practically the same because the water level in the tank is much higher than the tap, and friction losses in the pipe are minimal compared to the tank head. - **The "Tank Head"** represents the dominant, unchanging pressure source (like a centrifugal pump delivering nearly constant head). - **The "Tap"** represents the control valve. - **Minimal pipe friction** represents a system where the static head of the piping system does not consume a significant portion of the available pressure. In such a setup, no matter how much water flows, the **pressure drop across the tap remains constant**. This is exactly what we mean by the **Constant Static Head** condition in process control. ## 1.3 Context in Process Control In the KTU 2024 scheme (PEICT743), this topic falls under **Module 4: Control Valves**, specifically under the broader umbrella of **Inherent and Installed Characteristics of Control Valves**. The constant static head condition represents the **idealized case** where: > [!NOTE] > The system's static head (friction losses, elevation changes, and equipment head losses) is **negligible or invariant** compared to the total pump head available. Consequently, the entire pump head manifests as the pressure drop across the control valve. This condition is contrasted with **Variable Static Head** conditions (where ΔPv changes significantly with flow) and **Partial Constant Static Head** conditions. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown To fully grasp **Constant Static Head**, we must understand the interplay between a centrifugal pump, the piping system, and the control valve. The energy balance of a typical flow system is governed by: $$H_p = H_s + \Delta P_v$$ Where: - $H_p$ = Total head delivered by the pump (in head units, e.g., meters of fluid) - $H_s$ = Static head consumed by the piping system (friction + elevation + fittings) - $\Delta P_v$ = Pressure drop across the control valve ### Step-by-Step Logic: - **Step 1: Understanding Pump Behavior** A centrifugal pump delivers a head $H_p$ that varies with flow $Q$. However, if the system resistance is small, the variation in $H_p$ with $Q$ is small, and $H_p \approx \text{constant}$. - **Step 2: Understanding System Static Head** The system static head $H_s$ depends on flow (because friction loss $h_f \propto Q^2$). When the system is short, with large diameter pipes and minimal fittings, $H_s$ remains nearly constant. - **Step 3: Constant Static Head Emerges** When both $H_p$ and $H_s$ are essentially independent of flow, then: $$\Delta P_v = H_p - H_s \approx \text{constant}$$ - **Step 4: Inherent vs Installed Characteristic** - **Inherent Characteristic**: The relationship $Q$ vs. stem position $y$ at **constant** $\Delta P_v$. - **Installed Characteristic**: The relationship $Q$ vs. stem position $y$ under actual system conditions where $\Delta P_v$ may vary. - Under **Constant Static Head**, since $\Delta P_v$ is truly constant, the **installed characteristic equals the inherent characteristic**. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Parameter / Equation** | **Formula** | **Description** | **Units / Notes** | |--------------------------|-------------|-----------------|-------------------| | Energy Balance | $H_p = H_s + \Delta P_v$ | Pump head = system head + valve drop | meters (m) of fluid | | Valve Flow Equation (Liquid) | $Q = C_v \cdot N \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta P_v}{\rho}}$ | Basic flow through valve | m³/hr or GPM | | Friction Loss (Darcy) | $h_f = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{v^2}{2g}$ | Head loss due to pipe friction | meters | | Inherent Characteristic (Linear) | $Q = Q_{max} \cdot y$ | Linear valve: flow proportional to stem position | Dimensionless $y \in [0,1]$ | | Inherent Characteristic (Equal Percentage) | $Q = Q_{max} \cdot R^{(y-1)}$ | Equal percentage valve | $R$ = rangeability | | Constant Static Head Condition | $\Delta P_v = \text{constant}$ | Pressure drop across valve is invariant | Pa or bar | | Reynolds Number | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ | Flow regime check | Dimensionless | | Pump Affinity Law | $\dfrac{Q_1}{Q_2} = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2}$ | For constant impeller diameter | RPM dependent | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Exam Note:** When $\Delta P_v$ is constant, the relationship $Q \propto \sqrt{\Delta P_v}$ collapses to $Q \propto \sqrt{\text{constant}}$, meaning flow varies solely due to the valve's inherent flow coefficient $C_v(y)$. This is the **theoretical ideal** for control valve performance. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility The constant static head condition is encountered in: - **Chemical injection systems** with high-pressure positive displacement pumps. - **Boiler feedwater control** where pump pressure is regulated and is much larger than pipe losses. - **Hydraulic systems** in industrial presses and machine tools. - **Laboratory flow loops** with oversized piping and high-pressure sources. Engineers exploit this condition because control valves operate with their **true inherent characteristics** (linear, equal percentage, etc.), enabling **predictable and linear process control** without complicated installed characteristic corrections. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Installed Characteristic under Constant Static Head ### Starting Point: Generalized Valve Flow Equation The general flow equation for an incompressible fluid through a control valve is given by: $$Q = C_v(y) \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta P_v}{\rho \cdot g}}$$ Where $C_v(y)$ is the valve flow coefficient that depends on the stem position $y$ (dimensionless, ranging from 0 to 1). ### Step 1: Express the Pressure Drop Available For a system where the pump delivers a total head $H_p$ and the static system head is $H_s(Q)$ (which depends on flow due to friction), the pressure drop across the valve is: $$\Delta P_v = H_p - H_s(Q)$$ ### Step 2: Substitute into Flow Equation $$Q = C_v(y) \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{H_p - H_s(Q)}{\rho \cdot g}}$$ ### Step 3: Apply the Constant Static Head Condition Under **constant static head**, we assume that $H_s(Q)$ is negligible compared to $H_p$ AND does not vary appreciably with $Q$. Mathematically: $$H_s(Q) \approx H_{s,0} \approx \text{constant}$$ $$H_p - H_s(Q) \approx H_p - H_{s,0} = \Delta P_{v,0} = \text{constant}$$ ### Step 4: Simplification Substituting the constant pressure drop back into the flow equation: $$Q = C_v(y) \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta P_{v,0}}{\rho \cdot g}}$$ Since $\sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta P_{v,0}}{\rho \cdot g}}$ is a constant (let's call it $K$), we have: $$Q = K \cdot C_v(y)$$ This shows that **flow $Q$ is directly proportional to the valve flow coefficient $C_v(y)$**, which itself is a function of stem position $y$. ### Step 5: Inherent Characteristic Definition By definition, the **inherent characteristic** is the relationship between $Q$ and $y$ when $\Delta P_v$ is held constant. Therefore: $$\boxed{Q_{\text{installed}}(y) = Q_{\text{inherent}}(y) \quad \text{when} \quad \Delta P_v = \text{constant}}$$ ### Step 6: Linear Valve Example For a **linear inherent characteristic** valve: $$C_v(y) = C_{v,\max} \cdot y$$ Therefore: $$Q = K \cdot C_{v,\max} \cdot y = Q_{max} \cdot y$$ The installed characteristic is a straight line passing through the origin with slope $Q_{max}$. ### Step 7: Equal Percentage Valve Example For an **equal percentage inherent characteristic** valve: $$C_v(y) = C_{v,\max} \cdot R^{(y-1)}$$ Where $R$ is the rangeability (typically 50). Then: $$Q = K \cdot C_{v,\max} \cdot R^{(y-1)} = Q_{max} \cdot R^{(y-1)}$$ Again, the installed characteristic exactly mirrors the inherent logarithmic characteristic. ## 3.2 Comparison Matrix: Constant vs Variable Static Head | **Aspect** | **Constant Static Head** | **Variable Static Head** | |------------|--------------------------|--------------------------| | Pressure Drop $\Delta P_v$ | Invariant with $Q$ | Decreases as $Q$ increases | | Installed = Inherent? | **Yes** | No | | System Friction | Negligible | Significant ($h_f \propto Q^2$) | | Typical Pump Type | Positive displacement, or high-head centrifugal | Centrifugal with flat curve | | Control Loop Performance | Predictable, linear | Non-linear, requires characterization | | Valve Selection | Direct match to inherent characteristic | Must compensate for distortion | | Common in | Hydraulic systems, lab setups | Long pipelines, HVAC, process plants | ## 3.3 Mathematical Verification: Worked Numerical Example **Problem:** A control valve with linear inherent characteristic has $C_{v,max} = 100$ gpm/psi$^{0.5}$. The pump provides a constant head equivalent to $\Delta P_v = 25$ psi. The fluid specific gravity is 1.0. Calculate the flow at stem positions $y = 0.2, 0.5, 0.8, 1.0$. ### Solution: **Step 1:** Write the flow equation for a liquid (using US customary units): $$Q = C_v \cdot \sqrt{\Delta P_v / G}$$ Where $G$ is specific gravity. **Step 2:** Substitute known values: $$Q = C_v \cdot \sqrt{25 / 1.0} = C_v \cdot 5$$ **Step 3:** Compute $C_v(y)$ for each stem position using linear characteristic $C_v(y) = 100 \cdot y$: $$\begin{aligned} y = 0.2 &\Rightarrow C_v = 20 \Rightarrow Q = 20 \times 5 = 100 \text{ gpm} \\ y = 0.5 &\Rightarrow C_v = 50 \Rightarrow Q = 50 \times 5 = 250 \text{ gpm} \\ y = 0.8 &\Rightarrow C_v = 80 \Rightarrow Q = 80 \times 5 = 400 \text{ gpm} \\ y = 1.0 &\Rightarrow C_v = 100 \Rightarrow Q = 100 \times 5 = 500 \text{ gpm} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4:** Verify the proportionality $Q / Q_{max} = y$: $$\begin{aligned} 0.2 = 100/500 \quad &\checkmark \\ 0.5 = 250/500 \quad &\checkmark \\ 0.8 = 400/500 \quad &\checkmark \\ 1.0 = 500/500 \quad &\checkmark \end{aligned}$$ The linearity is preserved, confirming the **installed characteristic equals the inherent characteristic** under constant static head. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 System-Level Block Diagram: Constant Static Head Configuration ```mermaid flowchart LR P[Pump - Constant Head Source] -->|Hp constant| PIPE1[Short Large Diameter Pipe] PIPE1 -->|Minimal Hs| VALVE[Control Valve - dPv Constant] VALVE -->|Regulated Flow Q| PROCESS[Process / Load] PROCESS -->|Return Line| PIPE2[Return Piping] PIPE2 --> P VALVE -.Position Signal y.-> CTRL[Controller / DCS] CTRL -.Actuator Command.-> VALVE CTRL <-.Process Variable PV.-> PROCESS ``` > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation:** The pump delivers a constant head $H_p$. The piping system consumes negligible head $H_s$. Hence, virtually all of $H_p$ appears as a constant pressure drop across the control valve. The controller adjusts stem position $y$ to achieve the desired flow. ## 4.2 Functional Architecture Flow: Energy Distribution ```mermaid flowchart TD INPUT[Total Pump Head Hp] --> SPLIT{Head Distribution} SPLIT -->|Negligible fraction| SYS[Static System Head Hs] SPLIT -->|Dominant fraction| VALVE[Valve Pressure Drop dPv] VALVE -->|Constant| QFLOW[Regulated Flow Q] SYS -->|Near zero| CONST[Constant Static Head Condition] CONST -->|Result| MATCH[Installed Characteristic equals Inherent Characteristic] QFLOW --> MATCH MATCH --> OUT[Predictable Linear Control Response] ``` ## 4.3 Comparative Block Diagram: Constant vs Variable Static Head ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph CONSTANT[CONSTANT STATIC HEAD] C1[Inherent Char: Linear] --> C2[Installed Char: Linear] C2 --> C3[Predictable Control] end subgraph VARIABLE[VARIABLE STATIC HEAD] V1[Inherent Char: Linear] --> V2[Installed Char: Quick Opening or Distorted] V2 --> V3[Nonlinear Control - Needs Compensation] end ``` ## 4.4 Conceptual Mapping: Pressure vs Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Flow Q increases] --> B{System Type} B -->|Constant Static Head| C[Friction Loss negligible
dPv stays constant] B -->|Variable Static Head| D[Friction Loss grows with Q squared
dPv decreases] C --> E[Installed equals Inherent] D --> F[Installed deviates from Inherent] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (2 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Short Answer **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **Q: Define the term "Constant Static Head" in the context of control valve performance.** **Model Answer:** Constant static head refers to an operating condition in a fluid flow system where the pressure drop across the control valve remains essentially **constant** irrespective of the flow rate through the valve. This occurs when the static head consumed by the piping system (due to friction, elevation, and fittings) is either negligible or invariant compared to the total head delivered by the pump. Under this condition, the installed valve characteristic is identical to the inherent valve characteristic. ### Question 2: Short Answer **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **Q: State any two conditions under which a control valve operates under constant static head.** **Model Answer:** 1. When the system piping is **short** with **large diameter**, leading to negligible friction losses compared to the pump head. 2. When the pump is of the **positive displacement type** delivering a constant head regardless of flow variations. ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each - KTU Internal Choice Pattern) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 / Model Paper]** **(a)** Explain the concept of **inherent and installed characteristics** of a control valve with neat sketches. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Derive the mathematical relationship showing that under **constant static head**, the installed characteristic is identical to the inherent characteristic. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution: **(a) Inherent and Installed Characteristics:** **Inherent Characteristic:** The inherent characteristic of a control valve is the relationship between the **flow rate $Q$** through the valve and the **stem position $y$** (or fractional lift) when the **pressure drop across the valve is held constant**. Common inherent characteristics include: - **Quick Opening**: Large flow at small lifts; $Q$ rises rapidly initially. - **Linear**: Flow is directly proportional to stem position; $Q = Q_{max} \cdot y$. - **Equal Percentage**: Equal increments of stem position produce equal percentage changes in flow; $Q = Q_{max} \cdot R^{(y-1)}$. ``` Quick Opening: Q rises steeply then plateaus Linear: Q = straight line through origin Equal %: Q = exponential curve (concave up) ``` **Installed Characteristic:** The installed characteristic is the relationship between flow $Q$ and stem position $y$ **under actual operating conditions** where the pressure drop across the valve $\Delta P_v$ varies with flow due to system resistance changes. **Neat Sketch Description:** | Inherent | Installed (Variable Head) | Installed (Constant Head) | |----------|---------------------------|---------------------------| | Linear | Quick opening distortion | Same as inherent (linear) | | Equal % | Slightly less curved | Same as inherent (equal %) | **(b) Mathematical Derivation:** We begin with the generalized valve flow equation for an incompressible fluid: $$Q = C_v(y) \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta P_v}{\rho \cdot g}}$$ The system energy balance gives: $$\Delta P_v = H_p - H_s(Q)$$ Under **constant static head**, $H_s(Q)$ is negligible and constant, so: $$\Delta P_v \approx H_p - H_{s,0} = \text{constant} = \Delta P_{v,0}$$ Therefore: $$Q = C_v(y) \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta P_{v,0}}{\rho \cdot g}} = K \cdot C_v(y)$$ Since $K$ is a constant, $Q$ depends **only** on $C_v(y)$. This is precisely the definition of inherent characteristic. Hence: $$\boxed{Q_{\text{installed}}(y) \equiv Q_{\text{inherent}}(y)}$$ For a **linear inherent characteristic** with $C_v(y) = C_{v,max} \cdot y$: $$Q = K \cdot C_{v,max} \cdot y = Q_{max} \cdot y$$ This confirms a linear installed characteristic identical to the inherent one. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) - Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam - May 2023 / Model Paper]** **(a)** A control valve with linear inherent characteristic is installed in a system where the static head is constant. The valve has $C_{v,max} = 120$ and the constant pressure drop is $\Delta P_v = 36$ psi. The fluid has specific gravity $G = 1.0$. Compute the flow at stem positions $y = 0.25, 0.50, 0.75, 1.0$. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Discuss the practical implications of constant static head on **control loop stability and valve selection**. Compare it with the **variable static head** condition. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution: **(a) Numerical Computation:** **Given:** - Linear inherent characteristic: $C_v(y) = 120 \cdot y$ - $\Delta P_v = 36$ psi (constant) - $G = 1.0$ **Formula:** $$Q = C_v \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta P_v}{G}}$$ **Step 1:** Compute the constant factor: $$\sqrt{\dfrac{36}{1.0}} = 6$$ $$Q = C_v \cdot 6$$ **Step 2:** Calculate $Q$ for each stem position: | Stem Position $y$ | $C_v = 120y$ | $Q = 6 C_v$ (gpm) | |-------------------|--------------|-------------------| | 0.25 | 30 | 180 | | 0.50 | 60 | 360 | | 0.75 | 90 | 540 | | 1.00 | 120 | 720 | **Verification of Linearity:** $$\begin{aligned} Q/Q_{max} &= 180/720 = 0.25 \quad \checkmark \\ Q/Q_{max} &= 360/720 = 0.50 \quad \checkmark \\ Q/Q_{max} &= 540/720 = 0.75 \quad \checkmark \\ Q/Q_{max} &= 720/720 = 1.00 \quad \checkmark \end{aligned}$$ The installed characteristic is a **perfectly linear** function, identical to the inherent characteristic. **(b) Practical Implications:** **Control Loop Stability under Constant Static Head:** - The relationship between controller output and process variable flow is **linear and predictable**. - Controller tuning (PID) is straightforward because the process gain remains constant across the operating range. - No need for **valve characterizers** or **characterized positioners**. **Valve Selection Implications:** - A **linear valve** is chosen when the process gain is constant with flow (e.g., level control). - An **equal percentage valve** is chosen when the process gain varies inversely with flow (e.g., temperature control with heat exchangers). - The selection directly matches the inherent characteristic without compensation. **Comparison with Variable Static Head:** | Aspect | Constant Static Head | Variable Static Head | |--------|----------------------|----------------------| | Linearity | Preserved | Distorted | | Valve Selection | Direct | May require characterized cam | | Process Gain | Constant | Variable | | Tuning Difficulty | Low | High | | Loop Performance | Stable | May oscillate | --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing "Constant Static Head" with "Constant Pump Head":** These are related but distinct concepts. Constant static head refers to the **system head $H_s$** being constant, which makes $\Delta P_v$ constant even if $H_p$ varies slightly. > 2. **Forgetting Units:** In the valve flow equation $Q = C_v \sqrt{\Delta P_v / G}$, students often forget the specific gravity $G$ under the square root, leading to dimensional errors. > 3. **Misapplying the Equal Percentage Formula:** Many students write $Q = Q_{max} \cdot R^y$ instead of the correct $Q = Q_{max} \cdot R^{(y-1)}$. The latter ensures $Q = Q_{max}$ when $y = 1$. > 4. **Skipping the Energy Balance:** A direct derivation must begin with $H_p = H_s + \Delta P_v$. Skipping this loses **2-3 marks** in the derivation step. > 5. **Not Drawing Sketches:** In Part (a) of 14-mark questions, even a hand-drawn rough plot of $Q$ vs $y$ is mandatory for full marks. ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Definition**: Constant static head means $\Delta P_v$ across the valve is **constant** regardless of flow rate. - **Cause**: System head $H_s$ is negligible or invariant; pump head $H_p$ dominates. - **Key Result**: Installed Characteristic = Inherent Characteristic. - **Energy Balance**: $H_p = H_s + \Delta P_v$. Under constant static head, $H_s \approx \text{const}$ and $\Delta P_v \approx \text{const}$. - **Valve Flow Equation**: $Q = C_v(y) \cdot \sqrt{\Delta P_v / G}$. - **Linear Valve**: $C_v(y) = C_{v,max} \cdot y$, gives $Q \propto y$. - **Equal Percentage Valve**: $C_v(y) = C_{v,max} \cdot R^{(y-1)}$, gives exponential $Q$-$y$ curve. - **Real-world examples**: Hydraulic systems, lab flow loops, positive displacement pump circuits. - **KTU favorite question**: Derive installed = inherent under constant static head — must start from energy balance. - **Common trap**: Variable static head causes installed characteristic to deviate (typically toward quick-opening for linear valves). - **Practical benefit**: Predictable, linear control response; simpler PID tuning. - **Selection rule**: Choose inherent characteristic based on process gain behavior — linear for constant gain, equal percentage for variable gain.

topics.title: Friction Pressure Component
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT743 - INDUSTRIAL PROCESS CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Friction Pressure Component # ⚙️ FRICTION PRESSURE COMPONENT IN INDUSTRIAL PROCESS CONTROL ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 📘 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) > **[!NOTE]** > **Friction Pressure Component (ΔPf):** The portion of total system pressure that is irreversibly dissipated as mechanical energy (heat) due to viscous shearing between the moving fluid and the stationary pipe wall, plus the turbulent dissipation caused by fittings, bends, valves, sudden expansions/contractions, and other inline hardware. It is the non-recoverable pressure loss that cannot be converted back into useful mechanical work. In the generalized **Mechanical Energy Balance** for a flowing fluid, the friction pressure loss is the term that accounts for energy dissipation between two points (Point 1 → Point 2) in a process piping network: $$ \frac{P_1}{\rho} + \frac{v_1^{2}}{2} + g z_1 \;=\; \frac{P_2}{\rho} + \frac{v_2^{2}}{2} + g z_2 \;+\; \frac{\Delta P_f}{\rho} $$ Here, $\Delta P_f$ is the **Friction Pressure Component** — the entire subject of this module. ### 🌊 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > **[!IMPORTANT]** > **The Garden Hose Analogy:** Imagine water flowing through a long garden hose. At the tap (high pressure) versus at the nozzle (lower pressure), you can feel a difference. But if the hose is very long, has kinks, or you attach a narrow sprayer, the water pressure at the nozzle drops even more — not because the tap pressure changed, but because energy was "lost" to friction along the way. > > - **Long hose (long pipe)** → More friction loss > - **Kinks & bends (fittings)** → Extra friction loss > - **Narrow sprayer (valve)** → Major friction loss > - **Smoother inner wall** → Less friction loss > > This "lost" pressure is the **Friction Pressure Component** — it represents energy that has been converted from useful flow energy into heat (thermal dissipation) and is no longer available to do work. ### 🎯 Physical Significance in Process Control | Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit | Physical Meaning | |---|---|---|---| | Friction Pressure Drop | $\Delta P_f$ | **Pa (N/m²)** or **bar** or **psi** | Energy dissipated per unit volume | | Fanning Friction Factor | $f$ | dimensionless | Wall shear stress parameter | | Darcy Friction Factor | $f_D$ | dimensionless | $f_D = 4f$ | | Pipe Roughness | $\varepsilon$ | **m** or **mm** | Surface irregularity height | | Reynolds Number | $Re$ | dimensionless | Flow regime indicator | | Equivalent Length | $L_e$ | **m** | Length of straight pipe giving same ΔP | | Resistance Coefficient (K) | $K$ | dimensionless | Local loss coefficient for fittings | > **[!NOTE]** > **Standard Metric:** In SI units, friction pressure is universally expressed in **Pascals (Pa) = N/m²**, with **1 bar = 10⁵ Pa** and **1 psi = 6894.76 Pa** as the most common industry conversions. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 🧠 Conceptual Logic Breakdown The friction pressure component arises from **two distinct physical mechanisms**, which KTU examiners love to test separately: #### 🔹 A) Distributed (Skin) Friction — Along Straight Pipe Walls - Caused by viscous shear at the boundary layer - Depends on: pipe length $L$, diameter $D$, fluid velocity $v$, density $\rho$, viscosity $\mu$, and wall roughness $\varepsilon$ - **Proportional to $L$** (longer pipe → more loss) - **Proportional to $1/D$** (smaller pipe → more loss per unit length) #### 🔹 B) Local (Form) Friction — At Fittings, Valves, Bends - Caused by flow separation, turbulence, and reattachment - Depends on geometry of the fitting, not on pipe length - Each fitting has a **K-factor** (resistance coefficient) or **equivalent length $L_e$** ### 📐 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > **[!IMPORTANT]** > **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS — Memorize These for KTU ESE** | # | Formula Name | Equation | Units | Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **General Mechanical Energy Balance** | $\frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho} + \frac{v_1^{2} - v_2^{2}}{2} + g(z_1 - z_2) = \frac{\Delta P_f}{\rho} + W_s$ | J/kg | Master equation | | 2 | **Darcy-Weisbach Equation** | $\Delta P_f = f_D \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho v^{2}}{2}$ | Pa | Pipe friction loss | | 3 | **Fanning Form** | $\Delta P_f = 4f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho v^{2}}{2}$ | Pa | Pipe friction (Fanning) | | 4 | **Local Loss (K-method)** | $\Delta P_f = \sum_{i=1}^{n} K_i \cdot \frac{\rho v_i^{2}}{2}$ | Pa | Fittings, valves, bends | | 5 | **Equivalent Length Method** | $L_e = \frac{K \cdot D}{f_D}$ | m | Convert K to length | | 6 | **Hagen-Poiseuille (Laminar)** | $\Delta P_f = \frac{32 \mu L v}{D^{2}}$ | Pa | $Re < 2100$ | | 7 | **Reynolds Number** | $Re = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \frac{v D}{\nu}$ | — | Flow regime | | 8 | **Hydraulic Diameter** | $D_h = \frac{4 \times \text{Cross-section Area}}{\text{Wetted Perimeter}}$ | m | Non-circular ducts | | 9 | **Control Valve Loss** | $\Delta P_v = K_v \cdot \frac{\rho v^{2}}{2}$ | Pa | Across control valve | | 10 | **Volumetric Flow via ΔPf** | $Q = C \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta P_f}{\rho}}$ | m³/s | Resistance law | > **[!NOTE]** > **CRITICAL CONVERSION:** $f_D = 4 f_{Fanning}$. KTU examiners often give a problem where the friction factor is given in one form and expect the answer in the other. Always check the convention before plugging into the formula! ### 🏭 Real-World Engineering Utility The friction pressure component governs several critical industrial design decisions: 1. **Pump Sizing** — The pump must generate enough head to overcome all friction losses plus static head. 2. **Control Valve Sizing** — The valve must be selected such that its ΔP is neither too small (poor rangeability) nor too large (excessive wear). 3. **Pipe Diameter Selection** — Larger diameter → less friction → smaller pumping cost, but higher capital cost. Optimization is required. 4. **Network Analysis** — In complex piping systems, friction losses are additive along each path. 5. **Process Safety** — Excessive friction loss in emergency relief systems can prevent adequate depressurization. 6. **Heat Exchanger Design** — Friction on the shell/tube side determines allowable pressure drop. 7. **Dynamic Process Response** — In control loop tuning, the friction term introduces **nonlinearity** in the flow process dynamics. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 📘 Derivation 1: The Darcy-Weisbach Equation (from Navier-Stokes) We start from the **force balance on a fluid element** in a horizontal pipe of length $L$ and diameter $D$ at steady state. **Step 1 — Identify forces on the cylindrical fluid element:** $$ \text{Net Pressure Force} = (P_1 - P_2) \cdot \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} $$ $$ \text{Wall Shear Force} = \tau_w \cdot (\pi D L) $$ At steady state, the net force is zero (no acceleration): $$ (P_1 - P_2) \cdot \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} = \tau_w \cdot \pi D L $$ **Step 2 — Solve for the pressure drop:** $$ P_1 - P_2 = \frac{4 \tau_w L}{D} $$ **Step 3 — Define the Fanning friction factor:** By definition, the wall shear stress is related to the kinetic energy of the flow: $$ \tau_w = f \cdot \frac{\rho v^{2}}{2} $$ where $f$ is the **Fanning friction factor** (dimensionless). **Step 4 — Substitute back:** $$ P_1 - P_2 = 4 f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho v^{2}}{2} $$ **Step 5 — Convert to Darcy form using $f_D = 4f$:** $$ \boxed{\;\Delta P_f = f_D \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho v^{2}}{2}\;} $$ This is the celebrated **Darcy-Weisbach Equation**. > **[!NOTE]** > **Conversion Insight:** Many chemical engineering textbooks (Coulson & Richardson) use the Fanning form, while mechanical/civil engineering texts (White, Munson) use the Darcy form. The factor of 4 difference is a frequent trap in KTU problems. --- ### 📘 Derivation 2: Hagen-Poiseuille Equation (Laminar Flow Special Case) For fully developed **laminar flow** in a circular pipe, the velocity profile is parabolic. The volumetric flow rate is given by the **Hagen-Poiseuille law**: $$ Q = \frac{\pi R^{4} (P_1 - P_2)}{8 \mu L} $$ **Step 1 — Express velocity in terms of flow rate:** $$ v = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{Q}{\pi R^{2}} $$ **Step 2 — Solve for pressure drop:** $$ P_1 - P_2 = \frac{8 \mu L Q}{\pi R^{4}} $$ **Step 3 — Substitute $R = D/2$ and express in terms of average velocity:** $$ P_1 - P_2 = \frac{128 \mu L Q}{\pi D^{4}} = \frac{32 \mu L v}{D^{2}} $$ $$ \boxed{\;\Delta P_{f,\text{laminar}} = \frac{32 \mu L v}{D^{2}}\;} $$ **Step 4 — Derive the Fanning friction factor for laminar flow:** Equate the two expressions for ΔPf: $$ \frac{32 \mu L v}{D^{2}} = 4 f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho v^{2}}{2} $$ Simplify by cancelling $L$ and one $D$: $$ \frac{32 \mu v}{D} = 4 f \cdot \frac{\rho v^{2}}{2} = 2 f \rho v^{2} $$ Solve for $f$: $$ f = \frac{16 \mu}{\rho v D} = \frac{16}{Re} $$ $$ \boxed{\;f_{\text{Fanning, laminar}} = \frac{16}{Re}\;} $$ > **[!IMPORTANT]** > **This is a favorite KTU 2-mark question:** "Show that the Fanning friction factor for fully developed laminar flow in a circular pipe equals $16/Re$." --- ### 📘 Derivation 3: Local Loss Coefficient (K-Factor) via Equivalent Length Each fitting can be characterized by either: - A **resistance coefficient $K$** (dimensionless), or - An **equivalent length $L_e$** (meters of straight pipe producing the same loss) **Equating the two loss expressions:** $$ \Delta P_{\text{fitting}} = K \cdot \frac{\rho v^{2}}{2} = f_D \cdot \frac{L_e}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho v^{2}}{2} $$ Cancel common terms: $$ K = f_D \cdot \frac{L_e}{D} $$ Solving for equivalent length: $$ \boxed{\;L_e = \frac{K \cdot D}{f_D}\;} $$ **Typical K-Factor Values (KTU Reference):** | Fitting Type | K-Factor (typical) | |---|---| | 90° standard elbow | **0.9** | | 90° long-radius elbow | **0.4** | | 45° elbow | **0.4** | | Tee, line flow | **0.6** | | Tee, branch flow | **1.8** | | Fully open globe valve | **6.0 to 10** | | Fully open gate valve | **0.15** | | Fully open ball valve | **0.05** | | Sudden expansion | $(1 - A_1/A_2)^{2}$ | | Sudden contraction | $0.5 (1 - A_2/A_1)$ | | Pipe entrance (sharp) | **0.5** | | Pipe exit | **1.0** | --- ### 🧮 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) > **Problem:** Water ($\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $\mu = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s) flows at $Q = 0.005$ m³/s through a horizontal commercial steel pipe of diameter $D = 0.05$ m and length $L = 100$ m. The line contains two 90° standard elbows and one fully open globe valve. Estimate the total friction pressure drop. **Step 1 — Calculate average velocity:** $$ A = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.05)^{2}}{4} = 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m}^{2} $$ $$ v = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{0.005}{1.963 \times 10^{-3}} = 2.546 \; \text{m/s} $$ **Step 2 — Calculate Reynolds Number:** $$ Re = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \frac{1000 \times 2.546 \times 0.05}{1.0 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.273 \times 10^{5} $$ Since $Re > 4000$, flow is **turbulent**. **Step 3 — Estimate Friction Factor (using Haaland equation):** $$ \frac{1}{\sqrt{f_D}} = -1.8 \log_{10}\left[ \left(\frac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7}\right)^{1.11} + \frac{6.9}{Re} \right] $$ For commercial steel, $\varepsilon = 0.045$ mm, so $\varepsilon/D = 0.045/50 = 0.0009$. $$ \frac{1}{\sqrt{f_D}} = -1.8 \log_{10}\left[ \left(\frac{0.0009}{3.7}\right)^{1.11} + \frac{6.9}{1.273 \times 10^{5}} \right] $$ $$ \frac{1}{\sqrt{f_D}} = -1.8 \log_{10}\left[ (2.432 \times 10^{-4})^{1.11} + 5.42 \times 10^{-5} \right] $$ $$ \frac{1}{\sqrt{f_D}} = -1.8 \log_{10}\left[ 1.12 \times 10^{-4} + 5.42 \times 10^{-5} \right] = -1.8 \log_{10}(1.66 \times 10^{-4}) $$ $$ \frac{1}{\sqrt{f_D}} = -1.8 \times (-3.78) = 6.80 $$ $$ f_D = \frac{1}{(6.80)^{2}} = 0.0216 $$ **Step 4 — Pipe friction loss:** $$ \Delta P_{\text{pipe}} = f_D \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho v^{2}}{2} = 0.0216 \times \frac{100}{0.05} \times \frac{1000 \times (2.546)^{2}}{2} $$ $$ \Delta P_{\text{pipe}} = 0.0216 \times 2000 \times 3240.6 = 1.400 \times 10^{5} \; \text{Pa} $$ **Step 5 — Local losses (2 elbows + 1 globe valve):** $$ \sum K = 2(0.9) + 8.0 = 9.8 $$ $$ \Delta P_{\text{local}} = \sum K \cdot \frac{\rho v^{2}}{2} = 9.8 \times 3240.6 = 3.176 \times 10^{4} \; \text{Pa} $$ **Step 6 — Total Friction Pressure Drop:** $$ \boxed{\;\Delta P_{f,\text{total}} = 1.400 \times 10^{5} + 0.318 \times 10^{5} \approx 1.72 \times 10^{5} \; \text{Pa} \approx 1.72 \; \text{bar}\;} $$ --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 🔷 Figure 1: Block Diagram — Total Pressure Decomposition in a Flow System ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Total Pressure Available
at Source P1] --> B[Static Pressure
Component
P_static] A --> C[Velocity / Kinetic
Pressure Component
rho v squared by 2] A --> D[Elevation / Potential
Pressure Component
rho g h] A --> E[Friction Pressure
Component
DELTA P_f] E --> E1[Distributed Friction
Pipe Wall Shear
fD L by D] E --> E2[Local Friction
Fittings and Valves
Sigma Ki] E1 --> F[Total Friction
DELTA P_f total] E2 --> F B --> G[Pressure at
Destination P2] C --> G D --> G F --> G ``` ### 🔷 Figure 2: Block-Level Architecture — Friction Pressure Computation Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR IN1[Process Inputs
Fluid rho and mu
Pipe L and D
Velocity v
Roughness epsilon] --> CALC1[Compute Reynolds
Number Re] CALC1 --> REGIME{Flow Regime} REGIME -->|Re less than 2100| LAM[Laminar Correlation
f equals 16 by Re] REGIME -->|Re greater than 2100| TURB[Turbulent Correlation
Haaland or Colebrook] LAM --> FF[Friction Factor fD] TURB --> FF FF --> PIPE[Pipe Wall Loss
DELTA P_pipe] IN2[Fittings K factors
from lookup table] --> LOC[Local Losses
DELTA P_local] IN1 --> LOC PIPE --> SUM[Total Friction
DELTA P_f total] LOC --> SUM SUM --> OUT[Output to Pump Sizing
and Control Valve Sizing] ``` ### 🔷 Figure 3: Process Control Loop — Role of Friction Pressure in Dynamics ```mermaid flowchart TD SP[Setpoint SP] --> COMP[Comparator] PV[Process Variable
Flow Rate Q] --> COMP COMP --> ERR[Error Signal e t] ERR --> CTRL[Controller
PID] CTRL --> OP[Controller Output
P t] OP --> CV[Control Valve] CV --> CVP[Friction Pressure
across Valve
DELTA P_v] CVP --> PROC[Flow Process
Pipes and Fittings] PROC --> FRIC[Friction Pressure
in Process
DELTA P_f] FRIC --> FLOW[Resulting Flow Q] FLOW --> PV FLOW --> METER[Flow Meter] METER --> PV ``` ### 🔷 Figure 4: Decision Flowchart — Choosing Friction Loss Method ```mermaid flowchart TD START[Identify Problem Type] --> Q1{Long Straight
Pipe Dominant?} Q1 -->|Yes| PIPE[Use Darcy Weisbach
DELTA P equals fD L by D rho v squared by 2] Q1 -->|No| Q2{Many Fittings
or Valves?} Q2 -->|Yes| FIT[Use K factor Method
DELTA P equals K rho v squared by 2] Q2 -->|No| Q3{Laminar Flow
Re less than 2100?} Q3 -->|Yes| LAM[Use Hagen Poiseuille
DELTA P equals 32 mu L v by D squared] Q3 -->|No| COMB[Use Combined
Total DELTA P equals Pipe Loss plus Local Loss] PIPE --> RESULT[Total Friction Pressure Drop] FIT --> RESULT LAM --> RESULT COMB --> RESULT ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Define the friction pressure component in a flowing fluid system. List the two main categories of friction losses with one example each.** **Model Answer:** > **[!NOTE]** > The **friction pressure component** is the irreversible pressure loss (energy dissipated as heat) experienced by a fluid as it flows through a pipe and associated fittings, due to viscous shear and turbulent dissipation. **Two main categories:** 1. **Distributed (Skin) Friction Loss:** - Occurs along the straight length of the pipe - Caused by viscous shear at the fluid–wall boundary layer - **Example:** Pressure drop along a 100 m straight pipe carrying water - Governed by: $\Delta P_f = f_D \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho v^{2}}{2}$ 2. **Local (Form) Friction Loss:** - Occurs at fittings, bends, valves, expansions, contractions - Caused by flow separation and reattachment turbulence - **Example:** Pressure drop across a 90° elbow or a partially closed control valve - Governed by: $\Delta P_f = K \cdot \frac{\rho v^{2}}{2}$ *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for each category with example)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **State and explain the Darcy-Weisbach equation. What is the relationship between the Darcy and Fanning friction factors?** **Model Answer:** > **[!IMPORTANT]** > The **Darcy-Weisbach equation** is the universal formula for computing frictional pressure loss in a pipe: $$ \Delta P_f = f_D \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho v^{2}}{2} $$ **Where:** - $\Delta P_f$ = Friction pressure drop (Pa) - $f_D$ = Darcy friction factor (dimensionless) - $L$ = Length of pipe (m) - $D$ = Internal diameter (m) - $\rho$ = Fluid density (kg/m³) - $v$ = Average fluid velocity (m/s) **Relationship with Fanning Friction Factor:** The Fanning friction factor is defined using the wall shear stress: $$ \tau_w = f \cdot \frac{\rho v^{2}}{2} $$ Equating with the Darcy form yields: $$ f_D = 4 f $$ Therefore, the Fanning form is: $$ \Delta P_f = 4 f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho v^{2}}{2} $$ > **For laminar flow in a circular pipe:** $f = 16/Re$, hence $f_D = 64/Re$. *(3 marks: 1 for Darcy-Weisbach statement, 1 for relationship, 1 for laminar special case)* --- ### 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** > A liquid process stream flows at a rate of **0.02 m³/s** through a horizontal commercial steel pipe of diameter **0.075 m** and total straight length **150 m**. The line contains the following fittings: 4 standard 90° elbows, 2 fully open gate valves, and 1 fully open globe valve. The fluid has density **$\rho = 950$ kg/m³** and viscosity **$\mu = 2.5 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s**. For commercial steel, the roughness is **$\varepsilon = 0.045$ mm**. > > **(a)** Calculate the total friction pressure drop in the line, including both pipe and local losses. (7 marks) > > **(b)** If the same line is inclined upward at an angle of 30° over its entire 150 m length, determine the additional pressure (beyond friction) that the upstream pump must provide to overcome the static head. Comment on whether friction or static head dominates. (7 marks) --- #### **Model Solution for Q-A (a):** **Step 1 — Compute cross-sectional area and velocity:** $$ A = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.075)^{2}}{4} = 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m}^{2} $$ $$ v = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{0.02}{4.418 \times 10^{-3}} = 4.527 \; \text{m/s} $$ **Step 2 — Compute Reynolds Number:** $$ Re = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \frac{950 \times 4.527 \times 0.075}{2.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.290 \times 10^{5} $$ Since $Re \gg 4000$, flow is fully **turbulent**. **Step 3 — Compute relative roughness:** $$ \frac{\varepsilon}{D} = \frac{0.045 \times 10^{-3}}{0.075} = 6.0 \times 10^{-4} $$ **Step 4 — Estimate Darcy friction factor using the Haaland equation:** $$ \frac{1}{\sqrt{f_D}} = -1.8 \log_{10}\left[ \left(\frac{6.0 \times 10^{-4}}{3.7}\right)^{1.11} + \frac{6.9}{1.29 \times 10^{5}} \right] $$ $$ = -1.8 \log_{10}\left[ (1.62 \times 10^{-4})^{1.11} + 5.35 \times 10^{-5} \right] $$ $$ = -1.8 \log_{10}\left[ 8.06 \times 10^{-5} + 5.35 \times 10^{-5} \right] $$ $$ = -1.8 \log_{10}(1.341 \times 10^{-4}) = -1.8 \times (-3.873) = 6.97 $$ $$ f_D = \frac{1}{(6.97)^{2}} = 0.0206 $$ **Step 5 — Pipe friction loss:** $$ \Delta P_{\text{pipe}} = 0.0206 \times \frac{150}{0.075} \times \frac{950 \times (4.527)^{2}}{2} $$ $$ = 0.0206 \times 2000 \times 9732 = 4.010 \times 10^{5} \; \text{Pa} \approx 4.01 \; \text{bar} $$ **Step 6 — Sum of K-factors for local fittings:** $$ \sum K = 4 \times 0.9 \;(\text{elbows}) + 2 \times 0.15 \;(\text{gate valves}) + 1 \times 8.0 \;(\text{globe valve}) $$ $$ = 3.6 + 0.3 + 8.0 = 11.9 $$ **Step 7 — Local losses:** $$ \Delta P_{\text{local}} = 11.9 \times \frac{950 \times (4.527)^{2}}{2} = 11.9 \times 9732 = 1.158 \times 10^{5} \; \text{Pa} \approx 1.16 \; \text{bar} $$ **Step 8 — Total Friction Pressure Drop:** $$ \boxed{\;\Delta P_{f,\text{total}} = 4.01 \times 10^{5} + 1.16 \times 10^{5} \approx 5.17 \times 10^{5} \; \text{Pa} \approx 5.17 \; \text{bar}\;} ``` --- #### **Model Solution for Q-A (b):** **Step 1 — Compute vertical rise:** $$ h = L \sin(30°) = 150 \times 0.5 = 75 \; \text{m} $$ **Step 2 — Compute static head pressure:** $$ \Delta P_{\text{static}} = \rho g h = 950 \times 9.81 \times 75 = 6.990 \times 10^{5} \; \text{Pa} \approx 6.99 \; \text{bar} $$ **Step 3 — Compare friction vs. static:** $$ \frac{\Delta P_f}{\Delta P_{\text{static}}} = \frac{5.17}{6.99} \approx 0.74 $$ > **[!NOTE]** > **Conclusion:** In this inclined-line scenario, the **static head (6.99 bar) dominates** over the friction loss (5.17 bar), contributing approximately **57% of the total pump pressure requirement**. The pump must therefore provide the **sum of both**: $$ \boxed{\;P_{\text{pump}} = \Delta P_f + \Delta P_{\text{static}} = 5.17 + 6.99 \approx 12.16 \; \text{bar}\;} $$ This illustrates the principle that for **long, inclined lines with high density fluids**, the static head often dominates, while for **horizontal lines with high velocity**, friction dominates. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam – June 2023]** > **(a)** Derive the Hagen-Poiseuille equation for laminar flow of a Newtonian fluid in a horizontal circular pipe. Show clearly that the Fanning friction factor equals $16/Re$ for this case. (7 marks) > > **(b)** A control valve is installed in a water line ($\rho = 1000$ kg/m³). The flow coefficient of the valve is $C_v = 50$ (US gpm/psi^0.5). The pressure drop across the valve is measured as **$\Delta P_v = 4$ psi**. Calculate the volumetric flow rate through the valve in m³/s. If the upstream pipe friction accounts for an additional 30% of the valve pressure drop, determine the total friction pressure loss in the line segment. (7 marks) --- #### **Model Solution for Q-B (a):** **Step 1 — Start from the Navier-Stokes equation in cylindrical coordinates:** For steady, fully developed, laminar flow in a horizontal pipe, only the axial component survives: $$ 0 = -\frac{\partial P}{\partial z} + \mu \left[ \frac{1}{r} \frac{\partial}{\partial r}\left( r \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial r} \right) \right] $$ **Step 2 — Integrate once with respect to r:** Since $\partial P / \partial z$ is a constant (call it $-(\Delta P / L)$): $$ \frac{1}{r} \frac{d}{dr}\left( r \frac{dv_z}{dr} \right) = \frac{1}{\mu}\frac{\Delta P}{L} $$ Multiply both sides by $r$ and integrate: $$ r \frac{dv_z}{dr} = \frac{r^{2}}{2\mu} \frac{\Delta P}{L} + C_1 $$ **Step 3 — Apply boundary conditions:** - **B.C. 1:** Finite velocity at $r = 0$ → $C_1 = 0$ (avoids logarithmic singularity at center) - **B.C. 2:** No-slip at wall $r = R$ → $v_z = 0$ Reintegrating: $$ v_z(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L} (R^{2} - r^{2}) $$ This is the **parabolic velocity profile**. **Step 4 — Integrate over the cross-section to get volumetric flow:** $$ Q = \int_{0}^{R} v_z (2\pi r) dr = \frac{2\pi \Delta P}{4 \mu L} \int_{0}^{R} (R^{2} - r^{2}) r \, dr $$ $$ = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2 \mu L} \left[ \frac{R^{2} r^{2}}{2} - \frac{r^{4}}{4} \right]_{0}^{R} = \frac{\pi \Delta P R^{4}}{8 \mu L} $$ $$ \boxed{\;Q = \frac{\pi R^{4} \Delta P}{8 \mu L}\;} $$ **Step 5 — Express in terms of average velocity $v = Q / (\pi R^{2})$:** $$ v = \frac{R^{2} \Delta P}{8 \mu L} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Delta P = \frac{8 \mu L v}{R^{2}} = \frac{32 \mu L v}{D^{2}} $$ **Step 6 — Equate with Darcy-Weisbach to find $f_D$:** $$ \frac{32 \mu L v}{D^{2}} = 4 f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho v^{2}}{2} $$ $$ \frac{32 \mu v}{D} = 2 f \rho v^{2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad f = \frac{16 \mu}{\rho v D} = \frac{16}{Re} $$ And $f_D = 4 f = 64 / Re$. > **[!NOTE]** > **Key Insight:** The friction factor for laminar flow is **independent of pipe roughness** — it depends only on the Reynolds number. This is one of the most elegant results in fluid mechanics and a frequent KTU exam question. --- #### **Model Solution for Q-B (b):** **Step 1 — Apply the valve flow equation:** For liquids, the US-standard valve flow equation is: $$ Q \;[\text{gpm}] = C_v \sqrt{\frac{\Delta P \;[\text{psi}]}{G}} $$ where $G$ is the specific gravity of the liquid relative to water. For water, $G = 1$. $$ Q = 50 \times \sqrt{\frac{4}{1}} = 50 \times 2 = 100 \; \text{gpm} $$ **Step 2 — Convert gpm to m³/s:** $$ 1 \; \text{gpm (US)} = 6.309 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{m}^{3}/\text{s} $$ $$ Q = 100 \times 6.309 \times 10^{-5} = 6.309 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m}^{3}/\text{s} \approx 6.31 \; \text{L/s} $$ **Step 3 — Additional friction from pipe:** $$ \Delta P_{\text{pipe}} = 0.30 \times \Delta P_v = 0.30 \times 4 = 1.2 \; \text{psi} $$ **Step 4 — Total Friction Pressure Loss:** $$ \boxed{\;\Delta P_{f,\text{total}} = 4 + 1.2 = 5.2 \; \text{psi} = 3.585 \times 10^{4} \; \text{Pa} \approx 0.36 \; \text{bar}\;} $$ > **Conclusion:** The control valve contributes **~77%** of the total friction loss in this line, demonstrating why valve sizing (avoiding oversized valves) is critical to pump energy efficiency. --- ### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > **[!WARNING]** > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Friction Pressure Problems:** > > 1. **Forgetting the factor of 4** between Darcy and Fanning friction factors. The question statement often specifies one form; the answer key uses another. Always declare which convention you are using. > > 2. **Mixing up the local loss formula:** $\Delta P_{f,\text{local}} = K \cdot \dfrac{\rho v^{2}}{2}$ — students frequently write $K \cdot \rho v^{2}$ (missing the division by 2).
