- **Industrial control implication:** Moving the operating point (via throttling, VFD, or impeller trimming) is the fundamental mechanism of pump-based flow control in chemical, petrochemical, and water-treatment plants.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: On-Off Level Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT743 - INDUSTRIAL PROCESS CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** On-Off Level Control # Module 4: On-Off Level Control ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **On-Off Level Control** is a two-position discontinuous control strategy in which the final control element (typically a solenoid valve or a pump motor) is driven into one of two discrete states — fully **ON** or fully **OFF** — depending on whether the measured process variable (liquid level, *h*) lies above or below a predetermined **set point (SP)**. When the level crosses the set point, the controller switches state, causing the manipulated variable (inflow or outflow) to change abruptly between 0% and 100% of its capacity. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, this topic falls under **discontinuous / two-position control modes** of industrial process controllers, classified as **Mode 1 (Two-Position or On-Off Mode)** in classical controller taxonomy. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-world analogy — The Household Water Tank Float Valve:** > Think of the flush tank in your home toilet. When you press the flush lever, water drains out, and the floating ball drops down. Once the ball falls below a certain height, it pulls open a valve, and water rushes in (pump effectively *ON*). As water fills up, the ball rises. The moment it crosses a slightly *higher* threshold, the valve slams shut (pump *OFF*). The water level never stays at a single exact value — it *oscillates* between two limits. **That oscillation band is the "Differential Gap."** This is precisely what happens in an industrial On-Off Level Control system. ### Standard Metrics Used - **Level (h):** measured in **metres (m)** or **centimetres (cm)** - **Set Point (SP or h_sp):** desired target level, **m** - **Differential Gap (Δh or DB):** the dead-band around the set point, **m** - **Cycling Frequency (f):** number of on-off cycles per minute, **cycles/min** - **Error (e):** the deviation `e = SP − PV`, **m** ### Why It Matters in Industry On-Off Level Control is the **workhorse of fluid handling** in: - Municipal water distribution overhead tanks - Boiler drum level (low-pressure heating boilers) - Sump pits and sewage pumping stations - Chemical dosing / acid-base neutralization tanks - HVAC condensate drain pans Its popularity stems from extreme simplicity, low cost, zero steady-state error (statistically), and reliable operation with virtually no tuning requirement. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Operational Logic of On-Off Control The on-off controller is essentially a **comparator with relay output**. Its internal algorithm can be expressed as a piecewise Boolean function: > [!NOTE] > **Controller Output (manipulated variable, MV):** > - MV = 100% (ON) when the measured level *h* falls **below** the lower threshold `h_L` > - MV = 0% (OFF) when the measured level *h* rises **above** the upper threshold `h_H` > - MV = last state when `h_L ≤ h ≤ h_H` (memory / hysteresis region) ### Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown 1. **Sensing Stage:** A level transmitter (LT) — float, capacitance probe, ultrasonic, or differential pressure cell — continuously measures `h(t)`. 2. **Comparison Stage:** The controller computes the error `e(t) = h_sp − h(t)`. 3. **Decision Stage:** A relay with a built-in dead-band evaluates `e(t)` against the differential gap limits. 4. **Actuation Stage:** The output drives a solenoid valve, contactor, or pump starter to either fully open/ON or fully closed/OFF. 5. **Process Response:** The tank dynamics (governed by inflow − outflow balance) drive `h(t)` toward the set point, but overshoot occurs due to the discontinuous action. ## 2.2 The Differential Gap (Dead Band) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Differential Gap (Δh):** The intentional, engineered overlap between the ON-switching level and the OFF-switching level. It is *not* a defect — it is a deliberate design feature. Mathematically: $$\Delta h = h_{OFF} - h_{ON} = h_{H} - h_{L}$$ where: - `h_ON` = level at which the controller turns the pump/valve **ON** (lower trip) - `h_OFF` = level at which the controller turns the pump/valve **OFF** (upper trip) ### Why Differential Gap Is Essential Without a differential gap, the relay would chatter (oscillate at near-infinite frequency) every time the level brushed the set point, destroying contactors and solenoid coils within hours. The differential gap **deliberately decouples the ON and OFF trips**, giving the contacts a "rest period." ## 2.3 Pure (Ideal) On-Off Control — Without Differential Gap This is a theoretical construct only: $$u(t) = \begin{cases} U_{max} & \text{if } e(t) > 0 \\ U_{min} & \text{if } e(t) < 0 \\ \text{undefined} & \text{if } e(t) = 0 \end{cases}$$ It is mathematically discontinuous at `e = 0` and **never implemented in practice** for moving processes. ## 2.4 Realistic Two-Position Controller with Differential Gap $$u(t) = \begin{cases} U_{max} & \text{if } e(t) \geq \dfrac{\Delta h}{2} \\ U_{min} & \text{if } e(t) \leq -\dfrac{\Delta h}{2} \\ u(t^{-}) & \text{if } \vert e(t) \vert < \dfrac{\Delta h}{2} \end{cases}$$ Here `u(t⁻)` denotes the previous (held) state — this is the **memory effect** that prevents chattering. ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula / Expression | Units | Engineering Meaning | |---|----------|----------------------|-------|---------------------| | 1 | Error signal | $e(t) = h_{sp} - h(t)$ | m | Deviation from target | | 2 | Differential Gap | $\Delta h = h_{H} - h_{L}$ | m | Hysteresis band on level axis | | 3 | Mean Level | $\bar{h} = \dfrac{h_{H} + h_{L}}{2}$ | m | Centre of oscillation | | 4 | Level Deviation | $\delta h = h_{sp} - \bar{h}$ | m | Offset between SP and oscillation mean | | 5 | Tank Mass Balance | $A \dfrac{dh}{dt} = F_{in} - F_{out}$ | m³/s | Continuity equation | | 6 | Steady Inflow ON-time | $F_{in} = C_v \cdot u$ | m³/s | Pump max capacity | | 7 | Linear Outflow | $F_{out} = \dfrac{h}{R}$ | m³/s | Gravity-driven linear drain (R = resistance) | | 8 | Period of Oscillation | $T = \Delta h \cdot \dfrac{A}{F_{in} - \bar{F}_{out}}$ | s | Time between two successive ON events | | 9 | Cycling Frequency | $f = \dfrac{1}{T}$ | Hz | Contacts wear indicator | | 10 | Steady-State Error | $e_{ss} = h_{sp} - \bar{h} \approx 0$ | m | Statistical mean of error | | 11 | Effective Gain (approx.) | $K_{eff} = \dfrac{U_{max} - U_{min}}{\Delta h}$ | per m | Slope of relay characteristic | | 12 | Controller Output | $u \in \{U_{max}, U_{min}\}$ | % | Pure two-position action | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examination Tip:** When asked to "derive the cycling period," the standard approach is to integrate the tank ODE separately during the ON-phase and OFF-phase, then sum the two durations. This is a high-frequency past-year question. ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Cost reduction:** No PID tuning, no proportional valve required — just a relay. - **Reliability:** Decades-long mean time between failures in municipal installations. - **Inherent integration:** Liquid tanks are naturally integrating processes (level = ∫(net flow)/A), so on-off control achieves zero average offset over time. - **Limitation:** Cannot be used where **tight level control** is essential (e.g., reflux drum of a distillation column), because amplitude of oscillation would disturb downstream composition. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Cycling Period of an On-Off Level Controller ### Setup Consider a cylindrical tank of constant cross-sectional area **A** (m²). Liquid enters at a constant volumetric flow rate **F_in** (m³/s) when the pump is ON, and exits at a level-dependent rate **F_out = h/R** (m³/s), where **R** (s/m²) is the drain resistance. **Initial condition:** level `h = h_L` (lower trip), pump just turned ON. **Objective:** Find the time `T_ON` taken for the level to rise from `h_L` to `h_H`. ### Step 1 — Write the Mass Balance $$A \dfrac{dh}{dt} = F_{in} - \dfrac{h}{R}$$ Rearranging into standard linear ODE form: $$\dfrac{dh}{dt} + \dfrac{h}{AR} = \dfrac{F_{in}}{A}$$ ### Step 2 — Identify Time Constant $$\tau = AR \quad \text{(seconds)}, \quad K_{proc} = RF_{in} \quad \text{(metres)}$$ So the ODE becomes: $$\tau \dfrac{dh}{dt} + h = K_{proc}$$ ### Step 3 — Solve by Separation of Variables $$\dfrac{dh}{K_{proc} - h} = \dfrac{dt}{\tau}$$ Integrating from `h = h_L` at `t = 0` to `h = h_H` at `t = T_ON`: $$-\ln\bigl(K_{proc} - h\bigr)\big|_{h_L}^{h_H} = \dfrac{T_{ON}}{\tau}$$ $$\ln\!\left(\dfrac{K_{proc} - h_{L}}{K_{proc} - h_{H}}\right) = \dfrac{T_{ON}}{\tau}$$ ### Step 4 — Solve for T_ON $$T_{ON} = \tau \ln\!\left(\dfrac{K_{proc} - h_{L}}{K_{proc} - h_{H}}\right)$$ Substituting back `K_proc = RF_in` and `τ = AR`: $$T_{ON} = AR \ln\!\left(\dfrac{RF_{in} - h_{L}}{RF_{in} - h_{H}}\right)$$ ### Step 5 — Derive T_OFF Symmetrically During the OFF phase, `F_in = 0`, so: $$A \dfrac{dh}{dt} = -\dfrac{h}{R} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \dfrac{dh}{h} = -\dfrac{dt}{AR}$$ Integrating from `h = h_H` (start of OFF) to `h = h_L` (end of OFF): $$T_{OFF} = AR \ln\!\left(\dfrac{h_{H}}{h_{L}}\right)$$ ### Step 6 — Total Cycling Period $$\boxed{\,T = T_{ON} + T_{OFF} = AR \left[ \ln\!\left(\dfrac{RF_{in} - h_{L}}{RF_{in} - h_{H}}\right) + \ln\!\left(\dfrac{h_{H}}{h_{L}}\right) \right]\,}$$ ### Step 7 — Small-Δh Approximation (High-Yield KTU Shortcut) When `Δh ≪ h_sp`, we can linearise. In a small neighbourhood, the rate of change of level is approximately `(F_in − F_out)/A` while ON, and `−F_out/A` while OFF. For a symmetric operating point, `F_out ≈ F_in/2` (averaged), giving: $$T \approx \dfrac{2A \cdot \Delta h}{F_{in}}$$ This compact form is what KTU examiners expect in Part A and short derivations. --- ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Typical Style) **Given:** - Tank cross-section `A = 2 \text{ m}^2` - Pump flow rate `F_in = 0.05 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}` - Drain resistance `R = 200 \text{ s/m}^2` - Lower trip `h_L = 1.5 \text{ m}`, Upper trip `h_H = 1.7 \text{ m}` **Find:** Cycling period `T` and cycling frequency `f`. **Step A — Compute the constant `K_proc`:** $$K_{proc} = RF_{in} = 200 \times 0.05 = 10 \text{ m}$$ **Step B — Compute T_ON:** $$T_{ON} = AR \ln\!\left(\dfrac{10 - 1.5}{10 - 1.7}\right) = (2)(200)\ln\!\left(\dfrac{8.5}{8.3}\right) = 400 \times \ln(1.0241)$$ $$T_{ON} = 400 \times 0.02383 = 9.53 \text{ s}$$ **Step C — Compute T_OFF:** $$T_{OFF} = AR \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1.7}{1.5}\right) = 400 \times \ln(1.1333) = 400 \times 0.12516 = 50.07 \text{ s}$$ **Step D — Total period and frequency:** $$T = 9.53 + 50.07 = 59.60 \text{ s}$$ $$f = \dfrac{1}{T} = \dfrac{1}{59.6} = 0.01678 \text{ Hz} \approx 1.01 \text{ cycles/min}$$ > [!NOTE] > The OFF phase dominates because the pump is much stronger than gravity drainage. In real installations this asymmetry is corrected by **inlet throttling** to balance ON and OFF times, giving even contactor wear. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Ideal vs Practical On-Off | Property | Ideal On-Off (Δh = 0) | Practical On-Off (Δh > 0) | Continuous (P/PI) Controller | |----------|----------------------|---------------------------|-------------------------------| | Output States | 2 (theoretical) | 2 (with memory) | Infinite (analog) | | Steady-State Error | 0 (asymptotic) | 0 (statistical mean) | Non-zero for P; 0 for PI | | Cycling Frequency | Infinite (chatter) | Finite, bounded | 0 (no cycling) | | Contact Wear | Catastrophic | Acceptable | N/A (proportional valve) | | Cost | Lowest | Low | Moderate–High | | Tuning Required | None | Δh setting only | P, I, D constants | | Implementation Complexity | Trivial | Trivial | Moderate | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 System-Level Block Diagram — On-Off Level Control Loop ```mermaid flowchart LR SP[Set Point h_sp] --> SUM[ Summing Junction e = SP - PV] LT[Level Transmitter
Float or DP Cell] --> PV[Measured Level h] PV --> SUM SUM --> CON[On-Off Controller
with Differential Gap] CON -->|u = 100 percent| ACT[Actuator
Pump Motor Starter
or Solenoid Valve] ACT --> PROC[Tank Process
dh over dt = Fin - Fout over A] PROC --> LT PROC -.-> DIST[Disturbance
Outlet Demand Variation] DIST -.-> PROC style CON fill:#FFD966,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style PROC fill:#A9D08E,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style ACT fill:#F4B084,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.2 Functional Architecture of the Two-Position Decision Block ```mermaid flowchart TD IN1[Input: h measured] --> COMP1{Is h less than h L} COMP1 -->|Yes| ONSTATE[Output u = U max ON] COMP1 -->|No| COMP2{Is h greater than h H} COMP2 -->|Yes| OFFSTATE[Output u = U min OFF] COMP2 -->|No| MEM[HOLD Previous State
u = u previous] ONSTATE --> OUT[Output to Actuator] OFFSTATE --> OUT MEM --> OUT style ONSTATE fill:#C6EFCE,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style OFFSTATE fill:#FFC7CE,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px style MEM fill:#FFEB9C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px ``` ## 4.3 Hysteresis Loop — Level vs. Controller Output ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Level rising: h crosses h L
Controller still OFF] --> B[Level reaches h H
Controller switches OFF to ON] B --> C[Pump ON
Level begins falling] C --> D[Level drops to h L
Controller switches ON to OFF] D --> E[Level falling: h crosses h H
Controller still ON] E --> A style A fill:#DDEBF7,stroke:#333 style B fill:#F4B084,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style D fill:#F4B084,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reading the diagrams:** The transition **B** (rising trip) and **D** (falling trip) occur at *different* levels. The horizontal distance between B and D on the level axis is exactly the **differential gap Δh**. This hysteresis loop is the *signature* of two-position control. ## 4.4 Physical Reference Sketch (Text-Based Schematic) ``` | ← Tank wall | h_H |───────────────── ← Upper trip (pump OFF) | | | ~ ~ ~ ~ ~ ~ | ← Water surface oscillates | | h_sp |─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ─ ← Set point (target) | | | | h_L |───────────────── ← Lower trip (pump ON) | | |_________________| ← Tank base A (m²) ↙ ↘ F_out → → F_out (drain) (drain) ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define **On-Off control** with reference to industrial level applications. Why is a differential gap provided in practical implementations? **Model Answer:** On-Off control is a two-position discontinuous control mode in which the final control element is switched fully ON or fully OFF depending on whether the process variable is above or below the set point. In level control, when `h < h_sp`, the pump turns ON; when `h > h_sp`, the pump turns OFF. A **differential gap (Δh)** is provided to prevent rapid cycling (chattering) of the relay contacts around the set point. The gap decouples the ON and OFF switching levels, so the controller holds its last state while the level lies within the dead band, giving finite and acceptable cycling frequency. In practical terms, it protects the solenoid coils and contactors from rapid switching damage. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for level-specific application, 1 for justification of differential gap.)* ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** List **any four advantages** and **any two disadvantages** of on-off level control compared with continuous PID level control. **Model Answer:** **Advantages:** 1. Extremely simple to implement — a single relay and one level sensor. 2. Very low cost; no proportional valve required. 3. Zero tuning effort — only Δh needs to be set. 4. Zero long-term average error (statistical) for integrating processes like tanks. **Disadvantages:** 1. Continuous oscillation of the process variable within ±Δh/2 of the set point. 2. Cannot achieve tight level control required in critical processes (e.g., reflux drums, reactor level). *(3 marks: 1 mark per advantage × 2 + ½ mark per disadvantage × 2 = 3.)* --- ## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** *Answer any one full question from the choice provided. Each question carries 14 marks split as (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.* --- ### ✅ QUESTION A — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Adapted]** **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the working of an **On-Off level control system** for a process tank. Clearly mark the differential gap, set point, upper trip, and lower trip on your diagram. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A tank of cross-sectional area `A = 1.5 \text{ m}^2` is supplied by a pump that delivers `F_in = 0.04 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}` when ON. The drain is linear with resistance `R = 250 \text{ s/m}^2`. The lower trip is `h_L = 2.0 \text{ m}` and the upper trip is `h_H = 2.2 \text{ m}`. Calculate the **ON-time, OFF-time, total cycling period, and cycling frequency** of the controller. *(7 marks)* #### Model Solution — Part (a) 1. **Block diagram** (draw or refer to Section 4.1 of these notes): The loop consists of Set Point → Comparator → On-Off Controller (with Δh) → Pump Starter/Contactor → Tank Process → Level Transmitter (LT) → back to comparator. *(3 marks for diagram with all blocks labelled.)* 2. **Operating sequence:** When the level drops below `h_L`, the comparator output goes HIGH, energising the pump contactor. Water enters the tank, level rises. When the level crosses `h_H`, the controller output goes LOW, de-energising the contactor. The pump stops, and the level falls under gravity drain. The cycle repeats. *(2 marks for sequence.)* 3. **Differential gap explanation:** The region `[h_L, h_H]` is the dead band where the controller holds its previous state, ensuring finite cycling. *(2 marks.)* #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Given:** `A = 1.5 \text{ m}^2`, `F_in = 0.04 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}`, `R = 250 \text{ s/m}^2`, `h_L = 2.0 \text{ m}`, `h_H = 2.2 \text{ m}`. **Step 1 — Process gain constant:** $$K_{proc} = RF_{in} = 250 \times 0.04 = 10 \text{ m}$$ **Step 2 — Time constant:** $$\tau = AR = 1.5 \times 250 = 375 \text{ s}$$ **Step 3 — ON-time:** $$T_{ON} = \tau \ln\!\left(\dfrac{K_{proc} - h_L}{K_{proc} - h_H}\right) = 375 \ln\!\left(\dfrac{10 - 2.0}{10 - 2.2}\right)$$ $$T_{ON} = 375 \ln\!\left(\dfrac{8.0}{7.8}\right) = 375 \times \ln(1.02564) = 375 \times 0.02532 = 9.49 \text{ s}$$ **Step 4 — OFF-time:** $$T_{OFF} = \tau \ln\!\left(\dfrac{h_H}{h_L}\right) = 375 \ln\!\left(\dfrac{2.2}{2.0}\right) = 375 \times 0.09531 = 35.74 \text{ s}$$ **Step 5 — Total period:** $$T = T_{ON} + T_{OFF} = 9.49 + 35.74 = 45.23 \text{ s}$$ **Step 6 — Cycling frequency:** $$f = \dfrac{1}{T} = \dfrac{1}{45.23} = 0.02211 \text{ Hz} = 1.327 \text{ cycles/min}$$ *(7 marks: 1 for setup, 1 each for T_ON and T_OFF, 1 for T, 1 for f, 2 for correct unit handling and final answer presentation.)* --- ### ✅ QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]** **(a)** Explain the difference between **ideal (mathematical) on-off control** and **practical on-off control with differential gap**, using the controller output vs error characteristics. State the role of **hysteresis** in two-position control. *(7 marks)* **(b)** For a process tank under on-off level control, the pump delivers 30 L/min when ON, and the outlet draws a constant 15 L/min. The tank area is `0.5 \text{ m}^2`. The differential gap is set to **10 cm** with set point at 1.5 m. Calculate the approximate **cycling period and frequency** using the small-Δh approximation. Comment on whether this on-off strategy is suitable for a reflux drum of a distillation column. *(7 marks)* #### Model Solution — Part (a) 1. **Ideal On-Off (Δh = 0):** The output switches instantaneously between `U_max` and `U_min` at `e = 0`. The input-output graph is a vertical line with a single switching point. Mathematically, the gain at e = 0 is **infinite**, which is non-physical. *(2 marks.)* 2. **Practical On-Off (Δh > 0):** Output is `U_max` when `e ≥ +Δh/2` and `U_min` when `e ≤ −Δh/2`; in between, output holds its last state. The input-output characteristic is a **rectangular hysteresis loop**. *(2 marks.)* 3. **Role of hysteresis:** It establishes the **differential gap** that prevents relay chattering, ensures deterministic switching points, and protects electromechanical actuators. It is also called the **dead band** of the controller. *(3 marks.)* #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Given:** - `F_in = 30 \text{ L/min} = 0.030 \text{ m}^3/\text{min} = 0.0005 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}` (pump rate) - `F_out = 15 \text{ L/min} = 0.00025 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}` (constant draw) - `A = 0.5 \text{ m}^2` - `Δh = 0.10 \text{ m}` - `h_sp = 1.5 \text{ m}` **Step 1 — Apply small-Δh approximation:** $$T \approx \dfrac{2A \cdot \Delta h}{F_{in}}$$ **Step 2 — Plug values:** $$T = \dfrac{2 \times 0.5 \times 0.10}{0.0005} = \dfrac{0.10}{0.0005} = 200 \text{ s}$$ **Step 3 — Frequency:** $$f = \dfrac{1}{T} = \dfrac{1}{200} = 0.005 \text{ Hz} = 0.30 \text{ cycles/min}$$ **Step 4 — Amplitude of oscillation:** $$h(t) \in [1.45 \text{ m}, \; 1.55 \text{ m}]$$ **Step 5 — Suitability for reflux drum:** A reflux drum of a distillation column requires **very tight level control** (typically ±1–2 cm) because level fluctuations propagate to the reflux pump, disturbing column hydraulics and ultimately product composition. An oscillation of ±5 cm (i.e., 10% of the set point) is **unacceptable**. Therefore, **on-off control is NOT suitable** for a reflux drum — a **PI level controller with a pneumatic or electrical control valve** is mandatory. *(3 marks for the comment + reasoning.)* *(7 marks: 1 each for T, f, amplitude, and 3 for the qualitative discussion.)* --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students commonly lose marks in On-Off Level Control questions:** > 1. **Forgetting the natural logarithm in the T_ON derivation** — Writing `T_ON = AR × (h_H − h_L) / (...)` is the most common error. The mass-balance ODE is *first-order linear*, not algebraic, so the solution is exponential. Always integrate to get `ln(...)`. > 2. **Ignoring the held-state (memory) region in the controller output** — Examiners specifically test whether you know that between `h_L` and `h_H`, the output *retains* its previous value, not that it snaps to a default. > 3. **Confusing "differential gap" with "dead zone" of a PID controller** — The differential gap is a property of the *relay*, not the *process*. Do not write `Δh = e_ss`. > 4. **Mixing units** — In numerical problems, convert L/min to m³/s *before* using the formula. A 30% mark deduction applies in Section (b) if the unit conversion is missing. > 5. **Drawing the hysteresis loop as a single line** — It is a *rectangle* (or a stepped function), not a straight line. Examiners award 2 marks specifically for the correct loop shape. --- ## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **On-Off Level Control** is a two-position discontinuous control mode used widely for non-critical tank-level applications. - The output of the controller is **strictly binary**: pump fully ON or fully OFF (or valve fully open / fully closed). - **Differential gap (Δh)** is an *intentional* hysteresis band that prevents relay chattering; it is set symmetrically about the set point. - The tank's mass-balance ODE is **first-order linear** with time constant `τ = AR`, and integrating this gives the cycling period as a *logarithmic* expression of the trip levels. - **Cycling period (small-Δh form):** `T ≈ 2A·Δh / F_in` — the most-tested compact formula in KTU exams. - The process variable (level) **oscillates continuously** within `[h_L, h_H]`; the long-term mean equals the set point, so **statistical steady-state error is zero**. - **Mean level:** `h̄ = (h_H + h_L) / 2` — should equal `h_sp` for symmetric differential gap. - **Cycling frequency** is the inverse of the period; high cycling frequency damages relay contacts — keep `Δh` sufficiently large. - **Major limitation:** Cannot be used in applications demanding tight level control (reflux drums, reactor level, boiler drum at high pressure). - **Common sensors:** Float switches, capacitance probes, ultrasonic, differential pressure (DP) cells, guided-wave radar. - **Common actuators:** Solenoid valves, magnetic contactors, motor starters, pneumatically-actuated on-off valves. - **Block diagram essentials for the exam:** Set Point → Comparator → On-Off Controller (with Δh) → Actuator → Tank → Sensor → back to comparator (closed loop). - **In the Mermaid / sketch:** Always show the differential gap as a *gap between the upper and lower trip lines*, and the level as a wavy line oscillating between them. - **Industry-standard rule of thumb:** Set `Δh` between **2% and 5%** of the set point for water storage tanks; larger for slow processes, smaller for fast processes.

topics.title: On-Off Flow Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT743 - INDUSTRIAL PROCESS CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** On-Off Flow Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**On-Off Flow Control** (also called **Two-Position Flow Control** or **Bang-Bang Control**) is a discontinuous control strategy in which the final control element (typically a solenoid valve or a centrifugal pump) assumes only **two discrete operating states**:

- **Fully ON (100% controller output)**: when the process variable (flow rate) falls *below* the setpoint minus the neutral zone.
- **Fully OFF (0% controller output)**: when the process variable (flow rate) rises *above* the setpoint plus the neutral zone.

The controller output $m(t)$ is mathematically defined as a discontinuous function of the error $e(t) = SP - PV$:

$$m(t) = \begin{cases} M_{max} = 100\% & \text{if } e(t) \ge +\delta \\ 0 & \text{if } e(t) \le -\delta \\ \text{Hold Last State} & \text{if } \vert e(t) \vert < \delta \end{cases}$$

where:
- $SP$ = Setpoint (desired flow rate in m³/hr)
- $PV$ = Process Variable (measured flow rate)
- $\delta$ = Half-width of the **Neutral Zone (NZ)** or **Differential Gap (DG)**
- Total Neutral Zone = $2\delta$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the terms **Neutral Zone (NZ)**, **Differential Gap (DG)**, and **Dead Band (DB)** are used interchangeably for two-position controllers. Always specify whether you mean the **half-width** $\delta$ or the **total width** $2\delta$ in numerical answers.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **domestic water pump** connected to an overhead tank with a float switch. The pump has only two modes: **ON** (water gushing in) or **OFF** (dead silent). It does not throttle to 50% power.

- When the water level in the tank falls below the lower float (the setpoint minus $\delta$), the float switch closes an electrical contact — the pump **SNAPS ON** at full speed.
- When the water rises above the upper float (setpoint plus $\delta$), the switch opens — the pump **CLICKS OFF** instantly.
- Between the two floats (the **neutral zone**), the pump holds its last state. This prevents the pump from rapidly cycling ON-OFF-ON-OFF every second due to small ripples in the tank.

**In flow control, the same idea applies**: instead of a tank, we control the flow rate $F$ in a pipe. Instead of a float, we use a **flow transmitter (orifice + DP cell, magnetic flowmeter, or Coriolis meter)**. Instead of a pump, we use a **solenoid valve** or a **fixed-speed pump**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is On-Off Control so popular for flow loops?** Flow processes are intrinsically **fast loops** with large process gains and small time constants (often $T_p < 1$ second). Continuous PID control of such fast loops can cause excessive valve wear and high-frequency oscillations. The simple, robust On-Off controller handles them elegantly.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Metric | KTU Board Notation |
|---|---|---|
| Flow rate | $\text{m}^3/\text{hr}$ (Q) or Litres/min (LPM) | $F$ or $Q$ |
| Setpoint | % of full scale or engineering units (e.g., m³/hr) | $SP$ |
| Process Variable | Same units as $SP$ | $PV$ or $F_m$ |
| Error signal | $e = SP - PV$ (same units) | $e(t)$ |
| Neutral Zone half-width | % of span or engineering units | $\delta$ or $DB/2$ |
| Cycle Time | seconds (s) | $T_c$ |
| Frequency of cycling | Hz = $1/T_c$ | $f_c$ |
| Process gain (flow loops) | Typically $\mathbf{high}$ (1 to 10 %PV/%CO) | $K_p$ |
| Process time constant | Typically $\mathbf{low}$ (0.1 to 5 s) | $T_p$ or $\tau$ |

> [!TIP]
> A reliable KTU board-exam mnemonic: **"Flow is fast, Level is slow, Temperature is slower, Pressure is in-between."** This is the order in which On-Off control is practically used.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of the Two-Position Flow Controller

The On-Off controller is a **nonlinear, discontinuous** controller. Its behaviour can be broken into the following structured logic steps:

### Step 1: Error Generation
The measured flow $PV$ from the flow transmitter is compared with the setpoint $SP$.

$$e(t) = SP - PV(t)$$

A **positive error** means flow is **too low**; a **negative error** means flow is **too high**.

### Step 2: Decision Based on the Neutral Zone
The controller checks the magnitude of the error against the pre-set neutral-zone half-width $\delta$.

- If $e(t) \ge +\delta$ → flow is dangerously low → open the valve fully → $m(t) = 100\%$
- If $e(t) \le -\delta$ → flow is dangerously high → close the valve fully → $m(t) = 0\%$
- If $\vert e(t) \vert < \delta$ → flow is acceptably close to setpoint → **do nothing** (hold last output)

### Step 3: Actuation
The 100% or 0% output is sent to the **solenoid-actuated on-off valve** or the **motor starter** of the pump. The final control element is bistable.

### Step 4: Process Response
The flow responds rapidly (fast process). Once $PV$ crosses the setpoint boundary, the controller re-evaluates and toggles its output — completing **one cycle**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" of the Neutral Zone:** A pure two-position controller *without* a neutral zone would cause the final element to chatter at theoretically infinite frequency around the setpoint. The neutral zone **deliberately introduces a tolerance band** to limit the cycling rate to a finite, acceptable value, thereby **prolonging the life of the solenoid and the valve seat**.

## 2.2 Hysteresis vs. Dead Band — A Critical Distinction

In many KTU question papers, students confuse **Hysteresis** with **Dead Band (DB)**. Let us settle this once and for all:

- **Dead Band (DB) or Neutral Zone**: A *symmetric* tolerance band around the setpoint within which **no change in output** occurs. It is a property of the controller's design.
- **Hysteresis**: The output-switching point *depends on the direction of motion* of the error. For example, the valve may turn ON at $e = +0.5$ but turn OFF at $e = -0.3$ due to mechanical friction or magnetic latch behaviour.

A controller with a pure DB and **no hysteresis** is called an **ideal two-position controller**. A controller exhibiting hysteresis is called a **two-position controller with differential gap**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **definitive formula sheet** for On-Off Flow Control questions in KTU 2024 Scheme. Bookmark it.

| # | Formula / Equation | Meaning of Symbols | Typical Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $e(t) = SP - PV(t)$ | Error signal | m³/hr | First step of every control problem |
| 2 | $m(t) = M_{max}$ if $e \ge +\delta$, else $0$ if $e \le -\delta$ | Controller output law | % (0 or 100) | Defines two-position action |
| 3 | $DB = 2\delta$ | Total dead band | m³/hr or % span | Symmetric neutral zone |
| 4 | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{T_c}$ | Cycling frequency | Hz (cycles per second) | Determines valve wear rate |
| 5 | $T_c = T_{on} + T_{off}$ | Total cycle time | seconds (s) | Sum of ON and OFF durations |
| 6 | $PV(t) = PV_{ss} \cdot (1 - e^{-t/\tau_p})$ | First-order flow response (charging) | m³/hr | Approximate flow rise dynamics |
| 7 | $PV(t) = PV_{ss} \cdot e^{-t/\tau_p}$ | First-order flow response (discharging) | m³/hr | Approximate flow decay dynamics |
| 8 | $\tau_p = R \cdot C$ | Process time constant = Resistance × Capacitance | s | For flow: $R = \Delta P / \Delta F$, $C = A_{pipe}$ |
| 9 | $K_p = \dfrac{\Delta PV}{\Delta m}$ | Process gain | %PV/%CO | High for flow (≈ 1 to 10) |
| 10 | $T_c \approx 2 \cdot \tau_p \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1 + DB/2}{1 - DB/2}\right)$ | Approx. cycle time for symmetric DB | s | Estimating solenoid life |
| 11 | $f_c \approx \dfrac{1}{2\tau_p \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1+DB/2}{1-DB/2}\right)}$ | Approx. cycling frequency | Hz | Frequencies above 1 Hz damage solenoids |
| 12 | $D = \dfrac{PV_{max} - PV_{min}}{PV_{avg}} \times 100$ | % Overshoot / Damping indicator | % | Quality of cycling behaviour |

> **Critical LaTeX note for KTU board answers:** Always use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute value. For example, write $\vert e(t) \vert$ as $\lvert e(t) \rvert$ or use the command `\vert e(t) \vert`. Avoid raw `|...|` which breaks markdown tables.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

On-Off flow control is the **workhorse** of:

1. **Domestic water booster pumps** — controlled by pressure switches with a fixed dead band (typically 1 to 2 bar).
2. **HVAC chilled-water circulation** — solenoid valves gate water flow to cooling coils based on room temperature.
3. **Chemical dosing systems** — fixed-volume chemical injection via timed solenoid pulses.
4. **Oil & gas well-head control** — emergency shutdown valves (ESDVs) that snap shut on overpressure.
5. **Irrigation systems** — solenoid valves on lateral pipes opened/closed by a simple timer or moisture sensor.
6. **Pharmaceutical batch reactors** — when precise stoichiometry is not critical, solenoid-driven reagent flow is preferred for its **simplicity and fail-safe nature**.

> [!TIP]
> **Fail-safe rule:** If the power fails, an On-Off solenoid valve is usually designed to be either **Normally Closed (NC)** (fail-safe for supply-line flow) or **Normally Open (NO)** (fail-safe for cooling-water lines). KTU examiners love this design question.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Cycle Time $T_c$ for a First-Order Flow Process

### Setup
Consider a flow process approximated as a **first-order system** with process time constant $\tau_p$ and process gain $K_p$. Let the controller have a symmetric dead band of total width $DB = 2\delta$ around the setpoint $SP$.

The controller output switches between $0\%$ and $100\%$. The measured flow $PV(t)$ therefore rises or decays exponentially.

### Step 1: Charging Equation (Valve Open)
When the valve opens, the flow rises from its lower boundary $PV_{low} = SP - \delta$ toward its steady-state value $PV_{ss,on}$. The first-order rise equation is:

$$PV(t) = PV_{ss,on} - (PV_{ss,on} - PV_{low}) \cdot e^{-t/\tau_p}$$

> *Logic*: At $t = 0$, $PV = PV_{low}$. As $t \to \infty$, $PV \to PV_{ss,on}$. The exponential term represents the gap between current value and final value.

### Step 2: Time to Reach Upper Boundary
The controller turns OFF when $PV(t)$ reaches the upper boundary $PV_{high} = SP + \delta$. Setting $PV(T_{on}) = PV_{high}$:

$$PV_{high} = PV_{ss,on} - (PV_{ss,on} - PV_{low}) \cdot e^{-T_{on}/\tau_p}$$

Rearranging:

$$e^{-T_{on}/\tau_p} = \dfrac{PV_{ss,on} - PV_{high}}{PV_{ss,on} - PV_{low}}$$

Taking natural log of both sides:

$$-\dfrac{T_{on}}{\tau_p} = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{PV_{ss,on} - PV_{high}}{PV_{ss,on} - PV_{low}}\right)$$

Therefore:

$$T_{on} = \tau_p \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{PV_{ss,on} - PV_{low}}{PV_{ss,on} - PV_{high}}\right)$$

### Step 3: Discharge Equation (Valve Closed)
When the valve closes, the flow decays from $PV_{high}$ toward $PV_{ss,off}$ (often 0 if no other inflow). The first-order decay equation is:

$$PV(t) = PV_{ss,off} + (PV_{high} - PV_{ss,off}) \cdot e^{-t/\tau_p}$$

### Step 4: Time to Reach Lower Boundary
Setting $PV(T_{off}) = PV_{low}$:

$$PV_{low} = PV_{ss,off} + (PV_{high} - PV_{ss,off}) \cdot e^{-T_{off}/\tau_p}$$

Solving for $T_{off}$:

$$T_{off} = \tau_p \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{PV_{high} - PV_{ss,off}}{PV_{low} - PV_{ss,off}}\right)$$

### Step 5: Total Cycle Time
$$T_c = T_{on} + T_{off} = \tau_p \left[ \ln\!\left(\dfrac{PV_{ss,on} - PV_{low}}{PV_{ss,on} - PV_{high}}\right) + \ln\!\left(\dfrac{PV_{high} - PV_{ss,off}}{PV_{low} - PV_{ss,off}}\right) \right]$$

### Step 6: Simplification for Symmetric Dead Band
If we assume the process has equal magnitudes of rise and fall (e.g., $PV_{ss,on} - SP = SP - PV_{ss,off}$), and the dead band is symmetric ($\delta$ on each side), the expression collapses to the **canonical KTU formula**:

$$\boxed{\,T_c \approx 2 \cdot \tau_p \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1 + \delta / \Delta}{1 - \delta / \Delta}\right)\,}$$

where $\Delta$ is the half-range of process variable variation (i.e., $(PV_{ss,on} - PV_{ss,off})/2$). In normalized form, if the dead band is expressed as a fraction of span $\beta = DB / \text{Span}$:

$$T_c \approx 2 \cdot \tau_p \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1 + \beta}{1 - \beta}\right)$$

### Step 7: Numerical Illustration
Let $\tau_p = 5$ s, $\beta = 0.1$ (i.e., 10% dead band).

$$\begin{aligned}
T_c &\approx 2 \times 5 \times \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1 + 0.1}{1 - 0.1}\right) \\
&= 10 \times \ln(1.2222) \\
&= 10 \times 0.2007 \\
&= 2.007 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}$$

So $f_c = 1/T_c \approx 0.498$ Hz, or roughly 30 cycles per minute — within the acceptable range for industrial solenoids.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: How the Cycle Time Changes with Parameters

| Parameter Change | Effect on $T_c$ | Effect on $f_c$ | Effect on Valve Wear |
|---|---|---|---|
| Increase $\tau_p$ (slower process) | $T_c$ increases | $f_c$ decreases | Less wear ✓ |
| Increase $DB$ (wider neutral zone) | $T_c$ increases | $f_c$ decreases | Less wear ✓ |
| Increase $K_p$ (more sensitive process) | $T_c$ decreases | $f_c$ increases | More wear ✗ |
| Add hysteresis (asymmetric DB) | $T_{on} \ne T_{off}$ → irregular cycles | Variable | Variable |
| Pure on-off (zero DB) | $T_c \to 0$ | $f_c \to \infty$ | **Catastrophic wear ✗✗** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Pitfall:** A *zero* dead band is **physically unrealistic** in practice. Any answer claiming a theoretically infinite cycling frequency will receive **partial credit only** unless you explicitly justify why it is unattainable (solenoid response time, process lag).

## 3.3 Worked Example (Board-Style)

**Problem:** A water flow loop has process gain $K_p = 2$ %PV/%CO, time constant $\tau_p = 3$ s, and a controller setpoint of $SP = 50$ % of scale. The On-Off controller has a dead band of $DB = \pm 4$ % of scale. The valve output is either 0% or 100%. Estimate the cycling frequency and the total cycle time.

**Solution:**

Step 1 — Normalize the dead band:
$$\beta = \dfrac{DB/2}{100} = \dfrac{4}{100} = 0.04$$

Step 2 — Apply the canonical cycle time formula:
$$\begin{aligned}
T_c &\approx 2 \cdot \tau_p \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1 + \beta}{1 - \beta}\right) \\
&= 2 \times 3 \times \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1.04}{0.96}\right) \\
&= 6 \times \ln(1.0833) \\
&= 6 \times 0.0800 \\
&= 0.480 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}$$

Step 3 — Cycling frequency:
$$f_c = \dfrac{1}{T_c} = \dfrac{1}{0.480} \approx 2.083 \text{ Hz}$$

Step 4 — Engineering interpretation:
A cycling frequency above 2 Hz is **too fast** for most industrial solenoids (typical rating: 1 Hz max). The remedy: **widen the dead band** to $\pm 10\%$ (i.e., $\beta = 0.10$).

> This is exactly the kind of design-then-evaluate question KTU 2024 ESE papers love.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Closed-Loop On-Off Flow Control System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP[Setpoint SP] --> SUM[Summing Junction]
    FT[Flow Transmitter] --> PV[Process Variable PV]
    PV --> SUM
    SUM -->|e(t)| NZE[Neutral Zone Element]
    NZE -->|m(t) 0 or 100%| SOL[Solenoid Valve or Pump]
    SOL --> PROC[Flow Process]
    PROC --> FT
    PROC -.feedback.-> FT
```

**Functional Walkthrough of the Block Diagram:**

1. The operator (or upstream controller) sets the **Setpoint (SP)** — the desired flow rate in m³/hr.
2. The **Flow Transmitter (FT)** — typically an orifice + DP cell, magnetic flowmeter, or vortex meter — measures the actual flow rate in the pipe and outputs a proportional signal (4-20 mA or 3-15 psi pneumatic).
3. The **Summing Junction (SUM)** computes the error: $e(t) = SP - PV$.
4. The **Neutral Zone Element (NZE)** is the heart of the On-Off controller. It applies the dead-band logic and outputs either 0% or 100%.
5. The **Solenoid Valve** (or pump starter) is the final control element. It is bistable: either fully open or fully closed.
6. The **Flow Process (PROC)** responds rapidly (small $\tau_p$) to the valve position, and the new measured flow feeds back to the transmitter.

## 4.2 State-Transition Diagram of the On-Off Controller

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> ValveClosed
    ValveClosed --> ValveOpen : error >= +delta
    ValveOpen --> ValveClosed : error <= -delta
    ValveOpen --> ValveOpen : error in neutral zone hold last
    ValveClosed --> ValveClosed : error in neutral zone hold last
```

**Interpretation of the State Diagram:**

- The controller has exactly **two states**: `ValveClosed` and `ValveOpen`.
- Transitions occur **only** when the error crosses the threshold $\pm \delta$.
- Within the neutral zone, the controller **holds** its current state (no toggling).
- This discrete-state machine behaviour is the mathematical essence of the On-Off controller.

## 4.3 Functional Architecture Flow for Industrial On-Off Flow Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CONTROL_PANEL[Control Panel Layer]
        SP_KNOB[Setpoint Knob] --> CONT[On-Off Controller with DB]
    end
    subgraph FIELD[Field Instrumentation Layer]
        CONT --> SOL_VLV[Solenoid Valve]
        SOL_VLV --> PIPE[Flow Pipe with Process Fluid]
        PIPE --> MAGMFM[Magnetic Flowmeter]
        MAGMFM --> CONT
    end
    subgraph PROTECTION[Safety and Diagnostics Layer]
        CONT --> ALARM[High Low Flow Alarm]
        CONT --> SOL_STATE[Valve State Indicator ON OFF]
    end
```

**Block-Level Functional Architecture Explanation:**

- **Control Panel Layer:** Houses the operator interface and the controller hardware. The controller chip implements the dead-band logic in firmware.
- **Field Instrumentation Layer:** The solenoid valve and flowmeter form the physical closed loop. The flowmeter's signal is fed back to the controller input.
- **Safety and Diagnostics Layer:** Alarms and indicators are *additive* to the basic On-Off loop — they do not participate in the control law but inform the operator.

## 4.4 Typical PV vs. Time Response (Sawtooth Waveform)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Valve Opens PV rises exponentially toward PVss on] --> B[PV crosses upper boundary Controller OFF]
    B --> C[PV decays exponentially toward PVss off]
    C --> D[PV crosses lower boundary Controller ON]
    D --> A
```

> **Visual note:** The resulting $PV(t)$ waveform is a **sawtooth-like** oscillation centred around the setpoint, with peaks at $SP + \delta$ and troughs at $SP - \delta$. The slope of each segment depends on the process time constant $\tau_p$.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define On-Off (Two-Position) control. Explain the role of the neutral zone in such a controller.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

On-Off (Two-Position) control is a discontinuous control strategy in which the final control element is driven to one of two extreme positions — fully ON or fully OFF — depending on the sign of the error $e(t) = SP - PV$.

The **neutral zone (or dead band)** is a symmetric tolerance band of total width $DB = 2\delta$ around the setpoint. When the error lies within this band, the controller holds its last output and does not toggle. The neutral zone **prevents excessive cycling** of the final control element, prolongs solenoid life, and reduces wear. It is essential in practice because a zero dead band would cause the valve to chatter at infinite frequency — physically impossible and harmful to the equipment. **(3 marks)**

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Why is On-Off control particularly suitable for flow control loops compared to temperature or composition loops?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Flow loops are intrinsically **fast processes** with large process gain $K_p$ and small time constant $\tau_p$ (often < 1 second). Their dynamics are dominated by the fluid inertia and pipe resistance, with negligible capacitance delay. On-Off control works best on such fast loops because:

1. The rapid response of the flow variable limits the deviation from setpoint, even with discontinuous actuation.
2. Continuous (PID) control of such fast loops can cause valve wear and high-frequency oscillation, which On-Off control avoids.
3. The cost and simplicity of solenoid valves and fixed-speed pumps make On-Off economically attractive for flow.

In contrast, temperature and composition loops are slow (large $\tau_p$), so On-Off control would produce large, slow oscillations and significant deviation from the setpoint. **(3 marks)**

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a closed-loop **On-Off flow control system** for a chemical dosing pump. Label all blocks clearly and describe the function of each block. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **cycle time $T_c$** of a two-position controller applied to a first-order process with a symmetric dead band. Hence, compute $T_c$ and the cycling frequency $f_c$ for a flow loop with $\tau_p = 4$ s and a dead band of $\pm 5\%$ of span. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

#### Part (a) — Block Diagram and Operation (7 marks)

**Block Diagram** (as in Section 4.1, reproduced here for completeness):

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP[Setpoint SP] --> SUM[Summing Junction]
    FT[Flow Transmitter] --> PV[Process Variable PV]
    PV --> SUM
    SUM -->|e(t)| NZE[Neutral Zone Element]
    NZE -->|m(t) 0 or 100%| SOL[Solenoid Valve or Pump]
    SOL --> PROC[Flow Process]
    PROC --> FT
```

**Operation (function of each block):**

1. **Setpoint (SP) Block:** Represents the desired flow rate entered by the operator. For a chemical dosing pump, this is typically the required chemical feed rate in LPH. **(1 mark)**
2. **Flow Transmitter (FT):** Measures the actual flow rate in the dosing line. Common types: magnetic flowmeter (for conductive chemicals), Coriolis meter (for mass flow), or positive-displacement meter. Output is a standardized 4-20 mA signal proportional to flow. **(1 mark)**
3. **Summing Junction (SUM):** Computes the error signal $e(t) = SP - PV$. This is the heart of feedback control. **(1 mark)**
4. **Neutral Zone Element (NZE):** The On-Off controller core. Implements the two-position logic:
   - If $e \ge +\delta$ → output = 100% (turn on pump)
   - If $e \le -\delta$ → output = 0% (turn off pump)
   - If $\vert e \vert < \delta$ → hold last state.
   **(1 mark)**
5. **Solenoid Valve / Pump Starter (SOL):** The final control element. A bistable device that either delivers 100% or 0% of the maximum flow. For a dosing pump, this is typically a fixed-speed peristaltic pump or a solenoid-actuated pinch valve. **(1 mark)**
6. **Flow Process (PROC):** The chemical dosing line — pipe, fittings, and the receiving tank. The process exhibits first-order dynamics with gain $K_p$ and time constant $\tau_p = R \cdot C$, where $R$ is fluid resistance and $C$ is line capacitance. **(1 mark)**
7. **Closed Loop:** The measured flow is continuously fed back to the summing junction, completing the negative-feedback loop. Any deviation is corrected by snapping the pump ON or OFF. **(1 mark)**

---

#### Part (b) — Derivation and Numerical Evaluation (7 marks)

**Derivation** (as derived in Section 3.1, summarised here):

For a first-order process with steady-state values $PV_{ss,on}$ (valve open) and $PV_{ss,off}$ (valve closed), and boundaries $PV_{high} = SP + \delta$, $PV_{low} = SP - \delta$:

$$T_{on} = \tau_p \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{PV_{ss,on} - PV_{low}}{PV_{ss,on} - PV_{high}}\right) \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$

$$T_{off} = \tau_p \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{PV_{high} - PV_{ss,off}}{PV_{low} - PV_{ss,off}}\right) \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$

For a symmetric dead band and symmetric process swing, the sum simplifies to:

$$T_c = 2 \cdot \tau_p \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1 + \beta}{1 - \beta}\right) \quad \text{where } \beta = \dfrac{\delta}{\Delta} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

**Numerical Evaluation** (with $\tau_p = 4$ s, $\beta = 0.05$):

$$\begin{aligned}
T_c &= 2 \times 4 \times \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1 + 0.05}{1 - 0.05}\right) \\
&= 8 \times \ln(1.1053) \\
&= 8 \times 0.1001 \\
&= 0.8008 \text{ s} \quad \text{(1 mark)} \\[6pt]
f_c &= \dfrac{1}{T_c} = \dfrac{1}{0.8008} \approx 1.249 \text{ Hz} \quad \text{(1 mark)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Engineering Comment:** A cycling frequency of ~1.25 Hz is acceptable for industrial solenoids but is on the higher side. If the operator observes premature solenoid failure, the dead band should be widened to $\pm 10\%$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` *(Module Internal Choice Alternative)*

**(a)** Compare **On-Off control** with **Proportional (P) control** for a flow loop. Discuss three advantages and two disadvantages of each strategy. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An On-Off flow controller with a dead band of $\pm 3\%$ is used to control flow in a pipe. The flow process is first-order with $\tau_p = 6$ s. The maximum flow on valve opening is 100% of scale, and the minimum flow on valve closing is 0%. Sketch the steady-state $PV(t)$ waveform over two full cycles, marking the upper and lower boundaries and the setpoint. Compute $T_c$, $f_c$, and the percentage overshoot $D$. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### Part (a) — Comparison of On-Off vs. P Control (7 marks)

| Feature | On-Off Control | Proportional Control |
|---|---|---|
| Controller output | Two values: 0% or 100% | Continuous, proportional to error |
| Steady-state error | Averages to zero (cycling) | Non-zero offset (proportional band) |
| Stability | Inherently stable but oscillatory | Can be tuned stable, but may oscillate |
| Cost | Very low (relay + solenoid) | Moderate (electronic controller + I/P converter) |
| Final element wear | High (frequent cycling) | Moderate (smaller, smoother movements) |
| Accuracy near setpoint | Limited by dead band | Better, governed by proportional band |
| Suitability for flow | Excellent (fast process) | Excellent (with proper tuning) |
| Implementation complexity | Trivial | Requires tuning of $K_c$ |

**Advantages of On-Off:** **(3 marks)**
1. Extremely simple and cheap to implement — a single relay and solenoid suffice.
2. Fail-safe in emergencies — the valve either fully opens or fully closes on signal loss.
3. Very fast response to large disturbances; no integral windup issues.

**Disadvantages of On-Off:** **(2 marks)**
1. Continuous cycling around the setpoint causes wear on the final control element.
2. Cannot achieve tight control — error can persist up to the dead-band width.

**Advantages of P Control:** **(1 mark)**
1. Smoother control action; less wear on the final control element.
2. No inherent cycling at the setpoint.

**Disadvantages of P Control:** **(1 mark)**
1. Introduces a steady-state offset that requires integral action to remove.
2. Tuning of $K_c$ is required; poor tuning causes instability.

---

#### Part (b) — Waveform Sketch and Numerical Evaluation (7 marks)

**Sketch of the steady-state PV(t) waveform:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Valve Opens PV rises toward 100 percent] --> B[PV hits upper boundary 53 percent Controller OFF]
    B --> C[PV decays toward 0 percent]
    C --> D[PV hits lower boundary 47 percent Controller ON]
    D --> A
```

The waveform is a **sawtooth-like oscillation** with peaks at 53% of scale and troughs at 47% of scale, centred on the 50% setpoint. Each charging segment is an exponential approach to 100%, and each discharge segment is an exponential decay to 0%.

**Numerical Evaluation:**

Given: $\tau_p = 6$ s, $DB = \pm 3\%$, so $\delta = 3\%$ and $\beta = 0.03$.

$$\begin{aligned}
T_c &= 2 \cdot \tau_p \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1 + \beta}{1 - \beta}\right) \\
&= 2 \times 6 \times \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1.03}{0.97}\right) \\
&= 12 \times \ln(1.0619) \\
&= 12 \times 0.0601 \\
&= 0.7208 \text{ s} \quad \text{(2 marks)} \\[6pt]
f_c &= \dfrac{1}{0.7208} \approx 1.387 \text{ Hz} \quad \text{(1 mark)} \\[6pt]
D &= \dfrac{PV_{max} - PV_{min}}{PV_{avg}} \times 100 = \dfrac{53 - 47}{50} \times 100 = 12\% \quad \text{(2 marks)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Engineering Comment:** The cycling frequency of 1.387 Hz is moderately high. The 12% peak-to-peak variation is the *minimum possible* with this dead band — it cannot be reduced without widening $DB$. **(2 marks for the engineering interpretation)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting units** in the final answer: $T_c$ must be in **seconds**, $f_c$ in **Hz**. Marks are deducted for unit-less answers.
> 2. **Confusing half-width and total width of DB**: If the question says "dead band = 4%", use $\beta = 0.04$, not $\beta = 0.02$. Read carefully.
> 3. **Ignoring the natural log**: Many students write $T_c = 2\tau_p \cdot \dfrac{1+\beta}{1-\beta}$ instead of the logarithm. This is a **1-mark deduction** minimum.
> 4. **Skipping the block diagram in 7-mark theory questions**: Even if the question is theory, a diagram carries **at least 1-2 marks** in KTU valuation.
> 5. **Not justifying the choice of On-Off vs PID**: Always state *why* On-Off is suitable (fast process) before defining it.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for On-Off Flow Control**

- **Definition:** Two-position discontinuous control with output = 0% or 100%. **(High priority)**
- **Dead Band (DB) = $2\delta$**: Symmetric neutral zone. **(High priority)**
- **Why DB exists:** Prevents infinite cycling; saves the solenoid. **(High priority)**
- **Three branches of the control law:** $e \ge +\delta \to$ ON; $e \le -\delta \to$ OFF; $\vert e \vert < \delta \to$ HOLD. **(High priority)**
- **Cycle time formula:** $T_c = 2 \tau_p \ln\!\left(\dfrac{1+\beta}{1-\beta}\right)$ where $\beta = \delta / \Delta$. **(High priority)**
- **Cycling frequency:** $f_c = 1/T_c$. **(High priority)**
- **Flow loops are fast:** Small $\tau_p$, high $K_p$, hence On-Off is the natural choice. **(Medium priority)**
- **Fail-safe design:** Solenoid valves are NC (Normally Closed) or NO (Normally Open). **(Medium priority)**
- **Hysteresis vs DB:** Hysteresis is direction-dependent; DB is symmetric and direction-independent. **(Medium priority)**
- **First-order process approximation:** $PV(t) = PV_{ss} - (PV_{ss} - PV_0)e^{-t/\tau_p}$ for charging; $PV(t) = PV_{ss} \cdot e^{-t/\tau_p}$ for discharging. **(Medium priority)**
- **% Overshoot / Damping:** $D = \dfrac{PV_{max} - PV_{min}}{PV_{avg}} \times 100\%$. **(Low priority)**
- **Block diagram:** Always draw setpoint, comparator, controller with DB, solenoid, process, and transmitter in a closed loop. **(High priority for diagrams)**
- **KTU Mnemonic:** "Flow is fast → use On-Off; Temperature is slow → use PID." **(High priority)**
- **Cycle frequency above 1 Hz damages solenoids.** Widening the DB reduces $f_c$. **(High priority for design questions)**

> **Final Tip:** In KTU 2024 Scheme ESE papers, On-Off control is often the *introductory* question of Module 4 because it is conceptually simple but mathematically rich. Master the cycle-time derivation and the block diagram — they appear in nearly every past paper.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:

> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** On-Off Pressure Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **On-Off Pressure Control (Two-Position Control):** A discontinuous control mode in which the controller output can assume only **two discrete states** — fully **ON** (Maximum Manipulated Output, $M_{max}$) or fully **OFF** (Minimum Manipulated Output, $M_{min}$) — based on whether the measured process variable (pressure, $P$) is **above** or **below** the **setpoint** ($P_{sp}$).

In the KTU 2024 Scheme classification, On-Off control is the simplest member of the **discontinuous controller family** (which also includes Multi-Position and Floating controllers). It is widely used in:

- Domestic water pump pressure switches
- Air compressor cut-out systems
- HVAC duct pressure regulation
- Boiler steam pressure safety interlocks
- Refrigeration high/low pressure cut-offs

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Domestic Water Pump Pressure Switch:**
> Imagine a small pressure pump in your home. A pressure switch is mounted on the pipeline:
> - When tank pressure drops **below 1.5 bar**, the switch **closes** → pump **turns ON** (full 100% power).
> - When tank pressure rises **above 3.0 bar**, the switch **opens** → pump **turns OFF** (0% power).
> - The pump oscillates between these two states — it never runs at "half speed."
> 
> This is On-Off control. The band between 1.5 bar and 3.0 bar is called the **Differential Gap (Dead Band)**.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Syllabus-Standard)

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Setpoint | $P_{sp}$ | Desired pressure value |
| Measured Variable | $P_{PV}$ | Actual process pressure |
| Error | $e(t)$ | $P_{sp} - P_{PV}$ |
| Differential Gap (Dead Band) | $\delta$ | Hysteresis band around setpoint |
| Upper Trip Point | $P_{sp} + \delta/2$ | Pressure at which output switches OFF |
| Lower Trip Point | $P_{sp} - \delta/2$ | Pressure at which output switches ON |
| Manipulated Output | $m(t)$ | Either $M_{max}$ or $M_{min}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Dead Band ($\delta$) — KTU Critical Concept:** Without a differential gap, the controller would "chatter" — switching ON/OFF hundreds of times per second as the error hovers near zero. The dead band **prevents mechanical wear** and **contact arcing** in real switches. It is the **single most important parameter** in On-Off controller design.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Representation of On-Off Controller

The On-Off controller with dead band can be expressed mathematically as a **piecewise discontinuous function**:

$$
m(t) = \begin{cases} M_{max} & \text{if } e(t) \geq +\delta/2 \\[4pt] M_{min} & \text{if } e(t) \leq -\delta/2 \\[4pt] m(t^{-}) & \text{if } \vert e(t) \vert < \delta/2 \end{cases}
$$

Where:
- $m(t^{-})$ denotes the **previous output state** (memory effect / hysteresis)
- $e(t) = P_{sp} - P_{PV}(t)$
- $\delta$ is the differential gap (in engineering units of pressure: bar, psi, kPa)

## 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Reasoning

1. **Sensing Stage:** The pressure transmitter continuously measures $P_{PV}$ and compares it to $P_{sp}$.
2. **Error Computation:** Error $e(t) = P_{sp} - P_{PV}(t)$ is computed.
3. **Decision Logic:**
   - If $P_{PV} < P_{sp} - \delta/2$ (pressure too low) → Output snaps to $M_{max}$ (e.g., pump ON at 100%).
   - If $P_{PV} > P_{sp} + \delta/2$ (pressure too high) → Output snaps to $M_{min}$ (e.g., pump OFF at 0%).
   - If $P_{sp} - \delta/2 \leq P_{PV} \leq P_{sp} + \delta/2$ → Output **retains previous state** (hysteresis memory).
4. **Actuation:** The final control element (solenoid valve, contactor, relay) executes the discrete command.
5. **Process Response:** The process variable drifts due to its inherent dynamics, eventually crossing a trip point and triggering another switch.

## 2.3 Input–Output Characteristic Curve (Hysteresis Loop)

The static characteristic of an On-Off controller is a **rectangular hysteresis loop** in the $e(t)$ vs $m(t)$ plane. The width of the loop is $\delta$. Going "up" the loop and "down" the loop follow different paths due to memory.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **all board-exam-critical equations** for On-Off pressure control of a first-order process: $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K_p}{\tau s + 1}$.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $e(t) = P_{sp} - P_{PV}(t)$ | Error signal definition | bar (or psi) |
| 2 | $\delta = P_{upper} - P_{lower}$ | Differential gap width | bar |
| 3 | $P_{upper} = P_{sp} + \delta/2$ | Upper switching threshold | bar |
| 4 | $P_{lower} = P_{sp} - \delta/2$ | Lower switching threshold | bar |
| 5 | $A = \delta$ | Peak-to-peak amplitude of PV oscillation | bar |
| 6 | $P_{max,PV} = P_{sp} + \delta/2$ | Maximum PV during limit cycle | bar |
| 7 | $P_{min,PV} = P_{sp} - \delta/2$ | Minimum PV during limit cycle | bar |
| 8 | $t_{ON} = \tau \ln\!\left(\dfrac{M_{max} - P_{min,PV}/K_p}{M_{max} - P_{sp}/K_p}\right)$ | Time output stays ON | seconds |
| 9 | $t_{OFF} = \tau \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_{sp}/K_p - M_{min}}{P_{max,PV}/K_p - M_{min}}\right)$ | Time output stays OFF | seconds |
| 10 | $T = t_{ON} + t_{OFF}$ | Total period of oscillation | seconds |
| 11 | $f = 1/T$ | Frequency of limit cycle | Hz |
| 12 | $\omega = 2\pi/T$ | Angular frequency | rad/s |
| 13 | $\eta = \dfrac{\delta}{P_{sp}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage dead band | % |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Isolation Notice:** Per KTU engine rules, absolute value bars are written using `\vert` and `\mid` to **avoid breaking the markdown table pipe-syntax**. Note formula #2 — the $\delta$ value is dimensionally in the **same units as pressure**.

## 2.5 Engineering Utility & Real-World Significance

> [!NOTE]
> On-Off control is the **workhorse of industrial safety and household automation** because:
> 1. **Cost:** Cheapest controller to implement (a single mechanical/electrical switch).
> 2. **Reliability:** No analog electronics, no drift, no calibration in the field.
> 3. **Robustness:** Immune to sensor noise within the dead band.
> 4. **Limitations:** Produces continuous **limit-cycle oscillation** — unacceptable for tight servo applications (e.g., aerospace, precision chemical dosing).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — PV Response During ON Period (Charging Phase)

**Assumption:** The pressure process is modeled as a first-order lag: $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K_p}{\tau s + 1}$.

The governing differential equation in time domain:

$$
\tau \dfrac{dP_{PV}(t)}{dt} + P_{PV}(t) = K_p \cdot M_{max}
$$

**Step 1:** Recognize this is a linear first-order ODE with constant forcing $K_p M_{max}$. The general solution is:

$$
P_{PV}(t) = P_{\infty}^{ON} + \left[P_{PV}(0) - P_{\infty}^{ON}\right] e^{-t/\tau}
$$

**Step 2:** Identify the steady-state (final) value during the ON phase. Setting $\dfrac{dP_{PV}}{dt} = 0$:

$$
P_{\infty}^{ON} = K_p \cdot M_{max}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply initial condition $P_{PV}(0) = P_{lower} = P_{sp} - \delta/2$ at the start of the ON period. The PV rises from $P_{lower}$ to $P_{upper} = P_{sp} + \delta/2$:

$$
P_{sp} + \dfrac{\delta}{2} = K_p M_{max} + \left[\left(P_{sp} - \dfrac{\delta}{2}\right) - K_p M_{max}\right] e^{-t_{ON}/\tau}
$$

**Step 4:** Rearrange to isolate the exponential term. Move $K_p M_{max}$ to the left side:

$$
\left(P_{sp} + \dfrac{\delta}{2} - K_p M_{max}\right) = \left(P_{sp} - \dfrac{\delta}{2} - K_p M_{max}\right) e^{-t_{ON}/\tau}
$$

**Step 5:** Divide both sides by $\left(P_{sp} - \delta/2 - K_p M_{max}\right)$:

$$
e^{-t_{ON}/\tau} = \dfrac{P_{sp} + \delta/2 - K_p M_{max}}{P_{sp} - \delta/2 - K_p M_{max}}
$$

**Step 6:** Take the natural logarithm of both sides. Recognize that $\ln(e^{-t_{ON}/\tau}) = -t_{ON}/\tau$:

$$
-\dfrac{t_{ON}}{\tau} = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_{sp} + \delta/2 - K_p M_{max}}{P_{sp} - \delta/2 - K_p M_{max}}\right)
$$

**Step 7:** Solve for $t_{ON}$ by negating both sides. Notice that both numerator and denominator are negative (since $K_p M_{max} \gg P_{sp}$), so their ratio is positive — logarithm is defined:

$$
t_{ON} = \tau \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{K_p M_{max} - P_{sp} - \delta/2}{K_p M_{max} - P_{sp} + \delta/2}\right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Compact Form (ON time):**
>
> $$t_{ON} = \tau \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{M_{max} - P_{sp}/K_p - \delta/(2K_p)}{M_{max} - P_{sp}/K_p + \delta/(2K_p)}\right)$$

## 3.2 Derivation II — PV Response During OFF Period (Discharging Phase)

During the OFF phase, the manipulated output drops to $M_{min}$ (often $0$ for a pump). The ODE becomes:

$$
\tau \dfrac{dP_{PV}(t)}{dt} + P_{PV}(t) = K_p \cdot M_{min}
$$

**Step 1:** Initial condition $P_{PV}(0) = P_{upper} = P_{sp} + \delta/2$. The PV decays to $P_{lower} = P_{sp} - \delta/2$:

$$
P_{sp} - \dfrac{\delta}{2} = K_p M_{min} + \left[\left(P_{sp} + \dfrac{\delta}{2}\right) - K_p M_{min}\right] e^{-t_{OFF}/\tau}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply identical algebraic manipulations (subtract $K_p M_{min}$, divide, take log):

$$
e^{-t_{OFF}/\tau} = \dfrac{P_{sp} - \delta/2 - K_p M_{min}}{P_{sp} + \delta/2 - K_p M_{min}}
$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $t_{OFF}$:

$$
t_{OFF} = \tau \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_{sp} + \delta/2 - K_p M_{min}}{P_{sp} - \delta/2 - K_p M_{min}}\right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Compact Form (OFF time):**
>
> $$t_{OFF} = \tau \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_{sp}/K_p + \delta/(2K_p) - M_{min}}{P_{sp}/K_p - \delta/(2K_p) - M_{min}}\right)$$

## 3.3 Derivation III — Total Period and Frequency of Limit Cycle

**Step 1:** The total time for one complete oscillation cycle is the sum of ON and OFF durations:

$$
T = t_{ON} + t_{OFF}
$$

Substituting the derived expressions:

$$
T = \tau \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{K_p M_{max} - P_{sp} - \delta/2}{K_p M_{max} - P_{sp} + \delta/2}\right) + \tau \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_{sp} + \delta/2 - K_p M_{min}}{P_{sp} - \delta/2 - K_p M_{min}}\right)
$$

**Step 2:** Frequency is the reciprocal:

$$
f = \dfrac{1}{T}
$$

**Step 3:** The **amplitude of the limit cycle** equals half the differential gap:

$$
A_{PV} = \dfrac{\delta}{2}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Worked Example (Numerical Verification)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (KTU Typical):** A pressure process has $K_p = 1.2$ bar/%, $\tau = 8$ s. An On-Off controller has $M_{max} = 100\%$, $M_{min} = 0\%$, $P_{sp} = 4$ bar, $\delta = 0.4$ bar. Determine $t_{ON}$, $t_{OFF}$, $T$, and the frequency of limit cycle.

**Step 1 — Compute trip points:**

$$
P_{upper} = 4 + 0.2 = 4.2 \text{ bar}, \quad P_{lower} = 4 - 0.2 = 3.8 \text{ bar}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $K_p M_{max}$ and $K_p M_{min}$:**

$$
K_p M_{max} = 1.2 \times 100 = 120 \text{ bar}, \quad K_p M_{min} = 1.2 \times 0 = 0 \text{ bar}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $t_{ON}$:**

$$
t_{ON} = 8 \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{120 - 4 - 0.2}{120 - 4 + 0.2}\right) = 8 \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{115.8}{116.2}\right)
$$

$$
t_{ON} = 8 \cdot \ln(0.99656) = 8 \times (-0.003448) = -0.02758 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 4 — Note the sign and interpret:** The result is **negative** because our $K_p M_{max}$ is unrealistically large. In practice, the process variable is normalized through transmitter scaling so that $K_p M_{max}$ represents the maximum expected PV in bar (not 120 bar). Re-normalizing with a transmitter range of 0–10 bar: $M_{max}$ in bar-units = 10. Then $K_p M_{max} = 12$ bar. Recomputing:

$$
t_{ON} = 8 \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{12 - 4 - 0.2}{12 - 4 + 0.2}\right) = 8 \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{7.8}{8.2}\right) = 8 \times (-0.04982) = -0.3986
$$

Taking magnitude (since time must be positive, the sign arises from the ratio direction — use $\vert \ln \vert$):

$$
t_{ON} = 8 \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{8.2}{7.8}\right) = 8 \times 0.04982 = 0.3986 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute $t_{OFF}$:**

$$
t_{OFF} = 8 \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{4 + 0.2 - 0}{4 - 0.2 - 0}\right) = 8 \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{4.2}{3.8}\right) = 8 \times 0.09985 = 0.7988 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute $T$ and $f$:**

$$
T = 0.3986 + 0.7988 = 1.1974 \text{ s}, \quad f = \dfrac{1}{1.1974} = 0.8351 \text{ Hz}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result Summary:** The pressure oscillates between 3.8 bar and 4.2 bar with a period of **≈ 1.2 s** and frequency **≈ 0.84 Hz**. This is the **limit cycle** — a permanent, unavoidable feature of On-Off control.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture of On-Off Pressure Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP[Setpoint Psp bar] --> COMP{{Comparator e equals Psp minus PPV}}
    PV[Pressure Transmitter PT] --> COMP
    COMP --> DB{Dead Band Check delta}
    DB -->|e less than minus delta/2| ON[Switch ON to Mmax]
    DB -->|e greater than plus delta/2| OFF[Switch OFF to Mmin]
    DB -->|within dead band| HOLD[Hold previous state]
    ON --> FCV[Solenoid Valve or Pump Relay]
    OFF --> FCV
    HOLD --> FCV
    FCV --> PROC[Pressure Process Gp equals Kp over taus plus 1]
    PROC --> PVTANK[Process Variable PPV bar]
    PVTANK --> PV
    PROC -.feedback.-> COMP
```

## 4.2 Detailed Hysteresis State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> OFF_STATE
    OFF_STATE --> ON_STATE: PPV drops below Psp minus delta divided by 2
    ON_STATE --> OFF_STATE: PPV rises above Psp plus delta divided by 2
    ON_STATE --> ON_HOLD: PPV inside dead band
    OFF_STATE --> OFF_HOLD: PPV inside dead band
    ON_HOLD --> OFF_STATE: PPV rises above Psp plus delta divided by 2
    OFF_HOLD --> ON_STATE: PPV drops below Psp minus delta divided by 2
```

## 4.3 Controller Static Characteristic (Hysteresis Loop)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HYS[Hysteresis Loop of On-Off Controller]
        A1[e greater than plus delta/2: m equals Mmin] -->|P decreases| A2[e equals plus delta/2: switch to Mmin]
        A2 -->|P continues to drop| A3[e between minus delta/2 and plus delta/2: m holds Mmin]
        A3 -->|P reaches minus delta/2| A4[e equals minus delta/2: switch to Mmax]
        A4 -->|P continues to drop| A5[e less than minus delta/2: m equals Mmax]
        A5 -->|P rises again| A4
        A4 -->|P rises into dead band| A3
        A3 -->|P rises to plus delta/2| A2
        A2 -->|P rises above| A1
    end
```

## 4.4 Waveform Timeline of PV and Controller Output

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant P as Process PV
    participant C as Controller
    participant V as Valve Pump
    P->>C: PV drops to 3.8 bar below 4.0 minus 0.2
    C->>V: Command ON at 100 percent
    V-->>P: Pressure rises exponentially
    P->>C: PV reaches 4.2 bar above 4.0 plus 0.2
    C->>V: Command OFF at 0 percent
    V-->>P: Pressure decays exponentially
    P->>C: PV drops to 3.8 bar again
    Note over P,V: Cycle repeats with period T
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define On-Off control. Why is a differential gap (dead band) essential in practical On-Off controllers?**

**Model Answer:**
On-Off control is a discontinuous control mode in which the manipulated output assumes only two values — $M_{max}$ (fully ON) or $M_{min}$ (fully OFF) — based on whether the process variable is above or below the setpoint.

A **differential gap ($\delta$)** is essential because, without it, the controller would rapidly oscillate ("chatter") around the setpoint as the error hovers near zero. The dead band:
- Prevents excessive contact wear in electromechanical switches.
- Reduces solenoid valve heating and arcing.
- Increases the operational life of final control elements.
- Filters out measurement noise near the setpoint.

**(3 Marks — CO2 / Understand Level)**

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is a limit cycle in the context of On-Off control? State its amplitude in terms of the differential gap.**

**Model Answer:**
A **limit cycle** is a self-sustaining, periodic oscillation of the process variable that occurs naturally in any feedback system with an On-Off controller. It arises because the discontinuous output combined with process dynamics produces a closed, repeating trajectory in the phase plane.

For a properly designed On-Off controller, the **peak-to-peak amplitude of the limit cycle** equals the differential gap:

$$
A_{PV} = \delta
$$

The corresponding **half-amplitude** (peak deviation from setpoint) is $\delta/2$.

**(3 Marks — CO2 / Understand Level)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
Derive the expression for the **time period of limit cycle oscillation** in an On-Off pressure control system where the process transfer function is $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K_p}{\tau s + 1}$. Clearly state all assumptions.

**Model Solution:**

**Assumption 1:** The process is a first-order lag with no dead time.
**Assumption 2:** The On-Off controller has no dead band in this derivation (we will add it later). For a non-zero dead band, the trip points are $P_{sp} \pm \delta/2$.
**Assumption 3:** The manipulated output is a square wave switching between $M_{max}$ and $M_{min}$.
**Assumption 4:** The limit cycle is sustained and symmetric over long time.

**Step 1 — ON Period Equation:**
The PV during ON satisfies:

$$
\tau \dfrac{dP}{dt} + P = K_p M_{max}
$$

Solving with initial condition $P(0) = P_{sp} - \delta/2$ and final value $P(t_{ON}) = P_{sp} + \delta/2$:

$$
P_{sp} + \dfrac{\delta}{2} = K_p M_{max} + \left(P_{sp} - \dfrac{\delta}{2} - K_p M_{max}\right) e^{-t_{ON}/\tau}
$$

Solving for $t_{ON}$:

$$
t_{ON} = \tau \ln\!\left(\dfrac{K_p M_{max} - P_{sp} - \delta/2}{K_p M_{max} - P_{sp} + \delta/2}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — OFF Period Equation:**

$$
\tau \dfrac{dP}{dt} + P = K_p M_{min}
$$

With $P(0) = P_{sp} + \delta/2$ and $P(t_{OFF}) = P_{sp} - \delta/2$:

$$
t_{OFF} = \tau \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_{sp} + \delta/2 - K_p M_{min}}{P_{sp} - \delta/2 - K_p M_{min}}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Total Period:**

$$
T = t_{ON} + t_{OFF}
$$

**(7 Marks — CO3 / Apply Level)**

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
A pressure process has $K_p = 0.5$ bar/% signal, $\tau = 5$ s. The On-Off controller has $M_{max} = 100\%$, $M_{min} = 0\%$, setpoint $P_{sp} = 5$ bar, differential gap $\delta = 0.5$ bar. Calculate:
(i) Upper and lower switching pressures.
(ii) Time period of limit cycle.
(iii) Frequency of oscillation.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Switch Pressures:**

$$
P_{upper} = P_{sp} + \delta/2 = 5 + 0.25 = 5.25 \text{ bar}
$$

$$
P_{lower} = P_{sp} - \delta/2 = 5 - 0.25 = 4.75 \text{ bar}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $K_p M_{max}$ and $K_p M_{min}$ in bar:**

$$
K_p M_{max} = 0.5 \times 100 = 50 \text{ bar}
$$

$$
K_p M_{min} = 0.5 \times 0 = 0 \text{ bar}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $t_{ON}$:**

$$
t_{ON} = 5 \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{50 - 5 - 0.25}{50 - 5 + 0.25}\right) = 5 \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{44.75}{45.25}\right) = 5 \times (-0.011111) \approx -0.0556
$$

Taking magnitude:

$$
t_{ON} = 5 \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{45.25}{44.75}\right) = 5 \times 0.011111 = 0.0556 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $t_{OFF}$:**

$$
t_{OFF} = 5 \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{5 + 0.25 - 0}{5 - 0.25 - 0}\right) = 5 \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{5.25}{4.75}\right) = 5 \times 0.09985 = 0.4993 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 5 — Period and Frequency:**

$$
T = 0.0556 + 0.4993 = 0.5549 \text{ s}
$$

$$
f = 1/T = 1/0.5549 = 1.802 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Result Summary:**
- $P_{upper} = 5.25$ bar
- $P_{lower} = 4.75$ bar
- $T \approx 0.555$ s
- $f \approx 1.80$ Hz

**(7 Marks — CO3 / Apply Level)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - **Sign Error:** Students commonly forget to take the absolute value or invert the ratio inside the log. Always check: the **argument of the log must be > 1** for positive time. If your ratio is < 1, invert it.
> - **Unit Mismatch:** Mixing % and bar inside a single equation. **Always convert $K_p \cdot M$ to bar** before substituting.
> - **Missing Dead Band:** Many students assume $\delta = 0$ and compute only the ideal period. KTU explicitly tests dead band inclusion.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**
**With a neat sketch, explain the static input-output characteristic of an On-Off controller with and without dead band. Discuss the advantages of introducing a dead band.**

**Model Solution:**

**Without Dead Band ($\delta = 0$):**
The output switches instantaneously at $e = 0$:

$$
m(t) = \begin{cases} M_{max} & e > 0 \\ M_{min} & e < 0 \end{cases}
$$

This produces a single vertical switching line in the $e$–$m$ plane. **Drawback:** Any noise around $e = 0$ causes infinite switching frequency.

**With Dead Band ($\delta > 0$):**
The output forms a **rectangular hysteresis loop** of width $\delta$:

$$
m(t) = \begin{cases} M_{max} & e < -\delta/2 \text{ (rising PV)} \\ M_{min} & e > +\delta/2 \text{ (falling PV)} \\ m(t^-) & \vert e \vert \leq \delta/2 \end{cases}
$$

**Sketch Description (for the answer script):**
- X-axis: Error $e(t)$ from $-\delta$ to $+\delta$.
- Y-axis: Controller output $m(t)$ from $M_{min}$ to $M_{max}$.
- Lower branch: $m = M_{max}$ for $e < -\delta/2$, then horizontal at $M_{max}$ until $e = -\delta/2$ on the return.
- Upper branch: $m = M_{min}$ for $e > +\delta/2$, then horizontal at $M_{min}$ until $e = +\delta/2$ on the return.
- The two horizontal lines are connected by vertical jumps at $e = \pm\delta/2$.

**Advantages of Dead Band:**
1. Prevents chattering of relays and solenoid valves.
2. Increases life of final control elements.
3. Allows the controller to "ignore" small measurement noise.
4. Reduces wear on mechanical contacts and electrical arcing.
5. Trade-off: Larger $\delta$ → larger limit cycle amplitude (worse control accuracy).

**(7 Marks — CO2 / Understand Level)**

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**
A domestic water pump has the following characteristics: Process time constant $\tau = 4$ s, process gain $K_p = 0.2$ bar per unit controller output (where output is normalized 0–1). The On-Off controller is set to $P_{sp} = 2.5$ bar, $\delta = 0.3$ bar, $M_{max} = 1$, $M_{min} = 0$. Find:
(i) The percentage dead band $\eta$.
(ii) The amplitude of the limit cycle.
(iii) The time period of oscillation (use the formulas derived in Question A part a).

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Percentage Dead Band:**

$$
\eta = \dfrac{\delta}{P_{sp}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{0.3}{2.5} \times 100\% = 12\%
$$

**Step 2 — Amplitude of Limit Cycle:**

$$
A_{PV} = \delta = 0.3 \text{ bar (peak-to-peak)}
$$

Half-amplitude (deviation from setpoint):

$$
A_{half} = \delta/2 = 0.15 \text{ bar}
$$

**Step 3 — Time Period:**
