Compute $K_p M_{max} = 0.2 \times 1 = 0.2$ bar, $K_p M_{min} = 0$.

$$
t_{ON} = 4 \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{0.2 - 2.5 - 0.15}{0.2 - 2.5 + 0.15}\right) = 4 \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{-2.45}{-2.15}\right) = 4 \cdot \ln(1.1395) = 4 \times 0.1307 = 0.5227 \text{ s}
$$

$$
t_{OFF} = 4 \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{2.5 + 0.15 - 0}{2.5 - 0.15 - 0}\right) = 4 \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{2.65}{2.35}\right) = 4 \times 0.1201 = 0.4804 \text{ s}
$$

$$
T = 0.5227 + 0.4804 = 1.0031 \text{ s}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- $\eta = 12\%$
- $A_{PV} = 0.3$ bar (peak-to-peak)
- $T \approx 1.00$ s

**(7 Marks — CO3 / Apply Level)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall for Question B:**
> - **Sketch Quality:** A vague or absent sketch of the hysteresis loop costs 2 marks immediately. KTU mandates a **neat, labeled diagram** with axes, dead band marked, and both branches clearly visible.
> - **Confusing $\delta$ and $\eta$:** $\delta$ is in bar (absolute), $\eta$ is dimensionless %. Do not interchange.
> - **Linear Approximation Validity:** If the process is non-linear (e.g., square-root flow relationship), the derived formulas are only approximate. KTU may award partial credit for noting this limitation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — On-Off Pressure Control**

- ✅ On-Off control is a **two-position** discontinuous controller with output $M_{max}$ or $M_{min}$.
- ✅ **Differential gap ($\delta$)** is essential to prevent relay/valve chattering.
- ✅ Hysteresis loop has width $\delta$ in the $e$–$m$ plane.
- ✅ **Limit cycle** is an inherent, unavoidable feature of On-Off control.
- ✅ Limit cycle **amplitude (peak-to-peak) = $\delta$** for a properly tuned On-Off system.
- ✅ Time period: $T = t_{ON} + t_{OFF}$, where $t_{ON}$ and $t_{OFF}$ are computed from exponential charging/discharging of the first-order process.
- ✅ Frequency $f = 1/T$, Angular frequency $\omega = 2\pi/T$.
- ✅ **Percentage dead band** $\eta = (\delta / P_{sp}) \times 100\%$ — measures controller precision.
- ✅ Increasing $\delta$ → larger amplitude oscillation, smaller switching frequency, longer equipment life.
- ✅ Decreasing $\delta$ → smaller amplitude, higher switching frequency, equipment wear.
- ✅ **Trade-off:** The KTU-typical design compromise is $\eta = 5\%$ to $10\%$.
- ✅ For a first-order lag process, the formulas for $t_{ON}$ and $t_{OFF}$ are **logarithmic** in the differential gap.
- ✅ **Applications:** Domestic pumps, air compressors, refrigeration cut-outs, boiler pressure interlocks, HVAC duct pressure.
- ✅ **Limitations:** Not suitable for precision servo systems, multi-variable coupled processes, or processes with very long dead times.
- ✅ **Memory (hysteresis):** Within the dead band, the controller retains the previous output state.
- ✅ KTU **favorite exam trick:** Always include the differential gap; never assume $\delta = 0$ unless explicitly stated.
- ✅ **Symmetric trip points:** $P_{upper} = P_{sp} + \delta/2$ and $P_{lower} = P_{sp} - \delta/2$.
- ✅ The **sign of the log argument** must be positive. If the ratio is less than 1, **invert it** before applying $\ln$.
- ✅ Always **convert all quantities to consistent units** (bar or % — never mix) before substitution.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Throttling Flow Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT743 - INDUSTRIAL PROCESS CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Throttling Flow Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Throttling Flow Control** is a method of regulating the rate of fluid flow in a process plant by introducing a **variable restriction** (most commonly a pneumatically or electrically actuated **control valve**) into the flow path. The valve modulates its effective cross-sectional area, dissipating fluid energy through a controlled **pressure drop ($\Delta P_v$)**, thereby manipulating the volumetric or mass flow rate $Q$ delivered to the process.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this is classified as the **"Final Control Element"** of a feedback/feedforward control loop, where the manipulated variable is the valve stem position $h$ (or fractional travel $l = h/h_{max}$), and the controlled variable is the process flow $Q$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> Think of the **water tap (faucet)** in your kitchen. When you turn the knob slightly, a small opening allows a thin stream of water. As you turn further, the opening enlarges and more water flows. A control valve in a refinery or chemical plant does **exactly the same thing** — but with millimetre precision, driven by an air signal (typically **20–100 kPa (3–15 psi)** pneumatic) or a **4–20 mA** current signal from a controller.

The difference between a household tap and an industrial control valve:
- The industrial valve is **continuously variable** (not just on/off).
- It is **automated** by an actuator receiving a controller output.
- It is **sized precisely** so that at maximum opening, the required maximum flow occurs at the available pressure drop.

## 1.3 Why Throttling is Used in Process Control

| Alternative Method | Why Throttling is Preferred |
|---|---|
| On/Off (bang-bang) control | Causes oscillations, equipment wear, poor regulation |
| Variable speed pump | Expensive for high capacities, limited turndown |
| Bypass line with manual valve | Not automated, no feedback action |
| **Throttling valve** | **Continuous, accurate, fast, widely applicable** |

## 1.4 Key Physical Quantities (Bold Highlights)

- **$C_v$ — Valve Flow Coefficient** (dimensionless but has implicit units of US gpm/psi$^{0.5}$): the gallons per minute of water at 60 °F that flow through a fully open valve with a 1 psi pressure drop.
- **$\Delta P_v$ — Permanent Pressure Drop across the valve** (in **kPa** or **bar**).
- **$R$ — Rangeability**, ratio of maximum to minimum controllable flow (typically **20:1 to 50:1**).
- **$l$ — Fractional Valve Travel** (dimensionless, $0 \le l \le 1$).
- **$\beta$ — Pressure Recovery Factor** of the valve trim.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Fundamental Flow Equation

For an **incompressible fluid**, the steady-state volumetric flow $Q$ through a restriction is given by the orifice-type equation:

$$
Q = C_v \, N \, F_p \, \sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta P_v}{G_s}}
$$

Where:
- $Q$ = volumetric flow rate (**m³/h** in SI, **gpm** in US units)
- $C_v$ = valve flow coefficient (manufacturer tabulated)
- $N$ = numerical constant (1.0 for SI m³/h; **0.865** for SI L/s; 1.0 for US gpm)
- $F_p$ = piping geometry correction factor (1.0 for standard reducers)
- $\Delta P_v$ = pressure drop across the valve in **bar** (or psi)
- $G_s$ = specific gravity of fluid (water = 1.0)

### Why this formula matters (intuition)
The square-root law ($Q \propto \sqrt{\Delta P_v}$) is the **root cause of nonlinearity** in flow loops. This is why flow controllers usually contain a **square-root extractor** to linearize the signal before applying PID.

## 2.2 Inherent Valve Characteristics

The **inherent characteristic** of a valve describes the relationship between fractional travel $l$ and flow rate $Q$ when the **upstream and downstream pressures are held constant** (i.e., a constant $\Delta P_v$).

| Characteristic | Mathematical Form | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Quick-Opening** | $Q/Q_{max} = \sqrt{l}$ | On/Off service, safety relief, two-position control |
| **Linear** | $Q/Q_{max} = l$ | Equal percentage substituted; rare in practice |
| **Equal Percentage** | $Q/Q_{max} = R^{(l-1)}$ where $R$ = rangeability | Most common — compensates for $\sqrt{\Delta P}$ nonlinearity |

### The "Equal Percentage" Logic (Why it dominates)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Because the **process** has $Q \propto \sqrt{\Delta P}$, the installed gain changes drastically with load. An **equal-percentage trim** introduces an **inverse nonlinearity** such that the **installed characteristic** (valve + process) becomes approximately **linear**. This is a classic example of **nonlinearity compensation through element design**.

## 2.3 Installed Characteristic

In the real plant, the pressure drop is split between the **valve ($\Delta P_v$)** and the **piping/system ($\Delta P_s$)**:

$$
\Delta P_{total} = \Delta P_v + \Delta P_s
$$

The installed characteristic is obtained by combining:
- **Valve curve** (inherent $Q$ vs $l$ at constant $\Delta P_v$)
- **System curve** ($\Delta P_s = K_s Q^2$)

The dimensionless pressure drop ratio is defined as:

$$
x = \dfrac{\Delta P_{v,0}}{\Delta P_{total}}
$$

where $\Delta P_{v,0}$ is the pressure drop across the fully open valve at maximum flow. A typical design choice is $x = 0.5$ — i.e., **50% of total system drop is across the valve at design flow**, leaving 50% margin for valve authority.

## 2.4 Valve Authority and Rangeability

$$
\text{Valve Authority at design point} = \dfrac{\Delta P_{v,0}}{\Delta P_{total}}
$$

$$
\text{Effective Rangeability} = R_{eff} = R_{valve} \times \sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta P_{v,min}}{\Delta P_{v,max}}}
$$

| Term | Definition | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| **Rangeability ($R$)** | Ratio $Q_{max, ctrl}/Q_{min, ctrl}$ for the valve itself | 20:1 to 50:1 |
| **Turndown** | Actual operating range considering installed curve | Usually **half** of $R$ |
| **Valve Authority** | $\Delta P$ share of valve vs system at design | **0.3 to 0.7** ideal |

## 2.5 Cavitation and Flashing

> [!WARNING]
> When the static pressure downstream of the valve falls **below the vapour pressure** of the liquid, vapour bubbles form and collapse violently, eroding the trim. This is **cavitation**.

A related phenomenon is **flashing**, where the pressure drop is so large that the liquid partially vaporises, creating a two-phase flow downstream — damaging to piping and fittings.

The cavitation index is computed as:

$$
\sigma = \dfrac{P_1 - P_v}{P_1 - P_2}
$$

If $\sigma < \sigma_{critical}$ (vendor-specified), cavitation occurs.

## 2.6 Actuators and Positioners

- **Pneumatic Diaphragm Actuator**: Standard signal **20–100 kPa** (3–15 psi), fails open or fails closed based on spring direction.
- **Piston Actuator**: For higher thrust; uses compressed air on both sides of a piston.
- **Electric Actuator**: 4–20 mA input; uses a motor and gear train; suitable for remote locations without instrument air.
- **Positioner**: A local feedback loop that drives the actuator until the stem position matches the controller output — **eliminates stem friction and packing effects**, improves dynamic response.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = C_v N F_p \sqrt{\Delta P_v / G_s}$ | Valve flow equation | m³/h |
| 2 | $Q/Q_{max} = R^{(l-1)}$ | Equal-percentage inherent curve | — |
| 3 | $Q/Q_{max} = \sqrt{l}$ | Quick-opening curve | — |
| 4 | $\Delta P_{tot} = \Delta P_v + \Delta P_s$ | Pressure drop partition | kPa |
| 5 | Valve authority $= \Delta P_{v,0}/\Delta P_{tot}$ | Design ratio | — |
| 6 | $\sigma = (P_1 - P_v)/(P_1 - P_2)$ | Cavitation index | — |
| 7 | $\tau = V/A$ | Time constant of piping volume | s |
| 8 | $K_v = $ valve gain at operating point | $dQ/dl$ at the operating point | m³/h per unit travel |
| 9 | $K_p = \partial Q / \partial \Delta P_v$ | Process gain w.r.t. valve drop | m³/h per kPa |
| 10 | Loop gain $K_{loop} = K_c K_v K_p K_t$ | Used in stability analysis | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility**: Throttling flow control is the **workhorse of every DCS/PLC-based plant** — oil refineries, petrochemical complexes, power plants, water treatment, food & beverage, pharma. Almost every flow loop in a P&ID terminates in a throttling valve.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Flow Through a Restriction (Bernoulli + Continuity)

**Starting Point**: Steady-state, incompressible, inviscid flow through a sudden restriction.

**Step 1 — Apply continuity equation** between upstream (1) and vena contracta (2):

$$
A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2
$$

This gives $v_2 = (A_1/A_2) v_1$. The ratio of areas $A_1/A_2$ becomes large as the valve throttles.

**Step 2 — Apply Bernoulli's equation** (no elevation change, no friction loss through ideal restriction):

$$
P_1 + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v_1^{2} = P_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v_2^{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $v_2$**:

$$
P_1 - P_2 = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho(v_2^{2} - v_1^{2}) = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v_2^{2} \left(1 - (A_2/A_1)^{2}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Introduce discharge coefficient $C_d$** to account for real (viscous) losses and vena contracta:

$$
v_2 = C_d \sqrt{\dfrac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho\left[1-(A_2/A_1)^2\right]}}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute volumetric flow $Q = A_2 v_2$**:

$$
Q = C_d A_2 \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho \left[1-(A_2/A_1)^2\right]}}
$$

**Step 6 — Group all geometric terms into a single constant $K$** and convert density to specific gravity $G_s = \rho/\rho_{water}$:

$$
Q = K \sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta P}{G_s}}
$$

This is the **theoretical basis** for $Q = C_v \cdot N \cdot F_p \cdot \sqrt{\Delta P_v / G_s}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The $C_v$ coefficient is **experimentally determined** by the manufacturer for each valve size and trim. It is tabulated for water at 60 °F, and the conversion formula above lets you use it for any fluid.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Equal-Percentage Characteristic

**Given**: The installed gain $K_{inst} = dQ/dl$ should remain **constant** over the entire operating range, to give the controller a linear plant.

**Step 1**: Take a constant-gain condition:

$$
\dfrac{dQ}{dl} = K_{const} \cdot Q
$$

(Note: gain proportional to the operating flow, NOT constant — this is the defining differential equation for "equal percentage".)

**Step 2**: Separate variables:

$$
\dfrac{dQ}{Q} = K_{const} \, dl
$$

**Step 3**: Integrate from $l = 0$ (closed) to $l = 1$ (fully open), and let $Q(0) = Q_{min}$, $Q(1) = Q_{max}$:

$$
\int_{Q_{min}}^{Q_{max}} \dfrac{dQ}{Q} = K_{const} \int_{0}^{1} dl
$$

$$
\ln\left(\dfrac{Q_{max}}{Q_{min}}\right) = K_{const}
$$

**Step 4**: Define the rangeability $R = Q_{max}/Q_{min}$:

$$
K_{const} = \ln R
$$

**Step 5**: Substitute back and integrate the general ODE:

$$
Q(l) = Q_{min} \, e^{(\ln R)\,l} = Q_{min} \, R^{l}
$$

**Step 6**: Apply the boundary condition $Q(1) = Q_{max}$ to obtain the **standard form**:

$$
\dfrac{Q(l)}{Q_{max}} = R^{(l-1)}
$$

This is the **canonical equal-percentage formula** tested in KTU exams.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Installed Characteristic (Linear Valve Example)

**Given**: A linear valve installed in a system with parabolic system curve. We will plot the **installed curve** — flow $Q$ vs travel $l$ at constant pump pressure.

**Step 1**: Define system curve:

$$
\Delta P_s = K_s \, Q^{2}
$$

**Step 2**: Define total available pressure drop (constant because pump is constant head):

$$
\Delta P_{tot} = \Delta P_v + \Delta P_s = \text{constant} = C
$$

**Step 3**: For a linear valve, the inherent curve at constant $\Delta P_v$ is:

$$
Q_{inh}(l) = Q_{max,full} \cdot l \quad \text{(at rated } \Delta P_v\text{)}
$$

But at any actual $\Delta P_v$, the flow scales as $\sqrt{\Delta P_v}$. So the **installed** flow is:

$$
Q_{inst}(l) = Q_{max,full} \cdot l \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta P_v(l)}{\Delta P_{v,rated}}}
$$

**Step 4**: Express $\Delta P_v$ in terms of $Q$ from Step 2:

$$
\Delta P_v(l) = C - K_s Q_{inst}^{2}
$$

**Step 5**: Solve the implicit equation (iteratively or graphically) to obtain $Q_{inst}$ vs $l$. The resulting curve **bows downward** compared to the inherent line — a phenomenon called **"characteristic distortion"**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Insight**: Designers choose **equal-percentage trim** when the system curve is steep (large $K_s$), and **linear trim** when the system is nearly flat (small $K_s$, e.g., high authority). This restores linearity in the installed characteristic.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Summary of Key Quantities

| Quantity | Symbol | Equation | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Valve flow coefficient | $C_v$ | $C_v = Q \sqrt{G_s/\Delta P_v}$ | Flow capacity |
| Valve authority | $N$ | $N = \Delta P_{v,0}/\Delta P_{tot}$ | Control authority |
| Inherent gain (linear) | $K_{inh}$ | $K_{inh} = Q_{max}/1$ | Slope of inherent line |
| Inherent gain (eq. %) | $K_{inh}(l)$ | $K_{inh}(l) = Q_{max} \cdot \ln(R) \cdot R^{l-1}$ | Position-dependent |
| Cavitation index | $\sigma$ | $\sigma = (P_1-P_v)/(P_1-P_2)$ | Liquid vapour risk |
| Rangeability | $R$ | $R = Q_{max,ctrl}/Q_{min,ctrl}$ | Controllable range |
| Pneumatic signal | $P_a$ | $P_a = 20 + 80 l$ kPa | Actuator pressure |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Throttling Flow Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP[Setpoint SP] --> EC[Error Calculator e = SP - PV]
    PV[Process Variable PV] --> EC
    EC --> CTRL[Controller PID]
    CTRL --> OUT[Controller Output 4-20 mA or 3-15 psi]
    OUT --> POS[Valve Positioner]
    POS --> ACT[Actuator Diaphragm or Piston]
    ACT --> VALVE[Control Valve Throttling Element]
    VALVE --> PROC[Process Pipe / Flow Stream]
    PROC --> FT[Flow Transmitter]
    FT --> PV
    PROC -. pressure drop ΔPv .-> VALVE
```

## 4.2 Architecture — Components of a Throttling Valve Assembly

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph valveAssembly[Control Valve Assembly]
        A1[Controller Signal 4-20 mA] --> A2[I/P Converter]
        A2 --> A3[Positioner]
        A3 --> A4[Actuator Pneumatic / Electric]
        A4 --> A5[Stem and Packing]
        A5 --> A6[Plug and Seat Trim]
        A6 --> A7[Valve Body Globe / Ball / Butterfly]
    end
    A7 --> A8[Process Fluid Inlet]
    A7 --> A9[Process Fluid Outlet]
    A9 --> A10[Flow measured by FT]
    A10 --> A1
```

## 4.3 Comparison Chart — Inherent Characteristics (Flow vs Travel)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph chars[Inherent Valve Characteristics]
        QO[Quick Opening: Q/Qmax = sqrt l]
        LIN[Linear: Q/Qmax = l]
        EP[Equal Percentage: Q/Qmax = R power l minus 1]
    end
    QO -->|Low slope at high l| REG1[High initial gain]
    LIN -->|Constant slope| REG2[Uniform gain]
    EP -->|Exponentially rising| REG3[Compensates sqrt nonlinearity]
```

## 4.4 Installed vs Inherent Characteristic (Visual Concept)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Xaxis[Valve Travel l: 0 to 1] --> Yaxis[Flow Q]
    Yaxis --> CI[Inherent Curve: 100% ΔP across valve]
    Yaxis --> CI2[Installed Curve: ΔP split with system]
    CI --> DIFF[Installed curve bends downward]
    CI2 --> DIFF
    DIFF --> NOTE[Distortion due to system pressure drop]
```

## 4.5 Decision Tree — Selection of Valve Characteristic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{What fraction of total ΔP is across the valve at design flow?}
    Q1 -->|More than 70 percent| A1[Use LINEAR trim]
    Q1 -->|Between 30 and 70 percent| A2[Use EQUAL PERCENTAGE trim]
    Q1 -->|Less than 30 percent| A3[Use EQUAL PERCENTAGE with high R, or reconsider sizing]
    A1 --> R1[Typical: small system drop]
    A2 --> R2[Typical: standard design, x approx 0.5]
    A3 --> R3[Typical: long pipe runs, control near closed]
```

## 4.6 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Examination View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph sensingLayer[Sensing Layer]
        FT1[Flow Transmitter FT]
        PT1[Pressure Transmitter PT]
        TT1[Temperature Transmitter TT]
    end
    subgraph controlLayer[Control Layer DCS]
        LIC[Flow Controller FIC]
        CALC[Square Root Extractor]
    end
    subgraph finalControl[Final Control Element Throttling]
        POS1[Positioner]
        ACT1[Actuator]
        VLV1[Control Valve]
    end
    FT1 --> CALC --> LIC
    PT1 --> LIC
    TT1 --> LIC
    LIC --> POS1 --> ACT1 --> VLV1
    VLV1 -. manipulates .-> FT1
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
> **Define throttling flow control. List any two advantages of using a throttling valve over on/off (bang-bang) control.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) **Definition**: Throttling flow control is a method of regulating fluid flow rate by continuously varying the effective cross-sectional area of a control valve, which dissipates fluid energy as a permanent pressure drop.
- (1 mark) **Advantage 1**: Provides **continuous, smooth regulation** of flow rather than discrete on/off steps, eliminating process oscillations.
- (1 mark) **Advantage 2**: Allows **precise setpoint tracking** and stable operation, plus energy-efficient operation since flow is matched exactly to demand.

---

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
> **What is valve authority? Why is it preferred to keep it between 0.3 and 0.7 in practical designs?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) **Definition**: Valve authority $N = \Delta P_{v,0}/\Delta P_{total}$ is the fraction of the total system pressure drop that occurs across the fully open valve at design flow.
- (1 mark) **Reason 1**: If $N < 0.3$, the valve has insufficient authority; flow becomes dominated by system pressure, control is sluggish, and rangeability collapses.
- (1 mark) **Reason 2**: If $N > 0.7$, an excessive pressure drop is wasted across the valve, increasing pumping cost and risking cavitation. The 0.3–0.7 range balances controllability with energy efficiency.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Q3A.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / Model Paper]`
> **(a)** With the help of neat sketches, explain the construction and working of a **pneumatically actuated globe valve** with a positioner. Discuss its inherent flow characteristic. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** Derive the expression for the **equal-percentage inherent characteristic** of a control valve. A control valve has a rangeability $R = 50$. Calculate the fractional flow at valve travels of 0%, 25%, 50%, 75%, and 100%. (7 marks)

### Model Solution for Q3A

#### (a) Pneumatic Globe Valve with Positioner — 7 marks

**Construction (3 marks):**
1. **Valve body** — contains the seat; threaded or flanged to the pipe. The fluid enters and exits through the body.
2. **Plug and stem** — the plug is connected to the stem; vertical motion of the stem changes the gap between plug and seat, varying the flow area.
3. **Diaphragm actuator** — receives pneumatic signal (20–100 kPa) on top of a flexible diaphragm; the diaphragm deflects against a spring, producing thrust on the stem.
4. **Stem packing** — prevents leakage of process fluid along the stem (typically PTFE or graphite rings).
5. **Positioner** — receives the controller's pneumatic/electrical signal and compares it with the actual stem position (via a cam/lever feedback link). It bleeds/supplies air to the actuator until the stem position matches the input — **eliminating hysteresis and packing friction**.

**Working (2 marks):**
- As the controller output increases from 20 kPa to 100 kPa, the diaphragm deflects downward, pushing the stem down, the plug moves closer to the seat, restricting the flow. (For **air-to-close / spring-to-open** reverse-acting; the opposite is **air-to-open / spring-to-close**.)
- With a positioner, the stem achieves precise positioning proportional to the signal.

**Inherent Characteristic (2 marks):**
- The globe valve's **inherent characteristic depends on the trim shape**. A contoured plug produces an **equal-percentage** characteristic. A flat or sharp-edged plug gives a **linear** or **quick-opening** characteristic. The relationship is plotted as $Q/Q_{max}$ vs $l$ at constant pressure drop.

#### (b) Equal-Percentage Derivation + Numerical — 7 marks

**Derivation (4 marks):**

For the installed gain to be **proportional to flow** (to compensate for the $\sqrt{\Delta P}$ nonlinearity), we impose:

$$
\dfrac{dQ}{dl} = K \cdot Q
$$

Separating variables and integrating between $l = 0, Q = Q_{min}$ and $l = 1, Q = Q_{max}$:

$$
\int_{Q_{min}}^{Q_{max}} \dfrac{dQ}{Q} = K \int_{0}^{1} dl
$$

$$
\ln\left(\dfrac{Q_{max}}{Q_{min}}\right) = K
$$

With rangeability $R = Q_{max}/Q_{min}$, we have $K = \ln R$. Substituting back:

$$
Q(l) = Q_{min} \cdot R^{l}
$$

Using the boundary condition $Q(1) = Q_{max}$:

$$
\dfrac{Q(l)}{Q_{max}} = R^{(l-1)}
$$

**Numerical Computation (3 marks):**

Given $R = 50$. For each $l$, compute $Q/Q_{max} = 50^{(l-1)}$:

| Travel $l$ | Exponent $(l-1)$ | $Q/Q_{max} = 50^{(l-1)}$ | % Flow |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0.00 | −1.000 | 0.0200 | 2.0% |
| 0.25 | −0.750 | 0.0562 | 5.6% |
| 0.50 | −0.500 | 0.1414 | 14.1% |
| 0.75 | −0.250 | 0.3981 | 39.8% |
| 1.00 | 0.000 | 1.0000 | 100% |

This shows the **exponentially increasing** flow — typical of equal-percentage trims.

---

> **Q3B (Alternative Choice).** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`
> **(a)** Explain the difference between **inherent** and **installed** characteristics of a control valve. How does the **system pressure drop** distort the inherent characteristic? (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** A flow control loop has the following data: Pump discharge pressure = 4 bar, Static head at valve outlet = 0.5 bar, Maximum design flow = 100 m³/h, Specific gravity = 0.95. If the valve is to operate with 50% authority at design flow, calculate the required valve $C_v$. (7 marks)

### Model Solution for Q3B

#### (a) Inherent vs Installed — 7 marks

**Definitions (2 marks):**
- **Inherent characteristic**: The relationship between fractional valve travel $l$ and flow rate $Q$ when the **pressure drop across the valve is held constant** (laboratory test condition).
- **Installed characteristic**: The relationship between $l$ and $Q$ when the valve is **mounted in the actual process piping**, with the system pressure drop present.

**Distortion Mechanism (3 marks):**
- In the real plant, the total available pressure $\Delta P_{tot}$ is **shared** between the valve and the piping. As flow increases, the system pressure drop $\Delta P_s = K_s Q^2$ rises, leaving **less pressure drop** available across the valve.
- Since $Q \propto \sqrt{\Delta P_v}$, when $\Delta P_v$ falls with rising flow, the actual $Q$ grows **more slowly** than the inherent curve predicts.
- The result: the installed curve **bows downward** compared to the inherent curve, especially for **linear** valves.

**Design Implication (2 marks):**
- Designers pre-compensate by selecting **equal-percentage** trim when the system curve is steep, restoring near-linearity in the installed characteristic.
- A high valve authority ($N \approx 0.5$) keeps the distortion small.

#### (b) $C_v$ Calculation — 7 marks

**Step 1: Compute total pressure drop available to the system (3 marks):**

$$
\Delta P_{tot} = P_{pump} - P_{static} = 4.0 - 0.5 = 3.5 \text{ bar}
$$

**Step 2: Compute pressure drop across the valve at design flow (1 mark):**

With valve authority $N = 0.5$:

$$
\Delta P_v = N \cdot \Delta P_{tot} = 0.5 \times 3.5 = 1.75 \text{ bar}
$$

**Step 3: Apply the valve flow equation (3 marks):**

$$
Q = C_v \, N_{c} \, F_p \, \sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta P_v}{G_s}}
$$

Using $N_c = 1.0$ (m³/h-bar units), $F_p = 1.0$, $G_s = 0.95$:

$$
C_v = \dfrac{Q}{\sqrt{\Delta P_v / G_s}} = \dfrac{100}{\sqrt{1.75/0.95}}
$$

$$
C_v = \dfrac{100}{\sqrt{1.8421}} = \dfrac{100}{1.3573} = 73.68
$$

**Step 4: Select the next standard valve size (1 mark):**

A valve with $C_v \ge 73.68$ is required. Standard globe valve sizes: $C_v = 75, 95, 115\ldots$ — select the $C_v = 75$ valve.

> **Answer**: Required $C_v = 73.7$; select standard valve with $C_v = 75$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in Throttling Flow Control Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $C_v$ units** — using gpm-psi units instead of m³/h-bar or vice-versa. Always state the unit system first.
> 2. **Forgetting specific gravity $G_s$** in the flow equation. Water-only assumption is acceptable only if the fluid is explicitly water.
> 3. **Wrong boundary in equal-percentage derivation** — the standard form is $R^{(l-1)}$, NOT $R^{l}$ or $R^{(1-l)}$. Mistake loses 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Treating valve authority as a fixed property of the valve** — it depends on the system! Authority = $\Delta P_{v,0}/\Delta P_{tot}$ at design flow.
> 5. **Ignoring cavitation** — when $P_2 < P_v$, the flow equation breaks down and the actual $C_v$ is degraded; many students skip this check.
> 6. **Mixing up inherent vs installed** — the inherent curve is at constant $\Delta P_v$, the installed is in the real system.
> 7. **Linearizing the square-root incorrectly** — $dQ/d(\Delta P) = C_v/(2\sqrt{\Delta P \cdot G})$, not $C_v/\sqrt{\Delta P}$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ Throttling flow control = **variable restriction** in a flow path to regulate $Q$.
- ✅ Governing equation: $Q = C_v N F_p \sqrt{\Delta P_v / G_s}$.
- ✅ **Inherent characteristic** = constant $\Delta P_v$ test; **Installed characteristic** = real plant.
- ✅ **Equal-percentage** trim compensates for $\sqrt{\Delta P}$ nonlinearity — most common in flow loops.
- ✅ Formula: $Q/Q_{max} = R^{(l-1)}$.
- ✅ **Valve authority** $N = \Delta P_{v,0}/\Delta P_{tot}$ ideally **0.3 to 0.7**.
- ✅ **Rangeability** = ratio of max to min controllable flow; affected by installed curve.
- ✅ **Cavitation** occurs when $P_2 < P_v$ (vapour pressure); check the cavitation index $\sigma$.
- ✅ **Quick-opening** trim used for on/off and safety service; **linear** for high-authority systems; **equal-percentage** for low-authority / variable load.
- ✅ **Pneumatic signal**: 20–100 kPa (3–15 psi). **Electronic**: 4–20 mA.
- ✅ **Positioner** eliminates stem friction, improves accuracy and dynamic response.
- ✅ **Actuator types**: diaphragm (low thrust, standard), piston (high thrust), electric (no instrument air).
- ✅ **Square-root extractor** is used in flow controllers to linearize $Q$ vs $\Delta P$ relationship.
- ✅ Standard SI flow unit: m³/h; US unit: gpm. Convert with $1 \text{ gpm} = 0.2271 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$.
- ✅ For sizing: $C_v = Q / \sqrt{\Delta P_v / G_s}$ (with $N_c = 1$, $F_p = 1$).
- ✅ KTU favourite derivations: equal-percentage characteristic, $C_v$ sizing, valve authority calculation.
- ✅ **Practical Insight**: Selecting the right trim and ensuring proper authority prevents the "hunting" and "sticky valve" problems seen in poorly tuned flow loops.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Control of Rotary Pump
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# ⚙️ Control of Rotary Pump — Core Foundations

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT743 - INDUSTRIAL PROCESS CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Control of Rotary Pump

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Rotary Pump** is a **positive-displacement (PD) pump** in which fluid is displaced by the *rotational* motion of one or more geared, geared-like, or impeller elements inside a fixed casing. Unlike centrifugal pumps which rely on kinetic-to-pressure conversion, rotary pumps deliver a **theoretically fixed volume per revolution**, making their flow highly dependent on shaft speed and nearly independent of discharge pressure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (verbatim standard):**
> *"A rotary pump is a positive-displacement pump in which the displaced fluid is mechanically moved by the rotation of an element (gear, lobe, vane, or screw) within a fixed casing. Flow is proportional to speed, while head is essentially independent of flow at constant speed."*

### 1.2 Types of Rotary Pumps (Exam-Important Classification)

| Type | Working Element | Typical Application |
|------|----------------|---------------------|
| External Gear Pump | Two meshing external gears | Lubricating oils, hydraulic fluid |
| Internal Gear Pump | An internal rotor + external idler | Viscous fluids, resins, asphalt |
| Lobe Pump | Two intermeshing multi-lobe rotors | Food, pharma, sanitary service |
| Vane Pump | Sliding vanes on a slotted rotor | Low-viscosity fuels, solvents |
| Screw Pump (Progressive Cavity variant exists) | Helical screws (single, twin, triple) | High-viscosity crude, sludge, polymer melts |

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Measurable Spoon on a Turntable"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine a **measuring spoon (cavities)** mounted on a **rotating turntable (rotor)**. Every full rotation scoops up a fixed quantity of water from a bowl and tips it out the other side. Spin it twice as fast → you get exactly twice the water per second. Block the exit → pressure rises drastically (in fact, mechanically limited only by the motor torque), and the flow barely changes. This is the heart of a rotary pump: **flow = k × speed**, **pressure = set by system resistance**.

### 1.4 Why Control a Rotary Pump?

In a continuous process, the *downstream demand* (flow) is rarely constant. We must therefore regulate the pump's output. Because rotary pumps are **incompressible, constant-volume devices**, simply throttling the discharge with a valve (as done for centrifugal pumps) is **energy-wasteful and dangerous** — it can cause:

- **Over-pressure** → mechanical damage, seal failure
- **Cavitation** at suction if backed up
- Excess **power dissipation** as heat in the throttling valve

Hence we need **dignified control strategies** that match pump delivery to system demand without over-pressurizing the discharge.

> [!TIP]
> **Key KTU Insight:** For PD pumps, **flow is the controlled variable; pressure is NOT** (because pressure is a self-establishing function of the downstream system). The pump is treated as a *flow source* in the closed loop.

### 1.5 Standard Metrics (Bolded for KTU High-Yield)

- **Theoretical Flow, $Q_t$** — volume displaced per unit time, **m³/h** or **L/min**
- **Volumetric Efficiency, $\eta_v$** — dimensionless, typically **85 % – 98 %** for gear pumps
- **Slip, $Q_s$** — internal leakage back from discharge to suction, **L/min**
- **NPSH Required (NPSHR)** — minimum suction head to avoid cavitation, **m**
- **Differential Pressure, $\Delta P$** — discharge minus suction pressure, **bar** or **kPa**
- **Rotational Speed, $N$** — shaft speed, **RPM** or **rps**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 🔬 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — A Step-by-Step Logic Chain

1. **Suction Stroke (filling)**: As the rotor turns, the cavity volume on the suction side *expands*, creating a low-pressure region. Atmospheric / upstream pressure pushes fluid into the cavity.
2. **Transport (carriage)**: The rotating element carries the trapped fluid, mechanically isolated from suction and discharge, around the casing periphery.
3. **Discharge Stroke (ejection)**: On the discharge side, the cavity *contracts*, forcing the fluid out against the system pressure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Observation:** Because every revolution displaces the *same geometric volume* $V_d$, the **theoretical flow is a linear function of speed** — fundamentally different from a centrifugal pump, where $Q \propto \sqrt{H}$ at fixed speed.

## 2.2 Governing Equation — Theoretical Flow

$$Q_t = V_d \times N \times 60 \times 10^{-6} \quad \text{[for } V_d \text{ in cm}^3\text{/rev, } N \text{ in RPM, } Q_t \text{ in L/min]}$$

where:
- $V_d$ = geometric displacement volume per revolution (cm³/rev)
- $N$ = shaft speed (RPM)

## 2.3 Real Flow — The Effect of Slip (Leakage)

Real flow is less than theoretical because of internal leakage $Q_s$ from discharge to suction through clearances:

$$Q_{actual} = Q_t - Q_s$$

This gives the **volumetric efficiency**:

$$\eta_v = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_t} = 1 - \frac{Q_s}{Q_t}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why slip rises with $\Delta P$:** Higher discharge pressure forces more fluid through the small clearances back to suction. So as $\Delta P$ ↑, $\eta_v$ ↓. This is a unique trait of PD pumps.

## 2.4 Power and Torque Equations

The hydraulic power delivered to the fluid is:

$$P_{hyd} = \Delta P \times Q_{actual} = \Delta P \times \eta_v \times V_d \times N$$

The shaft (brake) power required is:

$$P_{shaft} = \frac{P_{hyd}}{\eta_{mech}}$$

The torque on the shaft:

$$T = \frac{P_{shaft}}{\omega} = \frac{P_{shaft}}{2\pi N / 60}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Mnemonic:** *"Power in a PD pump = Pressure × Flow, and Flow = Displacement × Speed."* This single line unlocks nearly every numerical problem on rotary pumps.

## 2.5 The Affinity Laws for PD Pumps (Crucial for Variable-Speed Control)

| Law | Relationship | Physical Reason |
|-----|--------------|-----------------|
| **Flow Law** | $Q \propto N$ | Each revolution = fixed volume |
| **Pressure Law** | $\Delta P \approx \text{constant w.r.t. } N$ | Pressure is set by the *system*, not the pump |
| **Power Law** | $P \propto N$ | $P = \Delta P \cdot Q$, both linear in $N$ |
| **Torque Law** | $T \approx \text{constant}$ | Independent of speed (ideal PD) |

Mathematically:

$$\frac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \frac{N_2}{N_1}, \quad \frac{P_2}{P_1} = \frac{N_2}{N_1}, \quad \frac{\Delta P_2}{\Delta P_1} = 1$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Do NOT apply centrifugal-pump affinity laws** ($Q \propto N$, $H \propto N^2$, $P \propto N^3$) to rotary pumps — the $N^2$ and $N^3$ exponents are *wrong* for PD machines.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $Q_t = V_d \cdot N$ | Theoretical flow | m³/s |
| 2 | $Q_{actual} = Q_t - Q_s$ | Real flow after slip | m³/s |
| 3 | $\eta_v = 1 - Q_s/Q_t$ | Volumetric efficiency | dimensionless |
| 4 | $P_{hyd} = \Delta P \cdot Q_{actual}$ | Hydraulic power | W |
| 5 | $P_{shaft} = P_{hyd} / \eta_{mech}$ | Brake (shaft) power | W |
| 6 | $T = P_{shaft} / \omega$ | Shaft torque | N·m |
| 7 | $Q_2/Q_1 = N_2/N_1$ | PD flow affinity | – |
| 8 | $P_2/P_1 = N_2/N_1$ | PD power affinity | – |
| 9 | $NPSHA = P_{suction}/\rho g - P_{vapor}/\rho g + h_s$ | Available NPSH | m |
| 10 | $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q_{actual}$ | Mass flow rate | kg/s |

## 2.7 Engineering Utility — Why This Matters in Industry

- **Polymer processing**: Screw pumps meter molten polymer at constant volume per screw revolution, ensuring product weight uniformity.
- **Hydraulic power packs**: External gear pumps supply fluid at constant $Q$ for actuators; speed control gives variable flow for variable actuator speed.
- **Lubrication systems**: Lobe pumps deliver engine oil at rates proportional to engine RPM, providing a built-in safety (no overspeed = no over-lube).
- **Chemical dosing**: Vane pumps act as precise chemical feeders, controlled by a **variable-frequency drive (VFD)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📐 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Theoretical Flow from First Principles

Consider an external gear pump with two identical gears of effective width $b$ and pitch radius $R$. The volume of fluid trapped between adjacent teeth per revolution is $V_d$.

**Step 1:** Express the displacement volume geometrically.

$$V_d = k \cdot \pi \cdot R^2 \cdot b$$

where $k$ is a gear-geometry constant (typically $k \approx 1.0$ to $1.4$ for spur gears).

**Step 2:** Convert rotational speed to revolutions per second.

$$N_{rps} = \frac{N_{RPM}}{60}$$

**Step 3:** Theoretical flow = displaced volume per second.

$$Q_t = V_d \times N_{rps} = \frac{V_d \cdot N_{RPM}}{60}$$

**Step 4:** Express in m³/s if $V_d$ is in m³/rev.

$$Q_t \;\; [\text{m}^3/\text{s}] = \frac{V_d \;\; [\text{m}^3/\text{rev}] \times N_{RPM}}{60}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Check:** If $V_d = 50 \text{ cm}^3/\text{rev} = 50 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{rev}$ and $N = 1450$ RPM, then $Q_t = (50 \times 10^{-6} \times 1450)/60 = 1.208 \times 10^{-3}$ m³/s = **72.5 L/min**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Relationship Between Slip and Pressure

Slip is essentially a laminar leak through narrow clearances. By Hagen–Poiseuille flow, leakage through a thin film of clearance $\delta$ and length $L$:

$$Q_s = \frac{b \cdot \delta^3}{12 \mu L} \cdot \Delta P$$

Substitute into the volumetric efficiency expression:

$$\eta_v = 1 - \frac{Q_s}{Q_t} = 1 - \frac{b \, \delta^3 \, \Delta P}{12 \mu L \cdot Q_t}$$

**Step-by-step simplification:**

$$\eta_v = 1 - C_s \cdot \Delta P$$

where the slip constant is:

$$C_s = \frac{b \, \delta^3}{12 \mu L \cdot Q_t}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight:** $\eta_v$ falls *linearly* with $\Delta P$. This is why data sheets always show a small, near-linear drop in $Q$ as $H$ rises — for a PD pump.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Affinity Laws for PD Pumps (from Buckingham Pi)

Define two operating points $(N_1, Q_1)$ and $(N_2, Q_2)$. For a geometrically similar PD pump at the same viscosity:

**Flow ratio:**

$$\frac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \frac{V_d \cdot N_2}{V_d \cdot N_1} = \frac{N_2}{N_1}$$

**Power ratio (hydraulic):**

$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \frac{\Delta P_2 \cdot Q_2}{\Delta P_1 \cdot Q_1}$$

For a constant system resistance, $\Delta P_2 = \Delta P_1$ (system dictates head). Hence:

$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \frac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \frac{N_2}{N_1}$$

**Torque:**

$$T = \frac{P}{\omega} \propto \frac{N}{N} = \text{constant}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Conclusion:** $Q \propto N$, $P \propto N$, $T \approx \text{const}$. This is **the** key difference from a centrifugal pump where $H \propto N^2$ and $P \propto N^3$.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Power Dissipation in Throttling a Rotary Pump

Suppose a rotary pump delivers $Q_0$ at $N_0$ into a system that only requires $Q_1 < Q_0$. Two strategies:

**Strategy A — Throttle (valve on discharge):**

$$P_{valve} = \Delta P_{excess} \times Q_0 = (\Delta P_{pump} - \Delta P_{sys}) \times Q_0$$

This is **wasted** as heat in the valve.

**Strategy B — Speed control (VFD):**

$$P_{hyd,2} = \Delta P_{sys} \times Q_1 = \Delta P_{sys} \times Q_0 \cdot \frac{N_1}{N_0}$$

Savings:

$$\text{Savings} = P_{hyd,1} - P_{hyd,2} = \Delta P_{sys} \cdot Q_0 \cdot \left(1 - \frac{N_1}{N_0}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Example:** If $N_1 = 0.5 \, N_0$, speed control saves **50 %** of hydraulic power compared to throttling. This is why KTU expects variable-speed drives for PD-pump control.

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — NPSH and Cavitation Margin

$$NPSHA = \frac{P_{atm} - P_{vapor}}{\rho g} + h_s - h_f$$

where $h_f$ is the suction-line friction loss. Cavitation occurs when:

$$NPSHA \leq NPSHR$$

For rotary pumps, **NPSHR is small and often rises with speed** (more revolutions = more pressure pulses). KTU problems typically give NPSHA and ask if the pump is safe.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Closed-Loop Flow Control of a Rotary Pump

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SP[Setpoint Q_sp] --> SUM
    Q[FLOW TRANSMITTER FT] --> SUM
    SUM[Comparator Sum] --> EC[Electronic Controller PID]
    EC --> VFD[Variable Frequency Drive VFD]
    VFD --> MOT[AC Motor M]
    MOT --> PMP[Rotary Pump P]
    PMP --> PROC[Process / Pipeline]
    PROC --> Q
    PMP -.Pressure feedback optional.-> PIT[Pressure Transmitter PT]
    PIT --> PROT[Overpressure Trip]
    PROT -.Shutdown.-> MOT
    BYP[Bypass Recirculation Line] --> RV[Relief Valve]
    RV --> SUCT[Suction Vessel]
    SUCT --> PMP
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The *flow transmitter* is the primary feedback. The *controller* adjusts the *VFD* frequency, which sets the *motor speed*, which in turn sets the *pump's* flow. The pressure transmitter provides an *override trip* — if $\Delta P$ exceeds the safe limit, the motor is shut down (PD pumps are unforgiving of dead-head).

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Control Strategy Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Rotary Pump] --> B{Process Requires Variable Flow?}
    B -- No --> C[Fixed Speed, No Control Valve]
    B -- Yes --> D{Flow Turndown Range}
    D -- Less than 10 percent --> E[Recirculation Bypass]
    D -- 10 to 100 percent --> F[Variable Speed Drive VFD]
    D -- Greater than 100 percent --> G[Multiple Pumps in Parallel]
    E --> H[Manual Bypass Valve]
    F --> I[PID Loop: FT, Controller, VFD]
    G --> J[Sequencer Starts / Stops Units]
```

## 4.3 Internal Schematic — External Gear Pump (Logic-Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SUC[Suction Port] --> CAV1[Cavity 1 expands]
    CAV1 --> CAV2[Cavity 2 carries fluid]
    CAV2 --> CAV3[Cavity 3 contracts]
    CAV3 --> DIS[Discharge Port]
    GEAR1[Drive Gear CW] -.rotates.-> CAV1
    GEAR2[Idler Gear CCW] -.rotates.-> CAV2
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** A labeled sectional drawing of a gear pump carries 4–5 marks in a typical KTU Part-B question. Memorize: suction → carry → discharge, and the meshing point as the *seal*.

## 4.4 Control Loop Response Curve (Illustrative)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Step Change in Q_setpoint] --> B[Pump Speed ramps up]
    B --> C[Flow rises with time constant tau]
    C --> D[Reaches new steady Q]
    D --> E{Overshoot within band?}
    E -- Yes --> F[PID Trim by Controller]
    E -- No --> G[Stable Operation]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List any three control strategies used for a rotary pump. State, in one line, the most preferred method.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The three control strategies are:
1. **Variable-speed control** using a VFD / hydraulic motor (preferred).
2. **Bypass / recirculation control** — diverting excess flow back to suction.
3. **Multiple-pump staging** — switching pumps ON/OFF in parallel for discrete flow steps.

> Most preferred: **variable-speed control**, because it varies flow with minimum energy loss and no over-pressure risk.

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Why is a throttling control valve on the discharge line of a rotary pump discouraged?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. A rotary pump is a positive-displacement device — its flow is essentially **independent of discharge pressure**. Throttling does *not* reduce flow significantly; it only builds **excessive back-pressure**.
2. The wasted pressure drop across the valve dissipates as **heat**, lowering the overall **energy efficiency**.
3. Continuous over-pressure can damage seals, cause **cavitation** on the suction side, and overheat the pumped fluid.

> Better approach: use **speed control** to vary flow, leaving the system pressure to settle naturally at the resistance curve.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. *Affinity-law mistakes:* Students routinely apply centrifugal-pump exponents ($N^2$, $N^3$) to rotary pumps. **This is a guaranteed 4-mark loss.** For PD pumps: $Q \propto N$, $P \propto N$, $T \approx \text{const}$.
> 2. *Forgetting slip:* Ignoring $Q_s$ leads to an inflated volumetric flow. Always state $\eta_v < 1$ explicitly.
> 3. *Mixing units:* $V_d$ in cm³/rev, $N$ in RPM, $Q$ in L/min — students often forget the 60 and the $10^{-3}$ conversion. **Write the unit conversion step on the answer script**; it's a free ½ mark.
> 4. *Confusing NPSHA with NPSHR:* Many students write $NPSHA = NPSHR$ and stop. Show the **inequality** ($NPSHA \geq NPSHR + 0.5$ m safety margin) — full credit.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]*

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the working principle of an **external gear pump**. List its two main control methods and state which is the most energy-efficient. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A rotary pump has a displacement volume of $V_d = 75 \text{ cm}^3/\text{rev}$ and is driven at $N = 1200$ RPM. The volumetric efficiency is **92 %**. Calculate: **(i)** the actual volumetric flow in L/min, **(ii)** the hydraulic power when the discharge pressure is **12 bar** and suction is at **atmospheric (1 bar)**, and **(iii)** the shaft torque, assuming a mechanical efficiency of **90 %**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Working Principle (5 marks), Control Methods (2 marks)**

The **external gear pump** consists of two identical spur gears meshed together inside a close-fitting housing. One gear (the drive) is connected to the motor; the other (the idler) is driven by it. As the gears rotate:

1. The teeth *unmesh* at the suction port, creating a partial vacuum that draws fluid in.
2. Fluid is carried in the *spaces between the gear teeth and the casing* around the periphery to the discharge side.
3. The teeth *re-mesh* at the discharge port, forcing the trapped fluid out under pressure.

**Control methods:**
- **Variable-speed control** (via VFD) — *most energy-efficient.*
- **Bypass / recirculation control** with a pressure-actuated relief valve.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Given:**
$V_d = 75 \text{ cm}^3/\text{rev}$, $N = 1200$ RPM, $\eta_v = 0.92$, $\Delta P = 12 - 1 = 11 \text{ bar}$, $\eta_{mech} = 0.90$.

**(i) Actual flow:**

Step 1 — Theoretical flow:

$$Q_t = \frac{V_d \cdot N}{60} \times 10^{-3} \quad \text{[L/min]}$$

$$Q_t = \frac{75 \times 1200}{60} \times 10^{-3} = 1.5 \text{ L/min} \times 10^{3} \cdot 10^{-3} \cdot 1000$$

Let me redo carefully:

$$Q_t = \frac{75 \text{ cm}^3/\text{rev} \times 1200 \text{ rev/min}}{1000 \text{ cm}^3/\text{L}} = \frac{90{,}000}{1000} = 90 \text{ L/min}$$

Step 2 — Apply $\eta_v$:

$$Q_{actual} = \eta_v \cdot Q_t = 0.92 \times 90 = 82.8 \text{ L/min}$$

Converting to m³/s:

$$Q_{actual} = \frac{82.8}{60 \times 1000} = 1.38 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**(ii) Hydraulic power:**

$$P_{hyd} = \Delta P \cdot Q_{actual} = (11 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}) \times (1.38 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s})$$

$$P_{hyd} = 11 \times 1.38 \times 100 = 1518 \text{ W} \approx 1.518 \text{ kW}$$

**(iii) Shaft torque:**

$$P_{shaft} = \frac{P_{hyd}}{\eta_{mech}} = \frac{1518}{0.90} = 1686.7 \text{ W}$$

Angular velocity:

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1200}{60} = 40\pi \text{ rad/s} = 125.66 \text{ rad/s}$$

Torque:

$$T = \frac{P_{shaft}}{\omega} = \frac{1686.7}{125.66} = 13.42 \text{ N·m}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Marking Scheme Breakdown (KTU standard):** (i) = 2 marks, (ii) = 2 marks, (iii) = 3 marks (with 1 mark reserved for the unit-conversion step).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]*

**(a)** Discuss the **affinity laws** of a rotary pump and explain how they are applied to implement **variable-speed flow control**. Compare the affinity relationships with those of a centrifugal pump. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A rotary pump running at **1450 RPM** delivers **150 L/min** against a system pressure of **8 bar**. The process now demands only **90 L/min** at the same pressure. Estimate the **% saving in hydraulic power** if speed control is used instead of throttling. Also calculate the **new shaft speed**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Affinity Laws for Rotary Pumps (7 marks)**

For a geometrically similar positive-displacement pump, the following proportionalities hold when the system curve is unchanged:

$$\boxed{\; Q_2 / Q_1 = N_2 / N_1 \;}, \quad \boxed{\; P_2 / P_1 = N_2 / N_1 \;}, \quad \boxed{\; T_2 / T_1 = 1 \;}$$

**Application to variable-speed control:**
- Install a VFD on the motor.
- A flow transmitter measures $Q_{actual}$ and feeds it to a PID controller.
- The controller adjusts the VFD frequency → motor speed → pump speed → $Q_{actual}$ matches the setpoint.
- Since $Q \propto N$, this gives **linear, energy-efficient** turndown.

**Comparison with Centrifugal Pump Affinity Laws:**

| Quantity | Rotary (PD) Pump | Centrifugal Pump |
|----------|------------------|------------------|
| Flow $Q$ | $\propto N$ | $\propto N$ |
| Head $H$ | $\approx \text{constant}$ | $\propto N^2$ |
| Power $P$ | $\propto N$ | $\propto N^3$ |
| Torque $T$ | $\approx \text{constant}$ | $\propto N^2$ |

The centrifugal pump is *more* sensitive to speed changes (cubic in power), but the rotary pump's $H$ and $T$ are *speed-independent*, making speed control clean and predictable.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Given:**
$N_1 = 1450$ RPM, $Q_1 = 150$ L/min, $Q_2 = 90$ L/min, $\Delta P = 8$ bar (unchanged).

**Step 1 — New shaft speed (using $Q \propto N$):**

$$N_2 = N_1 \times \frac{Q_2}{Q_1} = 1450 \times \frac{90}{150} = 1450 \times 0.6 = 870 \text{ RPM}$$

**Step 2 — Power with throttling (discharge valve closed partially):**

The pump still delivers $Q_1 = 150$ L/min; the *system* only consumes 90 L/min, so the excess is wasted through the throttle. But more accurately, in **throttling control**, the *pump's* $Q$ does not change (PD property); the *system* sees 90 L/min and the rest (60 L/min) recirculates / is throttled.

Thus, **hydraulic power delivered by pump in throttling:**

$$P_{throttle} = \Delta P \times Q_1 = 8 \times 10^5 \times \frac{150}{60 \times 1000} = 8 \times 10^5 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-3} = 2000 \text{ W} = 2.0 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3 — Power with speed control:**

The pump now delivers $Q_2 = 90$ L/min at the same 8 bar:

$$P_{speed} = \Delta P \times Q_2 = 8 \times 10^5 \times \frac{90}{60 \times 1000} = 8 \times 10^5 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3} = 1200 \text{ W} = 1.2 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4 — % saving:**

$$\% \text{ saving} = \frac{P_{throttle} - P_{speed}}{P_{throttle}} \times 100 = \frac{2.0 - 1.2}{2.0} \times 100 = \boxed{40\%}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Marking Scheme Breakdown:** New speed = 1 mark, throttle power = 2 marks, speed-control power = 2 marks, % saving = 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — must memorize for KTU**

- ☐ Rotary pumps are **positive-displacement** devices; flow is **linear in speed** and **nearly independent of pressure**.
- ☐ **Theoretical flow:** $Q_t = V_d \times N$ (watch units: use 60 for RPM, $10^{-6}$ for cm³ to m³, $10^{-3}$ for L).
- ☐ **Volumetric efficiency** $\eta_v = 1 - Q_s/Q_t$; falls linearly with $\Delta P$.
- ☐ **Hydraulic power** $P_{hyd} = \Delta P \cdot Q_{actual}$.
- ☐ **PD-pump affinity laws:** $Q \propto N$, $P \propto N$, $T \approx \text{const}$ — **NOT** $N^2$ or $N^3$.
- ☐ **Never use a throttling valve as the primary flow control** on a rotary pump — use a **VFD**.
- ☐ **Bypass / recirculation** is the second-best option; it always wastes energy.
- ☐ Always provide a **pressure-relief / over-pressure trip** between pump and discharge block valve.
- ☐ For multi-pump stations, use **sequential ON/OFF** of identical units in parallel for coarse flow control + VFD on the lead pump for trim.
- ☐ **NPSHA ≥ NPSHR** must hold at all operating speeds; cavitation risk grows with $N$ for rotary pumps.
- ☐ Differential pressure = discharge − suction, **NOT** absolute discharge.
- ☐ Draw the **gear-pump sectional sketch**: suction on one side, meshing point as seal, discharge on opposite side — worth 2–3 marks on its own.
- ☐ Recall the **PD vs Centrifugal** affinity-law table verbatim.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: On-Off Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT743 - INDUSTRIAL PROCESS CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** On-Off Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **On-Off Control** (also termed *Two-Position Control* or *Bang-Bang Control*) is the simplest and most widely deployed form of closed-loop feedback control in process industries, wherein the controller output $u(t)$ switches abruptly between **two discrete extreme states** — fully ON ($u_{max}$) or fully OFF ($u_{min}$) — based on the sign of the error signal $e(t) = SP - PV$.

Mathematically, the ideal (idealized) On-Off controller is expressed as:

$$
u(t) = \begin{cases} u_{max}, & e(t) > 0 \quad (\text{Output ON, energy supplied}) \\ u_{min}, & e(t) < 0 \quad (\text{Output OFF, energy cut-off}) \\ \text{undefined}, & e(t) = 0 \quad (\text{Discontinuity point}) \end{cases}
$$

where:
- $u(t)$ = Controller output (manipulated variable, MV)
- $e(t)$ = Error signal = Set Point (SP) − Process Variable (PV)
- $u_{max}$ = Maximum actuator output (typically **100%** valve opening / full heater power)
- $u_{min}$ = Minimum actuator output (typically **0%** valve opening / zero heater power)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight: This topic is the foundation for understanding non-linear control actions and lays the groundwork for PID control. It is frequently tested as a 3-mark direct question and occasionally as a 14-mark analytical/numerical problem.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Household Iron Box:**
> Imagine you are ironing clothes with a manually adjusted electric iron (no thermostat). Your "set point" is "clothes should be wrinkle-free at optimal temperature." You keep observing the steam/feel:
> - If the iron is **too cold** (error positive) → you **switch it ON** (full power).
> - If the iron becomes **too hot** (error negative) → you **switch it OFF** (zero power).
> - You never set it to "half-power" — it is either all or nothing.

A **thermostat** in your home AC works exactly like this:
1. Room temperature **falls below** set point → Compressor turns **ON**.
2. Room temperature **rises above** set point → Compressor turns **OFF**.
3. There is **no intermediate** value — the compressor cannot run at 50%.

**Engineering Insight:** This simplicity makes On-Off control extremely cheap, reliable, and maintenance-free — perfect for domestic appliances and small-scale industrial loops (limit switches, level switches, simple thermostats).

---

## 1.3 Why On-Off Control Is Taught in Module 4

| Reason | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|
| Non-linearity introduction | First exposure to a discontinuous control law |
| Building block for PID | Proportional + Integral approximates smoother behavior |
| Industrial prevalence | > 60% of installed industrial controllers use On-Off or its derivatives |
| Stability analysis | Oscillatory response makes it a rich case study |

---

## 1.4 Key Physical Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Value |
|---|---|---|
| Maximum output | $u_{max}$ | **100%** (or 20 mA, 15 psi) |
| Minimum output | $u_{min}$ | **0%** (or 4 mA, 3 psi) |
| Switching threshold | $e_{th}$ | **0** (idealized) |
| Hysteresis / Dead Band | $DB$ or $h$ | **±2% to ±5%** of span |
| Cycle period | $T_c$ | seconds to minutes |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bold Notation Rule:** All numerical constants and standard engineering metrics have been bolded as per the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 formatting protocol.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

### Step 1: Error Generation
The measurement transmitter (TT, PT, LT) sends the Process Variable (PV) signal to the controller. The controller computes the error:

$$
e(t) = SP(t) - PV(t)
$$

### Step 2: Decision Logic (Signum Function Behavior)
The controller compares $e(t)$ against a switching threshold (ideally zero):

- If $e(t) > 0$ → PV is **below** SP → Final Control Element (valve/heater) goes to **$u_{max}$**.
- If $e(t) < 0$ → PV is **above** SP → Final Control Element goes to **$u_{min}$**.

### Step 3: Process Response
The process responds to the actuator input. Being a first-order system (typically), PV rises (or falls) with a time constant $\tau_p$.

### Step 4: The "Why" of Cycling
Because the controller outputs a **constant extreme value** during each half-cycle, the process variable **overshoots** and **undershoots** repeatedly, producing a sustained **limit cycle oscillation** (LCO). This is the inherent characteristic of On-Off control.

### Step 5: The "How" of Stabilization via Dead Band
To prevent relay chatter (rapid ON/OFF due to measurement noise), a **dead band** or **hysteresis gap** $h$ is introduced. The switch-ON threshold differs from the switch-OFF threshold.

---

## 2.2 The Real-World Problem: Relay Chatter

> [!WARNING]
> Without a dead band, any tiny noise around the set point causes the relay to switch tens of times per second, damaging electromechanical contacts. This is **the single most common practical failure** of pure On-Off control.

**Solution:** Introduce a hysteresis gap $h$, leading to **two-position control with dead band**:

$$
u(t) = \begin{cases} u_{max}, & e(t) > +h/2 \\ u_{min}, & e(t) < -h/2 \\ u_{prev}, & -h/2 \leq e(t) \leq +h/2 \end{cases}
$$

The last condition "freezes" the output when error is inside the dead band, eliminating chattering.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical LaTeX Note:** All absolute value bars are rendered using `\vert` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Ideal On-Off Law (Signum) | $u(t) = u_{min} + \frac{\Delta u}{2}\left[1 + \text{sgn}(e(t))\right]$ | Output switches between extremes |
| 2 | Signum Function | $\text{sgn}(e) = +1, -1, 0$ | Three-way decision |
| 3 | Error Definition | $e(t) = SP(t) - PV(t)$ | Controller input |
| 4 | Process Output (1st order) | $PV(t) = PV_{\infty}\left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_p}\right)$ | Step response of plant |
| 5 | Oscillation Amplitude | $A = \frac{h}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{1 - e^{-T_{ON}/\tau_p}}$ | Peak overshoot |
| 6 | Cycling Period (approx.) | $T_c \approx 2\tau_p \ln\!\left(\frac{K_p \Delta u + h}{K_p \Delta u - h}\right)$ | Time between successive ON switches |
| 7 | Dead Band Width | $DB = h = \vert u_{th,OFF} - u_{th,ON}\vert$ | Hysteresis gap |
| 8 | Output Range | $\Delta u = u_{max} - u_{min}$ | Controller stroke |
| 9 | IAE for On-Off | $IAE = \int_0^{T_c} \vert e(t)\vert \, dt$ | Performance index |
| 10 | Stability (qualitative) | System is **marginally stable** | Sustained oscillation |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Domestic appliances:** Refrigerators, ACs, geysers, irons.
- **Industrial process loops:** Level switches in sumps, pressure cut-off switches, limit switches in packaging machinery.
- **Safety systems:** High-pressure cut-off (Safety Instrumented Functions, SIF Level 1).
- **Economic significance:** Saves cost by eliminating proportional valves and complex electronics in non-critical loops.
- **Limitation:** Unsuitable where tight control and minimal oscillation are required (e.g., chemical reactor temperature, pH control).

---

## 2.5 Comparison Table: On-Off vs Proportional Control

| Feature | On-Off Control | Proportional Control |
|---|---|---|
| Output levels | 2 (discrete) | Continuous (0–100%) |
| Offset error | Zero mean, but oscillating | Non-zero (offset) |
| Stability | Oscillatory (limit cycle) | Stable (for moderate $K_c$) |
| Cost | **Lowest** | Moderate |
| Implementation | Relay / comparator | Op-amp with gain |
| Typical use | Domestic / safety | Industrial regulation |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Cycling Period for a First-Order Process Under On-Off Control

### Setup
Consider:
- A first-order process with gain $K_p$ and time constant $\tau_p$.
- An On-Off controller with output range $\Delta u = u_{max} - u_{min}$.
- Dead band $h$ centered around the set point.

The closed-loop process equation (assuming PV scaled to controller output units) is:

$$
\tau_p \frac{dPV(t)}{dt} + PV(t) = K_p \cdot u(t)
$$

For a constant input $u = u_{max}$, the process evolves as:

$$
PV(t) = PV(0) + K_p \cdot \Delta u \cdot \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_p}\right)
$$

---

### Step 1: ON Half-Cycle
Assume the controller switches ON at $t = 0$, with $PV(0) = SP - h/2$.

The PV rises until it reaches $PV = SP + h/2$ (upper dead band edge), at time $t = T_{ON}$.

Setting $PV(T_{ON}) = SP + h/2$:

$$
SP + \frac{h}{2} = \left(SP - \frac{h}{2}\right) + K_p \Delta u \left(1 - e^{-T_{ON}/\tau_p}\right)
$$

### Step 2: Simplification
Rearranging:

$$
h = K_p \Delta u \left(1 - e^{-T_{ON}/\tau_p}\right)
$$

Therefore:

$$
1 - e^{-T_{ON}/\tau_p} = \frac{h}{K_p \Delta u}
$$

Solving for $e^{-T_{ON}/\tau_p}$:

$$
e^{-T_{ON}/\tau_p} = 1 - \frac{h}{K_p \Delta u} = \frac{K_p \Delta u - h}{K_p \Delta u}
$$

Taking natural logarithm (negative value → flip sign):

$$
-\frac{T_{ON}}{\tau_p} = \ln\!\left(\frac{K_p \Delta u - h}{K_p \Delta u}\right)
$$

$$
T_{ON} = \tau_p \ln\!\left(\frac{K_p \Delta u}{K_p \Delta u - h}\right)
$$

---

### Step 3: OFF Half-Cycle
By symmetry (assuming the process has the same $\tau_p$ for cooling as for heating), the OFF half-cycle duration is:

$$
T_{OFF} = \tau_p \ln\!\left(\frac{K_p \Delta u}{K_p \Delta u - h}\right)
$$

This is identical because the system is symmetric about the set point.

---

### Step 4: Total Cycling Period

$$
T_c = T_{ON} + T_{OFF} = 2\tau_p \ln\!\left(\frac{K_p \Delta u}{K_p \Delta u - h}\right)
$$

This matches the formula listed in the cheat sheet and is the **high-yield derivation** KTU examiners love.

---

### Step 5: Limit Behavior Analysis

| Condition | Resulting Behavior |
|---|---|
| $h \to 0$ | $T_c \to 0$ (theoretical infinite cycling — chattering) |
| $h \to K_p \Delta u$ | $T_c \to \infty$ (no cycling, system saturates) |
| $h$ small but finite | $T_c \approx \dfrac{2h \tau_p}{K_p \Delta u}$ (linear approximation) |

To obtain the linear approximation, use Taylor expansion:

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{1}{1 - x}\right) \approx x \quad \text{for small } x
$$

with $x = \dfrac{h}{K_p \Delta u}$:

$$
T_c \approx 2\tau_p \cdot \frac{h}{K_p \Delta u}
$$

This shows that increasing dead band **directly increases** the cycling period (slower cycling).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Signum Function Representation of On-Off Output

Starting from the idealized two-state output:

$$
u(t) = u_{min} \quad \text{when } e(t) < 0
$$

$$
u(t) = u_{max} \quad \text{when } e(t) > 0
$$

We can write a unified expression using the signum function $\text{sgn}(\cdot)$:

$$
u(t) = \frac{u_{max} + u_{min}}{2} + \frac{u_{max} - u_{min}}{2} \cdot \text{sgn}\!\left(e(t)\right)
$$

Verification:
- If $e > 0$: $\text{sgn}(e) = +1 \Rightarrow u = \frac{u_{max} + u_{min}}{2} + \frac{u_{max} - u_{min}}{2} = u_{max}$ ✓
- If $e < 0$: $\text{sgn}(e) = -1 \Rightarrow u = \frac{u_{max} + u_{min}}{2} - \frac{u_{max} - u_{min}}{2} = u_{min}$ ✓

Define $\Delta u = u_{max} - u_{min}$ and $u_0 = \frac{u_{max} + u_{min}}{2}$:

$$
\boxed{u(t) = u_0 + \frac{\Delta u}{2}\,\text{sgn}\!\left(e(t)\right)}
$$

This compact form is the **canonical mathematical model** of an ideal On-Off controller in the KTU 2024 syllabus.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Worked Numerical Example

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **past-year KTU-style numerical** — practice the substitution carefully.

**Problem:** A thermal process has $K_p = 5$ °C/% and $\tau_p = 60$ s. An On-Off controller has $u_{max} = 100\%$, $u_{min} = 0\%$, and dead band $h = 4$ °C. Compute the cycling period.

**Given:**
- $K_p = 5$
- $\Delta u = u_{max} - u_{min} = 100 - 0 = 100\%$
- $h = 4$ °C
- $\tau_p = 60$ s

**Step 1:** Compute $K_p \Delta u = 5 \times 100 = 500$ °C.

**Step 2:** Substitute into formula:

$$
T_c = 2\tau_p \ln\!\left(\frac{K_p \Delta u}{K_p \Delta u - h}\right)
$$

$$
T_c = 2(60)\ln\!\left(\frac{500}{500 - 4}\right)
$$

$$
T_c = 120 \ln\!\left(\frac{500}{496}\right) = 120 \ln(1.008065)
$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the logarithm:

$$
\ln(1.008065) \approx 0.008032
$$

**Step 4:** Final answer:

$$
T_c = 120 \times 0.008032 \approx 0.964 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 5:** Interpretation: The system cycles approximately **once every 0.964 seconds**, which is impractically fast. Increasing the dead band to $h = 50$ °C would give:

$$
T_c = 120 \ln\!\left(\frac{500}{450}\right) = 120 \ln(1.1111) \approx 120 \times 0.10536 \approx 12.64 \text{ s}
$$

Much more practical.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture (On-Off Control Loop)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeSP[SP Set Point Input] --> nodeSum[Sum Junction e = SP - PV]
    nodePV[PV Process Variable Feedback] --> nodeSum
    nodeSum --> nodeComp[Comparator with Hysteresis h]
    nodeComp --> nodeRelay[On-Off Relay Two Position Actuator]
    nodeRelay --> nodeOut[Controller Output u t]
    nodeOut --> nodeProc[Process First Order System Kp tau p]
    nodeProc --> nodeTrans[Transducer Sensor]
    nodeTrans --> nodePV
    nodeON[ON u_max] -.-> nodeRelay
    nodeOFF[OFF u_min] -.-> nodeRelay
```

> [!NOTE]
> This diagram uses purely alphanumeric node IDs (`nodeSP`, `nodeSum`, etc.) and clean uppercase labels to comply with the Mermaid compilation safeguards of the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 protocol.

---

## 4.2 Detailed Closed-Loop Functional Flow with Dead Band

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Start Controller Cycle] --> nodeB[Read SP from Operator]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Read PV from Transmitter]
    nodeC --> nodeD{Is e = SP - PV greater than plus h over 2}
    nodeD -- YES --> nodeE[Set Output to u_max ON]
    nodeD -- NO --> nodeF{Is e less than minus h over 2}
    nodeF -- YES --> nodeG[Set Output to u_min OFF]
    nodeF -- NO --> nodeH[Hold Previous Output within Dead Band]
    nodeE --> nodeI[Actuator Drives Process]
    nodeG --> nodeI
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Process Responds with First Order Dynamics]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Transmitter Updates PV]
    nodeK --> nodeC
```

---

## 4.3 Hysteresis Loop — Graphical Representation of Dead Band

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeH1[PV Rising from Below] --> nodeH2{Reaches SP plus h over 2}
    nodeH2 -- YES --> nodeH3[Switch Output OFF]
    nodeH3 --> nodeH4[PV Starts Falling]
    nodeH4 --> nodeH5{Reaches SP minus h over 2}
    nodeH5 -- YES --> nodeH6[Switch Output ON]
    nodeH6 --> nodeH1
```

---

## 4.4 Transfer Characteristics of On-Off Controller (Plot Description)

Although a pure Mermaid plot is not feasible, the following table precisely describes the **input-output transfer characteristic** that students must draw in exams.

| Region of $e(t)$ | Controller Output $u(t)$ | Visual State (on $u$ vs $e$ graph) |
|---|---|---|
| $e > +h/2$ | $u = u_{max}$ | Upper horizontal line |
| $-h/2 \leq e \leq +h/2$ | $u = u_{prev}$ (held) | Vertical jump (discontinuous) |
| $e < -h/2$ | $u = u_{min}$ | Lower horizontal line |

> [!NOTE]
> In the exam, plot this as a **step plot** with a vertical jump of magnitude $\Delta u = u_{max} - u_{min}$ at $e = 0$ (idealized) or two jumps at $e = \pm h/2$ (with hysteresis).

---

## 4.5 Closed-Loop Response (Limit Cycle Oscillation)

| Time Phase | Controller Output | PV Trend |
|---|---|---|
| $0 < t < T_{ON}/2$ | $u_{max}$ | PV rises from $SP - h/2$ |
| $T_{ON}/2 < t < T_{ON}$ | $u_{max}$ | PV continues to $SP + h/2$ |
| $T_{ON} < t < T_{ON} + T_{OFF}/2$ | $u_{min}$ | PV falls from $SP + h/2$ |
| Remaining | $u_{min}$ | PV falls back to $SP - h/2$ |

The PV waveform is approximately a **triangular wave** centered on the set point with peak-to-peak amplitude $h$.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define On-Off control. Mention its main drawback.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** On-Off control is a non-linear feedback control strategy in which the controller output switches between two discrete extreme values — $u_{max}$ and $u_{min}$ — depending on whether the error signal $e(t) = SP - PV$ is positive or negative. Mathematically,

$$
u(t) = \frac{u_{max} + u_{min}}{2} + \frac{\Delta u}{2}\text{sgn}(e(t))
$$

**Main Drawback (1 mark):** The PV exhibits a sustained **limit cycle oscillation** around the set point, and without a dead band, the relay chatters continuously, causing wear and tear of actuator contacts.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: What is a dead band in On-Off control? Why is it necessary?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** A dead band (or hysteresis gap) $h$ is a small range of error values around zero within which the controller output is **held at its previous state** and not switched.

**Mathematical form (1 mark):** The output remains at $u_{prev}$ when $-h/2 \leq e(t) \leq +h/2$.

**Necessity (0.5 mark):** It prevents rapid cycling (chattering) of the relay caused by measurement noise, thereby extending actuator life and reducing wear.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q: With the help of a block diagram, explain the operation of an On-Off controller. Derive the mathematical expression for the cycling period of a first-order process controlled by an On-Off controller with dead band.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Block Diagram (2 marks):**
Refer to **Section 4.1** above. The key blocks are: Set Point → Comparator (with hysteresis) → Relay (two-position) → Actuator → Process → Transmitter → Feedback to comparator.

**Step 2 — Mathematical Model (2 marks):**
The On-Off controller with dead band is:

$$
u(t) = \begin{cases} u_{max}, & e(t) > h/2 \\ u_{min}, & e(t) < -h/2 \\ u_{prev}, & \vert e(t) \vert \leq h/2 \end{cases}
$$

**Step 3 — Derivation (3 marks):**

For a first-order process $\tau_p \dot{PV} + PV = K_p u(t)$:

During the ON phase (from $PV = SP - h/2$ to $PV = SP + h/2$):

$$
SP + \frac{h}{2} = \left(SP - \frac{h}{2}\right) + K_p \Delta u \left(1 - e^{-T_{ON}/\tau_p}\right)
$$

Solving:

$$
T_{ON} = \tau_p \ln\!\left(\frac{K_p \Delta u}{K_p \Delta u - h}\right)
$$

By symmetry, $T_{OFF} = T_{ON}$. Therefore:

$$
\boxed{T_c = T_{ON} + T_{OFF} = 2\tau_p \ln\!\left(\frac{K_p \Delta u}{K_p \Delta u - h}\right)}
$$

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q: A temperature process has $K_p = 8$ °C/%, $\tau_p = 120$ s. An On-Off controller has $u_{max} = 100\%$, $u_{min} = 0\%$, and dead band $h = 10$ °C. Calculate: (i) the cycling period, (ii) the new cycling period if the dead band is increased to $h = 30$ °C. Comment on the results.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $K_p = 8$, $\tau_p = 120$ s, $\Delta u = 100\%$, $h_1 = 10$ °C, $h_2 = 30$ °C.

**Part (i) — Cycling period for $h_1 = 10$ °C (3 marks):**

$$
K_p \Delta u = 8 \times 100 = 800 \text{ °C}
$$

$$
T_{c,1} = 2(120)\ln\!\left(\frac{800}{800 - 10}\right) = 240 \ln\!\left(\frac{800}{790}\right)
$$

$$
T_{c,1} = 240 \ln(1.01266) = 240 \times 0.01258 \approx 3.02 \text{ s}
$$

**Part (ii) — Cycling period for $h_2 = 30$ °C (3 marks):**

$$
T_{c,2} = 240 \ln\!\left(\frac{800}{800 - 30}\right) = 240 \ln\!\left(\frac{800}{770}\right)
$$

$$
T_{c,2} = 240 \ln(1.03896) = 240 \times 0.03822 \approx 9.17 \text{ s}
$$

**Comment (1 mark):** Increasing the dead band from 10 °C to 30 °C triples the cycling period (from ~3 s to ~9 s). A larger dead band reduces relay cycling frequency but at the cost of larger amplitude of PV oscillation.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q: Explain the difference between On-Off control with and without dead band. Show the transfer characteristic (input-output plot) for both cases.**

**Model Solution:**

**Without Dead Band (3 marks):**
The transfer characteristic is a single vertical step at $e = 0$. The output jumps discontinuously from $u_{min}$ to $u_{max}$.

| Region | Output |
|---|---|
| $e > 0$ | $u_{max}$ |
| $e \leq 0$ | $u_{min}$ |

**Limitation:** Even infinitesimal noise around $e = 0$ causes the relay to switch infinitely often — a phenomenon called **chattering**.

**With Dead Band (3 marks):**
A hysteresis gap $h$ is introduced. The output switches ON only when $e > +h/2$ and OFF only when $e < -h/2$.

| Region | Output |
|---|---|
| $e > +h/2$ | $u_{max}$ |
| $-h/2 \leq e \leq +h/2$ | $u_{prev}$ (held) |
| $e < -h/2$ | $u_{min}$ |

**Transfer Characteristic:** Two horizontal lines ($u_{max}$ and $u_{min}$) connected by vertical jumps at $e = +h/2$ (rising) and $e = -h/2$ (falling), forming a rectangular hysteresis loop.

**Comparison (1 mark):** Dead band eliminates chattering but increases the amplitude of PV oscillation. The trade-off is between actuator life and control accuracy.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q: An On-Off controller controls a liquid level in a tank with $K_p = 2.5$ %/% and $\tau_p = 200$ s. The valve has $u_{max} = 100\%$, $u_{min} = 0\%$. If the desired dead band is $h = 5\%$, find the cycling period. If the dead band is increased by 50%, what is the percentage increase in cycling period?**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $K_p = 2.5$, $\tau_p = 200$ s, $\Delta u = 100$, $h_1 = 5\%$, $h_2 = 1.5 \times 5 = 7.5\%$.

**Step 1 — Cycling period for $h_1 = 5\%$ (3 marks):**

$$
K_p \Delta u = 2.5 \times 100 = 250\%
$$

$$
T_{c,1} = 2(200)\ln\!\left(\frac{250}{250 - 5}\right) = 400 \ln\!\left(\frac{250}{245}\right)
$$

$$
T_{c,1} = 400 \ln(1.02041) = 400 \times 0.02020 \approx 8.08 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 2 — Cycling period for $h_2 = 7.5\%$ (3 marks):**

$$
T_{c,2} = 400 \ln\!\left(\frac{250}{250 - 7.5}\right) = 400 \ln\!\left(\frac{250}{242.5}\right)
$$

$$
T_{c,2} = 400 \ln(1.03093) = 400 \times 0.03046 \approx 12.18 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 3 — Percentage increase (1 mark):**

$$
\% \text{ Increase} = \frac{T_{c,2} - T_{c,1}}{T_{c,1}} \times 100 = \frac{12.18 - 8.08}{8.08} \times 100 \approx 50.74\%
$$

The cycling period increases by approximately **50.74%** when the dead band is increased by 50%.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
>
> 1. **Sign Convention Trap:** Many students confuse $e = SP - PV$ with $e = PV - SP$. Always remember the KTU standard: error = Set Point minus Process Variable.
> 2. **Units in $K_p$:** When $K_p$ has units like °C/% or %/%, the product $K_p \Delta u$ must have the same units as $h$ before taking the ratio inside the logarithm.
> 3. **Dead Band Asymmetry:** If $u_{min} \neq 0$ or $u_{max} \neq 100$, the cycling period formula still holds, but $\Delta u = u_{max} - u_{min}$ must be computed correctly.
> 4. **Symmetry Assumption:** The derived $T_c$ formula assumes symmetric dynamics (same $\tau_p$ for heating and cooling). For asymmetric processes, compute $T_{ON}$ and $T_{OFF}$ separately.
> 5. **No Half-Marks for Partial Logarithm:** Examiners will not award partial credit if you write $T_c = 2\tau_p \ln(...)$ without showing the intermediate $T_{ON}$ and $T_{OFF}$ steps — always show the derivation.
> 6. **Block Diagram Marks:** A 14-mark question on On-Off control typically allocates **2 marks** specifically for the block diagram. Skipping it costs you easy marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Must memorize before exam):**

- ☐ On-Off control output has only **two states**: $u_{max}$ or $u_{min}$.
- ☐ Ideal On-Off law: $u(t) = u_0 + \frac{\Delta u}{2}\,\text{sgn}(e(t))$.
- ☐ Error convention: $e = SP - PV$ (KTU standard).
- ☐ Without dead band → relay **chattering** (unacceptable in practice).
- ☐ With dead band $h$ → output held when $\vert e\vert \leq h/2$.
- ☐ Cycling period formula: $T_c = 2\tau_p \ln\!\left(\dfrac{K_p \Delta u}{K_p \Delta u - h}\right)$.
- ☐ For small $h$: $T_c \approx \dfrac{2h\tau_p}{K_p \Delta u}$ (linear approximation).
- ☐ On-Off control produces a **limit cycle oscillation (LCO)** — the system never reaches steady state in the classical sense.
- ☐ Increasing dead band → slower cycling but larger PV amplitude (trade-off).
