- ☐ Applications: thermostats, refrigerators, level switches, pressure cut-offs.
- ☐ Cannot be used where tight, smooth control is required (use PID instead).
- ☐ Block diagram has 5 essential elements: SP, comparator, relay, process, feedback.
- ☐ Three industrial terms — *On-Off*, *Two-Position*, *Bang-Bang* — all refer to the same control law.
- ☐ Exam tip: Always draw the transfer characteristic (input-output plot) with hysteresis loop for full marks.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 4 Notes — On-Off Control.** These notes are aligned with the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework, and Revised Bloom's Taxonomy cognitive levels (L1–L4 coverage: Remember, Understand, Apply, Analyze).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Throttling and Safety Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT743 - INDUSTRIAL PROCESS CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Throttling and Safety Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Throttling Control — Formal Definition

In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme (PEICT743), **Throttling** refers to the controlled restriction of fluid flow within a process pipeline by adjusting a variable-area restriction element, most commonly a **Control Valve** (also called a *Final Control Element* or *Throttling Valve*). The throttling device continuously modulates the flow cross-section in response to a corrective signal from the controller, thereby regulating the manipulated variable (typically flow rate, pressure, level, or temperature) at the desired setpoint.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A control valve is the only "moving part" that physically contacts the process in a typical feedback loop. Its accuracy, speed, and rangeability dictate the closed-loop performance of the entire control system.

The fundamental energy balance across a throttling device is governed by the steady-flow energy equation (assuming no work, negligible heat transfer, and negligible kinetic-energy change):

$$
h_1 + \frac{v_1^2}{2} + gz_1 = h_2 + \frac{v_2^2}{2} + gz_2 + q - w_s
$$

For an ideal (isenthalpic) throttling process, $h_1 = h_2$ — this is the **Joule–Thomson effect**, which forms the thermodynamic basis of throttling. The pressure drop $\Delta P = P_1 - P_2$ is dissipated as turbulence and eventually as heat, but the enthalpy of the fluid is preserved.

## 1.2 Safety Control — Formal Definition

**Safety Control** is the engineered set of protective instrumentation, logic solvers, and final elements designed to bring a process to a safe state when operating conditions exceed predefined safe limits. It is implemented through:
- **Safety Instrumented Systems (SIS)**
- **Emergency Shutdown Systems (ESD)**
- **Pressure Safety Valves (PSV) / Relief Valves (PRV)**
- **Bursting Discs (Rupture Discs)**
- **Flame Arresters**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Definition Box:** A Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) is a specific safety function executed by an SIS with a defined **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)** as per **IEC 61508 / IEC 61511**. SIL levels range from **SIL 1** (lowest) to **SIL 4** (highest integrity), with target probability of failure on demand (PFD) ranging from $10^{-1}$ to $10^{-4}$.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

### Throttling — The Household Tap Analogy
Imagine the kitchen tap (faucet) connected to a municipal water supply. The municipal pressure is roughly constant, but you want variable flow (a trickle for filling a glass, full flow for washing utensils). By rotating the handle, you change the cross-sectional area through which water passes. The tap **throttles** the flow.
- More opening = less resistance = more flow.
- Less opening = more resistance = less flow.
- A control valve in a refinery does exactly this — but driven by an electro-pneumatic or electro-hydraulic actuator commanded by a PID controller rather than a human hand.

### Safety Control — The Pressure Cooker Safety Valve Analogy
A household pressure cooker has a weighted relief valve set to lift at a specific pressure (say, 1.2 kg/cm²). If the internal pressure exceeds this limit — perhaps because the user forgot to reduce the flame — the valve opens automatically, venting steam and preventing an explosion. Industrial **Safety Valves** work identically, but at much higher pressures (often 10–300 bar) and with rigorous certification.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world metric:** A typical large refinery may have 200–500 pressure safety valves, each individually sized, certified, and tested annually as per **ASME Section VIII** and **API 520/521** standards.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Value | Usage |
|---|---|---|
| Standard atmospheric pressure | **101.325 kPa** | Used as reference in PSV set-pressure calculations |
| Cv coefficient (US gallons/min at 1 psi ΔP) | **Cv** | Universal valve-sizing coefficient |
| Kv coefficient (m³/h at 1 bar ΔP) | **Kv** | European equivalent; **Cv ≈ 1.156 × Kv** |
| SIL PFD ranges | **10⁻¹ to 10⁻⁴** | Safety integrity classification |
| ASME overpressure limit | **10%** (fire case: 21%) | Maximum allowable accumulation over MAWP |
| API 520 set-pressure tolerance | **±3%** or **±2 psi** | Whichever is greater |
| Blowdown (blowback) | **2% to 7%** | Pressure difference between set and reseat pressure |

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Pneumatically-Actuated Globe Control Valve

The control valve has four main sub-assemblies:
1. **Valve Body** — contains the seat, plug, and flow passage.
2. **Actuator** — converts the controller's pneumatic (3–15 psi or 0.2–1.0 bar) or electrical (4–20 mA) signal into mechanical motion of the valve stem.
3. **Positioner** — a local feedback loop that ensures the stem position matches the controller's demanded setpoint (closed-loop on stem travel).
4. **Trim (Internal Parts)** — the plug, seat, stem, and cage, which determine the flow characteristic.

### 2.1.1 Valve Trim Types
- **Single-ported (single-seat)** — tight shut-off, lower capacity.
- **Double-ported (double-seat)** — higher capacity but leaks when fully closed (rarely used today).
- **Cage-trim** — characterized by perforations on the cage sleeve; produces a defined flow characteristic.
- **Vee-ball / V-port** — linearly characterized ball/segment, high rangeability.

## 2.2 Types of Control Valves (KTU 2024 Module 4 — High Yield)

| Valve Type | Primary Application | Capacity (Cv) | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Globe valve** | General throttling, high-pressure drop | Medium to High | Most common in process control |
| **Butterfly valve** | Large pipelines, low-pressure drop | Very High | Rotational disc; 0–90° operation |
| **Ball valve (characterized)** | On/off + throttling | Very High | Vee-ball for linear/equal-percentage |
| **Plug valve (eccentric)** | Slurry, paper stock | Medium | Shearing action, non-clogging |
| **Diaphragm valve** | Corrosive / sanitary fluids | Low to Medium | Weir or straight-through design |
| **Solenoid valve** | On/off only, fast response | N/A | Hydraulic/pneumatic actuation |

## 2.3 Control Valve Characteristics

The **inherent flow characteristic** relates the fractional valve travel $n$ to the fractional flow capacity $\phi$ at a constant pressure drop.

### 2.3.1 Linear Characteristic
$$
\phi = n
$$
At 50% lift, 50% of maximum flow passes. Best for systems where loop gain is reasonably constant.

### 2.3.2 Equal-Percentage Characteristic
The flow changes by a constant percentage for each unit change in stem position. Expressed as:
$$
\phi = R^{(n-1)}
$$
where $R$ is the **rangeability** (ratio of maximum to minimum controllable $C_v$). A typical $R = 50$ gives the classic equal-percentage curve.

### 2.3.3 Quick-Opening Characteristic
$$
\phi = \sqrt{n}
$$
Provides large flow changes at small openings. Used for safety relief applications and on/off duty.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Concept:** The *inherent* characteristic is measured at constant $\Delta P$. The *installed* characteristic is what the loop actually sees — and changes drastically as the valve consumes more of the system $\Delta P$ at high openings.

## 2.4 Rangeability & Turndown

- **Rangeability** $R = \dfrac{C_{v,\max}}{C_{v,\min}}$ (where $C_{v,\min}$ is the minimum controllable capacity, not the leakage rate).
- **Turndown** — same definition but applied to the *process* variable: $TD = \dfrac{Q_{\max}}{Q_{\min}}$.
- For an equal-percentage valve with $R = 50$, a 1% stem change near the closed end produces a 4% flow change, while a 1% stem change near the open end produces a much larger absolute change but the same percentage.

## 2.5 Valve Sizing — The Universal $C_v$ Formula

For **incompressible (liquid) service**:
$$
C_v = \frac{Q \sqrt{G}}{\sqrt{\Delta P}}
$$

For **compressible (gas/vapor) service**, when $\Delta P < 0.5 P_1$ (sub-critical, non-choked):
$$
C_v = \frac{Q}{N_1} \sqrt{\frac{G \cdot T}{\Delta P \cdot P_2}}
$$

For **choked gas** ($\Delta P \geq 0.5 P_1$):
$$
C_v = \frac{Q}{0.5 \, N_1} \sqrt{\frac{G \cdot T}{P_1 \cdot P_2}}
$$

where:
- $Q$ = volumetric flow rate
- $G$ = specific gravity (water = 1.0; air = 1.0)
- $\Delta P$ = pressure drop across the valve = $P_1 - P_2$ (psi for $C_v$; bar for $K_v$)
- $N_1$ = numerical constant (39.3 for $C_v$ with $Q$ in SCFH; 1.0 if using $Q$ in GPM)
- $T$ = absolute temperature (°R or K)
- $P_1, P_2$ = inlet and outlet pressures (psia or bara)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Liquid sizing correction (avoid cavitation):** When the calculated $\Delta P$ exceeds a critical value, the $C_v$ must be corrected for choked flow, viscosity, or cavitation. The **Cavitation Index** $K_c$ and **Pressure Recovery Factor** $F_L$ are critical sub-topics in the KTU 2024 syllabus.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (Print This!)

| # | Formula | Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\phi_{\text{lin}} = n$ | Linear characteristic | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $\phi_{\text{eq\%}} = R^{(n-1)}$ | Equal-percentage | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $\phi_{\text{quick}} = \sqrt{n}$ | Quick-opening | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $C_v = \dfrac{Q\sqrt{G}}{\sqrt{\Delta P}}$ | Liquid sizing | GPM, psi |
| 5 | $C_v = \dfrac{Q}{N_1}\sqrt{\dfrac{GT}{\Delta P \, P_2}}$ | Sub-critical gas | SCFH, psi, °R |
| 6 | $C_v = \dfrac{Q}{0.5 \, N_1}\sqrt{\dfrac{GT}{P_1 P_2}}$ | Choked gas | SCFH, psi, °R |
| 7 | $K_v = 1.156 \cdot C_v$ | Metric conversion | m³/h, bar |
| 8 | $R = \dfrac{C_{v,\max}}{C_{v,\min}}$ | Rangeability | Dimensionless |
| 9 | $\text{PFD} \approx 10^{-\text{SIL}}$ | Safety integrity | Probability |
| 10 | $\text{Overpressure} = \dfrac{P_{\text{set}} - \text{MAWP}}{\text{MAWP}} \leq 0.10$ | ASME Section VIII | % |
| 11 | $\text{Blowdown} = \dfrac{P_{\text{set}} - P_{\text{reseat}}}{P_{\text{set}}} \times 100\%$ | PSV performance | % (2–7% typical) |
| 12 | $Q = C_v \sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta P}{G}}$ | Reverse sizing (find Q from Cv) | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** The pipe-size correction factor $F_p$ (for $C_v$ to pipe geometry) and the **Reynolds correction factor** $F_R$ (for highly viscous laminar flow, $Re < 10^4$) are common 3–4 mark sub-parts in KTU ESE.

## 2.7 Real-World Utility

Throttling and safety control are **the two pillars of operational safety and efficiency** in any modern plant. A few examples:
- **Refineries:** Throttling valves regulate crude-feed to distillation columns; PSVs protect fired heaters from over-pressure.
- **Power Plants:** Throttling governs feedwater to boilers; safety valves protect the steam drum.
- **Pharmaceuticals:** Diaphragm valves throttle CIP (clean-in-place) chemicals; sanitary relief valves protect bioreactors.
- **LNG / Cryogenic:** Cryogenic throttling valves regulate LNG vaporization; rupture discs protect storage tanks.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Equal-Percentage Characteristic Equation

We start with the differential definition. By definition, an equal-percentage valve produces a flow change of $\Delta\phi/\phi$ that is a constant percentage for each unit of stem travel $\Delta n$. That is:

$$
\frac{d\phi}{\phi} = k \, dn
$$

**Step 1:** Separate the variables.

$$
d\phi = k \phi \, dn
$$

**Step 2:** Divide both sides by $\phi$ to obtain a standard separable form.

$$
\frac{d\phi}{\phi} = k \, dn
$$

**Step 3:** Integrate from the lower limit (valve just cracked: $\phi = 1$, $n = 0$) to an arbitrary operating point $(\phi, n)$.

$$
\int_{1}^{\phi} \frac{d\phi}{\phi} = \int_{0}^{n} k \, dn
$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the integrals. The left side is $\ln(\phi) - \ln(1) = \ln \phi$.

$$
\ln \phi = k \cdot n
$$

**Step 5:** Exponentiate both sides to obtain the explicit characteristic.

$$
\phi = e^{k n}
$$

**Step 6:** Recognize that at $n = 1$ (fully open), $\phi = R$ (rangeability). Substitute to find the constant $k$.

$$
R = e^{k \cdot 1} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad k = \ln R
$$

**Step 7:** Substitute back.

$$
\phi = e^{n \ln R} = R^n
$$

But the conventional KTU / ISA definition uses $R^{(n-1)}$ so that the characteristic starts at unity when $n = 0$ in some references. Both are valid; the KTU-preferred form is:

$$
\boxed{\;\phi = R^{(n - 1)}\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** At $n = 0$: $\phi = R^{-1}$ (valve barely cracked). At $n = 1$: $\phi = R^{0} = 1$ (full flow normalized). This satisfies the normalization at full open only when we define $\phi$ as flow/max-flow, which is the KTU convention.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Liquid Sizing $C_v$ from Bernoulli + Continuity

We begin with the steady-state mechanical-energy balance between upstream section (1) and the vena contracta (vc) just downstream of the valve restriction.

**Step 1:** Bernoulli's equation (ideal, no pump, no friction, no elevation change) between (1) and (vc):

$$
\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} = \frac{P_{vc}}{\rho g} + \frac{v_{vc}^2}{2g}
$$

**Step 2:** In a pipe, $v_1 \ll v_{vc}$ (vena contracta is the smallest cross-section), so $v_1$ is neglected.

$$
\frac{P_1 - P_{vc}}{\rho g} = \frac{v_{vc}^2}{2g}
$$

**Step 3:** Solve for the velocity at the vena contracta.

$$
v_{vc} = \sqrt{\frac{2 (P_1 - P_{vc})}{\rho}}
$$

**Step 4:** Recognize that the actual valve pressure drop $\Delta P = P_1 - P_2$ is greater than $P_1 - P_{vc}$ because of pressure recovery. Define the **pressure-recovery factor**:

$$
F_L^2 = \frac{\Delta P_{\text{vena contracta}}}{\Delta P_{\text{valve}}} = \frac{P_1 - P_{vc}}{P_1 - P_2}
$$

Thus $P_1 - P_{vc} = F_L^2 (P_1 - P_2) = F_L^2 \Delta P$.

**Step 5:** Substitute back.

$$
v_{vc} = \sqrt{\frac{2 F_L^2 \Delta P}{\rho}}
$$

**Step 6:** Mass flow rate: $\dot{m} = \rho A_{vc} v_{vc} = \rho C_d A_{vc} v_{vc}$, where $C_d$ is the discharge coefficient that lumps $A_{vc}$ and non-idealities.

$$
\dot{m} = C_d A_{vc} \sqrt{2 \rho F_L^2 \Delta P}
$$

**Step 7:** Convert to volumetric flow $Q$ in **GPM** with $\Delta P$ in **psi**, water as reference (SG = 1), and define $C_v$ as the GPM that produces 1 psi drop with $C_d A_{vc}$ appropriately embedded. For water ($\rho = 62.4 \text{ lbm/ft}^3$, $g_c = 32.174$):

$$
C_v = \frac{Q \sqrt{G}}{\sqrt{\Delta P}}
$$

**Step 8:** Final KTU result — confirmed by the **ISA-75.01.01** standard. The constant of proportionality is unit-dependent; for $Q$ in GPM and $\Delta P$ in psi, no extra constant is needed.

$$
\boxed{\;C_v = \frac{Q \sqrt{G}}{\sqrt{\Delta P}}\;}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Choked-Flow Correction for Gas Service

When $\Delta P$ exceeds $0.5 \, P_1$, the flow becomes **choked** at the vena contracta, reaching the local sonic velocity. The maximum mass flow is:

**Step 1:** The critical pressure ratio across the vena contracta is:

$$
\frac{P_{vc}}{P_1} = \left( \frac{2}{\gamma + 1} \right)^{\gamma / (\gamma - 1)}
$$

For air ($\gamma = 1.4$): $P_{vc}/P_1 \approx 0.528$. This is the limit of "critical flow".

**Step 2:** The maximum (choked) flow equation is:

$$
\dot{m}_{\max} = C_d A_{vc} P_1 \sqrt{\frac{\gamma}{R T_1} \left( \frac{2}{\gamma + 1} \right)^{(\gamma + 1)/(\gamma - 1)}}
$$

**Step 3:** Substituting the dimensionless constants for air and converting to SCFH yields, in the KTU form:

$$
C_v = \frac{Q}{0.5 \, N_1} \sqrt{\frac{G \, T}{P_1 P_2}}
$$

This is identical to the sub-critical equation, but with an extra factor of $1/0.5 = 2$ in the denominator, reflecting that the effective pressure drop is bounded at $0.5 P_1$.

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (Part-B Style)

**Problem:** Size a control valve for water flow: $Q = 250 \text{ GPM}$, $G = 1.0$, $\Delta P = 15 \text{ psi}$. Find $C_v$ and select the nearest standard size with a 1-inch trim.

**Step 1:** Apply the liquid $C_v$ formula.

$$
C_v = \frac{Q \sqrt{G}}{\sqrt{\Delta P}}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute numerical values.

$$
C_v = \frac{250 \times \sqrt{1.0}}{\sqrt{15}}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the square root of 15.

$$
\sqrt{15} = 3.873
$$

**Step 4:** Final division.

$$
C_v = \frac{250}{3.873} = 64.55
$$

**Step 5:** Round up to the next standard $C_v$ (e.g., 65 or 70). **Recommended selection: 70** (next standard size) to avoid over-sizing.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often forget the $\sqrt{G}$ term and write $C_v = Q/\Delta P$, which is **wrong by a factor of $\sqrt{\Delta P}$**!

## 3.5 Safety Relief Valve Sizing

For a saturated-steam relief valve on a fire-case scenario:

$$
A = \frac{W}{C K P_1 K_{SH} K_N}
$$

where:
- $A$ = required discharge area (in²)
- $W$ = required mass flow (lbm/hr)
- $C$ = coefficient from API 520 (typically 0.235 for steam)
- $K$ = capacity correction (back-pressure correction, $K_b$, for balanced bellows; otherwise $K_w$ or $K_c$).
- $P_1$ = set pressure + overpressure allowance (psia)
- $K_{SH}$ = superheat correction (1.0 for saturated)
- $K_N$ = Napier correction (typically 1.0 for ASME Section I)

This is a **14-mark, multi-part** problem in KTU ESE.

## 3.6 Safety Integrity Levels (SIL) — Decision Matrix

| SIL | PFDavg | Risk Reduction Factor (RRF) | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 4 | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-5}$ | 10,000 to 100,000 | Nuclear, aviation |
| 3 | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-4}$ | 1,000 to 10,000 | Refinery SIS, burners |
| 2 | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-3}$ | 100 to 1,000 | ESD on turbines, fire suppression |
| 1 | $10^{-1}$ to $10^{-2}$ | 10 to 100 | Alarms, secondary trips |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define throttling in the context of industrial process control. Mention two differences between a throttling valve and an on-off (isolation) valve.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Throttling:** A throttling device is a final control element that continuously varies its flow area in response to a controller signal (typically 3–15 psi pneumatic or 4–20 mA electronic) so as to regulate a process variable to a setpoint.

**Two differences vs. an on-off (isolation) valve:**

| # | Throttling Valve | On-Off / Isolation Valve |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Operates over the full 0–100% travel range continuously | Operates only in fully open or fully closed position |
| 2 | Designed for fine proportional control; tight shut-off is secondary | Designed for bubble-tight shut-off to isolate equipment |
| 3 | Equipped with a positioner for accurate stem positioning | Generally no positioner; uses a hand-wheel or actuator |
| 4 | Characterized trim (linear, equal %, quick-opening) | Uncharacterized — flow area is constant when open |

(Any two points, 1.5 marks each. Full marks = 3.)

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is a Safety Instrumented System (SIS)? Briefly state the meaning of SIL.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**SIS Definition (2 marks):** A Safety Instrumented System is an independent, dedicated system comprising sensors, logic solvers, and final elements specifically designed to take a process to a safe state when predetermined conditions are violated. It is separate from the Basic Process Control System (BPCS) and operates in **fail-safe** or **fault-tolerant** mode as per **IEC 61511**.

**SIL (1 mark):** **Safety Integrity Level** is a discrete classification (1, 2, 3, or 4) indicating the required probability of failure on demand (PFD) of a Safety Instrumented Function. SIL 1 is the lowest integrity (PFD ≈ $10^{-1}$); SIL 4 is the highest (PFD ≈ $10^{-5}$).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a pneumatically-actuated globe control valve with a positioner. Discuss its inherent flow characteristics.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** A control valve is required to handle 200 GPM of oil (specific gravity 0.85) with a pressure drop of 12 psi across the valve. Determine the required $C_v$ and recommend a standard valve size.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Construction (4 marks):**
- **Body and Bonnet:** Cast or forged steel body houses the trim; the bonnet provides stem packing.
- **Trim:** Plug, seat, stem, and cage. Single- or double-seated; characterized by cage perforations or plug contour.
- **Actuator:** Diaphragm actuator (spring-opposed); 3–15 psi signal deflects the diaphragm against the spring to move the stem.
- **Positioner:** A local pneumatic closed-loop that compares the I/P-converted controller signal (3–15 psi) with the stem-position feedback and modulates the actuator supply pressure to eliminate hysteresis and provide accurate stem positioning.
- **I/P Converter:** Translates 4–20 mA electronic signal to 3–15 psi pneumatic signal.

**Working (1.5 marks):**
- Increased controller output → higher air pressure on diaphragm → stem moves down (air-to-close in direct-acting) → flow decreases.
- The positioner continuously trims the actuator air to match stem position to controller demand.

**Inherent Flow Characteristics (1.5 marks):**
- **Linear:** $\phi = n$ — flow proportional to stem travel.
- **Equal-Percentage:** $\phi = R^{(n-1)}$ — flow changes by a fixed % per unit stem travel.
- **Quick-Opening:** $\phi = \sqrt{n}$ — large flow change at small opening.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1:** Write the liquid $C_v$ formula.

$$
C_v = \frac{Q \sqrt{G}}{\sqrt{\Delta P}}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute: $Q = 200$ GPM, $G = 0.85$, $\Delta P = 12$ psi.

$$
C_v = \frac{200 \times \sqrt{0.85}}{\sqrt{12}}
$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the square roots.

$$
\sqrt{0.85} = 0.9220, \qquad \sqrt{12} = 3.4641
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the numerator and denominator.

$$
C_v = \frac{200 \times 0.9220}{3.4641} = \frac{184.40}{3.4641}
$$

**Step 5:** Final result.

$$
\boxed{\;C_v = 53.23\;}
$$

**Recommendation (1 mark):** Select a valve with $C_v = 58$ (next standard size) to provide 10–15% over-capacity margin. With a 2-inch trim, a globe valve with an equal-percentage characteristic is recommended for general process control.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** Explain the working of a Pressure Safety Valve (PSV). Define set pressure, blowdown, and overpressure as per **API 520**.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** A saturated steam PSV on a boiler is set at 12 bar (g). The MAWP is 11.5 bar (g). The required relief load is 8000 kg/hr. Using API 520 coefficients, size the discharge area.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**PSV Working (3 marks):**
A PSV is a spring-loaded valve held closed by a spring force exceeding the upward hydraulic force on the disc. When system pressure exceeds the set pressure:
1. The hydraulic lift force overcomes the spring force.
2. The disc lifts off the nozzle.
3. Fluid discharges to a safe vent/flare.
4. When pressure falls below the **reseat pressure**, the spring pushes the disc back onto the nozzle, resealing the system.

**Definitions (4 marks):**

- **Set Pressure (3.5):** The inlet gauge pressure at which the PSV begins to open. As per **API 520**, set pressure should be ≥ MAWP and ≤ MAWP × 1.10 (10% accumulation).
- **Blowdown (3.5):** The difference between set pressure and reseat pressure, expressed as a percentage of set pressure: $\text{Blowdown} = \dfrac{P_{\text{set}} - P_{\text{reseat}}}{P_{\text{set}}} \times 100\%$. Typical: 2–7% for modulating, 5–10% for conventional.
- **Overpressure (3.5):** The pressure rise above the set pressure during discharge. Limited to **10%** for non-fire cases and **21%** for fire exposure as per **ASME Section VIII** (UG-125).
- **Reseat Pressure (1.5):** The pressure at which the disc re-establishes contact with the nozzle after blowdown.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1:** Convert to SI units. Set pressure = 12 bar(g) = 13.013 bar(a) (assuming 1.013 bar atm). MAWP = 11.5 bar(g) = 12.513 bar(a).

**Step 2:** Check the overpressure limit. Allowable set pressure = 11.5 × 1.10 = 12.65 bar(g). Since set pressure = 12 bar(g) ≤ 12.65 bar(g), the design is **acceptable**.

**Step 3:** Apply the API 520 area sizing formula for steam:

$$
A = \frac{W}{C \, K \, P_1 \, K_{SH} \, K_N}
$$

**Step 4:** Identify coefficients.
- $C = 0.235$ (saturated steam, ASME Section I).
- $K = 1.0$ (no back-pressure correction; atmospheric discharge assumed).
- $K_{SH} = 1.0$ (saturated).
- $K_N = 1.0$ (no Napier correction needed).
- $P_1 = 12 + 1.013 = 13.013$ bar(a) = 188.7 psia.

**Step 5:** Substitute $W = 8000$ kg/hr.

$$
A = \frac{8000}{0.235 \times 1.0 \times 13.013 \times 1.0 \times 1.0} \times \text{unit factor}
$$

Converting to API 520 form (lb/hr, psia, in²):

$W = 8000 \text{ kg/hr} \times 2.2046 = 17{,}637 \text{ lbm/hr}$

$$
A = \frac{17{,}637}{0.235 \times 188.7} = \frac{17{,}637}{44.34}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;A = 397.7 \, \text{in}^2\;}
$$

**Step 6:** Select the next standard orifice size (API 520 sizes: D, E, F, G, H, J, K, L, M, N, P, Q, R, T). Orifice **R** has $A_0 = 424 \text{ in}^2$ — appropriate selection.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do not** confuse set pressure (bar gauge) with absolute pressure in the formula — use $P_1$ in **psia**.
> - **Do not** use $C = 0.235$ for gases; that coefficient is **steam-specific**.
> - **Do not** forget to verify the **10% overpressure rule** before computing the area.
> - **Do not** confuse *blowdown* (expressed as % of set pressure) with *overpressure* (expressed as % of MAWP).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Throttling** = continuous, variable restriction of flow via a control valve. Governed by $h_1 = h_2$ (Joule–Thomson).
- ✅ A control valve has four parts: **Body + Trim + Actuator + Positioner**.
- ✅ Three inherent characteristics: **Linear** ($\phi = n$), **Equal-Percentage** ($\phi = R^{(n-1)}$), **Quick-Opening** ($\phi = \sqrt{n}$).
- ✅ **Rangeability** $R$ = ratio of $C_{v,\max}$ to $C_{v,\min}$ (controllable minimum, not leakage).
- ✅ **Liquid $C_v$ formula:** $C_v = \dfrac{Q \sqrt{G}}{\sqrt{\Delta P}}$ (Q in GPM, ΔP in psi).
- ✅ **Gas $C_v$ formulas:** sub-critical vs. choked — check $\Delta P$ vs. $0.5 P_1$.
- ✅ **Kv = 1.156 × Cv** (metric/European unit conversion).
- ✅ Pressure safety valve is a **spring-loaded** protective device; certified to **ASME Section VIII** and **API 520**.
- ✅ **Set Pressure** ≥ MAWP; **Overpressure** ≤ 10% (non-fire) or 21% (fire).
- ✅ **Blowdown** = 2–7% of set pressure for modulating service.
- ✅ **SIS** is **independent** of BPCS; classified by **SIL 1–4** per **IEC 61511**.
- ✅ **PFD** of a SIL-1 system ≈ $10^{-1}$; SIL-3 ≈ $10^{-3}$.
- ✅ **LOPA** uses seven independent protection layers; pressure relief is the **last** active layer before physical protection.
- ✅ Standard orifice letters (API 520): D, E, F, G, H, J, K, L, M, N, P, Q, R, T (memorize the order for KTU 14-mark questions).
- ✅ Common exam traps: forgetting $\sqrt{G}$ in $C_v$ formula; using gauge pressure where absolute is required; using $C = 0.235$ for non-steam service.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Control of Centrifugal Compressors: Capacity Control Method Used
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT743 - INDUSTRIAL PROCESS CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Control of Centrifugal Compressors: Capacity Control Method Used

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

A **centrifugal compressor** is a *radial-flow dynamic turbomachine* that imparts kinetic energy to a gas via a high-speed rotating impeller and subsequently converts this kinetic energy into pressure energy within a stationary diffuser (volute or vaneless space). It is governed by the **Euler Turbomachinery Equation**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Capacity Control** of a centrifugal compressor refers to the systematic adjustment of the compressor's operating point (head, flow, and power) to match the **process demand curve** while maintaining stable operation **above the surge line** and **below the stonewall (choke) line**.

Mathematically, the operating point of the compressor is the **intersection of the compressor performance curve** (Head $H$ vs. Flow $Q$) and the **system resistance curve** (Pressure $\Delta P$ vs. Flow $Q$). Capacity control shifts one or both of these curves to achieve a new equilibrium.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Picture)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Garden Hose Pump:**
> Imagine pumping water through a hose. If you **partially block the suction side** with your thumb, less water enters the pump per revolution. This is **suction throttling**.
> If you **partially kink the delivery hose**, back-pressure rises and less water flows. This is **discharge throttling**.
> If you **adjust a tilting vane at the inlet** to redirect the water entering the impeller, the energy transferred per unit mass changes. This is **variable inlet guide vanes (IGV)**.
> If you **change the pump's motor speed** (using a VFD), both flow and pressure change together according to physical laws. This is **variable-speed control**.

All these actions serve the same purpose: **match the pump's output to what the process actually needs**, avoiding waste and preventing destructive low-flow instabilities (surge).

### 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit |
|-----------|--------|---------------|
| Volumetric flow rate | $Q$ | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ |
| Mass flow rate | $\dot{m}$ | $\text{kg}/\text{s}$ |
| Polytropic head | $H_p$ | $\text{J}/\text{kg}$ |
| Compressor speed | $N$ | $\text{rpm}$ |
| Specific speed | $N_s$ | dimensionless |
| Isentropic exponent | $\gamma$ | $\approx 1.4$ (air) |
| Surge margin | $SM$ | **%** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle of Capacity Control

The compressor characteristic at constant speed is described by:

$$H = a - bQ^2$$

where $a$ and $b$ are constants that depend on impeller geometry and rotational speed. This **downward-opening parabolic curve** dictates that head falls as flow rises. The system curve (for a throttled discharge) is:

$$\Delta P_{sys} = K Q^2$$

where $K$ is the **system resistance coefficient**. The operating point is the intersection of these two curves.

### 2.2 The Compressor Performance Map — Three Critical Curves

For any fixed speed $N$, three curves define the operating envelope:

1. **Head-Flow Curve ($H$ vs $Q$)** : Parabolic, decreasing.
2. **Power-Flow Curve ($P$ vs $Q$)** : Approximately linear-to-quadratic, increasing with $Q$.
3. **Efficiency-Flow Curve ($\eta$ vs $Q$)** : Dome-shaped, peaks at **Best Efficiency Point (BEP)**.

### 2.3 Operating Envelope: Surge and Stonewall

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Surge Line** (Left Boundary): The minimum flow limit below which the compressor experiences **flow reversal and violent pulsations**. Operate strictly to the right of this line.
> - **Stonewall / Choke Line** (Right Boundary): The maximum flow limit where sonic velocity is reached at the impeller eye or diffuser throat. No further increase in $Q$ is possible.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Valid Range |
|---|---------|---------------|-------------|
| 1 | $Q \propto N$ | Flow scales linearly with speed | Affinity law |
| 2 | $H \propto N^2$ | Head scales with speed squared | Affinity law |
| 3 | $P \propto N^3$ | Power scales with speed cubed | Affinity law |
| 4 | $H = a - bQ^2$ | Compressor head curve (fixed $N$) | $Q_{surge} \le Q \le Q_{choke}$ |
| 5 | $\Delta P_{sys} = K Q^2$ | System resistance curve | All flows |
| 6 | $N_s = \dfrac{N \sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}}$ | Specific speed classification | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $SM = \dfrac{Q_{op} - Q_{surge}}{Q_{op}} \times 100$ | Surge margin | Reported in **%** |
| 8 | $\dot{m} = \rho \, A \, V$ | Mass flow relation | At inlet |
| 9 | $W_{isen} = c_p T_1 \left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1\right]$ | Isentropic compressor work | Ideal gas |
| 10 | $\dfrac{Q_1}{Q_2} = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2}$ | Speed-flow affinity | Geometrically similar machines |

> **Note on Notation:** Absolute values in the table above use `\vert` only when required; the formulas are rendered with standard arithmetic.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

Capacity control of centrifugal compressors is the cornerstone of **petrochemical FCC units, LNG plants, air separation units (ASUs), pipeline booster stations, and large refrigeration loops**. Modern installations integrate **Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs)**, **Anti-Surge Recycle Valves**, and **Inlet Guide Vane (IGV) actuators** in a **Distributed Control System (DCS)** to maintain operation within the safe envelope with **minimum specific energy consumption (SEC)**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Compressor Affinity Laws

Starting from the **Euler turbomachinery equation** for a radial machine:

$$H_{th} = U_2 \, c_{\theta 2} - U_1 \, c_{\theta 1}$$

For a centrifugal compressor with negligible inlet pre-whirl, $c_{\theta 1} \approx 0$, so:

$$H_{th} = U_2 \, c_{\theta 2}$$

**Step 1 — Express blade speed $U_2$ in terms of rotational speed $N$:**

The peripheral velocity of the impeller tip is:

$$U_2 = \pi D_2 N / 60$$

So $U_2$ is linearly proportional to $N$, meaning $U_2 \propto N$.

**Step 2 — Express absolute tangential velocity $c_{\theta 2}$:**

For a geometrically similar machine, the absolute velocities are proportional to blade tip speed (because flow angles remain fixed):

$$c_{\theta 2} \propto U_2 \propto N$$

**Step 3 — Combine to obtain head:**

$$H = U_2 \, c_{\theta 2} \propto N \cdot N = N^2$$

Thus:

$$\boxed{H \propto N^2}$$

**Step 4 — Derive flow scaling law:**

Volumetric flow $Q$ is given by $Q = A \cdot V$, where $A$ is the impeller discharge area (fixed for a given machine) and $V$ is the mean meridional velocity. For geometrically similar operation:

$$V \propto U_2 \propto N$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{Q \propto N}$$

**Step 5 — Derive power scaling law:**

Shaft power $P = \rho Q H$. Substituting the two scaling relations:

$$P \propto N \cdot N^2 = N^3$$

Hence:

$$\boxed{P \propto N^3}$$

### 3.2 Derivation of the Compressor Operating Point Equation

The operating point $(Q^*, H^*)$ satisfies simultaneous equality of compressor and system curves:

$$H_{comp}(Q^*) = \Delta P_{sys}(Q^*)$$

At a fixed speed $N$:

$$a - b(Q^*)^2 = K (Q^*)^2$$

Rearranging:

$$a = (b + K) (Q^*)^2$$

Solving for the equilibrium flow:

$$Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{a}{b + K}}$$

**Interpretation:** Increasing the system resistance $K$ (e.g., partially closing a discharge throttle valve) **reduces the equilibrium flow** $Q^*$, thereby achieving capacity reduction.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix of Capacity Control Methods

| Method | Mechanism | Curve Shifted | Turndown | Energy Efficiency | Surge Risk if Mismanaged |
|--------|-----------|---------------|----------|-------------------|--------------------------|
| **Suction Throttling** | Reduces inlet pressure $\rightarrow$ reduces inlet density $\rightarrow$ reduces mass flow | Compressor $H$-$Q$ curve shifts down | **Poor** (~ 50 %) | Low | Moderate |
| **Discharge Throttling** | Adds artificial system resistance | System curve steepens | Poor (~ 40 %) | **Lowest** (wastes power in valve) | Low |
| **Variable Inlet Guide Vanes (IGV)** | Pre-swirls incoming gas opposite to impeller rotation $\rightarrow$ reduces $c_{\theta 2}$ and head | Compressor $H$-$Q$ curve shifts and rotates | Good (~ 65 %) | High | Low (well-controlled) |
| **Variable Speed Control** | Changes $N$, shifting entire $H$-$Q$ curve by $N^2$ | Whole compressor curve shifts | **Excellent** (~ 80 %) | **Highest** | Low (if ramped) |
| **Hot Gas Bypass / Recycle** | Recycles part of discharge back to suction to artificially raise flow | Moves operating point right on the same curve | Moderate | Low (recompression cost) | **Eliminated** (by design) |

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example

> **Problem:** A centrifugal compressor delivers $Q_1 = 12,000 \, \text{m}^3/\text{hr}$ at $N_1 = 6000 \, \text{rpm}$, developing a head $H_1 = 4500 \, \text{m}$. The driver speed is changed to $N_2 = 5000 \, \text{rpm}$. Compute the new flow $Q_2$ and new head $H_2$.

**Solution using affinity laws:**

Apply the flow affinity law:

$$\dfrac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1} = \dfrac{5000}{6000} = \dfrac{5}{6}$$

$$Q_2 = 12000 \times \dfrac{5}{6} = 10000 \, \text{m}^3/\text{hr}$$

Apply the head affinity law:

$$\dfrac{H_2}{H_1} = \left(\dfrac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2 = \left(\dfrac{5}{6}\right)^2 = \dfrac{25}{36}$$

$$H_2 = 4500 \times \dfrac{25}{36} = 3125 \, \text{m}$$

**Power change (for reference):**

$$\dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\dfrac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3 = \left(\dfrac{5}{6}\right)^3 \approx 0.578$$

A 16.7 % reduction in speed yields ~ 42 % reduction in power consumption — this is why **VFD control is the most energy-efficient capacity control method**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Centrifugal Compressor Capacity Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Process Demand Setpoint"] --> B["DCS Capacity Controller"]
    B --> C["Control Signal Selector"]
    C --> D1["IGV Actuator"]
    C --> D2["VFD Speed Reference"]
    C --> D3["Suction Throttle Valve"]
    B --> E["Anti Surge Controller"]
    E --> F["Recycle Bypass Valve"]
    F --> G["Compressor Suction Manifold"]
    D1 --> H["Compressor Casing"]
    D2 --> H
    D3 --> H
    H --> I["Flow Transmitter FT"]
    H --> J["Discharge Pressure Transmitter PT"]
    H --> K["Temperature Transmitter TT"]
    I --> B
    J --> B
    K --> B
    H --> L["Process User Load"]
    L --> M["System Resistance Curve"]
```

### 4.2 Decision Flow — Selection of Capacity Control Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S["Start: Turndown Required"] --> Q1["Turndown less than 30 percent"]
    Q1 --> A1["Use Discharge Throttling"]
    Q2["Turndown 30 to 60 percent"] --> A2["Use Suction Throttling or IGV"]
    Q3["Turndown greater than 60 percent and Energy Critical"] --> A3["Use Variable Speed VFD"]
    Q4["Compressor Near Surge Limit"] --> A4["Use Hot Gas Recycle Anti Surge"]
    Q5["Driver Constraints Steam Turbine Limits"] --> A5["Use IGV as Primary"]
```

### 4.3 Schematic — Operating Map with Control Method Footprints

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph OP["Operating Envelope"]
        SW["Stonewall Line Right Boundary"]
        SL["Surge Line Left Boundary"]
        BEP["Best Efficiency Point BEP"]
        OP1["Operating Point 1 Full Load"]
        OP2["Operating Point 2 IGV Closed"]
        OP3["Operating Point 3 Reduced Speed"]
    end
    OP1 -->|"Move to"| OP2
    OP1 -->|"Move to"| OP3
    SL --- OP1
    SW --- OP1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Footprint $OP_2$ represents the new operating point after IGV closure — the curve rotates/tilts but the speed remains the same. Footprint $OP_3$ represents the new operating point after speed reduction via VFD — the entire curve shifts left/down with energy savings proportional to $N^3$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]** Define the **surge phenomenon** in a centrifugal compressor. Why is surge control essential for safe operation?

**Model Answer:**
Surge is a self-excited, axisymmetric, low-frequency **flow oscillation** that occurs when the compressor's delivered flow falls below a critical minimum. The compressed gas momentarily reverses direction (backflow) before being re-pumped forward, producing violent pulsations in pressure and flow. The phenomenon is destructive — repeated surge events damage bearings, seals, impeller blades, and downstream piping. Surge control is essential because the compressor must always operate to the right of its **surge line** on the performance map; this is enforced using **anti-surge recycle valves**, **IGV control**, or **speed modulation**.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Sample]** Why is **variable-speed control** considered the most energy-efficient method of capacity control for centrifugal compressors?

**Model Answer:**
For geometrically similar operation, the affinity laws give $Q \propto N$, $H \propto N^2$, and $P \propto N^3$. A reduction in speed therefore causes a **cubic reduction in absorbed power**, whereas throttling methods only dissipate excess head as heat across a control valve with no energy recovery. With modern **Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs)**, the driver efficiency remains high across a wide speed range (typically 50–100 % of rated speed), giving turndowns of up to 80 % with the lowest specific energy consumption (kWh per unit of compressed gas).

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module — Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
>
> **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **head–flow (H–Q) and power–flow (P–Q) characteristics** of a centrifugal compressor operating at constant speed. Mark the **surge line**, **stonewall line**, and **best efficiency point (BEP)** on the diagram. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss the working principle of **variable inlet guide vanes (IGV)** as a method of capacity control. Compare its energy efficiency with **discharge throttling**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

The **head–flow curve** of a centrifugal compressor at constant speed is described by:

$$H = a - b Q^2$$

This is a **downward-opening parabola** — head decreases as flow increases. Key points to mark:

- **Stonewall line (right boundary):** Vertical asymptote at $Q = Q_{choke}$ where sonic conditions occur at the impeller eye or diffuser throat.
- **Surge line (left boundary):** Vertical (or near-vertical) line at $Q = Q_{surge}$ below which the compressor cannot maintain stable delivery.
- **Best Efficiency Point (BEP):** The flow at which the dome-shaped efficiency curve attains its peak. The BEP should ideally coincide with the rated design flow.

The **power–flow curve** is **monotonically increasing** and is often fitted as:

$$P = P_0 + c Q + d Q^2$$

where $P_0$ is the no-flow power (significant because the impeller still does work on the trapped fluid). Power at surge is non-zero, while efficiency at surge is essentially zero (because $Q \to 0$).

**Sketch (Mermaid Block Fallback):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Head H axis Y"] --> B["Flow Q axis X"]
    C["Surge Line Qmin Vertical"] --> D["Operating Region"]
    E["Stonewall Line Qmax Vertical"] --> D
    F["H-Q Parabolic Curve Decreasing"] --> D
    G["P-Q Curve Increasing"] --> D
    H["BEP Marked at Design Flow"] --> D
```

**Model Solution (b):**

**IGV Working Principle:**
Variable Inlet Guide Vanes are a ring of **adjustable stationary vanes** located upstream of the impeller eye. By rotating these vanes, the operator controls the **inlet pre-whirl** $c_{\theta 1}$ of the gas entering the impeller.

From the Euler equation:

$$H_{th} = U_2 c_{\theta 2} - U_1 c_{\theta 1}$$

When IGVs are **closed** (more throttled), they impart a **swirl component opposite** to the impeller rotation, increasing $c_{\theta 1}$ in the negative direction. This reduces $c_{\theta 2}$ effectively and therefore reduces the Euler head. The H-Q curve **tilts and shifts downward**, allowing capacity reduction at the same speed without moving toward surge.

**Energy Comparison:**

| Feature | IGV Control | Discharge Throttling |
|---------|-------------|----------------------|
| Energy dissipated as heat | Minimal (pre-whirl is recovered partly) | Maximum (all excess head lost in valve) |
| Turndown range | Up to 65 % | Only 30–40 % |
| Response time | Fast (1–3 s) | Fast |
| Surge protection | Inherent (curve shape changes safely) | None |
| Capital cost | Moderate | Low |

IGVs are **far more energy-efficient** than discharge throttling because they modulate head generation itself rather than wasting it across a valve.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
>
> **(a)** State and derive the **compressor affinity laws** for geometrically similar machines. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A centrifugal compressor delivers $Q_1 = 8000 \, \text{Nm}^3/\text{hr}$ at $N_1 = 5500 \, \text{rpm}$ with a polytropic head $H_1 = 4200 \, \text{m}$. If the speed is reduced to $N_2 = 4400 \, \text{rpm}$, calculate the new flow, new head, and the percentage reduction in absorbed power. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

See derivation in **Section 3.1** above. Summary of results:

$$\boxed{Q \propto N}, \quad \boxed{H \propto N^2}, \quad \boxed{P \propto N^3}$$

The derivations rely on the Euler turbomachinery equation, geometric similarity of the velocity triangles, and the conservation relations $P = \rho Q H$.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — New Flow:**

$$\dfrac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1} = \dfrac{4400}{5500} = 0.8$$

$$Q_2 = 8000 \times 0.8 = 6400 \, \text{Nm}^3/\text{hr}$$

**Step 2 — New Head:**

$$\dfrac{H_2}{H_1} = \left(\dfrac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2 = (0.8)^2 = 0.64$$

$$H_2 = 4200 \times 0.64 = 2688 \, \text{m}$$

**Step 3 — Percentage Power Reduction:**

$$\dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\dfrac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3 = (0.8)^3 = 0.512$$

Power reduction:

$$\Delta P \% = (1 - 0.512) \times 100 = 48.8 \, \%$$

**Conclusion:** A 20 % drop in speed yields a 48.8 % drop in absorbed power — illustrating the **strong energy-saving advantage** of variable-speed capacity control.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do not confuse** the **surge line** with the **stonewall line**. Many students swap left/right boundaries, costing 2 marks instantly on Part (a).
> - In affinity-law problems, **always state the assumptions**: geometrically similar flow, fixed fluid, no pre-whirl, etc. Failure to state assumptions deducts up to 1 mark.
> - For power calculations, examiners expect **explicit use of $P \propto N^3$** — do not compute power via $P = \rho g Q H$ unless densities are given.
> - On IGV questions, mention **pre-whirl**, **negative swirl component**, and **curve tilt (not shift)** for full credit.
> - Use correct **units**: $\text{m}^3/\text{hr}$ for flow, $\text{m}$ (of fluid column) or $\text{J}/\text{kg}$ for head, **never Pa directly** unless stated as pressure rise.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A centrifugal compressor has a **downward-opening parabolic H-Q curve** at fixed speed.
- The operating point is the **intersection** of the compressor curve and the system resistance curve.
- **Surge line** (left) and **stonewall line** (right) bound the safe operating envelope.
- **Anti-surge control** is mandatory in any industrial installation.
- The **compressor affinity laws** are: $Q \propto N$, $H \propto N^2$, $P \propto N^3$.
- **Variable-speed control** is the **most energy-efficient** capacity control method.
- **IGV control** is the second most efficient and is preferred when the driver is a constant-speed steam turbine.
- **Discharge throttling** is the **least efficient** method and is rarely used in modern plants.
- **Suction throttling** reduces inlet density and hence mass flow capacity.
- **Hot-gas recycle / bypass** is the standard **anti-surge strategy** — it moves the operating point to the right of the surge line.
- **Surge margin** is reported as a percentage: $SM = \left(Q_{op} - Q_{surge}\right) / Q_{op} \times 100$.
- Modern capacity control uses **VFD + IGV + anti-surge recycle** in an integrated DCS strategy.
- **Specific speed** $N_s$ classifies a compressor as radial / mixed / axial and indicates its most efficient operating regime.
- For KTU problems, always **show units, show formulas, and state assumptions** before plugging in numbers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Curves of Centrifugal Compressor
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT743 - INDUSTRIAL PROCESS CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Curves of Centrifugal Compressor

# Performance Curves of Centrifugal Compressor

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Official Definition:**
> A **centrifugal compressor** is a *dynamic (rotodynamic) turbomachine* that uses a rotating impeller to impart kinetic energy to a compressible working fluid (gas/vapour), which is subsequently converted into a rise in static pressure within a stationary diffuser (volute or vaneless space). The **performance curves** are a family of graphical relationships plotted between dependent variables (head, efficiency, power, pressure ratio) and the independent variable (volume flow rate $Q$) at fixed rotational speeds $N$, used to predict off-design behaviour, match the compressor to the system, and identify stable operating limits.

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

Imagine you are riding a stationary bicycle whose wheel has scoops on the rim. As you pedal faster, the scoops fling air outward with greater force, and the *resistance* you feel on the pedals increases. 

- **Flow rate $Q$** is like the volume of air scooped per second (more scoops turning = more air moved).
- **Head $H$** is like the "throw distance" of the air — the pressure the impeller can generate.
- **Power $P$** is how hard *you* have to pedal.
- **Efficiency $\eta$** is how cleverly the wheel converts your pedalling effort into useful air movement.

If you pedal very slowly, the air wobbles back and forth chaotically (this is called **surge**). If you pedal extremely fast, the air cannot enter the scoops fast enough and the flow "chokes" (this is called **stone-wall**). The **performance curves** are simply graphs showing how head, power, and efficiency behave between these two limits as you vary the pedalling speed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why these curves matter in Industry:**
> In petrochemical plants (Kerala's major process industry sector), LNG re-liquefaction units, ammonia plants, and air-separation plants (ASU), every centrifugal compressor must be selected such that its *operating point* (intersection of compressor curve and system resistance curve) lies comfortably inside the stable envelope, away from the **surge line** by typically **10 %–15 %**.

### 1.2 Key Physical Quantities on the Curves

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Unit | Significance |
|--------|----------|---------------|--------------|
| $Q$ | Volume flow rate | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ | Throughput of the compressor |
| $H$ | Compressor head | $\text{m}$ of fluid (or $\text{J/kg}$) | Energy added per unit weight |
| $\eta$ | Polytropic/isentropic efficiency | dimensionless (or **%**) | Conversion quality |
| $P$ | Shaft power | $\text{W}$ or $\text{kW}$ | Driver input required |
| $r_p$ | Pressure ratio $P_2/P_1$ | dimensionless | Compression strength |
| $N$ | Rotational speed | **rpm** or **rps** | Operating speed |
| $u_2$ | Impeller tip speed | $\text{m/s}$ | Blade peripheral speed |
| $D_2$ | Impeller outer diameter | $\text{m}$ | Geometric parameter |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For a **centrifugal compressor** (compressible flow), $H$ is normally expressed in **Joules per kilogram (J/kg)** rather than metres of fluid, because the gas density changes significantly from inlet to outlet.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Primary Performance Curves

A centrifugal compressor is conventionally characterised by **four families of curves** at *constant rotational speed*:

1. **Head vs Flow Curve** ($H$–$Q$ curve)
2. **Efficiency vs Flow Curve** ($\eta$–$Q$ curve)
3. **Power vs Flow Curve** ($P$–$Q$ curve)
4. **Pressure Ratio vs Flow Curve** ($r_p$–$Q$ curve)

These four families, drawn together on a single chart, are called the **compressor performance map** or **compressor characteristic map**.

### 2.2 Detailed Description of Each Curve

#### (a) Head–Flow Curve ($H$–$Q$)

- The curve is **rising from surge** (low-flow end) and **falling towards choke** (high-flow end).
- Maximum head occurs near the **best-efficiency point (BEP)** but slightly to the *left* of it for typical backward-curved impellers.
- Mathematical form (dimensionless head coefficient $\psi$ as function of flow coefficient $\phi$):

$$\psi = \frac{g H}{u_2^{\,2}} = f(\phi)$$

where the **flow coefficient** is:

$$\phi = \frac{Q}{\pi D_2 b_2 u_2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU exam derivations, you will frequently be asked to derive the **Euler head equation** which forms the theoretical basis of the $H$–$Q$ curve:
> 
> $$H_{th} = \frac{u_2 \, c_{2\theta} - u_1 \, c_{1\theta}}{g}$$

For a radial-entry machine $c_{1\theta} \approx 0$, so:

$$H_{th} = \frac{u_2 \, c_{2\theta}}{g}$$

#### (b) Efficiency–Flow Curve ($\eta$–$Q$)

- A typical curve has a **single peak** at the **Best Efficiency Point (BEP)**.
- The curve is asymmetric: efficiency drops more steeply towards surge than towards choke.
- Polytropic efficiency for a compressible machine:

$$\eta_{poly} = \frac{\dfrac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma} \, R \, T_1 \, \left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\tfrac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1\right]}{W_{shaft}/(\dot{m})}$$

#### (c) Power–Flow Curve ($P$–$Q$)

- Power **rises continuously with flow** for a centrifugal compressor (unlike a centrifugal *pump*, where power has a minimum near shut-off).
- This is because higher flow means higher fluid velocities and hence higher friction losses.

$$P = \dot{m} \cdot \frac{H \cdot g}{\eta} = \rho Q \cdot \frac{H \cdot g}{\eta}$$

or in shaft form:

$$P_{shaft} = \frac{P_2 - P_1}{\rho \, \eta_{poly}} \cdot \dot{m}$$

#### (d) Pressure Ratio–Flow Curve ($r_p$–$Q$)

- Pressure ratio $r_p = P_2 / P_1$ *decreases* as flow increases (similar trend to head curve).
- For multistage compressors, the *overall* $r_p$ is the product of stage pressure ratios.

### 2.3 The Stable Operating Envelope

The **surge line** (left boundary) and the **stone-wall / choke line** (right boundary) bound a region of stable operation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Surge** — A self-excited, low-frequency, large-amplitude oscillation where flow momentarily reverses. Caused by inability of the system downstream to accept the instantaneous discharge. Surge is **destructive** and must be avoided.
> 
> **Stone-wall / Choke** — The condition at maximum flow where the velocity of sound is reached at some point in the flow path (typically impeller inlet). No further increase in flow is possible regardless of how low the downstream pressure falls.

### 2.4 The Compressor Map (Multiple Speed Curves)

When the compressor speed is changed, the entire set of curves shifts. By plotting a family of curves at $N_1, N_2, N_3 \ldots$ on a single $H$–$Q$ plane, one obtains the **compressor map**.

The lines of *constant efficiency* (iso-efficiency lines) and *constant surge margin* are also superimposed to guide operation.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cheat-Sheet for KTU 2024 Examinations — Print and Memorise**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $H_{Euler} = \dfrac{u_2 c_{2\theta} - u_1 c_{1\theta}}{g}$ | Euler head equation (energy added per unit weight) | $\text{m}$ |
| 2 | $\psi = \dfrac{g H}{u_2^{\,2}}$ | Head coefficient (dimensionless) | — |
| 3 | $\phi = \dfrac{Q}{\pi D_2 b_2 u_2}$ | Flow coefficient (dimensionless) | — |
| 4 | $H_{poly} = c_p T_1 \left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\tfrac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}} - 1\right]\dfrac{1}{g}$ | Polytropic head in terms of pressure ratio | $\text{m}$ |
| 5 | $r_p = \dfrac{P_2}{P_1}$ | Pressure ratio | dimensionless |
| 6 | $P_{shaft} = \dfrac{\rho Q g H}{\eta}$ | Shaft power requirement | $\text{W}$ |
| 7 | $\eta_{poly} = \dfrac{H_{ideal}}{H_{actual}}$ | Polytropic efficiency | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\eta_{isen} = \dfrac{T_{2s} - T_1}{T_{2a} - T_1}$ | Isentropic (adiabatic) efficiency | dimensionless |
| 9 | $\dfrac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1}$ | Affinity Law 1 (flow) | — |
| 10 | $\dfrac{H_2}{H_1} = \left(\dfrac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^{2}$ | Affinity Law 2 (head) | — |
| 11 | $\dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\dfrac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^{3}$ | Affinity Law 3 (power) | — |
| 12 | $\Delta h_{loss} \propto Q^{2}$ | Frictional / recirculation loss (origin of falling $H$–$Q$ curve) | $\text{J/kg}$ |
| 13 | $SM = \dfrac{Q_{BEP} - Q_{OP}}{Q_{BEP}} \times 100$ | Surge Margin (operational safety metric) | **%** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - In **HVAC** systems, the $H$–$Q$ curve is matched to the duct static-pressure curve for damper sizing.
> - In **oil & gas**, surge control valves (anti-surge recycle) are sized using the surge line position at every operating speed.
> - In **aerospace**, surge margin is one of the most critical specifications of an engine — a turbofan compressor must keep surge margin above a fixed minimum across the entire flight envelope.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Origin of the Falling $H$–$Q$ Characteristic

The **theoretical head** supplied by an ideal, loss-free centrifugal compressor is given by the Euler equation. For a radial-entry impeller:

$$H_{th} = \frac{u_2 \, c_{2\theta}}{g}$$

For an *ideal* (no-slip) impeller, the whirl component at exit follows the relation $c_{2\theta} = u_2 - c_{r2}/\tan\beta_2$. Substituting:

$$H_{th} = \frac{u_2}{g}\left(u_2 - \frac{c_{r2}}{\tan\beta_2}\right)$$

Recognising that $c_{r2} \propto Q$ for geometrically similar operation:

$$\frac{c_{r2}}{\tan\beta_2} \propto Q$$

Hence:

$$H_{th} = \frac{u_2^{\,2}}{g} - \underbrace{\frac{u_2 \, c_{r2}}{g \, \tan\beta_2}}_{\propto \, Q}$$

So the theoretical head decreases linearly with flow. But in a **real** machine, additional losses appear:

| Loss Type | Proportional to | Effect on $H$–$Q$ |
|-----------|-----------------|--------------------|
| Friction in impeller & diffuser | $Q^{2}$ | Lowers $H$ at high $Q$ |
| Incidence loss | $(Q - Q_{design})$ | Lowers $H$ away from design $Q$ |
| Recirculation loss (off-design) | complex | Creates the **steep rise** near surge |
| Clearance loss | $(1 - Q/Q_{max})$ | Always subtracts |

**Combined** actual head:

$$\begin{aligned}
H_{actual} &= H_{th} - \Delta h_{fric} - \Delta h_{incidence} - \Delta h_{recirc} - \Delta h_{clearance} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{u_2^{\,2}}{g} - K_1 Q - K_2 Q^{2} - K_3(Q - Q_{BEP})^{2} - K_4\left(1 - \frac{Q}{Q_{max}}\right)
\end{aligned}$$

This is the mathematical origin of the *hump-shaped* or *monotonically falling* $H$–$Q$ curve we plot.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Power Curve

Starting from the Euler head and recognising that power = (mass flow × head × g / efficiency):

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{shaft} &= \dot{m} \cdot \frac{H \cdot g}{\eta} \\[4pt]
&= \rho \, Q \cdot \frac{H \cdot g}{\eta}
\end{aligned}$$

For a *constant* speed and approximately *constant* efficiency near the design point, $H \approx H_{th} - K Q$, hence:

$$P_{shaft} \propto Q \cdot (a - bQ) = aQ - bQ^{2}$$

Differentiating to find the minimum power:

$$\frac{dP}{dQ} = a - 2bQ = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q_{min\,P} = \frac{a}{2b} = \frac{Q_{design}}{2}$$

This explains why **pumps** have a minimum-power point at half-design flow, but in compressors the efficiency drop near surge is so steep that power **rises monotonically** throughout the operating range.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Affinity Laws (Constant-Diameter Scaling)

Affinity laws relate performance at two different rotational speeds for the *same physical machine* (geometry unchanged). Apply dimensional analysis on Euler head:

$$H = \frac{u_2 \, c_{2\theta}}{g} \quad \text{with} \quad u_2 \propto N, \; c_{2\theta} \propto N$$

Hence $H \propto N^{2}$.

For flow, $Q = \pi D_2 b_2 c_{r2}$. With $D_2, b_2$ unchanged, $c_{r2} \propto u_2 \propto N$, therefore:

$$Q \propto N$$

For power, $P = \rho Q g H \propto N \cdot N^{2} = N^{3}$.

The three affinity laws can be written compactly:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{Q_2}{Q_1} &= \frac{N_2}{N_1} \\[4pt]
\frac{H_2}{H_1} &= \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^{2} \\[4pt]
\frac{P_2}{P_1} &= \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^{3}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Note:** Affinity laws apply only when:
> 1. The fluid is the same (same $\rho$, $\gamma$, $R$).
> 2. Geometric similarity is preserved.
> 3. The *efficiency* at the two operating points is the same (i.e., the points lie on the same iso-efficiency curve on the compressor map).
> 4. The flow is in the *turbulent Reynolds-independent* regime ($Re > 10^{5}$).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Surge Margin (KTU Hot Topic)

Surge margin is a *dimensionless safety index* that quantifies how far the operating point lies from the surge line. The standard aerodynamic definition is:

$$SM = \frac{\Delta H_{surge} - \Delta H_{OP}}{\Delta H_{surge}}$$

where $\Delta H$ values are read from the compressor map at the *same reduced flow*. A common alternate KTU formulation:

$$SM = \frac{Q_{BEP} - Q_{operating}}{Q_{BEP}} \times 100 \;\; (\%)$$

**Worked Numerical Example (typical KTU numerical):**

> A centrifugal compressor has a design flow of **$12\,\text{m}^3/\text{s}$** at the best-efficiency point. The actual operating flow is **$9.5\,\text{m}^3/\text{s}$**. Calculate the surge margin.

$$SM = \frac{12 - 9.5}{12} \times 100 = \frac{2.5}{12} \times 100 = 20.83\,\%$$

Since $SM > 10\,\%$, the operating point is safely away from surge.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Centrifugal Compressor vs Reciprocating Compressor

| Feature | Centrifugal Compressor | Reciprocating Compressor |
|---------|------------------------|--------------------------|
| $H$–$Q$ curve shape | Continuous, falling | Flat (constant delivery) |
| Flow regulation | By speed or recycle | By clearance pockets / unloaders |
| Surge risk | High | None |
| Efficiency curve | Has peak (BEP) | Almost flat at high values |
| Power–flow curve | Monotonically rising | Falls with decreasing flow |
| Pressure ratio per stage | 3–5 typical | up to 10+ |
| Pulsation | None | Significant (needs pulsation bottles) |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture of the Compressor Performance Curve System

The following block diagram illustrates how the physical impeller dynamics, fluid properties, and operating conditions combine to produce the four families of performance curves, and how these curves interact with the downstream *system resistance curve* to determine the final operating point.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[ROTATIONAL SPEED N] --> B[IMPELLER TIP SPEED u2]
    B --> C[EULER HEAD EQUATION]
    D[VOLUME FLOW Q] --> C
    C --> E[IDEAL HEAD Hth]
    F[LOSS MODEL fric, incidence, recirculation, clearance] --> G[ACTUAL HEAD H actual]
    E --> G
    G --> H[H VERSUS Q CURVE FAMILY]
    D --> I[POWER = rho Q g H over eta]
    G --> I
    J[POLYTROPIC ETA MODEL] --> I
    I --> K[P VERSUS Q CURVE]
    D --> L[ETA FROM HEAD AND POWER]
    G --> L
    I --> L
    L --> M[ETA VERSUS Q CURVE]
    G --> N[PRESSURE RATIO rp via POLYTROPIC RELATION]
    N --> O[rp VERSUS Q CURVE]
    P[SURGE LINE COMPUTATION] --> Q[STABLE OPERATING ENVELOPE]
    H --> Q
    R[CHOKE LINE COMPUTATION] --> Q
    Q --> S[OPERATING POINT MATCHING]
    T[SYSTEM RESISTANCE CURVE Hs = Ks Q squared] --> S
    S --> U[SELECTED OPERATING POINT]
    U --> V[SURGE MARGIN CHECK]
    V --> W[ANTI SURGE VALVE ACTION]
```

## 4.2 Reading Order for the Performance Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Start: Identify Speed N] --> B[Draw H versus Q]
    B --> C[Plot Efficiency Iso Lines]
    C --> D[Mark Best Efficiency Point BEP]
    D --> E[Draw Surge Line at Left]
    E --> F[Draw Choke Line at Right]
    F --> G[Overlay System Curve]
    G --> H{Intersection inside envelope?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Operating Point OP chosen]
    H -- No --> J[Re-select compressor or throttle]
    I --> K[Compute Surge Margin SM]
    K --> L[Compute Driver Power]
```

## 4.3 Symbolic Curve Set on a Common Plot

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph PLOT["Compressor Performance Map at Constant Speed"]
        HQ[H versus Q curve falling left to right]
        ETA[Eta versus Q curve single peak at BEP]
        POW[P versus Q curve rising monotonically]
        RP[rp versus Q curve falling]
        SURGE[Vertical line at Q surge left boundary]
        CHOKE[Vertical line at Q choke right boundary]
        OP[Circle marking Operating Point]
    end
    HQ --- ETA
    ETA --- POW
    POW --- RP
    SURGE --- CHOKE
    OP --- HQ
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Map — Examiner's Tip:** When KTU questions ask you to *"sketch the performance curves"*, always draw the four curves on the *same* $Q$-axis, mark the **BEP**, the **surge point**, the **choke point**, and the **operating point**. A sketch lacking any one of these four markers will lose marks.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define the term "Surge" as applied to a centrifugal compressor. Under what operating condition does it occur, and why is it dangerous?**

**Model Answer:**

Surge is a **self-excited, low-frequency, large-amplitude flow oscillation** that occurs in a centrifugal compressor when the actual flow rate drops *below* a critical minimum (the surge flow $Q_{surge}$) at a given speed.

It occurs when the compressor continues to deliver a head greater than what the downstream system can absorb. The pressure at the compressor discharge rises rapidly until flow momentarily **reverses** (back-flow), pressure collapses, forward flow resumes, and the cycle repeats — typically at $0.5\,\text{Hz}$ to $10\,\text{Hz}$.

It is **dangerous** because:
1. The reverse-flow bursts cause **mechanical impact loading** on the impeller blades, leading to fatigue failure.
2. It produces high-amplitude **torque fluctuations** on the shaft, damaging bearings and couplings.
3. It causes **overheating** of the gas due to repeated compression–expansion cycles.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Students often confuse surge with **choke**. Remember: Surge = *low-flow* instability, Choke = *high-flow* sonic limit.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**State the three affinity laws for a centrifugal compressor and write one limitation of their applicability.**

**Model Answer:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{Q_2}{Q_1} &= \frac{N_2}{N_1} \\[4pt]
\frac{H_2}{H_1} &= \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^{2} \\[4pt]
\frac{P_2}{P_1} &= \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^{3}
\end{aligned}$$

**Limitation:** Affinity laws are valid only when (i) the geometry remains unchanged, (ii) the fluid properties ($\rho$, $\gamma$) remain unchanged, and (iii) the operating points lie on the *same iso-efficiency line* on the compressor map. They are not accurate near surge or near choke.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Set A]`

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the four performance curves of a centrifugal compressor at constant speed. Label the surge point, choke point, BEP, and operating point. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A centrifugal compressor delivers $Q_1 = 8\,\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ of air at $H_1 = 4500\,\text{m}$ and $N_1 = 6000\,\text{rpm}$, drawing $P_1 = 1200\,\text{kW}$. The speed is now raised to $N_2 = 7500\,\text{rpm}$. Estimate the new flow, head, and power using the affinity laws. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

The four performance curves drawn on a common $Q$-axis are:

1. **Head–Flow ($H$–$Q$):** Starts high at surge, falls monotonically (with a possible hump in some designs) towards choke.
2. **Efficiency–Flow ($\eta$–$Q$):** Has a single peak at BEP.
3. **Power–Flow ($P$–$Q$):** Rises continuously with flow.
4. **Pressure Ratio–Flow ($r_p$–$Q$):** Falls with rising flow.

```
        H, eta, P, rp
            |
   H -----| /\                (H curve)
          |/  \
   eta --|----/\----          (eta curve peak at BEP)
          |   /\
   P -----| /   \              (P curve rising)
          |/     \
   rp ----|-------\            (rp curve falling)
          |        \
   _______|_________\__________ Q
         Qs         Qchoke
        Surge        Choke
                 
   * OP marked at intersection with system curve
   * BEP marked at peak of eta curve
```

**Marking scheme (KTU Examiner's Expectation):**

- Correct axes and curve shapes: **3 marks**
- Identification of surge and choke points: **2 marks**
- Identification of BEP and operating point: **2 marks**

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

Apply affinity laws directly:

**Step 1 — New flow:**

$$Q_2 = Q_1 \cdot \frac{N_2}{N_1} = 8 \cdot \frac{7500}{6000} = 8 \cdot 1.25 = 10\,\text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — New head:**

$$H_2 = H_1 \cdot \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^{2} = 4500 \cdot (1.25)^{2} = 4500 \cdot 1.5625 = 7031.25\,\text{m}$$

**Step 3 — New power:**

$$P_2 = P_1 \cdot \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^{3} = 1200 \cdot (1.25)^{3} = 1200 \cdot 1.953125 = 2343.75\,\text{kW}$$

**Summary:**

| Quantity | Original | New |
|----------|----------|-----|
| $Q$ | $8\,\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ | $\mathbf{10\,\text{m}^3/\text{s}}$ |
| $H$ | $4500\,\text{m}$ | $\mathbf{7031.25\,\text{m}}$ |
| $P$ | $1200\,\text{kW}$ | $\mathbf{2343.75\,\text{kW}}$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Many students write $(7500/6000) = 1.25$ and forget to *square* it for head or *cube* it for power. The cube and square brackets are the most-skipped step. Always rewrite the affinity law formula before substituting.

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Set B]`

**(a)** Derive the Euler head equation for a centrifugal compressor. Clearly state the assumptions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain with a sketch what is meant by the *compressor map*. Describe how iso-efficiency lines and surge lines are used for selection and operation. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — Derivation of the Euler Head Equation

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady, one-dimensional flow at impeller inlet and outlet.
2. Fluid is treated as a continuum; no-slip relative to blade surface at mean radius (although we recognise it fails in reality).
3. Inlet and outlet velocities are uniform across the respective flow areas.
4. Compressible gas behaviour handled separately via polytropic relations; here we work with the energy-per-unit-weight form.

**Derivation:**

Consider the absolute velocity triangle at impeller exit. By definition:

$$\vec{c_2} = \vec{u_2} + \vec{w_2}$$

where:
- $\vec{c_2}$ = absolute velocity at impeller exit,
- $\vec{u_2}$ = blade peripheral velocity,
- $\vec{w_2}$ = relative velocity at impeller exit.

Multiplying both sides by $u_2$ vectorially:

$$u_2 \cdot c_{2\theta} = u_2^{2} - u_2 \cdot w_{2\theta} = u_2^{2} - u_2 \, w_2 \cos\beta_2$$

Applying the **Work–Energy Theorem** (Euler's Turbomachine Equation), the specific work done per unit mass by the impeller on the fluid is:

$$W_{Euler} = u_2 c_{2\theta} - u_1 c_{1\theta}$$

Per unit weight (head):

$$\boxed{H_{Euler} = \frac{u_2 c_{2\theta} - u_1 c_{1\theta}}{g}}$$

For **radial entry** (no pre-whirl), $c_{1\theta} = 0$, so:

$$H_{Euler} = \frac{u_2 \, c_{2\theta}}{g}$$

**Marking Scheme:**
- Velocity triangle setup: **2 marks**
- Work–energy theorem application: **2 marks**
- Final equation with radial entry simplification: **2 marks**
- Assumptions statement: **1 mark**

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — Compressor Map

A **compressor map** is a single chart on which the $H$–$Q$ curves are plotted at *several* rotational speeds ($N_1, N_2, N_3, \ldots$), with *iso-efficiency* contours (curves of equal $\eta$) superimposed, together with the *surge line* (left boundary passing through the surge point of each speed curve) and the *choke line* (right boundary).

**Sketch (Mermaid-safe ASCII representation):**

```
   H ↑        N3 > N2 > N1
    |       N3:  /   ___
    |      N2: /---/   \---   (each curve falls)
    |      N1:/-/         \- 
    |        /             \
    |       /               \
    |      /  iso-eta        \
    |     /   lines           \
    |    /_____________________\  ---- Surge Line (left)
    |    \_____________________\ ---- Choke Line (right)
    |____________________________________→ Q
```

**Selection Procedure:**
1. Overlay the **system resistance curve** $H_s = K_s Q^{2}$ on the map.
2. The intersection with the chosen speed curve gives the operating point.
3. Ensure the operating point lies inside the *surge margin* envelope (typically $SM \geq 10\,\%$).
4. Select the speed curve whose BEP lies closest to the chosen operating point to maximise efficiency.

**Operational Procedure:**
1. If $Q$ drops (system pressure rises), the operating point approaches the surge line.
2. When $SM$ falls below the minimum acceptable value, an **anti-surge valve** opens to recycle gas from discharge to suction, artificially raising the flow and preventing surge.

**Marking Scheme:**
- Definition of compressor map with multiple speed curves: **2 marks**
- Iso-efficiency lines and their use: **2 marks**
- Surge line and selection criterion: **2 marks**
- Anti-surge operating principle: **1 mark**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the radial-entry assumption** in Euler head derivation → -1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing the head curve shape** (pump vs compressor). Pump head usually rises with flow up to BEP then falls; compressor head *usually falls* throughout. Drawing a pump-shaped curve for a compressor = wrong.
> 3. **Mixing up surge and stone-wall** — Surge is *left*, choke is *right*. Direction confusion is penalised.
> 4. **Not labelling units** on axes of the sketch (Head in $\text{m}$ or $\text{J/kg}$; Flow in $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$) → -0.5 marks.
> 5. **Affinity laws applied near surge** — they are inaccurate there. Examiners often test whether you note this caveat.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist — Centrifugal Compressor Curves**

- ☐ **Euler Head Equation:** $H = \dfrac{u_2 c_{2\theta} - u_1 c_{1\theta}}{g}$; for radial entry, $H = \dfrac{u_2 c_{2\theta}}{g}$.
- ☐ **Four Performance Curves:** $H$–$Q$ (falling), $\eta$–$Q$ (single peak at BEP), $P$–$Q$ (rising), $r_p$–$Q$ (falling).
- ☐ **Stable Envelope:** Bounded on the **left by surge line**, on the **right by choke line**; operating point must lie *inside*.
- ☐ **Surge:** Low-flow instability; flow reversal; can damage blades; prevented by anti-surge recycle valve.
- ☐ **Choke (Stone-Wall):** Sonic limit at impeller inlet; maximum achievable flow.
- ☐ **BEP:** Best Efficiency Point — operate as close as possible to it.
- ☐ **Affinity Laws:** $Q \propto N$, $H \propto N^{2}$, $P \propto N^{3}$.
- ☐ **Affinity Law Validity Conditions:** Same fluid, same geometry, same efficiency iso-line, away from surge/choke.
- ☐ **Surge Margin Formula:** $SM = \dfrac{Q_{BEP} - Q_{OP}}{Q_{BEP}} \times 100\,\%$; require $SM \geq 10\,\%$ in industrial practice.
- ☐ **Power Equation:** $P = \dfrac{\rho Q g H}{\eta}$ (shaft) — increases monotonically with $Q$ in compressors.
- ☐ **Head Coefficient:** $\psi = \dfrac{gH}{u_2^{\,2}}$ and **Flow Coefficient:** $\phi = \dfrac{Q}{\pi D_2 b_2 u_2}$ — dimensionless analysis of compressor curves.
- ☐ **Compressor Map:** Multiple speed curves + iso-efficiency contours + surge + choke lines on a single chart.
- ☐ **Anti-Surge Protection:** Mandatory on every industrial centrifugal compressor — recycle valve opening curve derived from surge line.

> [!NOTE]
> **Topper's Strategy:** When asked to *"explain the performance curves"*, always combine (a) **sketch with labelled BEP, surge, choke, OP**, (b) **mathematical relation**, and (c) **industrial significance**. KTU 2024 Scheme examiners award marks across all three dimensions (Recall, Comprehension, Application).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Control of Centrifugal Compressor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT743 - INDUSTRIAL PROCESS CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Control of Centrifugal Compressor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — CONTROL OF CENTRIFUGAL COMPRESSOR

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

A **centrifugal compressor** is a *radial-flow dynamic turbomachine* in which a rotating impeller imparts kinetic energy to a compressible process gas, which is subsequently converted into a static pressure rise inside a stationary diffuser and volute casing. Because the gas is compressible, its density changes appreciably between suction and discharge, and the head–flow–efficiency characteristics are strongly speed-dependent. The **compressor map** is the locus of (Head *H* vs Flow *Q*) curves at constant speed with iso-efficiency and constant-power lines superimposed.

The **objective of control** is to keep the operating point on the desired characteristic curve, satisfy process throughput demand, minimise specific power, and—most importantly—**prevent surge**, which is the unstable low-flow operating regime where the compressor cannot sustain unidirectional flow and violent axial pulsations occur.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Surge is the single most important reason a centrifugal compressor is "controlled" at all.** Without an anti-surge controller, a process upset (e.g., a downstream valve closing) will push the compressor into surge and destroy the machine within seconds.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Garden Hose + Nozzle" Picture

Imagine you are spraying a garden hose with your thumb partly blocking the nozzle.

- **Increase flow** (open the nozzle): the jet is fast and steady. This is the normal operating region — high flow, stable pressure rise.
- **Reduce flow** (squash the nozzle harder): the jet slows, the hose begins to shudder, and at a critical point the water **pulses back and forth** through the nozzle with a loud hammering noise. This back-and-forth oscillation is exactly **surge** in a gas compressor.
- **Close the nozzle fully**: the pump stalls. In a compressor this is called **stone-wall** operation (discharge valve fully shut, max head, zero flow).

So a centrifugal compressor has two forbidden boundaries:
1. **Surge line** (low-flow side) — unstable, destructive pulsations.
2. **Stone-wall line** (high-flow / low-head side) — choking, max discharge pressure, zero margin.

The control system must always keep the operating point *away* from the surge line by a safe **surge margin** (typically 5–15 %).

> [!NOTE]
