KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question Paper Set A]** ### Q1. Define surge in a centrifugal compressor. What are its consequences? (3 Marks) **Model Answer:** Surge is an unstable operating condition in a dynamic compressor characterized by a momentary reversal of flow through the machine, accompanied by violent oscillations in discharge pressure, temperature, and rotor vibration. It occurs when the operating point moves to the left of the surge line on the compressor's H–Q map. **Consequences:** - Axial thrust reversal → bearing failure. - Impeller blade fatigue and cracking. - High discharge temperature → thermal damage to seals. - Process trip and loss of production. --- > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question Paper]** ### Q2. What is the Greitzer B-parameter? What does it signify? (3 Marks) **Model Answer:** The Greitzer B-parameter is a dimensionless number that classifies the dynamic stability of a compressor–plenum system. It is defined as: $$B = \frac{U}{2 a_0} \sqrt{\frac{A_c}{L_p} \cdot \frac{\rho_{in}}{P_{in}} \cdot \frac{RT_{in}}{A_p}}$$ A value of B < 0.4 indicates stable operation, 0.4–0.8 indicates mild surge / rotating stall, and B > 0.8 indicates full classical surge. The higher the B value, the more unstable the system. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 ESE – Module 4 Internal Choice Set]** ### Question A (14 Marks) **Q A(a).** With a neat sketch, explain the centrifugal compressor performance map and define the surge line, stone-wall line, and operating point. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** The compressor performance map is a plot of **polytropic head (H)** versus **inlet volumetric flow (Q)** at a given rotational speed. For each speed, a curve is drawn. **Key features:** - **Surge line:** Left boundary curve. Below this, surge occurs. - **Stone-wall (choke) line:** Right boundary curve. To the right, the flow is choked and head collapses. - **Constant speed lines:** Family of H–Q curves for different N. - **Operating point:** Intersection of the compressor's H–Q curve and the system resistance curve. The **system curve** represents the head required by the downstream piping and process, which increases with flow. The intersection to the right of the surge line is the **stable operating point**. For illustration: ``` H | N3 (max speed) _______stone wall | / N2 ________/ | / N1 ____ / | / surge \ / |/_________\/________ Q ``` The **safe operating envelope** is enclosed by the surge line on the left, the stone-wall line on the right, and the speed limits (N_min, N_max) on top and bottom. --- **Q A(b).** Explain the Antisurge Control loop with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the antisurge valve and its fail-safe position. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Block Diagram:** ``` SP FT +----+----+ +----+----+ | Set | | Flow | | Point | | TX | +----+----+ +----+----+ | | +--->(+) <-------+ | e v +-----+-----+ +--------+ +--------+ | AS-PIC | signal | Valve | air | Antisurge | (PI) |-------->| Pos. |------>| Valve | +-----------+ +--------+ +----+---+ | v (Recycle line to suction) ``` **Operation:** 1. **FT** measures the actual inlet flow $Q_a$. 2. **PT** measures discharge pressure $P_d$. 3. The **Setpoint Calculator** computes the surge line head $H_{surge}(Q_a)$ and adds a margin (typically 5–10%) to get the controller setpoint. 4. The **AS-PIC** compares $H_{actual}$ (from $P_d$) with the setpoint. If $H_{actual}$ falls below the setpoint, the error is positive and the controller **opens** the antisurge valve. 5. The opened valve allows gas to **recycle from discharge back to suction**, increasing the flow through the compressor and pushing the operating point back to the safe region. **Role of the Antisurge Valve:** - It is a **fast-acting control valve** (typically pneumatic, positioner-controlled). - **Fail-safe position: FAIL OPEN** (spring-opened, air-to-close). This ensures that on loss of air or signal, the valve opens and prevents surge — the compressor is protected even under failure conditions. - Sized to pass **1.3 to 1.5 × design recirculation flow**. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #1:** > Students often write that the antisurge valve is **fail-closed**. This is **WRONG and costs 2 marks**. The antisurge valve must be **fail-open** because loss of instrument air must NOT cause surge. Always remember: **antisurge = fail open; antisurge valve saves the machine on air failure.** --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice **Q B(a).** Derive the Greitzer B-parameter for a centrifugal compressor and explain how it predicts surge. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Derivation steps:** **Step 1:** Write the **momentum balance** for the inlet pipe of length $L_p$ and area $A_p$: $$\rho L_p \frac{dU_p}{dt} = (P_c - P_{in}) - F_f$$ **Step 2:** Write the **mass balance** for the plenum volume $V$: $$\frac{dP_{in}}{dt} = \frac{a_0^2 \rho}{V}(A_p U_p - A_c U_c)$$ **Step 3:** Linearize the compressor head characteristic: $$P_c = P_{c,0} + H_0 \rho \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial \phi} \bigg|_0 \cdot (U_c - U_{c,0})$$ **Step 4:** Non-dimensionalize using $\tau = t \cdot a_0 / L_p$, $\delta u = U_p / a_0$, $\delta p = (P_c - P_{c,0})/(\rho a_0^2)$. **Step 5:** Combine and obtain: $$\frac{d^2 \delta p}{d\tau^2} + B^2 \delta p = -B^2 \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial \phi} \delta u$$ **Step 6:** The natural frequency of oscillation is exactly $B$, hence: $$\boxed{B = \frac{U}{2 a_0} \sqrt{\frac{A_c}{L_p} \cdot \frac{R T_{in}}{a_0^2}} = \frac{U}{2 a_0} \sqrt{\frac{A_c}{L_p} \cdot \frac{L_p^*}{1}}}$$ **Surge prediction:** - $B < 0.4$ — Static stability dominates, no surge. - $0.4 < B < 0.8$ — Mild surge / rotating stall appears. - $B > 0.8$ — Deep surge with full flow reversal. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #2:** > Marks are lost if you **skip the non-dimensionalization step** (Step 4) or if you **do not state the cutoff values** of B (Step 6). The cutoffs are mandatory for a complete answer. --- **Q B(b).** A centrifugal compressor has the surge-line equation $H_{surge} = 90 + 0.020 Q^2$ (kJ/kg, Q in m³/s). The design point is $Q_d = 1.4$ m³/s, $H_d = 160$ kJ/kg. If the required flow margin is 8% and head margin is 4%, compute the antisurge setpoint coordinates and determine the recirculated flow needed. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given:** - $H_{surge}(Q) = 90 + 0.020 Q^2$ - $Q_d = 1.4$ m³/s, $H_d = 160$ kJ/kg - Flow margin $m = 0.08$ - Head margin $k_{sh} = 0.04$ **Step 1: Surge head at design flow** $$H_{surge}(1.4) = 90 + 0.020 \times (1.4)^2 = 90 + 0.020 \times 1.96 = 90 + 0.0392$$ $$H_{surge} = 90.0392 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ **Step 2: Setpoint flow with flow margin** $$Q_{sp} = Q_d \cdot (1 + m) = 1.4 \times 1.08 = 1.512 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$ **Step 3: Setpoint head with head margin** $$\Delta H = k_{sh} \cdot H_d = 0.04 \times 160 = 6.4 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ $$H_{sp} = H_{surge}(Q_{sp}) + \Delta H = [90 + 0.020 \times (1.512)^2] + 6.4$$ $$H_{sp} = 90 + 0.020 \times 2.286 + 6.4 = 90 + 0.0457 + 6.4$$ $$\boxed{H_{sp} = 96.446 \text{ kJ/kg}}$$ **Setpoint coordinate:** $(Q_{sp}, H_{sp}) = (1.512 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}, \ 96.45 \text{ kJ/kg})$ **Step 4: Recirculated flow** At design point, the inlet flow to the compressor is $Q_{in} = Q_d = 1.4$ m³/s. The discharge flow to the process $Q_{proc}$ is found by: $$Q_{rec} = Q_{in} - Q_{proc}$$ Assuming the process takes 80% of design flow at the surge margin: $$Q_{proc} = 0.80 \times 1.4 = 1.12 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$ $$Q_{rec} = 1.4 - 1.12 = 0.28 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 1008 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}$$ **Step 5: Antisurge valve $C_v$** (if $G_f = 0.6$, $\Delta P = 0.5$ bar): $$C_v = 1008 \times \sqrt{0.6 / 0.5} = 1008 \times 1.095 = 1104$$ Recommended valve size: **1.3 × 1104 ≈ 1435** (oversize by 30%). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout #3:** > Common errors in numerical questions: > 1. **Unit mismatch:** Always convert $Q$ from m³/hr to m³/s **before** substituting into $H_{surge}$ equation. > 2. **Direction of setpoint:** The setpoint is **right** of the surge line, not left. A common confusion. > 3. **Margins are added to surge, not subtracted.** The setpoint is *above and right* of surge. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Antisurge Control** - ☐ **Surge = flow reversal** in dynamic compressors; occurs when operating point moves left of the **surge line** on the H–Q map. - ☐ **Surge line** is the **left boundary** of the safe operating envelope; **stone-wall line** is the right boundary. - ☐ **Antisurge valve is FAIL-OPEN** (fail-safe to protect the machine). Do NOT mark it fail-closed. - ☐ **Recycle loop** (discharge → suction) is the standard antisurge topology. Blow-off to atmosphere is used for gases that cannot be returned. - ☐ **Greitzer B-parameter** predicts stability: B < 0.4 stable, 0.4–0.8 mild surge, B > 0.8 deep surge. - ☐ **B-parameter formula** must be memorized with all variables: $B = (U/2a_0) \sqrt{(A_c/L_p) \cdot (RT_{in}/a_0^2) \cdot 1}$. - ☐ **Setpoint margin** typically **5–10% in flow** and **3–5% in head** above the surge line. - ☐ **Polytropic head formula** uses $H_p = \frac{n}{n-1} \cdot (ZRT_{in}/M) \cdot [(P_d/P_s)^{(n-1)/n} - 1]$. - ☐ **Polytropic–isentropic relation:** $\frac{n-1}{n} = \frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma} \cdot \eta_p$. - ☐ **Valve sizing:** $C_v = Q_{rec} \sqrt{G_f / \Delta P_{valve}}$; oversize by 30–50%. - ☐ **Antisurge controller is PI/PID with very fast response** (loop time < 1 second), often with rate-of-change override. - ☐ **Symptoms of surge** the operator sees: low flow, low discharge pressure, high vibration, high discharge temperature, audible "gulping." - ☐ **Consequences:** bearing failure, seal damage, blade cracking, compressor destruction. - ☐ **Two antisurge methods:** (1) **Recycle** (open to suction) — most common; (2) **Blow-off** (open to atmosphere) — used for dirty, hazardous, or non-recyclable gases. - ☐ **Decoupling:** Antisurge control overrides the process (capacity) controller — the antisurge loop has higher priority. - ☐ **Speed control interaction:** Reducing N shifts the surge line; the controller must adapt its setpoint as a function of N. > [!TIP] > **Final KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "explain antisurge control," always begin with a **diagram of the compressor map**, then the **block diagram of the control loop**, and finish with the **role of the valve and its fail-safe state**. A 14-mark answer without a diagram is graded as incomplete.
